> The KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly requires** understanding of two fundamental categories of entity sets:

### A. Strong Entity Set
- Has a **primary key** (one or more attributes that uniquely identify each entity).
- Its existence **does NOT depend** on any other entity.
- Represented in ER diagrams by a **single rectangle**.

### B. Weak Entity Set
- Does **NOT** have a sufficient primary key of its own.
- Its existence **depends on** a strong (owner) entity set, usually via an *identifying relationship*.
- Represented in ER diagrams by a **double rectangle**.
- Identified by combining its **partial key** (discriminator) with the primary key of the owner entity set.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Strong entity sets map directly to standalone SQL tables. Weak entity sets map to tables whose **primary key includes a foreign key** from the owner table — a critical concept for normalized database design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Entity Set — The "A-P-K" Trinity

Every entity set in the ER model is fully characterized by three conceptual pillars:

### 2.1.1 Attributes (A)
Properties that **describe** the entities in the set. Formally, an attribute $a$ is a function that maps an entity set $E$ to a value domain:

$$a: E \rightarrow \text{Domain}(a)$$

For example, `GPA : STUDENT → [0.0, 10.0]`.

**Classification of Attributes (KTU-Favorite Question!):**

| Attribute Type | Symbol/Notation | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple / Atomic** | Single oval | Cannot be subdivided meaningfully | `Age`, `Roll_No` |
| **Composite** | Oval with sub-ovals | Can be broken into smaller sub-parts | `Name` → {First, Middle, Last} |
| **Single-valued** | Single oval | Holds exactly one value per entity | `Date_of_Birth` |
| **Multivalued** | **Double oval** | Can hold multiple values | `Phone_Numbers`, `Skills` |
| **Derived** | **Dashed oval** | Value computed from another attribute | `Age` (derived from `DOB`) |
| **Key Attribute** | Oval with **underline** | Uniquely identifies an entity | `Roll_No`, `Emp_ID` |
| **Partial Key (Discriminator)** | Oval with **dashed underline** | Uniquely identifies a weak entity *within* its owner set | `Payment_Number` (in a loan) |

---

### 2.1.2 Primary Key (P)
A **minimal set of attributes** whose values uniquely identify every entity in the entity set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** A primary key must satisfy:
> 1. **Uniqueness** — No two entities share the same key value.
> 2. **Non-nullability** — Key attributes cannot be `NULL`.
> 3. **Minimality** — No proper subset of the key can uniquely identify entities.

Mathematically, the key constraint is:

$$\forall e_1, e_2 \in E, \; e_1 \neq e_2 \implies K(e_1) \neq K(e_2)$$

where $K$ is the key attribute set.

---

### 2.1.3 Cardinality (C)
The number of entity instances currently in the set. KTU loves asking about **mapping cardinalities** in Part B questions (covered in Section 3).

---

## 2.2 Strong vs Weak Entity Set — Deep Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind Weak Entity Sets:** Some real-world objects have no globally unique identifier of their own. For instance, a *room* in a building has no global Room_ID — it is only unique within its building. Hence a *Room* is a **weak entity** that depends on the *Building* (strong entity).

| Feature | Strong Entity Set | Weak Entity Set |
|---|---|---|
| **Existence** | Independent | Depends on a strong (owner) entity |
| **Primary Key** | Self-sufficient | Needs owner's PK + partial key |
| **ER Notation** | Single rectangle | **Double rectangle** |
| **Identifying Relationship** | Not required | **Required** (double diamond) |
| **Total Participation** | Optional | **Mandatory** in identifying relationship |
| **SQL Mapping** | Standalone table | Table with composite FK + PK |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — it covers 90% of what examiners test on Entity Sets!**

| Concept | Formal Notation | Constraint/Rule | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entity set | $E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots\}$ | Set must be **finite** (in practice) | ★★★ |
| Attribute domain | $a: E \to D$ | $D$ is the value domain | ★★ |
| Key uniqueness | $\forall e_i, e_j \in E, \; e_i \neq e_j \implies K(e_i) \neq K(e_j)$ | No duplicates | ★★★ |
| Composite key size | $n$ attributes | All $n$ needed for uniqueness | ★★ |
| Weak entity primary key | $PK_{weak} = PK_{owner} \cup \{\text{partial key}\}$ | Concatenation rule | ★★★★★ |
| Strong entity key domains | $\vert D \vert \geq N_E$ | Domain size must $\geq$ entity count | ★★ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **enterprise software engineering**, entity sets are the **first conceptual artifact** in any database design. They are used to:

1. **Communicate with non-technical stakeholders** — ER diagrams are universal.
2. **Drive the logical schema** — Every strong entity set becomes a base table; weak entity sets become child tables.
3. **Detect design errors early** — Misidentifying a strong vs weak entity can corrupt entire data hierarchies.
4. **Support Object-Relational Mapping (ORM)** — Frameworks like Hibernate (Java) and SQLAlchemy (Python) directly map ER entities to classes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Primary Key Construction for a Weak Entity Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU Board Exams on this topic!**

**Problem:** A `DEPENDENT` weak entity set is owned by `EMPLOYEE` (strong entity). The `EMPLOYEE` entity set has primary key `Emp_ID`. The partial key (discriminator) of `DEPENDENT` is `Dependent_Name`. Derive the primary key of the `DEPENDENT` entity set.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Identify the strong entity's primary key.**
From the given, the primary key of `EMPLOYEE` is:
$$K_{strong} = \{\text{Emp\_ID}\}$$

**Step 2: Identify the partial key (discriminator) of the weak entity set.**
The partial key of `DEPENDENT` is:
$$K_{partial} = \{\text{Dependent\_Name}\}$$

**Step 3: Apply the weak-entity key construction rule.**
The complete primary key of a weak entity set is the **union (set union) of the strong entity's primary key and the weak entity's partial key**:
$$K_{weak} = K_{strong} \cup K_{partial}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**
$$K_{weak} = \{\text{Emp\_ID}\} \cup \{\text{Dependent\_Name}\}$$

**Step 5: Finalize the composite key.**
$$\boxed{K_{weak} = \{\text{Emp\_ID}, \text{Dependent\_Name}\}}$$

**Conversion logic explanation:** The composite key works because (a) a single employee's dependents are uniquely distinguished by name within that employee's family, and (b) when combined with `Emp_ID`, the pair is unique across the entire database.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mapping Cardinality Formulas

Mapping cardinality defines how many entities of one set can associate with entities of another. Let:
- $E_1$ = first entity set, with $\vert E_1 \vert = n_1$ entities
- $E_2$ = second entity set, with $\vert E_2 \vert = n_2$ entities
- $R$ = the relationship between them

### 3.2.1 One-to-One (1:1)
Each entity in $E_1$ relates to **at most one** entity in $E_2$, and vice versa.
$$\vert R \vert \leq \min(n_1, n_2)$$

**Example:** Each `PERSON` has exactly one `PASSPORT`, and each `PASSPORT` belongs to exactly one `PERSON`.

### 3.2.2 One-to-Many (1:N)
Each entity in $E_1$ can relate to many entities in $E_2$, but each $E_2$ entity relates to at most one $E_1$ entity.
$$\vert R \vert \leq n_2 \quad \text{when } n_1 = 1 \text{ per relationship}$$

**Example:** One `DEPARTMENT` employs many `EMPLOYEES`, but each employee works in only one department.

### 3.2.3 Many-to-Many (M:N)
Multiple entities on both sides can relate freely.
$$\vert R \vert \leq n_1 \times n_2$$

**Example:** `STUDENTS` enroll in many `COURSES`, and each `COURSE` has many `STUDENTS`.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying a Weak Entity Set

**Scenario:** A bank database has `LOAN` (strong entity) and `PAYMENT` (transactions against a loan). Each payment is identified only by a sequence number *within* that specific loan.

**Analytical Steps:**

1. **Examine for independent existence:** Does a `PAYMENT` make sense without a `LOAN`? **No** — a payment is meaningless without the loan it satisfies. → Candidate weak entity.

2. **Check for a self-sufficient primary key:** Does `PAYMENT` have a globally unique ID like `Payment_ID`? **No** — only `Payment_Number` (1, 2, 3, ...) per loan. → Confirmed weak.

3. **Identify the partial key:** `Payment_Number` is the **discriminator**.

4. **Identify the identifying relationship:** `LOAN_PAYMENT` (double diamond).

5. **Apply the key formula:**
$$PK_{PAYMENT} = \{\text{Loan\_Number}\} \cup \{\text{Payment\_Number}\}$$

6. **Confirm total participation:** Every `PAYMENT` **must** participate in the `LOAN_PAYMENT` relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic to SQL:** The resulting `PAYMENT` table will have `Loan_Number` as a foreign key, and the primary key `(Loan_Number, Payment_Number)` will be a composite key with a foreign key reference to `LOAN`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid ER Diagram — Strong vs Weak Entity Sets

```mermaid
erDiagram
    EMPLOYEE ||--o{ DEPENDENT : "has (identifying)"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "employs"
    PROJECT }o--o{ EMPLOYEE : "works on"

    EMPLOYEE {
        string Emp_ID PK
        string Emp_Name
        date Date_of_Birth
        float Salary
    }

    DEPENDENT {
        string Emp_ID FK
        string Dependent_Name
        string Relationship
        int Age
    }

    DEPARTMENT {
        string Dept_ID PK
        string Dept_Name
        string Location
    }

    PROJECT {
        string Project_ID PK
        string Project_Name
        float Budget
    }
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram (as a KTU examiner would expect):**
> - `||--o{` = **mandatory one-to-many** (the `||` denotes total participation of the left entity).
> - `}o--o{` = **optional many-to-many** (the `o` denotes partial participation).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Entity Set Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Object e] --> B{Independent Existence?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Strong Entity Set E_strong]
    B -- No --> D{Has Self-Sufficient Key?}
    D -- Yes --> C
    D -- No --> E[Weak Entity Set E_weak]
    C --> F[Assign Primary Key K_strong]
    E --> G[Identify Owner Entity]
    G --> H[Identify Partial Key K_partial]
    H --> I[Composite Key K_weak = K_strong U K_partial]
    F --> J[Single Rectangle in ER]
    I --> K[Double Rectangle in ER]
    J --> L[Standalone SQL Table]
    K --> M[SQL Table with Composite FK PK]
```

**Architecture Flow Explanation:**

1. **A → B:** Start with a candidate real-world object.
2. **B → C or D:** Branch on the "Independent Existence" test.
3. **D → E:** If no key exists, mark as weak.
4. **G → H → I:** Construct the composite key for the weak entity using the union rule.
5. **J, K:** Apply the correct ER notation.
6. **L, M:** Translate to the relational schema — a critical step in KTU's logical database design module.

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart mirrors the exact decision tree the KTU board examiner expects students to articulate in 14-mark Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Entity Set. Differentiate between a strong entity set and a weak entity set with an example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Entity Set:** An entity set is a collection of entities that share common attributes but differ in attribute values. Formally, an entity set $E$ is the set $\{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_n\}$ where each $e_i$ is an individual entity of the same type.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Feature | Strong Entity Set | Weak Entity Set |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Has its own primary key | Cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone |
| **Existence** | Independent of other entities | Depends on an owner (strong) entity |
| **ER Symbol** | Single rectangle | Double rectangle |
| **Example** | `STUDENT` (key: `Roll_No`) | `DEPENDENT` of an employee (key: `Emp_ID` + `Dependent_Name`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** Strong = "Stands alone"; Weak = "Wants help from a strong entity."

---

### Question 2: What is a partial key (discriminator)? Why is it required for a weak entity set? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **partial key** (also called a **discriminator**) is a set of attributes of a weak entity set that, although **not sufficient to uniquely identify** the entity in the entire database, can **uniquely distinguish** the entity **within the owner entity set** to which it is linked.

**Why it is required:**
A weak entity set has no globally unique identifier. Consider a `DEPENDENT` of an `EMPLOYEE`: two employees may both have a dependent named "Rahul," but within one employee's family, "Rahul" is unique. The partial key `Dependent_Name` is therefore necessary to **disambiguate** the weak entity instances. Combined with the owner's primary key, it forms the **full primary key** of the weak entity set:

$$K_{weak} = K_{owner} \cup K_{partial}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions are 14 marks and **have an internal choice** (Module 1 students pick **either** Question A **or** Question B).

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Q.** (a) Explain the concept of an **Entity Set** in the ER model with a suitable example. List and explain the different **types of attributes** with proper ER notation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Design an ER diagram for a University database with the following requirements. Identify the entity sets, their attributes (with keys), and classify each entity set as **strong** or **weak**:
   - `STUDENT` (Roll_No, Name, DOB, Branch)
   - `COURSE` (Course_ID, Course_Name, Credits)
   - `ENROLLMENT` (a student's enrollment in a course, with `Semester` and `Grade` attributes)
   - `HOSTEL_ROOM` (Room_No, Block, Floor) — depends on `HOSTEL`
   - `HOSTEL` (Hostel_ID, Hostel_Name, Warden_Name) **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

An **entity set** is a logical grouping of entities that have the same attributes. For example, the `STUDENT` entity set in a university database contains all currently enrolled students. Each student entity has attributes like Roll_No, Name, etc., but with different values.

**Types of Attributes (with ER Notation):**

| # | Attribute Type | Definition | ER Symbol | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple/Atomic** | Cannot be subdivided | Plain oval | `Age` |
| 2 | **Composite** | Has sub-parts | Oval with sub-ovals | `Name` → {First, Middle, Last} |
| 3 | **Single-Valued** | Holds one value | Plain oval | `DOB` |
| 4 | **Multivalued** | Holds multiple values | **Double oval** | `Phone_Numbers` |
| 5 | **Derived** | Computed from another | **Dashed oval** | `Age` (from `DOB`) |
| 6 | **Key** | Uniquely identifies | Oval with underline | `Roll_No` |
| 7 | **Partial Key** | Discriminator for weak entity | Oval with **dashed underline** | `Room_No` in `HOSTEL_ROOM` |

#### Model Solution (b):

**ER Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "registers"
    COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "offered in"
    HOSTEL ||--o{ HOSTEL_ROOM : "contains"

    STUDENT {
        string Roll_No PK
        string Name
        date DOB
        string Branch
    }

    COURSE {
        string Course_ID PK
        string Course_Name
        int Credits
    }

    ENROLLMENT {
        string Roll_No FK
        string Course_ID FK
        string Semester
        string Grade
    }

    HOSTEL {
        string Hostel_ID PK
        string Hostel_Name
        string Warden_Name
    }

    HOSTEL_ROOM {
        string Hostel_ID FK
        string Room_No
        string Block
        int Floor
    }
```

**Classification Table:**

| Entity Set | Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `STUDENT` | **Strong** | Has self-sufficient PK `Roll_No` |
| `COURSE` | **Strong** | Has self-sufficient PK `Course_ID` |
| `ENROLLMENT` | **Weak** | No independent identity; exists only via `STUDENT`–`COURSE` association; partial key = `Semester` + `Grade` context. PK = `Roll_No + Course_ID + Semester` |
| `HOSTEL` | **Strong** | Has self-sufficient PK `Hostel_ID` |
| `HOSTEL_ROOM` | **Weak** | `Room_No` is unique only within a hostel. PK = `Hostel_ID + Room_No` |

**Key Derivation for Weak Entities:**

$$K_{HOSTEL\_ROOM} = \{Hostel\_ID\} \cup \{Room\_No\} = \{Hostel\_ID, Room\_No\}$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q.** (a) What is a **weak entity set**? Explain with a real-world example. Derive the rule for constructing the primary key of a weak entity set. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Consider a BANK database with entity sets `BRANCH` (Branch_ID, Branch_Name, City) and `LOAN` (Loan_Number, Amount, Type). Each loan has multiple `PAYMENT` records (Payment_Number, Amount, Date), where Payment_Number is only unique within a specific loan. Draw the ER diagram and clearly identify strong vs weak entity sets. State the primary key of every entity set. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Weak Entity Set:** A weak entity set is one whose entities cannot be uniquely identified by their own attributes alone and depend on a strong (owner) entity set for identification. They have a **partial key (discriminator)** that uniquely distinguishes them only within the owner entity.

**Real-World Example:** A `ROOM` in a hotel. The room number 101 may exist in multiple hotels, but within one hotel, it is unique. Hence `ROOM` is a weak entity, owned by `HOTEL` (strong entity).

**Primary Key Construction Rule (Derivation):**

Let $E_s$ be a strong entity set with primary key $K_s$. Let $E_w$ be a weak entity set with partial key $K_p$, related to $E_s$ via the identifying relationship $R$. The primary key of $E_w$ is the **set union**:

$$K_w = K_s \cup K_p$$

**Proof of Uniqueness:** Suppose two entities $w_1, w_2 \in E_w$ share the same composite key. Then both $K_s(w_1) = K_s(w_2)$ (same owner) and $K_p(w_1) = K_p(w_2)$ (same partial key). Since $K_p$ uniquely identifies $w$ within the owner set $E_s$, this forces $w_1 = w_2$. Hence the composite key is unique. ∎

#### Model Solution (b):

**ER Diagram:**

```mermaid
erDiagram
    BRANCH ||--o{ LOAN : "disburses"
    LOAN ||--|{ PAYMENT : "receives (identifying)"

    BRANCH {
        string Branch_ID PK
        string Branch_Name
        string City
    }

    LOAN {
        string Loan_Number PK
        string Branch_ID FK
        float Amount
        string Type
    }

    PAYMENT {
        string Loan_Number FK
        int Payment_Number
        float Amount
        date Date
    }
```

**Classification and Primary Keys:**

| Entity Set | Type | Primary Key | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `BRANCH` | **Strong** | `Branch_ID` | Self-sufficient unique identifier |
| `LOAN` | **Strong** | `Loan_Number` | Loan numbers are globally unique (typically issued bank-wide) |
| `PAYMENT` | **Weak** | `(Loan_Number, Payment_Number)` | `Payment_Number` is unique only within a loan; needs owner's PK |

**Note on Cardinality:** `LOAN` → `PAYMENT` uses `||--|{` notation because **every loan must have at least one payment** (total participation on the loan side is `||`, total on the payment side is also `|`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Entity Type vs Entity Set** — Many students interchange the two terms. Memorize: *Type = blueprint; Set = instances.*
> 2. **Forgetting the double-rectangle / double-oval notations** — A weak entity in a single rectangle is **deducted 2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Incorrect weak-entity primary key** — Forgetting to **union** (not intersect) the owner's key with the partial key is the #1 error.
> 4. **Missing identifying relationship (double diamond)** — A weak entity without its identifying relationship is an incomplete ER model.
> 5. **Total vs Partial Participation** — `||` vs `o` distinction often confused; the `||` means **every** entity must participate (total participation), which is **mandatory** for weak entities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Entity:** Real-world distinguishable object.
- **Entity Type:** Schema/blueprint (structure).
- **Entity Set:** Collection of current instances.
- **Attribute:** Descriptive property of an entity.
- **Key Attribute:** Uniquely identifies an entity (underlined).
- **Partial Key:** Discriminator for weak entity (dashed underline).
- **Strong Entity:** Has its own PK; independent existence; **single rectangle**.
- **Weak Entity:** Depends on owner; needs owner's PK; **double rectangle**.

### 🔢 Must-Know Notation
| Concept | ER Symbol |
|---|---|
| Strong Entity | Single rectangle |
| Weak Entity | **Double rectangle** |
| Identifying Relationship | **Double diamond** |
| Multivalued Attribute | **Double oval** |
| Derived Attribute | **Dashed oval** |
| Key Attribute | Oval with **underline** |
| Partial Key | Oval with **dashed underline** |
| Total Participation | Double line connecting to relationship |
| Partial Participation | Single line connecting to relationship |

### ⚡ High-Yield Formulas
1. **Weak Entity PK:** $K_{weak} = K_{owner} \cup K_{partial}$ (union, not intersection!)
2. **Key Uniqueness:** $\forall e_i, e_j \in E,\; e_i \neq e_j \implies K(e_i) \neq K(e_j)$
3. **M:N Cardinality:** $\vert R \vert \leq n_1 \times n_2$
4. **1:N Cardinality:** $\vert R \vert \leq n_2$ (one parent → many children)
5. **Domain Size Constraint:** For a key attribute $k$, $\vert D_k \vert \geq \vert E \vert$

### 🎯 Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Treating "Multivalued" and "Composite" as the same — they are different!
- ❌ Using `∩` (intersection) instead of `∪` (union) for weak entity keys.
- ❌ Drawing a weak entity without its identifying relationship.
- ❌ Forgetting to underline key attributes in the ER diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "design an ER diagram," **always** explicitly state: (1) the entity sets with **classification** (strong/weak), (2) attributes with **key markings**, and (3) the **identifying relationship** for every weak entity. Examiners award 2–3 bonus marks for this clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Relationship Set:** A **relationship set** is a mathematical set $R = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots, r_n\}$ consisting of all current (existing) relationships of the same type. Formally, given entity sets $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ (not necessarily distinct), a **relationship set** $R$ is a subset of the Cartesian product:
> $$R \subseteq E_1 \times E_2 \times \dots \times E_n$$
> where each individual $r_i = (e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n)$ is a **relationship instance** linking exactly one entity from each participating entity set.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Terminology Hierarchy (KTU Standard):**
> - **Relationship Type / Relationship Schema:** The *blueprint* defining the structure (e.g., *Enrolled_In* between *Student* and *Course*).
> - **Relationship Set:** The *current collection* of all such relationship instances at a given point in time.
> - **Relationship Instance:** A single *tuple* (a1, a2, …, an) — a concrete association.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **dance class**. The **Relationship Type** is the rule "Student–Tutor pairs for dance lessons." The **Relationship Set** is the *actual list* of pairs dancing *right now* (Riya–Mr. John, Anu–Ms. Mary, …). Each entry in that list is a **Relationship Instance**.

| Concept | Dance Analogy |
|---------|---------------|
| Entity Set | All students, all tutors |
| Relationship Type | "is being taught by" |
| Relationship Set | The current active tutor–student pairings |
| Relationship Instance | One specific pairing: *Riya* taught by *Mr. John* |

### 1.3 Key Distinctions (Frequently Confused in KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> - **Entity Set vs Relationship Set:** An entity set is a collection of *similar entities*; a relationship set is a collection of *similar associations between entities*.
> - **Degree of a Relationship Set = Number of participating entity sets.**
> - **Descriptive Attributes** belong to the relationship, not to any individual entity (e.g., `grade` on Enrolled_In).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Relationship Set

A relationship set $R$ defined on entity sets $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ has the following components:

- **Participating Entity Sets:** $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ — may repeat in recursive relationships.
- **Descriptive Attributes (if any):** $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$ — properties of the association itself.
- **Relationship Schema Notation:** $R(E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n; A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m)$.

#### 2.1.1 Degree Classification

- **Unary / Recursive Relationship (Degree 1):** A single entity set participates in more than one role. Example: *Employee supervises Employee*.
- **Binary Relationship (Degree 2):** Most common in KTU problems. Example: *Student takes Course*.
- **Ternary Relationship (Degree 3):** Three entity sets simultaneously. Example: *Vendor supplies Part to Project*.
- **n-ary Relationship (Degree n):** General case.

### 2.2 Mapping Cardinalities (Cardinality Ratios)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mapping Cardinality** expresses the maximum number of relationship instances an entity can participate in. For a binary relationship $R$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$:

| Cardinality | Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| **One-to-One (1:1)** | Each $e_1 \in E_1$ links to at most one $e_2 \in E_2$ and vice versa. | $\forall e_1 \exists^{\le 1} e_2$ |
| **One-to-Many (1:N)** | One $e_1$ links to many $e_2$, but each $e_2$ links to one $e_1$. | $\vert R \vert \le \vert E_1 \vert$ |
| **Many-to-One (N:1)** | Many $e_1$ link to one $e_2$, but each $e_1$ links to one $e_2$. | $\vert R \vert \le \vert E_2 \vert$ |
| **Many-to-Many (M:N)** | Any number of $e_1$ can link to any number of $e_2$. | $\vert R \vert \le \vert E_1 \vert \times \vert E_2 \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **The KTU "arrow notation":** Draw an arrow $\rightarrow$ pointing to the "one" side. Example: `Instructor → Teaches → Class` means 1:N.

### 2.3 Participation Constraints

- **Total Participation (Existence Dependency):** Every entity in the set MUST participate. Drawn as a **double line**.
- **Partial Participation:** Some entities may not participate. Drawn as a **single line**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Definition / Formula | Use |
|------------------|----------------------|-----|
| Relationship Set $R$ | $R \subseteq E_1 \times E_2 \times \dots \times E_n$ | Define any relationship set mathematically |
| Max instances in 1:1 | $\le \min(\vert E_1 \vert, \vert E_2 \vert)$ | Bound on 1:1 cardinality |
| Max instances in 1:N | $\le \vert E_1 \vert$ (one side) | Bound on 1:N cardinality |
| Max instances in M:N | $\le \vert E_1 \vert \times \vert E_2 \vert$ | Bound on M:N cardinality |
| Conversion to Relation | Keys of all participating entity sets $\cup$ descriptive attributes | KTU relational mapping question |
| Primary Key of M:N Relation | Combination of all participating keys | Standard KTU rule |
| Primary Key of 1:1 Relation | Either side's key (candidate keys) | KTU rule |
| Primary Key of 1:N Relation | The "many" side's key | KTU rule |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** `Customer HAS Account` (1:N) — a customer can hold many accounts.
- **ERP Systems (SAP/Oracle):** `Employee Belongs_to Department` (N:1) — foundational for HR schemas.
- **IoT Networks:** `Sensor Monitors_Asset` (M:N) — flexible telemetry graph design.
- **Social Networks:** `User Friends User` (recursive, M:N) — modeled via self-join or join tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Converting a Relationship Set to a Relational Schema

> **Problem:** Given a relationship set $R$ between entity sets $A$ and $B$ with descriptive attribute $d$, derive the relational schema and identify the primary key under each cardinality ratio.

**General Rule Derivation:**

Let $K_A$ = primary key of $A$, $K_B$ = primary key of $B$. A relationship instance $r \in R$ is uniquely identified by the participating entities. When the ratio is M:N, the combination of both keys is the minimal superkey because:
- $K_A$ alone is insufficient (one $A$ can link to many $B$).
- $K_B$ alone is insufficient (one $B$ can link to many $A$).
- Therefore, $K_A \cup K_B$ is required.

#### Case 1: Many-to-Many (M:N)

$$\text{Schema}(R) = R(\underline{K_A}, \underline{K_B}, d)$$

* **Primary Key:** $(K_A, K_B)$ — composite.
* **Foreign Keys:** $K_A$ references $A$; $K_B$ references $B$.

#### Case 2: One-to-Many (1:N, $A$ is the "one" side)

Since each $B$ entity links to exactly one $A$ entity, the relationship can be absorbed into $B$'s relation without creating a separate table. The primary key of $B$ uniquely determines its corresponding $A$.

$$\text{Schema}(R) \text{ merged into } B \text{ as } B(\underline{K_B}, \text{other attrs}, K_A, d)$$

* **Primary Key:** $K_B$ (unchanged).
* **Foreign Key:** $K_A$ referencing $A$.

#### Case 3: One-to-One (1:1)

$$\text{Schema}(R) = R(\underline{K_A}, \underline{K_B}, d) \quad \text{or merged into either } A \text{ or } B$$

* **Primary Key:** Either $K_A$ or $K_B$ (both are candidate keys).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cardinality vs. Schema Choice

| Cardinality | Separate Relation? | Primary Key of Relation | Foreign Keys |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------------|--------------|
| 1:1 | Optional (often merged) | Either side's key | Other side's key |
| 1:N | Usually NO (merge into "many" side) | "Many" side's key | "One" side's key |
| N:1 | Usually NO (merge into "many" side) | "Many" side's key | "One" side's key |
| M:N | YES (mandatory) | Composite: both keys | Both keys |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Total Number of Relationship Instances

> **Problem:** Entity set $E_1$ has 50 entities, $E_2$ has 30 entities. Relationship $R$ between them is 1:N. Find the maximum and minimum number of relationship instances.

**Step 1 — Identify the "one" side:**
The "one" side is $E_1$ (each $E_1$ entity connects to many $E_2$).

**Step 2 — Maximum instances:**
Every $E_1$ must connect to all 30 $E_2$ entities:
$$\text{Max} \vert R \vert = \vert E_1 \vert \times \vert E_2 \vert = 50 \times 30 = 1500$$

**Step 3 — Minimum instances:**
At least one $E_1$ entity exists and links to at least one $E_2$:
$$\text{Min} \vert R \vert = 1 \quad \text{(if no total participation)}$$

**Step 4 — With total participation on $E_2$:**
Every $E_2$ must link to some $E_1$, so:
$$\text{Min} \vert R \vert = \vert E_2 \vert = 30$$

### 3.4 Complete Conversion Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Given:** Entity sets `STUDENT(SID, SName, Dept)` and `COURSE(CID, CName, Credits)`. Relationship `ENROLLED_IN` (M:N) with descriptive attribute `Grade`. Total participation by STUDENT.
> 
> **Derived Schema:**
> $$\text{ENROLLED\_IN}(\underline{\text{SID}}, \underline{\text{CID}}, \text{Grade})$$
> $$\text{FK: SID} \rightarrow \text{STUDENT}, \quad \text{FK: CID} \rightarrow \text{COURSE}$$
> 
> **Primary Key:** Composite (SID, CID).
> **Total participation** is enforced by NOT NULL constraint on SID in the ENROLLED_IN table.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ER Diagram: Cardinality Ratios Visualized

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Entity Set A] -- 1:1 --> B[Entity Set B]
    C[Entity Set C] -- 1:N --> D[Entity Set D]
    E[Entity Set E] -- M:N --> F[Entity Set F]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1f5
    style F fill:#ffe1f5
```

> **Read this diagram:** Top row shows 1:1 (one-to-one), middle row shows 1:N (one-to-many), bottom row shows M:N (many-to-many) — the three KTU-required cardinality patterns.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Relationship Set Internal Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph RS[Relationship Set R]
        R1[Instance r1: e1, e2, ..., en]
        R2[Instance r2: e1', e2', ..., en']
        R3[Instance r3: e1'', e2'', ..., en'']
    end
    
    subgraph ES[Entity Sets]
        E1[Entity Set E1]
        E2[Entity Set E2]
        EN[Entity Set En]
    end
    
    subgraph ATT[Descriptive Attributes]
        A1[Attribute a1]
        A2[Attribute a2]
    end
    
    E1 -.contains.-> R1
    E2 -.contains.-> R1
    EN -.contains.-> R1
    A1 -.attached to.-> R1
    A2 -.attached to.-> R2
    
    style RS fill:#f0f8ff
    style ES fill:#fffacd
    style ATT fill:#f5deb3
```

> **Interpretation:** The relationship set is a *set container* that links entities from multiple entity sets, and may also carry its own descriptive attributes that belong to the *association*, not the entities.

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Mapping Cardinality Identification

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Relationship R between E1 and E2] --> Q1{Can one E1 link to many E2?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can one E2 link to many E1?}
    Q1 -- No --> OneOne[1:1 Cardinality]
    Q2 -- Yes --> ManyMany[M:N Cardinality]
    Q2 -- No --> OneMany[1:N Cardinality]
    
    style Start fill:#e6e6fa
    style OneOne fill:#90ee90
    style OneMany fill:#ffd700
    style ManyMany fill:#ff69b4
```

### 4.4 Participation Constraint Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    Dept[Department] ==>|total| HOD[Head_Of Relationship]
    Faculty[Faculty] -->|partial| Works_In[Works_In Relationship]
    
    style Dept fill:#ffcccc
    style Faculty fill:#cce5ff
    style HOD fill:#ffe1e1
    style Works_In fill:#e1e9ff
```

> **Double line (==>)** = Total participation (every Department MUST have a Head_Of). **Single line (-->)** = Partial participation (some Faculty may not be assigned).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
*Define the following terms with a suitable example each:*
*(i) Relationship Set  (ii) Mapping Cardinality  (iii) Participation Constraint*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Relationship Set:** A relationship set $R$ is a set of all relationship instances of the same type. For example, if `STUDENT` and `COURSE` are two entity sets, then `ENROLLED_IN` is a relationship set containing all current student–course enrollments. Formally:
$$R \subseteq \text{STUDENT} \times \text{COURSE}$$

**(ii) Mapping Cardinality:** It is the number of relationship instances an entity can participate in. For binary relationships, the four types are 1:1, 1:N, N:1, and M:N. *Example:* `Person` married to `Person` (1:1); `Department employs Employee` (1:N).

**(iii) Participation Constraint:** Specifies whether every entity must participate in the relationship. *Total participation* (double line) means every entity must take part; *Partial participation* means participation is optional. *Example:* Every `Loan` account MUST be linked to a `Customer` (total); an `Employee` may or may not manage a `Department` (partial).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** 
*Distinguish between a Relationship Type, a Relationship Set, and a Relationship Instance using a library management system example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Library Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Relationship Type** | The schema/blueprint of an association | `BORROWS(Member, Book; BorrowDate)` |
| **Relationship Set** | The current set of all such associations | All currently active borrowings in 2024 |
| **Relationship Instance** | A single concrete association | `(M101, ISBN-978-0-13, 12-Aug-2024)` — Member M101 borrowed "Database Systems" on 12-Aug-2024 |

The three are hierarchically related: an *instance* is a member of a *set*, and the *set* is governed by the *type*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Relationship Set** in the ER model. Differentiate between **unary, binary, and ternary** relationship sets with one example each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: A `PROJECT` is assigned to exactly one `DEPARTMENT`, but a `DEPARTMENT` can have many `PROJECT`s. Each `EMPLOYEE` works on many `PROJECT`s, and each `PROJECT` has many `EMPLOYEE`s. An `EMPLOYEE` belongs to exactly one `DEPARTMENT`.

*(i) Identify the entity sets and relationship sets.  (ii) Specify the mapping cardinality for each relationship.  (iii) Draw the ER diagram.  (iv) Convert the ER diagram into relational schema with primary and foreign keys.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept & Degree Classification (7 Marks):**
- **Relationship Set** = set of associations of the same type; formally $R \subseteq E_1 \times E_2 \times \dots \times E_n$.
- **Unary (Recursive) Relationship — Degree 1:** A single entity set participates in multiple roles. *Example:* `Employee supervises Employee` (manager is also an employee).
- **Binary Relationship — Degree 2:** Two entity sets. *Example:* `Student takes Course`.
- **Ternary Relationship — Degree 3:** Three entity sets simultaneously. *Example:* `Supplier supplies Part to Project` (cannot be decomposed into binary without losing semantics).

**(b) ER Modeling (7 Marks):**

**(i) Entity Sets:** `DEPARTMENT(DNo, DName)`, `PROJECT(PNo, PName)`, `EMPLOYEE(ENo, EName)`.

**Relationship Sets:**
- `ASSIGNED_TO` between DEPARTMENT and PROJECT.
- `WORKS_ON` between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT.
- `BELONGS_TO` between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT.

**(ii) Mapping Cardinalities:**
- `ASSIGNED_TO`: DEPARTMENT (1) : PROJECT (N) → **1:N**.
- `WORKS_ON`: EMPLOYEE (M) : PROJECT (N) → **M:N** with descriptive attribute `Hours`.
- `BELONGS_TO`: EMPLOYEE (N) : DEPARTMENT (1) → **N:1**.

**(iii) ER Diagram (described in text):**
- Double rectangle → strong entity sets DEPARTMENT, PROJECT, EMPLOYEE.
- Diamond `ASSIGNED_TO` with arrow pointing to DEPARTMENT (1 side) and single line to PROJECT.
- Diamond `WORKS_ON` with single lines on both sides + attribute `Hours`.
- Diamond `BELONGS_TO` with arrow pointing to DEPARTMENT.

**(iv) Relational Schema Conversion:**

```
DEPARTMENT(DNo, DName)
  PK: DNo

PROJECT(PNo, PName, DNo)
  PK: PNo
  FK: DNo → DEPARTMENT(DNo)

EMPLOYEE(ENo, EName, DNo)
  PK: ENo
  FK: DNo → DEPARTMENT(DNo)

WORKS_ON(ENo, PNo, Hours)
  PK: (ENo, PNo)
  FK: ENo → EMPLOYEE(ENo)
  FK: PNo → PROJECT(PNo)
```

*Note:* `ASSIGNED_TO` is merged into PROJECT (the "many" side carries the FK).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Define **Mapping Cardinality** and **Participation Constraint**. Explain the four types of cardinality ratios (1:1, 1:N, N:1, M:N) for a binary relationship set with arrow notation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An `INSURANCE` company has the following requirements:
- Each `POLICY` is held by exactly one `CUSTOMER`; each `CUSTOMER` may hold multiple `POLICY`s.
- Each `POLICY` covers one or more `VEHICLE`s; a `VEHICLE` is covered by exactly one `POLICY`.
- Each `CLAIM` is filed against one `POLICY` and one `VEHICLE`. A `POLICY` may have many `CLAIM`s; a `VEHICLE` may have many `CLAIM`s.

*(i) Identify entity sets, attributes, and relationship sets.  (ii) Identify all keys and mapping cardinalities.  (iii) Convert to relational schema.  (iv) State the primary key of each relation.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mapping Cardinality & Participation Constraint (7 Marks):**

**Mapping Cardinality** = maximum number of relationship instances an entity can belong to. The four types for a binary $R(E_1, E_2)$:

| Type | Rule | Arrow Notation |
|------|------|----------------|
| 1:1 | Each $e_1$ ↔ at most one $e_2$ and vice versa | `E1 → — E2` (or both arrows) |
| 1:N | One $e_1$ ↔ many $e_2$, but each $e_2$ ↔ one $e_1$ | `E1 → E2` (arrow on E1 side) |
| N:1 | Many $e_1$ ↔ one $e_2$ | `E1 E2 →` (arrow on E2 side) |
| M:N | Many $e_1$ ↔ many $e_2$ | No arrows |

**Participation Constraint** states whether every entity must participate:
- **Total (Existence Dependency):** every entity participates → **double line**.
- **Partial:** participation optional → **single line**.

**(b) Insurance ER Modeling (7 Marks):**

**(i) Entity Sets & Attributes:**
- `CUSTOMER(CustID, CName, Address)`
- `POLICY(PolID, Premium, Type)`
- `VEHICLE(VIN, Model, RegNo)`
- `CLAIM(ClaimID, ClaimDate, Amount)`

**Relationship Sets:**
- `HOLDS` (CUSTOMER — POLICY): 1:N
- `COVERS` (POLICY — VEHICLE): 1:N
- `FILED_AGAINST` (POLICY — VEHICLE — CLAIM): ternary, but with constraint that each CLAIM links to one POLICY and one VEHICLE → effectively two 1:N binary relationships.

**(ii) Mapping Cardinalities:**
- HOLDS: CUSTOMER(1) : POLICY(N)
- COVERS: POLICY(1) : VEHICLE(N)
- FILED_AGAINST: POLICY(1) : CLAIM(N); VEHICLE(1) : CLAIM(N)

**(iii) & (iv) Relational Schema:**

```
CUSTOMER(CustID, CName, Address)
  PK: CustID

POLICY(PolID, Premium, Type, CustID)
  PK: PolID
  FK: CustID → CUSTOMER

VEHICLE(VIN, Model, RegNo, PolID)
  PK: VIN
  FK: PolID → POLICY

CLAIM(ClaimID, ClaimDate, Amount, PolID, VIN)
  PK: ClaimID
  FK: (PolID, VIN) → (POLICY, VEHICLE)
```

*Note:* If the ternary FILED_AGAINST is treated as genuine ternary with M:N semantics among all three, then a separate junction relation with composite PK (PolID, VIN, ClaimID) would be needed.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Type vs Set vs Instance:** Students often use these terms interchangeably — examiners deduct 1–2 marks per misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting Foreign Key Declaration:** When converting to relational schema, you MUST mention FK references explicitly. A schema without FKs is considered incomplete.
> 3. **M:N Cardinality ALWAYS needs a separate relation.** Many students wrongly merge an M:N into one of the entity tables — this is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Arrow Notation Direction:** Arrow points FROM the "many" side TO the "one" side. Reversing this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Total Participation Notation:** Use **double lines** (`==`), not thick lines. Examiners specifically check this.
> 6. **Ternary relationships CANNOT always be decomposed** into binary relationships without losing semantic information — a common KTU trick question.
> 7. **For 1:1, either side can be the "primary"** but the chosen side's key is the PK of the merged relation. Justify your choice briefly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Relationship Set $R \subseteq E_1 \times E_2 \times \dots \times E_n$** is the formal definition — memorize this Cartesian product form.
- [x] **Degree = number of participating entity sets** (unary = 1, binary = 2, ternary = 3, n-ary = n).
- [x] **Mapping Cardinalities (binary):** 1:1, 1:N, N:1, M:N — four cases only.
- [x] **Arrow notation:** arrow points TO the "one" side.
- [x] **Double line = total participation; single line = partial participation.**
- [x] **Conversion Rule (critical for KTU):**
  - 1:1 → merge optional; separate relation uses either side's key.
  - 1:N / N:1 → merge into "many" side; no separate relation needed.
  - M:N → **always** a separate relation with composite primary key.
- [x] **Primary Key Rule:** Combination of all participating entity sets' keys (for M:N); "many" side's key (for 1:N).
- [x] **Descriptive attributes** go on the relationship, not on the entities, when they describe the association (e.g., `grade` for enrollment).
- [x] **Recursive (Unary) relationships** require role labels to disambiguate (e.g., `supervisor` vs `subordinate`).
- [x] **Ternary vs Binary:** Don't decompose a true ternary into binaries unless the KTU question explicitly permits it — it may change cardinality semantics.
- [x] **Existence Dependency:** A weak entity set has total participation in its identifying relationship — this concept frequently combines with relationship sets in KTU problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **Relational Model** and the **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model**, an **Attribute** is a named property, characteristic, or descriptor that captures a specific piece of information about an **entity** or a **relationship**.

Formally, if $E$ is an entity set, then an attribute $A_i$ is a function:

$$A_i : E \rightarrow \text{Dom}(A_i)$$

where $\text{Dom}(A_i)$ is the **domain** of the attribute, i.e., the set of all permitted atomic values for $A_i$.

In a relational schema $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$, every attribute $A_i$ corresponds to a **column header** of a relation (table), while a **tuple** $t \in R$ is a row whose $i$-th component $t[A_i]$ must belong to $\text{Dom}(A_i)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> Attributes form the atomic descriptive units of the relational data model. The classification of attributes (simple, composite, single-valued, multi-valued, derived, null) is a **frequently tested concept** in Part A and Part B questions, and is the foundation for mapping ER diagrams to relational schemas.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are filling out an admission form at a college. The form has blank fields such as:

- **Name**
- **Date of Birth**
- **Roll Number**
- **Phone Number**
- **Branch**

Each blank is an **attribute** — a placeholder that describes **one specific piece of information** about *you* (the entity). Without these attributes, the college would not be able to identify you, contact you, or place you in a branch.

Translating the analogy to DBMS terminology:

| Real-World Item | DBMS Term |
| :--- | :--- |
| You (the applicant) | Entity |
| Set of all applicants | Entity Set |
| Name, DOB, Roll No, etc. | Attributes |
| Form (template) | Schema / Relation |
| Filled form (your record) | Tuple / Row |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An attribute is **always associated** with either an entity type, a relationship type, or a key. It is the **smallest named data element** in a database that retains meaning on its own.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used Across KTU Papers

- **Domain ($\text{Dom}$):** The set of allowable atomic values for an attribute. Example: $\text{Dom}(\text{Marks}) = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 100\}$.
- **Schema:** The structural description of a relation, given by its name and the list of attributes: $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$.
- **Degree (or Arity):** The number of attributes $n$ in a relation's schema.
- **Atomic Value:** A value that cannot be meaningfully subdivided within the database context (e.g., a single phone number, a single date).
- **Null:** A special marker indicating that the value is *unknown*, *missing*, or *not applicable*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Attributes

Attributes in KTU syllabus are classified along **five orthogonal dimensions**. Each dimension answers one specific design question.

### 2.1.1 Atomicity Dimension — Simple vs Composite

- **Simple (Atomic) Attribute:** Cannot be subdivided into smaller meaningful sub-parts.
  - Example: `Age`, `RollNo`, `Salary`
- **Composite Attribute:** Can be broken down into smaller sub-parts, each with its own meaning.
  - Example: `Address` $\rightarrow$ (`HouseNo`, `Street`, `City`, `Pincode`)
  - Example: `Name` $\rightarrow$ (`FirstName`, `MiddleName`, `LastName`)

> [!TIP]
> A composite attribute is just a **nesting of simple attributes**. It is used purely for logical clarity; physically, the database stores only the leaf-level atomic values.

### 2.1.2 Cardinality Dimension — Single-Valued vs Multi-Valued

- **Single-Valued Attribute:** Holds exactly one value for a given entity instance.
  - Example: `DateOfBirth`, `EmployeeID`
- **Multi-Valued Attribute:** Can hold zero, one, or many values from the same domain for a single entity.
  - Example: `PhoneNumbers` (a person may have many), `EmailIDs`, `Skills`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Normal Form (1NF) forbids multi-valued attributes.** Hence multi-valued attributes must be converted into a separate relation during ER-to-Relational mapping. This is a **mandatory KTU 2024 exam concept**.

### 2.1.3 Storage Dimension — Stored vs Derived

- **Stored Attribute:** Its value is physically stored in the database.
  - Example: `DateOfBirth`
- **Derived Attribute:** Its value is computed at runtime using other attributes; it is **not stored**.
  - Example: `Age` is derived from `DateOfBirth` and the current date.
  - Example: `TotalMarks` derived from summing individual subject marks.

> Derived attributes are often drawn with **dashed/dotted ovals** in ER diagrams.

### 2.1.4 Null Dimension — Null vs Not Null

- **Null Attribute:** The value is not known, is missing, or is not applicable.
  - Three interpretations: *Value exists but unknown*, *Value does not exist*, *Attribute not applicable*.
- **Not Null / Mandatory:** A value must always be supplied (typically enforced via `NOT NULL` constraint).

### 2.1.5 Key Dimension — Key vs Non-Key

- **Key Attribute:** An attribute (or minimal combination) that **uniquely identifies** an entity.
  - Example: `RollNo` in STUDENT, `ISBN` in BOOK
- **Non-Key Attribute:** Any attribute that is not part of any candidate key.
  - Example: `Name`, `Address` in STUDENT

> **Primary Key** is the chosen candidate key; **Alternate Keys** are the unchosen ones; **Foreign Key** references the primary key of another relation.

---

## 2.2 Domain — The "Type System" of Attributes

A **Domain** is a named, finite (or potentially infinite) set of homogeneous atomic values. Domains restrict the values an attribute can legally take.

Formal specification of a domain:

$$\text{Dom}(A) = (\text{Name}, \text{DataType}, \text{Constraints})$$

**Example:**
$$\text{Dom}(\text{Grade}) = (\text{Grade}, \text{CHAR}(2), \text{Value} \in \{'A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'F'\})$$

Domains are crucial for **data integrity** — they prevent the storage of nonsensical values (e.g., a negative age, a string in a numeric field).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation | Key Rule | ER Notation |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Simple Attribute | $A$ | Cannot be subdivided | Solid oval |
| 2 | Composite Attribute | $A = (A_1, A_2, \dots)$ | Sub-parts have own meaning | Oval with sub-ovals |
| 3 | Single-Valued Attribute | $\vert A \vert = 1$ | Exactly one value | Solid oval |
| 4 | Multi-Valued Attribute | $\vert A \vert \geq 0$ | One or more values | Double oval |
| 5 | Stored Attribute | $A \in \text{DB}$ | Physically stored | Solid oval |
| 6 | Derived Attribute | $A = f(A_1, \dots)$ | Computed at runtime | Dashed oval |
| 7 | Null Attribute | $A = \text{NULL}$ | Missing / unknown | Solid oval |
| 8 | Key Attribute | $A$ is in PK | Uniquely identifies | Oval with underlined text |
| 9 | Domain Constraint | $A \in \text{Dom}(A)$ | Restricts valid values | N/A (logical constraint) |
| 10 | Not Null Constraint | $A \neq \text{NULL}$ | Mandatory value | N/A (SQL constraint) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite + Multi-valued attributes combined are the trickiest mapping scenarios** in KTU Module 1 questions. Memorize the conversion rule: a multi-valued attribute always spawns a **new separate relation** whose primary key is the combination of the parent entity's key and the multi-valued attribute.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Account number (key), transaction amount (single-valued stored), customer phones (multi-valued).
- **Hospital Management:** Patient name (composite → first/last), age (derived from DOB), blood group (single-valued), previous medical history (multi-valued).
- **E-Commerce:** Product dimensions (composite: length, width, height), product images (multi-valued URLs), discount price (derived from MRP - discount%).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **formal mapping rules** from attribute classification to a relational schema, with full algebraic justification.

---

## 3.1 Rule 1 — Mapping a Simple (Atomic) Single-Valued Stored Attribute

**Rule:** A simple, single-valued, stored attribute is represented as a **column** in the parent relation.

**Justification (Algebraic):**

Let $E$ be an entity set with primary key $PK$. For an attribute $A$ such that:
- $A$ is atomic
- $\forall e \in E : \vert A(e) \vert = 1$
- $A$ is stored

We construct a relation schema:
$$R(\underline{PK}, A, \dots)$$

The under-line indicates the primary key. The domain constraint is:
$$\forall t \in R : t[A] \in \text{Dom}(A)$$

**Example:**

Given entity $E = \text{STUDENT}$ with attributes `RollNo` (key), `Name` (simple), `DOB` (simple):

$$R_{\text{STUDENT}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB})$$

---

## 3.2 Rule 2 — Mapping a Composite Attribute

**Rule:** A composite attribute $C$ with sub-parts $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k$ is decomposed into its leaf-level sub-attributes; only the sub-attributes are stored.

**Justification:**

Let $C$ be a composite attribute where $C = (C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k)$ and each $C_i$ is atomic. The atomicity principle (1NF) forbids storing non-atomic values, so we map:

$$R(\underline{PK}, C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k, \dots)$$

**Example:**

Let `Address` be a composite attribute of STUDENT:
$$\text{Address} = (\text{HouseNo}, \text{Street}, \text{City}, \text{Pincode})$$

Decomposed schema:
$$R_{\text{STUDENT}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \text{Name}, \text{HouseNo}, \text{Street}, \text{City}, \text{Pincode})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the **name** "Address" itself disappears from the schema; only its atomic sub-parts survive. This is a common KTU exam question — students must show all four sub-columns explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Rule 3 — Mapping a Multi-Valued Attribute

**Rule:** A multi-valued attribute $M$ of entity $E$ is moved to a **new relation**. The new relation's primary key is the **composite** of $E$'s primary key and $M$ itself.

**Justification:**

For attribute $M$ where $\exists e \in E : \vert M(e) \vert \geq 2$, 1NF is violated if $M$ is stored as a column. We must create:

$$R_M(\underline{PK_E}, \underline{M})$$

This guarantees that each $(PK_E, M)$ pair is unique, allowing one row per value.

**Example:**

Let `PhoneNumbers` be multi-valued for STUDENT. The mapping yields **two relations**:

$$R_{\text{STUDENT}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB})$$

$$R_{\text{STUDENT\_PHONE}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \underline{\text{PhoneNo}})$$

Here, $\text{RollNo}$ in $R_{\text{STUDENT\_PHONE}}$ is a **Foreign Key** referencing $R_{\text{STUDENT}}$.

---

## 3.4 Rule 4 — Mapping a Derived Attribute

**Rule:** A derived attribute $D$ is **excluded** from the base relation; it is computed dynamically when needed (typically via a SQL `VIEW` or computed column).

**Justification:**

Since $D = f(A_1, A_2, \dots)$, storing $D$ causes **redundancy** and **update anomalies**. The schema simply omits $D$.

**Example:**

If `Age` is derived from `DOB`:
$$R_{\text{STUDENT}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB})$$

`Age` is omitted from the schema and computed at query time:
$$\text{Age} = \text{YEAR}(\text{CURRENT\_DATE}) - \text{YEAR}(\text{DOB})$$

In SQL:
```sql
SELECT RollNo, Name, DOB,
       YEAR(CURDATE()) - YEAR(DOB) AS Age
FROM STUDENT;
```

> [!TIP]
> In PostgreSQL, a derived attribute can be implemented using a `GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED` column. In KTU theory exams, you just write the expression and mention it is not physically stored in the base table.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Attribute Conversion Table

| Original Attribute | Type | Mapping Action | Resulting Schema Fragment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RollNo | Simple + Key | Keep as PK | $\underline{\text{RollNo}}$ |
| Name | Simple | Keep as column | Name |
| Address | Composite | Decompose to leaves | HouseNo, Street, City, Pincode |
| PhoneNumbers | Multi-valued | Move to new table | New $R(\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \underline{\text{PhoneNo}})$ |
| Age | Derived | Omit from base | Computed at query time |
| Email | Single-valued, NULL allowed | Keep as column, allow NULL | Email (NULL allowed) |
| Branch | Simple, NOT NULL | Keep as column, NOT NULL | Branch (NOT NULL) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (End-to-End Mapping)

**Problem:** Consider an entity $E = \text{EMPLOYEE}$ with attributes:
- `EmpID` (key)
- `Name` (simple)
- `Address` (composite: HouseNo, City, Pincode)
- `Skills` (multi-valued)
- `Salary` (simple, stored)
- `Bonus` (derived as 10% of Salary)
- `JoiningDate` (simple)

**Step 1 — Decompose composite attributes.**

$\text{Address} \rightarrow (\text{HouseNo}, \text{City}, \text{Pincode})$

**Step 2 — Remove derived attributes.**

`Bonus` is removed; will be computed.

**Step 3 — Extract multi-valued attributes into a new table.**

`Skills` becomes a new relation.

**Step 4 — Construct final schemas.**

$$R_1 = \text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{Name}, \text{HouseNo}, \text{City}, \text{Pincode}, \text{Salary}, \text{JoiningDate})$$

$$R_2 = \text{EMPLOYEE\_SKILLS}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \underline{\text{Skill}})$$

$$R_3 = \text{EMPLOYEE\_BONUS} \text{ (View)} : \text{Bonus} = 0.10 \times \text{Salary}$$

> This three-relation output is the **canonical KTU-style answer** for an attribute-mapping question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Complete Classification of Attributes

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of the attribute taxonomy, as covered in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ATTRIBUTE] --> B[CLASSIFICATION DIMENSION]
    B --> D1[ATOMICITY]
    B --> D2[CARDINALITY]
    B --> D3[STORAGE]
    B --> D4[NULL STATUS]
    B --> D5[KEY STATUS]

    D1 --> S1[SIMPLE ATOMIC]
    D1 --> S2[COMPOSITE]

    D2 --> C1[SINGLE VALUED]
    D2 --> C2[MULTI VALUED]

    D3 --> ST1[STORED]
    D3 --> ST2[DERIVED]

    D4 --> N1[NULL PERMITTED]
    D4 --> N2[NOT NULL]

    D5 --> K1[KEY ATTRIBUTE]
    D5 --> K2[NON KEY ATTRIBUTE]

    S2 --> EX1[Example Address to HouseNo Street City Pincode]
    C2 --> EX2[Example PhoneNumbers Skills Emails]
    ST2 --> EX3[Example Age from DOB Total from parts]
    K1 --> EX4[Example RollNo EmpID ISBN]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — ER-to-Relational Mapping Pipeline

This block shows the **architectural flow** of how each attribute type gets transformed into relational schema components. It is the answer template expected by KTU examiners.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[ENTITY E WITH ATTRIBUTES] --> P1{CLASSIFY EACH ATTRIBUTE}

    P1 --> P2[SIMPLE ATOMIC SINGLE VALUED STORED]
    P1 --> P3[COMPOSITE]
    P1 --> P4[MULTI VALUED]
    P1 --> P5[DERIVED]
    P1 --> P6[KEY]

    P2 --> Q1[RETAIN AS COLUMN IN R PARENT]
    P3 --> Q2[DECOMPOSE TO LEAVES IN R PARENT]
    P4 --> Q3[CREATE NEW RELATION RM WITH COMPOSITE PK]
    P5 --> Q4[OMIT FROM BASE COMPUTE AT RUNTIME]
    P6 --> Q5[DESIGNATE AS PRIMARY KEY OF R PARENT]

    Q1 --> Z1[FINAL RELATIONAL SCHEMA]
    Q2 --> Z1
    Q3 --> Z1
    Q4 --> Z1
    Q5 --> Z1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Concrete STUDENT Entity Example

The following diagram visualizes a typical STUDENT entity with all five attribute types annotated.

```mermaid
graph TD
    STU[STUDENT ENTITY]
    STU --> A1[RollNo KEY]
    STU --> A2[Name SIMPLE]
    STU --> A3[Address COMPOSITE]
    STU --> A4[PhoneNumbers MULTI VALUED]
    STU --> A5[Age DERIVED]
    STU --> A6[Branch SIMPLE NOT NULL]

    A3 --> A3A[HouseNo]
    A3 --> A3B[Street]
    A3 --> A3C[City]
    A3 --> A3D[Pincode]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define an attribute. What is meant by the domain of an attribute?**

**Model Answer:**

An **attribute** is a named property or characteristic that describes an entity or a relationship in a database. Formally, for an entity set $E$, an attribute $A$ is a function $A : E \rightarrow \text{Dom}(A)$ that maps each entity to an atomic value belonging to its allowed value set.

The **domain** of an attribute is the set of all **permissible atomic values** that the attribute can legally take. It restricts the values to maintain data integrity.

**Example:** For attribute `Marks`, the domain is:
$$\text{Dom}(\text{Marks}) = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 100\}$$

Domains enforce type and value constraints (e.g., `CHAR(2)`, `INTEGER`, `NOT NULL`).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**Q: Distinguish between a composite attribute and a multi-valued attribute. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Composite Attribute | Multi-Valued Attribute |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | An attribute that can be **subdivided** into smaller meaningful sub-parts | An attribute that can hold **multiple values** for a single entity |
| Sub-structure | Has sub-attributes with own names | Has multiple instances of the same simple value |
| 1NF Compliance | Must be decomposed before storage | Must be moved to a new table |
| ER Notation | Oval connected to sub-ovals | Double oval |
| Example | `Name` $\rightarrow$ (`FirstName`, `LastName`) | `PhoneNumbers` $\rightarrow$ (9876543210, 9123456780) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two alternatives provided; answer **ONE** full set.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]`

**(a) Explain the different types of attributes used in the ER model with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The ER model classifies attributes along five dimensions:

**1. Simple (Atomic) Attribute:** Cannot be subdivided.  
Example: `Age`, `Salary`, `RollNo`.

**2. Composite Attribute:** Subdivides into meaningful sub-parts.  
Example: `Address` $\rightarrow$ (`HouseNo`, `Street`, `City`, `Pincode`). It provides logical grouping.

**3. Single-Valued Attribute:** Has exactly one value per entity.  
Example: `DateOfBirth` for a person.

**4. Multi-Valued Attribute:** Can hold a set of values.  
Example: `PhoneNumbers`, `EmailIDs`. (Drawn as a double oval in ER diagrams.)

**5. Derived Attribute:** Computed from other attributes; not stored.  
Example: `Age` is derived from `DateOfBirth` and the current date. (Drawn with a dashed oval.)

**6. Null Attribute:** Value is unknown, missing, or not applicable.  
Example: `MiddleName` of a person who has no middle name.

**7. Key Attribute:** Uniquely identifies an entity.  
Example: `RollNo` of STUDENT, `EmpID` of EMPLOYEE. (Shown with underlined text inside the oval.)

A real-world example using a **STUDENT** entity:

| Attribute | Type |
| :--- | :--- |
| RollNo | Key (Simple, Single-Valued, Stored) |
| Name | Composite (FirstName, LastName) |
| DOB | Simple, Single-Valued, Stored |
| Age | Simple, Derived (from DOB) |
| PhoneNumbers | Multi-Valued |
| Branch | Simple, Single-Valued, NOT NULL |

---

**(b) Map the following entity to a relational schema, clearly handling composite and multi-valued attributes. (7 Marks)**

**Entity:** `LIBRARY_BOOK(BookID, Title, AuthorName, Publisher, YearOfPublication, Keywords, Price)`

Where:
- `BookID` is the key
- `AuthorName` is composite (`FirstName`, `LastName`)
- `Publisher` is composite (`PublisherName`, `City`)
- `Keywords` is multi-valued
- `Price` is simple

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose composite attributes.**

$\text{AuthorName} \rightarrow (\text{AuthorFirstName}, \text{AuthorLastName})$  
$\text{Publisher} \rightarrow (\text{PublisherName}, \text{PublisherCity})$

**Step 2 — Extract multi-valued attribute `Keywords` into a new relation.**

**Step 3 — Final relational schemas:**

$$R_1 = \text{LIBRARY\_BOOK}(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \text{Title}, \text{AuthorFirstName}, \text{AuthorLastName}, \text{PublisherName}, \text{PublisherCity}, \text{YearOfPublication}, \text{Price})$$

$$R_2 = \text{BOOK\_KEYWORDS}(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \underline{\text{Keyword}})$$

**Step 4 — Define constraints:**
- Primary Key of $R_1$: `BookID`
- Primary Key of $R_2$: Composite `(BookID, Keyword)`
- Foreign Key in $R_2$: `BookID` references $R_1(\text{BookID})$

This mapping satisfies 1NF because all attributes in both relations are atomic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) Define an attribute. Discuss the role of domains in ensuring data integrity. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**  
An **attribute** is a named data element that represents a property of an entity or relationship. In the relational model, an attribute is a column of a relation, and each cell of the column contains an atomic value drawn from a predefined domain.

**Role of Domains in Data Integrity:**

A **domain** $\text{Dom}(A)$ is a named set of atomic, homogeneous values. Domains enforce three layers of integrity:

**1. Type Integrity:** Each attribute has a fixed data type (e.g., `INTEGER`, `CHAR`, `DATE`). The DBMS rejects values of the wrong type.

**2. Value Integrity (Range Constraints):** The domain may restrict values to a finite set, e.g.:
$$\text{Dom}(\text{Grade}) = \{A, B, C, D, F\}$$

**3. Nullability Constraint:** A domain may declare the attribute `NOT NULL`, ensuring a value is always provided for mandatory fields.

**Example Domain Definition in SQL:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    RollNo  CHAR(8)        NOT NULL,
    Name    VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    Marks   INTEGER        CHECK (Marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100),
    Grade   CHAR(1)        CHECK (Grade IN ('A','B','C','D','F'))
);
```

Here, the domain of `Marks` is the integers from 0 to 100, and the domain of `Grade` is the explicit set $\{A, B, C, D, F\}$. Domains thus act as the **type system of the database**, preventing nonsensical data entry.

---

**(b) Explain the mapping of derived and multi-valued attributes to the relational model with an example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derived Attributes:**

A derived attribute is one whose value is **computed from other stored attributes** rather than being physically stored. The motivation is to:
- Avoid **redundancy** (no duplicate storage).
- Avoid **update anomalies** (a single change updates the derived value automatically).
- Save **storage space**.

**Mapping Rule:** A derived attribute is **omitted** from the base relation schema. It is computed at runtime using a SQL expression, a view, or a computed column.

**Example:** `Age` derived from `DOB`:
$$R_{\text{STUDENT}} = (\underline{\text{RollNo}}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB})$$
```sql
SELECT RollNo, Name, YEAR(CURDATE()) - YEAR(DOB) AS Age
FROM STUDENT;
```

**Multi-Valued Attributes:**

A multi-valued attribute violates **First Normal Form (1NF)**, which mandates that every cell hold an atomic value. Hence it must be moved out of the parent relation.

**Mapping Rule:** Create a **new relation** whose primary key is the combination of the parent entity's primary key and the multi-valued attribute itself.

**Example:** `Skills` is multi-valued for `EMPLOYEE`:
$$R_1 = \text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{Name}, \text{Salary})$$
$$R_2 = \text{EMP\_SKILLS}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \underline{\text{Skill}})$$

The schema $R_2$ allows:
$$\text{Dom}(\text{Skill}) = \text{STRING}, \quad \forall t \in R_2 : t[\text{Skill}] \neq \text{NULL}$$

This decomposition preserves all information and conforms to 1NF.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to underline the Primary Key.** KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if the PK is not explicitly underlined in the schema.
> 2. **Leaving the composite attribute's parent name in the schema.** When you decompose `Address` into `HouseNo, Street, City, Pincode`, do **not** retain a column literally called "Address" — only its sub-parts survive. Many students lose marks here.
> 3. **Storing derived attributes as columns.** If the question says `Age` is derived, do **not** include `Age` in the final base table. Mention that it is computed at query time.
> 4. **Missing the Foreign Key declaration.** When extracting a multi-valued attribute to a new table, explicitly write: "The parent key becomes a Foreign Key referencing the original relation." Examiners reward this precision.
> 5. **Conflating composite with multi-valued.** A common trap: `Name = (First, Last)` is **composite**, not multi-valued. `PhoneNumbers = {98765..., 91234...}` is **multi-valued**, not composite. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **No domain specification.** For 7-mark mapping questions, mention at least **one** domain constraint in your answer — e.g., "Marks must lie in $[0, 100]$". This adds 1 mark of "extra credit" in valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- [ ] An **attribute** is a named property of an entity or relationship; in relational terms, it is a **column** of a relation.
- [ ] **Domain** of an attribute = set of permitted atomic values. Always state the domain when defining a schema.
- [ ] **Composite** attributes must be **flattened** to atomic sub-parts during mapping.
- [ ] **Multi-valued** attributes must be moved to a **separate relation** with a composite primary key (parent PK + attribute).
- [ ] **Derived** attributes are **never stored**; they are computed via SQL functions or views.
- [ ] **Key** attributes uniquely identify an entity; the chosen one is the **Primary Key**.
- [ ] **Null** indicates missing / unknown / not-applicable values; it is **not** zero or blank.
- [ ] 1NF forbids multi-valued and composite (non-atomic) attributes at the cell level.
- [ ] In ER diagrams: simple = solid oval, multi-valued = **double oval**, derived = **dashed oval**, key = **underlined text inside oval**.
- [ ] The mapping pipeline: **Classify → Decompose composite → Extract multi-valued → Drop derived → Designate key**.
- [ ] Always declare **Foreign Key** relationships explicitly when extracting a new relation.
- [ ] Always **underline** the Primary Key in your final schema diagrams.
- [ ] For SQL mapping, the formula is: `GENERATED ALWAYS AS (expression) STORED` for derived columns (PostgreSQL/SQL:2003 standard).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "Composite → flatten, Multi-valued → new table, Derived → omit, Key → underline." Memorize this 4-step mantra and you can solve any attribute-mapping question in KTU Module 1 within 4 minutes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition**
> In the context of the **Relational Data Model**, a **Constraint** is a declarative rule or restriction enforced upon the columns (attributes) of a relation (table) to restrict the set of permissible values that can be legally inserted, updated, or stored in the database. Constraints are a cornerstone of **Data Integrity** and are mathematically derived from **Set Theory** and **First-Order Predicate Logic** applied to tuples within a relation.

In simpler terms, constraints are the **"traffic rules"** of your database. Just as traffic rules prevent vehicles from going in the wrong direction or parking in no-parking zones, database constraints prevent garbage, inconsistent, or illogical data from entering your tables.

A relation schema **R** is formally defined as:
$$R(A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots, A_n)$$

Where each attribute $A_i$ has an associated **domain** $D_i$. Constraints restrict the valid **instances** $r(R)$ such that:
$$r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3 \times \dots \times D_n$$

Only those tuples that satisfy **all** declared constraints are permitted in the relation state.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Government ID Office

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Ration Card Office**
> Imagine you walk into a Government Ration Card Registration Office. Before accepting your application, the clerk applies several **rules**:
>
> 1. **Name field cannot be empty** → This is the **NOT NULL** constraint.
> 2. **Aadhaar number must be unique** — no two people can share the same Aadhaar → This is the **UNIQUE** constraint.
> 3. **Age must be between 0 and 120** → This is the **CHECK** constraint.
> 4. **District defaults to "Kerala" if not provided** → This is the **DEFAULT** constraint.
> 5. **Ration Card number uniquely identifies each family** → This is the **PRIMARY KEY** constraint.
> 6. **A village code in the address must exist in the Village Master table** → This is the **FOREIGN KEY** (Referential Integrity) constraint.
>
> These rules ensure the database of ration cards remains **clean, reliable, and trustworthy**. Without them, anyone could enter age = -50 or duplicate IDs, causing chaos.

## 1.3 Why Constraints Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (PBERT504, Module 1)**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, constraints form the foundation for understanding:
> - **Schema Refinement** (covered later in Module 2)
> - **Normalization** (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF)
> - **Transaction Integrity** (ACID properties — Module 4)
> - **SQL DDL Commands** (CREATE, ALTER, DROP)

The integrity of a relational database rests on **three classical integrity rules** that are directly tied to the constraints discussed in this module.

| S.No | Integrity Rule | Associated Constraint Type | Description |
|------|----------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Entity Integrity** | PRIMARY KEY | No primary key attribute can be NULL |
| 2 | **Referential Integrity** | FOREIGN KEY | Foreign key must match a primary key in the parent table or be NULL |
| 3 | **Domain Integrity** | NOT NULL, CHECK, DEFAULT | Every attribute must contain a legal value from its domain |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of SQL Constraints

Constraints in SQL (the standard query language mandated by KTU syllabus) are broadly classified into two categories:

### A. Column-Level Constraints
Applied to a **single column** at the time of table definition. They are part of the column definition itself.

### B. Table-Level Constraints
Applied to one or more **columns** as a separate clause. They are necessary when a constraint involves multiple columns (e.g., composite primary key, composite foreign key).

## 2.2 The Seven Canonical Constraint Types

### 1. NOT NULL Constraint
- **Purpose**: Ensures that a column cannot contain a NULL value.
- **Why**: NULL represents "unknown" or "missing". For critical fields like employee name, registration number, etc., allowing NULL would break the meaning of the data.
- **Where defined**: Column-level only.
- **KTU Frequency**: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Most frequently asked)

### 2. UNIQUE Constraint
- **Purpose**: Ensures all values in a column (or set of columns) are distinct — no duplicates allowed.
- **Difference from PRIMARY KEY**: A UNIQUE column **can accept one NULL value** (in standard SQL, this is implementation-dependent). A table can have **multiple** UNIQUE constraints but only **one** PRIMARY KEY.
- **KTU Exam Trick**: Often tested against PRIMARY KEY to test student understanding of subtle differences.

### 3. PRIMARY KEY Constraint
- **Purpose**: Uniquely identifies each row in a table.
- **Properties**: 
  - Unique values
  - Cannot be NULL
  - Each table has **exactly one** primary key
- **Composite Primary Key**: When the primary key consists of 2 or more columns.
- **Mathematical Representation**: If **PK** is a primary key of relation R, then for any two tuples $t_1, t_2$ in $r(R)$:
$$t_1[PK] \neq t_2[PK] \quad \forall \quad t_1 \neq t_2$$

### 4. FOREIGN KEY Constraint (Referential Integrity)
- **Purpose**: Establishes a link between two tables. A foreign key in one table refers to the primary key in another table.
- **Referential Integrity Rule**: For every foreign key value in table R, either:
  - It must **match** an existing primary key value in the referenced table S, **OR**
  - It must be **NULL** (if the foreign key column allows NULL).
- **Mathematical Expression**: If **FK** in relation $R$ references **PK** in relation $S$:
$$\forall t_R \in r(R): \quad t_R[FK] = t_S[PK] \quad \text{for some} \quad t_S \in r(S) \quad \text{OR} \quad t_R[FK] = NULL$$

### 5. CHECK Constraint
- **Purpose**: Limits the values that can be placed in a column based on a Boolean expression.
- **Examples**: `CHECK (age >= 18)`, `CHECK (salary > 0)`, `CHECK (gender IN ('M', 'F', 'O'))`.
- **KTU Exam Focus**: Students often forget that CHECK can be applied at table level too for multi-column conditions.

### 6. DEFAULT Constraint
- **Purpose**: Provides a default value for a column when no value is specified during INSERT.
- **Example**: `DEFAULT 'Kerala'`, `DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE`.
- **Difference from other constraints**: It does **not restrict** insertion; it **supplies a value** when none is given.

### 7. INDEX Constraint (Implicit)
- **Purpose**: Although technically not an integrity constraint, **PRIMARY KEY** and **UNIQUE** automatically create an **index** to speed up lookups.
- **Explicit CREATE INDEX**: Not a constraint per se, but a performance optimization related to constraint enforcement.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save This Table — Critical for 3-Mark & 14-Mark Questions**

| Constraint | NULL Allowed? | Duplicates Allowed? | No. per Table | Column/Table Level | When Violated |
|------------|---------------|---------------------|---------------|--------------------|---------------|
