> 8. **Omitting foreign-key notation** — Always label `[FK]` on the foreign-key column for examiner clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **E-A-R primitives:** *Entities, Attributes, Relationships* — the three pillars of ER modeling.
- **Entity types:** *Strong* (single rect., own key) vs *Weak* (double rect., needs owner's key + partial key).
- **Five attribute types:** *Simple, Composite, Multivalued, Derived, Key*. Know the notation for each (single/double/dashed ellipse; underline for key).
- **Relationship degree:** *Unary (recursive), Binary, Ternary, n-ary*. Role names are mandatory for recursive relationships.
- **Cardinality ratios:** *1:1, 1:N, M:N* — expressed either by numeric labels (Chen) or by crow's foot / bar notation.
- **Participation:** *Total* (double line) means every entity instance *must* participate; *Partial* (single line) means participation is optional.
- **Mapping algorithm:** 8-step procedure. Strong entity → direct. Weak entity → composite PK with FK. 1:1 → FK on total side or new relation. 1:N → FK on N side. M:N → new relation with composite PK. Multivalued → new relation. n-ary → new relation with n-key PK. Specialization → choose one of 3 approaches.
- **EER additions:** *Specialization, Generalization, Aggregation, Inheritance*. Constraints: *disjoint vs overlapping*, *total vs partial*.
- **Notation matrices:** Chen (academic), Crow's Foot (engineering practice), UML (software engineering). Know all three — KTU 2024 accepts any, but Chen is the *default* in written exams.
- **Common exam triggers:** weak entity + identifying relationship; M:N with descriptive attribute; ternary relationship; specialization with disjoint/total constraints.
- **Valuation safety net:** Always label primary keys (underline), foreign keys ([FK]), cardinalities, and participation. Missing any one of these is a 1-mark deduction in a 14-mark question.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SME-PRAW-CT"**: **S**trong/Multivalued/Entity-weak → **P**articipation **R**atio (1:1, 1:N, M:N) → **A**ttribute types → **W**eak entity owner → **C**omposite key → **T**otal vs partial. Walk this checklist for every ER question in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Relational Model - Structure of Relational Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Relational Model - Structure of Relational Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Relational Model** is a data model proposed by **Dr. Edgar F. Codd** in his landmark 1970 paper *"A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks"*. It represents the database as a **collection of relations** (which are physically perceived as **tables**). Each relation consists of a **schema** (the structural/intensional description) and an **instance** (the actual data rows that change over time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> A *relational database* is a database where all data visible to the user is organized strictly as **tables (relations)** with values at the intersection of rows and columns, and where the operations on those tables produce only other tables as results (the **closure property**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Excel Spreadsheet" Mental Model

Think of a **library register book**:
- The **entire register** is a *Database*.
- Each **page of the register** dedicated to one category (say, "Members" or "Books") is a *Relation (Table)*.
- The **column headings** (Name, ID, Phone, Join Date) are *Attributes*.
- The **set of allowed values** for a column (e.g., only integers for ID, dates for Join Date) is the *Domain*.
- Each **row entry** (Mr. Raj, 1023, 9876543210, 2024-01-15) is a *Tuple (Record)*.
- The **rule** that "every member must have a unique ID" is a *Key Constraint*.
- The **relation between Members page and Books Issue page** (Member ID in Issue matches ID in Members) is a *Foreign Key* — this is what gives the database its "**relational**" nature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Relational"?**
> Despite the misleading name, "relational" does **not** mean tables are connected by arrows in the mathematical sense. It comes from the **mathematical concept of a "relation"** (a subset of the Cartesian product of sets) in set theory.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of the Relational Model

| Pillar | Purpose | Key Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | What does the data look like? | Tables, Domains, Schemas |
| **Integrity** | What rules must the data obey? | Keys, Domain Constraints, Referential Integrity |
| **Manipulation** | How do we query/modify? | Relational Algebra, Relational Calculus, SQL |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish between **Schema** (the blueprint, e.g., `STUDENT(ID, Name, Age)`) and **Instance** (the actual snapshot of rows at a moment in time).

## 1.4 Essential Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Synonym | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Relation | Table | `STUDENT` |
| Tuple | Row / Record | `(101, 'Anu', 20)` |
| Attribute | Column / Field | `Name` |
| Domain | Allowed value set | Integers, Strings, Dates |
| Cardinality | Number of tuples | 500 students |
| Degree / Arity | Number of attributes | 4 attributes |
| Schema | Structure / Intension | `STUDENT(ID, Name, Age, Dept)` |
| Instance | Snapshot / Extension | Current set of 500 rows |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation (Set Theory Basis)

The relational model is grounded in **set theory** and **first-order predicate logic**. Every relation is mathematically defined as:

> A **relation** $R$ is a **subset of the Cartesian product** of its domains.

This precise mathematical underpinning is what gives the model its rigor and enables declarative query languages (SQL).

## 2.2 Hierarchical Structural Breakdown

### Layer 1 — The Domain
- A **domain** $D$ is a set of **atomic (indivisible) values** of the same type.
- Examples: `integer`, `string`, `date`, `boolean`, `phone_number(10-digit)`.
- All domains are required to be **atomic** — meaning no domain value can itself be a relation or a set (this is **First Normal Form**).

### Layer 2 — The Attribute Schema
- An **attribute** $A_i$ is the **name** given to a domain $D_i$ within a relation schema.
- Formally, an attribute is a **(role-playing domain)** — the same domain can serve different roles.
  - Example: Domain `integer` can play the role `StudentID` in one table and `CourseID` in another.

### Layer 3 — The Relation Schema
- A **relation schema** $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$ is a list of attributes and their corresponding domains.
- Notation: $R = (A_1: D_1, A_2: D_2, \ldots, A_n: D_n)$
- This is the **intensional** part — it does not change often.

### Layer 4 — The Relation Instance
- A **relation instance** $r(R)$ is a **set of $n$-tuples** $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_m$ where each $t_i$ is an ordered list of $n$ values.
- Mathematically: $r(R) \subseteq (D_1 \times D_2 \times \cdots \times D_n)$
- This is the **extensional** part — it changes with every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.

### Layer 5 — The Database Schema
- A **relational database schema** $S$ is a set of relation schemas: $S = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_k\}$ along with **integrity constraints** (ICs).
- A **database instance** $DB$ is a set of relation instances: $DB = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k\}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formal Meaning | Example / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | A domain (atomic value set) | $D_{age} = \{0, 1, \ldots, 150\}$ |
| $D_1 \times D_2$ | Cartesian product of two domains | All $(x,y)$ pairs |
| $r(R)$ | A relation instance of schema $R$ | Current rows of `STUDENT` |
| $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$ | Relation schema with $n$ attributes | `STUDENT(ID, Name, Age)` |
| $\text{deg}(R)$ or arity | Number of attributes in $R$ | 3 for `STUDENT` |
| $\vert r(R) \vert$ or $\text{card}(r)$ | Cardinality — number of tuples | 500 rows |
| $t[A_i]$ | Value of attribute $A_i$ in tuple $t$ | $t[\text{Name}] = \text{'Anu'}$ |
| $t[A_i, A_j]$ | Sub-tuple (projection over attributes) | $t[\text{Name}, \text{Age}]$ |
| $r_1 \bowtie r_2$ | Natural join of two instances | (Covered in Module 2) |
| $R_1 \cap R_2 = \emptyset$ | Disjoint schemas (no common attributes) | `STUDENT` vs `BOOK` |
| $K \rightarrow A$ | Functional dependency — $K$ determines $A$ | `ID \rightarrow Name` |
| $\text{DOM}(A_i)$ | Domain function for attribute $A_i$ | $\text{DOM}(\text{Age}) = \text{integer}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Use the vertical bar `\vert` (NOT the pipe character `\vert`) for absolute value and cardinality to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. E.g., write $\vert r(R) \vert$ not $\vert r(R) \vert$.

## 2.4 Properties of Relations (Exam-Favorite!)

Every relation in a relational database **must** satisfy the following properties:

1. **No Duplicate Tuples** — A relation is a *set* of tuples, not a multiset. Every tuple is unique.
2. **Tuples are Unordered** — Rows have no inherent sequence (insertion order is irrelevant to the model).
3. **Attributes are Unordered** — Columns can be reordered without changing the relation.
4. **Attribute Values are Atomic** — No lists, no sets, no relations within a cell (this enforces **1NF**).
5. **All Attribute Values are from the Same Domain** — for a given attribute column.
6. **Each Tuple has a Unique Key** — Guaranteed uniqueness via the primary key.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **"relations are unordered"** (a model property) with **"SQL tables can be displayed in any order via ORDER BY"** (a query feature). The model says order is meaningless; SQL just lets you impose one for display.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The relational model underpins nearly every modern enterprise system: **Banking (transaction ledgers)**, **E-commerce (orders, products, customers)**, **Healthcare (patient records)**, **University ERP systems** (KTU's own student portal uses an RDBMS), and **ERP/CRM platforms** (SAP, Oracle). Its mathematical foundation makes it provably correct, enabling query optimization that handles **petabyte-scale** data warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation: From Domain to Relation

Let us now **derive the relational model step by step** from first principles, the way a KTU examiner expects a 14-mark answer to be structured.

### Step 1 — Define the Atomic Domains

Let us declare three domains:

$$
D_1 = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 999\}
$$

$$
D_2 = \{\text{"Anu"}, \text{"Raj"}, \text{"Meera"}, \text{"Vijay"}\}
$$

$$
D_3 = \{18, 19, 20, 21, 22\}
$$

These represent `StudentID`, `StudentName`, and `StudentAge` respectively.

### Step 2 — Compute the Cartesian Product $D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3$

The Cartesian product is the set of **all possible ordered triples** $(d_1, d_2, d_3)$ where $d_1 \in D_1$, $d_2 \in D_2$, $d_3 \in D_3$. Formally:

$$
D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3 = \{(d_1, d_2, d_3) \mid d_1 \in D_1 \ \land \ d_2 \in D_2 \ \land \ d_3 \in D_3\}
$$

$$
|D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3| = |D_1| \times |D_2| \times |D_3| = 999 \times 4 \times 5 = 19980
$$

So there are **19,980 possible tuples** mathematically, but **only a few are meaningful** for a real database.

### Step 3 — Define the Relation Schema

$$
R(\text{ID}: D_1, \ \text{Name}: D_2, \ \text{Age}: D_3)
$$

The **degree** of $R$ is:

$$
\text{deg}(R) = 3
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Relation Instance as a Subset of the Cartesian Product

A relation instance $r(R)$ is a **subset** of the Cartesian product. We select only those triples that are *currently valid students*:

$$
r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3
$$

A concrete instance:

$$
r(R) = \{(101, \text{"Anu"}, 20), \ (102, \text{"Raj"}, 21), \ (103, \text{"Meera"}, 19)\}
$$

The **cardinality** is:

$$
|r(R)| = 3
$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Properties

| Property | Verification | Result |
|---|---|---|
| No duplicate tuples | All 3 tuples have distinct IDs (101, 102, 103) | ✅ Satisfied |
| Atomic values | Each value is a single integer or string | ✅ Satisfied |
| All values from domain | $101 \in D_1$, "Anu" $\in D_2$, $20 \in D_3$ | ✅ Satisfied |
| Unordered tuples | Tuples can be permuted freely | ✅ Satisfied |
| Unordered attributes | Columns can be permuted freely | ✅ Satisfied |

## 3.2 Derivation: Keys in the Relational Model

A **key** is a minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples. We derive it formally.

### Step 3.2.1 — Super Key

A set of attributes $K \subseteq \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ is a **super key** if for any two tuples $t_1, t_2 \in r(R)$:

$$
t_1[K] = t_2[K] \implies t_1 = t_2
$$

Equivalently: **no two distinct tuples can agree on all attributes of $K$.**

For our `STUDENT` example, possible super keys:
- $\{\text{ID}\}$ — works because IDs are unique.
- $\{\text{ID}, \text{Name}\}$ — also works (still unique).
- $\{\text{ID}, \text{Name}, \text{Age}\}$ — also works.
- $\{\text{Name}\}$ — does **not** work (two students could share a name in theory, though not in our instance).

### Step 3.2.2 — Candidate Key (Minimal Super Key)

A **candidate key** is a super key $K$ such that **no proper subset of $K$ is a super key**.

$$
K \text{ is a candidate key} \iff \forall K' \subsetneq K, \ K' \text{ is not a super key}
$$

For our `STUDENT` instance:
- $\{\text{ID}\}$ is a **candidate key** (no smaller subset exists).
- $\{\text{ID}, \text{Name}\}$ is **not** a candidate key (because $\{\text{ID}\}$ alone is sufficient).

### Step 3.2.3 — Primary Key

The **primary key** is the candidate key **chosen by the database designer** to be the principal identifier. It cannot have `NULL` values (**Entity Integrity Constraint**).

$$
\text{PK}(R) = \{\text{ID}\}
$$

### Step 3.2.4 — Foreign Key

A **foreign key** in relation $R_1$ is an attribute (or set) that **references the primary key of another relation** $R_2$. Formally:

$$
\text{FK}(R_1) \subseteq R_1, \quad \text{FK}(R_1) \text{ references } \text{PK}(R_2)
$$

For every value $v$ appearing in the foreign key column of $R_1$, the value $v$ **must exist** as a primary key value in $R_2$ (or be `NULL`). This is the **Referential Integrity Constraint**.

### Step 3.2.5 — Alternate / Unique Key

Any candidate key that is **not** chosen as the primary key is an **alternate key** (also called a *unique key*). It still enforces uniqueness but may allow `NULL` (typically only one `NULL` in some DBMSs).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Student Confusions

| Confusion | Correct Understanding |
|---|---|
| "A relation is a table with rows and columns" | Mathematically, a relation is a *set* of tuples. The "table" is just a visual aid. |
| "A relation is a relationship between tables" | **No.** "Relational" refers to the mathematical term "relation" (subset of Cartesian product). |
| "Primary key must always be a single column" | False. A **composite primary key** spans multiple columns (e.g., `(StudentID, CourseID)` in an enrollment table). |
| "Foreign key must have the same name as the primary key" | False. Only the **values** must match; column names can differ. |
| "All candidate keys are alternate keys if not primary" | True, but the chosen PK is the *primary* one. Alternate keys are still candidate keys. |
| "Schema changes with every INSERT" | **No.** Schema (intension) is stable; only the instance (extension) changes. |
| "NULL means zero or empty string" | **No.** NULL means *unknown* or *not applicable* — it is the absence of any value. |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Identifying Keys in a Sample Relation

Given the relation:

$$
R(\text{EmpID}, \text{SSN}, \text{Name}, \text{DeptID}, \text{Salary})
$$

Assuming `EmpID` is unique company-wide and `SSN` is unique per person, and that `DeptID` is not unique (multiple employees per department):

| Attribute Set | Super Key? | Candidate Key? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{\text{EmpID}\}$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Unique by assumption |
| $\{\text{SSN}\}$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Unique by assumption |
| $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{SSN}\}$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Not minimal (proper subset is a CK) |
| $\{\text{Name}\}$ | ❌ No | ❌ No | Two employees can share a name |
| $\{\text{DeptID}\}$ | ❌ No | ❌ No | Multiple employees in one dept |
| $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{Name}\}$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Redundant (EmpID alone suffices) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Killer Tip:** When asked *"find all candidate keys"*, always list each **minimal** super key separately, and clearly state *why* larger supersets are not candidate keys (lack of minimality).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of a Relational Database

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the structural hierarchy from raw data values all the way up to the complete database.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer5["LAYER 5: DATABASE INSTANCE (Extension)"]
        DBInst["DB = {r1, r2, r3, ...} - Current set of all relation instances"]
    end
    subgraph Layer4["LAYER 4: DATABASE SCHEMA (Intension)"]
        DBSchema["S = {R1, R2, R3, ...} + Integrity Constraints"]
    end
    subgraph Layer3["LAYER 3: RELATION INSTANCE r(R)"]
        RelInst["Set of n-tuples t1, t2, ..., tm"]
        T1["Tuple 1: (101, Anu, 20)"]
        T2["Tuple 2: (102, Raj, 21)"]
        T3["Tuple 3: (103, Meera, 19)"]
    end
    subgraph Layer2["LAYER 2: RELATION SCHEMA R(A1..An)"]
        RelSchema["R(ID:D1, Name:D2, Age:D3) - Degree n=3"]
    end
    subgraph Layer1["LAYER 1: DOMAINS (Atomic Value Sets)"]
        D1["D1 = Integers 1..999 (StudentID)"]
        D2["D2 = {Anu, Raj, Meera, Vijay} (StudentName)"]
        D3["D3 = {18, 19, 20, 21, 22} (StudentAge)"]
    end
    D1 --> RelSchema
    D2 --> RelSchema
    D3 --> RelSchema
    RelSchema --> RelInst
    T1 --> RelInst
    T2 --> RelInst
    T3 --> RelInst
    RelInst --> DBSchema
    DBSchema --> DBInst
```

## 4.2 Entity-Relationship Conceptual → Relational Mapping

This diagram shows how a real-world **University domain** gets mapped into a relational schema, illustrating keys and foreign-key relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEPT["DEPARTMENT Relation"]
        DEPT_ID["DeptID (PK)"]
        DEPT_NAME["DeptName"]
        DEPT_HOD["HOD"]
    end
    subgraph STUD["STUDENT Relation"]
        STU_ID["StudentID (PK)"]
        STU_NAME["Name"]
        STU_AGE["Age"]
        STU_DEPT["DeptID (FK -> DEPARTMENT)"]
    end
    subgraph ENROLL["ENROLLMENT Relation"]
        ENR_SID["StudentID (PK,FK)"]
        ENR_CID["CourseID (PK,FK)"]
        ENR_GRADE["Grade"]
    end
    subgraph COURSE["COURSE Relation"]
        CRS_ID["CourseID (PK)"]
        CRS_NAME["CourseName"]
        CRS_CRED["Credits"]
    end
    DEPT_ID -.Referenced by.-> STU_DEPT
    STU_ID -.Referenced by.-> ENR_SID
    CRS_ID -.Referenced by.-> ENR_CID
    DEPT --- STUD
    STUD --- ENROLL
    COURSE --- ENROLL
```

## 4.3 Key Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K["KEY (set of attributes ensuring tuple uniqueness)"]
    K --> SK["SUPER KEY: Any set of attrs that uniquely identifies tuples"]
    K --> CK["CANDIDATE KEY: Minimal super key"]
    SK --> CK
    CK --> PK["PRIMARY KEY: Chosen candidate key (NOT NULL)"]
    CK --> AK["ALTERNATE / UNIQUE KEY: Unchosen candidate keys"]
    CK --> FK["FOREIGN KEY: References PK of another relation"]
    FK -.May also be.-> CK
```

## 4.4 Schema vs Instance — The "Blueprint vs Building" Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCH["SCHEMA (Intension) - Blueprint - Rarely changes"]
        S1["STUDENT (StudentID, Name, Age, DeptID)"]
        S2["COURSE (CourseID, CourseName, Credits)"]
        S3["ENROLLMENT (StudentID, CourseID, Grade)"]
    end
    subgraph INS["INSTANCE (Extension) - Snapshot - Changes every transaction"]
        I1["STUDENT = {(101,Anu,20,CS), (102,Raj,21,EC)}"]
        I2["COURSE = {(CS201,DBMS,4), (EC202,Circuits,3)}"]
        I3["ENROLLMENT = {(101,CS201,A), (102,EC202,B+)}"]
    end
    S1 --- I1
    S2 --- I2
    S3 --- I3
    INS["INSTANCE (Extension) - Snapshot - Changes every transaction"]:::mut
    SCH["SCHEMA (Intension) - Blueprint - Rarely changes"]:::stable
    classDef stable fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mut fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Relation, (ii) Tuple, (iii) Attribute, (iv) Domain.**

**Model Answer (≈120 words):**
- **Relation:** A table/relation $R$ is a subset of the Cartesian product of one or more domains. Example: `STUDENT` table.
- **Tuple:** A single row of a relation; an ordered list of $n$ attribute values. Example: `(101, 'Anu', 20)`.
- **Attribute:** A named column of a relation; the role a domain plays in that relation. Example: `Name` in `STUDENT`.
- **Domain:** The set of permitted atomic values for an attribute. Example: `Age` has domain `integer ≥ 0`.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Schema and Instance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (≈80 words):**
The **schema** is the *intensional* structure of a database — the names of tables, their attributes, and data types. It is fixed at design time. The **instance** is the *extensional* data — the actual rows present at a particular moment. It changes with every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Example: `STUDENT(ID, Name, Age)` is the schema, while the 500 actual student rows constitute the instance. Schema is like a class definition; instance is like the set of objects in memory.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]*

**Consider the following relational schema for a Library Management System:**

$$
\text{BORROWER}(\underline{\text{BID}}, \ \text{Name}, \ \text{Phone}, \ \text{MembershipType})
$$

$$
\text{BOOK}(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \ \text{Title}, \ \text{Author}, \ \text{ISBN})
$$

$$
\text{LOAN}(\underline{\text{BID}}, \ \underline{\text{BookID}}, \ \text{IssueDate}, \ \text{DueDate}, \ \text{ReturnDate})
$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Identify the primary keys, foreign keys, and alternate keys (if any) in the above schema. Justify each identification with one reason.**

**Model Solution:**

| Relation | Primary Key | Justification | Foreign Key | References | Alternate Key |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BORROWER | `BID` | Uniquely identifies each borrower; assumed unique | None | — | `Phone` (if unique) |
| BOOK | `BookID` | Uniquely identifies each book | None | — | `ISBN` (uniquely identifies a book by its standard number) |
| LOAN | `(BID, BookID)` composite | A borrower can borrow many books and a book can be borrowed many times, but a specific borrower-book-loan combination is unique | `BID` | `BORROWER(BID)` | None |
| LOAN | — | — | `BookID` | `BOOK(BookID)` | None |

**Key Reasoning Steps:**
1. **BORROWER** — The underline beneath `BID` indicates it is the PK. `Phone` could be a candidate key if no two borrowers share a phone; if so, it is the alternate key.
2. **BOOK** — `BookID` is underlined (PK). `ISBN` is a globally unique book identifier — hence it is a **candidate/alternate key** (alternate if not chosen as PK).
3. **LOAN** — Underlines under both `BID` and `BookID` indicate a **composite primary key**. A borrower can issue the same book only once at a given moment in this model, making the combination unique.
4. **LOAN** has two foreign keys (`BID` and `BookID`) establishing referential links to BORROWER and BOOK respectively — these are the **relations** in "relational database".

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**State and explain the three types of integrity constraints that must hold on the above schema. Also explain the closure property of the relational model.**

**Model Solution:**

**Integrity Constraints:**

1. **Entity Integrity Constraint:**
   - The primary key of any relation must have a **non-NULL value** for every tuple.
   - Reasoning: If the PK were NULL, we could not uniquely identify the tuple, defeating its purpose.
   - Applied here: `BORROWER.BID`, `BOOK.BookID`, and `LOAN.(BID, BookID)` must never be NULL.

2. **Referential Integrity Constraint:**
   - Every foreign key value in a referencing relation must **either match a primary key value in the referenced relation OR be NULL**.
   - Applied here:
     - `LOAN.BID` must match some existing `BORROWER.BID`.
     - `LOAN.BookID` must match some existing `BOOK.BookID`.
   - This prevents dangling references and ensures relationships are valid.

3. **Domain Integrity Constraint:**
   - Every attribute value must come from its specified domain (atomic, valid type).
   - Applied here: `Phone` must be a 10-digit string; `IssueDate`/`DueDate` must be valid `DATE` types; `ReturnDate` ≥ `IssueDate` (a *semantic* domain rule).

**Closure Property of the Relational Model:**

The closure property states that the **result of any relational operation is itself a relation (table)**. Operations like SELECT, PROJECT, JOIN, UNION, etc., all take one or more relations as input and produce another relation as output. This means operations can be **nested** arbitrarily, enabling powerful compositional queries. Formally:

$$
\forall \text{ operation } \ \theta, \ \forall \text{ relations } r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k: \ \theta(r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k) = r_{\text{new}}
$$

where $r_{\text{new}}$ is also a relation. This is why SQL queries can be embedded inside other SQL queries seamlessly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Alternative]*

**Given the following relation instances for a university database:**

$$
R_1 = \text{STUDENT} = \{ (101, \text{Anu}, \text{CS}), (102, \text{Raj}, \text{EC}), (103, \text{Meera}, \text{CS}), (104, \text{Vijay}, \text{ME}) \}
$$

$$
R_2 = \text{COURSE} = \{ (\text{CS201}, \text{DBMS}, 4), (\text{CS202}, \text{OS}, 3), (\text{EC201}, \text{Circuits}, 4) \}
$$

$$
R_3 = \text{ENROLL} = \{ (101, \text{CS201}), (102, \text{EC201}), (103, \text{CS202}), (104, \text{CS201}) \}
$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Identify the candidate keys, primary keys, and any foreign keys. State the degree and cardinality of each relation.**

**Model Solution:**

| Relation | Attributes | Degree $\text{deg}(R)$ | Cardinality $\vert r(R) \vert$ | Primary Key | Foreign Keys |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| STUDENT | ID, Name, Dept | 3 | 4 | `ID` | None |
| COURSE | CCode, CName, Credits | 3 | 3 | `CCode` | None |
| ENROLL | SID, CCode | 2 | 4 | `(SID, CCode)` composite | `SID` → STUDENT, `CCode` → COURSE |

**Reasoning:**

- **STUDENT:** The `ID` is unique by inspection (101, 102, 103, 104 are all distinct). Thus `{ID}$ is a candidate key and is chosen as the primary key. `Name` is not a candidate key (assume two students could share a name in general, and even `Name="Anu"` is unique only by accident). `Dept` is not a candidate key (CS appears twice).
- **COURSE:** The `CCode` is unique (CS201, CS202, EC201 are all distinct). So `{CCode}$ is the candidate key/primary key. `(CName, Credits)` is a candidate key too if no two courses share both — but in this instance it is also unique. Hence `CCode` is the chosen PK; `(CName, Credits)` is the alternate key.
- **ENROLL:** Both `SID` and `CCode` separately have duplicates (e.g., SID=101 and SID=103 both appear for CS courses). But the **combination** `(SID, CCode)` is unique. Hence this composite is the primary key. The individual components are foreign keys referencing the other two relations.

**Mathematical summary:**

$$
\text{deg}(\text{STUDENT}) = 3, \quad |\text{STUDENT}| = 4
$$

$$
\text{deg}(\text{COURSE}) = 3, \quad |\text{COURSE}| = 3
$$

$$
\text{deg}(\text{ENROLL}) = 2, \quad |\text{ENROLL}| = 4
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**List and explain the six properties that every relation in a relational database must satisfy. Why is each important?**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Property of Uniqueness of Tuples (Set Property):** A relation is a mathematical **set**, not a multiset. Therefore, no two tuples can be identical in all attribute values. *Importance:* Prevents data redundancy at the tuple level; allows set-theoretic operations.

2. **Property of Tuple Unorderliness:** The tuples in a relation have **no inherent order**. They can be permuted, stored, or retrieved in any order. *Importance:* The model is independent of physical storage; the DBMS is free to optimize storage and access paths.

3. **Property of Attribute Unorderliness:** The attributes of a relation are also **unordered** in the model. They are identified by name, not by position. *Importance:* Allows schema evolution (adding/removing columns) without breaking applications; queries reference columns by name.

4. **Property of Atomic Attribute Values (1NF):** Every value at the intersection of a row and column must be **atomic** (indivisible) — no sets, lists, or relations within a cell. *Importance:* Simplifies data manipulation; eliminates nested structures; enables first-order logic queries.

5. **Property of Uniform Domain per Attribute:** All values in a given attribute column must belong to the **same domain** (same data type and semantic meaning). *Importance:* Ensures data integrity; enables type-checking and consistent comparisons.

6. **Property of Existence of a Primary Key:** Every relation must have a **primary key** (a unique, non-null identifier for tuples). *Importance:* Guarantees every tuple is individually addressable; foundational for entity integrity and foreign key references.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing the **mathematical definition** of a relation (subset of Cartesian product) with the **physical table**.
> 2. Stating *NULL = zero* or *NULL = empty string* in Part A answers. **NULL means unknown or undefined.**
> 3. Forgetting to mention **entity integrity** when discussing primary keys.
> 4. Saying *"the primary key is the first column"* — no positional ordering exists in the model.
> 5. Marking all underlined attributes as primary keys without verifying **composite PKs** (e.g., in `LOAN`).
> 6. Confusing **alternate key** with **secondary key** — alternate keys are *candidate keys not chosen as PK*, while secondary keys are *indexing* concepts (not part of the relational model).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Proposer:** Edgar F. Codd, IBM, **1970** (paper: *"A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks"*).
- **Relational Model is built on:** **Set Theory** + **First-Order Predicate Logic**.
- **Relation** = mathematical *set of tuples* ⊆ Cartesian product of domains.
- **Table is the visual representation** of a relation — not the relation itself.
- **Six Properties of Relations:** Uniqueness, Tuple Unordered, Attribute Unordered, Atomic Values (1NF), Uniform Domain, Existence of PK.
- **Schema (Intension)** = structure, *stable*, declared at design time.
- **Instance (Extension)** = current data, *dynamic*, changes with every DML.
- **Degree (Arity)** = number of *attributes* (columns) — a property of the schema.
- **Cardinality** = number of *tuples* (rows) — a property of the instance, varies with time.
- **Key Hierarchy:** Super Key ⊇ Candidate Key (minimal) → one chosen as **Primary Key**, rest become **Alternate Keys**.
- **Foreign Key** = attribute in $R_1$ that references the **Primary Key of $R_2$**; establishes the relationship.
- **Three Integrity Constraints:** Domain Integrity, Entity Integrity (PK ≠ NULL), Referential Integrity (FK must match or be NULL).
- **Closure Property:** The result of any relational operation is itself a relation — enables nested queries and compositionality.
- **First Normal Form (1NF)** is built into the relational model itself (atomic values).
- **Common student pitfalls:** confusing schema with instance; thinking NULL = 0; ignoring composite keys; missing alternate keys; confusing functional dependency notation $K \to A$ with foreign key references.
- **Notation to remember:** $R(A_1:D_1, A_2:D_2, \ldots, A_n:D_n)$ for schema, $r(R)$ for instance, $t[A_i]$ for tuple-component access, $\text{deg}(R)$ for degree, $\vert r(R) \vert$ for cardinality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Database Schema # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Database Schema** is the *logical structure* or *blueprint* of an entire database, defined during the database design phase, before any data is actually stored. It describes the **organization of data**, the **relationships among data entities**, the **data types**, the **constraints**, and the **operations** that can be performed on the data. In KTU-syllabus terms, a database schema is described using a **data definition language (DDL)** and is stored in the **data dictionary / system catalog** of the DBMS. The schema is *time-invariant* — it does not change frequently, while the *data* (instances) inside the schema change constantly. The KTU 2024 scheme refers to schema in three distinct layers following the **ANSI-SPARC architecture**: - **Internal Schema** (Physical Level) — How data is physically stored. - **Conceptual Schema** (Logical Level) — What data is stored and the relationships. - **External Schema** (View Level) — How individual users/groups see the data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Key Definition (Verbatim Use):** > *"A database schema is the overall logical/physical structure of the database, often expressed in a formal language supported by the DBMS. It represents the description of a database, including its schema name, integrity constraints, and is stored in the data dictionary."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Model Imagine you are building a **house**. Before laying even a single brick, the architect draws a **blueprint** showing: - How many rooms there are (entities/tables). - Where the doors and windows are (attributes/columns). - How the kitchen connects to the dining hall (relationships/foreign keys). - What materials the walls are made of (data types — *brick, glass, wood*). - Rules — *“no window in the bathroom facing the road”* (integrity constraints). Now compare: - **Blueprint = Database Schema** (designed *once*, changes rarely) - **House (the actual built structure, with people living in it) = Database Instance** (the *current data* living inside, changes all the time) - **Real-estate contract handed to the buyer = External Schema/View** (different buyers see different details) > [!NOTE] > **Memorize this analogy:** *Schema is the design of the database. Instance is a snapshot of the data in the database at a given moment in time.* ## 1.3 Key Characteristics (Bold Highlights for Board Exam) - **Schema is Meta-Data:** It is data *about* data. - **Time-Invariant:** Schema rarely changes; data (instances) change constantly. - **DDL-Defined:** Created using Data Definition Language commands like `CREATE TABLE`, `ALTER TABLE`. - **Stored in Data Dictionary:** A special system catalog maintained by the DBMS. - **Three Logical Levels:** External, Conceptual, and Internal — each with its own schema. - **Schema Mapping:** Transformations between levels are managed by the DBMS automatically. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Schema Architecture (ANSI-SPARC Model) The **ANSI-SPARC architecture** (proposed by the ANSI Standards Planning and Requirements Committee, **1975**) is the standard KTU-recommended framework for understanding database schemas. It separates the user application from the physical database through three independent levels of schemas. ### Level 1 — Internal Schema (Physical Level) - Describes the **physical storage structure** of the database. - Includes details like file organization (B+ tree, hashing), indexing, record placement, compression, and encryption. - Concerned with **storage allocation and access paths**. - Defined using a **Physical Data Description Language (PDDL)**. - *Example:* Student records are stored in a clustered B+ tree index on the `Roll_No` attribute, with 16 KB pages on disk. ### Level 2 — Conceptual Schema (Logical Level) - Represents the **entire database's logical structure** for the community of users. - Hides physical storage details; focuses on **entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints**. - Defined using a **Conceptual/Logical Data Description Language (CDDL/LDDL)** — typically an ER or relational model. - Only **one conceptual schema per database** (most important). - *Example:* `STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, DOB, Department)` with `Roll_No` as the primary key. ### Level 3 — External Schema (View Level) - Describes the portion of the database relevant to a **specific user or application group**. - Multiple external schemas can exist (e.g., a Student View, a Faculty View, an Accounts View). - Hides sensitive or irrelevant data (security/privacy). - Defined using a **View Definition Language**, often a sub-schema in DDL. - *Example:* A student portal showing only their own grades, not others'. > [!TIP] > **Why Three Levels?** — *Data Independence.* When the physical storage changes, the conceptual schema is *unaffected*; when the conceptual schema changes, the external schemas are *unaffected* (if no logical conflict). ## 2.2 Schema Mapping and Correspondence The DBMS transforms requests and results between the three levels automatically: | From → To | Mapping Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | External → Conceptual | **External / Conceptual Mapping** | Translates user-level view to logical schema | | Conceptual → Internal | **Conceptual / Internal Mapping** | Translates logical schema to physical storage | | Internal → OS / Disk | **Storage Mapping** | Maps DBMS storage to OS files & physical blocks | ## 2.3 Two Major Types of Data Independence - **Logical Data Independence** — The capacity to change the **conceptual schema** without having to change external schemas or application programs. *Example:* Adding a new attribute to a table without rewriting user apps. - **Physical Data Independence** — The capacity to change the **internal schema** without having to change the conceptual schema. *Example:* Reorganizing the on-disk file structure from heap files to B+ trees. ## 2.4 Schema vs. Instance — The KTU Favourite Comparison | Feature | **Database Schema** | **Database Instance** | |---|---|---| | What it is | Logical design / blueprint | Current data stored at a moment | | Also called | Intension | Extension | | When defined | At design time | At run time | | How often it changes | Rarely (only on schema evolution) | Every time data is inserted/updated/deleted | | Stored in | Data dictionary / system catalog | Disk blocks / memory buffers | | Analogy | Class definition in OOP | An object (instance) of the class | | Example | `STUDENT(Roll_No INT PK, Name VARCHAR(50))` | `(101, 'Anu'), (102, 'Rahul')` | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Notation Table | Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Standard Use | |---|---|---| | $S_{ext}$ | External Schema | View level description | | $S_{con}$ | Conceptual Schema | Logical level description | | $S_{int}$ | Internal Schema | Physical level description | | $I$ | Database Instance | Snapshot of data at time $t$ | | $DD$ | Data Dictionary | Stores all schema definitions | | $M_{ec}$ | External-Conceptual Mapping | Transforms views to logical | | $M_{ci}$ | Conceptual-Internal Mapping | Transforms logical to physical | | $LDI$ | Logical Data Independence | $S_{ext}$ unaffected by $S_{con}$ change | | $PDI$ | Physical Data Independence | $S_{con}$ unaffected by $S_{int}$ change | | $D(t)$ | Database state at time $t$ | $D(t) = \{ \text{rows of all relations at time } t \}$ | > [!NOTE] > **The "Schema Evolution" Function:** A schema may be evolved using the operation > $S_{new} = f(S_{old}, \Delta C)$ > where $\Delta C$ is the *set of schema change operations* (add column, drop table, change constraint, etc.) and $f$ is the schema evolution transform. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, database schema design underpins: - **Banking Systems** — Customer, Account, Transaction schemas. - **Hospital Information Systems** — Patient, Doctor, Prescription schemas. - **E-Commerce Platforms** — Product, Order, Customer, Cart schemas. - **IoT & Sensor Networks** — Time-series device-data schemas. - **AI/ML Pipelines** — Feature-store schemas with version control. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Formal Construction of a Relational Schema A **relational schema** $R$ is defined as a set of attributes $A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ together with a set of integrity constraints $IC$. We can express this formally: $$R \;=\; \langle \, A, \, IC \, \rangle$$ where $$A \;=\; \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$$ and $$IC \;=\; \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$$ For example, the schema for a `STUDENT` table: $$R_{STUDENT} \;=\; \langle \, \{ \text{RollNo}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB}, \text{Dept} \}, \, \{ \text{RollNo is PRIMARY KEY}, \, \text{Dept} \in \text{Valid\_Departments} \} \, \rangle$$ ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Schema Definition in SQL (DDL) This is the *practical realization* of the schema defined above. Each command corresponds to a DDL construct, and the DBMS automatically inserts the schema definition into its data dictionary. ### Step 1: Create the Database Container ```sql CREATE DATABASE KTU_University; ``` **Logic:** This statement allocates physical storage and registers a new database in the DBMS catalog. The conceptual schema of `KTU_University` is now empty but exists. ### Step 2: Create a Table (Defining the Schema) ```sql CREATE TABLE STUDENT ( RollNo INT NOT NULL, Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, DOB DATE NOT NULL, Dept VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, CGPA DECIMAL(3,2) CHECK (CGPA BETWEEN 0 AND 10), PRIMARY KEY (RollNo) ); ``` **Logic Row-by-Row:** - `RollNo INT NOT NULL` — defines attribute *domain* as integer, *null constraint* as forbidden. - `Name VARCHAR(50)` — variable-length string up to 50 chars. - `CGPA DECIMAL(3,2) CHECK (CGPA BETWEEN 0 AND 10)` — domain + integrity constraint. - `PRIMARY KEY (RollNo)` — entity integrity constraint (no duplicates, no NULLs). The DBMS now stores this in its **Data Dictionary** as the conceptual schema entry for `STUDENT`. ### Step 3: Add a Foreign Key (Relational Schema Mapping) ```sql ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD CONSTRAINT fk_dept FOREIGN KEY (Dept) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DCode); ``` **Logic:** This adds a *referential integrity constraint* — a schema-level rule that ensures the `Dept` value in `STUDENT` must exist in `DEPARTMENT.DCode`. This is a **schema mapping** between two relations. ### Step 4: Create a View (External Schema) ```sql CREATE VIEW Student_View AS SELECT RollNo, Name, Dept FROM STUDENT WHERE CGPA >= 8.0; ``` **Logic:** This defines an **external schema** for high-achieving students. The DBMS stores the view definition in the catalog; the data is materialized only on query (lazy evaluation). ## 3.3 Derivation — Schema-Instance Relationship Let $D$ be a database, and let $S$ be its conceptual schema. At any time $t$, the **state** (or instance) is: $$I_t \;=\; \{ r_1(t), \, r_2(t), \, \ldots, \, r_m(t) \}$$ where $r_i(t)$ is the *tuple-set* of relation $R_i$ at time $t$. **Step 1:** A schema $S = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m\}$ is a *set of relation schemas*. **Step 2:** Each $R_i$ has a *fixed attribute set* and *fixed constraints*. **Step 3:** At time $t$, the actual data $I_t$ is drawn from the Cartesian product of the attribute domains: $$r_i(t) \;\subseteq\; \text{dom}(A_1) \times \text{dom}(A_2) \times \cdots \times \text{dom}(A_n)$$ **Step 4:** The state is *valid* iff every tuple satisfies all the integrity constraints in $IC_i$: $$I_t \;\models\; IC \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall i, \; \forall t \in r_i(t) : \; t \;\text{satisfies}\; IC_i$$ **Step 5:** When the schema changes ($S \to S'$), the instance is automatically *mapped* to $I_t'$ by the DBMS using the schema evolution function $f$: $$I_t' \;=\; f(I_t, S \to S')$$ This is the formal meaning of *schema evolution* — preserving as much of the existing data as possible while applying the schema change. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Traps | Student Often Thinks | Correct Concept | KTU Mark Loss if Wrong | |---|---|---| | Schema and instance are the same | Schema = design, Instance = data | -2 | | Logical schema and physical schema are synonyms | Logical = conceptual; Physical = internal | -2 | | One database has only one external schema | Many external schemas (one per user group) | -1 | | Schema is stored with the data | Schema is stored in the **data dictionary** | -2 | | Views store data physically | Views are *virtual*; data comes from base tables | -2 | | Dropping a table changes the schema | Yes — the schema is *modified*; data is *deleted* | -1 | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Schema (ANSI-SPARC) Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph extLayer[External Level] ext1[External Schema 1
Student View] ext2[External Schema 2
Faculty View] ext3[External Schema 3
Accounts View] end subgraph conLayer[Conceptual Level] con1[Conceptual Schema
Entities Relationships Constraints] end subgraph intLayer[Internal Level] int1[Internal Schema
Physical Storage B Tree Hashing Indexing] end ext1 -->|External Conceptual Mapping| con1 ext2 -->|External Conceptual Mapping| con1 ext3 -->|External Conceptual Mapping| con1 con1 -->|Conceptual Internal Mapping| int1 int1 -->|Storage Mapping| diskLayer[(Physical Disk OS Files)] diskLayer[(Physical Disk OS Files)] ``` ## 4.2 Schema vs. Instance Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph schemaSide[Schema Side - Intension] s1[STUDENT Table Definition] s2[Attributes: RollNo Name DOB] s3[Constraints: PK FK CHECK] s4[Stored In: Data Dictionary] end subgraph instanceSide[Instance Side - Extension] i1[Row 1: 101 Anu 2003 05 12 CS] i2[Row 2: 102 Rahul 2003 11 04 EC] i3[Row 3: 103 Meera 2002 07 19 ME] i4[Stored In: Disk Pages] end schemaSide -.defines shape of.-> instanceSide instanceSide -.conforms to.-> schemaSide ``` ## 4.3 Database Definition Language Pipeline (DDL Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart TB start([User Issues DDL Statement]) --> parser[SQL Parser] parser --> validate[Validator Against Catalog] validate --> catalog{Schema Already Exists} catalog -- No --> createMeta[Create Meta Data Entry in Data Dictionary] catalog -- Yes --> error[Error: Object Already Exists] createMeta --> optimizer[Query Optimizer Not Needed for DDL] optimizer --> executor[DDL Executor] executor --> storage[Update Physical Storage and Lock Catalog] storage --> done([Schema Updated in Catalog]) ``` ## 4.4 Schema Evolution Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Current Schema S old] --> B[Identify Change delta C] B --> C{Type of Change} C -- Add Column --> D1[Alter Table Add Column] C -- Drop Column --> D2[Alter Table Drop Column] C -- Modify Type --> D3[Alter Table Alter Column] C -- Add Constraint --> D4[Alter Table Add Constraint] D1 --> E[Apply f to old Instance] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[New Schema S new and New Instance I new] F --> G[Validate Integrity] G -- Pass --> H([Schema Evolution Complete]) G -- Fail --> I([Rollback to S old]) ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > Define the following with a suitable example: > (i) Database Schema > (ii) Database Instance > (iii) Data Dictionary **Model Answer:** **(i) Database Schema:** The logical/physical structure of a database, defined at the design phase. It describes the tables, columns, data types, and constraints. It is stored in the data dictionary and rarely changes. *Example:* `STUDENT(RollNo: INT, Name: VARCHAR(50), DOB: DATE)`. **(ii) Database Instance:** The actual data stored in the database at a particular moment in time. It is a snapshot of the rows in the tables. It changes with every insert, update, or delete. *Example:* `{(101, 'Anu', 2003-05-12), (102, 'Rahul', 2003-11-04)}` is one instance of `STUDENT`. **(iii) Data Dictionary:** A system-managed catalog/store of meta-data within the DBMS. It contains the schema definitions, user information, integrity constraints, and authorization rules. It is also called the **system catalog**. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > List and briefly explain the three levels of the ANSI-SPARC architecture. **Model Answer:** The ANSI-SPARC architecture defines three levels: 1. **Internal Level (Physical Schema):** Describes the physical storage of the database — file organization, indexing methods, and access paths. It is closest to the operating system. 2. **Conceptual Level (Logical Schema):** Represents the entire database's community view — entities, relationships, and constraints. There is exactly one conceptual schema per database. 3. **External Level (View Schema):** Describes the user/application-specific view of the database. Multiple external schemas can exist, hiding sensitive or irrelevant data for security and customization. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 1, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` > **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-schema (ANSI-SPARC) architecture** in detail. Discuss the different types of mappings between the levels. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Define **Data Independence**. Explain **Logical Data Independence** and **Physical Data Independence** with examples. Why is data independence a desirable property of a DBMS? *(7 marks)* #### 📝 Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks The **ANSI-SPARC three-schema architecture** is a classical framework proposed in 1975 to separate user applications from the physical database. It has three levels: 1. **Internal Schema (Physical Level):** Describes the *physical storage structure* of the database. This includes file organization (e.g., B+ tree, hashing), indexing strategies, record placement, block sizes, and data compression. It uses a **Physical Data Description Language (PDDL)**. 2. **Conceptual Schema (Logical Level):** Represents the *logical structure of the entire database* — all entities, relationships, attributes, and integrity constraints — for the community of users. It hides physical storage details. Only **one conceptual schema per database** exists. It is defined using a **Conceptual Data Description Language** (often the relational model or ER model). 3. **External Schema (View Level):** Describes the portion of the database relevant to a specific *user group* or *application*. Multiple external schemas can exist, each tailored to a different need. Provides **security, simplicity, and customization**. Implemented via **views** in SQL. **Mappings:** - **External / Conceptual Mapping** — transforms user-level view requests into the conceptual schema. - **Conceptual / Internal Mapping** — transforms logical schema queries into physical storage operations. - **Internal / Storage Mapping** — manages how the OS stores the data on physical disks. **Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1) **Marks distribution:** Diagram — 2 marks, Three levels explained — 3 marks, Mappings — 2 marks. --- #### 📝 Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Data Independence** is defined as the capacity to change the schema at one level of the database system without having to change the schema at the next higher level. **1. Logical Data Independence (LDI):** - The ability to change the **conceptual schema** without affecting the external schemas or application programs. - *Example:* Adding a new column `Email` to the `STUDENT` table, or splitting `STUDENT` into `STUDENT_PERSONAL` and `STUDENT_ACADEMIC`. Existing user applications and views continue to work without modification. - Harder to achieve than physical data independence, because it may require new attribute mappings. **2. Physical Data Independence (PDI):** - The ability to change the **internal schema** (physical storage) without affecting the conceptual schema. - *Example:* Switching from sequential file organization to a B+ tree index, or moving the database to a new disk with a different block size. The conceptual schema and external schemas remain unchanged. - Easier to achieve; handled by the DBMS storage manager transparently. **Why Data Independence is Desirable:** - **Application Stability:** Programs do not need to be rewritten on schema changes. - **Maintenance:** Easier to upgrade, refactor, and tune the database. - **Portability:** A logical schema can be ported across different physical environments. - **Security:** External schemas can be modified without touching the global logical structure. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** *Many students confuse logical and physical data independence. Remember the mnemonic:* > - **P**hysical data independence → change **I**nternal (the 'I' is for Internal) > - **L**ogical data independence → change **C**onceptual (logical → conceptual) > *Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks easily.* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a)** Differentiate between **Database Schema** and **Database Instance**. Explain the concept of **Schema Evolution** with an example. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Write a **detailed note on the Data Dictionary**. Discuss the different types of information stored in it and its role in a DBMS. *(7 marks)* #### 📝 Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Difference between Schema and Instance:** | **Parameter** | **Schema** | **Instance** | |---|---|---| | Definition | Logical/physical structure of the DB | Snapshot of the data at a given time | | Also called | Intension | Extension | | When defined | At design time | At run time | | Frequency of change | Rarely | Frequently | | Storage | Data dictionary | Disk / memory | | Analogy | Class definition | Object of the class | **Schema Evolution:** Schema Evolution is the process of *modifying the database schema* over time to accommodate new application requirements, while preserving as much of the existing data as possible. **Example:** Consider the original `STUDENT` schema: $$R_{STUDENT} \;=\; \{ \text{RollNo}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB} \}$$ After a new requirement (e.g., storing the student's email), the schema is evolved: $$R_{STUDENT}' \;=\; \{ \text{RollNo}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB}, \text{Email} \}$$ The schema change $\Delta C = \{ \text{ADD COLUMN Email VARCHAR(100)} \}$ is applied via: ```sql ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD Email VARCHAR(100); ``` The DBMS automatically handles the data migration — existing rows get `Email = NULL`, and new rows can include an email. The schema is now $R_{STUDENT}'$ while older data is preserved. Common schema evolution operations include: - Adding/dropping a column. - Changing a column's data type. - Adding/dropping integrity constraints. - Renaming a table or column. - Splitting or merging tables. --- #### 📝 Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Data Dictionary (System Catalog):** A **Data Dictionary** is a centralized repository of *meta-data* (data about data) maintained automatically by the DBMS. It stores information about the database schema, users, constraints, indexes, and access privileges. **Information Stored in the Data Dictionary:** 1. **Schema Information:** - Names of tables, views, and indexes. - Attribute names, data types, and sizes. - Integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, unique, check). 2. **User Information:** - User IDs, passwords (hashed), roles, and groups. 3. **Authorization and Access Control:** - Privileges (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) granted to users. 4. **Storage Information:** - File names, locations, sizes, and block allocation. 5. **Index Information:** - Types of indexes, indexed columns, and storage details. 6. **Statistical Information:** - Table sizes, cardinality, and value distributions (used by the query optimizer). **Role of the Data Dictionary in a DBMS:** - **Centralized Meta-Data Management:** Provides a single source of truth for all schema definitions. - **Supports Data Independence:** Maps between external, conceptual, and internal schemas are recorded in the dictionary. - **Query Optimization:** The optimizer consults the dictionary for table sizes, indexes, and statistics. - **Access Control:** Enforces security by checking user privileges before executing queries. - **Schema Evolution Tracking:** Records historical versions of schemas for auditing and migration. - **Consistency Enforcement:** Verifies that all DDL and DML operations respect the schema's integrity constraints. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. *Writing only 3–4 types of information when asked to discuss — examiners expect at least 5–6 points for full marks.* > 2. *Confusing the data dictionary with a regular user table — it is a **system-managed catalog**, not a user table.* > 3. *Forgetting to mention the role of the data dictionary in query optimization — a key 2-mark point.* > 4. *In Question A, not drawing the three-schema diagram clearly — always label all three levels and the mappings.* --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **Database Schema** is the *blueprint*; a **Database Instance** is the *current data*. - ✅ Schema is also called the **intension**; instance is called the **extension**. - ✅ Schema is **time-invariant**; instance is **time-variant**. - ✅ Schema is stored in the **Data Dictionary**; data is stored on **disk blocks**. - ✅ The **ANSI-SPARC architecture** has three levels: **External, Conceptual, Internal**. - ✅ Only **one conceptual schema** exists per database, but **many external schemas** are possible. - ✅ **Mappings** between levels: External-Conceptual, Conceptual-Internal, Internal-Storage. - ✅ **Logical Data Independence** → protects external schemas from conceptual changes. - ✅ **Physical Data Independence** → protects conceptual schema from internal changes. - ✅ **Schema Evolution** modifies a schema while preserving data using operations like `ALTER TABLE`. - ✅ **Views** are *virtual external schemas*; they do not store data themselves. - ✅ The **Data Dictionary** stores meta-data: schema, users, constraints, indexes, privileges, statistics. - ✅ A schema is defined using **DDL** (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`). - ✅ A relational schema is formally written as $R = \langle A, IC \rangle$ (attributes + integrity constraints). - ✅ **Logical Data Independence is harder to achieve** than Physical Data Independence. - ✅ Always **draw the three-schema diagram** in exam answers — it is worth 2–3 marks easily.

topics.title: Keys
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Keys

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Key

A **Key** in a Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) is a single attribute, or a set of attributes (a composite key), that is used to uniquely identify a row (tuple) in a relation (table). Formally, a key is a minimal set of attributes whose values uniquely determine all other attributes of the tuple and contain no redundant attributes.

Mathematically, if $R(A_1, A_2, A_3, ..., A_n)$ is a relation schema with attributes $A_1, A_2, ..., A_n$, and $K \subseteq \{A_1, A_2, ..., A_n\}$, then $K$ is a **super key** if and only if for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in any legal relation state $r$ of $R$, $t_1[K] = t_2[K] \Rightarrow t_1[A_i] = t_2[A_i]$ for every attribute $A_i$ in $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept Highlight (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A key is the cornerstone of the **Entity Integrity Constraint** and **Referential Integrity Constraint** in the relational model. Without proper key selection, normalization, anomaly elimination, and join operations cannot be performed correctly.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **university** issuing ID cards to every student:
- The **Roll Number** (e.g., `KTE21CS001`) is like a **Primary Key** — it is unique to every student and never changes.
- The **Aadhaar Number** could also uniquely identify a student — it acts as an **Alternate Key** (another candidate that could have been chosen as primary).
- A combination of **First Name + Last Name + Date of Birth** might uniquely identify a student — this is a **Composite Key** (multiple columns together).
- The **Library Card Number** a student uses to borrow books from another table is like a **Foreign Key** — it links the Student table to the Borrow table.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need Keys?**
> - To prevent **duplicate records** in a table.
> - To establish **relationships** between two or more tables (Relational Integrity).
> - To enable fast **retrieval, indexing, and search** operations (e.g., B-Tree indexing on primary keys).
> - To enforce **business rules** (e.g., two employees cannot have the same Employee ID).

## 1.3 Formal Terminology Used in KTU Examinations

| KTU Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Attribute** | A named column of a relation. |
| **Tuple** | A single row (record) in a relation. |
| **Schema** | The structural definition of a relation (table). |
| **Super Key** | Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples (may have redundant attributes). |
| **Candidate Key** | A **minimal** super key — no proper subset is a super key. |
| **Primary Key** | The candidate key chosen by the database designer as the principal unique identifier. |
| **Alternate Key** | Candidate keys that were **not** selected as the primary key. |
| **Foreign Key** | An attribute in one relation that references the primary key of another relation. |
| **Composite Key** | A key made up of **two or more** attributes. |
| **Unique Key** | A key that allows a `NULL` value (in some dialects), unlike primary keys which typically do not. |
| **Surrogate Key** | A system-generated artificial key (e.g., auto-increment ID). |
| **Partial Key** | A set of attributes that uniquely identifies a **weak entity** in an ER model. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Keys

Keys in DBMS form a strict hierarchy. Every Primary Key is a Candidate Key, every Candidate Key is a Super Key, but the reverse is **not** true. This is one of the most frequently tested KTU 2-mark and 3-mark concepts.

$$\text{Super Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Candidate Key} \;\supseteq\; \text{Primary Key}$$

### 2.1.1 Super Key
A super key is any combination of attributes that can uniquely identify a tuple. It is the **most general** form of a key and is guaranteed to exist (at minimum, the set of **all attributes** of a relation is always a trivial super key).

**Properties:**
- Uniqueness: No two tuples share the same value of the super key.
- It may contain **redundant attributes** (i.e., attributes that are not strictly required for uniqueness).

### 2.1.2 Candidate Key
A candidate key is a **minimal super key** — a super key from which no attribute can be removed without losing the uniqueness property.

**Properties:**
- It is a **minimal** set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples.
- A relation can have **multiple** candidate keys.
- All candidate keys are **functionally determined** by the closure of their attributes (see Section 3).

### 2.1.3 Primary Key
The primary key is the candidate key **selected by the database designer** to be the principal unique identifier of tuples in a relation.

**Properties:**
- **Not NULL** (the `NOT NULL` constraint is implicit).
- **Unique**.
- **Immutable** (ideally, though not strictly enforced by the DBMS).
- Only **one** primary key per table.
- Used as the target of foreign key references.

### 2.1.4 Alternate Key
Any candidate key that is **not** selected as the primary key is called an alternate key. Example: In a `STUDENT` table, both `{RollNo}` and `{AadhaarNo}` could uniquely identify a student. If `RollNo` is chosen as the primary key, then `AadhaarNo` becomes the alternate key.

### 2.1.5 Foreign Key
A foreign key is an attribute (or set of attributes) in a relation $R_2$ whose values must match the values of the primary key in some relation $R_1$.

**Properties:**
- Enforces **Referential Integrity**.
- The referenced attribute(s) must be a primary key (or unique key) in the parent table.
- Foreign keys **can** be `NULL` (representing an unknown or non-existent reference), unless explicitly restricted.

### 2.1.6 Composite Key
A composite key is a candidate (or primary) key that consists of **two or more** attributes. Example: In an `ENROLLMENT(StudentID, CourseID, Date)` table, the combination of all three attributes uniquely identifies a tuple.

### 2.1.7 Unique Key
A unique key is functionally similar to a primary key in enforcing uniqueness, but with two differences:
1. It **may allow a `NULL` value** (in many DBMS implementations, a single NULL is allowed).
2. A table can have **multiple** unique keys.

### 2.1.8 Surrogate Key
A surrogate key is an **artificially generated** key (such as an auto-incremented integer, UUID, or GUID) that has no business meaning. It is added by the system to provide a unique identifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** A common 2-mark question is: *"Is NULL a valid value for a primary key?"* The answer is **NO** — primary keys are subject to the Entity Integrity Constraint, which forbids NULLs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Super Key | Set of attributes $K$ such that $K \rightarrow \{A_1, A_2, ..., A_n\}$ | $\{RollNo\}$, $\{RollNo, Name\}$, $\{Aadhaar\}$ |
| Candidate Key | Minimal Super Key | $\{RollNo\}$, $\{Aadhaar\}$ |
| Primary Key | Selected Candidate Key | $\{RollNo\}$ |
| Alternate Key | Unselected Candidate Key | $\{Aadhaar\}$ |
| Foreign Key | Attribute in $R_2$ referencing PK of $R_1$ | `DeptID` in `EMP` references `DeptID` in `DEPT` |
| Composite Key | Key with $\geq 2$ attributes | $\{StudentID, CourseID\}$ in `ENROLLMENT` |
| Unique Key | Unique but allows 1 NULL | `{Email}` in `USERS` |
| Surrogate Key | System-generated, no semantic meaning | `Auto-Increment ID = 1024` |
| Partial Key | Discriminator of a weak entity | `{Date, SeatNo}` in `THEATRE_SEAT` |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Keys

| Application Domain | Use of Keys |
|---|---|
| **Banking Systems** | Account Number as Primary Key; Customer ID as Foreign Key in Transaction table. |
| **Hospital Management** | UHID (Unique Health ID) as Primary Key in `PATIENT`; DoctorID as FK in `APPOINTMENT`. |
| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | OrderID as PK in `ORDERS`; ProductID as PK in `PRODUCTS`; linking via FK in `ORDER_ITEMS`. |
| **IoT Systems** | DeviceID as Surrogate/Primary Key in sensor tables. |
| **Library Management** | BookID and MemberID as composite keys in `ISSUE` table. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Proper key design is the **first step** in normalization. Without a well-defined primary key, the database cannot enforce referential integrity, leading to **insertion, deletion, and update anomalies** (discussed in later modules on Normalization).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Finding All Candidate Keys — The Attribute Closure Algorithm

The canonical method (Elmasri & Navathe, used in KTU textbooks) to find candidate keys of a relation $R$ given a set of Functional Dependencies (FDs) is the **Attribute Closure** of an attribute set $X$, denoted $X^+$.

**Definition:** $X^+$ is the set of all attributes that can be functionally determined by $X$ using the given set of FDs $F$.

### 3.1.1 The Closure Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

**Input:** Attribute set $X$, set of FDs $F$.
**Output:** $X^+$

**Step 1:** Initialize $X^+ = X$.

**Step 2:** Repeat the following until no new attribute is added to $X^+$:

- For each FD $A \rightarrow B$ in $F$:
  - If $A \subseteq X^+$ (the left side is already in the closure), then set $X^+ = X^+ \cup B$.

**Step 3:** Return $X^+$.

**Rule for Candidate Key:** $X$ is a **super key** if and only if $X^+$ contains **all attributes** of the relation. $X$ is a **candidate key** if and only if no proper subset of $X$ is a super key.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:**
Given a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ and a set of Functional Dependencies:
$$F = \{ AB \rightarrow C,\; C \rightarrow D,\; D \rightarrow E,\; E \rightarrow F,\; F \rightarrow A \}$$

**Find all candidate keys of $R$.**

---

**Step 1: Identify attributes that NEVER appear on the right-hand side (RHS) of any FD.**

These attributes **must** be part of every candidate key (they cannot be functionally determined by anything else).

| FD | Right-side Attribute | Appears on RHS? |
|---|---|---|
| $AB \rightarrow C$ | $C$ | ✓ |
| $C \rightarrow D$ | $D$ | ✓ |
| $D \rightarrow E$ | $E$ | ✓ |
| $E \rightarrow F$ | $F$ | ✓ |
| $F \rightarrow A$ | $A$ | ✓ |

| Attribute | Appears on RHS? | Appears on LHS? | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Yes (in $F \rightarrow A$) | Yes (in $AB \rightarrow C$) | May or may not be in CK |
| $B$ | **NO** | Yes (in $AB \rightarrow C$) | **Must be in every CK** |
| $C$ | Yes | Yes | May or may not be in CK |
| $D$ | Yes | Yes | May or may not be in CK |
| $E$ | Yes | Yes | May or may not be in CK |
| $F$ | Yes | Yes | May or may not be in CK |

Since only $B$ never appears on the RHS, $B$ **must** be present in every candidate key.

---

**Step 2: Test if $\{B\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ (i.e., $B$ alone is a key).**

$$\{B\}^+ = \{B\}$$

Adding FDs whose LHS is a subset of $\{B\}^+$: None of the FDs has only $B$ on the LHS. So the closure does not grow. **$B$ alone is not a key.**

---

**Step 3: Test if $\{B, A\}^+ = R$.**

$$\{B, A\}^+ = \{A, B\}$$

Apply FD $AB \rightarrow C$: Since $A, B \in \{A, B\}^+$, we can add $C$.

$$\{A, B\}^+ = \{A, B, C\}$$

Apply FD $C \rightarrow D$: Add $D$.

$$\{A, B\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$$

Apply FD $D \rightarrow E$: Add $E$.

$$\{A, B\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$

Apply FD $E \rightarrow F$: Add $F$.

$$\{A, B\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R$$

✅ **$\{A, B\}$ is a super key.**

Is it a candidate key? Test proper subsets:
- $\{A\}^+$: Apply $F \rightarrow A$? Cannot. No FD has only $A$ on LHS. Stuck at $\{A\}$.
- $\{B\}^+$: Stuck at $\{B\}$.

So neither $\{A\}$ nor $\{B\}$ is a super key. Therefore, **$\{A, B\}$ is a candidate key.**

---

**Step 4: Are there other candidate keys?**

Test $\{B, C\}^+$:
$$\{B, C\}^+ = \{B, C\} \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow A = R$$ ✅

Test $\{B, D\}^+$:
$$\{B, D\}^+ = \{B, D\} \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow A$$
But we have no FD that produces $C$. So $\{B, D\}^+ = \{A, B, D, E, F\}$. ❌ Not a key.

Test $\{B, F\}^+$:
$$\{B, F\}^+ = \{B, F\} \rightarrow A$$
But we have no FD that produces $C$ (we need $AB$). Stuck. ❌ Not a key.

So the **only candidate keys are $\{A, B\}$ and $\{B, C\}$.**

---

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Set Tested | Closure Result | Super Key? | Candidate Key? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{B\}$ | $\{B\}$ | ❌ | ❌ |
| $\{A, B\}$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $\{B, C\}$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $\{B, D\}$ | $\{A, B, D, E, F\}$ | ❌ | ❌ |
| $\{B, E\}$ | $\{A, B, D, E, F\}$ (no $C$) | ❌ | ❌ |
| $\{B, F\}$ | $\{A, B, F\}$ (no $C$) | ❌ | ❌ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Candidate Keys of $R$ are $\{A, B\}$ and $\{B, C\}$.
> 
> If the database designer chooses $\{A, B\}$ as the **Primary Key**, then $\{B, C\}$ becomes the **Alternate Key**.

## 3.2 Key Selection Rules — Decision Matrix

When choosing a primary key from a set of candidate keys, follow this KTU-recommended decision matrix:

| Selection Criterion | Preferred Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of attributes** | Single-attribute key | Simpler joins, smaller index size |
| **Stability (immutability)** | Attribute that rarely changes | Avoid cascading updates |
| **Non-nullity** | Attribute that is guaranteed `NOT NULL` | Entity Integrity Compliance |
| **Familiarity** | Attribute already known to users (e.g., RollNo) | Better user experience |
| **Simplicity** | Surrogate integer key if no natural key fits | Avoid composite complexity |

## 3.3 Foreign Key Integrity Rules (Referential Integrity)

For any foreign key $FK$ in relation $R_2$ referencing the primary key $PK$ in relation $R_1$:

1. **Insert Rule:** A new tuple can be inserted into $R_2$ only if the $FK$ value either is `NULL` or matches an existing $PK$ value in $R_1$.
2. **Delete Rule (on parent $R_1$):**
   - **RESTRICT:** Reject the deletion if referencing tuples exist in $R_2$.
   - **CASCADE:** Delete all referencing tuples in $R_2$.
   - **SET NULL:** Set $FK$ to `NULL` in referencing tuples.
   - **SET DEFAULT:** Set $FK$ to a default value.
   - **NO ACTION:** Similar to RESTRICT (default in many systems).
3. **Update Rule (on parent $R_1$ $PK$):** Similar choices as Delete Rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Key Hierarchy in DBMS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[All Attributes of Relation] --> A1[Super Key]
    A1 --> A2[Minimal Super Key]
    A2 --> A3[Candidate Key]
    A3 --> A4[Primary Key]
    A3 --> A5[Alternate Key]
    A2 --> A6[Unique Key]
    A4 --> A7[Simple Primary Key]
    A4 --> A8[Composite Primary Key]
    A8 --> A9[Compound Key]
    A0 --> A10[Foreign Key]
    A10 --> A11[Referential Integrity]
    A0 --> A12[Surrogate Key]
    A0 --> A13[Partial Key for Weak Entity]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **top** of the diagram represents the most general form: the set of all attributes.
- The **bottom** represents specialized key types with additional constraints.
- Arrows indicate a **refinement** relationship — a Primary Key is a special kind of Candidate Key, which is a special kind of Super Key.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Dependency Chain for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Attribute A] --> C[Attribute C]
    B[Attribute B] --> C
    C --> D[Attribute D]
    D --> E[Attribute E]
    E --> F[Attribute F]
    F --> A
    B --> CK1[Candidate Key AB]
    B --> CK2[Candidate Key BC]
    A --> CK1
    C --> CK2
```

**Interpretation:**
- The arrows show the **functional dependency direction** — for example, $A \rightarrow C$ means "$A$ functionally determines $C$."
- Note the **cycle**: $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow A$, which is why $A$ alone can determine $C$ indirectly (via $F \rightarrow A$).
- However, $A$ alone **cannot determine $B$**, so $\{A\}$ is not a key.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Primary-Foreign Key Linkage

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DeptTable[DEPARTMENT Table]
        D1[DeptID PRIMARY KEY]
        D2[DeptName]
        D3[Location]
    end
    subgraph EmpTable[EMPLOYEE Table]
        E1[EmpID PRIMARY KEY]
        E2[EmpName]
        E3[Salary]
        E4[DeptID FOREIGN KEY]
    end
    D1 -.REFERENCES.-> E4
    EmpTable -->|JOIN ON DeptID| DeptTable
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The `DEPARTMENT` table has a primary key `DeptID`.
- The `EMPLOYEE` table has a foreign key `DeptID` that **references** the primary key of the `DEPARTMENT` table.
- The dotted arrow represents the **referential link** between the two tables.
- This is the foundational architecture of a **one-to-many relationship** (one Department → many Employees).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: ER Notation for Keys (Weak Entity Example)

```mermaid
graph TB
    STRONG[STRONG ENTITY: BUILDING] -->|Identifying Relationship OWNS| WEAK[WEAK ENTITY: APARTMENT]
    STRONG -->|PK| B_ID[BuildingID]
    WEAK -->|Partial Key Discriminator| A_NUM[ApartmentNumber]
    WEAK -->|Composite Key B_ID + A_NUM| COMPOSITE[Primary Key of APARTMENT]
```

**Interpretation:**
- A `BUILDING` is a **strong entity** (has its own primary key, `BuildingID`).
- An `APARTMENT` is a **weak entity** — it cannot be uniquely identified without reference to the building.
- The `ApartmentNumber` is a **partial key** (discriminator) — apartment "12" exists in many buildings.
- The **composite key** `{BuildingID, ApartmentNumber}` uniquely identifies an apartment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define the following with respect to relational databases:**
**(a)** Super Key, **(b)** Candidate Key, **(c)** Primary Key.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Super Key:** A super key is a set of one or more attributes that, when taken collectively, allow us to identify uniquely a tuple in a relation. Since a super key may contain extraneous attributes, any superset of a candidate key is also a super key. For example, in a `STUDENT(RollNo, Aadhaar, Name, DOB)` table, $\{RollNo\}$, $\{Aadhaar\}$, $\{RollNo, Name\}$, and even $\{RollNo, Aadhaar, Name, DOB\}$ are all super keys.

**(b) Candidate Key:** A candidate key is a **minimal super key** — a super key for which no proper subset is a super key. In the example above, $\{RollNo\}$ and $\{Aadhaar\}$ are candidate keys because removing any attribute from them (there are no other attributes) destroys the unique identification property.

**(c) Primary Key:** The primary key is the **candidate key selected by the database designer** to uniquely identify tuples in the relation. It must satisfy two conditions: (1) it cannot have a `NULL` value, and (2) it must be unique for every tuple. In the example, the designer may choose `RollNo` as the primary key, making `Aadhaar` an alternate key.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Retest]`
**Differentiate between Primary Key, Unique Key, and Foreign Key. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Primary Key | Unique Key | Foreign Key |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniqueness** | Enforced | Enforced | Not enforced (values can repeat) |
| **NULL values** | Not allowed | Allowed (typically one NULL) | Allowed |
| **Number per table** | Exactly one | Multiple allowed | Multiple allowed |
| **Purpose** | Main row identifier | Additional uniqueness constraint | Referential link to another table |
| **Indexing** | Clustered index (default) | Non-clustered index | Non-clustered index |

> **Key Insight for 3 marks:** Primary and Unique keys both enforce uniqueness, but only Primary Key forbids NULLs. Foreign Key is fundamentally different — it enforces referential integrity, not uniqueness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
