> KTU students often lose marks because they write π and σ in the **wrong order**. Remember: **σ first (rows), then π (columns)**. This is the same order a SQL `WHERE` clause executes before `SELECT`.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Natural Join Derivation

**English Query**: *"List names of students enrolled in DBMS along with their grades."*

**Step 1 — Identify required relations**:
We need STUDENT (for name), ENROLL (for grade), and COURSE (for course name).

**Step 2 — Perform natural join on STUDENT and ENROLL** (common attribute: SID):

$$\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \text{ENROLL}$$

$$= \pi_{\text{unique}}\big(\sigma_{\text{STUDENT.SID} = \text{ENROLL.SID}}(\text{STUDENT} \times \text{ENROLL})\big)$$

**Step 3 — Join with COURSE** (common attribute: CID):

$$(\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \text{ENROLL}) \bowtie \text{COURSE}$$

**Step 4 — Apply selection for the DBMS course**:

$$\sigma_{\text{CName} = \text{"DBMS"}}\big((\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \text{ENROLL}) \bowtie \text{COURSE}\big)$$

**Step 5 — Project name and grade**:

$$\boxed{\pi_{\text{SName, Grade}}\big(\sigma_{\text{CName} = \text{"DBMS"}}\big((\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \text{ENROLL}) \bowtie \text{COURSE}\big)\big)}$$

**Resulting tuples**: $\{(\text{Anu, A}), (\text{Binu, A}), (\text{Chitra, A})\}$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Division (The "For All" Operator)

**English Query**: *"Find SIDs of students who have enrolled in ALL courses."*

**Step 1 — Set up the division**:
- Dividend R = $\pi_{\text{SID, CID}}(\text{ENROLL})$
- Divisor S = $\pi_{\text{CID}}(\text{COURSE})$

**Step 2 — Apply the symbolic division formula**:

$$R \div S = \pi_{\text{SID}}(R) - \pi_{\text{SID}}\big((\pi_{\text{SID}}(R) \times S) - R\big)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\pi_{\text{SID}}(R)$**:

$$\pi_{\text{SID}}(R) = \{\text{S1}, \text{S2}, \text{S3}\}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\pi_{\text{SID}}(R) \times S$**:

$$\{\text{S1}, \text{S2}, \text{S3}\} \times \{\text{C1}, \text{C2}, \text{C3}\} = 9 \text{ pairs}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract R from the product to find "missing" enrollments**:

$$(\text{Product}) - R = \{(\text{S2, C2}), (\text{S3, C2}), (\text{S3, C3})\}$$

**Step 6 — Project SID from the missing pairs**:

$$\pi_{\text{SID}}\big(\{(\text{S2, C2}), (\text{S3, C2}), (\text{S3, C3})\}\big) = \{\text{S2}, \text{S3}\}$$

**Step 7 — Final subtraction**:

$$\{\text{S1}, \text{S2}, \text{S3}\} - \{\text{S2}, \text{S3}\} = \{\text{S1}\}$$

**Result**: Only **S1 (Anu)** is enrolled in *all* courses. ✅

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operator Composition Equivalences

This is the **high-yield identity table** KTU examiners love. Memorize these:

| # | LHS Expression | RHS Equivalent | Logical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{c_1 \land c_2}(R)$ | $\sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R))$ | AND decomposition |
| 2 | $\sigma_{c_1 \lor c_2}(R)$ | $\sigma_{c_1}(R) \cup \sigma_{c_2}(R)$ | OR decomposition |
| 3 | $\pi_{A}(\sigma_{c}(R))$ | $\sigma_{c}(\pi_{A}(R))$ *only if* A includes all attrs in c | Push selection under projection |
| 4 | $R \bowtie S$ | $\pi_{U}(\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S))$ | Join-as-product-then-select |
| 5 | $R \bowtie (S \cup T)$ | $(R \bowtie S) \cup (R \bowtie T)$ | Join distributes over union |
| 6 | $R \cap S$ | $R - (R - S)$ | Intersection via difference |
| 7 | $R \bowtie S$ (natural) | $\pi_{A \cup B \cup C}\big(\sigma_{R.B = S.B}(R \times S)\big)$ | Natural join formal form |

> [!NOTE]
> The identities in rows **5** and **7** are favourite 7-mark questions: *"Prove that natural join is associative"* or *"Show that join distributes over union"*.

---

### 3.6 Detailed Proof: $R \bowtie S = \pi(\sigma(R \times S))$

Let $R$ have attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m$ and $S$ have attributes $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ sharing a common attribute $X$ (so the common set is $C$).

**LHS**: $R \bowtie S$ (natural join)
- A tuple $t$ is in the result iff there exist $t_r \in R$ and $t_s \in S$ such that $t_r[X] = t_s[X]$ for every common attribute $X$, and $t$ agrees with $t_r$ on $R$'s attributes and with $t_s$ on $S$'s exclusive attributes.

**RHS**: $\pi_{A \cup B}(\sigma_{R.X = S.X}(R \times S))$
- $R \times S$ contains every combination of $t_r \in R$ and $t_s \in S$.
- $\sigma_{R.X = S.X}$ filters to those where the common attributes match.
- $\pi_{A \cup B}$ projects to the union of attribute sets, removing the duplicated common attribute.

$$\therefore R \bowtie S \equiv \pi_{A \cup B}\big(\sigma_{\bigwedge_{X \in C}(R.X = S.X)}(R \times S)\big) \quad \blacksquare$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Taxonomy of Relational Algebra Operators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Relational Algebra Operators] --> B[Traditional Set Operations]
    A --> C[Special Relational Operations]
    A --> D[Extended Operations]

    B --> B1[Union U]
    B --> B2[Intersection N]
    B --> B3[Set Difference -]
    B --> B4[Cartesian Product X]

    C --> C1[Selection sigma]
    C --> C2[Projection pi]
    C --> C3[Rename rho]
    C --> C4[Join bowtie]
    C --> C5[Division div]

    C4 --> C4a[Theta Join]
    C4 --> C4b[Equi Join]
    C4 --> C4c[Natural Join]
    C4 --> C4d[Outer Join]
    C4 --> C4e[Semi Join]
    C4 --> C4f[Self Join]

    D --> D1[Generalized Projection]
    D --> D2[Aggregate Functions]
    D --> D3[Outer Joins]
    D --> D4[Group By]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Query Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[User Query] --> P[Parser and Syntax Tree]
    P --> RA[Convert to Relational Algebra Tree]
    RA --> OPT[Query Optimizer Uses Equivalence Rules]
    OPT --> EXEC[Execution Plan]
    EXEC --> RES[Result Relation]

    RA --> op1[Selection sigma]
    RA --> op2[Projection pi]
    RA --> op3[Join bowtie]
    RA --> op4[Union U]
    RA --> op5[Division div]

    op1 --> RES
    op2 --> RES
    op3 --> RES
    op4 --> RES
    op5 --> RES

    OPT -.applies.- id1[Push selection down]
    OPT -.applies.- id2[Join commutativity]
    OPT -.applies.- id3[Projection before join]
    OPT -.applies.- id4[Selection before join]
```

### 4.3 Operator Closure Property (Theoretical Core)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Relation R] --> op1[Operator]
    S1[Relation S] --> op1
    op1 --> R2[New Relation R Prime]
    R2 --> op2[Another Operator]
    T1[Relation T] --> op2
    op2 --> R3[Another Relation R Double Prime]

    note1[Closure Property: Output is always a relation that can be re-used as input] -.describes.- op1
    note1 -.describes.- op2
```

### 4.4 Data Flow: Natural Join in Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STUDENT table] --> X[Cartesian Product]
    B[ENROLL table] --> X
    X --> Y[Selection on SID equals SID]
    Y --> Z[Projection remove duplicate SID]
    Z --> OUT[Joined Result]
```

### 4.5 Decision Flow: Choosing the Correct Operator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[What does the query need?] --> q1{Need to filter rows?}
    q1 -->|Yes| sel[Use Selection sigma]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Need to choose columns?}
    q2 -->|Yes| proj[Use Projection pi]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Need to combine tables?}
    q3 -->|Yes on condition| join[Use Join bowtie]
    q3 -->|Yes all combinations| prod[Use Cartesian Product]
    q3 -->|No| q4{For all query?}
    q4 -->|Yes| div[Use Division]
    q4 -->|No| set[Use Union Intersection or Difference]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define *Relational Algebra*. List any four fundamental operations with their symbols.

**Model Answer**:
Relational Algebra is a procedural query language used to query relational databases. It takes relations as input and produces new relations as output (closure property). Four fundamental operations are:
- Selection: $\sigma$ (filters rows)
- Projection: $\pi$ (selects columns)
- Cartesian Product: $\times$ (combines all tuples)
- Union: $\cup$ (combines union-compatible relations)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is *union compatibility*? Why is it required?

**Model Answer**:
Two relations R and S are union-compatible if (i) they have the same number of attributes (same degree) and (ii) corresponding attributes have identical domains. It is required for set operations like $\cup, \cap, -$ because these operations pair tuples positionally — the *i*-th column of R must be combinable with the *i*-th column of S. Without union compatibility, the result of such operations would be meaningless or type-incoherent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Consider the following two relations:

| **EMPLOYEE** | | |
|---|---|---|
| EID | EName | Dept |
| E1 | Anu | HR |
| E2 | Binu | Finance |
| E3 | Chitra | HR |

| **PROJECT** | | |
|---|---|---|
| PID | PName | Dept |
| P1 | Payroll | HR |
| P2 | Audit | Finance |

Write relational algebra expressions for the following queries. Each subpart carries **7 marks**.

**(a-i)** Find the names of employees working in the **HR** department. **(3 marks)**

**Solution**:
$$\pi_{\text{EName}}\big(\sigma_{\text{Dept} = \text{"HR"}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\big)$$

**Step-by-step evaluation**:
- Apply $\sigma$ on EMPLOYEE: keep rows where Dept = "HR" → $\{$(E1, Anu, HR), (E3, Chitra, HR)$\mid$
- Apply $\pi_{\text{EName}}$: drop other columns → $\{$(Anu), (Chitra)$\mid$

**(a-ii)** Find the names of employees and the names of projects that share the **same department**. **(4 marks)**

**Solution**:

$$\pi_{\text{EName, PName}}\big(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie_{\text{EMPLOYEE.Dept} = \text{PROJECT.Dept}} \text{PROJECT}\big)$$

**Evaluation**:
- Cartesian Product: $3 \times 2 = 6$ tuples
- Selection on Dept equality: 3 tuples survive — (E1, Anu, HR) with (P1, Payroll, HR); (E3, Chitra, HR) with (P1, Payroll, HR)
- Projection of (EName, PName): $\{$(Anu, Payroll), (Chitra, Payroll)$\mid$

**(b)** Explain **Natural Join** and **Outer Join** with suitable examples. State the situations where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Natural Join (⋈)**: A natural join performs an equi-join on all attributes that have the **same name** in both relations, and then **removes the duplicate columns** from the result.

$$R \bowtie S = \pi_{\text{all distinct attrs}}\big(\sigma_{\bigwedge R.A_i = S.A_i}(R \times S)\big)$$

**Example**: $R(A, B)$, $S(B, C)$. Then $R \bowtie S$ matches rows on attribute B and yields a relation with schema (A, B, C).

**Outer Join**: An outer join **preserves tuples that would be lost in a regular join** by padding missing values with **NULL**.

- **Left Outer Join** ($R \supset\!\ltimes S$): Keep all tuples of R; pad NULLs for S's missing attributes.
- **Right Outer Join** ($R \ltimes\!\subset S$): Keep all tuples of S.
- **Full Outer Join** ($R \supset\!\ltimes\!\subset S$): Keep tuples of both R and S.

**When to use**:
- Use **Natural Join** when both relations have well-named common attributes and you want a clean schema (default for most queries).
- Use **Outer Join** when you need to retain unmatched rows (e.g., list all employees, even those not assigned to a project — typical of reporting queries).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]

**(a)** Given the relations:

- **BOOK** (BookID, Title, Author, Price)
- **BORROW** (MemberID, BookID, Date)

Write relational algebra expressions for:
**(a-i)** List the titles of all books borrowed by Member 'M101'. **(3 marks)**

**Solution**:

$$\pi_{\text{Title}}\big(\sigma_{\text{MemberID} = \text{"M101"}}(\text{BOOK} \bowtie \text{BORROW})\big)$$

**Evaluation**:
- Natural join on BookID: combines matching book + borrow records.
- Selection on MemberID: keeps only M101's borrowings.
- Projection on Title: returns book titles.

**(a-ii)** Find the names of members who have borrowed **every** book in the library. **(4 marks)**

**Solution**: This is a classic *division* problem.

$$\text{MemberID} = \pi_{\text{MemberID, BookID}}(\text{BORROW}) \div \pi_{\text{BookID}}(\text{BOOK})$$

**Explanation**:
- $\pi_{\text{MemberID, BookID}}(\text{BORROW})$ gives the set of (member, book) pairs.
- $\pi_{\text{BookID}}(\text{BOOK})$ gives the universe of all books.
- Division returns only those MemberIDs whose pairs cover **every** BookID.

**(b)** Discuss the **closure property** of Relational Algebra. How does it help in building complex queries? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Closure Property**: The result of every relational algebra operation is itself a relation. This means:
- The output of one operation can be fed **directly** as input to another operation.
- Queries can be **nested** and **composed** without any type conversion or special handling.

**Why it is powerful**:

1. **Composability**: Complex queries are built by chaining simple operations — e.g., $\pi(\sigma(\bowtie(R, S)))$ is a 3-step nested expression.

2. **Mathematical Rigor**: Because the output is a relation, the system can be studied formally — equivalence rules, optimization transformations, and proofs of correctness are all valid.

3. **Query Optimization**: Optimizers rewrite queries using equivalence rules (e.g., $\sigma(\pi(R)) = \pi(\sigma(R))$ when conditions are satisfied) to find the cheapest execution plan — this is only possible because both LHS and RHS are relations.

4. **Practical Impact**: SQL itself inherits this property — subqueries in `FROM` clauses are relations, and CTE (Common Table Expressions) are essentially nested RA sub-expressions.

**Conclusion**: Closure is the **theoretical heart** of RA — without it, the language would be a collection of disjoint utilities rather than a unified query system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**:
>
> 1. **Order of operations**: Students write $\pi(\sigma(\cdot))$ correctly but apply **projection first in their head**, dropping attributes needed by the selection predicate. Always do σ before π unless you're certain projection preserves needed columns.
>
> 2. **Union compatibility check**: Marks are deducted for blindly using ∪, ∩, − on tables with different schemas. Always **explicitly state the compatibility check** in your answer.
>
> 3. **Division misuse**: Students often write a *join* or *intersection* when the question demands a *division*. Look for the words **"for all"**, **"every"**, **"all of"** in the English query — these are division triggers.
>
> 4. **Renaming ambiguity in self-joins**: Forgetting to use $\rho$ in self-joins. If you join EMPLOYEE with itself to find pairs of employees in the same department, **always rename** one copy: $\rho_{\text{E2}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$.
>
> 5. **Confusing natural join with cartesian product**: Natural join **filters and de-duplicates**. Many students present $R \times S$ as the final answer when the question expects $R \bowtie S$.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to remove duplicates in projection**: KTU strictly tests whether you know that π eliminates duplicates while σ does not.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relational Algebra** is a *procedural* query language with **closure** — output is always a relation.
- **Two main families** of operators: *Set operations* (∪, ∩, −, ×) and *Special relational* (σ, π, ρ, ⋈, ÷).
- **Selection (σ)** filters **rows** horizontally; **Projection (π)** filters **columns** vertically and removes duplicates.
- **Cartesian Product (×)** combines every tuple of R with every tuple of S — high cost, almost always used with a following selection.
- **Natural Join (⋈)** = *Cartesian Product + Selection on common attributes + Projection to remove duplicate columns*.
- **Outer Joins** preserve unmatched tuples using **NULL** padding.
- **Division (÷)** solves *"for all"* queries — dividend has extra attributes that get removed in the result.
- **Rename (ρ)** is the **disambiguator** — essential for self-joins and multi-table expressions.
- **Key equivalences** to memorize: $\sigma_{c_1 \land c_2} = \sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2})$; $R \bowtie (S \cup T) = (R \bowtie S) \cup (R \bowtie T)$; $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$.
- **Union compatibility** is a **pre-condition** for ∪, ∩, − — not for × or ⋈.
- **Closure property** enables query composition, optimization, and formal verification.
- RA is the **theoretical backbone** of SQL — every SQL query can be translated into an equivalent RA expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PBERT504 – Database Management Systems), **Synthesizing an ER Diagram to a Relational Schema** is the formal, algorithmic process of translating a conceptual data model — represented as an Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram — into a logically equivalent **Relational Schema** consisting of a set of well-defined relations (tables), their attributes, domains, primary keys, foreign keys, and integrity constraints (entity integrity, referential integrity, and domain constraints).

This process is formally catalogued as a set of **mapping (translation) rules** standardized by **Peter Pin-Shan Chen (1976)** — the inventor of the ER model — and further refined by researchers like **Teorey, Yang, and Fry** in modern texts such as *Elmasri & Navathe*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to map every ER construct (entity, weak entity, 1:1, 1:N, M:N, multivalued, derived, composite, n-ary, ISA hierarchy, aggregation) into a relational schema using a *systematic, rule-based* procedure. Marks are awarded for **showing the relation name, attributes, and the underlined primary key** explicitly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect** who has drawn a beautiful **blueprint** of a house (rooms, doors, windows, stairs, plumbing lines). The blueprint is conceptual — it shows *what* should exist and *how* it connects. But before the masons and carpenters can actually *build* the house, the blueprint must be converted into a **construction plan** with exact measurements, materials, and joinery.

| ER Diagram (Conceptual) | Relational Schema (Logical) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Blueprint of a house | Construction drawings with measurements |
| Entities (rectangles) | Relations / Tables |
| Attributes (ovals) | Columns / Fields |
| Primary Key (underlined oval) | Underlined PK in relation schema |
| Relationships (diamonds) | Foreign Keys in child table |
| Cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:N) | Placement rule of FK |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition in one line:** ER-to-Relational mapping is the **translator** that converts a *visual, semantic* model into a *rigorous, table-based* schema that an actual **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** like MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle can directly implement.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Relation:** A two-dimensional table with a unique name, consisting of rows (tuples) and columns (attributes).
- **Degree of a relation:** Number of attributes (columns). Standard: typically **2 ≤ degree ≤ 20+**.
- **Cardinality of a relation:** Number of tuples (rows). Variable; grows with data.
- **Primary Key (PK):** Uniquely identifies a tuple. Not null. Underscored in notation.
- **Foreign Key (FK):** Attribute(s) in one relation referencing the PK of another. Enforces **Referential Integrity**.
- **Domain $D_i$:** The set of permissible atomic values for attribute $A_i$ (e.g., $D_{\text{salary}} = \mathbb{R}^+ \cap [0, 10^7]$).
</br>**Formal relational notation:**

$$ R(A_1 : D_1,\ A_2 : D_2,\ \ldots,\ A_n : D_n) $$

</br>where $R$ is the relation name, $A_i$ are attributes, and $D_i$ are domains.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Standard Mapping Rules (Step-by-Step Logic)

Each rule is a *deterministic* transformation. KTU examiners test the *order* and *placement* of foreign keys carefully.

### Rule 1 — Mapping of Strong (Regular) Entities
- **Why?** A strong entity has its own independent existence and a primary key.
- **How?** Create a new relation $R$ that includes **all simple atomic attributes** of the entity.
- **Primary Key:** Choose one of the **key attributes** (usually the smallest natural key or a surrogate key).

> [!NOTE]
> **Composite attributes** are split into their constituent simple attributes. Only simple components enter the relation. Example: `Name(FirstName, MiddleName, LastName)` becomes three separate columns.

### Rule 2 — Mapping of Weak Entities
- **Why?** A weak entity has **no complete key** of its own; it depends on an *owner/identifying* entity.
- **How?** Create a relation $W$ for the weak entity, including:
  - All of its own (partial key + simple) attributes.
  - The **primary key of the owner entity** as a **foreign key**.
- **Primary Key of $W$:** Combination of **partial key + owner's PK**.

### Rule 3 — Mapping of Binary 1:1 Relationships
- **Why?** The relationship contributes no separate table, only a foreign-key link.
- **How?** Two strategies (KTU expects both):
  - **Strategy A (Foreign Key in $S_1$):** Place FK of $S_2$ inside $S_1$ (or vice versa). Choose the side with **total participation**.
  - **Strategy B (Merged relation):** If both sides have total participation, merge them into one relation.
- **Constraint:** Add a `UNIQUE` constraint on the FK (since 1:1).

### Rule 4 — Mapping of Binary 1:N Relationships
- **Why?** The "many" side holds the FK to the "one" side.
- **How?** Place the **primary key of the "1" side** as a **foreign key in the "N" side**.
- **No new relation** is created. This is the most common rule tested.

### Rule 5 — Mapping of Binary M:N Relationships
- **Why?** A separate relation is mandatory; FK on one side cannot represent both sides.
- **How?** Create a new relation $R_{MN}$ with:
  - **Primary keys of both participating entities** as composite PK.
  - Any **descriptive attributes of the relationship** itself.
- **Example:** `STUDENT(SSN)` and `COURSE(CID)` connected by M:N `ENROLLS` →
  `ENROLLS(SSN, CID, Grade)` with PK = {SSN, CID}.

### Rule 6 — Mapping of Multivalued Attributes
- **Why?** A multivalued attribute violates **1NF (First Normal Form)** if stored inside the parent table.
- **How?** Create a **new relation** $R_{MV}$ containing:
  - The **PK of the parent entity**.
  - The multivalued attribute itself.
- **Primary Key of $R_{MV}$:** Composite of (parent PK + multivalued attribute).

### Rule 7 — Mapping of N-ary Relationships
- **Why?** N-ary (ternary or higher) relationships cannot be decomposed without loss of meaning.
- **How?** Create a new relation $R_N$ with:
  - **Primary keys of all N participating entities** as a composite PK.
  - Any descriptive attributes of the relationship.

### Rule 8 — Mapping of Generalization / Specialization (ISA Hierarchy)
- **Why?** We must decide whether the superclass/subclass structure is preserved.
- **How?** **Four strategies:**
  1. **Multiple relations (superclass + each subclass):** Best for disjoint, total specialization. Subclass PK = Superclass PK (FK).
  2. **Multiple relations (only subclasses):** If specialization is total. Each subclass PK = its own key.
  3. **Single relation (with type discriminator):** All attributes of superclass + all subclasses in one table; add a `type` attribute.
  4. **Single relation (only superclass):** Not used if subclasses have specific attributes.

### Rule 9 — Mapping of Aggregation
- **Why?** Aggregation treats a relationship *as if* it were a higher-level entity participating in another relationship.
- **How?** Include the **primary key of the aggregated relationship** as a foreign key in the new (outer) relationship relation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic (KTU):** Whenever you see a diamond, the **default action is "no new table" — except for M:N, multivalued, n-ary, and aggregations**, where a *new table* is mandatory.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | ER Construct | New Relation? | Where FK / PK Goes | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Strong Entity | Yes (1 per entity) | Entity's own key = PK | Composite attrs split |
| 2 | Weak Entity | Yes (1 per weak entity) | Own partial key + Owner PK = composite PK | Owner PK is FK too |
| 3 | 1:1 Relationship | No | FK in either side (prefer total participation side) | Mark FK as UNIQUE |
| 4 | 1:N Relationship | No | FK of "1"-side placed in "N"-side | Most common rule |
| 5 | M:N Relationship | **Yes** | New table: PK1 + PK2 = composite PK | Add relationship attributes |
| 6 | Multivalued Attribute | **Yes** | New table: Parent PK + MV attr = composite PK | Enforces 1NF |
| 7 | N-ary (n ≥ 3) Relationship | **Yes** | New table: PKs of all n entities = composite PK | Preserve all participants |
| 8 | ISA: Generalization (Disjoint, Total) | Multiple | Subclass PK = Superclass PK (FK) | No redundancy of common attrs |
| 9 | ISA: Generalization (Overlapping) | Single + type | Add `type` discriminator | Saves joins |
| 10 | Aggregation | No | FK of inner relationship in outer relationship | Treats relationship as entity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU:** The notation **"PK (underlined) = A, B, C"** must be shown explicitly. Examiners do *not* award full marks for vague statements like "appropriate keys are assigned."

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

Below, a **single, large, comprehensive ER schema** is mapped *systematically* using **all nine rules** so you can see the complete pattern.

## 3.1 The Reference ER Diagram (Concept Specification)

Consider a **University** with the following ER constructs:

- **Entities:**
  - `DEPARTMENT` (strong) — attributes: `DCode` (key), `DName`, `HOD_Name`, `Estd_Year` (derived from current year).
  - `STUDENT` (strong) — attributes: `USN` (key), `SName`, `DOB`, `Email` (multivalued, e.g., personal + college).
  - `COURSE` (strong) — attributes: `CID` (key), `CName`, `Credits`.
  - `SECTION` (weak) — owner is `COURSE`; partial key: `SecNo`; attribute: `RoomNo`.
  - `FACULTY` (strong) — attributes: `FID` (key), `FName`, `Specialization`.
  - `PROJECT` (strong) — attributes: `PID` (key), `PTitle`, `StartDate`.
  - `INSTRUCTOR` (specialized subclass of `FACULTY`) — attributes: `Rank`, `OfficeRoom`.
  - `GRAD_STUDENT` (specialized subclass of `STUDENT`) — attributes: `ThesisTopic`, `GuideFID` (FK).
- **Relationships:**
  - `OFFERS` — DEPARTMENT (1) : COURSE (N). Attribute: `Semester`.
  - `BELONGS_TO` — STUDENT (N) : DEPARTMENT (1).
  - `ENROLLS` — STUDENT (M) : COURSE (N). Attribute: `Grade`, `EnrollDate`.
  - `TEACHES` — FACULTY (1) : SECTION (N) : COURSE (N) — **ternary relationship**. Attribute: `Year`.
  - `WORKS_ON` — FACULTY (M) : PROJECT (N). Attribute: `Hours`.
  - `SUPERVISES` — FACULTY (1) : PROJECT (N) [each project has one supervisor].
  - `GUIDES` — FACULTY (1) : GRAD_STUDENT (N).
  - `MANAGES` — aggregation — a `PROJECT` and a `FACULTY` participate in `WORKS_ON`; this aggregated entity is supervised by another `FACULTY` via `OVERSIGHT`.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Mapping (Exhaustive)

### Step 1 — Map Strong Entities (Rule 1)

**DEPARTMENT** (drop derived attribute `Estd_Year` — derived attrs are **not** stored, they are computed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{DCode}},\ \text{DName},\ \text{HOD\_Name})
\end{aligned}
$$

**STUDENT** (composite name `SName(First, Middle, Last)` flattened; multivalued `Email` excluded for now):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \text{FirstName},\ \text{MiddleName},\ \text{LastName},\ \text{DOB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**COURSE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CID}},\ \text{CName},\ \text{Credits})
\end{aligned}
$$

**FACULTY:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FACULTY}(\underline{\text{FID}},\ \text{FName},\ \text{Specialization})
\end{aligned}
$$

**PROJECT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \text{PTitle},\ \text{StartDate})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Map Weak Entity (Rule 2) — `SECTION`

Weak entity `SECTION` with owner `COURSE` and partial key `SecNo`. Create a new relation whose PK = `CID` (FK) + `SecNo`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SECTION}(\underline{\text{CID}},\ \underline{\text{SecNo}},\ \text{RoomNo}) \\
\text{with } \text{CID} \text{ as FK referencing } \text{COURSE(CID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Map Multivalued Attribute (Rule 6) — `Email` of STUDENT

Create a separate table where the PK is composite = `USN` + `Email`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STUDENT\_EMAIL}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \underline{\text{Email}}) \\
\text{with } \text{USN} \text{ as FK referencing } \text{STUDENT(USN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a separate table?** Because a student may have 1 to N emails. If we put `Email` inside STUDENT, we would violate **1NF**.

### Step 4 — Map 1:N Relationships (Rule 4) — `OFFERS`, `BELONGS_TO`, `SUPERVISES`, `GUIDES`

- `OFFERS` — DEPARTMENT (1) : COURSE (N). Place `DCode` as FK in COURSE.
- `BELONGS_TO` — STUDENT (N) : DEPARTMENT (1). Place `DCode` as FK in STUDENT.
- `SUPERVISES` — FACULTY (1) : PROJECT (N). Place `SupervisorFID` as FK in PROJECT.
- `GUIDES` — FACULTY (1) : GRAD_STUDENT (N). Place `GuideFID` as FK in GRAD_STUDENT.

Updated relations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CID}},\ \text{CName},\ \text{Credits},\ \text{DCode}) \\
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \text{FirstName},\ \text{MiddleName},\ \text{LastName},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{DCode}) \\
\text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \text{PTitle},\ \text{StartDate},\ \text{SupervisorFID}) \\
\text{GRAD\_STUDENT}(\ldots,\ \text{GuideFID})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Map 1:1 Relationship (Rule 3) — `MANAGES` (Department ↔ Faculty HoD)

If `MANAGES` (Department → HeadOfFaculty) is 1:1, we place FK in `DEPARTMENT`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{DCode}},\ \text{DName},\ \text{HOD\_Name},\ \text{HeadFID}) \\
\text{with } \text{HeadFID} \text{ as UNIQUE FK referencing } \text{FACULTY(FID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Map M:N Relationships (Rule 5) — `ENROLLS`, `WORKS_ON`

**ENROLLS** — STUDENT ↔ COURSE (M:N) with attribute `Grade`, `EnrollDate`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENROLLS}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \underline{\text{CID}},\ \text{Grade},\ \text{EnrollDate})
\end{aligned}
$$

**WORKS_ON** — FACULTY ↔ PROJECT (M:N) with attribute `Hours`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WORKS\_ON}(\underline{\text{FID}},\ \underline{\text{PID}},\ \text{Hours})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Map Ternary (N-ary) Relationship (Rule 7) — `TEACHES`

Ternary relationship: FACULTY, SECTION, COURSE. Create one new table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TEACHES}(\underline{\text{FID}},\ \underline{\text{CID}},\ \underline{\text{SecNo}},\ \text{Year})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `SecNo` alone is **not** a key for SECTION. The combination `(CID, SecNo)` uniquely identifies a section. Therefore, both must appear in TEACHES.

### Step 8 — Map ISA / Generalization (Rule 8)

Assume `INSTRUCTOR` ⊂ `FACULTY` and `GRAD_STUDENT` ⊂ `STUDENT` are **disjoint and partial**.

**Strategy: Multiple relations (superclass + each subclass).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FACULTY}(\underline{\text{FID}},\ \text{FName},\ \text{Specialization}) \\
\text{INSTRUCTOR}(\underline{\text{FID}},\ \text{Rank},\ \text{OfficeRoom}) \\
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \text{FirstName},\ \text{MiddleName},\ \text{LastName},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{DCode}) \\
\text{GRAD\_STUDENT}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \text{ThesisTopic},\ \text{GuideFID})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, `FID` in INSTRUCTOR is both PK and FK → enforces 1:1 with FACULTY.

### Step 9 — Map Aggregation (Rule 9) — `OVERSIGHT`

Suppose `FACULTY` and `PROJECT` participate in `WORKS_ON` (already mapped as a table), and a *senior faculty* `AuditorFID` oversees this works_on instance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OVERSIGHT}(\underline{\text{AuditorFID}},\ \underline{\text{WorkerFID}},\ \underline{\text{PID}})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, `(WorkerFID, PID)` is an FK referencing the PK of `WORKS_ON`.

## 3.3 Final Consolidated Relational Schema

After applying all nine rules, the complete KTU-style schema (relation names in CAPS, PKs underlined, FKs in italic where helpful):

| Relation Name | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DEPARTMENT | DCode, DName, HOD_Name, HeadFID | **DCode** | HeadFID → FACULTY |
| FACULTY | FID, FName, Specialization | **FID** | — |
| INSTRUCTOR | FID, Rank, OfficeRoom | **FID** | FID → FACULTY |
| STUDENT | USN, FirstName, MiddleName, LastName, DOB, DCode | **USN** | DCode → DEPARTMENT |
| GRAD_STUDENT | USN, ThesisTopic, GuideFID | **USN** | USN → STUDENT, GuideFID → FACULTY |
| STUDENT_EMAIL | USN, Email | **(USN, Email)** | USN → STUDENT |
| COURSE | CID, CName, Credits, DCode | **CID** | DCode → DEPARTMENT |
| SECTION | CID, SecNo, RoomNo | **(CID, SecNo)** | CID → COURSE |
| ENROLLS | USN, CID, Grade, EnrollDate | **(USN, CID)** | USN → STUDENT, CID → COURSE |
| PROJECT | PID, PTitle, StartDate, SupervisorFID | **PID** | SupervisorFID → FACULTY |
| WORKS_ON | FID, PID, Hours | **(FID, PID)** | FID → FACULTY, PID → PROJECT |
| TEACHES | FID, CID, SecNo, Year | **(FID, CID, SecNo)** | FK → FACULTY, (CID,SecNo) → SECTION |
| OVERSIGHT | AuditorFID, WorkerFID, PID | **(AuditorFID, WorkerFID, PID)** | (WorkerFID, PID) → WORKS_ON |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Mapping Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: ER Diagram] --> B[Identify All Entities]
    B --> C{Strong or Weak?}
    C -->|Strong| D[Rule 1: Create Relation with PK]
    C -->|Weak| E[Rule 2: Create Relation with composite PK including Owner PK]
    D --> F[Inspect Attributes]
    F --> G{Attribute Type?}
    G -->|Composite| H[Flatten into simple attributes]
    G -->|Multivalued| I[Rule 6: Create separate relation]
    G -->|Derived| J[Omit from schema]
    H --> K[Inspect Relationships]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Relationship Type?}
    L -->|1:1| M[Rule 3: Place FK in total-participation side]
    L -->|1:N| N[Rule 4: Place FK on the N side]
    L -->|M:N| O[Rule 5: New relation with composite PK]
    L -->|N-ary n >= 3| P[Rule 7: New relation with n-entity composite PK]
    L -->|ISA| Q[Rule 8: Choose 1 of 4 strategies]
    L -->|Aggregation| R[Rule 9: Treat inner relationship as entity]
    M --> S[Apply Entity and Referential Integrity]
    N --> S
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Final Relational Schema in 1NF 2NF 3NF]
```

## 4.2 ER-to-Relational Mapping Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Conceptual Layer]
        E1[DEPARTMENT Entity]
        E2[STUDENT Entity]
        E3[COURSE Entity]
        E4[FACULTY Entity]
        E5[SECTION Weak Entity]
        R1[OFFERS 1:N Relationship]
        R2[ENROLLS M:N Relationship]
        R3[TEACHES Ternary Relationship]
    end

    subgraph LogicLayer[Mapping Engine]
        MP1[Rule 4: FK on N-side]
        MP2[Rule 5: New relation with composite PK]
        MP3[Rule 7: New relation with all participating PKs]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Logical Layer Relational Schema]
        T1[DEPARTMENT DCode DName HeadFID]
        T2[STUDENT USN Name DOB DCode]
        T3[COURSE CID CName Credits DCode]
        T4[FACULTY FID FName Specialization]
        T5[SECTION CID SecNo RoomNo]
        T6[ENROLLS USN CID Grade EnrollDate]
        T7[TEACHES FID CID SecNo Year]
    end

    E1 --> R1 --> MP1 --> T3
    E2 --> R2 --> MP2 --> T6
    E3 --> R2
    E1 --> MP1 --> T1
    E2 --> MP1 --> T2
    E4 --> MP1 --> T4
    E5 --> MP3
    E4 --> R3 --> MP3 --> T7
    E3 --> R3
    E5 --> T5
```

## 4.3 Cardinality Decision Block (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Relationship Detected]) --> Card{What is the Cardinality?}
    Card -->|1 to 1| OneOne[Place FK on the side with total participation. Mark FK as UNIQUE. No new table.]
    Card -->|1 to N| OneN[Place FK of the 1-side in the N-side relation. No new table.]
    Card -->|M to N| MN[Create new relation. PK of both entities becomes composite PK. Add relationship attributes.]
    Card -->|N-ary where n >= 3| NAry[Create new relation. Include PKs of all n participating entities as composite PK.]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid does not natively render Chen's ER notation (rectangles, ovals, diamonds), so the diagrams above are **functional architecture equivalents** that capture the *decision logic* the KTU examiner expects you to follow.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by synthesizing an ER diagram into a relational schema? Why is it a necessary step in database design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Synthesizing (or mapping) an ER diagram to a relational schema is the **systematic translation of a conceptual data model into a logical, table-based representation** using a fixed set of rules. It is necessary because:
1. RDBMSs (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL) **do not directly understand ER diagrams** — they store only relations.
2. It enforces **1NF** and removes ambiguity by explicitly placing primary and foreign keys.
3. It bridges the gap between the *conceptual* design phase and the *logical/physical* implementation phase.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation tip (1 mark each):** Definition = 1 mark, Reason 1 = 1 mark, Reason 2 = 1 mark. Use the term **"conceptual-to-logical mapping"** for full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the mapping rule for a multivalued attribute with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Rule:** For every multivalued attribute $M$ of an entity $E$ with primary key $K_E$, create a **new relation** $R_M(K_E, M)$. The primary key of $R_M$ is the **composite of $K_E$ and $M$** (since either alone is insufficient to identify a unique row).

**Example:** `STUDENT(USN, Name)` has multivalued attribute `Phone_No`. Mapping yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \text{Name}) \\
\text{STUDENT\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{USN}},\ \underline{\text{Phone\_No}})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is required to preserve **1NF** (atomicity of values).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**Consider the following ER description for a Hospital Management System:**

- **Entities:** `DOCTOR` (strong, key: `DID`), `PATIENT` (strong, key: `PID`), `WARD` (strong, key: `Wno`), `DEPENDENT` (weak, owner = `PATIENT`, partial key: `DepName`).
- **Attributes:** `DOCTOR` has `DName`, `Specialization`, `Phone` (multivalued). `PATIENT` has `PName`, `DOB`, `Address(composite: HouseNo, Street, City, PIN)`.
- **Relationships:**
  - `ADMITS` — DOCTOR (1) : PATIENT (N) with attribute `AdmitDate`.
  - `ALLOTTED_TO` — PATIENT (M) : WARD (N) with attribute `BedNo`.
  - `TREATS` — DOCTOR (M) : DEPENDENT (N) with attribute `Relationship`.

#### (a) Map all strong and weak entities to relations. Show the primary keys clearly. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Rule 1 — Strong Entities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOCTOR}(\underline{\text{DID}},\ \text{DName},\ \text{Specialization}) \\
\text{PATIENT}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \text{PName},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{HouseNo},\ \text{Street},\ \text{City},\ \text{PIN}) \\
\text{WARD}(\underline{\text{Wno}},\ \ldots)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Composite `Address` flattened into 4 atomic attributes; multivalued `Phone` is **not** included here — handled in next sub-part.)

**Rule 2 — Weak Entity `DEPENDENT`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DEPENDENT}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \underline{\text{DepName}},\ \ldots) \\
\text{with } \text{PID} \text{ as FK referencing } \text{PATIENT(PID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The composite primary key `(PID, DepName)` is mandatory because `DepName` is only a **partial key** (unique only within one patient).

**Rule 6 — Multivalued attribute `Phone` of `DOCTOR`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOCTOR\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{DID}},\ \underline{\text{Phone}}) \\
\text{with } \text{DID} \text{ as FK referencing } \text{DOCTOR(DID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Map the three relationships into the schema, showing all foreign keys and integrity constraints. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**`ADMITS` (1:N)** — Place `DID` as FK in `PATIENT`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PATIENT}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \text{PName},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{HouseNo},\ \text{Street},\ \text{City},\ \text{PIN},\ \text{DID})
\end{aligned}
$$

`DID` is a **foreign key** referencing `DOCTOR(DID)`. The attribute `AdmitDate` is added to `PATIENT`.

**`ALLOTTED_TO` (M:N)** — Create a new relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALLOTTED\_TO}(\underline{\text{PID}},\ \underline{\text{Wno}},\ \text{BedNo})
\end{aligned}
$$

`PID` references `PATIENT`, `Wno` references `WARD`.

**`TREATS` (M:N) between DOCTOR and DEPENDENT** — Create a new relation. Since DEPENDENT has a composite PK `(PID, DepName)`, the FK from the weak side must include both:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TREATS}(\underline{\text{DID}},\ \underline{\text{PID}},\ \underline{\text{DepName}},\ \text{Relationship})
\end{aligned}
$$

`DID` references `DOCTOR(DID)`; `(PID, DepName)` references `DEPENDENT(PID, DepName)`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Consider the following ER description for an Airline Reservation System:**

- **Entities:** `AIRLINE` (strong, key: `ALID`), `FLIGHT` (strong, key: `FlightNo`), `PASSENGER` (strong, key: `PassportNo`), `TICKET` (weak, owner = `PASSENGER`, partial key: `TicketID`), `CREW` (strong, key: `EmpID`).
- **Relationships:**
  - `OPERATES` — AIRLINE (1) : FLIGHT (N).
  - `BOOKS` — PASSENGER (1) : TICKET (1) with attribute `BookingDate`. (Each passenger has exactly one ticket, each ticket belongs to one passenger.)
  - `RESERVES` — PASSENGER (M) : FLIGHT (N) through TICKET — i.e., a **ternary** relationship with `SeatNo`, `Class`, `Fare` as attributes.
  - `WORKS_ON` — CREW (M) : FLIGHT (N) with attribute `DutyHours`.

#### (a) Map all entities, including the weak entity and the multivalued attribute `LanguagesKnown` of CREW. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Rule 1 — Strong entities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AIRLINE}(\underline{\text{ALID}},\ \text{ALName},\ \text{Country}) \\
\text{FLIGHT}(\underline{\text{FlightNo}},\ \text{Source},\ \text{Destination},\ \text{DepTime}) \\
\text{PASSENGER}(\underline{\text{PassportNo}},\ \text{PName},\ \text{Nationality}) \\
\text{CREW}(\underline{\text{EmpID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Designation})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rule 2 — Weak entity `TICKET`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TICKET}(\underline{\text{PassportNo}},\ \underline{\text{TicketID}},\ \text{IssueDate}) \\
\text{with } \text{PassportNo} \text{ as FK referencing } \text{PASSENGER(PassportNo)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rule 6 — Multivalued `LanguagesKnown`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREW\_LANG}(\underline{\text{EmpID}},\ \underline{\text{Language}})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Map the OPERATES, BOOKS, RESERVES (ternary), and WORKS_ON relationships, applying Rules 3, 4, 5, 7. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**`OPERATES` (1:N)** — Rule 4. Place `ALID` as FK in `FLIGHT`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FLIGHT}(\underline{\text{FlightNo}},\ \text{Source},\ \text{Destination},\ \text{DepTime},\ \text{ALID})
\end{aligned}
$$

**`BOOKS` (1:1)** — Rule 3. Place FK in the side with total participation (assume TICKET has total participation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TICKET}(\underline{\text{PassportNo}},\ \underline{\text{TicketID}},\ \text{IssueDate},\ \text{BookingDate})
\end{aligned}
$$

`BookingDate` is a relationship attribute stored in the chosen side.

**`RESERVES` (ternary M:N:M)** — Rule 7. Create new relation with PKs of all three:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RESERVES}(\underline{\text{PassportNo}},\ \underline{\text{TicketID}},\ \underline{\text{FlightNo}},\ \text{SeatNo},\ \text{Class},\ \text{Fare})
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: Since TICKET has a composite PK `(PassportNo, TicketID)`, the FK from TICKET must include both fields.

**`WORKS_ON` (M:N)** — Rule 5. Create new relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WORKS\_ON}(\underline{\text{EmpID}},\ \underline{\text{FlightNo}},\ \text{DutyHours})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark FKs explicitly** — Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing FK reference. Always write *"DID as FK referencing DOCTOR(DID)"*.
> 2. **Conflating 1:N with M:N** — In 1:N, **never** create a separate table. In M:N, **always** create one. This is the single most-tested distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring weak entity composite PK** — A weak entity's PK is **not** just its partial key. You *must* include the owner's PK.
> 4. **Storing derived attributes** — Attributes like `Age` (from `DOB`) or `Estd_Year` (from `CurrentYear`) are **omitted** from the schema. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Multivalued attribute stored in main table** — Direct 1NF violation. Always create a separate relation.
> 6. **Composite attribute not flattened** — `Name(First, Last)` must be split. Marks are lost if the original oval is copied as-is.
> 7. **Confusing 1:1 FK placement** — When in doubt, place the FK on the side with **total participation** and add a `UNIQUE` constraint.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synthesizing** ER → Relational means **applying 9 systematic rules** to translate a conceptual diagram into a logical schema of relations.
- **Three rules create a new relation:** M:N relationships, Multivalued attributes, N-ary (n ≥ 3) relationships, and Aggregation.
- **Six rules do not create a new relation:** Strong entities, Weak entities, 1:1, 1:N, ISA (when chosen), and most other cases.
- **Primary Key is always shown underlined** in the final schema; **FKs must be explicitly named and referenced** to the parent relation's PK.
- **Composite attributes are flattened** into their constituent atomic attributes.
- **Multivalued attributes are extracted** to a new relation to satisfy 1NF.
- **Derived attributes are omitted** entirely from the schema (they are computed at query time).
- **Weak entities have a composite PK** consisting of (own partial key + owner entity's PK).
- **ISA hierarchies offer 4 strategies** — choose based on disjoint/total/overlapping/partial constraints.
- **Aggregation** treats a relationship as a higher-level entity, mapped with the inner relationship's PK as a single foreign key.
- **Always show Domain, PK, FK, and constraints** — these are non-negotiable in KTU valuation.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mantra for KTU exams:** *"Strong and weak entities get their own tables; 1:1 and 1:N relationships are absorbed as foreign keys; M:N, multivalued, and n-ary get a brand-new junction table."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Structured Query Language (SQL) - Overview of the SQL Query Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Structured Query Language (SQL) - Overview of the SQL Query Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SQL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structured Query Language (SQL)** is the standard, declarative, non-procedural, set-oriented, high-level database sublanguages used to define, manipulate, control, and query data held in a **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)**. It is formally pronounced as **"S-Q-L"** or **"sequel"** and is the internationally accepted standard governed by both **ANSI (American National Standards Institute)** and **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBERT504 – Database Management Systems**, SQL is positioned as the practical application layer that maps theoretical **Relational Algebra** and **Relational Calculus** operations into an English-like, human-readable syntax that the RDBMS engine can execute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SQL is called a "Sublanguage"** : A full programming language supports input/output, conditional branching, loops, etc. SQL on its own does only data work. Hence it is embedded inside host languages (C, Java, Python) and is called a *database sublanguage* (DSL – Domain Specific Language).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu Analogy

Imagine you walk into a large restaurant (the **Database**). You do not go into the kitchen to cook — you give orders to the **waiter** (the **DBMS**). The waiter takes your order, written in a standard, structured format that all chefs understand. That standard form of placing orders is **SQL**.

- **You (User / Application Program)** → write the request
- **The Waiter (SQL Engine / Query Processor)** → interprets, optimizes, and passes to the kitchen
- **The Kitchen (Database Engine + Storage)** → produces the dish (result set)
- **The Menu (Schema / Tables)** → defines what is *possible* to order

You only state **WHAT** you want (e.g., "give me all orders above ₹500"), not **HOW** to fetch them — that makes SQL a *declarative* language. The optimizer decides the access path (index scan, full scan, hash join, etc.).

## 1.3 Brief History & Evolution of SQL Standards

> [!NOTE]
> SQL has evolved through a series of formally numbered standards, often called "SQL-n" or "SQL:nnnn". The KTU Board frequently tests this chronology.

| Year | Standard | Notable Additions |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| **1986** | SQL-86 / SQL-87 | First ANSI standard; basic DDL, DML |
| **1989** | SQL-89 | Integrity constraints (PK, FK, CHECK) |
| **1992** | SQL-92 / SQL2 | Major revision – joins, subqueries, set ops |
| **1999** | SQL:1999 | Triggers, recursive queries, OLAP, **regular expressions** |
| **2003** | SQL:2003 | **XML** support, window functions, MERGE, sequences |
| **2006** | SQL:2006 | **XQuery/XPath** integration with XML |
| **2008** | SQL:2008 | `TRUNCATE`, `INSTEAD OF` triggers, minor cleanup |
| **2011** | SQL:2011 | **Temporal** database features, `PERIOD` definitions |
| **2016** | SQL:2016 | **JSON** native support, polymorphic table functions |
| **2019** | SQL:2019 | **Multidimensional arrays (MDA)**, JavaScript Object Notation enhancements |
| **2023** | SQL:2023 | Property graph queries (`GRAPH_TABLE`) |

## 1.4 Key Characteristics of SQL

- **Declarative (non-procedural)** → User specifies *what*, not *how*.
- **Set-oriented** → Operates on sets of rows, not one row at a time.
- **High-level** → Closer to English than to machine code.
- **Vendor-extensible** → All RDBMS add proprietary extensions (e.g., Oracle `DECODE`, PostgreSQL `RETURNING`).
- **Standardized** → ANSI/ISO compliance ensures portability.

## 1.5 SQL vs. Relational Algebra vs. Tuple Relational Calculus

| Aspect | Relational Algebra | Tuple Relational Calculus | SQL |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------------|-----|
| Type | Procedural | Non-procedural | Non-procedural |
| Basis | Algebraic operators | Predicate logic | English-like syntax |
| Output | New relation | Set of tuples | Result set (table) |
| Used in DBMS | Internally (query optimization) | Theoretical | **Practical user interface** |
| Order of operations | Explicit | Implicit | Implicit (optimizer decides) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"How does SQL relate to Relational Algebra?"* — answer: *SQL is the practical, commercial implementation of operations that are formally expressed in Relational Algebra and Relational Calculus. Every SQL query has an equivalent relational algebra expression used internally for optimization.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary

## 2.1 The Five Sub-Languages of SQL

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies SQL into **five functional categories**. This is one of the most frequently asked **2-mark / 3-mark** topics.

| Category | Full Form | Purpose | Example Commands |
|----------|-----------|---------|------------------|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Define / alter / drop schema objects | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Insert / modify / delete table data | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Retrieve data (single command) | `SELECT` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Permissions & security | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manage transactions | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`, `SET TRANSACTION` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Many textbooks group DQL inside DML because both deal with table contents. KTU accepts both views, but in formal SQL standards, `SELECT` is treated separately as **DQL** because it does not mutate the database.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a SQL Statement

The generic structure of a query (logical order of writing) is:

```sql
SELECT       [DISTINCT]  column_list            ← Projection
FROM         table_list                         ← Cartesian product source
[WHERE       condition]                         ← Selection (row filter)
[GROUP BY    grouping_columns]                  ← Aggregation grouping
[HAVING      group_condition]                   ← Filter on aggregates
[ORDER BY    sort_columns [ASC|DESC]]           ← Presentation order
[LIMIT n OFFSET m];                             ← Paging (vendor specific)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical vs. Physical Processing Order:** The *written* order is `SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY`. However, the *executed* order is `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`. This nuance is a **classic KTU trick question**.

## 2.3 SQL Data Types (Common Across Vendors)

| Category | Data Type | Usage Example |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Exact Numeric | `INTEGER`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT`, `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `NUMERIC` | Marks, IDs |
| Approximate Numeric | `FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION` | Scientific values |
| Character String | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `TEXT` | Names, addresses |
| Bit String | `BIT(n)`, `BIT VARYING(n)` | Flags |
| Date/Time | `DATE`, `TIME`, `TIMESTAMP`, `INTERVAL` | DOB, log times |
| Boolean | `BOOLEAN` | TRUE/FALSE flags |
| Large Object | `BLOB`, `CLOB` | Images, documents |
| XML / JSON | `XML`, `JSON`, `JSONB` | Semi-structured data |

## 2.4 Standard SQL Syntax Tokens

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions often ask to identify *which line of SQL has an error*. Memorize these tokens:

- **Literals** : String → `'Kerala'`, Numeric → `42`, Date → `DATE '2024-01-15'`
- **Identifiers** : Quoted `"Student Name"` or unquoted `student_name`
- **Operators** : Arithmetic (`+ - * / %`), Comparison (`= <> < > <= >=`), Logical (`AND OR NOT`)
- **Predicates** : `IN`, `BETWEEN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`, `EXISTS`, `UNIQUE`, `OVERLAPS`
- **Set Operators** : `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT` (or `MINUS` in Oracle)
- **Joins** : `INNER`, `LEFT [OUTER]`, `RIGHT [OUTER]`, `FULL [OUTER]`, `CROSS`, `NATURAL`
- **Comments** : `-- single line` and `/* multi-line */`

## 2.5 High-Yield Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exams

| Concept | One-Line Definition |
|---------|---------------------|
| Schema | Logical container of tables, views, indexes owned by a user |
| Catalog | Collection of schemas (top-level DBMS namespace) |
| Table | Set of rows with the same attributes; the basic unit of storage |
| View | Virtual table defined by a stored `SELECT` query |
| Index | Auxiliary data structure (usually B-Tree) for fast lookup |
| Primary Key | Uniquely identifies a row; NOT NULL + UNIQUE |
| Foreign Key | Referential integrity constraint linking two tables |
| Constraint | Rule enforced on column values (`PRIMARY KEY`, `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `FOREIGN KEY`) |
| Trigger | Procedure fired automatically on `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` |
| Stored Procedure | Pre-compiled set of SQL statements stored in the DB |
| Cursor | Pointer to a result set row-by-row processing mechanism |
| Embedding | Using SQL inside a host language (C, COBOL, Java) |
| Dynamic SQL | SQL constructed and executed at runtime via `PREPARE/EXECUTE` |
| Transaction | Logical unit of work with ACID properties |
| ACID | **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability |
| Catalog Information | Metadata available via `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of SQL** : SQL is the **de-facto backbone** of almost every modern data-driven system — banking ledgers, airline reservation systems, hospital information systems, e-commerce carts, IoT telemetry stores, and big-data warehouses (Hive, Spark SQL, Snowflake). Mastering SQL is non-negotiable for any software/IT engineer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because SQL is a declarative language, the "derivation" here is the **logical construction of a query from a natural-language request**, progressing from informal specification → relational algebra → SQL text. This is exactly the analytical flow KTU examiners look for.

## 3.1 Worked Example — From English to SQL

**Schema (for all derivations in this section):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id   INTEGER       PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id    INTEGER       PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name  VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL,
    salary    DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0),
    dept_id   INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
);
```

### Step 1 — Identify the **Output Attributes** (Projection)

> **English request:** *"List the names and salaries of all employees."*

- Output attributes → `{emp_name, salary}`
- Relation involved → `Employee`
- Relational Algebra form → $\pi_{\text{emp\_name, salary}}(\text{Employee})$
- **SQL form:**

```sql
SELECT emp_name, salary
FROM   Employee;
```

### Step 2 — Add a **Selection Condition** (Filter)

> **English request:** *"List names and salaries of employees earning more than 50,000."*

- Additional predicate → $\sigma_{\text{salary} > 50000}$
- Combined RA → $\pi_{\text{emp\_name, salary}}(\sigma_{\text{salary} > 50000}(\text{Employee}))$
- **SQL form:**

```sql
SELECT emp_name, salary
FROM   Employee
WHERE  salary > 50000;
```

### Step 3 — Perform a **Join** Across Two Tables

> **English request:** *"List each employee's name along with their department name."*

- Tables involved → `Employee ⋈ Department` (join on `dept_id`)
- RA → $\pi_{\text{emp\_name, dept\_name}}(\text{Employee} \bowtie_{\text{emp.dept\_id = dept.dept\_id}} \text{Department})$
- **SQL form (using explicit INNER JOIN):**

```sql
SELECT E.emp_name, D.dept_name
FROM   Employee E INNER JOIN Department D
       ON E.dept_id = D.dept_id;
```

### Step 4 — Apply **Aggregation** with `GROUP BY` & `HAVING`

> **English request:** *"For each department with more than 5 employees, display the total salary bill."*

- Grouping attribute → `dept_id`
- Aggregate → `SUM(salary)`
- Group filter → `COUNT(*) > 5`
- RA → $\pi_{\text{dept\_id, SUM(salary)}}(\text{Employee}) \;\text{grouped by dept\_id, having count} > 5$
- **SQL form:**

```sql
SELECT   dept_id, SUM(salary) AS total_salary
FROM     Employee
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY total_salary DESC;
```

### Step 5 — A **Set Operation** (UNION)

> **English request:** *"Display the union of employee IDs and department IDs."*

- RA → $\pi_{\text{emp\_id}}(\text{Employee}) \cup \pi_{\text{dept\_id}}(\text{Department})$
- **SQL form:**

```sql
SELECT emp_id  AS id FROM Employee
UNION
SELECT dept_id AS id FROM Department;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`UNION` vs `UNION ALL`:** `UNION` removes duplicates (extra sort), `UNION ALL` preserves them (faster). KTU questions sometimes test this performance nuance.

## 3.2 DDL Derivation — From Concept to Executable Code

**Goal:** Create a `Student` table with a primary key and one check constraint.

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no   INTEGER       PRIMARY KEY,
    name      VARCHAR(60)   NOT NULL,
    dob       DATE,
    cgpa      DECIMAL(4,2)  CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)
);
```

**Stepwise rationale:**

1. `CREATE TABLE Student` → defines a new relation.
2. `roll_no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` → ensures non-null, unique identification.
3. `name VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL` → string up to 60 chars, mandatory.
4. `dob DATE` → stores calendar date.
5. `cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)` → two decimal places, value restricted by domain.

## 3.3 DML Derivation — Insert, Update, Delete

```sql
-- INSERT a new row
INSERT INTO Student (roll_no, name, dob, cgpa)
VALUES (101, 'Anand', DATE '2004-05-12', 8.75);

-- UPDATE CGPA of a specific roll number
UPDATE Student
SET    cgpa = 9.10
WHERE  roll_no = 101;

-- DELETE a student record
DELETE FROM Student
WHERE  roll_no = 101;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`TRUNCATE` vs `DELETE`:** `TRUNCATE TABLE Student` removes **all rows** in one shot, is a DDL command (faster, cannot be rolled back in many DBs, resets identity), whereas `DELETE` is DML, can be filtered, and is fully transactional. KTU often asks this distinction.

## 3.4 Authorization (DCL) Derivation

```sql
GRANT  SELECT, INSERT ON Employee TO user_rahul WITH GRANT OPTION;
REVOKE INSERT        ON Employee FROM user_rahul CASCADE;
```

- `WITH GRANT OPTION` allows the grantee to further propagate the privilege.
- `CASCADE` revokes dependent privileges transitively.

## 3.5 Embedded SQL Concept (Descriptive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded SQL** = SQL statements placed inside a host language (C, COBOL, Java). The preprocessor (e.g., SQLCA in C) translates them into function calls. The host variable mechanism allows data exchange between the application and the database.

```c
EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION;
    int  v_empid;
    char v_name[51];
EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION;

EXEC SQL SELECT emp_name INTO :v_name
         FROM   Employee
         WHERE  emp_id = :v_empid;
```

> [!TIP]
> **Dynamic SQL** is constructed at runtime as a string and then prepared + executed (`PREPARE ... FROM ...; EXECUTE ...`). It is used when the exact query shape is unknown at compile time — e.g., report builders or search screens with arbitrary filters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SQL Functional Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User / Application Program] --> B[SQL Statement Text]
    B --> C[SQL Parser]
    C --> D[Query Optimizer]
    D --> E[Execution Engine]
    E --> F[(Database Storage Engine)]
    F --> G[Result Set]
    G --> A

    subgraph SQL_Subsystem[SQL Subsystem]
        C
        D
        E
    end

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#81C784,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#64B5F6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 SQL Sub-Language Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    SQL[SQL Structured Query Language] --> DDL[DDL Data Definition]
    SQL --> DML[DML Data Manipulation]
    SQL --> DQL[DQL Data Query]
    SQL --> DCL[DCL Data Control]
    SQL --> TCL[TCL Transaction Control]

    DDL --> DDL1[CREATE]
    DDL --> DDL2[ALTER]
    DDL --> DDL3[DROP]
    DDL --> DDL4[TRUNCATE]
    DDL --> DDL5[RENAME]

    DML --> DML1[INSERT]
    DML --> DML2[UPDATE]
    DML --> DML3[DELETE]

    DQL --> DQL1[SELECT]

    DCL --> DCL1[GRANT]
    DCL --> DCL2[REVOKE]

    TCL --> TCL1[COMMIT]
    TCL --> TCL2[ROLLBACK]
    TCL --> TCL3[SAVEPOINT]
    TCL --> TCL4[SET TRANSACTION]

    style SQL fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DDL fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DML fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DQL fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DCL fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style TCL fill:#F48FB1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Logical Query Processing Order of a SELECT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1. FROM source tables] --> B[2. WHERE row filter]
    B --> C[3. GROUP BY aggregation grouping]
    C --> D[4. HAVING group filter]
    D --> E[5. SELECT projection]
    E --> F[6. ORDER BY sorting]
    F --> G[7. LIMIT OFFSET paging]

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 SQL Query Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Parser
    participant V as Validator
    participant O as Optimizer
    participant E as Executor
    participant DB as Database

    U->>P: Submit SQL text
    P->>V: Tokens + Parse Tree
    V->>O: Validated Query Tree
    O->>O: Cost-based Optimization
    O->>E: Execution Plan
    E->>DB: Physical I/O Requests
    DB-->>E: Result Tuples
    E-->>U: Result Set / Status
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**What is SQL? List any four characteristics of SQL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SQL stands for *Structured Query Language*. It is a standard, non-procedural, set-oriented, high-level database sublanguage used to define, manipulate, control, and query relational databases. It is defined by both **ANSI** and **ISO**.

**Four Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Declarative / Non-procedural** — The user states *what* is required; the *how* is decided by the query optimizer.
2. **Set-oriented** — SQL operates on whole sets of rows in a single statement, not on individual records.
3. **High-level / English-like** — Syntax is close to natural English, making it easy to learn and use.
4. **Standardized & Portable** — ANSI/ISO standards allow SQL code to be portable across different RDBMS products (with minor extensions).

> *(Any other valid point — e.g., vendor-extensible, embedded-capable, supports views, security — accepted for full marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`
**Differentiate between DDL, DML, and DCL with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DDL (Data Definition Language) | DML (Data Manipulation Language) | DCL (Data Control Language) |
|--------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Purpose | Defines, alters, or removes database **schema** objects | Inserts, modifies, or deletes the **data** inside tables | Grants or revokes **privileges** on database objects |
| Auto-commit? | Yes (DDL is auto-committed in most DBs) | No (transactional) | No (transactional) |
| Example | `CREATE TABLE Student (...);` | `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (...);` | `GRANT SELECT ON Student TO user1;` |

> *Examples of each: DDL → `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`; DML → `UPDATE`, `DELETE`; DCL → `REVOKE`.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both are designed to be independently complete and equally weighted.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the different categories of SQL commands with suitable examples.*  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the following schema and write SQL queries for the requirements (i)–(iv):* **(7 marks)**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Author (
    author_id   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    author_name VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Book (
    book_id     INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    title       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price       DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0),
    author_id   INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES Author(author_id)
);
```

**Requirements:**
1. Insert a new book with `book_id = 501, title = 'DBMS Concepts', price = 599.00, author_id = 10`.
2. Increase the price of all books written by author_id 10 by 10%.
3. Display title and price of books whose price is between 400 and 800.
4. Delete all books whose price is below 100.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Sub-Category | Full Form | Purpose | Example |
|--------------|-----------|---------|---------|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines the structure (schema) of the database. | `CREATE TABLE Book (...);` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulates data already inside tables. | `INSERT INTO Book VALUES (...);` |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Retrieves data from one or more tables. | `SELECT title FROM Book;` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Manages user access rights. | `GRANT SELECT ON Book TO user1;` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Controls transactions. | `COMMIT;` |

> Each category carries **≈ 1.4 marks**; precise definitions and one correct example per category are essential for full credit.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

```sql
-- (i) Insert a new book (1.5 marks)
INSERT INTO Book (book_id, title, price, author_id)
VALUES (501, 'DBMS Concepts', 599.00, 10);

-- (ii) Increase price by 10% for author_id = 10 (2 marks)
UPDATE Book
SET    price = price * 1.10
WHERE  author_id = 10;

-- (iii) Display title and price where 400 <= price <= 800 (2 marks)
SELECT title, price
FROM   Book
WHERE  price BETWEEN 400 AND 800;

-- (iv) Delete books with price < 100 (1.5 marks)
DELETE FROM Book
WHERE  price < 100;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting the `FOREIGN KEY` constraint when inserting — the parent `Author` row must exist; otherwise the statement fails.
> 2. In (ii), using `price + 10` instead of `price * 1.10` — this only adds ₹10, not 10% of the price. KTU values percentage-change accuracy at 1 mark.
> 3. In (iii), mixing up `BETWEEN x AND y` with `x <= val <= y` — `BETWEEN` is **inclusive** of both bounds.
> 4. In (iv), using `DROP TABLE` instead of `DELETE` — this destroys the schema, not just the data.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Discuss the history and evolution of SQL standards. Highlight the major features introduced in SQL-92, SQL:1999, and SQL:2003.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the concept of embedded SQL and dynamic SQL with examples. Mention any two advantages of embedded SQL over static SQL.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **Origin (1 mark):** SQL originated in the early 1970s at **IBM's San Jose Research Laboratory** (now Almaden), developed by **Donald D. Chamberlin** and **Raymond F. Boyce**, originally called **SEQUEL (Structured English QUEry Language)** as part of the System R project. Oracle (then Relational Software Inc.) shipped the first commercial SQL implementation in 1979.
- **Standardization Timeline (3 marks):**
  * **1986 — SQL-86 (ANSI):** First official standard, basic DDL/DML.
  * **1989 — SQL-89:** Integrity constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, UNIQUE).
  * **1992 — SQL-92 / SQL2:** Major revision, mandated syntax for joins, subqueries, set operations, CASE expression, CAST.
  * **1999 — SQL:1999:** Triggers, stored procedures, user-defined functions, recursive `WITH`, regular expressions, Java integration (SQLJ).
  * **2003 — SQL:2003:** XML data type, `XQUERY`, window functions (`OVER (...)`), `MERGE` upsert, sequences, identity columns.
- **Why it matters (3 marks):** Standards ensure portability, vendor competition, and provide a stable teaching/learning vocabulary — all reasons KTU mandates a "SQL-standards-aware" syllabus.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **Embedded SQL (3 marks):** Embedding means writing SQL statements directly inside the source code of a host language (C, COBOL, Fortran, Java). A pre-compiler (e.g., Oracle's **Pro\*C**, IBM DB2's precompiler) converts them into function calls of the host language. Data is exchanged between the program and the database through **host variables** (prefixed with `:` in C, e.g., `:emp_name`). The `SQLCA` (SQL Communications Area) reports execution status.

```c
EXEC SQL SELECT name INTO :v_name
         FROM   Employee
         WHERE  emp_id = :v_id;
```

- **Dynamic SQL (2.5 marks):** Dynamic SQL is *constructed at runtime* as a character string. It is then passed through the **PREPARE** → **EXECUTE** cycle.

```sql
PREPARE stmt1 FROM :sql_text;
EXECUTE stmt1 USING :dept_id;
```

This is necessary when the exact query is not known at compile time (e.g., user-driven search forms).

- **Two Advantages of Embedded SQL over Static SQL (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
  1. **Better Performance (Compile-time optimization)** — Embedded SQL is preprocessed and bound to the database at compile time, enabling pre-parsed, optimized execution plans.
  2. **Compile-time error checking** — Syntax, table/column names, and data types are validated before the program runs, reducing runtime surprises.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Confusing the *origin* — SQL came from IBM's **SEQUEL** project, not from Oracle directly. Oracle adopted it for the first commercial RDBMS.
> 2. Confusing **embedded** vs **dynamic** SQL — Embedded = compile-time bound, fixed shape. Dynamic = runtime constructed, variable shape. Students often swap the two.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **host variables** when describing embedded SQL — this single keyword is worth 1 mark in KTU.
> 4. Writing "SQL-2000" — no such standard exists; the year-jumps go SQL-92 → SQL:1999.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] SQL = Structured Query Language, ANSI/ISO standard, non-procedural, set-oriented.
- [x] First proposed by Chamberlin & Boyce at IBM (1970s), originally called **SEQUEL**.
- [x] SQL is a **database sublanguage**, not a full programming language.
- [x] Five sub-languages: **DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, TCL** — know one example command each.
- [x] Generic SELECT skeleton: `SELECT … FROM … WHERE … GROUP BY … HAVING … ORDER BY …`.
- [x] **Logical vs. physical processing order** is different — a favourite KTU trick question.
- [x] `DELETE` is DML (transactional); `TRUNCATE` is DDL (auto-commit, faster, resets identity).
- [x] `UNION` removes duplicates, `UNION ALL` keeps them — performance nuance.
- [x] `BETWEEN` is **inclusive** of both bounds.
- [x] Constraints: `PRIMARY KEY`, `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `FOREIGN KEY`.
- [x] **Embedded SQL** = SQL inside a host language, compile-time bound, uses host variables.
- [x] **Dynamic SQL** = SQL string built at runtime, uses `PREPARE … EXECUTE`.
- [x] Major standards to remember: **SQL-86, SQL-89, SQL-92, SQL:1999, SQL:2003, SQL:2011, SQL:2016, SQL:2019, SQL:2023**.
- [x] `SELECT` is the **only DQL** command in standard SQL.
- [x] `GRANT … WITH GRANT OPTION` allows re-granting; `REVOKE … CASCADE` revokes transitively.
- [x] Schema → Catalog → Database → Table → Row → Column (hierarchy to recall).
- [x] ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability (foundational for SQL transactions).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Data Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQL Data Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SQL Data Definition Language (DDL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 covers the **Structured Query Language (SQL)** with specific focus on its Data Definition sub-language. This is a high-weightage topic for both Continuous Assessment (CA) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SQL Data Definition** refers to the subset of **Structured Query Language (SQL)** commands used by a Database Administrator (DBA) or developer to **define, modify, and remove the structural schema of a database**. In the ISO/IEC 9075 standard, this falls under the **SQL/Schemata** component.

The Data Definition Language (DDL) operates at the **schema level** (logical structure), as opposed to the **data level** (the actual rows/tuples) which is handled by DML (Data Manipulation Language).

The four primary DDL operations are:

| Operation | SQL Keyword | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Define** | `CREATE` | Constructs new schema objects (tables, views, indexes, domains) |
| **Modify** | `ALTER` | Modifies the structure of an existing object |
| **Remove** | `DROP` | Deletes an object from the database schema |
| **Rename** | `RENAME` | Renames an existing schema object |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of SQL DDL as the work of an architect building a house.**

- **Database** → The entire **plot of land**.
- **Schema / Tables** → The **rooms** inside the house.
- **Columns & Data Types** → The **dimensions and material** of each wall (e.g., a wall made of glass vs. brick).
- **Constraints** → The **building regulations** (e.g., "every room must have at least one window" → `NOT NULL`).
- **`CREATE`** → Drafting a **new blueprint**.
- **`ALTER`** → **Renovating** an existing room (adding a new door).
- **`DROP`** → **Demolishing** the room entirely.

Just as an architect works *before* people start living in the house, DDL is executed *before* the data (rows) gets inserted.

## 1.3 The Three-Level Schema Mapping

DDL statements map directly to the **ANSI/SPARC three-schema architecture**:

1. **External Schema (View Level):** Defined using `CREATE VIEW`.
2. **Conceptual Schema (Logical Level):** Defined using `CREATE TABLE`, domains, and constraints.
3. **Internal Schema (Physical Level):** Defined using `CREATE INDEX`, clustering, file organization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "Which level does DDL primarily target?" — the correct answer is the **Conceptual Schema** (for `CREATE TABLE`) and the **External Schema** (for `CREATE VIEW`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of SQL Data Definition Commands

```text
SQL Data Definition (DDL)
│
├── 1. Schema Definition
│   ├── CREATE SCHEMA
│   └── DROP SCHEMA
│
├── 2. Table Definition
│   ├── CREATE TABLE
│   ├── ALTER TABLE
│   ├── DROP TABLE
│   └── RENAME TABLE
│
├── 3. Domain Definition
│   └── CREATE DOMAIN
│
├── 4. Constraint Definition
│   ├── Column-level constraints
│   ├── Table-level constraints
│   └── ASSERTION (advanced)
│
├── 5. View Definition
│   ├── CREATE VIEW
│   └── DROP VIEW
│
└── 6. Index Definition
    ├── CREATE INDEX
    └── DROP INDEX
```

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Data Types Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize the categories, not the vendor-specific names.**

| Category | Standard SQL Types | Typical Usage in KTU Questions |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
