| **Exact Numeric** | `INTEGER`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT`, `NUMERIC(p,q)`, `DECIMAL(p,q)` | IDs, counts, money |
| **Approximate Numeric** | `FLOAT(n)`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION` | Scientific measurements |
| **Character String (Fixed)** | `CHAR(n)` | Codes, gender, status |
| **Character String (Variable)** | `VARCHAR(n)`, `CHARACTER VARYING(n)` | Names, descriptions |
| **Bit String** | `BIT(n)`, `BIT VARYING(n)` | Boolean flags (binary) |
| **Date / Time** | `DATE`, `TIME`, `TIMESTAMP`, `INTERVAL` | Audit logs, scheduling |
| **Boolean** | `BOOLEAN` (TRUE / FALSE / UNKNOWN) | Status flags |
| **Large Objects** | `CLOB`, `BLOB` | Documents, images |

### Formula for `NUMERIC(p, q)` Precision

The display of a `NUMERIC(p, q)` data type follows:

$$p = \text{total number of significant digits (precision)}$$
$$q = \text{number of digits after the decimal point (scale)}$$

Therefore, the number of digits **before** the decimal point is:

$$\text{Integer Digits} = p - q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** If a value exceeds the defined precision, the DBMS raises a **precision truncation error** (in strict mode) or performs **silent rounding** (in non-strict mode). Always assume **strict mode** in KTU examinations.

## 2.3 Constraints — The "Rules Engine" of DDL

**Constraints** are *integrity rules* enforced by the DBMS to prevent invalid data entry. They form the **core of KTU 14-mark questions**.

| Constraint | Purpose | Example Scenario |
|------------|---------|------------------|
| **NOT NULL** | Column cannot be empty | Every employee must have a name |
| **UNIQUE** | No duplicate values allowed | Email ID is unique per user |
| **PRIMARY KEY** | NOT NULL + UNIQUE (identifier) | Roll number of student |
| **FOREIGN KEY** | Referential integrity between tables | Dept_ID in Employee → Dept_ID in Department |
| **CHECK** | User-defined boolean condition | Salary > 0, Marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100 |
| **DEFAULT** | Assigns default if no value given | JoiningDate = CURRENT_DATE |

### Referential Integrity Action Rules (FOREIGN KEY)

When a referenced row is updated or deleted, the DBMS executes one of these actions:

$$ \text{Action} \in \{\text{CASCADE, SET NULL, SET DEFAULT, RESTRICT, NO ACTION}\} $$

- **CASCADE:** Automatically update/delete the dependent rows.
- **SET NULL:** Set the foreign key column to `NULL` in dependent rows.
- **RESTRICT / NO ACTION:** Reject the parent operation if dependents exist.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** `CASCADE` is used in **employee-project** relationships (delete project → remove all assignments). `RESTRICT` is used in **invoice-line item** relationships (cannot delete invoice with active line items).

## 2.4 Why and How — The Logic Behind DDL Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trigger:** Examiners frequently ask, *"Why is `DROP TABLE` irreversible in standard SQL?"*

**Why each DDL command exists:**

- **`CREATE SCHEMA`** → Provides a **namespace boundary** so two tables of the same name can coexist in different schemas (e.g., `library.Book` vs. `sales.Book`).
- **`CREATE TABLE`** → Maps an **Entity-Relationship (ER) entity set** to a physical storage structure.
- **`ALTER TABLE`** → Supports **schema evolution** (a real-world need: businesses grow, requirements change).
- **`CREATE INDEX`** → Trades off **storage space** for **faster query retrieval** (the B+ tree trade-off).
- **`CREATE VIEW`** → Implements the **external schema** and provides **logical data independence**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Analytical Layer:** This section provides exhaustive, derivation-grade examples of every DDL statement. Each example is decomposed to the level a KTU 2024 ESE Paper-Setter expects.

## 3.1 The `CREATE TABLE` Statement — Full Anatomy

The general syntax follows the ISO standard form:

```sql
CREATE TABLE TableName (
    ColumnName1  DataType  [ColumnConstraints],
    ColumnName2  DataType  [ColumnConstraints],
    ...
    [TableConstraints]
);
```

**Step-by-step decomposition:**

| Step | Token | Meaning |
|------|-------|---------|
| **1** | `CREATE TABLE` | Initiates schema-level object creation |
| **2** | `TableName` | Identifier (max 128 chars in most RDBMS) |
| **3** | `(` | Opens the column definitions block |
| **4** | `ColumnName DataType` | Defines attribute name and its domain |
| **5** | `ColumnConstraints` | Optional: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, DEFAULT |
| **6** | `TableConstraints` | Optional: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, composite UNIQUE |
| **7** | `);` | Closes and commits the DDL transaction |

## 3.2 Worked Example — University Database (ER-to-SQL Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A university database has three entities: **STUDENT**, **COURSE**, and **ENROLLMENT** (a many-to-many relationship). Department is associated with each Student.

### Step 1: Identify Entities, Attributes, and Keys

```text
STUDENT   (RollNo [PK], Name, DOB, DeptID [FK], Email)
COURSE    (CourseID [PK], Title, Credits)
ENROLLMENT(RollNo [FK,PK], CourseID [FK,PK], Grade)
DEPARTMENT(DeptID [PK], DeptName)
```

### Step 2: Translate `DEPARTMENT` Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE DEPARTMENT (
    DeptID    CHAR(4)        NOT NULL,
    DeptName  VARCHAR(40)    NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT pk_dept PRIMARY KEY (DeptID)
);
```

### Step 3: Translate `STUDENT` Table (with Foreign Key)

```sql
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    RollNo    INTEGER       NOT NULL,
    Name      VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL,
    DOB       DATE,
    DeptID    CHAR(4),
    Email     VARCHAR(60)   UNIQUE,
    CONSTRAINT pk_student   PRIMARY KEY (RollNo),
    CONSTRAINT fk_student_dept FOREIGN KEY (DeptID)
        REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(DeptID)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

### Step 4: Translate `COURSE` Table with `CHECK` Constraint

```sql
CREATE TABLE COURSE (
    CourseID  CHAR(6)        NOT NULL,
    Title     VARCHAR(80)    NOT NULL,
    Credits   INTEGER        NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT pk_course PRIMARY KEY (CourseID),
    CONSTRAINT chk_credits CHECK (Credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6)
);
```

### Step 5: Translate `ENROLLMENT` (Composite Primary Key + Two FKs)

```sql
CREATE TABLE ENROLLMENT (
    RollNo    INTEGER        NOT NULL,
    CourseID  CHAR(6)        NOT NULL,
    Grade     CHAR(2)        DEFAULT 'NA',
    CONSTRAINT pk_enroll PRIMARY KEY (RollNo, CourseID),
    CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_student FOREIGN KEY (RollNo)
        REFERENCES STUDENT(RollNo) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT fk_enroll_course  FOREIGN KEY (CourseID)
        REFERENCES COURSE(CourseID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_grade CHECK (Grade IN ('A','B','C','D','E','F','NA'))
);
```

## 3.3 The `ALTER TABLE` Statement — Schema Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** `ALTER TABLE` is a **DDL command** that allows *additive* or *destructive* modification to an existing table *without* losing data (for additive changes).

### Standard Forms

| Operation | Syntax | Effect |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Add column | `ALTER TABLE T ADD COLUMN C DataType;` | Adds new attribute |
| Drop column | `ALTER TABLE T DROP COLUMN C;` | Removes attribute (loses data) |
| Add constraint | `ALTER TABLE T ADD CONSTRAINT ...;` | Enforces new rule |
| Drop constraint | `ALTER TABLE T DROP CONSTRAINT ...;` | Removes rule |
| Modify column | `ALTER TABLE T ALTER COLUMN C SET NOT NULL;` | Tightens integrity |

### Worked Example

```sql
-- Step 1: Add a new phone number column
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD PhoneNo VARCHAR(15);

-- Step 2: Enforce that phone number must start with '+'
ALTER TABLE STUDENT
    ADD CONSTRAINT chk_phone CHECK (PhoneNo LIKE '+%');

-- Step 3: Remove the email uniqueness rule (if needed)
ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP CONSTRAINT unique_email;
```

## 3.4 CREATE INDEX — Performance Engineering

```sql
CREATE [UNIQUE] INDEX IndexName
    ON TableName (Column1 [ASC|DESC], Column2 [ASC|DESC], ...);
```

**Worked Example:**

```sql
-- B-Tree index for fast lookup (default)
CREATE INDEX idx_student_name ON STUDENT(Name);

-- Composite index for multi-attribute queries
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_lookup ON ENROLLMENT(CourseID, RollNo);

-- Unique index (also enforces uniqueness)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_student_email ON STUDENT(Email);
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Logical Question:** *"What is the cost of having an index?"*
> **Answer:** Every `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` must also update the index. **Index space is $O(n)$** where $n$ is the number of rows.

## 3.5 CREATE VIEW — Virtual Tables

```sql
CREATE VIEW ViewName [(ColumnList)] AS
    SELECT statement
    [WITH CHECK OPTION];
```

**Worked Example:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW v_student_dept AS
SELECT S.RollNo, S.Name, D.DeptName
FROM STUDENT S
JOIN DEPARTMENT D ON S.DeptID = D.DeptID;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`WITH CHECK OPTION`** ensures that any `INSERT` or `UPDATE` through the view does not violate the view's `WHERE` clause. It is a **mandatory** answer point in KTU view-based questions.

## 3.6 DROP Statement — Irreversible Deletion

```sql
DROP TABLE TableName [CASCADE | RESTRICT];
DROP SCHEMA SchemaName [CASCADE | RESTRICT];
DROP VIEW ViewName;
DROP INDEX IndexName;
```

- **CASCADE:** Automatically drop dependent objects (e.g., foreign keys in other tables).
- **RESTRICT:** Refuse to drop if any dependent object exists (default in standard SQL).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DDL Command Hierarchy and Object Dependency

The following Mermaid block illustrates how DDL commands interact with the three-schema architecture and their dependency relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SQL Data Definition DDL] --> B[Schema Level]
    A --> C[Table Level]
    A --> D[Domain Level]
    A --> E[View Level]
    A --> F[Index Level]

    B --> B1[CREATE SCHEMA]
    B --> B2[DROP SCHEMA]

    C --> C1[CREATE TABLE]
    C --> C2[ALTER TABLE]
    C --> C3[DROP TABLE]
    C --> C4[RENAME TABLE]

    D --> D1[CREATE DOMAIN]
    D --> D2[ALTER DOMAIN]
    D --> D3[DROP DOMAIN]

    E --> E1[CREATE VIEW]
    E --> E2[DROP VIEW]

    F --> F1[CREATE INDEX]
    F --> F2[DROP INDEX]

    C1 --> X1[Column Definitions]
    C1 --> X2[Column Constraints]
    C1 --> X3[Table Constraints]

    X2 --> Y1[NOT NULL]
    X2 --> Y2[UNIQUE]
    X2 --> Y3[CHECK]
    X2 --> Y4[DEFAULT]

    X3 --> Z1[PRIMARY KEY]
    X3 --> Z2[FOREIGN KEY]
    X3 --> Z3[Composite UNIQUE]

    Z2 --> W1[ON DELETE CASCADE]
    Z2 --> W2[ON DELETE SET NULL]
    Z2 --> W3[ON UPDATE CASCADE]
```

## 4.2 ER-to-SQL Translation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram maps the conceptual ER modeling process to its physical SQL DDL implementation, which is a high-value diagram for KTU module conversion questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONCEPTUAL[CONCEPTUAL LAYER ER MODEL]
        ER1[Entity Set STUDENT]
        ER2[Entity Set COURSE]
        ER3[Relationship ENROLLS]
    end

    subgraph LOGICAL[LOGICAL LAYER RELATIONAL SCHEMA]
        R1[STUDENT ROLLNO PK NAME DOB]
        R2[COURSE COURSEID PK TITLE]
        R3[ENROLLMENT ROLLNO FK COURSEID FK GRADE]
    end

    subgraph PHYSICAL[PHYSICAL LAYER SQL DDL]
        S1[CREATE TABLE STUDENT ...]
        S2[CREATE TABLE COURSE ...]
        S3[CREATE TABLE ENROLLMENT ...]
        S4[CREATE INDEX ...]
        S5[CREATE VIEW ...]
    end

    ER1 --> R1
    ER2 --> R2
    ER3 --> R3
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S2
    R3 --> S3
    S1 --> S4
    S1 --> S5
```

## 4.3 Referential Action Decision Tree

This flowchart guides the choice of referential action when defining a `FOREIGN KEY`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Dependent rows exist when parent is deleted?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Should dependent rows remain?}
    Q1 -- NO --> A1[Action: NO ACTION or RESTRICT]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Should FK become NULL or default value?}
    Q2 -- NO --> A2[Action: CASCADE]
    Q3 -- NULL --> A3[Action: SET NULL]
    Q3 -- DEFAULT --> A4[Action: SET DEFAULT]
    Q3 -- NOTHING --> A5[Action: RESTRICT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks), Part B (Choice-based × 14 marks = 14 marks). Total = 20 marks per module question.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between `CHAR(n)` and `VARCHAR(n)` data types. State one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `CHAR(n)` | `VARCHAR(n)` |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Storage | **Fixed length** (pads with spaces) | **Variable length** (uses only what is needed) |
| Performance | Slightly faster (predictable offset) | Slightly slower (length prefix required) |
| Space efficiency | Wastes space for short values | Efficient for variable-length data |
| Max length (KTU standard) | $n \leq 255$ | $n \leq 65535$ (MySQL row limit applies) |

- **`CHAR(n)` preferred:** Storing fixed-format codes like country codes (`'IN'`, `'US'`), gender (`'M'`, `'F'`), or semester codes (`'S1'`, `'S2'`).
- **`VARCHAR(n)` preferred:** Storing variable-length data like student names, addresses, or email IDs.

> **Marks split:** (2 marks for differentiation table, 1 mark for scenario examples.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**What is the difference between a `PRIMARY KEY` constraint and a `UNIQUE` constraint? Can a table have multiple `UNIQUE` constraints but only one `PRIMARY KEY`? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

**Differences:**

| Aspect | PRIMARY KEY | UNIQUE |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| NULL allowed? | **No** (implicitly NOT NULL) | **Yes** (one NULL allowed in standard SQL) |
| Number per table | **Only one** | **Multiple allowed** |
| Purpose | Row identifier | Alternate keys / business rules |
| Creates clustered index? | Yes (by default in most RDBMS) | No (creates non-clustered index) |

**Justification (Yes, the statement is true):**
- A table represents one entity set; only **one attribute (or minimal combination)** is the *surrogate* or *natural* identifier → exactly one `PRIMARY KEY`.
- However, there can be **many alternate business identifiers** (e.g., `Email`, `PhoneNo`, `EmployeeCode`) that must remain unique → multiple `UNIQUE` constraints.

> **Marks split:** (1 mark for each comparison row, 1 mark for justification.)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Pattern:** KTU provides an internal choice between two questions on the same module. Both questions are given below for practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a `LIBRARY` database with entities `BOOK`, `MEMBER`, and `BORROW`. Write the complete SQL DDL scripts to create these tables with appropriate primary keys, foreign keys, and at least one `CHECK` and one `UNIQUE` constraint each.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Schema Analysis**

| Entity | Attributes | Constraint Highlights |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| BOOK | BookID, ISBN, Title, Price, CopiesAvailable | ISBN UNIQUE, Price > 0 CHECK |
| MEMBER | MemberID, Name, Phone, Email | Email UNIQUE, Phone NOT NULL |
| BORROW | BorrowID, MemberID, BookID, BorrowDate, ReturnDate | FK CASCADE, ReturnDate > BorrowDate CHECK |

**Step 2: SQL DDL Script**

```sql
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    BookID           INTEGER       NOT NULL,
    ISBN             CHAR(13)      NOT NULL,
    Title            VARCHAR(200)  NOT NULL,
    Price            DECIMAL(8,2)  NOT NULL,
    CopiesAvailable  INTEGER       NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    CONSTRAINT pk_book PRIMARY KEY (BookID),
    CONSTRAINT uk_book_isbn UNIQUE (ISBN),
    CONSTRAINT chk_book_price CHECK (Price > 0),
    CONSTRAINT chk_book_copies CHECK (CopiesAvailable >= 0)
);

CREATE TABLE MEMBER (
    MemberID  INTEGER      NOT NULL,
    Name      VARCHAR(60)  NOT NULL,
    Phone     VARCHAR(15)  NOT NULL,
    Email     VARCHAR(80),
    CONSTRAINT pk_member PRIMARY KEY (MemberID),
    CONSTRAINT uk_member_email UNIQUE (Email),
    CONSTRAINT uk_member_phone UNIQUE (Phone)
);

CREATE TABLE BORROW (
    BorrowID    INTEGER      NOT NULL,
    MemberID    INTEGER      NOT NULL,
    BookID      INTEGER      NOT NULL,
    BorrowDate  DATE         NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    ReturnDate  DATE,
    CONSTRAINT pk_borrow PRIMARY KEY (BorrowID),
    CONSTRAINT fk_borrow_member FOREIGN KEY (MemberID)
        REFERENCES MEMBER(MemberID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT fk_borrow_book FOREIGN KEY (BookID)
        REFERENCES BOOK(BookID) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    CONSTRAINT chk_borrow_dates CHECK (ReturnDate IS NULL OR ReturnDate >= BorrowDate)
);
```

> **Marks Split (7):** Schema design (2) + BOOK & MEMBER DDL (3) + BORROW DDL with FK actions (2).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a `VIEW` in SQL. Create a view `v_active_borrowers` showing the names of members who have borrowed at least one book, along with the total books they have borrowed.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Conceptual Explanation of VIEW**

A **view** is a *virtual table* defined by a stored `SELECT` query. It does not physically store data; the data is computed dynamically each time the view is queried. Views provide:

- **Logical data independence** — underlying schema can change without affecting user queries.
- **Security** — users can be granted access to a view without accessing base tables.
- **Query simplification** — complex joins can be pre-packaged.
- **Update restrictions** — views are non-updatable by default unless they are based on a single table without aggregates.

**Step 2: SQL VIEW Definition**

```sql
CREATE VIEW v_active_borrowers AS
SELECT M.MemberID,
       M.Name,
       COUNT(B.BorrowID) AS TotalBooksBorrowed
FROM MEMBER M
JOIN BORROW B ON M.MemberID = B.MemberID
GROUP BY M.MemberID, M.Name
HAVING COUNT(B.BorrowID) >= 1;
```

**Step 3: Querying the View**

```sql
SELECT * FROM v_active_borrowers
ORDER BY TotalBooksBorrowed DESC;
```

> **Marks Split (7):** View concept explanation (3) + CREATE VIEW syntax (2) + Sample query using view (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a SQL `DOMAIN`. Write a `CREATE DOMAIN` statement to define a domain `positive_money` that only allows non-negative decimal values up to `999999.99`. Then use this domain in a `CREATE TABLE` for a `PAYROLL` table with columns: `EmpID`, `BasicSalary`, `HRA`, `Bonus`.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Definition of DOMAIN**

A **DOMAIN** is a user-defined data type that constrains the set of valid values a column can take. It is created at the schema level (once) and reused across multiple tables — promoting **consistency** and **centralized rule definition**. It is a feature of Standard SQL supported by PostgreSQL and DB2 (not directly by MySQL).

**Step 2: Syntax**

```sql
CREATE DOMAIN DomainName AS DataType
    [CHECK (condition)];
```

**Step 3: Implementation**

```sql
-- Step 1: Define the domain
CREATE DOMAIN positive_money AS DECIMAL(10,2)
    CHECK (VALUE >= 0 AND VALUE <= 999999.99);

-- Step 2: Use the domain in a table
CREATE TABLE PAYROLL (
    EmpID        INTEGER       NOT NULL,
    BasicSalary  positive_money NOT NULL,
    HRA          positive_money,
    Bonus        positive_money DEFAULT 0,
    CONSTRAINT pk_payroll PRIMARY KEY (EmpID)
);
```

> [!TIP]
> **Analytical Note:** If any of `BasicSalary`, `HRA`, or `Bonus` is later updated to a negative value, the DBMS will reject the transaction because the `CHECK` on the `positive_money` domain will fail.

> **Marks Split (7):** Definition of domain (2) + CREATE DOMAIN syntax (2) + PAYROLL table usage (3).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the differences between `DROP TABLE`, `TRUNCATE TABLE`, and `DELETE FROM` in SQL. Which of these is a DDL command? Justify your answer with respect to transaction rollback capabilities.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Comparative Analysis**

| Aspect | `DROP TABLE` | `TRUNCATE TABLE` | `DELETE FROM` |
|--------|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| **SQL Category** | DDL | DDL (in most RDBMS) | DML |
| **Effect** | Removes table + data + indexes + constraints | Removes all rows, retains structure | Removes selected/all rows |
| **Where clause** | Not applicable | Not applicable | Supported (`DELETE FROM T WHERE ...`) |
| **Triggers fired?** | No | No (in standard SQL) | Yes (per row) |
| **Space released?** | Yes (full) | Yes (usually) | No (marked as free) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Slowest |
| **Rollback possible?** | **No** (auto-committed in most RDBMS) | **No** (auto-committed) | **Yes** (transactional) |

**Step 2: Justification**

`DROP TABLE` and `TRUNCATE TABLE` are **DDL commands** because they manipulate the *schema/structure* of the database (the table object itself), not just the data tuples. They automatically trigger an internal `COMMIT`, which means:

- The transaction log for the operation is minimal.
- A `ROLLBACK` statement issued after a DDL command will **not** reverse it.
- This is why accidental `DROP TABLE` is a common cause of data loss in production environments.

In contrast, `DELETE FROM` is a **DML command** and is fully transactional — it can be wrapped inside `BEGIN TRANSACTION ... ROLLBACK` and undone.

**Step 3: Safe Practice Recommendation**

> Always maintain a **pre-drop snapshot** using `CREATE TABLE backup_T AS SELECT * FROM T;` before executing `DROP TABLE`.

> **Marks Split (7):** Comparison table (3) + DDL identification + justification (2) + Rollback explanation (2).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These to Score Full Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `CONSTRAINT name` keyword** — Examiners deduct **1 mark** when constraints are unnamed, as KTU expects named constraints for clarity.
> 2. **Wrong placement of constraints** — A `CHECK` constraint on a *referenced* column must be defined in the referenced table, not the referencing table. Mixing this up loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `TRUNCATE` with `DELETE`** — Students often write "TRUNCATE is DML" — it is **NOT**. It is DDL. This single error costs **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Missing `ON DELETE` / `ON UPDATE` clauses** — Without these, default behavior is `NO ACTION`. Always explicitly state the referential action in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Using MySQL-only syntax in standard SQL answers** — `AUTO_INCREMENT`, `ENGINE=InnoDB`, and backticks `` ` `` are **not standard SQL**. Use `GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY` (SQL standard) or omit them.
> 6. **Forgetting semicolons** — KTU evaluators often deduct **0.5 marks** for sloppy syntax in long DDL scripts.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — SQL Data Definition**

- **DDL** = Data Definition Language → defines *schema*, not *data*.
- Core DDL commands: **`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`** (the **CAD** triad).
- **Numeric types:** `INTEGER`, `DECIMAL(p,q)`, `FLOAT` → remember the **precision-scale** rule.
- **String types:** `CHAR(n)` (fixed) vs. `VARCHAR(n)` (variable).
- **Date/Time types:** `DATE`, `TIME`, `TIMESTAMP`, `INTERVAL`.
- **Six constraints:** `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`.
- **PRIMARY KEY** = NOT NULL + UNIQUE, and there can be only **one** per table.
- **FOREIGN KEY** referential actions: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `SET DEFAULT`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION`.
- **`ALTER TABLE`** enables **schema evolution** — additive changes preserve data.
- **Views** are virtual tables — no physical storage; provide **logical data independence**.
- **Indexes** trade storage for speed; penalize `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` performance.
- **`DROP TABLE`** is **DDL**, **irreversible**, and **auto-commits**.
- **`TRUNCATE`** is **DDL** (despite deleting rows); it resets the table structure.
- **`DELETE`** is **DML**; it is **transactional and rollback-able**.
- **Standard SQL** uses `GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY`, not `AUTO_INCREMENT`.
- A **DOMAIN** is a reusable user-defined data type with optional `CHECK` constraints.
- **`WITH CHECK OPTION`** prevents updates through a view that violate the view's `WHERE` clause.
- The three-schema architecture maps DDL to: **Conceptual** (`CREATE TABLE`), **External** (`CREATE VIEW`), **Internal** (`CREATE INDEX`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Structure of SQL Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure of SQL Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**SQL (Structured Query Language)** is the standard, declarative, high-level database sub-language used to define, manipulate, control, and query data in a **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)**. It is based on relational algebra and tuple relational calculus, and was standardized by **ANSI (American National Standards Institute)** in **1986** and by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in **1987**.

The **Basic Structure of an SQL Query** refers to the canonical three-clause skeleton (with two optional clauses) that forms the foundation of almost every data-retrieval statement in SQL:

```sql
SELECT  [DISTINCT] <attribute-list>
FROM    <relation-list>
[WHERE  <condition>]
[GROUP BY <grouping-attributes>]
[HAVING <group-condition>]
[ORDER BY <attribute-list> [ASC | DESC]];
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A query is interpreted as a *declarative* specification — the user states **WHAT** data is required, not **HOW** to retrieve it. The DBMS query optimizer decides the physical access plan.

### 1.2 Sub-languages of SQL (KTU Classification)

| Sub-language | Full Form | Purpose | Example Commands |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Define schema | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulate data | `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Control permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manage transactions | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Query data | `SELECT` (sometimes treated as a separate category) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You do not walk into the kitchen to cook; you tell the **waiter** (the DBMS):

1. **What** you want → `SELECT` (projection)
2. **From which menu** → `FROM` (source relation)
3. **Under what condition** → `WHERE` (selection)
4. **How it should be grouped** → `GROUP BY`
5. **Filter on groups** → `HAVING`
6. **In what order to serve** → `ORDER BY`

You declare the dish; the kitchen (the query optimizer + execution engine) decides the recipe, the route to the table, and the serving order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students understand that the *logical* order of writing a query is **NOT** the same as the *physical* order of execution.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Tuple** = a row in a relation.
- **Attribute** = a column in a relation.
- **Schema** = the structural definition of a relation.
- **Instance** = the data inside the relation at a given moment.
- **Cardinality** = number of tuples.
- **Degree** = number of attributes.
- **NULL** = absence of value (not zero, not empty string, not blank).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardinality and Degree are commonly asked 2-mark questions.** Memorize the definitions exactly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Three Mandatory Clauses

#### 2.1.1 The `SELECT` Clause — Projection

- Lists the **attributes** to appear in the result.
- The special symbol `*` denotes **all attributes**.
- The keyword `DISTINCT` forces **duplicate elimination** (set semantics).
- The keyword `ALL` (default) permits duplicates (bag/multiset semantics).
- Arithmetic expressions are allowed: `SELECT salary * 1.10 FROM emp;`
- The `AS` keyword provides a **rename/alias**: `SELECT salary * 12 AS annual_salary FROM emp;`

> [!NOTE]
> **String literals and constants** can be projected: `SELECT 'Active' AS status, name FROM emp;`

#### 2.1.2 The `FROM` Clause — Cartesian Product Source

- Specifies the **relations** to be accessed.
- If more than one relation is listed, the conceptual first step is a **Cartesian product** (later restricted by `WHERE`).
- Aliases for relations: `FROM emp AS e, dept AS d;`

#### 2.1.3 The `WHERE` Clause — Selection Predicate

- Contains the **Boolean condition** that filters tuples row-by-row.
- Uses **comparison operators**: `=`, `<>` or `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`.
- Uses **logical connectives**: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.
- Uses **range and set operators**: `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`.
- Uses **pattern matching** with `LIKE` and wildcards:
  - `%` matches any string of zero or more characters.
  - `_` matches exactly one character.

### 2.2 Order of Clauses (Mandatory Write Order)

The **mandatory writing order** in standard SQL is:

$$
\text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Changing this order results in a **syntax error** in standard SQL. The DBMS does not accept e.g. `FROM emp WHERE ... SELECT ...`.

### 2.3 Logical Order of Evaluation (Conceptual)

Although the *writing order* is fixed, the *evaluation order* the optimizer conceptually follows is:

$$
\text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}
$$

This is the **logical query processing pipeline**. It is the most repeated conceptual question in KTU boards.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Operator & Clause Reference)

| Construct | Syntax | Meaning | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Projection | `SELECT A1, A2` | Choose columns | `SELECT name, age` |
| Star | `SELECT *` | All columns | `SELECT * FROM student` |
| Distinct | `SELECT DISTINCT col` | Remove duplicates | `SELECT DISTINCT dept` |
| Alias | `expr AS alias` | Rename output | `SELECT sal*12 AS annual` |
| Selection | `WHERE cond` | Filter rows | `WHERE age >= 18` |
| Range | `BETWEEN a AND b` | Inclusive range | `WHERE marks BETWEEN 50 AND 90` |
| Set membership | `IN (v1, v2, ...)` | Match any in list | `WHERE dept IN ('CS','IT')` |
| Pattern | `LIKE 'pat%'` | String pattern | `WHERE name LIKE 'A%'` |
| NULL test | `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` | Test for NULL | `WHERE phone IS NULL` |
| Logical AND | `cond1 AND cond2` | Both true | `age>18 AND dept='CS'` |
| Logical OR | `cond1 OR cond2` | Either true | `dept='CS' OR dept='IT'` |
| Negation | `NOT cond` | Invert truth | `NOT (age < 18)` |
| Grouping | `GROUP BY col` | Form groups | `GROUP BY dept` |
| Group filter | `HAVING cond` | Filter groups | `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5` |
| Sort | `ORDER BY col [ASC $\vert$ DESC]` | Sort result | `ORDER BY gpa DESC` |
| Limit | `LIMIT n` (MySQL) | First n rows | `LIMIT 10` |
| Cartesian | `FROM R, S` | All pairs | Implicit in multi-relation |
| Join | `FROM R JOIN S ON cond` | Conditional pairing | `emp JOIN dept ON e.deptid=d.id` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `=` is the equality operator in SQL. `==` is **NOT** valid SQL — it is a C/Java construct. Examiners specifically test this.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Banking**: `SELECT SUM(balance) FROM accounts WHERE branch='Kochi';`
- **E-commerce**: `SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products;`
- **Healthcare**: `SELECT * FROM patients WHERE admission_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';`
- **Academia**: `SELECT name, AVG(marks) FROM student GROUP BY name HAVING AVG(marks) > 75;`

> [!NOTE]
> SQL is the **lingua franca of data**. Every modern data engineering, data analytics, and backend development role expects SQL fluency. Mastering the basic structure is the gateway to advanced topics like window functions, CTEs, and recursive queries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Working Schema (Used Throughout)

Consider the following relations for all derivations:

**STUDENT Table:**

| Id | Name | Age | Dept | GPA |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Asha | 20 | CS | 8.9 |
| 2 | Balu | 22 | IT | 7.2 |
| 3 | Chitra | 21 | CS | 9.1 |
| 4 | Deepa | 23 | EC | 6.8 |
| 5 | Eshan | 20 | CS | 8.4 |
| 6 | Fathima | 22 | IT | 7.9 |

**COURSE Table:**

| CourseId | CourseName | Credits | Dept |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| C1 | DBMS | 4 | CS |
| C2 | Networks | 3 | IT |
| C3 | Signals | 3 | EC |

### 3.2 Analytical Query 1 — Simple Projection with Filter

**Problem:** *Retrieve the name and GPA of all CS department students with GPA greater than 8.5.*

**Step 1 — Identify the source relation:** Only `STUDENT` is needed, so:

```sql
FROM STUDENT
```

**Step 2 — Identify the filter condition (WHERE):** Two conditions combined by `AND`:

- Department must be CS → `Dept = 'CS'`
- GPA must exceed 8.5 → `GPA > 8.5`

```sql
WHERE Dept = 'CS' AND GPA > 8.5
```

**Step 3 — Identify the projection attributes (SELECT):** Only `Name` and `GPA`:

```sql
SELECT Name, GPA
```

**Step 4 — Combine in mandatory clause order:**

```sql
SELECT Name, GPA
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  Dept = 'CS' AND GPA > 8.5;
```

**Step 5 — Conceptual evaluation pipeline:**

$$
\text{STUDENT} \xrightarrow{\text{Dept='CS' AND GPA>8.5}} \text{Filtered rows} \xrightarrow{\text{Project Name, GPA}} \text{Result}
$$

**Step 6 — Result tuples:**

| Name | GPA |
|:---|:---:|
| Asha | 8.9 |
| Chitra | 9.1 |
| Eshan | 8.4 |

### 3.3 Analytical Query 2 — Projection, Rename, Arithmetic

**Problem:** *Display the name, age, and a calculated field called "graduation_year" assuming all students are B.Tech 2024 batch.*

The formula for graduation year given 2024 batch is:

$$
\text{graduation\_year} = 2024 + (4 - \text{current\_sem\_estimate}) = 2024 + (4 - 0) = 2028
$$

But for a simpler arithmetic demonstration using the available attributes:

**Step 1 — Identify arithmetic:** Age in months is `Age * 12`.

**Step 2 — Apply alias `AS` for clarity:**

```sql
SELECT Name, Age, Age * 12 AS Age_In_Months
FROM   STUDENT;
```

**Step 3 — Result tuples:**

| Name | Age | Age_In_Months |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Asha | 20 | 240 |
| Balu | 22 | 264 |
| Chitra | 21 | 252 |
| Deepa | 23 | 276 |
| Eshan | 20 | 240 |
| Fathima | 22 | 264 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `DISTINCT` here?** Because `Age_In_Months` may duplicate (240, 264) but the *Name* attribute is unique, so the result tuples are all unique. `DISTINCT` would have no effect.

### 3.4 Analytical Query 3 — Logical Processing Order Demonstrated

**Problem:** *For each department, display the department name and the count of students having GPA > 8. Show only those departments where the count is more than 1, sorted by count in descending order.*

**Step 1 — Write the query with all clauses:**

```sql
SELECT   Dept, COUNT(*) AS Hcount
FROM     STUDENT
WHERE    GPA > 8
GROUP BY Dept
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY Hcount DESC;
```

**Step 2 — Step-by-step conceptual execution:**

1. **FROM** → load the entire `STUDENT` relation (cardinality 6).
2. **WHERE** → keep only rows where `GPA > 8`:

| Id | Name | Age | Dept | GPA |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Asha | 20 | CS | 8.9 |
| 3 | Chitra | 21 | CS | 9.1 |
| 5 | Eshan | 20 | CS | 8.4 |

3. **GROUP BY Dept** → partition into groups:
   - CS → {Asha, Chitra, Eshan}
   - (IT, EC groups are empty)
4. **HAVING COUNT(*) > 1** → drop IT, EC (empty groups fail the predicate). Keep only CS.
5. **SELECT** → produce `Dept='CS'`, `COUNT(*)=3`.
6. **ORDER BY Hcount DESC** → already single row.

**Step 3 — Final Result:**

| Dept | Hcount |
|:---|:---:|
| CS | 3 |

### 3.5 Analytical Query 4 — Pattern Matching with `LIKE`

**Problem:** *Find names of all students whose name begins with a vowel and ends with 'a'.*

**Step 1 — Identify pattern:**
- Vowels: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U` (case-insensitive in most RDBMS).
- Pattern: starts with vowel, ends with `a`.

**Step 2 — Write `LIKE` pattern:**

```sql
SELECT Name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  Name LIKE '[AEIOU]%a';
```

> [!NOTE]
> The bracket `[AEIOU]` syntax is **SQL Server specific**. In standard ANSI SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle), use `SIMILAR TO` or `REGEXP_LIKE`. For KTU theory, the universal pattern uses `LIKE 'A%a' OR LIKE 'E%a' OR ...` or `SIMILAR TO '[AEIOU]%a'`.

**Step 3 — Alternative ANSI form:**

```sql
SELECT Name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  Name SIMILAR TO '[AEIOUaeiou].*a';
```

**Step 4 — Result tuples:**

| Name |
|:---|
| Asha |
| Chitra |
| Fathima |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Clause-to-Operator Quick Map

| Clause | Filters | Applies To | Cannot Reference |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `WHERE` | Rows (tuples) | Individual tuples before grouping | Aggregate functions |
| `HAVING` | Groups (partitions) | Groups formed by `GROUP BY` | Column aliases defined in `SELECT` (in some DBs) |
| `SELECT` | Final projection | Final result attributes | — |
| `ORDER BY` | Ordering | Final output | — |
| `FROM` | Source relations | Cross-product source | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`WHERE` cannot use aggregate functions** like `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`. This is one of the most tested distinctions in KTU exams. Use `HAVING` for aggregate-level filtering.

### 3.7 Complete End-to-End Example (Multi-Relation)

**Problem:** *For every CS-department student who has taken the course 'DBMS', display the student's name, course name, and credits.*

**Step 1 — Identify the source relations:** `STUDENT`, `ENROLLMENT` (assumed: `Enroll(StudentId, CourseId, Grade)`), `COURSE`.

**Step 2 — Identify join conditions:**
- `STUDENT.Id = ENROLLMENT.StudentId`
- `ENROLLMENT.CourseId = COURSE.CourseId`

**Step 3 — Identify the filter:** `STUDENT.Dept = 'CS'` and `COURSE.CourseName = 'DBMS'`.

**Step 4 — Identify projection:** `STUDENT.Name`, `COURSE.CourseName`, `COURSE.Credits`.

**Step 5 — Final query:**

```sql
SELECT S.Name, C.CourseName, C.Credits
FROM   STUDENT S, ENROLLMENT E, COURSE C
WHERE  S.Id = E.StudentId
  AND  E.CourseId = C.CourseId
  AND  S.Dept = 'CS'
  AND  C.CourseName = 'DBMS';
```

**Step 6 — Modern equivalent using explicit `JOIN` syntax:**

```sql
SELECT S.Name, C.CourseName, C.Credits
FROM   STUDENT S
JOIN   ENROLLMENT E ON S.Id = E.StudentId
JOIN   COURSE    C ON E.CourseId = C.CourseId
WHERE  S.Dept = 'CS'
  AND  C.CourseName = 'DBMS';
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both are equivalent. The second form is **SQL-92 standard** and preferred in modern code, but the first form (implicit join in `WHERE`) is still asked in KTU theory questions to test relational algebra mapping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mandatory Clause Order and Logical Evaluation Pipeline

This diagram shows the *writing order* (top row) versus the *evaluation order* (bottom row) of an SQL query.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SELECT Clause - Project Attributes"]
    nodeB["FROM Clause - Source Relations"]
    nodeC["WHERE Clause - Row Filter"]
    nodeD["GROUP BY Clause - Partition Tuples"]
    nodeE["HAVING Clause - Group Filter"]
    nodeF["ORDER BY Clause - Sort Output"]

    nodeG["STEP 1 Load Relations from FROM"]
    nodeH["STEP 2 Apply WHERE Predicate"]
    nodeI["STEP 3 Partition with GROUP BY"]
    nodeJ["STEP 4 Filter Groups with HAVING"]
    nodeK["STEP 5 Project Attributes via SELECT"]
    nodeL["STEP 6 Sort Final Output via ORDER BY"]
    nodeM["FINAL RESULT TABLE RETURNED TO USER"]

    nodeA -.-> nodeB -.-> nodeC -.-> nodeD -.-> nodeE -.-> nodeF
    nodeB ==> nodeG
    nodeG ==> nodeH
    nodeH ==> nodeI
    nodeI ==> nodeJ
    nodeJ ==> nodeK
    nodeK ==> nodeL
    nodeL ==> nodeM

    classDef writeStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef evalStyle fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF writeStyle

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SQL Query Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeB["PARSER - Syntax Check"]
    nodeC["VALIDATOR - Semantic Check"]
    nodeD["QUERY REWRITER - View Substitution"]

    nodeG --> nodeF

```

    nodeA --> nodeC

flowchart TD

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the basic structure of an SQL query with the mandatory and optional clauses. State the order in which they are written.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The basic structure of an SQL query consists of the following clauses written in this fixed order:

1. **`SELECT`** *(mandatory)* — specifies the attributes (columns) to be projected into the result.
2. **`FROM`** *(mandatory)* — specifies the source relations (tables) to be accessed.
3. **`WHERE`** *(optional)* — applies a row-level filter using comparison and logical operators.
4. **`GROUP BY`** *(optional)* — partitions the result into groups of tuples sharing common attribute values.
5. **`HAVING`** *(optional)* — filters groups based on aggregate conditions.
6. **`ORDER BY`** *(optional)* — sorts the final result in ascending or descending order.

The mandatory clauses are `SELECT` and `FROM`. The optional clauses are `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, and `ORDER BY`. A query omitting all optional clauses is still syntactically valid (e.g., `SELECT * FROM emp;`).

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark for naming all clauses.
- 1 mark for mandatory vs optional classification.
- 1 mark for the fixed order.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between the `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses. Why can `WHERE` not use aggregate functions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `WHERE` | `HAVING` |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Applies to | Individual rows | Groups of rows |
| Used with | Comparison & logical operators | Aggregate functions |
| Execution order | Before `GROUP BY` | After `GROUP BY` |
| Filter granularity | Tuple-level | Group-level |

`WHERE` is evaluated **before** grouping. At that point, groups do not exist yet, so aggregate functions like `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG` have no defined value to compute. `HAVING` is evaluated **after** grouping, where aggregates are meaningful.

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT Dept, COUNT(*) FROM STUDENT
WHERE Age > 18                -- row-level
GROUP BY Dept
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;          -- group-level
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark for the basic difference (row vs group).
- 1 mark for execution order distinction.
- 1 mark for explaining why `WHERE` cannot host aggregates.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B, 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

Consider the following schema:

- `STUDENT(RegNo, Name, Age, Dept, GPA)`
- `COURSE(CourseId, CName, Credits, Dept)`
- `ENROLL(RegNo, CourseId, Grade)`

Write SQL queries for the following:

**(a)** *List the names of all distinct departments from the STUDENT table, sorted alphabetically.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For each course having more than 2 enrolments, display the course name and the average grade. Sort the result by average grade in descending order.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify requirement:** Unique department names, alphabetically sorted.

**Step 2 — Apply `DISTINCT` to remove duplicates.**

**Step 3 — Apply `ORDER BY` with `ASC` (default).**

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT Dept
FROM   STUDENT
ORDER BY Dept ASC;
```

**Step 4 — Explanation (for valuation):**
- `DISTINCT` eliminates duplicate department values (e.g., 'CS' may appear multiple times).
- `ORDER BY Dept ASC` sorts alphabetically in ascending order (ascending is the default; can be omitted).
- No `WHERE` is needed since we want all departments.

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- 2 marks — correct use of `DISTINCT`.
- 2 marks — correct `ORDER BY` clause.
- 2 marks — correctly formed complete query.
- 1 mark — neatness / accurate explanation.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify source relations:** `COURSE` (for course name) and `ENROLL` (for grades & counting).

**Step 2 — Identify join condition:** `COURSE.CourseId = ENROLL.CourseId`.

**Step 3 — Identify grouping attribute:** `COURSE.CourseId` (or `CName`).

**Step 4 — Identify group filter:** `COUNT(*) > 2` (must use `HAVING`, not `WHERE`).

**Step 5 — Identify projection:** `CName`, `AVG(Grade)`.

**Step 6 — Identify ordering:** `ORDER BY AVG(Grade) DESC`.

**Final query:**

```sql
SELECT   C.CName, AVG(E.Grade) AS AvgGrade
FROM     COURSE C
JOIN     ENROLL  E ON C.CourseId = E.CourseId
GROUP BY C.CourseId, C.CName
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 2
ORDER BY AvgGrade DESC;
```

**Step 7 — Explanation:**
- `JOIN` merges course and enrollment data.
- `GROUP BY` partitions enrollments per course.
- `HAVING COUNT(*) > 2` filters courses with insufficient enrollments.
- `AVG(Grade)` computes the mean grade per course.
- `ORDER BY AvgGrade DESC` sorts highest average first.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- 2 marks — correct `JOIN` and grouping.
- 2 marks — correct `HAVING` clause (not `WHERE`).
- 2 marks — correct `AVG` aggregate and projection.
- 1 mark — correct `ORDER BY DESC` ordering.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

Consider the same schema as above.

**(a)** *Retrieve the names of students whose name starts with 'A' and ends with 'a', or whose age is between 20 and 22. Display the result sorted by age in descending order.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Find the names of students who have not enrolled in any course. Use a subquery or `NOT IN` construct.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Decompose the conditions:**
- Condition 1: `Name LIKE 'A%a'` (pattern match).
- Condition 2: `Age BETWEEN 20 AND 22` (range).
- These are combined with `OR`.

**Step 2 — Projection:** `Name`.

**Step 3 — Order:** `Age DESC`.

```sql
SELECT Name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  Name LIKE 'A%a' OR Age BETWEEN 20 AND 22
ORDER BY Age DESC;
```

**Step 4 — Explanation:**
- The `LIKE` operator uses `%` to match any number of characters between 'A' and 'a'.
- `BETWEEN 20 AND 22` is inclusive of both endpoints.
- Without parentheses, `OR` is evaluated after `AND` (lower precedence). Since no `AND` is present, parentheses are not strictly necessary here, but using them improves clarity:

```sql
WHERE (Name LIKE 'A%a') OR (Age BETWEEN 20 AND 22)
```

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- 2 marks — correct `LIKE` pattern.
- 2 marks — correct `BETWEEN` usage.
- 2 marks — correct combination with `OR` and ordering.
- 1 mark — clarity / explanation.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the requirement:** Students with no enrollment record.

**Step 2 — Use `NOT IN` with a subquery that returns all `RegNo`s present in `ENROLL`.**

**Final query (Approach 1 — `NOT IN`):**

```sql
SELECT Name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  RegNo NOT IN (SELECT RegNo FROM ENROLL);
```

**Final query (Approach 2 — `NOT EXISTS`):**

```sql
SELECT S.Name
FROM   STUDENT S
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM ENROLL E WHERE E.RegNo = S.RegNo
);
```

**Final query (Approach 3 — `LEFT JOIN` with `NULL` check):**

```sql
SELECT S.Name
FROM   STUDENT S
LEFT JOIN ENROLL E ON S.RegNo = E.RegNo
WHERE  E.RegNo IS NULL;
```

**Step 3 — Explanation:**
- `NOT IN` returns all students whose `RegNo` does not match any value in the subquery list.
- If any `RegNo` in `ENROLL` is `NULL`, `NOT IN` returns no rows (a famous KTU trick question). The `NOT EXISTS` form avoids this pitfall.
- The `LEFT JOIN` form is preferred in modern practice and is **NULL-safe**.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- 3 marks — correct subquery / `NOT IN` logic.
- 2 marks — correct main query structure.
- 2 marks — explanation of the `NULL` pitfall and alternative approaches.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SQL Query Questions (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Writing clauses in wrong order** (`FROM ... SELECT ...`) — instant zero for the query. KTU evaluators are strict on syntax.
> 2. **Using aggregate in `WHERE`** — e.g., `WHERE COUNT(*) > 5` is a syntax error. Always use `HAVING`.
> 3. **Confusing `=` with `==`** — only `=` is valid in SQL.
> 4. **Forgetting `DISTINCT`** when the question says "unique" or "distinct".
> 5. **Using `LIKE` without `%` or `_`** — `LIKE 'A'` matches only the exact string "A", not names starting with A.
> 6. **Mixing `AND`/`OR` precedence without parentheses** — leads to wrong result sets. Always parenthesize complex predicates.
> 7. **Naming the result column twice in `SELECT`** without using an alias — may cause ambiguity in `ORDER BY`.
> 8. **Forgetting to qualify attributes** in multi-table queries — `WHERE S.Dept = 'CS'` vs `WHERE Dept = 'CS'` matters in joins.
> 9. **Treating `NULL` as zero or empty string** — `NULL = NULL` evaluates to **UNKNOWN**, not `TRUE`.
> 10. **Using `NOT IN` when the subquery can return `NULL`** — silently returns zero rows. KTU examiners sometimes set this as a deliberate trap.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Structure of SQL Queries**

- ✅ SQL stands for **Structured Query Language**, based on relational algebra and tuple calculus.
- ✅ The basic structure has **2 mandatory clauses** (`SELECT`, `FROM`) and **4 optional clauses** (`WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `ORDER BY`).
- ✅ **Mandatory writing order:** `SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY`.
- ✅ **Logical execution order:** `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY`.
- ✅ `SELECT` performs **projection** (column choice); `WHERE` performs **selection** (row filtering).
- ✅ `*` selects all attributes; `DISTINCT` removes duplicates; `ALL` (default) allows duplicates.
- ✅ `LIKE` uses `%` (zero or more chars) and `_` (exactly one char) for pattern matching.
- ✅ `BETWEEN` is **inclusive** of both endpoints; `IN` checks set membership.
- ✅ `IS NULL` tests for NULL; `= NULL` is **invalid**.
- ✅ `WHERE` filters rows **before** grouping; `HAVING` filters groups **after** aggregation.
- ✅ `WHERE` **cannot** contain aggregate functions; `HAVING` is the only place aggregates are allowed in the filtering chain.
- ✅ Aliases use `AS` keyword: `SELECT col AS new_name FROM ...`.
- ✅ Multi-table queries form a **Cartesian product** that is restricted by `WHERE` join conditions.
- ✅ `ORDER BY` defaults to `ASC`; use `DESC` for descending order.
- ✅ Subqueries in `WHERE` use `IN`, `NOT IN`, `EXISTS`, `NOT EXISTS`, comparison operators.
- ✅ `NOT IN` with a subquery returning `NULL` can produce empty results — prefer `NOT EXISTS` for NULL safety.
- ✅ SQL is **declarative** — user specifies *what*, DBMS decides *how*.
- ✅ The DBMS components involved in query processing: **Parser → Validator → Rewriter → Optimizer → Execution Engine → Storage Manager**.
- ✅ Standard SQL supports `JOIN ... ON ...` syntax (SQL-92) as a cleaner alternative to implicit `WHERE` joins.
- ✅ Common KTU interview/board questions: clause order, execution order, `WHERE` vs `HAVING`, `LIKE` wildcards, NULL semantics, aggregate restrictions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additional Basic Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Additional Basic Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Additional Basic Operations" in Relational Algebra?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Database Management Systems (PBERT504)**, Module 2 introduces **Relational Algebra** — the procedural query language that forms the theoretical foundation of SQL. After covering the *fundamental* operations (Select σ, Project π, Union ∪, Set Difference −, Cartesian Product ×, and Rename ρ), the syllabus moves to **Additional Basic Operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Additional Basic Operations are derived relational algebra operations that can be expressed in terms of the fundamental operations but are given dedicated symbols and shorthand notation to simplify query expression. They include **Set Intersection (∩), Natural Join (⋈), Theta Join (⋈θ), Assignment (←)**, and the **Outer Joins (⟕, ⟖, ⟗)**.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Counter"

Imagine you walk into a bakery that has **two counters**:

- **Counter A** sells cakes and pastries.
- **Counter B** sells pastries and breads.

The **fundamental operations** (Select, Project, Union, etc.) are like asking the bakery, *"Give me everything from Counter A"* or *"Combine both counters."* But real life is rarely that simple. You often want more **sophisticated** questions:

- *"Which items are sold in BOTH counters?"* → **Set Intersection (∩)**
- *"Match items by their natural name (pastry) and give me a combined menu?"* → **Natural Join (⋈)**
- *"Match items by a custom condition, like items cheaper than ₹100?"* → **Theta Join (⋈θ)**
- *"Keep my query in a temporary tray while I keep working?"* → **Assignment (←)**
- *"Show me everything from Counter A, but fill in blanks where Counter B had no match?"* → **Left Outer Join (⟕)**
- *"Show me only the pastries that Counter A sells, but using Counter B's price list?"* → **Semi-Join (⋉)** / **Anti-Join (▷)**

These are precisely the **Additional Basic Operations** — they make relational algebra *expressive, compact, and human-readable*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every Additional Operation **can be rewritten** using the six fundamental operations. The dedicated symbol is a **shorthand convenience**, not a new computational primitive. This is a frequently tested conceptual question worth **3 marks**.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Them?

| Need | Fundamental Op | Additional Op Used |
|------|----------------|--------------------|
| Find common tuples between two relations | Complex: $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$ | $R \cap S$ (direct) |
| Combine relations on equal attributes | Cartesian product + Select + Project (3 steps) | Natural Join $R \bowtie S$ (1 step) |
| Combine on custom condition | Cartesian product + Select (2 steps) | Theta Join $R \underset{\theta}{\bowtie} S$ (1 step) |
| Preserve unmatched rows | Not possible without Union extensions | Outer Joins (⟕, ⟖, ⟗) |

**Physical constants / Standard metrics:**
- A relation with $m$ tuples and another with $n$ tuples: Cartesian product has $m \times n$ tuples.
- Natural Join on relation with $m$ tuples and $n$ tuples: between $\max(m, n)$ and $m \times n$ tuples.
- Complexity of Set Intersection: $O(m + n)$ in implementation, but expressed as $O(m \times n)$ in pure algebraic form.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Additional Operations — Structured Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Set Intersection ($R \cap S$)

**Definition:** Returns tuples that are present in **both** relations $R$ and $S$.

**Why it matters:** It answers *"What do these two sets have in common?"* — a fundamental need in data analysis.

**How to express in fundamental operations:**

$$R \cap S = R - (R - S)$$

**Pre-condition:** $R$ and $S$ must be **union-compatible** (same number of attributes, same domains).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $R - (R - S)$ work?**
> - $R - S$ gives tuples in $R$ but NOT in $S$.
> - Subtracting these from $R$ leaves only tuples in $R$ that ARE in $S$.
> - Since $S$ is the universe of comparison, the result is exactly $R \cap S$.

#### 2.1.2 Natural Join ($R \bowtie S$)

**Definition:** Combines $R$ and $S$ on **all common attribute names**, eliminating duplicate columns.

**Why it matters:** It is the **most-used** operation in practice — almost every SQL `JOIN` is a Natural Join variant.

**How it works — Step by Step Logic:**

1. Find the **intersection of attribute names** between $R$ and $S$. Call this set $C$.
2. Compute the Cartesian product $R \times S$.
3. Apply a Select operation: $\sigma$ where attributes in $C$ are equal.
4. Project away one copy of each common attribute.

**Equivalent expression:**

$$R \bowtie S = \pi_{\text{attrs}(R) \cup \text{attrs}(S)} \big( \sigma_{R.A_1 = S.A_1 \land \cdots \land R.A_k = S.A_k}(R \times S) \big)$$

**Common pitfall:** If $R$ and $S$ have **no common attributes**, Natural Join degenerates to Cartesian Product.

#### 2.1.3 Theta Join ($R \underset{\theta}{\bowtie} S$)

**Definition:** A Cartesian product followed by a Select with an **arbitrary condition** $\theta$.

**Equivalent expression:**

$$R \underset{\theta}{\bowtie} S = \sigma_{\theta}(R \times S)$$

**Equi-Join** is a special case where $\theta$ consists **only of equality** conditions. Equi-Join **preserves duplicate attribute columns**; Natural Join **removes** them.

#### 2.1.4 Assignment Operation ($T \leftarrow E$)

**Definition:** Assigns the result of a relational algebra expression $E$ to a **temporary relation** $T$.

**Why it matters:** Breaks complex queries into **readable, named steps** — like variables in algebra.

**Example pattern:**

$$\text{Temp1} \leftarrow \sigma_{\text{salary} > 50000}(\text{Employee})$$

$$\text{Result} \leftarrow \pi_{\text{name, dept}}(\text{Temp1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The arrow $\leftarrow$ denotes assignment, **NOT** implication (which is $\Rightarrow$). The assignment symbol may be `:=` or `<-` depending on the textbook.

#### 2.1.5 Outer Joins (Left ⟕, Right ⟖, Full ⟗)

**Definition:** A join that **preserves unmatched tuples** by padding missing values with **NULL**.

- **Left Outer Join ($R \ ⟕ \ S$):** Keep all tuples of $R$; pad $S$'s missing attributes with NULL.
- **Right Outer Join ($R \ ⟖ \ S$):** Keep all tuples of $S$; pad $R$'s missing attributes with NULL.
- **Full Outer Join ($R \ ⟗ \ S$):** Keep all tuples of **both** $R$ and $S$; pad missing sides with NULL.

**Formally (Left Outer Join):**

$$R \ ⟕ \ S = (R \bowtie S) \cup (R - \pi_{\text{attrs}(R)}(R \bowtie S))$$

### 2.2 Bonus Operations Frequently Asked in KTU

#### 2.2.1 Semi-Join ($R \ltimes S$)

Returns tuples of $R$ that have **at least one match** in $S$ under the join condition.

$$R \ltimes S = \pi_{\text{attrs}(R)}(R \bowtie S)$$

#### 2.2.2 Anti-Join ($R \triangleright S$)

Returns tuples of $R$ that have **no match** in $S$.

$$R \triangleright S = R - \pi_{\text{attrs}(R)}(R \bowtie S)$$

#### 2.2.3 Division ($R \div S$)

Used for queries like *"Find suppliers who supply ALL parts."*

$$R \div S = \pi_{A}(R) - \pi_{A}\Big(\big(\pi_{A}(R) \times S\big) - R\Big)$$

where $A$ = attributes of $R$ not in $S$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Symbol | Equivalent in Fundamental Ops | Pre-condition | Cardinality Bound |
|---|-----------|--------|-------------------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Set Intersection | $R \cap S$ | $R - (R - S)$ | Union-compatible | $\le \min(\vert R \vert, \vert S \vert)$ |
| 2 | Natural Join | $R \bowtie S$ | $\pi \sigma (\times)$ | Common attributes exist | $\le \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ |
| 3 | Theta Join | $R \underset{\theta}{\bowtie} S$ | $\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S)$ | Any relations | $\le \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ |
| 4 | Equi-Join | $R \underset{A=B}{\bowtie} S$ | $\sigma_{A=B}(R \times S)$ | $A \in R, B \in S$ | $\le \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ |
| 5 | Left Outer Join | $R \ ⟕ \ S$ | $(R \bowtie S) \cup (R - \pi_R(R \bowtie S))$ | Any relations | $\ge \vert R \vert$ |
| 6 | Right Outer Join | $R \ ⟖ \ S$ | $(R \bowtie S) \cup (S - \pi_S(R \bowtie S))$ | Any relations | $\ge \vert S \vert$ |
| 7 | Full Outer Join | $R \ ⟗ \ S$ | $(R \ ⟕ \ S) \cup (R \ ⟖ \ S)$ | Any relations | $\ge \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert$ |
| 8 | Semi-Join | $R \ltimes S$ | $\pi_R(R \bowtie S)$ | Any relations | $\le \vert R \vert$ |
| 9 | Anti-Join | $R \triangleright S$ | $R - \pi_R(R \bowtie S)$ | Any relations | $\le \vert R \vert$ |
| 10 | Division | $R \div S$ | $\pi_A(R) - \pi_A(\pi_A(R) \times S - R)$ | $\text{attrs}(S) \subseteq \text{attrs}(R)$ | $\le \vert \pi_A(R) \vert$ |
| 11 | Assignment | $T \leftarrow E$ | N/A (syntactic shorthand) | $E$ is a valid expression | $= \vert E \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardinality Notation:** $\vert R \vert$ denotes the number of tuples (rows) in relation $R$. Throughout this document we use `\vert` (not the bare pipe `|`) inside LaTeX expressions to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Natural Join is used to combine *Customer* and *Account* tables on `customer_id`.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon/Flipkart):** Outer Joins preserve products with **zero reviews** (left unmatched, padded with NULL).
- **Healthcare Records:** Anti-Join identifies patients with **no scheduled appointments**.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Division finds suppliers who supply **all parts** in a given set.
- **Social Networks:** Semi-Join finds users who share **at least one group** with the current user.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Schemas

We will use the following two relations throughout:

- **EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, DeptID, Salary)**
- **DEPARTMENT(DeptID, DeptName, Location)**

Sample data:

| EmpID | Name   | DeptID | Salary |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1     | 60000  |
| E2    | Binu   | D2     | 45000  |
| E3    | Chitra | D1     | 70000  |
| E4    | Deepa  | D3     | 50000  |

| DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|--------|----------|----------|
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    |
| D2     | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| D3     | MECH     | Calicut  |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Set Intersection (3-Mark Standard)

**Query:** *Find EmpIDs of employees who work in a department located in 'Kochi' AND have salary > 55000.*

**Step 1:** Find employees in Kochi-located departments.

$$R_1 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{EmpID}}\big(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \sigma_{\text{Location}=\text{'Kochi'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT})\big)$$

Compute $\sigma_{\text{Location}=\text{'Kochi'}}(\text{DEPARTMENT})$:

| DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|--------|----------|----------|
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    |

Compute Natural Join on DeptID:

| EmpID | Name   | DeptID | Salary | DeptName | Location |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|----------|----------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1     | 60000  | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E3    | Chitra | D1     | 70000  | CSE      | Kochi    |

Apply Project on EmpID:

$$R_1 = \{\text{E1}, \text{E3}\}$$

**Step 2:** Find employees with salary > 55000.

$$R_2 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{EmpID}}\big(\sigma_{\text{Salary} > 55000}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\big)$$

After Select:

| EmpID | Name   | DeptID | Salary |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1     | 60000  |
| E3    | Chitra | D1     | 70000  |

After Project:

$$R_2 = \{\text{E1}, \text{E3}\}$$

**Step 3:** Intersect.

$$R_1 \cap R_2 = \{\text{E1}, \text{E3}\}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** EmpIDs `E1` and `E3`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$:**
> - $R_1 - R_2 = \emptyset$ (since they are equal).
> - $R_2 - (R_2 - R_1) = R_2 - \emptyset = R_2 = \{\text{E1}, \text{E3}\}$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Natural Join with Step-by-Step Expansion

**Query:** *List all employees along with their department names and locations.*

**Naive (Fundamental) Approach:**

$$R \times S = \text{EMPLOYEE} \times \text{DEPARTMENT}$$

This produces $4 \times 3 = 12$ tuples.

$$R \times S \text{ (Cartesian Product - abbreviated):}$$

| EmpID | Name   | E.DeptID | Salary | D.DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|-------|--------|----------|--------|----------|----------|----------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1       | 60000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E1    | Asha   | D1       | 60000  | D2       | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E1    | Asha   | D1       | 60000  | D3       | MECH     | Calicut  |
| E2    | Binu   | D2       | 45000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E2    | Binu   | D2       | 45000  | D2       | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E2    | Binu   | D2       | 45000  | D3       | MECH     | Calicut  |
| E3    | Chitra | D1       | 70000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E3    | Chitra | D1       | 70000  | D2       | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E3    | Chitra | D1       | 70000  | D3       | MECH     | Calicut  |
| E4    | Deepa  | D3       | 50000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E4    | Deepa  | D3       | 50000  | D2       | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E4    | Deepa  | D3       | 50000  | D3       | MECH     | Calicut  |

Apply $\sigma_{\text{E.DeptID} = \text{D.DeptID}}$:

| EmpID | Name   | E.DeptID | Salary | D.DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|-------|--------|----------|--------|----------|----------|----------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1       | 60000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E2    | Binu   | D2       | 45000  | D2       | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E3    | Chitra | D1       | 70000  | D1       | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E4    | Deepa  | D3       | 50000  | D3       | MECH     | Calicut  |

Apply Project to remove duplicate DeptID:

$$R \bowtie S:$$

| EmpID | Name   | DeptID | Salary | DeptName | Location |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|----------|----------|
| E1    | Asha   | D1     | 60000  | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E2    | Binu   | D2     | 45000  | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| E3    | Chitra | D1     | 70000  | CSE      | Kochi    |
| E4    | Deepa  | D3     | 50000  | MECH     | Calicut  |

**Compact relational algebra expression:**

$$\pi_{\text{EmpID, Name, DeptID, Salary, DeptName, Location}}\big(\sigma_{\text{EMPLOYEE.DeptID = DEPARTMENT.DeptID}}(\text{EMPLOYEE} \times \text{DEPARTMENT})\big)$$

**Short-hand using Natural Join:**

$$\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \text{DEPARTMENT}$$

✅ **Result:** 4 tuples with all 6 distinct attributes.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Left Outer Join (with NULL padding)

**Query:** *List all departments and their employees (if any), including departments with no employees.*

**Relation Setup:** Add a new department `D4 = CIVIL` with no employees.

| DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|--------|----------|----------|
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    |
| D2     | ECE      | Trivandrum|
| D3     | MECH     | Calicut  |
| D4     | CIVIL    | Kochi    |

**Left Outer Join $\text{DEPARTMENT} \ ⟕ \ \text{EMPLOYEE}$:**

Step 1 — Compute the natural join:

| DeptID | DeptName | Location | EmpID | Name | Salary |
|--------|----------|----------|-------|------|--------|
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    | E1    | Asha | 60000  |
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    | E3    | Chitra | 70000 |
| D2     | ECE      | Trivandrum| E2   | Binu | 45000  |
| D3     | MECH     | Calicut  | E4    | Deepa | 50000 |

Step 2 — Identify unmatched tuples of the **left** relation (DEPARTMENT):

- **D4 (CIVIL)** has no employee match.

Step 3 — Pad with NULL:

| DeptID | DeptName | Location | EmpID | Name | Salary |
|--------|----------|----------|-------|------|--------|
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    | E1    | Asha | 60000  |
| D1     | CSE      | Kochi    | E3    | Chitra | 70000 |
| D2     | ECE      | Trivandrum| E2   | Binu | 45000  |
| D3     | MECH     | Calicut  | E4    | Deepa | 50000  |
| D4     | CIVIL    | Kochi    | NULL  | NULL | NULL   |

**Compact expression:**

$$\text{DEPARTMENT} \ ⟕ \ \text{EMPLOYEE}$$

✅ **Result:** 5 tuples (4 + 1 padded with NULL).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Division (THE Classic KTU Question)

**Schemas:**

- **SUPPLIES(SID, PID)** — Supplier SID supplies Part PID.
- **PARTS(PID)** — Set of parts of interest.

**Data:**

| SUPPLIES ||  | PARTS   |
|----------|--|--------|
| SID | PID ||  | PID  |
| S1  | P1  ||  | P1   |
| S1  | P2  ||  | P2   |
| S1  | P3  ||  |      |
| S2  | P1  ||  |      |
| S2  | P2  ||  |      |
| S3  | P2  ||  |      |
| S3  | P3  ||  |      |

**Query:** *Find SIDs of suppliers who supply ALL parts listed in PARTS.*

**Algebraic expression using Division:**

$$\text{SUPPLIES} \div \text{PARTS}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\pi_{\text{SID}}(\text{SUPPLIES})$:

$$\{\text{S1}, \text{S2}, \text{S3}\}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\pi_{\text{SID}}(\text{SUPPLIES}) \times \text{PARTS}$:

| SID | PID |
|-----|-----|
| S1  | P1  |
| S1  | P2  |
| S2  | P1  |
| S2  | P2  |
| S3  | P1  |
| S3  | P2  |

**Step 3:** Subtract the original SUPPLIES from this Cartesian product:

| SID | PID |
|-----|-----|
| S2  | P1  |  ← S2 supplies P1, P2 — missing P1 in PARTS? Actually S2 supplies P1 and P2 only, so missing nothing... wait
| S3  | P1  |  ← S3 supplies P2, P3 — missing P1

Recompute carefully — the (Cartesian product − SUPPLIES) yields tuples where a supplier *would have been expected* to supply a part but **didn't**.

Result of $(\pi_{\text{SID}}(\text{SUPPLIES}) \times \text{PARTS}) - \text{SUPPLIES}$:

| SID | PID |
|-----|-----|
| S2  | P1  | ← Wait, S2 supplies P1, so it is IN SUPPLIES. Let me recheck data.

Corrected: S2 supplies P1, P2. So SID=S2, PID=P1 IS in SUPPLIES. So it should NOT appear.

Re-evaluate: The tuples in the Cartesian product but NOT in SUPPLIES are those where the supplier did not supply that part.

Suppliers: S1, S2, S3. Parts: P1, P2.

- S1–P1 ✓, S1–P2 ✓
- S2–P1 ✓, S2–P2 ✓
- S3–P1 ✗ (S3 doesn't supply P1), S3–P2 ✓

So the difference is:

| SID | PID |
|-----|-----|
| S3  | P1  |

**Step 4:** Project on SID:

$$\pi_{\text{SID}}\{(\pi_{\text{SID}}(\text{SUPPLIES}) \times \text{PARTS}) - \text{SUPPLIES}\} = \{\text{S3}\}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract from $\pi_{\text{SID}}(\text{SUPPLIES})$:

$$\{\text{S1}, \text{S2}, \text{S3}\} - \{\text{S3}\} = \{\text{S1}, \text{S2}\}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** S1 and S2 supply all parts (P1 and P2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing "supplies all parts" with "supplies at least one part." Division finds suppliers where the set of parts supplied is a **superset** of PARTS.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Selection Guide

| Query Intent | Best Operation | Reasoning |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|
| Common rows between two tables | $R \cap S$ | Direct intersection |
| Match rows on shared columns | $R \bowtie S$ | Eliminates duplicate columns automatically |
| Match rows on custom condition | $R \underset{\theta}{\bowtie} S$ | Flexible predicate |
| Keep all left rows, even unmatched | $R \ ⟕ \ S$ | Pads right with NULL |
| Keep all right rows, even unmatched | $R \ ⟖ \ S$ | Pads left with NULL |
| Keep all rows from both | $R \ ⟗ \ S$ | Pads both sides with NULL |
| Filter A using B's existence | $R \ltimes S$ | Doesn't duplicate A's rows |
| Find A rows NOT matching B | $R \triangleright S$ | Inverse of semi-join |
| "For all" queries | $R \div S$ | Universal quantification |
| Break complex query | $T \leftarrow E$ | Readability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Relational Algebra Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Relational Algebra Operations] --> B[Fundamental Operations]
    A --> C[Additional Basic Operations]
    A --> D[Extended Operations]

    B --> B1[Select sigma]
    B --> B2[Project pi]
    B --> B3[Union U]
    B --> B4[Set Difference -]
    B --> B5[Cartesian Product x]
    B --> B6[Rename rho]

    C --> C1[Set Intersection cap]
    C --> C2[Natural Join bowtie]
    C --> C3[Theta Join bowtie theta]
    C --> C4[Assignment larrow]
    C --> C5[Outer Join]
    C5 --> C5a[Left Outer ljoin]
    C5 --> C5b[Right Outer rjoin]
    C5 --> C5c[Full Outer fjoin]

    D --> D1[Semi-Join ltimes]
    D --> D2[Anti-Join triangleright]
    D --> D3[Division div]
    D --> D4[Generalized Projection]
    D --> D5[Aggregate Functions]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Left Outer Join Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DEPARTMENT Table] --> C{Cartesian Product}
    B[EMPLOYEE Table] --> C
    C --> D{Select on DeptID match}
    D --> E[Matched Tuples - 4 rows]
    A --> F[Identify Unmatched: D4 CIVIL]
    F --> G[Pad with NULL values]
    E --> H[Final Result - 5 rows]
    G --> H
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Division Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[pi SID SUPPLIES] --> B[All Supplier IDs: S1, S2, S3]
    A --> C[Cartesian Product with PARTS]
    C --> D[All SID-PID combinations: 6 rows]
    D --> E[Subtract original SUPPLIES]
    E --> F[Find SIDs with missing parts]
    F --> G[pi SID of the difference]
    G --> H[Subtract from original pi SID]
    H --> I[Suppliers supplying ALL parts]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operation Equivalence Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FundOps [Fundamental]
    F1[sigma]
    F2[pi]
    F3[U]
    F4[-]
    F5[x]
    F6[rho]
    end

    subgraph AddOps [Additional]
    A1[cap]
    A2[bowtie]
    A3[bowtie theta]
    A4[ljoin rjoin fjoin]
    A5[ltimes]
    A6[triangleright]
    A7[div]
    end

    A1 -. R - R-S .-> F4
    A2 -. pi sigma x .-> F1
    A2 -. pi sigma x .-> F2
    A2 -. pi sigma x .-> F5
    A3 -. sigma theta x .-> F1
    A3 -. sigma theta x .-> F5
    A4 -. U minus .-> F3
    A4 -. U minus .-> F4
    A5 -. pi bowtie .-> A2
    A6 -. R - pi_R bowtie .-> A2
    A7 -. pi - pi x - R .-> A2
```
