## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Natural Join operation in relational algebra. State the condition under which a Natural Join reduces to a Cartesian Product.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Natural Join** is an additional basic operation, denoted by ⋈, that combines two relations $R$ and $S$ by performing an **equi-join** on all pairs of attributes that share the same name in both relations, and then **removes duplicate attributes** from the result.
>
> **Mathematically:**
>
> $$R \bowtie S = \pi_{\text{attrs}(R) \cup \text{attrs}(S)}\big(\sigma_{R.A_1 = S.A_1 \land \cdots \land R.A_k = S.A_k}(R \times S)\big)$$
>
> where $\{A_1, \ldots, A_k\} = \text{attrs}(R) \cap \text{attrs}(S)$.
>
> **Condition for reduction to Cartesian Product:** When $R$ and $S$ have **no common attribute names** (i.e., $\text{attrs}(R) \cap \text{attrs}(S) = \emptyset$), the natural join degenerates to the Cartesian Product $R \times S$. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S3 CSE]**
*Express the Set Intersection operation $R \cap S$ using only the fundamental relational algebra operations. Explain why this equivalence holds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Set Intersection $R \cap S$ is expressed in terms of the Set Difference operation as:
>
> $$R \cap S = R - (R - S)$$
>
> **Explanation:**
> - $R - S$ returns tuples present in $R$ but **not** in $S$.
> - Subtracting these tuples from $R$ leaves only those tuples of $R$ that are **also** present in $S$.
> - Since $S$ shares the same schema (union-compatibility), the resulting tuples belong to both $R$ and $S$, which is precisely the definition of $R \cap S$.
> - The equivalence holds **only when** $R$ and $S$ are union-compatible (same arity and same attribute domains). (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** For Module 2 questions (14 marks), the student is given an internal choice between two full-length questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Consider the following relations:

- **STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Age, DeptID)**
- **ENROLL(RollNo, CourseID, Grade)**

Write relational algebra expressions using **Additional Basic Operations** for the following queries:

- **(i)** Find the names of students who are enrolled in **at least one** course. **(3 marks)**
- **(ii)** Find the RollNos of students who are enrolled in **all** courses offered (assume COURSE(CourseID) gives all course IDs). **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with an example the difference between:
- **(i)** Natural Join and Equi-Join **(3 marks)**
- **(ii)** Inner Join and Left Outer Join **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i)** Names of students enrolled in at least one course:

$$\pi_{\text{Name}}\big(\text{STUDENT} \bowtie \text{ENROLL}\big)$$

This performs a natural join on `RollNo` (the common attribute) and projects the `Name`. The result contains names of all students who have at least one matching row in `ENROLL`. **(3 marks)**

**(a)(ii)** RollNos of students enrolled in **all** courses:

This is a classic **Division** operation. The query is: find RollNos where the set of courses they are enrolled in is a **superset** of all courses in COURSE.

$$\pi_{\text{RollNo, CourseID}}(\text{ENROLL}) \div \pi_{\text{CourseID}}(\text{COURSE})$$

Equivalently, using the formula:

$$\pi_{\text{RollNo}}(\text{ENROLL}) - \pi_{\text{RollNo}}\Big(\big(\pi_{\text{RollNo}}(\text{ENROLL}) \times \pi_{\text{CourseID}}(\text{COURSE})\big) - \text{ENROLL}\Big)$$

**(4 marks)**

**(b)(i)** Difference between Natural Join and Equi-Join:

| Aspect | Equi-Join | Natural Join |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Definition | Join with a **theta condition** using only `=` operators | Equi-join on **all** common attribute names |
| Duplicate columns | **Preserved** (both `R.A` and `S.A` appear) | **Removed** (only one copy of common attribute) |
| Condition | Specified explicitly by the user | Implicit, on common attribute names |
| Result arity | Sum of arities of both relations | Sum minus number of common attributes |

Example:
- `STUDENT ⋈_{STUDENT.DeptID = DEPARTMENT.DeptID} DEPARTMENT` → Equi-Join (keeps both `DeptID` columns).
- `STUDENT ⋈ DEPARTMENT` → Natural Join (one `DeptID` column in result). **(3 marks)**

**(b)(ii)** Difference between Inner Join and Left Outer Join:

| Aspect | Inner Join (⋈) | Left Outer Join (⟕) |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Unmatched rows | **Discarded** | **Preserved** from the left relation |
| Padding | None | NULLs inserted for missing right-side attributes |
| Cardinality | $\le \min(\vert R \vert, \vert S \vert)$ | $\ge \vert R \vert$ |

Example: Suppose `STUDENT` has 4 rows, `ENROLL` has 3 rows, and one student (E5) is not enrolled.

- `STUDENT ⋈ ENROLL` returns 3 rows (the enrolled students only).
- `STUDENT ⟕ ENROLL` returns 4 rows (E5 included with NULL course/grade). **(4 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Given relations:
- **BOOK(BookID, Title, AuthorID)**
- **AUTHOR(AuthorID, AuthorName, Country)**
- **BORROW(MemberID, BookID, BorrowDate)**

Write relational algebra expressions for:
- **(i)** Find names of authors whose books have been borrowed by **every** member. **(4 marks)**
- **(ii)** Find titles of books that have **never been borrowed**. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** With neat examples, explain the **Assignment operation** and **Semi-Join** in relational algebra. Show how Semi-Join can be expressed using Natural Join and Project. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i)** Names of authors whose books have been borrowed by every member:

Step 1 — Find authors who have written books that have been borrowed:

$$A_1 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{AuthorID}}\big(\text{BOOK} \bowtie \text{BORROW}\big) \bowtie \text{AUTHOR}$$

Step 2 — Use **Division** to find authors whose books have been borrowed by all members:

$$A_2 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{AuthorID, MemberID}}\big(\text{BOOK} \bowtie \text{BORROW}\big) \div \pi_{\text{MemberID}}(\text{MEMBER})$$

Step 3 — Get the names:

$$\pi_{\text{AuthorName}}\big(A_2 \bowtie \text{AUTHOR}\big)$$

**(4 marks)**

**(a)(ii)** Titles of books never borrowed — this is a classic **Anti-Join** application:

$$\pi_{\text{Title}}\big(\text{BOOK} \triangleright \text{BORROW}\big)$$

Equivalently:

$$\pi_{\text{Title}}\big(\text{BOOK}\big) - \pi_{\text{Title}}\big(\text{BOOK} \bowtie \text{BORROW}\big)$$

**(3 marks)**

**(b) Assignment Operation:**

The assignment operation (denoted $\leftarrow$ or $:=$) assigns the result of a relational algebra expression to a **temporary relation variable**. It is a **syntactic convenience** that allows breaking complex queries into smaller, named steps.

Example:

$$T_1 \leftarrow \sigma_{\text{Salary} > 50000}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$

$$T_2 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{Name, DeptID}}(T_1)$$

$$T_3 \leftarrow T_2 \bowtie \text{DEPARTMENT}$$

$$T_4 \leftarrow \pi_{\text{Name, DeptName}}(T_3)$$

This makes the query **readable, debuggable, and modular** — like using variables in algebra. **(3 marks)**

**Semi-Join Operation:**

The semi-join $R \ltimes S$ returns tuples of $R$ that **have at least one matching tuple** in $S$ under the natural join condition. It does **not** include attributes from $S$ in the result.

**Expression using Natural Join and Project:**

$$R \ltimes S = \pi_{\text{attrs}(R)}(R \bowtie S)$$

**Example:** Find names of employees who work in a department that exists:

$$R \ltimes \text{DEPARTMENT} = \pi_{\text{EmpID, Name, DeptID, Salary}}\big(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \text{DEPARTMENT}\big)$$

If an employee's `DeptID` does not exist in `DEPARTMENT`, that employee is **excluded**. Notice that `DeptName` and `Location` (attributes of DEPARTMENT) are **not** in the result. **(4 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Equi-Join with Natural Join (–2 marks):** Many students write "Natural Join" when the question asks for "Equi-Join." Remember: Natural Join **removes duplicate columns**; Equi-Join **keeps them**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Union-Compatibility in Set Operations (–1 to –2 marks):** $\cap, \cup, -$ all require union-compatibility. The examiner checks for this pre-condition.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\bowtie$ (Natural Join) with $\times$ (Cartesian Product) (–2 marks):** Cartesian Product produces **all** combinations with no condition; Natural Join produces only those with **matching common attributes**.
>
> 4. **Division Notation Error (–3 marks):** Writing $R \div S$ as $R / S$ or omitting parentheses in the expansion loses full marks. Always show the complete expansion:
> $$\pi_A(R) - \pi_A\big((\pi_A(R) \times S) - R\big)$$
>
> 5. **Outer Join Symbol Confusion (–1 mark):** Memorize: **L**eft ⟕ (keeps **L**eft), **R**ight ⟖ (keeps **R**ight), **F**ull ⟗ (keeps **F**ull/both).
>
> 6. **Writing Assignment as Implication (–1 mark):** $\leftarrow$ is **assignment**, not $\Rightarrow$ (implication) or $\rightarrow$ (function mapping).
>
> 7. **Missing the "Common Attribute" Warning in Natural Join (–1 mark):** If two relations have **no common attribute**, Natural Join equals Cartesian Product. Mention this if the question allows it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Additional Basic Operations**

- ✅ **Set Intersection ($\cap$):** $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$. Requires union-compatibility. Cardinality $\le \min(\vert R \vert, \vert S \vert)$.
- ✅ **Natural Join ($\bowtie$):** Equi-join on **all common attributes** + **removes duplicates**. Degenerates to $\times$ if no common attributes exist.
- ✅ **Theta Join ($\underset{\theta}{\bowtie}$):** Cartesian product + Select with arbitrary predicate $\theta$.
- ✅ **Equi-Join:** Special case of Theta Join where $\theta$ uses **only `=`**; **keeps** duplicate columns.
- ✅ **Assignment ($\leftarrow$):** Syntactic shorthand to store intermediate results in a temporary variable. **Not** a new operation.
- ✅ **Left Outer Join ($\ ⟕ \ $):** Keeps **all** left tuples; pads right with **NULL**.
- ✅ **Right Outer Join ($\ ⟖ \ $):** Keeps **all** right tuples; pads left with **NULL**.
- ✅ **Full Outer Join ($\ ⟗ \ $):** Keeps **all** tuples of both; pads both sides with **NULL**.
- ✅ **Semi-Join ($\ltimes$):** $\pi_R(R \bowtie S)$. Filters $R$ based on existence in $S$.
- ✅ **Anti-Join ($\triangleright$):** $R - \pi_R(R \bowtie S)$. Returns unmatched $R$ tuples.
- ✅ **Division ($\div$):** Solves *"for all"* / *"every"* queries. Expansion: $\pi_A(R) - \pi_A((\pi_A(R) \times S) - R)$.
- ✅ **Cardinality Hierarchy:** $\vert R \bowtie S \vert \le \vert R \times S \vert$. Outer Join cardinality $\ge$ side being preserved.
- ✅ **Outer Join Symbol Memory Trick:** **L**eft = ⟕ (think "L-shape opening right"), **R**ight = ⟖, **F**ull = ⟗ (both).
- ✅ **All additional operations are expressible** in terms of the six fundamental operations — they are **convenience notations**, not primitives.
- ✅ **Division is the hardest** — practice it on supplier-parts and student-course examples before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Set Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Relational Algebra**, **Set Operations** are binary, set-theoretic operations applied to two compatible relations (tables) that produce a new relation as the result. The five classical set operations — **Union ($\cup$)**, **Intersection ($\cap$)**, **Set Difference ($-$)**, and **Cartesian Product ($\times$)** — form the backbone of query formulation in the relational model proposed by **E. F. Codd**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Union Compatibility (Type Compatibility):** Two relations $R$ and $S$ are said to be **union-compatible** (or *set-theoretic compatible*) if and only if:
> 1. They possess the **same degree** (same number of attributes/columns).
> 2. Corresponding attributes in the same positional order share the **same domain** (data type).
>
> Union compatibility is **mandatory** for $\cup$, $\cap$, and $-$, but **NOT required** for Cartesian Product $\times$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two **transparent plastic sheets (overhead projector transparencies)**, each printed with a list of student names:

- **Sheet A** lists students enrolled in the *Database* course.
- **Sheet B** lists students enrolled in the *Data Structures* course.

If you **stack** both sheets one on top of the other on a light table, you see *all* the names appearing at least once. That is the **UNION** — the combined roster.
If you look only at names that appear on **both** sheets simultaneously (perfectly aligned), that is the **INTERSECTION** — students taking *both* courses.
If you keep names that appear on Sheet A but **remove** any name that also appears on Sheet B, you get the **DIFFERENCE** — students taking *only* Database.
Finally, if you take *every* name from Sheet A and **pair** it with *every* name from Sheet B (all combinations), you get the **CARTESIAN PRODUCT** — a massive sheet showing every possible pairing.

This visual stacking is precisely how **Venn Diagrams** represent relational set operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Relational Algebra treats relations as **sets of tuples** (rows). Hence the set-theoretic rules of *no duplicates* and *unordered elements* apply naturally to its results. The resulting relation from any of these operations is itself a valid relation upon which further operations can be chained.

## 1.3 The Five Set Operations — Quick Glossary

| Symbol | Operation | Arity | Requires Union Compatibility? |
|:------:|:----------|:-----:|:-----------------------------:|
| $\cup$ | Union | Binary | **Yes** |
| $\cap$ | Intersection | Binary | **Yes** |
| $-$ | Set Difference | Binary | **Yes** |
| $\times$ | Cartesian Product | Binary | No |
| $\rho$ | Rename | Unary | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Each Operation

### 2.1.1 UNION ($R \cup S$)

- **Definition:** $R \cup S = \{ t \mid t \in R \ \text{or} \ t \in S \}$.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Verify $R$ and $S$ are union-compatible (same arity, same domains).
  2. Collect all tuples from $R$.
  3. Append all tuples from $S$.
  4. **Eliminate duplicate tuples** (set semantics; SQL `UNION` removes dupes, `UNION ALL` retains them).
- **"Why" it matters:** Models *inclusive* "OR" queries (e.g., "find all students who study CS *or* Maths").

### 2.1.2 INTERSECTION ($R \cap S$)

- **Definition:** $R \cap S = \{ t \mid t \in R \ \text{and} \ t \in S \}$.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Confirm union compatibility.
  2. Retain only those tuples that appear in **both** $R$ and $S$ with identical attribute values.
  3. Result degree equals the degree of $R$ (and $S$).
- **Derived Identity (KTU high-yield):** $R \cap S = R - (R - S) = S - (S - R)$.
- **Real-world utility:** Finding common customers, shared inventory items, mutual friends.

### 2.1.3 SET DIFFERENCE ($R - S$)

- **Definition:** $R - S = \{ t \mid t \in R \ \text{and} \ t \notin S \}$.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Confirm union compatibility.
  2. Take every tuple in $R$.
  3. Discard any tuple $t$ if a *matching* tuple exists in $S$.
  4. **Asymmetric:** $R - S \neq S - R$ in general.
- **Why it matters:** Models "anti-join" style queries — *only in R but not in S*.

### 2.1.4 CARTESIAN PRODUCT ($R \times S$)

- **Definition:** $R \times S = \{ t_R \frown t_S \mid t_R \in R, \ t_S \in S \}$, where $\frown$ denotes tuple concatenation.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. **No compatibility required** (different schemas are allowed).
  2. If $R$ has $n_1$ tuples and $S$ has $n_2$ tuples, result has $n_1 \times n_2$ tuples.
  3. If degree$(R) = k_1$ and degree$(S) = k_2$, result degree $= k_1 + k_2$.
  4. Attribute names in result are qualified: $R.A$, $S.A$ (use **Rename** $\rho$ if schemas share attribute names).
- **Real-world utility:** Foundation for **JOIN** operations. A theta-join is $\sigma_\theta(R \times S)$.

### 2.1.5 RENAME ($\rho$)

- **Definition:** $\rho_{X(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)}(E)$ renames the result of expression $E$ to $X$ with attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$.
- **Purpose:** Resolves attribute-name conflicts in self-joins and Cartesian Products.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Operation | Relational Algebra Form | Tuple Definition | Pre-condition | Result Degree | Result Cardinality |
|:-:|:---------:|:----------------------:|:----------------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | **Union** | $R \cup S$ | $\{t \mid t \in R \ \lor\ t \in S\}$ | Union compatible | $\text{deg}(R)$ | $\vert R\vert + \vert S\vert - \vert R \cap S\vert$ |
| 2 | **Intersection** | $R \cap S$ | $\{t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \in S\}$ | Union compatible | $\text{deg}(R)$ | $\vert R \cap S\vert$ |
| 3 | **Difference** | $R - S$ | $\{t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \notin S\}$ | Union compatible | $\text{deg}(R)$ | $\vert R\vert - \vert R \cap S\vert$ |
| 4 | **Cartesian Product** | $R \times S$ | $\{t_R t_S \mid t_R \in R, t_S \in S\}$ | None | $\text{deg}(R) + \text{deg}(S)$ | $\vert R\vert \times \vert S\vert$ |
| 5 | **Rename** | $\rho_{X(A_1,\ldots,A_n)}(E)$ | N/A | None | $\text{deg}(E)$ | $\vert E\vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Set Identities for Proofs (KTU favourite):**
> - $R \cup S = S \cup R$ (commutative)
> - $R \cap S = S \cap R$ (commutative)
> - $R \cup (S \cup T) = (R \cup S) \cup T$ (associative)
> - $R \cap (S \cap T) = (R \cap S) \cap T$ (associative)
> - $R - (S \cup T) = (R - S) \cap (R - T)$ (De Morgan)
> - $R - (S \cap T) = (R - S) \cup (R - T)$ (De Morgan)
> - $R \cap (S \cup T) = (R \cap S) \cup (R \cap T)$ (distributive)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Set operations underpin **data warehousing ETL pipelines**, **social network friend-recommendation engines** (intersection of friend lists), **inventory reconciliation** (difference between expected and actual stock), and **report generation** in ERP systems. In modern distributed databases, **map-reduce frameworks** implement set operations across partitioned data using shuffle-and-merge strategies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Schemas for Worked Examples

Let us define two relations, $\text{STUDENT\_DB}$ (students of Database course) and $\text{STUDENT\_DS}$ (students of Data Structures course). Both are union-compatible (same attributes: $\text{RollNo}$, $\text{Name}$).

**Table R: STUDENT_DB**
| RollNo | Name |
|:------:|:----:|
| 101 | Anu |
| 102 | Balu |
| 103 | Chitra |
| 104 | Deepak |

**Table S: STUDENT_DS**
| RollNo | Name |
|:------:|:----:|
| 102 | Balu |
| 104 | Deepak |
| 105 | Esther |
| 106 | Farhan |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: $R \cup S$

**Step 1 — Stack the tuples from $R$:**
$\{ (101, \text{Anu}),\ (102, \text{Balu}),\ (103, \text{Chitra}),\ (104, \text{Deepak}) \}$

**Step 2 — Append tuples from $S$, skipping any that already exist (set semantics):**
Add $(105, \text{Esther})$, $(106, \text{Farhan})$. Skip $(102, \text{Balu})$ and $(104, \text{Deepak})$ — already present.

**Step 3 — Final Union Result:**

$$R \cup S = \begin{Bmatrix} (101, \text{Anu}),\ (102, \text{Balu}),\ (103, \text{Chitra}), \\ (104, \text{Deepak}),\ (105, \text{Esther}),\ (106, \text{Farhan}) \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Verification using the formula:** $\vert R\vert + \vert S\vert - \vert R \cap S\vert = 4 + 4 - 2 = 6$ tuples. ✔

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: $R \cap S$

We need tuples that appear in **both** $R$ and $S$.

$$R \cap S = \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \in S \}$$

Comparing row-by-row: only $(102, \text{Balu})$ and $(104, \text{Deepak})$ exist in both.

$$R \cap S = \begin{Bmatrix} (102, \text{Balu}),\ (104, \text{Deepak}) \end{Bmatrix}$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: $R - S$ and $S - R$ (Asymmetry Proof)

For $R - S$: keep tuples of $R$ that are **not** in $S$.

$$R - S = \begin{Bmatrix} (101, \text{Anu}),\ (103, \text{Chitra}) \end{Bmatrix}$$

For $S - R$: keep tuples of $S$ that are **not** in $R$.

$$S - R = \begin{Bmatrix} (105, \text{Esther}),\ (106, \text{Farhan}) \end{Bmatrix}$$

Since $R - S \neq S - R$, **set difference is NOT commutative**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Cartesian Product $R \times S$

**Reference Table A: FACULTY** (degree 2, 2 tuples)
| FID | FName |
|:---:|:-----:|
| F1 | Prof. Roy |
| F2 | Prof. Iyer |

**Reference Table B: COURSE** (degree 2, 3 tuples)
| CID | CName |
|:---:|:-----:|
| C1 | DBMS |
| C2 | OS |
| C3 | CN |

**Cardinality check:** $\vert A\vert \times \vert B\vert = 2 \times 3 = 6$ tuples expected.
**Degree check:** $\text{deg}(A) + \text{deg}(B) = 2 + 2 = 4$ attributes.

**Pairing logic:** for each tuple $a \in A$, concatenate with **every** tuple $b \in B$.

$$
A \times B = \begin{aligned}
& \{ (F1, \text{Roy}, C1, \text{DBMS}),\ (F1, \text{Roy}, C2, \text{OS}),\ (F1, \text{Roy}, C3, \text{CN}), \\
& (F2, \text{Iyer}, C1, \text{DBMS}),\ (F2, \text{Iyer}, C2, \text{OS}),\ (F2, \text{Iyer}, C3, \text{CN}) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Selection Guide

| User Query Intent | Best Set Operation | Reason |
|:------------------|:-------------------|:-------|
| "Students in DB **or** DS" | Union | Inclusive OR; both groups |
| "Students in **both** DB and DS" | Intersection | Logical AND of membership |
| "Students in DB **but not** DS" | Difference $(R - S)$ | Anti-join style |
| "All faculty-course pairings" | Cartesian Product | Enumerate combinations |
| "Resolve schema clash" | Rename $\rho$ | Disambiguate attributes |
| "Students NOT in both" | $(R \cup S) - (R \cap S)$ | Symmetric difference |

## 3.7 Worked Theorem: Proving $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$

We start from the right-hand side and expand using the tuple definition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R - (R - S) &= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \notin (R - S) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ \lnot (t \in R \ \land\ t \notin S) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ (t \notin R \ \lor\ t \in S) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid (t \in R \ \land\ t \notin R) \ \lor\ (t \in R \ \land\ t \in S) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid \text{FALSE} \ \lor\ (t \in R \ \land\ t \in S) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \in S \} \\
&= R \cap S \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Set-Operation Universe — Architecture Block Flow

The diagram below shows how two relations $R$ and $S$ flow into a Set Operation Engine and produce a result relation, depending on the operator selected.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Relation R] --> C{Set Operation Engine}
    B[Relation S] --> C
    C -->|Union| D[R union S]
    C -->|Intersection| E[R intersect S]
    C -->|Difference| F[R minus S]
    C -->|Cartesian| G[R cross S]
    C -->|Rename| H[rho R as X]
    D --> I[Result Relation T]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Downstream Selection / Projection / Join]
```

## 4.2 Venn Diagram of Set Operations (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Universe_U[Universe U]
        R((Set R))
        S((Set S))
    end
    R --- |Union covers both| Out1[Union R plus S]
    S --- Out1
    R --- |Overlap region| Out2[Intersection R cap S]
    S --- Out2
    R --- |R minus S crescent| Out3[Difference R minus S]
    S --- |S minus R crescent| Out4[Difference S minus R]
```

## 4.3 Operation Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Core[Set Theory Foundation] --> U[Union]
    Core --> I[Intersection]
    Core --> D[Difference]
    Core --> CP[Cartesian Product]
    Core --> RN[Rename]
    U --> J[Join Operations]
    I --> J
    D --> J
    CP --> J
    CP --> NJOIN[Natural Join]
    CP --> TJ[Theta Join]
    RN --> SF[Self Join support]
    RN --> CC[Conflict Resolution]
```

## 4.4 Operation Decision Tree (Quick Selector for Students)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Union compatible?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Logical AND or OR?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Need all pairs?}
    Q2 -- OR --> OP1[Use Union]
    Q2 -- AND --> OP2[Use Intersection]
    Q2 -- Only in first --> OP3[Use Difference R minus S]
    Q4 -- Yes --> OP4[Use Cartesian Product]
    Q4 -- Attribute clash --> OP5[Apply Rename first then Cartesian]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define union compatibility of two relations. Why is it required for set operations in relational algebra?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Two relations $R$ and $S$ are said to be **union-compatible** if:
1. Both have the **same number of attributes** (same degree / arity), and
2. Corresponding attributes in the same positional order have **the same domain** (data type).

It is required for **Union ($\cup$)**, **Intersection ($\cap$)**, and **Set Difference ($-$)** so that the tuples of both relations can be meaningfully compared or merged into a single valid relation. **Cartesian Product ($\times$)** does not require union compatibility.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for the two conditions, 1 mark for listing which operations need it, 1 mark for stating that Cartesian Product is exempt.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**With an example, show that set difference in relational algebra is not commutative.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let $R = \{ (1, a),\ (2, b) \}$ and $S = \{ (2, b),\ (3, c) \}$.

Then $R - S = \{ (1, a) \}$ but $S - R = \{ (3, c) \}$.

Since $R - S \neq S - R$, **set difference is not commutative**. Asymmetry arises because the result of $R - S$ is bounded by tuples in $R$, while $S - R$ is bounded by tuples in $S$.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the following relational algebra set operations with suitable examples: **(7 marks)**
1. Union
2. Intersection
3. Set Difference

**(b)** Consider the following two relations and compute $R \cup S$, $R \cap S$, $R - S$, and $R \times S$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

| A | B | C |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | x | p |
| 2 | y | q |
| 3 | z | r |

| B | C | D |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 2 | y | s |
| 4 | w | t |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Union ($R \cup S$):** Combines all tuples from both relations, eliminating duplicates. Used for "OR" queries. Requires union compatibility.
- **Intersection ($R \cap S$):** Returns tuples present in both relations. Used for "AND" queries. Equivalent to $R - (R - S)$.
- **Set Difference ($R - S$):** Returns tuples in $R$ but **not** in $S$. Anti-join style. Non-commutative and non-associative.

*(Each operation: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for example, totalling 7 marks.)*

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**Note:** $R$ has attributes $(A, B, C)$; $T$ has attributes $(B, C, D)$. They share the same *domain* for $B$ and $C$ but the *attribute names* differ in part. For union-compatibility we assume positional domain alignment: $R.B$ with $T.B$, $R.C$ with $T.C$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \cup T &= \{ (1, x, p),\ (2, y, q),\ (3, z, r),\ (4, w, t) \} \\
R \cap T &= \{ (2, y, q) \} \quad \text{(only matching tuple)} \\
R - T &= \{ (1, x, p),\ (3, z, r) \} \\
R \times T &= \text{degree } 5,\ \text{cardinality } 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ tuples}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding $R \times T$ explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \times T = \{ & (A, R.B, R.C, T.B, T.C, D) \mid A \in \{1,2,3\}, (T.B, T.C, D) \in \{(2,y,s),(4,w,t)\} \} \\
= \{ & (1, x, p, 2, y, s),\ (1, x, p, 4, w, t), \\
& (2, y, q, 2, y, s),\ (2, y, q, 4, w, t), \\
& (3, z, r, 2, y, s),\ (3, z, r, 4, w, t) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define Cartesian Product. Given $R(A, B)$ with 3 tuples and $S(C, D)$ with 4 tuples, what are the degree and cardinality of $R \times S$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove using set identities that: $R - (S \cap T) = (R - S) \cup (R - T)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cartesian Product (7 marks):**

**Definition (3 marks):** $R \times S = \{ t_R \frown t_S \mid t_R \in R,\ t_S \in S \}$ where $\frown$ denotes tuple concatenation. The result contains every possible combination of a tuple from $R$ with a tuple from $S$.

**Degree (2 marks):** $\text{deg}(R \times S) = \text{deg}(R) + \text{deg}(S) = 2 + 2 = 4$ attributes, namely $(A, B, C, D)$.

**Cardinality (2 marks):** $\vert R \times S\vert = \vert R\vert \times \vert S\vert = 3 \times 4 = 12$ tuples.

**(b) Proof of $R - (S \cap T) = (R - S) \cup (R - T)$ (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} &= (R - S) \cup (R - T) \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \notin S \} \cup \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \notin T \} \\
&= \{ t \mid (t \in R \ \land\ t \notin S) \ \lor\ (t \in R \ \land\ t \notin T) \} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ (t \notin S \ \lor\ t \notin T) \} \quad \text{(factoring out } t \in R\text{)} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ \lnot(t \in S \ \land\ t \in T) \} \quad \text{(De Morgan's law)} \\
&= \{ t \mid t \in R \ \land\ t \notin (S \cap T) \} \\
&= R - (S \cap T) = \text{LHS} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark per significant step, with the final equality carrying the remaining mark.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting union-compatibility check:** Always state it before computing $\cup$, $\cap$, $-$. Examiners award 1 mark just for this precondition.
> 2. **Confusing tuple-set semantics with multiset semantics:** Relational Algebra uses **sets** (no duplicates). Writing duplicate tuples in your answer costs marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to qualify attribute names in $R \times S$:** If $R$ and $S$ share attribute names, use $\rho$ to rename. Writing ambiguous attributes is a common deduction point.
> 4. **Mistaking commutativity of difference:** $R - S \neq S - R$ — writing the wrong order is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Cardinality formula errors:** Remember intersection result cardinality is $\vert R \cap S\vert$, **not** $\min(\vert R\vert, \vert S\vert)$. The latter is only an upper bound.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Set operations** in relational algebra act on relations considered as **sets of tuples**.
- **Union, Intersection, Difference** require **union compatibility**; **Cartesian Product** does not.
- **Cartesian Product** has no precondition, but its result degree is the **sum** of operand degrees, and cardinality is the **product** of operand cardinalities.
- **Union and Intersection are commutative and associative**; **Difference is neither**.
- The **Rename** ($\rho$) operator is essential to disambiguate schemas before Cartesian Product or self-join.
- Key set identities (De Morgan, distributive, derived) are **favourite KTU 14-mark proof questions** — memorise them.
- Intersection can be **derived** from difference: $R \cap S = R - (R - S)$.
- Always express queries in **relational algebra notation first**, then optionally translate to SQL for completeness.
- Result of any set operation is itself a **valid relation**, enabling **composition** (e.g., $\sigma_{A > 5}(R \cup S)$).
- Cardinality bounds: $\vert R \cup S\vert \leq \vert R\vert + \vert S\vert$, and $\vert R \cap S\vert \leq \min(\vert R\vert, \vert S\vert)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Null Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Null Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the relational data model formalized by **Edgar F. Codd** (the father of relational databases), a **NULL** represents the *absence of any data value* in a field of a tuple. It is a marker indicating that a value is **missing, unknown, inapplicable, or not yet defined**, and is fundamentally distinct from the integer zero, the empty string `''`, or the boolean value `FALSE`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *“The null value is a special marker used in SQL to indicate that a data value does not exist in the database. For nullable columns, NULL signifies that the value is either unknown or undefined.”*

### 1.2 Why NULL Exists — The Real-World Necessity

Relational databases must store **incomplete real-world information**. Consider practical situations:

| Real-World Scenario | Why a Value is Missing |
|---|---|
| A new employee has not yet been allotted a **department code** | The information is *unknown* (pending assignment). |
| A customer declines to provide a **phone number** | The information is *withheld / undefined*. |
| A vehicle’s **airbag count** is being recorded for a bicycle | The attribute is *inapplicable* to the entity. |
| A patient’s **test result** is awaited from a laboratory | The information is *not yet available* (time-deferred). |

A database that forces a non-NULL placeholder (like `0` or `''`) would **semantically corrupt** the data, because `0` carries a legitimate quantitative meaning that is entirely different from “unknown.”

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The “Empty Box” Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a registration form with a field labelled *“Spouse’s Name.”* For an unmarried person, that box is **left blank**. That blank box is the NULL. It is **not** the same as writing the word `“none”` or drawing a `“0”` — those would assert information. The blank simply declares: *“There is no information to place here.”*

### 1.4 The Three Semantics of NULL (Per Codd’s Specification)

Codd distinguished three distinct meanings, and modern SQL collapses all three into a single NULL marker:

1. **Value Unknown** — A legitimate attribute exists, but the current data is not known (e.g., date of birth not recorded).
2. **Value Not Yet Supplied** — A deferred entry (e.g., end-date of an ongoing project).
3. **Attribute Not Applicable** — The attribute has no meaning for the tuple (e.g., *spouse_name* for an unmarried person).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** NULL is a **state**, not a **value**. This is why NULL cannot participate in ordinary comparison operators (`=`, `<`, `>`) — comparisons evaluate to **UNKNOWN**, a third logical state, not TRUE or FALSE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Valued Logic (3VL) — The Logical Backbone

Classical Boolean logic operates over the set $\{ \text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE} \}$. Because NULL is a non-value, the relational model **extends** this to a **three-valued logic (3VL)**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_3 = \{ \text{TRUE (T)}, \quad \text{FALSE (F)}, \quad \text{UNKNOWN (U)} \}
$$

The logical connective outcomes under 3VL are formally tabulated in §2.3. The critical rule is:

> A WHERE clause returns a row **only if the predicate evaluates to TRUE**. Rows evaluating to **FALSE or UNKNOWN are discarded**.

### 2.2 Classification of NULL Behavior Across SQL Operations

SQL handles NULL according to strict ANSI/ISO rules, summarized below:

#### 2.2.1 NULL in Arithmetic Expressions
Any arithmetic operation involving NULL yields **NULL**. This is called **NULL propagation**.

$$
\forall\, a \in \mathbb{R}, \quad a \, \text{op} \, \text{NULL} = \text{NULL}, \quad \text{op} \in \{ +, -, \times, \div \}
$$

#### 2.2.2 NULL in Comparison Expressions
All ordinary comparison operators return **UNKNOWN**, never TRUE or FALSE, when either operand is NULL.

$$
\text{NULL} = x \;\Rightarrow\; \text{UNKNOWN}, \qquad \text{NULL} \neq x \;\Rightarrow\; \text{UNKNOWN}
$$

#### 2.2.3 NULL in Aggregate Functions
- `COUNT(column)` **ignores** NULLs.
- `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX` **ignore** NULLs.
- `COUNT(*)` counts **all** rows including those with NULLs.

#### 2.2.4 NULL in DISTINCT and GROUP BY
`NULL` is treated as **one unique value** for `DISTINCT` and `GROUP BY` — multiple NULLs in a column collapse to a single group.

#### 2.2.5 NULL in JOINs
- `INNER JOIN` **eliminates** rows where the join key is NULL on either side.
- `LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN` **preserves** rows with NULL keys, producing NULLs in non-matching columns.
- **NULL ≠ NULL** in joins, so NULL-keyed rows do not match other NULL-keyed rows.

### 2.3 Truth Tables for 3VL — KTU Mandatory Tables

| A | B | A AND B | A OR B | NOT A |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | T | F |
| T | F | F | T | F |
| T | U | **U** | T | F |
| F | T | F | T | T |
| F | F | F | F | T |
| F | U | **F** | **U** | T |
| U | T | **U** | T | **U** |
| U | F | **F** | **U** | **U** |
| U | U | **U** | **U** | **U** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Commit the **AND-OR-NOT truth tables** with UNKNOWN to memory. KTU question papers frequently ask: *“Evaluate the expression `NULL AND TRUE`”* — the answer is **UNKNOWN**, not FALSE.

### 2.4 Functions for Explicit NULL Handling

| Function | Purpose | Syntax | Result for `NULL` Argument |
|---|---|---|---|
| `IS NULL` | Test for NULL | `col IS NULL` | `TRUE` if NULL, else `FALSE` |
| `IS NOT NULL` | Test for non-NULL | `col IS NOT NULL` | `TRUE` if non-NULL, else `FALSE` |
| `COALESCE(a, b, …)` | First non-NULL | `COALESCE(x, y, 0)` | `y` if `x` is NULL |
| `NULLIF(a, b)` | NULL if equal | `NULLIF(x, y)` | `NULL` if `x = y`, else `x` |
| `NVL(a, b)` *(Oracle)* | Replace NULL | `NVL(x, 0)` | `0` if `x` is NULL |
| `ISNULL(a, b)` *(SQL Server)* | Replace NULL | `ISNULL(x, 0)` | `0` if `x` is NULL |
| `IFNULL(a, b)` *(MySQL)* | Replace NULL | `IFNULL(x, 0)` | `0` if `x` is NULL |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Rule / Formula | Mathematical / Logical Form | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NULL propagation in arithmetic | $a \oplus \text{NULL} = \text{NULL}$ | Very High |
| 2 | NULL propagation in string concat | `s \mid\mid \text{NULL} = \text{NULL}` *(Oracle)* / `CONCAT(s, NULL) = s` *(MySQL)* | High |
| 3 | NULL in comparison | $\text{NULL} \theta\, x = \text{UNKNOWN}$ | Very High |
| 4 | NOT of UNKNOWN | $\neg \text{UNKNOWN} = \text{UNKNOWN}$ | Very High |
| 5 | WHERE filters UNKNOWN | $\text{result} = \{ r \;\vert\; P(r) = \text{TRUE} \}$ | Very High |
| 6 | COUNT(\*) vs COUNT(col) | $\text{COUNT(*)} \geq \text{COUNT(col)}$ | High |
| 7 | DISTINCT on NULL | $\mid \{ \text{NULL} \} \mid = 1$ | Medium |
| 8 | Aggregate NULL behaviour | $\text{AVG} = \frac{\sum_{x \neq \text{NULL}} x}{\mid \{ x \neq \text{NULL} \} \mid}$ | High |
| 9 | CHECK constraint with NULL | `CHECK (salary > 0)` — NULL passes *(UNKNOWN ≠ FALSE)* | High |
| 10 | UNIQUE with NULLs | SQL allows **multiple** NULLs in UNIQUE columns | Medium |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

NULL handling governs **data-integrity decisions** in production systems:

- **Banking**: A NULL in `account_closure_date` means the account is *still active* — a critical business rule.
- **Healthcare EMRs**: NULL `test_result` triggers re-order workflows, not deletion of records.
- **IoT Telemetry**: NULL sensor readings represent missing samples, addressed via interpolation or time-series imputation algorithms.
- **E-commerce**: NULL `discount_applied` defaults to `0` via `COALESCE` to prevent arithmetic faults in invoice generation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the rigorous evaluation of expressions involving NULLs. These derivations are **board-favourite derivations** and are tested every year under KTU Module 2.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Evaluating `NULL AND (TRUE OR FALSE)`

> **Problem:** Evaluate the Boolean expression `NULL AND (TRUE OR FALSE)` under three-valued logic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \text{NULL} \;\land\; (\text{TRUE} \lor \text{FALSE}) \\[4pt]
  &= \text{NULL} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \quad \text{(by identity law: } x \lor \text{F} = x\text{)} \\[4pt]
  &= \text{UNKNOWN} \quad \text{(row 3 of AND truth table: T, U} \to \text{U)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $E = \text{UNKNOWN}$. The row will be **excluded** from any `WHERE E` filter.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Evaluating `NOT (NULL OR FALSE)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \neg\,(\text{NULL} \lor \text{FALSE}) \\[4pt]
  &= \neg\,\text{UNKNOWN} \quad \text{(row 6 of OR table: F, U} \to \text{U)} \\[4pt]
  &= \text{UNKNOWN} \quad \text{(NOT of UNKNOWN is UNKNOWN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Reasoning:** Many students wrongly simplify `(NULL OR FALSE)` to FALSE. The rule is that **UNKNOWN dominates FALSE** in an OR operation — the result remains UNKNOWN.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Evaluating `(NULL = NULL)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \text{NULL} = \text{NULL} \\[4pt]
  &= \text{UNKNOWN} \quad \text{(definition: comparison with NULL yields UNKNOWN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most tested NULL concept in KTU** — the rule *“NULL is never equal to itself”* is counter-intuitive and examiners exploit it.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Verifying De Morgan’s Law under 3VL

Classical De Morgan’s law: $\neg(A \land B) \equiv (\neg A) \lor (\neg B)$. Verify for $A = \text{NULL},\; B = \text{TRUE}$.

**LHS:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg(A \land B) &= \neg(\text{NULL} \land \text{TRUE}) = \neg(\text{UNKNOWN}) = \text{UNKNOWN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**RHS:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(\neg A) \lor (\neg B) &= \neg(\text{NULL}) \;\lor\; \neg(\text{TRUE}) \\
&= \text{UNKNOWN} \lor \text{FALSE} \\
&= \text{UNKNOWN}
\end{aligned}
$$

LHS = RHS = UNKNOWN ✔ — **De Morgan’s law holds** under 3VL (unlike the classical two-valued case where every proposition is decidable).

### 3.5 Derivation 5: NULL Behaviour in Aggregate AVG

Consider the relation:

| emp_id | salary |
|---|---|
| 1 | 5000 |
| 2 | NULL |
| 3 | 7000 |

Compute `AVG(salary)`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AVG(salary)} &= \frac{\sum_{x \neq \text{NULL}} x}{\mid \{ x \neq \text{NULL} \} \mid} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5000 + 7000}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{12000}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= 6000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The NULL salary is **excluded** from both the numerator and the denominator. If the DBMS had substituted NULL as 0, the answer would incorrectly be $4000$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of NULL on Common SQL Constructs

| SQL Construct | NULL Behaviour | Example Snippet | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `WHERE x = NULL` | UNKNOWN — no row returned | `SELECT * FROM emp WHERE sal = NULL;` | Empty set |
| `WHERE x IS NULL` | TRUE for NULL rows | `SELECT * FROM emp WHERE sal IS NULL;` | All NULL-salary rows |
| `SUM(col)` | NULLs ignored | `SELECT SUM(sal) FROM emp;` | Sum of non-NULL |
| `COUNT(*)` | All rows counted | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM emp;` | Total tuples |
| `DISTINCT col` | NULL treated as one value | `SELECT DISTINCT dept FROM emp;` | Includes `NULL` once |
| `INNER JOIN ON a.x = b.y` | NULL-keys do not match | `emp JOIN dept ON emp.dept_id = dept.id;` | NULL-dept rows dropped |
| `ORDER BY col ASC` | NULLs placed at **start** *(Oracle)* or **end** *(MySQL/Postgres)* | `ORDER BY sal;` | Implementation-defined |
| `UNIQUE constraint` | Allows multiple NULLs (ANSI) | Schema-level | NULL permitted repeatedly |

### 3.7 Worked-Out SQL Examples (Kerala University Pattern)

**Example 1:** A relation `STUDENT(rollno, name, cgpa)`. What does `SELECT * FROM student WHERE cgpa > 8 OR cgpa <= 8` return?

> A student with `cgpa = NULL` evaluates both predicates to **UNKNOWN**, so the OR yields **UNKNOWN** and the row is excluded. Thus the query misses all students with unknown CGPAs — a classic **NULL trap**.

**Example 2:** Evaluate the output cardinality of `SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(salary), COUNT(DISTINCT department) FROM employee;` given a relation with 10 rows, 2 NULL salaries, and 3 NULL departments among 5 distinct departments.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COUNT(*)} &= 10 \\
\text{COUNT(salary)} &= 10 - 2 = 8 \\
\text{COUNT(DISTINCT department)} &= 4 \quad \text{(the 5 distinct non-NULL values collapse; NULLs are treated as one distinct marker but contribute no row to non-NULL distinct)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction:** Since `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` excludes NULLs by ANSI definition, we count only the 5 non-NULL distinct values: $\text{COUNT(DISTINCT department)} = 5$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Valued Logic Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SQL Predicate Evaluation] --> B{Does operand contain NULL?}
    B -- No --> C[Classical Boolean Logic]
    B -- Yes --> D[Enter Three-Valued Logic 3VL]
    C --> E[Result TRUE]
    C --> F[Result FALSE]
    D --> G[Compute with UNKNOWN propagation]
    G --> H{Final logical value}
    H -- TRUE --> I[Row INCLUDED in result]
    H -- FALSE --> J[Row EXCLUDED]
    H -- UNKNOWN --> K[Row EXCLUDED]
    I --> L[Output Result Set]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The single most important insight is that **both FALSE and UNKNOWN filter out rows**. Only TRUE retains a row. This explains why `WHERE salary = NULL` always returns an empty set — the predicate is UNKNOWN, not TRUE.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — NULL Handling Decision Tree in SQL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Column Value in Tuple] --> B{Is value NULL?}
    B -- No --> C[Use value in computation]
    B -- Yes --> D{Operation type?}
    D -- E1[Arithmetic] --> E1a[Result is NULL]
    D -- E2[Comparison =, less-than, greater-than] --> E2a[Result is UNKNOWN]
    D -- E3[Logical AND OR NOT] --> E3a[Apply 3VL truth table]
    D -- E4[Aggregate SUM AVG COUNT] --> E4a[Skip NULL row]
    D -- E5[GROUP BY DISTINCT] --> E5a[NULL forms single group]
    C --> Z[Final Output]
    E1a --> Z
    E2a --> Z
    E3a --> Z
    E4a --> Z
    E5a --> Z
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of NULL-Aware Query Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User SQL Query] --> B[Parser]
    B --> C[Query Optimizer]
    C --> D[Execution Engine]
    D --> E{NULL Handling Subsystem}
    E -- E1[Predicates] --> F1[3VL Evaluator]
    E -- E2[Aggregates] --> F2[NULL Skip Filter]
    E -- E3[Joins] --> F3[Outer Join Marker]
    E -- E4[Constraints] --> F4[NULL-aware CHECK]
    F1 --> G[Result Set]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
```

> This diagram mirrors the **internal layered architecture** used by RDBMS engines (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL) when processing a query that may encounter NULL values. The **3VL Evaluator** module is the heart of NULL-aware predicate evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a, Module 2]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *What is a NULL value in SQL? How is it different from zero and an empty string?*

**Model Answer:**

A NULL value in SQL is a special marker that indicates the **absence of a data value** in a column. It is used when the value is **unknown, undefined, or inapplicable** for that particular tuple.

- **NULL vs 0**: `0` is a legitimate numeric value representing a quantity; NULL represents the *non-existence* of any value.
- **NULL vs empty string `''`**: An empty string is a *valid string of length zero*; NULL is not even a string — it is a state.
- **NULL vs FALSE**: FALSE is a Boolean truth value; NULL is not a Boolean — comparisons with NULL yield the third logical state **UNKNOWN**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> Marks are frequently lost by students writing *“NULL means 0”* or *“NULL is an empty string”*. Use precise language: **“NULL is a marker indicating missing or inapplicable information; it is neither 0 nor an empty string.”**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.b, Module 2]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Explain three-valued logic in SQL. How does it affect the result of a WHERE clause?*

**Model Answer:**

SQL extends classical two-valued Boolean logic into a **three-valued logic (3VL)** with the truth values **TRUE, FALSE, and UNKNOWN**. The UNKNOWN value arises whenever a predicate involves a NULL operand, because the predicate’s truth cannot be determined.

**Effect on WHERE clause:** A row is returned by a WHERE clause **only if the predicate evaluates to TRUE**. Rows evaluating to either FALSE or UNKNOWN are filtered out.

**Example:** Consider `salary > 50000` when `salary = NULL`. The predicate yields UNKNOWN, so the row is **excluded** from the result, even though logically one might expect it to either match or not match.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Q10, Module 2]**

#### ⭐ Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Discuss the different meanings of NULL in relational databases. Explain how SQL handles NULL values in arithmetic, comparison, and aggregate operations with suitable examples.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Consider the following table:*

| emp_id | name | dept_id | salary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Anu | 10 | 50000 |
| 2 | Balu | NULL | 60000 |
| 3 | Chitra | 20 | NULL |
| 4 | Deepak | NULL | NULL |
| 5 | Esha | 30 | 70000 |

*Write SQL queries for the following and explain the output:*
1. `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM emp;`
2. `SELECT COUNT(salary) FROM emp;`
3. `SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp;`
4. `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT dept_id) FROM emp;`
5. `SELECT * FROM emp WHERE dept_id = NULL;`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — The Meanings of NULL:**

Codd specified three distinct semantics of NULL, all represented by the same NULL marker in SQL:

1. **Value Unknown** — the attribute exists but its value is not yet known.
2. **Value Not Yet Available** — the value will be supplied later (time-deferred).
3. **Attribute Inapplicable** — the attribute has no meaning for the entity.

**Behaviour of NULL in Operations:**

| Operation | NULL Behaviour | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic (`+ − × ÷`) | NULL propagation | `5000 + NULL` | NULL |
| Comparison (`= < > ≤ ≥ ≠`) | Yields UNKNOWN | `NULL = 5` | UNKNOWN |
| Logical (`AND OR NOT`) | 3VL evaluation | `NULL AND TRUE` | UNKNOWN |
| Aggregate (`SUM AVG MIN MAX`) | NULL rows ignored | `AVG(5000, NULL, 7000)` | 6000 |
| `COUNT(*)` | Counts all rows | 5 rows | 5 |
| `DISTINCT` / `GROUP BY` | NULL = one group | `DISTINCT dept_id` | includes NULL once |

**Part (b) — Query Solutions:**

1. `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM emp;`  
   $\Rightarrow$ **5** (counts all 5 rows including those with NULLs)

2. `SELECT COUNT(salary) FROM emp;`  
   $\Rightarrow$ **3** (rows 3 and 4 have NULL salary and are excluded; 5 − 2 = 3)

3. `SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp;`  
   $\Rightarrow$ $\dfrac{50000 + 60000 + 70000}{3} = \dfrac{180000}{3} = \mathbf{60000}$

4. `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT dept_id) FROM emp;`  
   $\Rightarrow$ **3** (distinct non-NULL values are 10, 20, 30; the two NULLs collapse to a single marker but are excluded from `DISTINCT` count by ANSI standard)

5. `SELECT * FROM emp WHERE dept_id = NULL;`  
   $\Rightarrow$ **Empty set** (no rows returned — comparison with NULL yields UNKNOWN, which is filtered out)

---

#### ⭐ Question B — (14 Marks)  *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the concept of three-valued logic. Construct the truth tables for the logical operators AND, OR, and NOT under three-valued logic. Show with an example how De Morgan’s laws hold in 3VL.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Write short notes on:*
1. `COALESCE()` and `NULLIF()` functions
2. NULL propagation in `INNER JOIN` and `OUTER JOIN`
3. Behaviour of `CHECK` and `UNIQUE` constraints on NULL values
4. The statement *“NULL is never equal to itself”* — justify with an example.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Three-Valued Logic and Truth Tables:**

Three-valued logic (3VL) extends classical Boolean logic to handle the **UNKNOWN** truth value that arises whenever a NULL is involved in a logical expression. The truth tables are:

**AND Truth Table:**

| A | B | A AND B |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| T | U | **U** |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | F |
| F | U | **F** |
| U | T | **U** |
| U | F | **F** |
| U | U | **U** |

**OR Truth Table:**

| A | B | A OR B |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | T |
| T | U | **T** |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | F |
| F | U | **U** |
| U | T | **T** |
| U | F | **U** |
| U | U | **U** |

**NOT Truth Table:**

| A | NOT A |
|---|---|
| T | F |
| F | T |
| U | **U** |

**De Morgan’s Verification:** $\neg(A \land B) \equiv (\neg A) \lor (\neg B)$ for $A = \text{NULL}, B = \text{TRUE}$:

LHS: $\neg(\text{NULL} \land \text{TRUE}) = \neg(\text{UNKNOWN}) = \text{UNKNOWN}$  
RHS: $\neg(\text{NULL}) \lor \neg(\text{TRUE}) = \text{UNKNOWN} \lor \text{FALSE} = \text{UNKNOWN}$  
**LHS = RHS ✔** — De Morgan’s law is preserved.

**Part (b) — Short Notes:**

1. **`COALESCE(a, b, c, ...)`**: Returns the **first non-NULL** argument. Example: `COALESCE(phone, mobile, 'N/A')` returns `'N/A'` if both phone and mobile are NULL.  
   **`NULLIF(a, b)`**: Returns NULL if `a = b`, otherwise returns `a`. Used to map specific values to NULL.

2. **NULL in JOINs**:  
   - `INNER JOIN` discards rows where the join key is NULL on either side (since `NULL = NULL` is UNKNOWN, not TRUE).  
   - `LEFT OUTER JOIN` preserves left-side rows with NULL keys, padding the right side with NULLs.  
   - `FULL OUTER JOIN` preserves NULL-keyed rows from **both** sides.

3. **CHECK and UNIQUE constraints with NULL**:  
   - `CHECK (salary > 0)` — a NULL salary yields UNKNOWN, which is **not FALSE**, so the constraint is **satisfied**. A NULL can thus bypass a CHECK constraint.  
   - `UNIQUE` on a column allows **multiple NULLs** because each NULL is treated as distinct from every other (in ANSI SQL).

4. **“NULL is never equal to itself”**: This is a direct consequence of the rule that any comparison involving NULL returns UNKNOWN rather than TRUE. Therefore `NULL = NULL` evaluates to UNKNOWN, not TRUE. To test for NULL, SQL provides the special `IS NULL` operator, which is the **only correct way** to test for the NULL state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing `WHERE col = NULL`** — returns empty set; correct form is `WHERE col IS NULL`. Full **2-mark penalty** in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 2. **Claiming “NULL means 0 or empty string”** — definition is **wrong**; cost: 1 of the 3 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Computing `AVG` by including NULL as 0** — cost: 1 mark in aggregate questions.
> 4. **Forgetting that `CHECK` constraints allow NULLs** — cost: 1 mark in constraint-based questions.
> 5. **Writing `NULL = NULL` is TRUE** — cost: 1–2 marks; the correct answer is **UNKNOWN**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Rapid Revision — NULL Values (Module 2)**

- ⭐ NULL is a **state**, not a value — it indicates *missing, unknown, or inapplicable* data.
- ⭐ NULL is **distinct from 0, empty string, and FALSE**.
- ⭐ Three-valued logic (3VL) has truth values **{TRUE, FALSE, UNKNOWN}**.
- ⭐ Arithmetic with NULL always yields **NULL** (NULL propagation).
- ⭐ Comparison with NULL always yields **UNKNOWN**, never TRUE or FALSE.
- ⭐ Logical operators use the **3VL truth tables** (memorize the AND/OR/NOT tables).
- ⭐ `WHERE` clause retains rows only when the predicate is **TRUE** — both FALSE and UNKNOWN filter rows out.
- ⭐ Use **`IS NULL`** and **`IS NOT NULL`** — never `=` or `!=` with NULL.
- ⭐ Aggregates (`SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `COUNT(col)`) **ignore NULLs**; only `COUNT(*)` counts them.
- ⭐ `DISTINCT` and `GROUP BY` treat NULL as **one unique marker**.
- ⭐ `COALESCE(a, b, …)` returns the first non-NULL; `NULLIF(a, b)` returns NULL if `a = b`.
- ⭐ `CHECK` constraints **pass** for NULLs (UNKNOWN ≠ FALSE).
- ⭐ `UNIQUE` constraint **allows multiple NULLs** in ANSI SQL.
- ⭐ `INNER JOIN` excludes NULL-key rows; `OUTER JOIN` preserves them.
- ⭐ `NULL = NULL` returns **UNKNOWN** — the famous *“NULL is not equal to itself”* rule.
- ⭐ De Morgan’s laws and other Boolean identities **still hold** under 3VL.
- ⭐ `ORDER BY` placement of NULLs (start vs. end) is **DBMS-specific**.
- ⭐ Always use **`COALESCE`** or **`ISNULL/NVL`** to substitute a default for NULLs in computation.
- ⭐ The 14-mark Module 2 question on NULLs is typically split as **7 marks theory + 7 marks SQL/table-based** evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) and Structured Query Language (SQL), **Aggregate Functions** are a class of built-in scalar-to-set functions that operate on a set of tuples (rows) from a relation and return a single, summarized value. They are formally defined as functions that perform a calculation on a **multiset of values** and return a single value, conforming to the operations of the **Relational Algebra** under the extended aggregate operators (γ — Gamma operator).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned syllabus for PBERT504), aggregate functions are positioned under the structured query language hierarchy, specifically used in conjunction with the `SELECT` statement's column projection, the `GROUP BY` clause for partitioning, and the `HAVING` clause for post-aggregation filtering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Importance):**
> Aggregate functions perform a calculation on a set of values and return a single value. The five standard aggregate functions mandated by the KTU syllabus (ISO/IEC 9075 SQL standard) are:
> 1. `COUNT()`
> 2. `SUM()`
> 3. `AVG()`
> 4. `MAX()`
> 5. `MIN()`

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **class teacher** with a register containing marks of **60 students** in a Database Management Systems exam. Instead of looking at each student's individual mark, you want to answer summary questions like:
- *"How many students appeared for the exam?"* → This is the **COUNT** function.
- *"What is the total sum of all marks?"* → This is the **SUM** function.
- *"What is the class average mark?"* → This is the **AVG** function.
- *"Who scored the highest mark?"* → This is the **MAX** function.
- *"Who scored the lowest mark?"* → This is the **MIN** function.

Now, extend this analogy: Suppose you want these statistics **separately for each section** (Section A, Section B, Section C). You would first **partition/group** the students by their section, and then **apply** the aggregate function to each group. This partitioning operation in SQL is performed by the **`GROUP BY`** clause.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Aggregate functions **collapse** many rows into one. They are the mathematical "reducers" of the database world — taking a bag of inputs and producing a single summarized output.

### Standard Aggregate Functions — The Big Five

| Function | Return Type | Applicable To | NULL Behavior |
|----------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| `COUNT(*)` | Integer | All rows including NULLs | Counts rows, not columns |
| `COUNT(column)` | Integer | Non-NULL values | Ignores NULLs |
| `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` | Integer | Unique non-NULL values | Ignores NULLs and duplicates |
| `SUM(column)` | Numeric (or auto-promoted) | Numeric columns only | Ignores NULLs |
| `AVG(column)` | Numeric (or auto-promoted) | Numeric columns only | Ignores NULLs |
| `MAX(column)` | Same as column type | Numeric, String, Date | Ignores NULLs |
| `MIN(column)` | Same as column type | Numeric, String, Date | Ignores NULLs |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Frequency Trap:** The single most tested pitfall in KTU examinations is the difference between `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT(column)`, and `COUNT(DISTINCT column)`. A failure to recognize NULL handling in these variants can cost a full 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Classification of Aggregate Functions (Per KTU Taxonomy)

**Based on the output cardinality:**

1. **Single-Row (Scalar) Aggregates:** Operate on the entire result set and return one value (e.g., `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employee;`).
2. **Grouped Aggregates:** Operate partition-wise using `GROUP BY` (e.g., `SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) FROM Employee GROUP BY Dept;`).
3. **Filtered Aggregates:** Apply a `WHERE` clause before aggregation and/or a `HAVING` clause after aggregation.

**Based on data type compatibility:**

1. **Numeric Aggregates:** `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN` (on numeric columns).
2. **Universal Aggregates:** `COUNT`, `MAX`, `MIN` (on strings, dates, numerics).
3. **Statistical Aggregates (Optional, not in KTU core):** `STDDEV`, `VARIANCE` (vendor-specific).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Aggregate Query

The execution of an SQL aggregate query follows a strict logical processing order. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this order. The order is:

**Logical Query Processing Order (per ISO SQL Standard):**

1. **FROM** → Identifies source tables and applies JOINs.
2. **WHERE** → Filters individual rows **before** aggregation.
3. **GROUP BY** → Partitions the filtered rows into groups.
4. **HAVING** → Filters the **groups** (post-aggregation).
5. **SELECT** → Computes the aggregate expressions and projects columns.
6. **ORDER BY** → Sorts the final result set.
7. **LIMIT / OFFSET** → Restricts the number of returned rows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this order matter?** The `WHERE` clause operates on **raw tuples**, while the `HAVING` clause operates on **aggregated groups**. You cannot use an aggregate function inside a `WHERE` clause in standard SQL — this is a foundational KTU question.

### 2.2 Detailed Function Semantics

**A. COUNT Function**

$$ \text{COUNT}(*) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 = n \quad \text{(where } n = \text{number of rows in the relation)} $$

$$ \text{COUNT}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(A_i \neq \text{NULL}) $$

$$ \text{COUNT}(\text{DISTINCT } A) = \vert \{A_i \mid A_i \neq \text{NULL}\} \vert $$

Where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function returning **1** if the condition holds, **0** otherwise.

**B. SUM Function**

$$ \text{SUM}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \quad \text{(ignoring NULL values)} $$

**C. AVG Function**

$$ \text{AVG}(A) = \frac{\text{SUM}(A)}{\text{COUNT}(A)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(A_i \neq \text{NULL})} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The denominator of `AVG` is `COUNT(A)`, **not** `COUNT(*)`. This means NULLs in the column reduce the denominator, which can subtly change the average compared to treating NULL as 0.

**D. MAX and MIN Functions**

$$ \text{MAX}(A) = \max\{A_i \mid A_i \neq \text{NULL}\} $$

$$ \text{MIN}(A) = \min\{A_i \mid A_i \neq \text{NULL}\} $$

These functions perform lexicographic comparison for strings and chronological comparison for date/time types.

### 2.3 The Relational Algebra Foundation

In extended Relational Algebra (proposed by Codd), aggregate functions are expressed using the **Gamma (γ) operator**:

$$ \gamma_{F_1(A_1), F_2(A_2), \ldots, F_n(A_n); \, G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_m}(R) $$

Where:
- $R$ is the input relation
- $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_m$ are the **grouping attributes** (corresponds to `GROUP BY`)
- $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$ are the **aggregate functions** applied to attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$

**SQL Translation Example:**

$$ \gamma_{\text{COUNT(*)}, \text{AVG(Salary)}; \, \text{Dept}}(\text{Employee}) $$

Translates to:

```sql
SELECT Dept, COUNT(*), AVG(Salary)
FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept;
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| COUNT(*) | Equals total row count, **including** rows with NULLs | High (2-mark short) |
| COUNT(col) | Equals non-NULL count of `col` | Very High |
| COUNT(DISTINCT col) | Equals cardinality of unique non-NULL values | Very High |
| SUM(col) | $\sum A_i$ over non-NULL values | High |
| AVG(col) | $\frac{\text{SUM(col)}}{\text{COUNT(col)}}$ | Very High |
| MAX/MIN | Lexicographic / numeric / date comparison | Medium |
| GROUP BY | Each non-aggregated SELECT column **must** appear in GROUP BY | Very High |
| HAVING vs WHERE | WHERE → pre-aggregation; HAVING → post-aggregation | Very High |
| NULL in aggregates | All aggregates (except COUNT(*)) ignore NULLs | Critical |
| Nested aggregates | Aggregates **cannot** be nested directly in standard SQL | Medium |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Banking Systems:** `SUM(TransactionAmount)` per customer for monthly statements.
2. **E-Commerce:** `AVG(Rating)` and `COUNT(Reviews)` for product ranking algorithms.
3. **IoT Databases:** `MAX(Temperature)` over time windows for threshold alerts.
4. **HR Analytics:** `MIN(Salary), MAX(Salary), AVG(Salary)` per department for compensation benchmarking.
5. **Telecommunications:** `COUNT(DISTINCT CallerID)` for unique caller metrics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Comprehensive Aggregate Query

Consider the following **`Employee`** relation (commonly used in KTU question papers):

| **EmpID** | **Name** | **Dept** | **Salary** | **JoiningDate** |
|-----------|----------|----------|------------|-----------------|
| E101 | Arjun | CS | 50000 | 2020-01-15 |
| E102 | Bhavya | CS | 60000 | 2019-03-20 |
| E103 | Chitra | EC | 55000 | 2021-07-11 |
| E104 | Deepak | CS | NULL | 2018-12-01 |
| E105 | Esha | EC | 70000 | 2017-05-25 |
| E106 | Farhan | ME | 45000 | 2022-02-10 |
| E107 | Gita | CS | 60000 | 2020-09-09 |
| E108 | Hari | EC | 55000 | 2019-11-30 |
| E109 | Isha | ME | NULL | 2023-01-05 |

**Query:** *"Find the number of employees, total salary expenditure, average salary, maximum salary, and minimum salary for each department. Exclude the Mechanical department and only include departments where the average salary exceeds 50,000."*

**Step 1: Translate to SQL**

```sql
SELECT Dept,
       COUNT(*)              AS TotalEmployees,
       COUNT(Salary)         AS NonNullSalaryCount,
       SUM(Salary)           AS TotalSalary,
       AVG(Salary)           AS AverageSalary,
       MAX(Salary)           AS HighestSalary,
       MIN(Salary)           AS LowestSalary
FROM   Employee
WHERE  Dept <> 'ME'
GROUP  BY Dept
HAVING AVG(Salary) > 50000;
```

**Step 2: Apply the WHERE clause (Filter individual rows)**

Eliminate rows where `Dept = 'ME'` (removes E106, E109):

| EmpID | Name | Dept | Salary | JoiningDate |
|-------|------|------|--------|-------------|
| E101 | Arjun | CS | 50000 | 2020-01-15 |
| E102 | Bhavya | CS | 60000 | 2019-03-20 |
| E103 | Chitra | EC | 55000 | 2021-07-11 |
| E104 | Deepak | CS | NULL | 2018-12-01 |
| E105 | Esha | EC | 70000 | 2017-05-25 |
| E107 | Gita | CS | 60000 | 2020-09-09 |
| E108 | Hari | EC | 55000 | 2019-11-30 |

**Step 3: Apply the GROUP BY clause (Partition into groups)**

**Group 1: Dept = 'CS'** → {E101, E102, E104, E107}
**Group 2: Dept = 'EC'** → {E103, E105, E108}

**Step 4: Compute aggregates for each group**

**For Dept = 'CS':**

$$
\text{COUNT(*)} = 4
$$

$$
\text{COUNT(Salary)} = 3 \quad \text{(E104 has NULL Salary)}
$$

$$
\text{SUM(Salary)} = 50000 + 60000 + \text{NULL} + 60000 = 170000
$$

$$
\text{AVG(Salary)} = \frac{170000}{3} = 56666.67
$$

$$
\text{MAX(Salary)} = 60000
$$

$$
\text{MIN(Salary)} = 50000
$$

**For Dept = 'EC':**

$$
\text{COUNT(*)} = 3
$$

$$
\text{COUNT(Salary)} = 3
$$

$$
\text{SUM(Salary)} = 55000 + 70000 + 55000 = 180000
$$

$$
\text{AVG(Salary)} = \frac{180000}{3} = 60000
$$

$$
\text{MAX(Salary)} = 70000
$$

$$
\text{MIN(Salary)} = 55000
$$

**Step 5: Apply the HAVING clause (Filter groups post-aggregation)**

$$
\text{AVG(Salary)}_{\text{CS}} = 56666.67 > 50000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Keep CS}
$$

$$
\text{AVG(Salary)}_{\text{EC}} = 60000 > 50000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Keep EC}
$$

**Step 6: Final Output**

| Dept | TotalEmployees | NonNullSalaryCount | TotalSalary | AverageSalary | HighestSalary | LowestSalary |
|------|----------------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| CS | 4 | 3 | 170000 | 56666.67 | 60000 | 50000 |
| EC | 3 | 3 | 180000 | 60000.00 | 70000 | 55000 |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: WHERE vs HAVING

| Property | WHERE Clause | HAVING Clause |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Execution Time** | Before GROUP BY | After GROUP BY |
| **Operates On** | Individual rows (tuples) | Groups (partitions) |
| **Can use aggregate functions?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Can use column aliases defined in SELECT?** | No | Depends on DBMS (MySQL allows) |
| **Can reference grouping columns?** | Yes | Yes |
| **Purpose** | Pre-aggregation filter | Post-aggregation filter |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High |

### 3.3 Common KTU Pitfall: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col) with NULLs

Given the Employee relation above, observe the subtle difference for Dept = 'CS':

$$
\text{COUNT(*)}_{\text{CS}} = 4
$$

$$
\text{COUNT(Salary)}_{\text{CS}} = 3
$$

$$
\text{COUNT(DISTINCT Salary)}_{\text{CS}} = 2 \quad \text{(50000, 60000)}
$$

**Why is `COUNT(DISTINCT Salary)` = 2 and not 3?**
Because the values are {50000, 60000, NULL, 60000} → unique non-NULL values are {50000, 60000}, giving cardinality 2.

### 3.4 Nested Aggregates and the Subquery Pattern

Standard SQL **forbids** nested aggregates like `AVG(MAX(Salary))` directly. The workaround is a subquery:

```sql
SELECT AVG(HighestSalary)
FROM (
    SELECT Dept, MAX(Salary) AS HighestSalary
    FROM Employee
    GROUP BY Dept
) AS DeptMaxima;
```

This computes the average of departmental maximums — a classic KTU 14-mark question pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Flow of an Aggregate Query

The diagram below depicts the end-to-end processing pipeline of a SQL query containing aggregate functions, showing how data flows from raw relations to grouped/aggregated output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Relation: Employee] --> B[FROM Clause: Source Identification]
    B --> C[WHERE Clause: Row-Level Filtering]
    C --> D{NULL Handling Layer}
    D --> E[GROUP BY Clause: Partition into Groups]
    E --> F[Apply Aggregate Functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN]
    F --> G[HAVING Clause: Group-Level Filtering]
    G --> H[SELECT Clause: Final Projection]
    H --> I[ORDER BY: Result Sorting]
    I --> J[Final Result Set]
    D -.-> K[NULL rows: Excluded from SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN]
    D -.-> L[COUNT star includes NULLs, COUNT col excludes]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map of the Five Aggregate Functions

```mermaid
graph LR
    Root[Aggregate Functions] --> N1[COUNT]
    Root --> N2[SUM]
    Root --> N3[AVG]
    Root --> N4[MAX]
    Root --> N5[MIN]
    N1 --> S1[Counts rows or non-NULL values]
    N1 --> S2[Returns INTEGER]
    N2 --> S3[Adds numeric values]
    N2 --> S4[Ignores NULLs]
    N3 --> S5[Divides SUM by COUNT]
    N3 --> S6[Returns numeric type]
    N4 --> S7[Compares and returns largest]
    N5 --> S8[Compares and returns smallest]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Aggregate Query Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        T1[Employee Table]
    end
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Pre-Aggregation]
        T1 --> W[WHERE Filter]
        W --> S[Selection of Rows]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: Aggregation Core]
        S --> G[GROUP BY Partitioner]
        G --> A1[Group 1: CS]
        G --> A2[Group 2: EC]
        G --> A3[Group 3: ME]
        A1 --> AG1[Apply: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN]
        A2 --> AG2[Apply: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN]
        A3 --> AG3[Apply: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3: Post-Aggregation]
        AG1 --> H1[HAVING Filter on Group 1]
        AG2 --> H2[HAVING Filter on Group 2]
        AG3 --> H3[HAVING Filter on Group 3]
    end
    subgraph Output
        H1 --> R[Final Result Set]
        H2 --> R
        H3 --> R
    end
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Aggregate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[What do you want to compute?] --> Q1{Total number of rows?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Z1[Use COUNT star]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Number of non-NULL values?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Z2[Use COUNT column]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Number of unique values?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Z3[Use COUNT DISTINCT column]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Type is numeric?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{Operation?}
    Q4 -->|No, string or date| Q6{Operation?}
    Q5 -->|Total| Z4[Use SUM]
    Q5 -->|Mean| Z5[Use AVG]
    Q6 -->|Largest| Z6[Use MAX]
    Q6 -->|Smallest| Z7[Use MIN]
    Q5 -->|Extreme value| Z4a[Use MAX or MIN]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the difference between the `WHERE` clause and the `HAVING` clause in SQL. Why can't aggregate functions be used in the `WHERE` clause?*

**Model Answer:**

The `WHERE` clause performs **row-level filtering** *before* the `GROUP BY` operation, whereas the `HAVING` clause performs **group-level filtering** *after* the aggregation has been computed. This distinction is rooted in the logical query processing order defined by the ISO SQL standard: `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY`.

Aggregate functions **cannot** be used in a `WHERE` clause because at the point of `WHERE` evaluation, the input relation has not yet been partitioned into groups, and aggregation requires a multiset of values to collapse into a single value. The `WHERE` clause operates on individual tuples where an aggregate over a "group" is undefined. Hence, aggregate expressions must be evaluated *after* grouping, in the `HAVING` or `SELECT` clause.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT(Salary)`, and `COUNT(DISTINCT Salary)` with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

Consider a relation with 10 rows, 7 having non-NULL `Salary` values, and only 5 distinct non-NULL salary figures.

- **`COUNT(*)`** returns **10**. It counts **all rows** in the relation, regardless of NULLs.
- **`COUNT(Salary)`** returns **7**. It counts only the rows where `Salary` is **not NULL**.
- **`COUNT(DISTINCT Salary)`** returns **5**. It counts the number of **unique non-NULL** salary values, eliminating duplicates.

All three functions can return different values, and understanding the difference is essential for accurate query semantics.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

*(a) Consider the following `Sales` relation:* **(7 marks)**

| SaleID | Product | Category | Amount | SaleDate |
|--------|---------|----------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Laptop | Electronics | 80000 | 2024-01-15 |
| 2 | Phone | Electronics | 40000 | 2024-01-20 |
| 3 | Shirt | Clothing | 2000 | 2024-02-05 |
| 4 | Laptop | Electronics | 75000 | 2024-02-10 |
| 5 | Jeans | Clothing | 3000 | 2024-02-15 |
| 6 | Tablet | Electronics | 30000 | 2024-03-01 |
| 7 | Shoes | Clothing | NULL | 2024-03-10 |

*Write SQL queries for the following:*
*(i) Find the total revenue per category.*
*(ii) Find categories with total revenue greater than 1,00,000.*
*(iii) Find the average revenue per category, ignoring NULLs.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total revenue per category:**

```sql
SELECT Category, SUM(Amount) AS TotalRevenue
FROM Sales
GROUP BY Category;
```

**Result:**

| Category | TotalRevenue |
|----------|--------------|
| Electronics | 225000 |
| Clothing | 5000 |

*(Note: NULL in row 7 is ignored by SUM.)*

**(ii) Categories with total revenue > 1,00,000:**

```sql
SELECT Category, SUM(Amount) AS TotalRevenue
FROM Sales
GROUP BY Category
HAVING SUM(Amount) > 100000;
```

**Result:**

| Category | TotalRevenue |
|----------|--------------|
| Electronics | 225000 |

**(iii) Average revenue per category, ignoring NULLs:**

```sql
SELECT Category, AVG(Amount) AS AverageRevenue
FROM Sales
GROUP BY Category;
```

**Result:**

| Category | AverageRevenue |
|----------|----------------|
| Electronics | 56250 |
| Clothing | 2500 |

*(For Electronics: (80000+40000+75000+30000)/4 = 56250. For Clothing: (2000+3000)/2 = 2500, since NULL is ignored.)*

---

*(b) With a suitable example, explain how aggregate functions can be combined with the `GROUP BY` and `HAVING` clauses. Discuss the role of NULL values in aggregate computations.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Combined `GROUP BY` + `HAVING` + Aggregate Pattern:**

The general structure of such a query is:

```sql
SELECT grouping_column, AGG_FUNCS
FROM table_name
[WHERE conditions]
GROUP BY grouping_column
HAVING AGG_FUNCS conditions
ORDER BY ...;
```

**Example:** *"Find departments with more than 2 employees and an average salary exceeding 40,000."*

```sql
SELECT Dept, COUNT(*) AS NumEmployees, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSalary
FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 AND AVG(Salary) > 40000;
```

This query first groups rows by `Dept`, then applies the `HAVING` filter to retain only those groups satisfying the count and average constraints.

**Role of NULLs in Aggregate Computations:**

1. **All aggregates (except `COUNT(*)`) ignore NULLs.** This means `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, and `MIN` only consider non-NULL values.
2. **`AVG` divides by the count of non-NULL values, not the total row count.** This can yield unexpected results if a column has many NULLs.
3. **`COUNT(*)` counts all rows**, including those with NULLs in any column, because it counts the entire tuple.
4. **`COUNT(DISTINCT col)` ignores both NULLs and duplicates**, returning the cardinality of unique non-NULL values.
5. **The `GROUP BY` clause treats NULLs as a single group.** All rows where the grouping column is NULL are aggregated together into a single NULL-group.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (from past 5 semesters):**
> 1. Students frequently write `WHERE AVG(Salary) > 50000` instead of `HAVING AVG(Salary) > 50000`. This is a **syntax error** in standard SQL and will result in **0 marks** for the sub-part. The reason: `WHERE` is evaluated before aggregation, so aggregates are not yet available.
> 2. Students forget that every non-aggregated column in the `SELECT` list **must** appear in the `GROUP BY` clause. Omitting this in MySQL with `ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY` disabled will fail; in strict ANSI SQL it is an error.
> 3. Conflating `COUNT(*)` with `COUNT(column)` is the most common 3-mark loss. Always specify the asterisk vs. column explicitly.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

*(a) Consider the `Orders` table with attributes (OrderID, CustomerID, Product, Quantity, Price, OrderDate). Write SQL queries to:* **(7 marks)**
