*(i) Display the total quantity sold for each product.*
*(ii) Display products where the total quantity sold exceeds 100.*
*(iii) Display the second highest `Price` using an aggregate function.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total quantity sold for each product:**

```sql
SELECT Product, SUM(Quantity) AS TotalQuantity
FROM Orders
GROUP BY Product;
```

**(ii) Products with total quantity > 100:**

```sql
SELECT Product, SUM(Quantity) AS TotalQuantity
FROM Orders
GROUP BY Product
HAVING SUM(Quantity) > 100;
```

**(iii) Second highest Price using an aggregate function (subquery approach):**

```sql
SELECT MAX(Price) AS SecondHighestPrice
FROM Orders
WHERE Price < (SELECT MAX(Price) FROM Orders);
```

This works by first finding the overall maximum, then finding the maximum of all values *strictly less than* that maximum.

**Alternative using `LIMIT` (vendor-specific, MySQL):**

```sql
SELECT Price
FROM Orders
ORDER BY Price DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;
```

---

*(b) Explain the difference between the relational algebra **Gamma (γ) operator** and SQL's `GROUP BY` clause. How do aggregate functions map to these formal constructs?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Relational Algebra Gamma (γ) Operator:**

The extended relational algebra includes the **γ (Gamma) operator** for aggregation. It is formally defined as:

$$
\gamma_{F_1(A_1), F_2(A_2), \ldots; \, G_1, G_2, \ldots}(R)
$$

Where:
- $R$ is the input relation
- $G_1, G_2, \ldots$ are the **grouping attributes** (partition keys)
- $F_1, F_2, \ldots$ are the **aggregate function list** (e.g., `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`)

**SQL `GROUP BY` Clause:**

The `GROUP BY` clause is the syntactic realization of the γ operator in SQL. The mapping is:

$$
\text{SELECT } G_1, G_2, F_1(A_1), F_2(A_2) \text{ FROM } R \text{ GROUP BY } G_1, G_2
$$

is equivalent to:

$$
\gamma_{F_1(A_1), F_2(A_2); \, G_1, G_2}(R)
$$

**Key Differences:**

1. **Null Semantics:** γ operator typically uses set semantics (no duplicates), while SQL uses multiset (bag) semantics by default — this is why `DISTINCT` is required in SQL for unique counting.
2. **Filtering:** γ has no built-in filter. SQL introduces the `HAVING` clause to provide this post-aggregation filter, which is an extension beyond classical relational algebra.
3. **Pre-aggregation filtering:** The `WHERE` clause in SQL corresponds to a σ (selection) operator **before** γ is applied.

**Composite Expression:**

$$
\pi_{\text{Dept, AvgSal}} \left( \sigma_{\text{AvgSal} > 50000} \left( \gamma_{\text{AVG(Salary)}; \, \text{Dept}}(R) \right) \right)
$$

This is the formal relational algebra equivalent of:

```sql
SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal
FROM R
GROUP BY Dept
HAVING AVG(Salary) > 50000;
```

This question tests the KTU 2024 learning outcome of mapping SQL constructs back to their formal relational algebra foundations — a high-weightage topic for ESE.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Aggregate Functions**

- The **five standard aggregates** in KTU syllabus: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`.
- **Logical query execution order:** `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY`.
- **`WHERE` ≠ `HAVING`:** WHERE filters rows; HAVING filters groups.
- **Aggregates cannot appear in `WHERE`** — only in `HAVING`, `SELECT`, or `ORDER BY`.
- **All aggregates ignore NULLs** except `COUNT(*)`, which counts every row.
- **`AVG(A) = SUM(A) / COUNT(A)`** — the denominator is non-NULL count, not row count.
- **`COUNT(DISTINCT col)`** returns the cardinality of unique non-NULL values.
- **Every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY** (SQL standard).
- **The γ (Gamma) operator** is the relational algebra foundation for `GROUP BY` + aggregates.
- **Nested aggregates** are not allowed in standard SQL — use subqueries.
- **`GROUP BY` treats NULLs as a single group.**
- **String/Date aggregates:** `MAX`/`MIN` work via lexicographic and chronological comparison.
- **HAVING can use aliases** (in some DBMS like MySQL) but WHERE cannot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested Subqueries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nested Subqueries** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Structured Query Language (SQL)**, a **subquery** is a `SELECT` statement that is embedded within the clause of another SQL statement, commonly referred to as the *outer query*. A **Nested Subquery** (also termed a *multi-level* or *hierarchical subquery*) is a specific structural arrangement where one or more subqueries are embedded *inside another subquery*, creating a multi-tiered evaluation hierarchy. Formally, if we denote the outer query as $Q_{outer}$ and the immediate inner query as $Q_1$, a nested subquery is defined recursively as: $$ Q_{outer} \leftarrow f(Q_1), \quad \text{where} \quad Q_1 \leftarrow f(Q_2), \quad Q_2 \leftarrow f(Q_3), \ldots $$ Here, the function $f$ represents the embedding operator (i.e., placing a `SELECT` inside a `WHERE`, `FROM`, or `SELECT` clause of the parent statement). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Nested subqueries are positioned under Module 2 (SQL & Relational Algebra). The expected cognitive level per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy is **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** — students must construct and optimize nested query expressions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Russian Nesting Doll" (Matryoshka) Analogy** > > Imagine a set of Russian nesting dolls. The outermost doll is your *main/outer query*. When you open it, you find a *smaller doll* (subquery 1). Open that one, and you find an *even smaller doll* (subquery 2), and so on. To answer the outermost question, the database engine must *first* open and evaluate the innermost doll, then the next layer up, until finally it can answer the outer question. The result of each inner layer "feeds" the layer just outside it. **Alternative Analogy — The "Office Hierarchy":** Think of a CEO (outer query) who asks a Manager (subquery 1) for a report. The Manager, in turn, asks a Team Lead (subquery 2) for specific data. The Team Lead's answer flows back to the Manager, whose consolidated answer finally reaches the CEO. Neither the CEO nor the Manager can produce their answer *until* the inner layer has finished. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot | **Dimension** | **Categories** | |---|---| | **Correlation** | Correlated vs. Non-Correlated | | **Return Cardinality** | Scalar (single value), Row, Column, Table | | **Position in Outer Query** | `WHERE`, `FROM`, `SELECT`, `HAVING` clause | | **Comparison Logic** | `IN`, `NOT IN`, `EXISTS`, `ANY`, `ALL`, `=`, `<`, `>`, `\leq`, `\geq`, `\neq` | > [!NOTE] > The standard metrics used in KTU problems for subquery evaluation are the **cardinality** of the result set (number of tuples) and the **degree** (number of attributes). These are typically expressed in **rows** and **columns** respectively. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics: How a Nested Subquery Executes A non-correlated nested subquery follows a strict **bottom-up evaluation** model. The engine first resolves the innermost `SELECT`, materializes its result (or streams it, depending on the optimizer), and then propagates that result upward to the next enclosing query. ### Step-by-Step Evaluation Logic 1. **Lexical Parsing**: The SQL parser identifies all `SELECT` blocks, builds a *parse tree*, and recognizes the nested structure. 2. **Query Rewriting**: The optimizer may rewrite the nested subquery into an equivalent **join** (e.g., a subquery with `IN` is often transformed into a semi-join). 3. **Innermost Execution**: The deepest subquery is executed first against the base tables, producing an intermediate result set $R_{inner}$. 4. **Predicate Binding**: The result $R_{inner}$ is substituted into the predicate of the parent query. 5. **Iterative Propagation**: Step 3 and 4 repeat for every enclosing layer, working outward. 6. **Final Output**: The outermost query projects the final answer to the user. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Bottom-Up Execution** > > SQL is *declarative* — you specify *what* you want, not *how* to get it. The bottom-up model respects the **data dependency** rule: a parent query can only consume data that its child has already produced. Attempting top-down execution would require predicting unknown data, which is logically impossible. ## 2.2 Correlated vs. Non-Correlated Subqueries This distinction is a **high-weightage KTU concept** and frequently appears in 2-mark and 14-mark questions. | **Attribute** | **Non-Correlated Subquery** | **Correlated Subquery** | |---|---|---| | **Dependency on outer query** | None — fully independent | References one or more columns of the outer query | | **Execution Frequency** | Executed **once** | Executed **once per row** of the outer query | | **Performance** | Generally faster | Can be $O(n \times m)$ in the worst case | | **When to Use** | Filtering against a static set of values | Filtering using row-by-row comparison logic | | **Typical KTU Example** | "Find employees earning more than the average salary" | "Find employees earning more than the average salary in *their own* department" | ## 2.3 The Quantified Comparison Operators: `ANY`, `ALL`, `EXISTS` These operators are critical for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. The following table provides the formal logical definitions. Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ be the result set of the subquery, and $v$ be the value from the outer query. The standard SQL semantics are: | **Operator** | **Formal Logic** | **Plain English Meaning** | |---|---|---| | `> ANY (S)` | $\exists \, s_i \in S : v > s_i$ | Greater than at least one value in $S$ | | `> ALL (S)` | $\forall \, s_i \in S : v > s_i$ | Greater than every value in $S$ | | `= ANY (S)` | $\exists \, s_i \in S : v = s_i$ | Equivalent to `IN (S)` | | `> ALL (S)` with empty $S$ | Vacuously `TRUE` | An empty set is treated as universally satisfied | | `EXISTS (S)` | $S \neq \emptyset$ | The subquery returns at least one row | > [!NOTE] > **Edge Case (Frequently Tested):** When $S$ is empty, `> ALL (S)` evaluates to `TRUE` (vacuous truth), while `> ANY (S)` evaluates to `FALSE`. This asymmetry is a classic KTU trick question. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following consolidated table maps every key construct to its evaluation logic for quick revision. | **Construct** | **Symbolic Form** | **Evaluation Rule** | **Common KTU Pitfall** | |---|---|---|---| | Scalar Subquery | $Q_{scalar} \to \{v \mid v \in \mathbb{R} \text{ or domain}\}$ | Must return exactly one row/column | Returning >1 row causes runtime error | | `IN` Subquery | $x \in \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ | Membership test against subquery output | `NULL` handling requires three-valued logic | | `EXISTS` | $\mid S \mid \geq 1$ | Boolean existence check | `NULL` columns in `WHERE` may yield unexpected results | | `NOT EXISTS` | $\mid S \mid = 0$ | Boolean non-existence check | Used heavily in **set-difference** problems | | `> ANY` | $\max(s_1, \ldots, s_n) < v$ | Strictly greater than the minimum of $S$ | Confusing `ANY` with `ALL` | | `> ALL` | $\min(s_1, \ldots, s_n) < v$ | Strictly greater than the maximum of $S$ | Confusing `ALL` with `ANY` | | Derived Table | $Q_{FROM} = \pi_{A,B}(\sigma_{\text{cond}}(R \bowtie S))$ | Subquery in `FROM` acts as a virtual table | Must be aliased — `SELECT * FROM (SELECT ...) AS T` | | Correlated Reference | $Q_{inner} \gets \text{column from } Q_{outer}$ | Inner query depends on outer row | Causes $O(n)$ re-execution | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > Nested subqueries are not merely an academic exercise. They are the *workhorse* of: > > - **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) systems** for computing rolling averages and period-over-period deltas. > - **Recommendation engines** that compute "users who bought X also bought Y" using 2-level nested set-membership tests. > - **Fraud detection pipelines** that correlate transaction-level data with historical patterns using `EXISTS` and `NOT EXISTS`. > - **HR Analytics dashboards** that rank employees using a *nested ranking* subquery inside a `SELECT` clause. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Schema (Used Throughout This Section) For all derivations below, we use the following canonical schema, which mirrors the standard KTU textbook examples: $$ \text{Schema} = \{ \text{EMP}(\text{EmpID}, \text{Ename}, \text{Sal}, \text{DeptID}), \; \text{DEPT}(\text{DeptID}, \text{Dname}, \text{Loc}) \} $$ The cardinalities are: $|\text{EMP}| = N_e$ and $|\text{DEPT}| = N_d$. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 1 — Non-Correlated Scalar Subquery **Problem:** Find the name of the employee earning the **maximum salary**. **Step 1 — Identify the innermost block.** The innermost block must return a single scalar: the maximum of `Sal`. $$ Q_1 : \text{SELECT MAX(Sal) FROM EMP} $$ Let us denote the result as $M_{sal} = \max(\text{EMP.Sal})$. **Step 2 — Embed $Q_1$ inside the outer query's `WHERE` clause.** $$ Q_{outer} : \text{SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE Sal} = (Q_1) $$ **Step 3 — Write the full nested SQL.** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE Sal = (SELECT MAX(Sal) FROM EMP); ``` **Step 4 — Formal evaluation order.** The engine first executes $Q_1$, producing the scalar $M_{sal}$. It then evaluates the outer query's predicate $Sal = M_{sal}$ for every row in EMP. Any row satisfying the predicate is projected to the output. **Step 5 — Result set cardinality.** $$ \mid \text{Result} \mid = \mid \{ e \in \text{EMP} \mid e.Sal = M_{sal} \} \mid $$ Since $M_{sal}$ is the global maximum, at least one tuple must satisfy this. If two employees share the maximum salary, $\mid \text{Result} \mid = 2$. **Step 6 — Time complexity analysis.** $$ T(n) = O(N_e) \text{ for } Q_1 + O(N_e) \text{ for } Q_{outer} = O(N_e) $$ This is **linear** in the size of EMP — an efficient execution. ## 3.3 Worked Derivation 2 — Nested Subquery with `IN` (Two Levels) **Problem:** Find the names of employees who work in a department located in **'NEW YORK'**. **Step 1 — Decompose the requirement.** We need: (a) Departments in New York, then (b) Employees belonging to those departments. **Step 2 — Innermost subquery (Level 2).** $$ Q_2 : \text{SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc} = \text{'NEW YORK'} $$ This returns a set of department IDs: $$ S_{DeptID} = \{ d.DeptID \mid d \in \text{DEPT} \land d.Loc = \text{'NEW YORK'} \} $$ **Step 3 — Middle subquery (Level 1) — using the result of $Q_2$.** Since the requirement has only two layers, Level 1 is actually the outer query. Employees whose `DeptID` is a member of $S_{DeptID}$ are selected. $$ Q_1 : \text{SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN } (Q_2) $$ **Step 4 — Full nested SQL.** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'NEW YORK'); ``` **Step 5 — Set-theoretic formalization.** $$ \text{Result} = \pi_{Ename}\big( \text{EMP} \bowtie_{DeptID \in S_{DeptID}} \text{DEPT} \big) $$ Equivalently, using relational algebra: $$ \pi_{Ename}\Big(\sigma_{Loc = \text{'NEW YORK'}}(\text{DEPT}) \bowtie_{DeptID = DeptID} \text{EMP}\Big) $$ **Step 6 — Cardinality.** $$ \mid \text{Result} \mid = \sum_{d \in \text{DEPT}, d.Loc = \text{'NEW YORK'}} \mid \pi_{EmpID}(\sigma_{DeptID = d.DeptID}(\text{EMP})) \mid $$ ## 3.4 Worked Derivation 3 — Three-Level Nested Subquery This is the **classic KTU 14-mark pattern** — three-level nesting with the `EXISTS` operator. **Problem:** Find the names of employees who earn a salary **higher than the average salary of their own department**, and who work in a department that has **at least 3 employees**. **Step 1 — Identify three semantic layers.** 1. *Innermost (L3):* Compute the average salary of a *given* department. 2. *Middle (L2):* Check whether a department has at least 3 employees. 3. *Outermost (L1):* Select employees meeting both conditions. **Step 2 — Innermost subquery ($Q_3$) — correlated, returns scalar.** $$ Q_3 : \text{SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID} = d.DeptID $$ $$ \bar{S}_{d} = \frac{1}{\mid \sigma_{DeptID = d.DeptID}(\text{EMP}) \mid} \sum_{e \in \text{EMP}, e.DeptID = d.DeptID} e.Sal $$ **Step 3 — Middle subquery ($Q_2$) — uses $Q_3$, checks department size.** $$ Q_2 : \text{SELECT DeptID FROM EMP e WHERE Sal} > (Q_3) \text{ GROUP BY DeptID HAVING COUNT(*)} \geq 3 $$ **Step 4 — Outermost subquery ($Q_1$) — uses $Q_2$, projects names.** $$ Q_1 : \text{SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN } (Q_2) $$ **Step 5 — Complete nested SQL.** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM EMP e WHERE Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID) GROUP BY DeptID HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3); ``` **Step 6 — Execution order (annotated).** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{For each row } e \in \text{EMP} \text{ in the outer query:} \\ &\quad \text{Step A: Execute } Q_3 \text{ for the row's department to get } \bar{S}_{d}. \\ &\quad \text{Step B: Check if } e.Sal > \bar{S}_{d}. \\ &\quad \text{Step C: If true, group by DeptID; if } \mid \text{Group} \mid \geq 3, \text{ keep DeptID.} \\ &\quad \text{Step D: Pass qualifying DeptIDs back to } Q_1. \\ &\quad \text{Step E: Project Ename for matching employees.} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Complexity analysis.** The innermost $Q_3$ is correlated and executes once per row in $Q_2$, which in turn iterates over all $N_e$ rows of EMP. The total cost is: $$ T(N_e, N_d) = O(N_e \cdot N_e) = O(N_e^2) $$ This quadratic cost is *why* correlated subqueries are performance-critical in real systems. ## 3.5 Worked Derivation 4 — Transforming `NOT IN` to `NOT EXISTS` This transformation is a **favorite KTU trick** to test whether students understand `NULL` semantics. **Original query:** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID NOT IN (SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'LONDON'); ``` If **any** `DeptID` in the DEPT subquery is `NULL`, then `NOT IN` returns an *empty result* (because `x NOT IN (..., NULL, ...)` evaluates to `UNKNOWN`, not `TRUE`). **Safe equivalent using `NOT EXISTS`:** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP e WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM DEPT d WHERE d.Loc = 'LONDON' AND d.DeptID = e.DeptID); ``` > [!NOTE] > The `EXISTS` form is **NULL-safe** because the correlation predicate `d.DeptID = e.DeptID` is only evaluated when a London department actually exists. `NULL`s in unrelated rows are never compared. ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Subquery Form | **Use Case** | **Recommended Construct** | **Why** | **Complexity Class** | |---|---|---|---| | Membership in a fixed set | `IN` (non-correlated) | Set-membership is a primitive SQL operation | $O(N_e \cdot N_d)$ | | Existence of a related row | `EXISTS` | Optimizer can short-circuit on first match | $O(N_e \cdot N_d)$, often faster in practice | | Universal quantification | `ALL` or `NOT EXISTS` | Cleaner than `NOT IN` with possible `NULL`s | $O(N_e \cdot N_d)$ | | Scalar aggregation | `(SELECT MAX/MIN/AVG ...)` | Single-value subquery in `WHERE` | $O(N_e)$ | | Derived table / virtual table | `(SELECT ...) AS T` in `FROM` | Enables layered data transformations | Depends on join cost | | Row-by-row comparison | Correlated subquery | Encapsulates per-row context | $O(N_e^2)$ worst case | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Execution Flow of a Nested Subquery The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **bottom-up evaluation** of a generic two-level nested subquery, which is the most common KTU pattern. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Submits Outer Query Q1] --> B[SQL Parser Identifies Q1 and Q2] B --> C[Optimizer Generates Execution Plan] C --> D[Execute Innermost Subquery Q2] D --> E[Q2 Returns Intermediate Result Set R2] E --> F[Bind R2 into Q1 Predicate] F --> G[Execute Q1 Using Bound Predicate] G --> H[Q1 Returns Final Result Set R1] H --> I[Return R1 to User] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style E fill:#ffe699,color:#000000 style G fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style H fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Three-Level Nested Subquery For the more complex three-level pattern (Worked Derivation 3), the architecture is best represented as a block diagram showing the data flow between layers. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph outerLayer["OUTER LAYER L1 - Q1 Outer Query"] O1[Input: EMP table] O2[Predicate: DeptID IN Result of L2] O3[Output: Ename of qualifying employees] O1 --> O2 --> O3 end subgraph middleLayer["MIDDLE LAYER L2 - Q2 Aggregation"] M1[Input: EMP table e] M2[Filter: e.Sal greater than Result of L3] M3[Group By: DeptID] M4[Having: COUNT asterisk greater than or equal to 3] M5[Output: Set of qualifying DeptIDs] M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 end subgraph innerLayer["INNER LAYER L3 - Q3 Scalar Subquery"] I1[Input: EMP table - correlated to e.DeptID] I2[Aggregate: AVG of Sal] I3[Output: Scalar average salary for the department] I1 --> I2 --> I3 end O2 -.uses.-> M5 M2 -.uses.-> I3 style outerLayer fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000 style middleLayer fill:#c8e6c9,color:#000000 style innerLayer fill:#ffcdd2,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Correlated vs. Non-Correlated Execution Comparison The following Mermaid sequence diagram contrasts the two execution paradigms — a key conceptual distinction in KTU exams. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant UQ as Outer Query participant IQ1 as Inner Subquery participant DB as Database Engine Note over UQ,DB: NON-CORRELATED EXECUTION UQ->>DB: Parse outer query DB->>IQ1: Execute inner subquery once IQ1-->>DB: Return static result set S DB->>UQ: Evaluate outer query using S UQ-->>DB: Return final answer Note over UQ,DB: CORRELATED EXECUTION UQ->>DB: Parse outer query loop For Each Outer Row R DB->>IQ1: Execute inner subquery using R values IQ1-->>DB: Return per-row result DB->>UQ: Evaluate predicate for row R end UQ-->>DB: Return final answer ``` ## 4.4 Operator Decision Tree The following flowchart helps students decide *which* SQL operator to use based on the question phrasing. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: What does the question ask] --> B{Does the subquery
reference outer columns?} B -- NO --> C[Non-Correlated Subquery] C --> D{How many rows
does the subquery return?} D -- Single Value --> E[Use Scalar Comparison
equals greater than less than] D -- Multiple Values --> F{What is the
logical relationship?} F -- At least one --> G[Use IN or ANY] F -- Every single one --> H[Use ALL] F -- Existence check --> I[Use EXISTS] B -- YES --> J[Correlated Subquery] J --> K{Is the check for
row existence?} K -- YES --> L[Use EXISTS or NOT EXISTS] K -- NO --> M[Use Correlated Scalar
with equals greater than less than] style A fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style C fill:#4caf50,color:#ffffff style J fill:#ff9800,color:#ffffff style E fill:#e1bee7,color:#000000 style G fill:#b3e5fc,color:#000000 style H fill:#b3e5fc,color:#000000 style I fill:#b3e5fc,color:#000000 style L fill:#ffccbc,color:#000000 style M fill:#ffccbc,color:#000000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: Define a nested subquery and distinguish between correlated and non-correlated subqueries with an example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **nested subquery** is a `SELECT` statement embedded within another SQL query, where the embedded query itself contains one or more further `SELECT` statements. This creates a hierarchical structure with multiple levels of query evaluation. **Distinction:** | **Attribute** | **Non-Correlated** | **Correlated** | |---|---|---| | **Outer Dependency** | None | Yes — references outer query columns | | **Execution Count** | Once | Once per outer row | | **Example Use** | Static filter | Row-by-row context-sensitive filter | **Example (Correlated):** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP e WHERE Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID); ``` **Example (Non-Correlated):** ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP); ``` --- ### Question A2: Explain the difference between `ANY` and `ALL` operators with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The `ANY` and `ALL` operators perform quantified comparisons between a value and a set returned by a subquery. - **`> ANY (S)`** — `TRUE` if the value is greater than **at least one** element in $S$. Formally: $\exists \, s_i \in S : v > s_i$. - **`> ALL (S)`** — `TRUE` if the value is greater than **every** element in $S$. Formally: $\forall \, s_i \in S : v > s_i$. **Example:** ```sql -- Find employees earning more than ANY clerk SELECT Ename, Sal FROM EMP WHERE Sal > ANY (SELECT Sal FROM EMP WHERE Job = 'CLERK'); -- Find employees earning more than ALL clerks SELECT Ename, Sal FROM EMP WHERE Sal > ALL (SELECT Sal FROM EMP WHERE Job = 'CLERK'); ``` The first query returns employees who out-earn at least one clerk. The second returns only those who out-earn *every* clerk. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Nested Subquery with `IN`, `EXISTS`, and Derived Tables `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Consider the following schema: $$ \text{Schema} = \{ \text{EMP}(\text{EmpID}, \text{Ename}, \text{Sal}, \text{DeptID}), \; \text{DEPT}(\text{DeptID}, \text{Dname}, \text{Loc}) \} $$ #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q:** Write an SQL query using a **nested subquery** to find the names of employees who work in a department located in **'CHICAGO'** and earn a salary greater than **3000**. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the innermost requirement — department IDs in Chicago. ```sql SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'CHICAGO'; ``` This returns the set $S_{Chicago} = \{ d.DeptID \mid d.Loc = \text{'CHICAGO'} \}$. **Step 2:** Embed this in the outer `WHERE` clause and add the salary filter. ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'CHICAGO') AND Sal > 3000; ``` **Step 3:** Verify the logic. The outer query iterates over EMP. For each row, it checks two predicates: (i) `DeptID` is in $S_{Chicago}$ and (ii) `Sal > 3000`. Only rows satisfying *both* are projected. **Step 4:** Relational algebra equivalent. $$ \pi_{Ename}\Big(\sigma_{Sal > 3000}\big(EMP \bowtie_{DeptID = DeptID} \sigma_{Loc = \text{'CHICAGO'}}(DEPT)\big)\Big) $$ **Step 5:** Complexity. $T(N_e, N_d) = O(N_e \cdot N_d)$ — one scan of EMP and one scan of DEPT. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q:** Using a **correlated nested subquery** with `EXISTS`, find the names of departments that have **at least one employee earning more than 4000**. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Recognize the correlation requirement. The outer query is over DEPT, but the filter depends on data in EMP for *that specific* department. **Step 2:** The correlated subquery must reference the outer query's `DeptID`. ```sql SELECT Dname FROM DEPT d WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM EMP e WHERE e.DeptID = d.DeptID AND e.Sal > 4000); ``` **Step 3:** Explanation of execution. For each department row $d$ in DEPT, the inner subquery checks whether there exists at least one employee $e$ in EMP such that $e.DeptID = d.DeptID$ and $e.Sal > 4000$. The `EXISTS` operator returns `TRUE` as soon as one matching row is found, after which the engine short-circuits and proceeds to the next department. **Step 4:** Why `EXISTS` is preferred over `IN` here. `EXISTS` short-circuits at the first match, whereas `IN` materializes the full set of `DeptID`s. For a large EMP table, `EXISTS` can be significantly faster. **Step 5:** Equivalent `IN` form (less efficient but logically equivalent). ```sql SELECT Dname FROM DEPT WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM EMP WHERE Sal > 4000); ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Three-Level Nesting and Transformation Rules `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q:** Consider the schema from Question A. Write a **three-level nested subquery** to find the names of employees who earn a salary greater than the **average salary of their own department**, and whose department is located in **'NEW YORK'**. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Deconstruct the three requirements. 1. *Level 3 (innermost):* Average salary of a specific department — correlated scalar subquery. 2. *Level 2 (middle):* Filter employees whose salary exceeds the Level 3 average. 3. *Level 1 (outer):* Restrict to departments in New York using the subquery result. **Step 2:** Innermost subquery ($Q_3$). ```sql SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID -- 'e' is the outer alias ``` This returns $\bar{S}_d$, the average salary of the department of the current row $e$. **Step 3:** Middle subquery ($Q_2$). ```sql SELECT EmpID, DeptID FROM EMP e WHERE Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID) ``` **Step 4:** Outer query ($Q_1$) with the New York constraint. ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM EMP e WHERE Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID)) AND DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'NEW YORK'); ``` **Step 5:** Optimization via `EXISTS`. A more elegant form combines both conditions: ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP e WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM DEPT d WHERE d.DeptID = e.DeptID AND d.Loc = 'NEW YORK') AND Sal > (SELECT AVG(Sal) FROM EMP WHERE DeptID = e.DeptID); ``` **Step 6:** Complexity. The innermost subquery is correlated and runs $O(N_e)$ times. The outer is $O(N_e)$. Total: $O(N_e^2)$ worst case. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q:** Explain the transformation of a `NOT IN` subquery to a `NOT EXISTS` subquery. Why is this transformation important when `NULL` values are present? **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** The `NOT IN` problem with `NULL`s. The expression `x NOT IN (subquery)` is equivalent to `NOT (x = s_1 OR x = s_2 OR ... OR x = s_n)`. In SQL's three-valued logic, if **any** $s_i$ is `NULL`, then `x = NULL` evaluates to `UNKNOWN`, and the entire `OR` chain becomes `UNKNOWN`. The result is that the outer query returns **zero rows** — an often surprising behavior. **Step 2:** Demonstration. Consider: ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP WHERE DeptID NOT IN (SELECT DeptID FROM DEPT WHERE Loc = 'LONDON'); ``` If even one London department has a `NULL` `DeptID`, the result is empty. **Step 3:** The `NOT EXISTS` rewrite. ```sql SELECT Ename FROM EMP e WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM DEPT d WHERE d.Loc = 'LONDON' AND d.DeptID = e.DeptID); ``` **Step 4:** Why this is `NULL`-safe. The correlation predicate `d.DeptID = e.DeptID` is only evaluated when a London row is *actually present*. `NULL` DeptIDs in non-London rows are never compared. The logic is: "Find employees for whom there is **no** London department with a matching DeptID." This is the *correct* semantic intent. **Step 5:** General transformation rule. $$ \text{`x NOT IN (SELECT y FROM ...)`} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{`NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ... WHERE y = x)`} $$ > The double-headed arrow is **valid only when `y` cannot be `NULL`**, or when `NULL`s are acceptable in the result. **Step 6:** Summary table. | **Form** | `NULL` in Subquery Result | `NULL` in Outer `x` | **Behavior** | |---|---|---|---| | `NOT IN` | Causes empty result | `x NOT IN` returns `UNKNOWN` | Problematic | | `NOT EXISTS` | Safe — ignored | `NOT EXISTS` returns `TRUE` | Robust | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > The following are the **top 5 reasons** students lose marks on nested subquery questions: > > 1. **Forgetting the alias for derived tables** — `SELECT * FROM (SELECT ...) AS T` is required in standard SQL; omitting `AS T` causes a syntax error. > 2. **Confusing `ANY` with `ALL`** — `> ANY` means "greater than at least one", not "greater than all". This mistake alone can cost 3–4 marks. > 3. **Ignoring `NULL` semantics in `NOT IN`** — Always check whether the subquery column can be `NULL`. If yes, use `NOT EXISTS`. > 4. **Returning more than one row from a scalar subquery** — If a subquery in a `=` comparison returns multiple rows, the database throws a runtime error. Use `IN` or `ANY` instead. > 5. **Missing the correlation variable** — In a correlated subquery, failing to reference the outer table's column is a common logic error that returns wrong results. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nested Subqueries** > > - **Definition:** A nested subquery is a `SELECT` within a `SELECT`, evaluated bottom-up (non-correlated) or row-by-row (correlated). > - **Three levels** of nesting are the practical maximum in KTU problems; four or more levels are rare and considered an anti-pattern. > - **Correlated subqueries** reference outer columns; they execute **once per outer row** and have **quadratic** worst-case cost. > - **Non-correlated subqueries** are independent; they execute **once** and have **linear** cost. > - **`IN` vs `EXISTS`:** `IN` materializes the subquery result; `EXISTS` short-circuits on the first match. Prefer `EXISTS` for large subquery results. > - **`NOT IN` vs `NOT EXISTS`:** Use `NOT EXISTS` when the subquery may contain `NULL`s — it is **NULL-safe**. > - **`ANY` and `ALL`** are quantified comparison operators: > - `> ANY` ↔ greater than the *minimum* of the set. > - `> ALL` ↔ greater than the *maximum* of the set. > - **Scalar subqueries** must return exactly one row/column — otherwise the query errors out at runtime. > - **Derived tables** (subqueries in `FROM`) must be aliased using `AS`. > - **Execution order** is *always* from the innermost block outward — never top-down. > - **Optimizer rewrites:** Most nested subqueries are internally converted to joins by the query optimizer for performance. > - **Set operations on subqueries:** `IN` corresponds to intersection, `NOT IN` to set difference, `EXISTS` to non-empty intersection. > - **Edge case — empty subquery result:** `> ALL (empty)` is `TRUE` (vacuous), but `> ANY (empty)` is `FALSE`. > - **KTU exam weightage:** Expect a 3-mark direct question and a 7- or 14-mark application question per exam cycle. ---

topics.title: Views
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Views

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Views in Database Management Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **View**: A *view* is a **virtual relation** defined by a query against one or more base relations (or other views) that is stored in the database as a named `SELECT` statement. It does **not** physically store tuples of its own; rather, the DBMS materializes the result of the view's defining query at the time the view is *referenced* in a user query. Conceptually, a view provides **data abstraction, logical data independence, and controlled security** by hiding subsets or combinations of underlying tables.

Mathematically, if a base relation $R$ exists, a view $V$ is defined as:

$$V \equiv \sigma_{\text{predicate}} (\pi_{\text{attributes}} (R \bowtie R_1 \bowtie R_2 \dots))$$

In other words, $V$ is the *result-set* of a stored query, treated as a single logical table.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a view as a "Window" or a "Saved Lens" into your database.**
>
> Imagine a large office building with many rooms (your **base tables** holding all raw data — employee salaries, department codes, project assignments, etc.). Most employees are not allowed to enter every room. So the management installs **windows** into specific rooms, each window showing a *custom, filtered view* of what's inside. A window does not *create* a new copy of the room — it just gives you a *pre-defined perspective*. If the room's contents change, every window looking into that room automatically reflects the new state. **That window is your VIEW.**

For example, a view named `CS_Students` might show only `roll_no`, `name`, and `cgpa` of students belonging to the `CSE` department — even if the underlying `Student` table actually has 20 columns. The user querying `CS_Students` never sees the other columns; the view *protects* and *simplifies* access.

---

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Storage Status |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **Base Table** | Physically stored relation with real tuples | Physically stored on disk |
| **View** | Virtual relation defined by a stored query | **Not physically stored** (no separate copy of data) |
| **Materialized View** | A view whose result is physically stored and periodically refreshed | Physically stored (trade-off: speed vs. freshness) |
| **View Definition** | The `SELECT` query that defines the view | Stored in the **System Catalog / Data Dictionary** |
| **View Materialization** | The act of executing the view's defining query to produce tuples on demand | Done at query time (lazy) or pre-computed (eager) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**: A view is stored only as a **definition** in the *Data Dictionary / System Catalog*. The tuples are produced **only when referenced** by a user query, a process called **view resolution** (also known as *query modification* in some textbooks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Views? — The Three Pillars

1. **Logical Data Independence** — If the base table schema changes (e.g., a column is split into two), we can recreate a view exposing the *old* column shape. Application programs that use the view continue to work unchanged.
2. **Security / Access Control** — A view exposes only selected rows/columns. We can grant a user permission `GRANT SELECT ON V TO user1` without giving access to the underlying base table.
3. **Query Simplicity & Customization** — Complex multi-table joins can be encapsulated as a view, so end-users query a "single table" instead of writing 5-way joins repeatedly.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Views

Views can be categorized along **four orthogonal axes**. Mastering this matrix is critical for KTU exams.

### Axis 1: Storage Strategy
| Type | Storage Behavior | Refresh |
|------|-----------------|---------|
| **Virtual (Non-materialized) View** | No physical storage; tuples generated on demand | Real-time (always fresh) |
| **Materialized View** | Result physically stored as a table | Periodic / on-commit / on-demand |

### Axis 2: Updatability (CRUD Capability)
| Type | Can we run `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` on it? |
|------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Updatable View** | Yes, under strict conditions (see §2.4) |
| **Read-Only View** | No — contains `DISTINCT`, `GROUP BY`, `JOIN`, aggregates, subqueries |
| **Inline View** | A subquery in the `FROM` clause — also called a *derived table* |

### Axis 3: Structural Complexity
| Type | Defining Query Uses |
|------|---------------------|
| **Simple View** | A single base table, no aggregates, no `GROUP BY`, no `DISTINCT` |
| **Complex View** | Joins, aggregations, subqueries, `GROUP BY`, `DISTINCT` |

### Axis 4: Row Origin
| Type | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Row-Subset View** | `WHERE` clause restricts rows (vertical/horizontal slicing) |
| **Column-Subset View** | `SELECT` clause restricts columns |
| **Joined View** | Combines columns from multiple tables |
| **Aggregated View** | Uses `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MIN`, `MAX` |
| **Partitioned View** | Union of disjoint row sets from multiple tables |

---

## 2.3 The `CREATE VIEW` Statement — KTU Reference Syntax

```sql
CREATE [OR REPLACE] [MATERIALIZED] VIEW view_name [(column_alias, ...)]
AS subquery
[WITH [CASCADED | LOCAL] CHECK OPTION];
```

**Component breakdown:**

- `OR REPLACE` — Recreates the view if it already exists (no need to `DROP` first).
- `MATERIALIZED` — (PostgreSQL / Oracle) physically stores the result.
- `column_alias` — Optional explicit names for the view's columns.
- `WITH CHECK OPTION` — **Refuses** any `INSERT` or `UPDATE` that would make a row *disappear* from the view because it no longer satisfies the view's `WHERE` clause. This **preserves view invariant**.

---

## 2.4 The KTU-Critical Rule: When is a View Updatable?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A view is **updatable** in standard SQL (and most DBMSs) **if and only if every row of the view maps to exactly one row of a single underlying base table**, and the DBMS can unambiguously translate the modification.

The view is **NOT updatable** if its defining query contains **any** of:

- `DISTINCT`
- Aggregate functions (`SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MIN`, `MAX`)
- `GROUP BY` or `HAVING`
- `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`
- A `JOIN` across multiple tables
- A **subquery in the `SELECT` list**
- A `FROM` clause referencing more than one table

Mathematically, the **view update translatability test** can be expressed as: for every tuple $t_v \in V$, the inverse mapping $f^{-1}(t_v)$ must yield a **unique** tuple $t_b \in R_{base}$.

$$\forall t_v \in V,\;\; \vert f^{-1}(t_v) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(uniqueness condition)}$$

If this condition fails, the view is read-only.

---

## 2.5 The `WITH CHECK OPTION` — Formal Semantics

Let a view $V$ be defined by a predicate $P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$. When `WITH CHECK OPTION` is specified:

$$\text{For every } \text{INSERT} / \text{UPDATE} \;\tau \;\text{on}\; V:\;\; P(\tau) \;=\; \text{TRUE}$$

If the modified tuple would violate $P$, the DBMS **rejects** the modification with an error such as:

> *"ORA-01402: view WITH CHECK OPTION where-clause violation"*

- `LOCAL CHECK OPTION` — The check is enforced only against the immediate view's predicate.
- `CASCADED CHECK OPTION` — The check is enforced against **all** underlying views' predicates in the view hierarchy.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | View definition | $V \equiv \text{stored}(Q(R_1, R_2, \dots))$ | Conceptual / 3-mark |
| 2 | Updatability | $\vert f^{-1}(t_v) \vert = 1$ | 7-mark derivation |
| 3 | `WITH CHECK OPTION` invariant | $\forall \tau_{\text{new}} \in V:\; P(\tau_{\text{new}}) = \text{TRUE}$ | 3-mark |
| 4 | View Resolution | Replace view name $V$ in user query with its defining $Q$ | Algorithm question |
| 5 | Materialized view cost | Space $= \vert V \vert$, Time$_{\text{refresh}} \propto \vert R_{\text{base}} \vert$ | Concept |
| 6 | `DROP VIEW` behavior (no `CASCADE`) | Fails if other views reference it | 3-mark |
| 7 | `DROP VIEW … CASCADE` | Drops view + all dependent views | 3-mark |
| 8 | `OR REPLACE` clause | Atomically redefines view preserving grants (in some DBMS) | 3-mark |
| 9 | `FORCE` view (Oracle) | Create view even if base tables don't exist | Concept |
| 10 | `NOFORCE` view | Default; requires base tables to exist | Concept |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems** — A *teller* view of the `Account` table hides the customer's `pin_hash` and `daily_limit_internal` columns.
- **Healthcare EHR** — A *nurse* view of the `Patient` table exposes allergies and vitals but hides psychiatric notes.
- **Data Warehousing** — *Materialized views* cache pre-aggregated OLAP cubes (e.g., monthly sales by region) for sub-second dashboard reporting.
- **Multi-Tenant SaaS** — Each tenant gets a view that filters rows by `tenant_id`; physical tables are shared.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Creating and Querying a Simple View

**Base Schema:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name       VARCHAR(50),
    dept_id    INT,
    salary     DECIMAL(10,2)
);

CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name  VARCHAR(50)
);
```

**Step 1 — Define a view that exposes only `CSE` department employees, with no salary column visible:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW CSE_Employees AS
SELECT emp_id, name, dept_id
FROM   Employee
WHERE  dept_id = (SELECT dept_id FROM Department WHERE dept_name = 'CSE');
```

**Step 2 — User queries the view (acts exactly like a table):**

```sql
SELECT * FROM CSE_Employees;
```

**Step 3 — The DBMS performs *view resolution* (query modification). The user's query is rewritten as:**

```sql
SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.dept_id
FROM   Employee e
WHERE  e.dept_id = (SELECT d.dept_id
                    FROM   Department d
                    WHERE  d.dept_name = 'CSE');
```

**Step 4 — Result: every row of `CSE_Employees` maps to exactly one row of `Employee`** (because `emp_id` is a primary key). Therefore this view is **updatable**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `WITH CHECK OPTION` Demonstration

**View Definition:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW High_Salary_Employees AS
SELECT emp_id, name, salary
FROM   Employee
WHERE  salary > 50000
WITH CHECK OPTION;
```

**Attempt 1 — Legitimate update (allowed):**

```sql
UPDATE High_Salary_Employees
SET    salary = 60000
WHERE  emp_id = 101;
```
Here the new `salary = 60000` still satisfies `salary > 50000`, so the predicate $P(\text{new tuple}) = \text{TRUE}$. ✔

**Attempt 2 — Illegal update (rejected):**

```sql
UPDATE High_Salary_Employees
SET    salary = 30000
WHERE  emp_id = 101;
```
The new row would have `salary = 30000$, which violates the predicate. The DBMS throws a check-option violation. ✘

**Formal verification of Attempt 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\tau_{\text{new}}) &= (\text{new\_salary} > 50000) \\
                     &= (30000 > 50000) \\
                     &= \text{FALSE}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $P(\tau_{\text{new}}) = \text{FALSE}$, the `WITH CHECK OPTION` rejects the operation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Detecting Whether a View is Updatable

Given the view:

```sql
CREATE VIEW Dept_Summary AS
SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS emp_count, AVG(e.salary) AS avg_sal
FROM   Department d LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP  BY d.dept_name;
```

**Decision steps:**

1. Does it use an aggregate? **Yes** (`COUNT`, `AVG`). → **Not updatable**.
2. Does it use `GROUP BY`? **Yes**. → **Not updatable**.
3. Does it use a `JOIN`? **Yes**. → **Not updatable**.
4. Can we map each view row to a single base table row? **No** (the row represents an aggregate of many employees). → **Not updatable**.

Conclusion: `Dept_Summary` is a **read-only** view. Any `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` against it must fail.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — View Resolution Algorithm (Pseudocode)

When a user issues `SELECT … FROM V …`, the DBMS executes the following **view resolution** procedure:

```
Algorithm ViewResolution(userQuery Q):
    1. Parse Q and build a parse tree T.
    2. Repeat until no view names remain in T:
         (a) Pick any leaf node V_i in T that is a view name.
         (b) Look up V_i's defining query Q_i in the System Catalog.
         (c) Substitute the leaf V_i in T with the parse tree of Q_i.
         (d) Apply column-alias renamings if V_i has explicit aliases.
    3. Optimize the expanded parse tree.
    4. Execute it and return tuples to the user.
```

**Subtlety for KTU**: If the view definition itself references *another* view, step 2(a)–(d) is applied recursively — this is called **nested view resolution** or **view composition**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — `CREATE VIEW` Clause × Effect

| Clause | Effect on Storage | Effect on Updates | KTU Pitfall |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| (none) | Virtual | Updatable if rules allow | Default; students forget column aliases |
| `OR REPLACE` | Virtual | Preserved | Does NOT preserve privileges in all DBMSs |
| `MATERIALIZED` | Physical | Not updatable directly | Must be `REFRESH`ed |
| `WITH CHECK OPTION` | Virtual | Restricted to invariant-preserving updates | Often missed in `INSERT` test questions |
| `CASCADED CHECK OPTION` | Virtual | Checks ALL underlying view predicates | Default in many DBMSs |
| `LOCAL CHECK OPTION` | Virtual | Checks only the immediate view predicate | Oracle-specific syntax |
| `FORCE` (Oracle) | Virtual | May be invalid until base tables exist | Common 3-mark question |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — How a View Fits Into the DBMS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA["End User or Application Program"] -->|"Issues SELECT against View V"| qe["Query Processor"]
    qe -->|"Lookup V in catalog"| catalog["System Catalog / Data Dictionary"]
    catalog -->|"Returns defining query Q of V"| qe
    qe -->|"View Resolution: substitute V with Q"| opt["Query Optimizer"]
    opt -->|"Optimized plan referencing base tables"| exec["Query Execution Engine"]
    exec -->|"Read pages"| disk[("Disk: Base Tables R1, R2, ...")]
    disk -->|"Tuples"| exec
    exec -->|"Materialized result of Q"| result["Result set returned to user"]
    result --> userA

    classDef user fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    class userA user
    class qe,opt,exec engine
    class disk storage
```

**Reading the diagram:** The view itself is *not* on the disk path. The view lives only as a definition in the **System Catalog**. The DBMS expands (resolves) the view into its underlying base-table query at execution time.

---

## 4.2 View Resolution Flowchart (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["User submits: SELECT * FROM V WHERE cond"]) --> parse["Parse SQL & Build Parse Tree"]
    parse --> scan{"Any view name V in parse tree?"}
    scan -->|"Yes"| lookup["Fetch Q_V from System Catalog"]
    lookup --> rename["Apply column aliases of V if any"]
    rename --> substitute["Substitute leaf V with parse tree of Q_V"]
    substitute --> scan
    scan -->|"No - all views expanded"| opt["Optimize expanded query"]
    opt --> exec["Execute against base tables"]
    exec --> out(["Return result rows to user"])

    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    class parse,lookup,rename,substitute,opt,exec proc
    class scan decision
    class start,out io
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Updatability Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["View Definition (subquery)"] --> a1{"Aggregates?"}
    a1 -->|"Yes"| ro1["Mark: Read-Only"]
    a1 -->|"No"| a2{"GROUP BY / HAVING?"}
    a2 -->|"Yes"| ro2["Mark: Read-Only"]
    a2 -->|"No"| a3{"DISTINCT / UNION?"}
    a3 -->|"Yes"| ro3["Mark: Read-Only"]
    a3 -->|"No"| a4{"JOIN of multiple tables?"}
    a4 -->|"Yes"| ro4["Mark: Read-Only"]
    a4 -->|"No"| a5{"Subquery in SELECT list?"}
    a5 -->|"Yes"| ro5["Mark: Read-Only"]
    a5 -->|"No"| ok["Mark: UPDATABLE - 1-to-1 row mapping exists"]
    ok --> check{"WITH CHECK OPTION specified?"}
    check -->|"Yes"| enforce["Restrict updates to preserve view predicate P"]
    check -->|"No"| free["Allow all updates that DBMS can translate"]
    ro1 --> out(["View is READ-ONLY"])
    ro2 --> out
    ro3 --> out
    ro4 --> out
    ro5 --> out

    classDef yes fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef no fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    class a1,a2,a3,a4,a5 yes
    class ok,enforce,free no
    class input,check,out neutral
```

---

## 4.4 View Hierarchy (Nested Views) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base1[("Base Table: Employee")] --> v1["V1: CSE_Employees"]
    base2[("Base Table: Department")] --> v1
    v1 --> v2["V2: CSE_Senior_Staff (salary > 70K)"]
    v2 --> v3["V3: CSE_TopPerformers (uses V2 + aggregate subquery)"]
    v1 --> v4["V4: CSE_Roster (simple rename of V1)"]
    v2 --> v5["V5: CSE_Payroll_Report (joins V2 with another base table)"]

    classDef base fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef view fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    class base1,base2 base
    class v1,v2,v3,v4,v5 view
```

**Interpretation:** A query against `V5` will recursively resolve `V5 → V2 → V1 → base tables`. This recursive substitution is exactly what the View Resolution algorithm in §3.4 performs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a view. How is it different from a base table?**

**Model Answer:**

> A *view* is a virtual table defined by a stored `SELECT` query in the database. Unlike a **base table**, a view does not store its own data physically; the result is computed from underlying base tables (or other views) every time the view is referenced.
>
> Differences:
> 1. **Storage** — Base tables physically store tuples on disk; views do not (except materialized views).
> 2. **Independence** — Views provide logical data independence; a schema change in a base table can be hidden by redefining the view.
> 3. **Updates** — Base tables are always updatable; views are updatable only under strict conditions.
> 4. **Purpose** — Base tables hold the actual data; views provide customized, secure perspectives on the data.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the purpose of the `WITH CHECK OPTION` clause in view definition?**

**Model Answer:**

> The `WITH CHECK OPTION` clause ensures that any `INSERT` or `UPDATE` performed *through* the view does not produce a row that violates the view's defining `WHERE` predicate. In other words, the modified row must still be *visible* through the view after the change. This preserves the view's **logical invariant** $P(\tau_{\text{new}}) = \text{TRUE}$ for every modified tuple $\tau$. It is used to maintain the consistency of data accessible via the view.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of views in DBMS. Discuss the different types of views with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is an updatable view? State the conditions under which a view is considered updatable. Write SQL statements to demonstrate the creation of an updatable view with `WITH CHECK OPTION` on a suitable schema. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — Types of Views (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A view is a virtual relation defined by a query stored in the data dictionary. The tuples are produced when the view is referenced — a process called **view resolution**.

**Types of views (5 marks):**

1. **Simple View** — Based on a single base table with no aggregates, `GROUP BY`, or `JOIN`.
   ```sql
   CREATE VIEW CS_Students AS
   SELECT roll_no, name FROM Student WHERE dept = 'CSE';
   ```

2. **Complex View** — Contains joins, aggregates, or subqueries.
   ```sql
   CREATE VIEW DeptStats AS
   SELECT d.dept_name, AVG(e.salary) AS avg_sal
   FROM Department d JOIN Employee e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
   GROUP BY d.dept_name;
   ```

3. **Materialized View** — Physically stores the result; needs explicit refresh.
   ```sql
   CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW MonthlySales AS
   SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM Sales GROUP BY region;
   ```

4. **Inline View (Derived Table)** — A subquery in the `FROM` clause of another `SELECT`; not stored in the catalog.
   ```sql
   SELECT * FROM (SELECT emp_id, salary FROM Employee) AS temp WHERE salary > 50000;
   ```

5. **Updatable vs Read-Only View** — Discussed in A(b) below.

6. **Partitioned / Joined / Aggregated / Row-Subset / Column-Subset** — Classify by the operation the view performs on the base relation.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Updatable Views with `WITH CHECK OPTION` (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** An *updatable view* is one through which the DBMS can unambiguously translate `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE` operations back to a single underlying base-table row.

**Conditions for updatability (3 marks):** A view is updatable if its defining query uses only a single base table and contains **none** of the following:
- `DISTINCT`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`
- Aggregate functions (`SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MIN`, `MAX`)
- `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`
- A `JOIN` across multiple tables
- A subquery in the `SELECT` list

Formally, the row-mapping must be one-to-one: $\vert f^{-1}(t_v) \vert = 1$.

**SQL demonstration (2 marks):**

```sql
-- Base table
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name   VARCHAR(50),
    dept   VARCHAR(20),
    salary DECIMAL(10,2)
);

-- Updatable view with CHECK OPTION
CREATE VIEW CSE_HighPaid AS
SELECT emp_id, name, salary
FROM   Employee
WHERE  dept = 'CSE' AND salary > 50000
WITH CHECK OPTION;

-- Allowed update (still satisfies predicate)
UPDATE CSE_HighPaid SET salary = 70000 WHERE emp_id = 1;

-- Rejected update (would violate predicate)
UPDATE CSE_HighPaid SET salary = 30000 WHERE emp_id = 1;
-- ERROR: view WITH CHECK OPTION where-clause violation
```

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the view resolution process with a suitable example. How does the query processor handle nested views? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of views. How does a materialized view differ from a regular view? When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — View Resolution (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks):** *View resolution* (also called *query modification*) is the process by which the DBMS substitutes a view name appearing in a user query with the view's defining `SELECT` subquery. The expanded query is then optimized and executed against the base tables.

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Parse the user query and build a parse tree.
2. Locate any view name in the parse tree.
3. Replace each view name with the parse tree of its defining query (recursively, for nested views).
4. Apply any column-alias renaming.
5. Optimize and execute the rewritten query.

**Example (2 marks):**

```sql
-- View
CREATE VIEW IT_Staff AS
SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM Employee WHERE dept = 'IT';

-- User query
SELECT name FROM IT_Staff WHERE salary > 60000;

-- After view resolution
SELECT name
FROM   Employee
WHERE  dept = 'IT' AND salary > 60000;
```

**Nested view handling:** If `IT_Staff` itself is defined using another view `Staff_Base`, the resolver applies the substitution recursively: `IT_Staff → Staff_Base → Employee`. The depth of recursion is limited only by the system's ability to detect cycles.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Advantages, Limitations, Materialized Views (7 marks)

**Advantages of Views (2.5 marks):**
1. **Data security** — Restrict column/row access without altering base tables.
2. **Logical data independence** — Schema evolution of base tables can be masked.
3. **Query simplicity** — Encapsulate complex joins for end users.
4. **Data integrity via `WITH CHECK OPTION`** — Enforces invariants on modifications.
5. **Customized user perspectives** — Different users see different slices of the same data.

**Limitations of Views (1.5 marks):**
1. **Performance overhead** — View resolution adds a parsing/substitution cost.
2. **Update restrictions** — Most complex views are read-only.
3. **No indexing** — Indexes cannot be created directly on (non-materialized) views.
4. **Schema-binding fragility** — Dropping or altering a base table can invalidate dependent views.

**Materialized vs Regular View (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Regular (Virtual) View | Materialized View |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Storage** | None (definition only) | Physical copy on disk |
| **Freshness** | Always current | Stale until refreshed |
| **Read speed** | Slower (computed on demand) | Much faster (pre-computed) |
| **Write cost** | No extra write cost | Refresh cost on base-table updates |
| **Indexable** | No | Yes |
| **Use case** | OLTP, security, simple abstractions | OLAP, reporting, dashboards, data warehouses |

**When to prefer (in conclusion):** Use **virtual views** for OLTP, security, and small datasets where freshness matters. Use **materialized views** for OLAP, BI dashboards, and large aggregation queries where read performance outweighs the cost of periodic refresh.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in view questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `CREATE VIEW` with `CREATE TABLE`** — Views do **not** store data; writing "a view stores a copy of the data" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the `WITH CHECK OPTION` clause's purpose** — Students often state it "checks for errors" without mentioning the **view predicate invariant**.
> 3. **Listing updatability conditions incompletely** — Examiners expect the *complete* list (no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY`, no `JOIN`, no aggregates, no subquery in `SELECT`). Missing one item ⇒ 0.5–1 mark lost.
> 4. **Confusing `LOCAL` vs `CASCADED CHECK OPTION`** — Default in most DBMSs is `CASCADED`; many students write "default is LOCAL".
> 5. **Writing the view's `CREATE` statement without the semicolon** — Often loses 0.5 mark in SQL-syntax sub-parts.
> 6. **Forgetting to qualify column names with table aliases** in multi-table view definitions — produces ambiguous column errors.
> 7. **Stating "view can always be updated"** — outright wrong; nearly every complex view is read-only.
> 8. **Mixing up `DROP VIEW` vs `DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW`** syntax in PostgreSQL/Oracle.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **view** is a *virtual table* defined by a stored query; it does **not** physically store tuples.
- The view's **definition** is stored in the **System Catalog / Data Dictionary**; its tuples are produced at query time via **view resolution** (query modification).
- Views are used for **security, logical data independence, query simplicity, and data abstraction**.
- A view is **updatable** only if its defining query has a **one-to-one row mapping** with a single base table — i.e., no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY`, no aggregates, no `JOIN`, no subquery in `SELECT`, no set operations.
- **`WITH CHECK OPTION`** enforces that every `INSERT`/`UPDATE` through the view continues to satisfy the view's `WHERE` predicate.
- **`CASCADED CHECK OPTION`** (often the default) enforces the predicate of *all* underlying views; **`LOCAL CHECK OPTION`** enforces only the immediate view's predicate.
- **`CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW`** atomically redefines an existing view without losing grants in many DBMSs.
- **`MATERIALIZED VIEW`** physically stores the result; needs explicit `REFRESH`; ideal for OLAP/reporting, unsuitable for OLTP freshness.
- **Inline view** = subquery in the `FROM` clause; not stored in the catalog.
- **Nested views** are resolved recursively: a query on view $V_3$ expands to $V_2 \to V_1 \to$ base tables.
- **`DROP VIEW name [CASCADE]`** removes a view; `CASCADE` also drops dependent views and integrity constraints referencing it.
- A view can be created using `FORCE` (Oracle) even before base tables exist, but it remains *invalid* until the base tables are created.
- A view that exposes only some columns is **column-subset**; one that filters rows is a **row-subset** view; one that combines tables is a **joined view** (often read-only).
- View **resolution** happens during query optimization; it is **transparent to the user** — the user simply queries the view as if it were a table.
- Views provide **logical data independence**: applications written against views survive base-table schema changes (e.g., column splits, renames).
- Views **cannot have primary keys, foreign keys, or unique constraints** defined directly on them (in standard SQL); the underlying base tables do.
- **`INSTEAD OF` triggers** (in Oracle/SQL Server) can be defined on otherwise non-updatable views to make them updatable — this is an advanced topic; KTU may ask it as a 3-mark concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assertions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assertions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **SQL:1999** standard (and subsequently adopted by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** DBMS syllabus under *Module 2 – Integrity Constraints*), an **Assertion** is a named, declarative schema-level constraint that is guaranteed by the database management system to remain true at **all times** for every possible database state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> An assertion is an expression whose truth value the DBMS is expected to maintain at all times. If an assertion is violated by any modification (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on any relation involved, the entire transaction is rejected and rolled back.

Mathematically, an assertion $A$ over a database schema $S$ is a predicate:

$$
A : \text{Inst}(S) \rightarrow \{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}
$$

where $\text{Inst}(S)$ is the set of all possible instances (states) of the schema $S$. The DBMS must ensure that for every committed instance $I \in \text{Inst}(S)$:

$$
A(I) = \text{TRUE}
$$

This is fundamentally different from `CHECK` constraints, which are **table-scoped**. Assertions are **schema-scoped** — they can reference multiple tables and any complex predicate expressible in SQL.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Vault Rule"**
>
> Imagine a bank where the golden rule is: *"The total cash in Vault A plus the total cash in Vault B must never exceed 10 million rupees."* This rule involves **two separate vaults** (two tables) and a mathematical sum (a cross-table computation).
>
> A `CHECK` constraint is like a rule applied to a **single vault's door** (e.g., "no box inside Vault A may weigh more than 50 kg"). An **Assertion** is like the **central bank auditor's rule** that simultaneously watches *all vaults together*. If any transaction in any vault could ever break the combined rule, the entire transaction is blocked.
>
> This is precisely why assertions are the most powerful (and computationally expensive) integrity mechanism in SQL.

## 1.3 Where Assertions Fit in the KTU Integrity Hierarchy

| Constraint Type | Scope | Enforced When | KTU 2024 Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NOT NULL** | Single Column | Row insertion | Foundational |
| **UNIQUE** | Single/Multiple Columns | Row insertion/update | Foundational |
| **PRIMARY KEY** | Single Table | Row insertion/update | High |
| **FOREIGN KEY** | Across Two Tables | Row insertion/update/delete | Very High |
| **CHECK** | Single Table (Row/Attribute) | Row insertion/update | High |
| **ASSERTION** | **Whole Schema (Multiple Tables)** | **Any modification affecting referenced tables** | **Module 2 (Current Focus)** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** Whenever a constraint references **two or more tables** or involves an **aggregate function** (SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN), the textbook answer is an **Assertion** — *not* a `CHECK` constraint.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an Assertion

Every assertion in SQL consists of three mandatory logical components. Understanding this anatomy is critical for the **ESE Part B (14 marks)** questions.

### Component 1: Name
A globally unique identifier within the schema, declared via `CREATE ASSERTION`.

### Component 2: Check Predicate
A Boolean expression enclosed within `CHECK (...)`. The predicate may include:
- Standard comparison operators ($=,\neq,<,>,\le,\ge$)
- Logical connectives ($\land, \lor, \lnot$)
- SQL aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN)
- Subqueries (nested SELECT statements)
- Quantifiers via subquery existence checks

### Component 3: Schema-wide Validity Window
The DBMS must re-evaluate the assertion **after every transaction** that touches any relation referenced in the predicate.

## 2.2 General Syntax (SQL Standard)

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION <assertion_name>
    CHECK ( <predicate_expression> );
```

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** The standard SQL syntax is `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK`. There is **no** `ON` clause, no `FOR` clause, and no trigger-style event specification. The DBMS handles enforcement transparently.

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 Module 2)

| # | Predicate Pattern | SQL Construct | Example Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\forall t \in R, P(t)$ | `NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM R WHERE NOT P)` | Every employee belongs to a valid department |
| 2 | $\exists t \in R, P(t)$ | `EXISTS (SELECT * FROM R WHERE P)` | At least one manager per department |
| 3 | $\sum_{t \in R} f(t) \le K$ | `CHECK ((SELECT SUM(salary) FROM Emp) <= K)` | Total salary budget cap |
| 4 | $\sum_{t \in R} f(t) = \sum_{s \in S} g(s)$ | `CHECK (A = B)` cross-table aggregate | Supply chain balance |
| 5 | $\|R\| \le K$ | `CHECK ((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM R) <= K)` | Maximum employee count |
| 6 | $\forall t \in R, t.x = \text{some constant}$ | `CHECK (NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM R WHERE x <> c))` | All loans from one branch |
| 7 | $\lnot \exists t, P(t)$ | `CHECK (NOT EXISTS (...))` | No student in two conflicting states |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Quantifier Translation Rule (Essential for KTU):**
>
> The KTU syllabus explicitly tests the conversion of English-language constraints into SQL. The most common conversion is:
>
> $$\forall t \in R, \, P(t) \iff \text{NOT EXISTS } (t \in R \text{ WHERE NOT } P)$$
>
> In words: "**For all tuples in R, P holds**" is logically equivalent to "**There is no tuple in R for which P does not hold**."

## 2.4 The KTU-Critical Distinction: `CHECK` vs. `ASSERTION`

This is the **single most frequently tested distinction** in KTU 2024 DBMS examinations. Students regularly lose marks by confusing the two.

| Attribute | `CHECK` Constraint | `ASSERTION` |
|---|---|---|
| **SQL Keyword** | `CREATE TABLE ... CHECK (...)` | `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK (...)` |
| **Scope of Reference** | Single table only (the one it is defined on) | **Any number of tables** in the schema |
| **Aggregate Functions** | **NOT permitted** in standard SQL (predicate evaluated per row) | **Permitted** across full tables |
| **Subqueries** | Limited / implementation-dependent | Fully permitted |
| **Enforcement Cost** | Low (row-level check) | **High** (full schema re-evaluation) |
| **Typical KTU Use Case** | `salary > 0` on a single table | "Total budget across all departments $\le$ 1 crore" |
| **Drop Command** | `ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT` | `DROP ASSERTION <name>` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Assertions are deployed in mission-critical database systems in domains such as:

- **Banking & Finance:** Ensuring that the sum of all loan amounts never exceeds the bank's regulatory capital ratio.
- **Aviation Scheduling:** Enforcing that the total flight hours assigned to any single pilot do not exceed legally mandated maximums.
- **Hospital Management:** Validating that the count of patients assigned to a nurse never exceeds a safety threshold, even across multiple wards.
- **Supply Chain:** Maintaining inventory balance equations (total inflow = total outflow + current stock).

The **trade-off** in real systems is that assertions are computationally expensive — many production DBMS (MySQL, Oracle) **deprecated** or never fully implemented them. However, they remain a **fundamental KTU syllabus concept** because they represent the purest form of declarative integrity in relational theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive worked derivations** translating English specifications into SQL Assertions. Each step is fully expanded — no shortcuts are used.

---

## Example 1: The Salary Cap Assertion (Single Table, Aggregate)

**English Specification:**
> "The sum of all salaries in the `Employee` table must not exceed ₹10,00,000."

### Step 1: Identify the Logical Predicate
The constraint is:
$$
\sum_{t \in \text{Employee}} t.\text{salary} \le 1000000
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Reference Scope
The constraint involves a **single table** but uses an **aggregate function** (`SUM`). Recall the high-yield rule: aggregates + CHECK are incompatible in standard SQL, so this **must be an assertion**.

### Step 3: Wrap the Predicate in a Subquery
The aggregate must appear inside a `SELECT` that returns a single scalar value, which is then compared against the threshold.

### Step 4: Form the Assertion

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION salary_cap
    CHECK ( (SELECT SUM(salary) FROM Employee) <= 1000000 );
```

### Step 5: Verification of Logic
- **If** the total salary is ₹9,00,000, then `(900000 <= 1000000)` evaluates to `TRUE` → the assertion holds → the transaction commits.
- **If** a new INSERT raises the total to ₹11,00,000, the DBMS detects `(1100000 <= 1000000)` is `FALSE` → the **entire transaction is aborted**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a CHECK constraint fails here:**
> A `CHECK` clause on `Employee` would be evaluated **per row**:
> ```sql
> CHECK (salary >= 0)   -- valid CHECK
> CHECK (SUM(salary) <= 1000000)   -- INVALID: aggregate in CHECK is rejected
> ```
> This is precisely why the KTU syllabus routes such questions to **assertions**.

---

## Example 2: The Cross-Table Loan Constraint (Two Tables, $\forall$ Quantifier)

**English Specification (Classic KTU Question — Banking Schema):**
> "Every loan recorded in the `Loan` table must appear in the `Borrower` table, meaning every loan must have at least one borrower."

### Step 1: Formalize the Predicate Using First-Order Logic
$$
\forall l \in \text{Loan}, \, \exists b \in \text{Borrower} : l.\text{loan\_number} = b.\text{loan\_number}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Universal-to-Existential Negation Trick
The KTU translation pattern is:

$$
\forall x, P(x) \iff \lnot \exists x, \lnot P(x)
$$

Applying this:

$$
\lnot \exists l \in \text{Loan} : \forall b \in \text{Borrower}, \, l.\text{loan\_number} \neq b.\text{loan\_number}
$$

Further negating the inner universal:

$$
\lnot \exists l \in \text{Loan} : \lnot \exists b \in \text{Borrower} : l.\text{loan\_number} = b.\text{loan\_number}
$$

### Step 3: Translate to SQL Using `NOT EXISTS`

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION every_loan_has_borrower
    CHECK ( NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT * FROM Loan AS L
        WHERE NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT * FROM Borrower AS B
            WHERE L.loan_number = B.loan_number
        )
    ) );
```

### Step 4: Step-by-Step Logical Trace
1. The **inner subquery** asks: "Is there a borrower for this loan?" If yes, it returns rows, and `NOT EXISTS` evaluates to `FALSE`.
2. The **outer subquery** asks: "Is there any loan for which the inner subquery returned no rows?" If yes, that loan is "orphaned."
3. The outermost `NOT EXISTS` says: "We forbid the existence of such orphaned loans."
4. If an orphaned loan is found, the assertion evaluates to `FALSE`, and the **causing transaction is rolled back** entirely.

---

## Example 3: The Budget Balance Assertion (Two Tables, Aggregate Equality)

**English Specification:**
> "The total value of all accounts in the `Account` table must equal the total deposit amount in the `Deposit` table."

### Step 1: Identify the Predicate

$$
\sum_{a \in \text{Account}} a.\text{balance} = \sum_{d \in \text{Deposit}} d.\text{amount}
$$

### Step 2: Subquery Translation

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION account_deposit_balance
    CHECK ( (SELECT SUM(balance) FROM Account) =
            (SELECT SUM(amount) FROM Deposit) );
```

### Step 3: Why an Assertion (Not a CHECK)
This is a textbook KTU 14-marker because it involves **two separate tables** and **cross-table aggregates**. Neither can be expressed as a `CHECK` constraint.

---

## Example 4: The Department Headcount Assertion (Count Bound)

**English Specification:**
> "No department may have more than 50 employees assigned to it."

### Step 1: Formalize Using Grouped Aggregation

$$
\forall d \in \text{Department}, \quad \lvert \{ e \in \text{Employee} : e.\text{dept\_id} = d.\text{dept\_id} \} \rvert \le 50
$$

### Step 2: Translate to SQL via `GROUP BY` Subquery

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION dept_headcount_limit
    CHECK ( 50 >= ALL (
        SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employee
        GROUP BY dept_id
    ) );
```

### Step 3: Logical Walkthrough
- The subquery `SELECT COUNT(*) ... GROUP BY dept_id` produces one row per department containing the employee count.
- The aggregate `>= ALL(...)` returns `TRUE` only if **every** department's count is $\le 50$.
- If any department exceeds 50, the assertion fails and the offending INSERT/UPDATE is rejected.

---

## Example 5: The Branch Account Pairing Assertion (Cross-Table $\lnot\exists$)

**English Specification:**
> "A customer cannot simultaneously hold both a checking account and a loan at the same branch."

### Step 1: Identify Tables
- `Account(customer, branch_name, account_type, ...)`
- `Loan(customer, branch_name, ...)`

### Step 2: Formalize the Predicate

$$
\lnot \exists a \in \text{Account}, \, l \in \text{Loan} :
a.\text{customer} = l.\text{customer} \land a.\text{branch} = l.\text{branch} \land a.\text{type} = \text{'checking'}
$$

### Step 3: SQL Assertion

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION no_checking_with_loan_at_same_branch
    CHECK ( NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT * FROM Account A, Loan L
        WHERE A.customer = L.customer
          AND A.branch_name = L.branch_name
          AND A.account_type = 'CHECKING'
    ) );
```

### Step 4: Comprehensive Step-by-Step Trace
1. The subquery performs a **natural join** on customer and branch between `Account` and `Loan`.
2. It filters for rows where the account type is `'CHECKING'`.
3. If **any row** results, it means a customer holds both a checking account and a loan at the same branch — a violation.
4. `NOT EXISTS` then negates this: it requires the subquery to return **zero rows**.
5. The DBMS enforces this on every relevant INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE across both tables.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Assertion Lifecycle Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **runtime decision flow** of the DBMS when an assertion is present in the schema. This is a high-frequency KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues SQL Statement INSERT UPDATE DELETE] --> B{Parse and Validate Syntax}
    B --> C[Optimizer Generates Execution Plan]
    C --> D[Identify All Assertions Referencing Modified Tables]
    D --> E[Execute Statement Tentatively in Workspace]
    E --> F[Re evaluate All Affected Assertions on Tentative State]
    F --> G{All Assertions Evaluate to TRUE}
    G --> H[Commit Transaction Permanent State Update]
    G --> I[Rollback Transaction Reject Statement]
    H --> J[Notify User Success]
    I --> K[Notify User Integrity Constraint Violation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The "tentative execution" step is crucial. The DBMS does **not** allow partial violations. Either **all** assertions hold after the change, or **none** of the change is persisted.

## 4.2 Constraint Scope Hierarchy Diagram
