This block diagram contrasts the operational scope of `CHECK` vs. `ASSERTION`, addressing the KTU 14-mark comparison question pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SchemaLevel[Schema Level]
        AS[ASSERTION]
    end
    subgraph TableLevel[Table Level]
        CK[CHECK Constraint]
    end
    subgraph ColumnLevel[Column Level]
        NN[NOT NULL]
        UN[UNIQUE]
        PK[PRIMARY KEY]
    end
    subgraph CrossTableLevel[Cross Table Level]
        FK[FOREIGN KEY]
    end
    AS -.->|References 1 or more tables| T1[Table Employee]
    AS -.->|References 1 or more tables| T2[Table Department]
    AS -.->|References 1 or more tables| T3[Table Project]
    CK -->|Restricted to single table| T1
    NN --> T1
    UN --> T1
    PK --> T1
    FK -->|Links two tables| T1
    FK -->|Links two tables| T2
```

## 4.3 SQL Statement-to-Assertion Translation Pipeline

This diagram visualizes the **structured translation process** from an English specification to a valid SQL `CREATE ASSERTION` statement. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this skill explicitly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Read English Specification Carefully] --> S2[Step 2 Identify All Tables and Attributes Involved]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Determine Logical Quantifier Type Forall Exists Negation]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Express Constraint in First Order Logic Notation]
    S4 --> S5{Does the Predicate Use an Aggregate Function or Reference Multiple Tables?}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Route to CREATE ASSERTION Path]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Route to CREATE TABLE CHECK Path]
    S6 --> S8[Step 5 Convert Universal Quantifier to NOT EXISTS Form if Needed]
    S8 --> S9[Step 6 Replace Logical Operators with SQL Equivalents AND OR NOT]
    S9 --> S10[Step 7 Wrap Subqueries in Parentheses and Verify Boolean Result]
    S10 --> S11[Step 8 Final SQL Statement CREATE ASSERTION Name CHECK Predicate]
    S7 --> S12[Final SQL Statement CREATE TABLE with inline CHECK]
```

## 4.4 Assertion Enforcement Mechanism Architecture

This architecture flow shows **what happens internally** in the DBMS storage engine when an assertion is being enforced during a multi-table transaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TXN[Incoming Transaction T] --> LOG[Transaction Log Begins]
    LOG --> MOD1[Modify Table A]
    LOG --> MOD2[Modify Table B]
    LOG --> MOD3[Modify Table C]
    MOD1 --> ASSESS[Assertion Evaluator Engine]
    MOD2 --> ASSESS
    MOD3 --> ASSESS
    ASSESS --> Q1[Query 1 Aggregate over Table A]
    ASSESS --> Q2[Query 2 Aggregate over Table B]
    ASSESS --> Q3[Query 3 Cross Join Probe]
    Q1 --> CMP[Boolean Comparator]
    Q2 --> CMP
    Q3 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Result TRUE| COMMIT[Write to Disk Commit T]
    CMP -->|Result FALSE| ROLLBACK[Discard Changes Abort T]
    COMMIT --> ACK1[Acknowledge Success to Client]
    ROLLBACK --> ACK2[Error Code Returned Integrity Violation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Insight:** Notice that the assertion evaluator is a **central bottleneck**. This is why assertions are *not* used casually in real-world OLTP systems. KTU students are expected to understand both the **logical correctness** and the **performance implications** of assertion-based design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define an assertion in SQL. How is it different from a CHECK constraint?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **assertion** is a schema-level constraint declared using `CREATE ASSERTION <name> CHECK (<predicate>)` that the DBMS is required to maintain as true for all database states.
>
> **Key distinctions from a CHECK constraint:**
> 1. **Scope:** Assertions span the **entire schema** and may reference multiple tables; CHECK constraints are **confined to a single table**.
> 2. **Aggregates:** Assertions **may use aggregate functions** like `SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`; CHECK constraints **may not** in standard SQL.
> 3. **Enforcement Cost:** Assertions trigger full schema re-evaluation, while CHECK constraints are evaluated per row.
>
> *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per distinct point.)*

---

### Question 2: Predicate Translation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Translate the following English constraint into an SQL assertion on the `Account` table:**
> "The total balance across all accounts must not exceed ₹50,00,000."

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION total_balance_cap
    CHECK ( (SELECT SUM(balance) FROM Account) <= 5000000 );
```

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The inner subquery `SELECT SUM(balance) FROM Account` computes the scalar total balance. The outer comparison `(sum <= 5000000)` evaluates the constraint. If the total exceeds the cap, the assertion fails and the transaction is rolled back.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Assertion Design `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**Consider the following banking schema:**
- `Branch(branch_name, branch_city, assets)`
- `Account(account_number, branch_name, balance)`
- `Loan(loan_number, branch_name, amount)`
- `Borrower(customer_id, loan_number)`
- `Depositor(customer_id, account_number)`

**Solve the following sub-parts:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Write an SQL assertion to enforce: "The total assets of all branches in the city of 'Kochi' must be at least 10 times the total loan amount issued by those branches."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formalize the predicate mathematically.**

$$
\sum_{b \in \text{Branch}, \, b.\text{branch\_city} = \text{'Kochi'}} b.\text{assets} \;\ge\; 10 \times \sum_{l \in \text{Loan}, \, l.\text{branch\_name} \in \text{Kochi\_Branches}} l.\text{amount}
$$

**Step 2: Build the SQL with correlated subqueries.**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION kochi_assets_loan_ratio
    CHECK (
        (SELECT SUM(assets) FROM Branch WHERE branch_city = 'Kochi')
        >=
        10 * (SELECT SUM(amount) FROM Loan
              WHERE branch_name IN (SELECT branch_name FROM Branch
                                    WHERE branch_city = 'Kochi'))
    );
```

**Step 3: Explanation (for marks).**
- The first subquery `SELECT SUM(assets) FROM Branch WHERE branch_city = 'Kochi'` returns the total assets of all Kochi branches.
- The second subquery first identifies all Kochi branches, then sums the loans issued by them.
- The factor `10 *` scales the loan total.
- The DBMS re-evaluates this assertion after every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on `Branch` or `Loan`.

*(Examiner's note: Award 4 marks for correct SQL, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for proper `CREATE ASSERTION` syntax.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an SQL assertion to enforce: "No customer may have more than 3 active loans simultaneously across all branches."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formalize the predicate.**

$$
\forall c \in \text{Borrower}, \quad \text{count}(l \in \text{Loan}: \exists b \in \text{Borrower}, b.\text{customer\_id} = c \land b.\text{loan\_number} = l.\text{loan\_number}) \le 3
$$

**Step 2: Apply the universal-to-existential translation.**

$$
\lnot \exists c \in \text{Customer} : \lvert \{ l \in \text{Loan} \mid \exists b \in \text{Borrower} : b.\text{customer\_id} = c \land b.\text{loan\_number} = l.\text{loan\_number} \} \rvert > 3
$$

**Step 3: SQL Assertion.**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION max_three_loans_per_customer
    CHECK ( NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT B.customer_id
        FROM Borrower B, Loan L
        WHERE B.loan_number = L.loan_number
        GROUP BY B.customer_id
        HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
    ) );
```

**Step 4: Explanation.**
- The inner query joins `Borrower` and `Loan` to count the loans per customer.
- `GROUP BY B.customer_id` partitions the rows by customer.
- `HAVING COUNT(*) > 3` filters for violators.
- If **any** such violator exists, the `NOT EXISTS` evaluates to `FALSE`, and the assertion rejects the transaction.

*(Examiner's note: Award 3 marks for correct `GROUP BY`/`HAVING` logic, 2 marks for `NOT EXISTS` wrapping, 1 mark for the outer `CREATE ASSERTION`, 1 mark for the explanation.)*

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Assertion Design `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**Consider the university schema:**
- `Department(dept_id, dept_name, hod_name)`
- `Student(roll_no, name, dept_id, year)`
- `Course(course_id, course_name, dept_id, credits)`
- `Enrollment(roll_no, course_id, grade)`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Write an SQL assertion ensuring: "Every student must be enrolled in at least one course in their own department."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formalize the universal predicate.**

$$
\forall s \in \text{Student}, \; \exists e \in \text{Enrollment}, c \in \text{Course} :
e.\text{roll\_no} = s.\text{roll\_no} \land e.\text{course\_id} = c.\text{course\_id} \land c.\text{dept\_id} = s.\text{dept\_id}
$$

**Step 2: Negate to use `NOT EXISTS`.**

$$
\lnot \exists s \in \text{Student} : \text{no matching enrollment in own department}
$$

**Step 3: SQL Assertion.**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION every_student_enrolled_in_own_dept
    CHECK ( NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT * FROM Student S
        WHERE NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT * FROM Enrollment E, Course C
            WHERE E.roll_no = S.roll_no
              AND E.course_id = C.course_id
              AND C.dept_id = S.dept_id
        )
    ) );
```

**Step 4: Verification.**
- For a given student $S$, the inner subquery looks for at least one enrollment in a course belonging to $S$'s department.
- If none exists, the inner `NOT EXISTS` becomes `TRUE`, and the outer subquery finds this "violator student."
- The outermost `NOT EXISTS` then negates it, making the assertion fail.

*(Examiner's note: This is a classic "double NOT EXISTS" pattern worth 7 marks. Award 3 marks for the inner subquery, 2 marks for the outer wrapping, 2 marks for the `CREATE ASSERTION` syntax.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an SQL assertion ensuring: "The total credits earned by any single student in a given academic year must not exceed 30."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formalize.**

$$
\forall s \in \text{Student}, \; \text{credit\_sum}(s) \le 30
$$

where the credit sum aggregates `Course.credits` over all enrollments of student $s$ where `grade` is not `'F'`.

**Step 2: SQL Assertion.**

```sql
CREATE ASSERTION max_credits_per_student
    CHECK ( 30 >= ALL (
        SELECT SUM(C.credits)
        FROM Student S
        JOIN Enrollment E ON S.roll_no = E.roll_no
        JOIN Course C ON E.course_id = C.course_id
        WHERE E.grade <> 'F'
        GROUP BY S.roll_no
    ) );
```

**Step 3: Explanation.**
- The join produces one row per (student, enrolled course with passing grade).
- `GROUP BY S.roll_no` partitions by student.
- `SUM(C.credits)` computes total earned credits per student.
- `30 >= ALL(...)` ensures **every** student's credit total is $\le 30$.

*(Examiner's note: Award 3 marks for join logic, 2 marks for `GROUP BY` and `ALL`, 2 marks for the assertion syntax.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Using `CHECK` instead of `ASSERTION` for multi-table/aggregate constraints:** This is the **#1 reason** students lose 2–4 marks on a 14-mark question. Any time the constraint touches **more than one table** or uses an **aggregate**, the correct answer is `CREATE ASSERTION`, never `CHECK`.
> 2. **Forgetting the outer `NOT EXISTS` in universal quantifier translations:** Many students translate "for all students..." directly without the logical negation, leading to incorrect semantics. Remember: `∀x, P(x) ⇔ ¬∃x, ¬P(x)`.
> 3. **Placing an aggregate function inside a `CHECK` constraint:** This is syntactically illegal in standard SQL and results in **zero marks** for the sub-part.
> 4. **Omitting the `CHECK` keyword inside `CREATE ASSERTION`:** The correct form is `CREATE ASSERTION <name> CHECK (<predicate>);`. Missing `CHECK` is a syntax error worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Using `WHERE` instead of `HAVING` after `GROUP BY`:** When filtering aggregated results, `WHERE` filters rows *before* grouping; `HAVING` filters *after*. This distinction is worth 1 mark in KTU evaluation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**
>
> - ✅ Assertion = **schema-level** constraint using `CREATE ASSERTION ... CHECK (predicate)`.
> - ✅ Assertions **may reference multiple tables**; CHECK constraints may not.
> - ✅ Assertions **may use aggregate functions** (SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN); CHECK constraints may not.
> - ✅ **Universal quantifier rule:** $\forall t, P(t) \iff \text{NOT EXISTS } (t \text{ WHERE NOT } P)$.
> - ✅ The DBMS **rejects the entire transaction** if any assertion is violated — no partial commits.
> - ✅ Syntax: `CREATE ASSERTION name CHECK ( subquery-based predicate );`
> - ✅ Drop syntax: `DROP ASSERTION name;`
> - ✅ Use `EXISTS` / `NOT EXISTS` for existential/universal quantification.
> - ✅ Use `GROUP BY` + `HAVING` when checking per-group aggregate bounds.
> - ✅ Use `>= ALL (...)` or `<= ALL (...)` to enforce uniform aggregate limits.
> - ✅ Assertions are **computationally expensive** — the DBMS re-evaluates them on every relevant modification.
> - ✅ Real DBMS like MySQL/Oracle have limited or no assertion support; PostgreSQL has historically lacked them too (a key KTU theory point).
> - ✅ Trigger logic can sometimes **emulate** assertions in commercial systems (mention this if asked about real-world alternatives).
> - ✅ **Forbidden in CHECK:** aggregates, subqueries in some dialects, cross-table references.
> - ✅ **Always test by tracing:** pick sample data, simulate an INSERT, and verify whether the assertion holds.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When you see a constraint involving the words *"total,"* *"sum,"* *"average,"* *"count,"* *"at most,"* *"at least,"* or referring to *two or more tables* — your first instinct must be to write a `CREATE ASSERTION` statement. This single heuristic will guide you through roughly **80% of KTU Module 2 questions** on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Triggers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **Trigger** is a specialized, named database object that is automatically executed (fired) in response to a specified data-modification event occurring on a specified table. Triggers implement the **ECA (Event–Condition–Action)** paradigm and are a cornerstone of **active database** behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A trigger is a procedural code that is automatically executed in response to certain events on a particular table or view in a database. Triggers are fundamentally tied to the **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** operations of `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE`.

Mathematically, a trigger $\mathcal{T}$ can be represented as a 4-tuple:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \langle E, \, C, \, A, \, T_p \rangle
$$

Where:
- $E$ = **Event** (e.g., INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
- $C$ = **Condition** (optional Boolean predicate evaluated against `OLD` / `NEW` row states)
- $A$ = **Action** (the procedural SQL block to execute)
- $T_p$ = **Triggering Point** in time (`BEFORE`, `AFTER`, or `INSTEAD OF`)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Security Alarm" Model

Imagine a bank vault. The vault itself is the **table** containing data (money). A **trigger** is analogous to a *programmed alarm system*:

- **Event ($E$):** Someone opens the door (`INSERT`), withdraws money (`UPDATE`), or removes a record (`DELETE`).
- **Condition ($C$):** The system checks — "Is it past 10 PM?" or "Is the withdrawal above ₹50,000?"
- **Action ($A$):** If the condition holds, the alarm rings, a log is written, or the police are called automatically — *without the thief having to do anything extra*.

Just like the alarm is **passive** (it does not need a user to invoke it), a trigger **listens** to a table and fires on its own when the event happens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Triggers are essential for enforcing **referential integrity**, **business rules**, **audit logging**, and **derived data maintenance** at the database level — which the KTU 2024 Scheme places under **CO2: Apply SQL and relational constraints for database design**.

## 1.3 Why Triggers? — The Procedural Extension of SQL

Standard SQL is **declarative** (you state *what* you want, not *how* to get it). Triggers extend SQL into the **procedural** domain using languages like **PL/pgSQL** (PostgreSQL) or **PL/SQL** (Oracle), allowing conditional logic, loops, and exception handling to be embedded *inside* the database engine.

> [!TIP]
> **Triggers vs. Constraints:** While `CHECK`, `PRIMARY KEY`, and `FOREIGN KEY` constraints can enforce *static* rules, triggers handle *dynamic, inter-table, and history-based* rules that constraints cannot express (e.g., "prevent a salary increase of more than 20% at once").

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The ECA Model — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

When a DML statement is issued against a triggered table, the DBMS executes the following precise sequence:

1. **Parse & Validate:** The DML statement (`INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`) is parsed by the query processor.
2. **Row Identification:** Affected rows are identified; for each, two pseudo-records are constructed:
   - `OLD` — state of the row *before* the operation (not available for `INSERT`).
   - `NEW` — state of the row *after* the operation (not available for `DELETE`).
3. **Trigger Selection:** The system checks the trigger catalog for any triggers defined on the target table for the current event.
4. **Timing Evaluation:** The trigger is queued based on its `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, or `INSTEAD OF` specification.
5. **Condition Check ($C$):** If a `WHEN` clause is present, the Boolean condition is evaluated using `OLD` / `NEW`.
6. **Action Execution ($A$):** If the condition is `TRUE` (or absent), the procedural body runs.
7. **Row/Statement Commit:** Depending on the trigger granularity (`FOR EACH ROW` vs `FOR EACH STATEMENT`), this is repeated per row or once per statement.

## 2.2 Classification Matrix of Triggers

Triggers are categorized along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU syllabus:

| Axis | Classification | Meaning | Use Case |
|------|---------------|---------|----------|
| **Timing** | `BEFORE` | Fires *before* the DML modifies the row | Validating / modifying `NEW` values |
| | `AFTER` | Fires *after* the DML succeeds | Auditing, logging, cascading updates |
| | `INSTEAD OF` | Fires *instead of* the DML (mainly on views) | Making non-updatable views updatable |
| **Granularity** | `FOR EACH ROW` | Fires once *per affected row* | Row-level business rules |
| | `FOR EACH STATEMENT` | Fires once *per SQL statement* | Bulk operation auditing |
| **Event** | `INSERT` | New row added | Default value population |
| | `UPDATE` | Row data modified | Audit trail of changes |
| | `DELETE` | Row removed | Archive deleted rows |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **canonical PostgreSQL syntax** tested in KTU 2024 Scheme practical and theory exams. Memorize the keyword ordering.

**Step 1 — Create the Trigger Function (procedural logic):**
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION trigger_function_name()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    -- procedural body
    RETURN NEW;       -- for INSERT / UPDATE
    -- RETURN OLD;    -- for DELETE
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

**Step 2 — Bind the Function to a Table as a Trigger:**
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trigger_name
{ BEFORE | AFTER | INSTEAD OF } { INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE }
ON table_name
[ FOR EACH { ROW | STATEMENT } ]
[ WHEN ( condition ) ]
EXECUTE FUNCTION trigger_function_name();
```

**Step 3 — Manage Triggers:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE table_name DISABLE TRIGGER trigger_name;
ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE  TRIGGER trigger_name;
DROP TRIGGER trigger_name ON table_name;
```

## 2.4 Pseudo-Record Reference Table (Row Variables)

| Trigger Event | `OLD` Available? | `NEW` Available? | Return Value |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|--------------|
| `INSERT` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `RETURN NEW;` |
| `UPDATE` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | `RETURN NEW;` |
| `DELETE` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | `RETURN OLD;` |

> [!WARNING]
> Forgetting to `RETURN NEW` (or `RETURN OLD` for `DELETE`) in a `BEFORE` trigger causes the operation to be **silently aborted** in some DBMSs — a common KTU exam pitfall!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Auto-debit audit logs on every transaction.
- **E-Commerce:** Maintaining a `cart_total` summary table synchronized with the `cart_items` table.
- **Hospital DBMS:** Preventing appointment booking past a doctor's max daily limit.
- **Inventory Management:** Auto-decrementing stock on a confirmed `ORDER` insert.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section constructs the complete trigger logic using **worked examples** in a step-by-step analytical format. Each example maps to a typical KTU 2024 Scheme question pattern.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `BEFORE INSERT` Trigger with Validation

**Problem Statement:** Create a trigger that prevents insertion of an employee with `salary < 0` into the `employee(emp_id, name, salary)` table.

### Step 1 — Define the Procedural Function
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION validate_salary()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Condition check
    IF NEW.salary < 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Salary cannot be negative. Given: %', NEW.salary;
    END IF;
    -- Allow the INSERT to proceed with (possibly modified) NEW
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

### Step 2 — Bind the Trigger
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_check_salary
BEFORE INSERT ON employee
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION validate_salary();
```

### Step 3 — Logical Flow Analysis

The complete control flow when a user issues:
```sql
INSERT INTO employee VALUES (101, 'Anu', -5000);
```

$$
\text{Parse} \rightarrow \text{Identify Event} = \text{INSERT} \rightarrow \text{Check Catalog for trg\_check\_salary}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Build NEW} = \{101, \text{'Anu'}, -5000\} \rightarrow \text{Fire trg\_check\_salary (BEFORE)}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Evaluate } C : (\text{NEW.salary} < 0) \Rightarrow (-5000 < 0) = \text{TRUE}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Execute } A : \text{RAISE EXCEPTION} \rightarrow \text{Transaction Aborted}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because the trigger fires **BEFORE** the actual write, no invalid row ever reaches disk — this is the **integrity-preserving** power of `BEFORE` triggers.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `AFTER UPDATE` Trigger for Audit Logging

**Problem Statement:** Maintain an `emp_audit(emp_id, old_salary, new_salary, changed_on)` table that automatically records every salary change.

### Step 1 — Create the Audit Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE emp_audit (
    audit_id    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_id      INTEGER,
    old_salary  NUMERIC(10,2),
    new_salary  NUMERIC(10,2),
    changed_on  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

### Step 2 — Define the Trigger Function
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_salary_change()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    IF OLD.salary IS DISTINCT FROM NEW.salary THEN
        INSERT INTO emp_audit(emp_id, old_salary, new_salary)
        VALUES (OLD.emp_id, OLD.salary, NEW.salary);
    END IF;
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

### Step 3 — Bind the Trigger
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_audit_salary
AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON employee
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION log_salary_change();
```

### Analytical Breakdown — Why `AFTER` and Why `IS DISTINCT FROM`?

$$
\text{Why AFTER?} \rightarrow \text{Because we need the UPDATE to succeed first; we are only recording history.}
$$

$$
\text{Why IS DISTINCT FROM?} \rightarrow \text{Null-safe inequality. } (\text{NULL} = \text{NULL}) \text{ is unknown in SQL, but IS DISTINCT FROM returns FALSE.}
$$

The truth table used by the DBMS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Old.salary} &= 50000, \quad \text{New.salary} = 60000 \\
\text{IS DISTINCT FROM} &\rightarrow \text{TRUE} \rightarrow \text{Log inserted.} \\
\text{Old.salary} &= 50000, \quad \text{New.salary} = 50000 \\
\text{IS DISTINCT FROM} &\rightarrow \text{FALSE} \rightarrow \text{No log.} \\
\text{Old.salary} &= \text{NULL}, \quad \text{New.salary} = 50000 \\
\text{IS DISTINCT FROM} &\rightarrow \text{TRUE} \rightarrow \text{Log inserted.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `INSTEAD OF` Trigger on a View

**Problem Statement:** Create a view `emp_dept` joining `employee` and `department`, and make it updatable using an `INSTEAD OF` trigger.

### Step 1 — Create the View
```sql
CREATE VIEW emp_dept AS
SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employee e
JOIN department d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

### Step 2 — Make the View Updatable
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_emp_dept()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    UPDATE employee
    SET name = NEW.name, salary = NEW.salary
    WHERE emp_id = OLD.emp_id;
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_view_update
INSTEAD OF UPDATE ON emp_dept
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION update_emp_dept();
```

### Analytical Insight

$$
\text{INSTEAD OF} \rightarrow \text{The original DML is REPLACED by the trigger body.}
$$

$$
\text{Use Case Mapping:} \quad \text{VIEWS (joins, aggregates)} \xrightarrow{\text{INSTEAD OF}} \text{Updatable}
$$

## 3.4 Trigger Execution Order Matrix (Multiple Triggers on Same Table)

When multiple triggers are defined for the same event, the KTU board expects you to know the **firing order**:

$$
\text{Firing Order} = \text{BEFORE-stmt} \rightarrow \text{BEFORE-row}_1 \rightarrow \text{BEFORE-row}_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{Actual DML} \rightarrow \text{AFTER-row}_1 \rightarrow \text{AFTER-row}_2 \rightarrow \text{AFTER-stmt}
$$

Alphabetical ordering is used among triggers of the same timing/granularity unless explicitly named with `BEFORE` / `AFTER` priority (Oracle-specific).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ECA Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues DML Statement] --> B[DBMS Parser Validates Syntax]
    B --> C[Identify Target Table and Affected Rows]
    C --> D{Does any Trigger exist for this Event?}
    D -- No --> E[Execute DML Normally]
    D -- Yes --> F[Build OLD and NEW Pseudo-Records]
    F --> G[Determine Trigger Timing: BEFORE / AFTER / INSTEAD OF]
    G --> H{Trigger Type?}
    H -- BEFORE --> I[Execute Trigger Action]
    I --> J{WHEN Condition True?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Apply OLD and NEW Modifications]
    J -- No --> L[Skip this Trigger]
    K --> M[Proceed to Actual DML Write]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Trigger Type?}
    N -- AFTER --> O[Execute AFTER Trigger Action]
    N -- INSTEAD OF --> P[Replace DML with Trigger Body]
    O --> Q[Commit Transaction]
    P --> Q
    E --> Q
    Q --> R[Return Status to User]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trigger Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[Triggers in SQL] --> Timing
    Root --> Granularity
    Root --> EventType
    Timing --> T1[BEFORE Trigger]
    Timing --> T2[AFTER Trigger]
    Timing --> T3[INSTEAD OF Trigger]
    Granularity --> G1[Row Level: FOR EACH ROW]
    Granularity --> G2[Statement Level: FOR EACH STATEMENT]
    EventType --> E1[INSERT Trigger]
    EventType --> E2[UPDATE Trigger]
    EventType --> E3[DELETE Trigger]
    T1 --> U1[Validates and Modifies NEW before Write]
    T2 --> U2[Audits and Cascades after Successful Write]
    T3 --> U3[Makes Views Updatable]
    G1 --> U4[Most Common in KTU Exams]
    G2 --> U5[Used for Bulk Operation Logging]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pseudo-Record Visibility Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSERT_Op [INSERT Operation]
        I1[OLD: Not Available] -.->|NULL| I2[NEW: Available with inserted values]
    end
    subgraph UPDATE_Op [UPDATE Operation]
        U1[OLD: Pre-update values] --> U2[NEW: Post-update values]
    end
    subgraph DELETE_Op [DELETE Operation]
        D1[OLD: Pre-delete values] -.->|NULL| D2[NEW: Not Available]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Trigger Interaction with DBMS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientLayer [Client Application Layer]
        A1[Application Code: JDBC or psql]
    end
    subgraph EngineLayer [DBMS Engine Layer]
        B1[Query Parser] --> B2[Query Optimizer]
        B2 --> B3[Execution Engine]
        B3 --> B4[Trigger Manager Module]
        B4 --> B5[Trigger Catalog: System Tables]
        B4 --> B6[Procedural Engine: PL pgSQL Interpreter]
    end
    subgraph StorageLayer [Storage Layer]
        C1[Base Tables: employee, department]
        C2[Audit Tables: emp_audit]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B6 --> B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Trigger Manager** is an internal DBMS module that intercepts DML execution, consults the **Trigger Catalog** (a system metadata table), invokes the **Procedural Engine** to execute the trigger body, and coordinates writes to both base and audit tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a trigger. Mention any two situations where triggers are useful in RDBMS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A trigger is a named database object that is automatically executed (fired) in response to a specified data-modification event (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`) on a specified table or view. Triggers follow the **Event–Condition–Action (ECA)** model and run within the same transaction as the triggering statement. *(1 mark)*

Two situations where triggers are useful: *(2 marks)*
1. **Enforcing complex business rules** that cannot be expressed using simple `CHECK` constraints (e.g., "salary increase cannot exceed 20% in one cycle").
2. **Maintaining audit logs** automatically — recording who changed what and when, without requiring application-level code.
3. *(Alternative)* **Maintaining derived data**, e.g., synchronizing a summary table with detailed transactions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between `BEFORE` and `AFTER` triggers with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `BEFORE` Trigger | `AFTER` Trigger |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Firing Time** | Executes *before* the DML modifies the row | Executes *after* the DML successfully modifies the row |
| **Can modify `NEW`?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (write is already committed) |
| **Typical Use** | Validation, default value population | Auditing, logging, cascading updates |
| **Example** | `BEFORE INSERT` to reject negative salary | `AFTER UPDATE` to log salary change to `emp_audit` |

*(1 mark per correct row, plus 1 mark for valid example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the components of the **ECA (Event–Condition–Action)** model used in active database systems. Differentiate between **row-level** and **statement-level** triggers with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following schema:
- `Library(book_id PK, title, available_copies)`
- `Issue(book_id, member_id, issue_date)`

Write **SQL trigger code** (PostgreSQL syntax) to ensure that:
1. The `available_copies` in `Library` is **decremented by 1** automatically when a new row is inserted into `Issue`.
2. The decrement is **prevented** if `available_copies = 0`.
3. The action is **logged** into a `Issue_Log(log_id, book_id, action_time)` table. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**ECA Components:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Event ($E$):** The DML operation that activates the trigger — `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`. The event also includes timing: `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, or `INSTEAD OF`.
2. **Condition ($C$):** An optional Boolean predicate (specified via the `WHEN` clause) evaluated using `OLD` and `NEW` row variables. If `FALSE` or `UNKNOWN`, the action is skipped.
3. **Action ($A$):** A procedural SQL block (`BEGIN ... END;`) that performs the side-effect — e.g., inserting into an audit table, raising exceptions, or modifying `NEW`.

**Row-Level vs Statement-Level:** *(4 marks)*

| Feature | `FOR EACH ROW` | `FOR EACH STATEMENT` |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Execution Frequency** | Once per affected row | Once per SQL statement |
| **Access to `OLD`/`NEW`** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only statement-level aggregates) |
| **Use Case** | Row-level business rules | Bulk operation auditing |
| **Example Use** | Validate each new salary > 0 | Log "1000 rows updated by user X" |

**Example — Row-Level:**
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER row_check BEFORE INSERT ON emp
FOR EACH ROW WHEN (NEW.salary < 0)
EXECUTE FUNCTION reject_negative_salary();
```

**Example — Statement-Level:**
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER stmt_log AFTER UPDATE ON emp
FOR EACH STATEMENT
EXECUTE FUNCTION log_bulk_update();
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Create the Log Table:** *(1 mark)*
```sql
CREATE TABLE Issue_Log (
    log_id      SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    book_id     INTEGER,
    action_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

**Step 2 — Trigger Function for Decrement with Validation:** *(4 marks)*
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_decrement_copies()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
DECLARE
    current_copies INTEGER;
BEGIN
    -- Fetch the current available copies
    SELECT available_copies INTO current_copies
    FROM Library
    WHERE book_id = NEW.book_id;

    -- Condition check: prevent issue if zero copies
    IF current_copies IS NULL THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Book ID % does not exist', NEW.book_id;
    ELSIF current_copies = 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'No copies available for book ID %', NEW.book_id;
    END IF;

    -- Decrement the count
    UPDATE Library
    SET available_copies = available_copies - 1
    WHERE book_id = NEW.book_id;

    -- Log the action
    INSERT INTO Issue_Log(book_id) VALUES (NEW.book_id);

    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```

**Step 3 — Bind the Trigger:** *(1 mark)*
```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_issue_book
BEFORE INSERT ON Issue
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION fn_decrement_copies();
```

**Step 4 — Trace the Execution:** *(1 mark)*

For an `INSERT INTO Issue VALUES (5, 201, CURRENT_DATE);`:

$$
\text{Event: INSERT} \rightarrow \text{Timing: BEFORE} \rightarrow \text{Read Library.available\_copies} \rightarrow \text{If } > 0 \rightarrow \text{UPDATE + LOG} \rightarrow \text{Allow INSERT}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a trigger? Explain the different types of triggers based on **timing** (`BEFORE`, `AFTER`, `INSTEAD OF`) with a real-world analogy for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the schema:
- `Student(roll_no PK, name, dept, total_marks)`
- `Marks(roll_no, subject, marks)`

Write a trigger that **automatically recomputes and updates** `Student.total_marks` whenever a row is inserted into `Marks`. Also write a trigger to **prevent deletion** of any student from `Student` who has marks records. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A trigger is an event-driven procedural code stored in the database that automatically executes in response to a DML event on a table or view. *(1 mark)*

**Types Based on Timing** *(6 marks)*:

1. **`BEFORE` Trigger** — *Analogy: A security gate at the entrance of a concert.* You check the ticket *before* letting the person in. If the ticket is invalid, the person is denied entry; the concert hall data is untouched.
   - *DBMS Use:* Validating and modifying values *before* a write.
   ```sql
   CREATE TRIGGER ... BEFORE INSERT ON ...
   ```

2. **`AFTER` Trigger** — *Analogy: A CCTV camera that records *after* a transaction occurs at the ATM.* The withdrawal happens first; the camera then captures and stores the receipt.
   - *DBMS Use:* Audit logging, cascading updates, sending notifications.
   ```sql
   CREATE TRIGGER ... AFTER DELETE ON ...
   ```

3. **`INSTEAD OF` Trigger** — *Analogy: A personal assistant who intercepts your request to "cancel the meeting" and instead sends a polite "rescheduling" email.* The original action is *replaced* with the assistant's action.
   - *DBMS Use:* Making non-updatable views (joins/aggregates) updatable.
   ```sql
   CREATE TRIGGER ... INSTEAD OF UPDATE ON view_name ...
   ```

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Trigger to Update Total Marks on Marks Insertion:** *(4 marks)*
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_update_total()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    UPDATE Student
    SET total_marks = (
        SELECT COALESCE(SUM(marks), 0)
        FROM Marks
        WHERE roll_no = NEW.roll_no
    )
    WHERE roll_no = NEW.roll_no;
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_recompute_total
AFTER INSERT ON Marks
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION fn_update_total();
```

**Step 2 — Trigger to Prevent Deletion of Students with Marks Records:** *(3 marks)*
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_prevent_student_delete()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
DECLARE
    marks_count INTEGER;
BEGIN
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO marks_count
    FROM Marks
    WHERE roll_no = OLD.roll_no;

    IF marks_count > 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Cannot delete student %: has % marks record(s).',
                        OLD.roll_no, marks_count;
    END IF;
    RETURN OLD;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_block_student_delete
BEFORE DELETE ON Student
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION fn_prevent_student_delete();
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting `RETURN NEW;` / `RETURN OLD;`** in the trigger function — silently aborts the operation. **−2 marks**
> 2. **Confusing `OLD` and `NEW` availability** (e.g., accessing `OLD` in an `INSERT` trigger). **−1 mark**
> 3. **Writing `BEFORE` triggers for audit logs** — wrong timing! Audit must be `AFTER`. **−2 marks**
> 4. **Not using `IS DISTINCT FROM`** for null-safe comparison in `UPDATE` triggers. **−1 mark**
> 5. **Forgetting the `LANGUAGE plpgsql;`** clause at the end of the function definition. **−1 mark**
> 6. **Using `INSTEAD OF` triggers on base tables** — they are valid **only on views**! **−2 marks**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **trigger** is a named, event-driven procedural object stored in the database catalog.
- [x] Triggers follow the **ECA (Event–Condition–Action)** paradigm.
- [x] The three timing options are `BEFORE`, `AFTER`, and `INSTEAD OF` (views only).
- [x] The two granularities are `FOR EACH ROW` (most common) and `FOR EACH STATEMENT`.
- [x] `OLD` and `NEW` are special pseudo-records available inside row-level triggers.
- [x] `OLD` is unavailable for `INSERT`; `NEW` is unavailable for `DELETE`.
- [x] Always end the trigger function with `RETURN NEW;` (for INSERT/UPDATE) or `RETURN OLD;` (for DELETE).
- [x] Use `IS DISTINCT FROM` for null-safe inequality checks.
- [x] `BEFORE` triggers are for **validation and modification**; `AFTER` triggers are for **logging and cascading**.
- [x] `INSTEAD OF` triggers are the **only** way to make non-updatable views updatable.
- [x] Always use `LANGUAGE plpgsql;` in PostgreSQL trigger functions.
- [x] Triggers execute **within the same transaction** as the triggering DML — a `RAISE EXCEPTION` rolls back the entire transaction.
- [x] Multiple triggers on the same event fire in **alphabetical order** (or by declared priority in Oracle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relational Database Design - Different Anomalies in Designing a Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relational Database Design - Different Anomalies in Designing a Database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Relational Database Design, **anomalies** are undesirable side effects or inconsistencies that occur when a relation (table) is *not* properly normalized — that is, when multiple distinct concepts are bundled into a single flat table. These anomalies violate the integrity, consistency, and maintainability of the database and are direct symptoms of a **poor relational schema design**.

The three classical anomalies recognized by the KTU 2024 scheme (PBERT504, Module 3) are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Database Anomaly** — *An unwanted, unintended, and inconsistent state in a relational table that arises due to redundant storage of data caused by improper grouping of attributes.*

1. **Insertion Anomaly** — Inability to insert new, logically valid data into a table because other, unrelated attributes are also required (and may be unknown or NULL).
2. **Deletion Anomaly** — Unintended loss of meaningful data when deleting a row, because two different pieces of information were stored in the same row.
3. **Update Anomaly** — Inconsistencies in data that arise when the same fact is stored in multiple rows, and a modification is applied to only some (not all) of those rows.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a single school register where the **headmaster** writes **every** piece of information on **one giant paper sheet** — student names, class teacher, teacher's phone number, subjects, exam marks, and the school's address.

- If a **new teacher** joins the school, the headmaster **cannot** enter the teacher's phone number in the register, because there is no "teacher only" row. → **Insertion Anomaly**
- If the **only student of Class 10** drops out and their row is removed, the information about the Class 10 teacher and their phone number is **lost forever**. → **Deletion Anomaly**
- If the Class 8 teacher's phone number changes, the headmaster must find **every single row** mentioning that teacher and update each one. If even one row is missed, the database now contains **two different phone numbers** for the same teacher. → **Update Anomaly**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Root Cause of ALL Anomalies:** A single table is being forced to represent **more than one real-world entity or relationship**. Normalization (Modules 4 & 5) is the systematic cure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Students must be able to (a) identify the anomaly, (b) name the anomaly, and (c) explain *why* the design flaw causes it. Identification carries the highest weight in board valuation.

---

### 1.3 The Standard KTU Demonstration Relation

Throughout this topic, KTU questions and notes commonly use a flat "all-in-one" relation to demonstrate anomalies. The classic structure is:

**Table Name: `STUDENT_COURSE` (Bad Design)**

| Roll_No | Student_Name | Course | Instructor | Instructor_Phone | Credits |
|---------|--------------|--------|------------|------------------|---------|
| 101     | Anu          | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       | 4       |
| 102     | Binu         | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       | 4       |
| 103     | Anu          | OS     | Dr. Meena  | 9123456780       | 3       |

We will use this and similar relations repeatedly to make every anomaly crystal clear.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Anomaly

#### 🔴 A. Insertion Anomaly

**Definition:** A situation where we **cannot insert a fact** about one real-world entity *without also supplying an unrelated fact* about another entity.

**Why it Happens:**
- The relation has attributes belonging to two or more different entities.
- At least one of those attributes cannot be supplied because the other entity does not yet exist in the table.
- Many RDBMS engines will either (a) reject the row due to `NOT NULL` constraints, or (b) store NULLs, which themselves cause inconsistency.

**Example using `STUDENT_COURSE`:**
Suppose a new instructor, **Dr. Kiran**, is appointed to teach a brand new elective — *Data Mining*. The class has not yet started, so no student has enrolled. We *cannot* insert this fact into `STUDENT_COURSE` because:
- The primary key (or a `NOT NULL` candidate key) involves `Roll_No`.
- We have no `Roll_No` to supply for Dr. Kiran's first student.

**Engineering Reality:** In an actual **college ERP** or **university portal**, this is exactly why instructor records live in a separate `INSTRUCTOR` table — they can be created *before* any course is offered.

---

#### 🔴 B. Deletion Anomaly

**Definition:** A situation where **deleting a single row** causes the *unintended loss* of other, completely unrelated information.

**Why it Happens:**
- Two different real-world facts are crammed into one row.
- Removing one fact forces removal of the row, and the other fact vanishes with it.

**Example using `STUDENT_COURSE`:**
Student **Binu (102)** drops out of the college. We delete his row to keep records clean. Unfortunately, that row is the **only row** containing the fact that *"Dr. Ravi teaches the DBMS course"*. After deletion, the database has **forgotten** who teaches DBMS.

**Engineering Reality:** In a **hospital management system**, if a doctor is assigned to one patient and that patient is discharged, deleting the patient row must not erase the doctor's specialization and contact details.

---

#### 🔴 C. Update Anomaly

**Definition:** A situation where an update to a fact must be applied to **multiple rows**, but due to human error or partial execution, **only some rows are updated**, leading to **inconsistent** or **contradictory** data in the database.

**Why it Happens:**
- The same fact is stored redundantly in many rows.
- There is no single source of truth (a violation of the conceptual principle of unique representation).

**Example using `STUDENT_COURSE`:**
Dr. Ravi changes his phone number from `9876543210` to `9000000000`. The fact *"Dr. Ravi's phone is 9876543210"* is stored in **2 rows** (Anu's and Binu's). If the DBA runs an update query that only changes Anu's row (e.g., due to a faulty `WHERE` clause), the database now reports:
- Row 1: Dr. Ravi → `9000000000`
- Row 2: Dr. Ravi → `9876543210`

The same instructor now has **two different phone numbers** in the same table — a serious integrity violation.

---

### 2.2 The Master Pattern: Why All Three Anomalies Coexist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's Golden Rule for Module 3:**
> Whenever you see an **Insertion**, **Deletion**, or **Update Anomaly** in a relation, the **root cause is always the same:** the relation contains attributes from more than one entity/relationship, leading to **functional dependencies between non-key attributes and partial-key attributes**. Decomposition (splitting the table) eliminates the redundancy, which in turn eliminates all three anomalies.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Anomaly Type | Symptom | Root Cause | Trigger Action | Resolution |
|--------------|---------|------------|----------------|------------|
| **Insertion** | Cannot add new, valid data | Required NULLs in non-key attributes | Trying to `INSERT` a new fact | Decompose into smaller relations |
| **Deletion** | Unintended data loss | Two facts stored in one row | Executing a `DELETE` | Decompose into smaller relations |
| **Update** | Inconsistent / duplicate data | Redundant storage of same fact | Executing an `UPDATE` | Decompose into smaller relations |
| **General** | Data redundancy | Mixed entities in one table | Any CRUD operation | Apply 1NF → 2NF → 3NF (Modules 4–5) |

**Mathematical Indicator of Anomalies:**

For a relation $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$ with key $K$, an anomaly-prone design has at least one **non-trivial functional dependency** of the form:

$$A_i \rightarrow A_j \quad \text{where } A_i \subseteq K \text{ and } A_j \text{ is a non-prime attribute}$$

When this exists, the fact $A_j$ becomes tied to a portion of the key, causing the three anomaly types to manifest whenever $K$ changes.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems** — Without decomposition, a customer's *account type* and *interest rate* would be duplicated in millions of transaction rows, making interest rate updates a nightmare.
- **E-Commerce** — Product categories and supplier details must be separated from the order table to allow adding new products without forcing a fake order.
- **Healthcare / Hospital ERPs** — Doctor details must not vanish when the last patient of a department is discharged.
- **Aviation & Railways** — Schedule updates (e.g., changing a gate) must update one row, not thousands.

In all these systems, **anomaly-free design is not optional** — it is a regulatory and operational necessity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Setting Up the Demonstration

To make the analysis exhaustive, let us formally define a *bad* relation. This mirrors the standard KTU textbook illustration.

**Relation $R$:**

$$R(\text{Roll\_No}, \text{Student\_Name}, \text{Course}, \text{Instructor}, \text{Instructor\_Phone})$$

**Functional Dependencies (FDs) Present:**

$$\text{Roll\_No} \rightarrow \text{Student\_Name}$$

$$\text{Course} \rightarrow \text{Instructor}$$

$$\text{Instructor} \rightarrow \text{Instructor\_Phone}$$

**Composite Candidate Key:** $(\text{Roll\_No}, \text{Course})$

**Step 1 — Identify the Problem:**

Since $(\text{Roll\_No}, \text{Course})$ is the key, the FD:

$$\text{Course} \rightarrow \text{Instructor}$$

is a **partial dependency** — the LHS is only a *part* of the candidate key. This single violation is the precise mathematical cause of every anomaly we will now demonstrate.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Anomaly Demonstration

#### ▶ Demonstration 1: Insertion Anomaly

**Step 1.** Suppose the college introduces a new course, **"Data Warehousing"**, to be taught by **"Dr. Kiran"** with phone `9999900000`.

**Step 2.** The relation $R$ requires a non-NULL `Roll_No` and `Course` to insert a row (these form the primary key).

**Step 3.** No student has yet enrolled in "Data Warehousing". So $\text{Roll\_No} = \text{NULL}$.

**Step 4.** Trying to execute the SQL:

$$\texttt{INSERT INTO R VALUES (NULL, NULL, 'DW', 'Dr. Kiran', '9999900000');}$$

**Step 5.** The RDBMS rejects the row (or stores NULLs in the key, violating Entity Integrity).

**Step 6.** **Conclusion:** We *cannot record* the fact that *"Dr. Kiran teaches Data Warehousing"* until at least one student enrolls. This is the **Insertion Anomaly**.

---

#### ▶ Demonstration 2: Deletion Anomaly

**Step 1.** Current state of $R$:

| Roll_No | Student_Name | Course | Instructor | Instructor_Phone |
|---------|--------------|--------|------------|------------------|
| 101     | Anu          | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       |
| 102     | Binu         | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       |
| 103     | Anu          | OS     | Dr. Meena  | 9123456780       |

**Step 2.** Student Binu (Roll_No 102) drops out. The DBA runs:

$$\texttt{DELETE FROM R WHERE Roll\_No = 102;}$$

**Step 3.** After deletion:

| Roll_No | Student_Name | Course | Instructor | Instructor_Phone |
|---------|--------------|--------|------------|------------------|
| 101     | Anu          | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       |
| 103     | Anu          | OS     | Dr. Meena  | 9123456780       |

**Step 4.** The DBMS still knows that Dr. Ravi teaches DBMS (row 101 is intact). Now consider a smaller department: suppose **OS** had only one student (Anu), and Anu drops out:

$$\texttt{DELETE FROM R WHERE Roll\_No = 103;}$$

**Step 5.** After this delete, **the entire row containing "Dr. Meena teaches OS, phone 9123456780" vanishes**.

**Step 6.** **Conclusion:** The information that *"Dr. Meena is the OS instructor"* is permanently erased from the database, even though Dr. Meena still teaches at the college. This is the **Deletion Anomaly**.

---

#### ▶ Demonstration 3: Update Anomaly

**Step 1.** Dr. Ravi's phone number must be updated from `9876543210` to `9000000000`.

**Step 2.** The fact *"Dr. Ravi's phone is 9876543210"* is stored in **two rows** (Anu's and Binu's).

**Step 3.** The DBA runs a correct query:

$$\texttt{UPDATE R SET Instructor\_Phone = '9000000000' WHERE Instructor = 'Dr. Ravi';}$$

**Step 4.** Both rows are updated — **no anomaly occurs**.

**Step 5.** Now consider a *faulty* update (a common real-world mistake):

$$\texttt{UPDATE R SET Instructor\_Phone = '9000000000' WHERE Roll\_No = 101;}$$

**Step 6.** Resulting inconsistent state:

| Roll_No | Student_Name | Course | Instructor | Instructor_Phone |
|---------|--------------|--------|------------|------------------|
| 101     | Anu          | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9000000000       |
| 102     | Binu         | DBMS   | Dr. Ravi   | 9876543210       |
| 103     | Anu          | OS     | Dr. Meena  | 9123456780       |

**Step 7.** The same instructor (Dr. Ravi) now has **two different phone numbers** in the same table. The database is internally inconsistent.

**Step 8.** **Conclusion:** Because `Instructor_Phone` is repeated, any incomplete update creates contradictions. This is the **Update Anomaly**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Operations to Anomalies

The following matrix maps each CRUD operation to the specific anomaly that can occur, and identifies the design feature that prevents it.

| Database Operation | What It Tries To Do | Anomaly It May Trigger | Design Flaw Responsible | Fix |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------|-----|
| `INSERT`           | Add a new fact      | **Insertion Anomaly** — fails if required fields are unknown | Mixed entities with shared key | Decompose into entity-specific tables |
| `DELETE`           | Remove a fact       | **Deletion Anomaly** — removes unrelated data | Two facts in one row | Decompose so each fact has its own table |
| `UPDATE`           | Modify a fact       | **Update Anomaly** — partial updates create inconsistencies | Redundant copies of the same fact | Move the fact to a single location (its own table) |
| `SELECT`           | Read data           | No anomaly, but may show contradictions | Stale or duplicate data | Same as above |

---

### 3.4 Logical Proof: Why Decomposition Eliminates Anomalies

**Step 1.** The bad relation $R$ has redundancy count:

$$\text{Storage of } (\text{Course}, \text{Instructor}, \text{Instructor\_Phone}) = N \text{ times}, \text{ where } N = \text{ number of students per course}$$

**Step 2.** After decomposition into:

$$R_1(\text{Roll\_No}, \text{Student\_Name}, \text{Course})$$

$$R_2(\text{Course}, \text{Instructor}, \text{Instructor\_Phone})$$

**Step 3.** In $R_2$, the fact *(Course → Instructor → Phone)* is stored **exactly once**.

**Step 4.** Therefore, an `UPDATE` of the instructor's phone requires touching **one row**, a `DELETE` cannot lose the course-faculty mapping, and an `INSERT` for a new course with no enrolled students is now possible (it goes into $R_2$ directly).

**Step 5.** **Conclusion:** Decomposition has eliminated redundancy, and with it, all three anomalies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Anomaly Taxonomy (Module 3 Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Poorly Designed Relation R"] --> B["Insertion Anomaly"]
    A --> C["Deletion Anomaly"]
    A --> D["Update Anomaly"]
    B --> E["Cannot insert new data due to required NULLs"]
    C --> F["Unintended data loss when deleting a row"]
    D --> G["Inconsistent data after partial update"]
    A --> H["Root Cause: Redundancy in Non-Key Attributes"]
    H --> I["Mixed Entities in One Table"]
    I --> J["Partial Functional Dependencies"]
    E --> K["Solution: Decomposition into 1NF / 2NF / 3NF"]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    K --> L["Insertion Possible"]
    K --> M["Deletion Safe"]
    K --> N["Update Consistent"]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Good vs Bad Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAD["BAD DESIGN: Single Flat Table STUDENT COURSE"]
        T1["Roll No"]
        T2["Student Name"]
        T3["Course"]
        T4["Instructor"]
        T5["Instructor Phone"]
        T1 --- T2
        T1 --- T3
        T3 --- T4
        T4 --- T5
    end

    subgraph GOOD["GOOD DESIGN: Decomposed Relations"]
        R1["STUDENT: Roll No, Student Name"]
        R2["ENROLLMENT: Roll No, Course"]
        R3["COURSE: Course, Instructor, Instructor Phone"]
        R1 --- R2
        R2 --- R3
    end

    BAD -->|Anomalies Occur| X["Inconsistent Database State"]
    GOOD -->|Anomalies Eliminated| Y["Consistent, Maintainable Database"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Anomaly Decision Tree (For Exam Identification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["A relational operation FAILED or PRODUCED WRONG DATA"] --> Q1{"What operation were you performing?"}
    Q1 -->|INSERT| Q2{"Were required fields unknown or NULL?"}
    Q1 -->|DELETE| Q3{"Did you lose unrelated data?"}
    Q1 -->|UPDATE| Q4{"Did the same attribute now have two different values?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1["INSERTION ANOMALY"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| A2["DELETION ANOMALY"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| A3["UPDATE ANOMALY"]
    A1 --> Fix["Decompose the relation"]
    A2 --> Fix
    A3 --> Fix
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Data Redundancy Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FLAT["FLAT TABLE - Data Repeated for Every Student"]
        S1["Anu - DBMS - Dr Ravi - 9876543210"]
        S2["Binu - DBMS - Dr Ravi - 9876543210"]
        S3["Anu - OS - Dr Meena - 9123456780"]
    end
    subgraph NORM["NORMALIZED - Each Fact Stored Once"]
        N1["STUDENT: 101 Anu / 102 Binu"]
        N2["ENROLL: 101 DBMS / 102 DBMS / 101 OS"]
        N3["COURSE: DBMS Dr Ravi 9876543210 / OS Dr Meena 9123456780"]
    end
    FLAT -->|Causes| An["All Three Anomalies"]
    NORM -->|Prevents| Clean["No Anomalies"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define database anomaly. List the three main types of anomalies that occur in a poorly designed relational database.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A *database anomaly* is an unwanted inconsistency or error that arises in a relational table due to data redundancy caused by poor schema design. The three main types are:
1. **Insertion Anomaly** — inability to insert a valid fact due to missing unrelated attributes.
2. **Deletion Anomaly** — unintended loss of data when removing a row.
3. **Update Anomaly** — inconsistency caused when the same fact is updated in only some of its duplicated locations.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. What is an Update Anomaly? How is it caused?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An *Update Anomaly* occurs when a single logical fact (e.g., an instructor's phone number) is stored in multiple rows of a relation, and a modification is applied to only some of those rows, leaving the database internally inconsistent. It is caused by **redundant storage of data** resulting from improper schema design where non-key attributes depend only on part of a composite key.

---

### 📕 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two independent alternative questions are provided.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a) Explain the three different types of anomalies that occur in a relational database design with suitable examples. State how they affect the integrity of data.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Introduction:*
When a relational schema is poorly designed, with multiple real-world entities stored in a single table, redundant data accumulates. This redundancy causes **anomalies** during INSERT, DELETE, and UPDATE operations.

*Step 2 — Insertion Anomaly:*
Consider a relation `EMP_DEPT(EmpID, EmpName, DeptName, DeptLocation, Project)`. If a new department `AI Research` is to be set up with location `Block-C` but no employee has been assigned yet, we cannot insert this fact because `EmpID` (part of the primary key) cannot be NULL. The valid information about the new department is therefore **rejected by the database**.

*Step 3 — Deletion Anomaly:*
Continuing the same relation, if the only employee working in department `Marketing` leaves the organization, deleting that employee's row causes the loss of the entire information about the `Marketing` department itself — its name and location are forgotten. This violates data preservation.

*Step 4 — Update Anomaly:*
If `Marketing`'s location changes from `Block-A` to `Block-B`, every row containing an employee of Marketing must be updated. If even one row is missed, the database now shows two different locations for the same department, leading to **inconsistency and loss of data integrity**.

*Step 5 — Effect on Integrity:*
- *Entity Integrity* is violated (NULLs in keys during insertion).
- *Referential Integrity* is broken (data lost during deletion).
- *Data Consistency* is compromised (update anomalies).

*Step 6 — Conclusion:*
These anomalies are cured by **decomposing** the relation into smaller, well-normalized ones (1NF, 2NF, 3NF).

---

**(b) Consider the following relation: `R(EmpID, EmpName, Project, ProjectLeader, LeaderPhone)`. The primary key is (EmpID, Project). The functional dependencies are: EmpID → EmpName and Project → ProjectLeader, ProjectLeader → LeaderPhone. Identify and explain the anomalies that can occur in R.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Analyze the FDs:*
- `EmpID → EmpName` (partial dependency — only part of the key determines EmpName).
- `Project → ProjectLeader` (partial dependency).
- `ProjectLeader → LeaderPhone` (transitive dependency).

These non-prime dependencies are the source of all anomalies.

*Step 2 — Insertion Anomaly:*
We cannot insert a new project `"Cyber Security"` led by `"Dr. Nair"` (phone `8888800000`) until at least one employee is assigned to it, because `EmpID` is part of the key and cannot be NULL. The valid fact about the new project is therefore blocked.

*Step 3 — Deletion Anomaly:*
If employee `E101` is the *only* person working on project `"IoT"`, deleting that employee's row also erases the information that `"Dr. Iyer leads IoT, phone 7777700000"`. The project leadership details vanish.

*Step 4 — Update Anomaly:*
Dr. Iyer's phone number is repeated in every row of employees working on `"IoT"`. If the DBA updates the phone in some rows but not all (due to a faulty WHERE clause or partial commit), the same leader `Dr. Iyer` will appear with **multiple different phone numbers** in the database — a direct violation of consistency.

*Step 5 — Resolution:*
Decompose R into:
- `EMP(EmpID, EmpName)`
- `ASSIGN(EmpID, Project)`
- `PROJECT(Project, ProjectLeader, LeaderPhone)`

After decomposition, no anomaly exists.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) Differentiate between Insertion, Deletion, and Update anomalies with the help of a single example relation. Mention the common root cause of all three.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Common Example:*
Let `STUDENT_COURSE(Roll_No, SName, Course, Instructor, IPhone)` where `(Roll_No, Course)` is the key and `Course → Instructor → IPhone` holds.

*Step 2 — Tabular Comparison:*

| Aspect | Insertion Anomaly | Deletion Anomaly | Update Anomaly |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Operation | `INSERT`           | `DELETE`          | `UPDATE`         |
| Symptom | Cannot add valid data | Unintended data loss | Inconsistent copies |
| Cause | Required NULL in non-key attribute | Two facts in one row | Redundant copies of same fact |
| Effect | Blocks new information | Erases other data | Breaks data integrity |

*Step 3 — Common Root Cause:*
All three anomalies arise from **redundancy caused by storing data of multiple entities in a single relation**, especially when non-prime attributes depend on a *part* of a composite key (partial dependency) or transitively on the key (transitive dependency).

*Step 4 — Cure:*
Decomposition into 1NF → 2NF → 3NF (covered in Modules 4 and 5).

---

**(b) A college stores the following data in one table `ENROLLMENT`:**
`ENROLLMENT(RegNo, StudentName, CourseCode, CourseTitle, FacultyID, FacultyName, FacultyCabin)`.
**Identify the anomalies that can occur and explain how normalization (decomposition) eliminates them.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify FDs:*
- `RegNo → StudentName`
- `CourseCode → CourseTitle, FacultyID`
- `FacultyID → FacultyName, FacultyCabin`

*Step 2 — Anomalies in the Current Design:*
- **Insertion:** A new faculty `"Prof. Maya"` with cabin `C-204` cannot be added until they are assigned a course and a student enrolls, because `RegNo` and `CourseCode` are part of the key.
- **Deletion:** Deleting the only student enrolled in `"CS304"` removes the only row containing `"Prof. Maya's faculty details"`.
- **Update:** Changing `Prof. Maya's` cabin number requires updating every row of every student enrolled in her course; missing one creates a contradiction.

*Step 3 — Decomposition:*
Split into:
- `STUDENT(RegNo, StudentName)`
- `ENROLL(RegNo, CourseCode)`
- `COURSE(CourseCode, CourseTitle, FacultyID)`
- `FACULTY(FacultyID, FacultyName, FacultyCabin)`

*Step 4 — Why Anomalies Vanish:*
- The fact `Prof. Maya → Cabin C-204` is now stored in **exactly one row** of `FACULTY`.
- A new faculty can be inserted without any student/course data.
- Deleting a student no longer touches faculty data.
- Updating a cabin requires touching only **one row**.

*Step 5 — Conclusion:* The decomposed design satisfies the principles of 2NF/3NF and is free from insertion, deletion, and update anomalies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the three anomalies** — many students mix up *deletion* and *update* anomalies. Remember: *Deletion = data loss*; *Update = data inconsistency*.
> 2. **Not stating the root cause** — KTU examiners award marks for explicitly mentioning *"redundancy due to partial / transitive dependency"*, not just describing the symptom.
> 3. **Using the SAME example for all three** — KTU rewards clarity. Use one consistent relation and trace each anomaly with the exact `INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE` statement.
> 4. **Skipping the cure** — at least 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question are reserved for the *decomposition / normalization* solution. Never end on the anomaly — always end with the fix.
> 5. **Writing SQL for non-CS** — in this specific course (CS-related), SQL snippets are acceptable, but never use code where plain English explanation is sufficient.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **anomaly** is an *unintended inconsistency* in a relation caused by data redundancy.
- ✅ The **three classical anomalies** are **Insertion**, **Deletion**, and **Update**.
- ✅ **Insertion Anomaly** = Cannot insert logically valid data because of required NULLs in non-key fields.
- ✅ **Deletion Anomaly** = Deleting one row unintentionally removes other useful data.
- ✅ **Update Anomaly** = The same fact stored in many rows leads to inconsistency when only some are updated.
- ✅ **Root cause** of all three = mixing multiple entities in one relation, creating **partial** and **transitive functional dependencies**.
- ✅ **The cure** = **Decomposition** of the relation into smaller, lossless-join relations via **normalization** (1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF).
- ✅ In KTU 2024 Module 3, you must be able to:
  - Identify the anomaly from a given scenario.
  - Trace the FD that caused it.
  - Suggest the decomposition that removes it.
- ✅ Anomaly-free design is **not optional** in real-world systems — banking, ERP, healthcare, and e-commerce all depend on it.
- ✅ Master one example (e.g., `STUDENT_COURSE` or `EMP_DEPT`) and reuse it for all three anomalies — this is the KTU-recommended answering strategy.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Relational Database Design: Different Anomalies in Designing a Database**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Idea of Normalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Idea of Normalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Normalization** is a formal, systematic, and step-wise process of organizing the attributes (columns) of a relational database into a series of related tables with the primary objective of **minimizing data redundancy** and eliminating undesirable data anomalies (insertion, update, and deletion anomalies). It is grounded in the mathematical theory of **functional dependencies** and proceeds through a hierarchy of progressively stricter rules called **Normal Forms (NFs)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBERT504 – Database Management Systems)**, normalization is treated as the central design refinement step that transforms a poorly-structured universal relation into a set of well-structured, lossless, and dependency-preserving relations suitable for relational database implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students are expected to understand the *motivation* (anomalies) behind normalization, the concept of functional dependencies, closure of attribute sets, and the application of 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF. Lossless-join and dependency-preservation properties are mandatory for full marks in design problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Filing Cabinet" Intuition

Imagine you maintain a single massive spreadsheet (one giant table) where you record every detail about students, the courses they enroll in, the professors who teach those courses, and the departments of those professors — all jumbled into a single flat sheet.

**Problems with this "one big sheet":**
- If a professor changes their phone number, you must hunt and update it in **every row** where the professor appears (Update Anomaly).
- If a new department is created but has no professor yet, you **cannot** record the department (Insertion Anomaly).
- If a student drops the only course they took, deleting the enrollment row also **erases** the student's personal data (Deletion Anomaly).

**Normalization** is like converting that one messy drawer of mixed files into a **labelled multi-drawer filing cabinet**: one drawer for students, one for courses, one for professors, with small *index cards* (foreign keys) connecting them. Information about a professor is stored **once**; everyone who needs it just refers to the index card.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Normalization is not about *adding* data; it is about *organizing* data to make future updates, inserts, and deletes safe, efficient, and consistent.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Relation / Table** | A 2-D structure of rows (tuples) and columns (attributes). |
| **Attribute** | A named column of a relation. |
| **Tuple** | A single row. |
| **Schema** | The structural definition of a relation: $R(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$. |
| **Functional Dependency (FD)** | A constraint $X \rightarrow Y$ meaning $Y$ is functionally determined by $X$. |
| **Key** | A minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple. |
| **Anomaly** | An undesirable side-effect of redundancy during INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Normalize? — The Three Anomalies

A *badly designed* relation (i.e., one with redundancy) is prone to three classic anomalies:

1. **Insertion Anomaly** — Certain facts cannot be recorded at all unless other unrelated facts are also supplied. *Example:* Inserting a new department that has no professor yet is impossible if department details co-exist with professor details in a single row.
2. **Update Anomaly** — A single logical fact is stored in multiple rows, so updating it requires changing *all* those rows. Miss one, and the database becomes inconsistent.
3. **Deletion Anomaly** — Deleting one piece of information unintentionally erases other, completely unrelated, information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root cause of all three anomalies: Redundancy caused by improper (or missing) functional dependencies between non-key and key attributes.**

### 2.2 Functional Dependency (FD) — The Theoretical Backbone

A functional dependency is a constraint between two sets of attributes $X$ and $Y$ of a relation $R$, written as:
$$X \rightarrow Y$$
It states that for any two tuples $t_1, t_2$ in $R$, if $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$, then $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$. In plain English: *"$X$ functionally determines $Y$"*, or *"$Y$ depends on $X$."*

- **Determinant:** The left-hand side ($X$).
- **Dependent:** The right-hand side ($Y$).

#### 2.2.1 Types of Functional Dependencies

| Type | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trivial FD** | $Y \subseteq X$ (always true) | $\{A, B\} \rightarrow A$ |
| **Non-trivial FD** | $X \cap Y = \emptyset$ | $A \rightarrow B$ |
| **Fully Functional FD** | For $X \rightarrow Y$, removing any attribute from $X$ breaks the dependency. | $\{Roll, Sub\} \rightarrow Marks$ |
| **Partial FD** | A proper subset of $X$ determines $Y$. | $Roll \rightarrow Name$ within $\{Roll, Sub\} \rightarrow Name$ |
| **Transitive FD** | $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, but $Y \nrightarrow X$. Then $X \rightarrow Z$ transitively. | $Roll \rightarrow Dept$, $Dept \rightarrow HOD$ implies $Roll \rightarrow HOD$ |
| **Multivalued FD (MVD)** | $X \twoheadrightarrow Y$ — for a given $X$, the set of $Y$ values is independent of other attributes. | $Student \twoheadrightarrow Hobby$ |

### 2.3 Keys — The Heartbeat of a Relation

- **Super Key:** Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple. May contain extra, unnecessary attributes.
- **Candidate Key:** A *minimal* super key — remove any attribute and uniqueness is lost.
- **Primary Key:** The candidate key *chosen* by the designer to be the unique identifier.
- **Alternate Key:** Candidate keys *not* chosen as the primary key.
- **Foreign Key:** An attribute in one relation that references the primary key of another (or the same) relation.
- **Composite Key:** A key composed of two or more attributes.
- **Prime Attribute:** An attribute that is a member of *any* candidate key.
- **Non-Prime Attribute:** An attribute that is not part of any candidate key.

### 2.4 Armstrong's Axioms (Inference Rules)

These rules are used to derive the **closure** of a set of FDs ($F^+$) from a given set $F$.

1. **Reflexivity:** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$.
2. **Augmentation:** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$.
3. **Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$.

**Derived (Secondary) Rules:**
4. **Union:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$.
5. **Decomposition / Projectivity:** If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$.
6. **Pseudo-transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $WY \rightarrow Z$, then $WX \rightarrow Z$.

### 2.5 Attribute Closure Algorithm — Finding All Attributes Determined by a Given Set

Given a relation $R$, a set of FDs $F$, and an attribute set $X$:

1. Initialize $X^+ = X$.
2. Repeat: For each FD $A \rightarrow B$ in $F$, if $A \subseteq X^+$, add $B$ to $X^+$.
3. Stop when $X^+$ no longer changes.

$X$ is a **candidate key** if and only if $X^+ = R$ and no proper subset of $X$ has this property.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Rule | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Functional Dependency | $X$ determines $Y$ | $X \rightarrow Y$ |
| 1NF | Atomic values + unique tuples | All attributes indivisible |
| 2NF | 1NF + No partial dependency | Non-prime attrs depend on full key |
| 3NF | 2NF + No transitive dependency | $X \rightarrow A$ allowed only if $X$ is superkey or $A$ is prime |
| BCNF | 3NF + Every determinant is a superkey | $X \rightarrow Y \Rightarrow X$ is superkey |
| Lossless Join | Decomposition is lossless if $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1 \;\text{or}\; (R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$ | — |
| Dependency Preserving | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \dots \cup F_n)^+ = F^+$ | — |
| Closure | Set of all FDs derivable from $F$ | $F^+$ |
| Attribute Closure | Set of all attributes determined by $X$ | $X^+$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Normalization underlies every well-engineered OLTP system (banking, e-commerce, inventory). Over-normalization, however, hurts read-heavy analytical (OLAP) workloads — this is why data warehousing often uses *denormalized* star schemas. The skill is to know the trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Definitions of Normal Forms (NF Hierarchy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Progression:** Every higher normal form automatically satisfies all lower normal forms. 3NF ⊃ 2NF ⊃ 1NF, and BCNF ⊃ 3NF.

#### 3.1.1 First Normal Form (1NF)
A relation $R$ is in **1NF** if and only if the domain of every attribute contains only **atomic (indivisible) values** and every attribute of $R$ holds a *single value* (no multi-valued attributes or repeating groups).

- **Violation Fix:** Create a new row for each repeated value, or split into multiple relations (one-to-many).

#### 3.1.2 Second Normal Form (2NF)
A relation $R$ is in **2NF** if:
1. $R$ is already in 1NF, **AND**
2. No **non-prime attribute** is *partially* dependent on any candidate key. Equivalently, every non-prime attribute is **fully functionally dependent** on every candidate key.

- **Violation Fix:** Remove partially dependent attributes into a new relation along with a copy of the determinant (the part of the key they depend on).

#### 3.1.3 Third Normal Form (3NF)
A relation $R$ is in **3NF** if:
1. $R$ is in 2NF, **AND**
2. For every FD $X \rightarrow A$ in $F^+$, **at least one** of the following holds:
   - $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$, **OR**
   - $A$ is a **prime attribute** (member of some candidate key).

- **Violation Fix:** Eliminate transitive dependencies by splitting.

#### 3.1.4 Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
A relation $R$ is in **BCNF** if for every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F^+$, $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$.

- **Strictness:** BCNF is strictly stricter than 3NF. Every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but not vice versa.
