- **Trade-off:** BCNF decomposition is *always* lossless, but may **not** be dependency-preserving. 3NF guarantees *both* lossless-join and dependency-preservation.

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Finding Candidate Keys & Normal Form

**Problem Statement (Classic KTU-style):**

Consider a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ with the following set of FDs $F$:
$$F = \{AB \rightarrow C,\; C \rightarrow D,\; D \rightarrow E,\; CF \rightarrow A,\; B \rightarrow F\}$$

**Step 1: Find the Candidate Keys using Attribute Closure.**

First, identify attributes that **never appear on the RHS** — these *must* be in every candidate key.

- $A$: appears on RHS in $CF \rightarrow A$. Not guaranteed to be in key.
- $B$: **never on RHS.** MUST be in every candidate key.
- $C$: appears on RHS. Not guaranteed.
- $D$: appears on RHS.
- $E$: appears on RHS.
- $F$: appears on RHS.

So $B$ is the only attribute that *must* be in every candidate key. Now compute $\{B\}^+$:

$$\{B\}^+ = \{B,\; F\} \quad \text{(using } B \rightarrow F\text{)}$$

$$= \{B,\; F,\; A\} \quad \text{(if any FD has only B and F on LHS — none here, so check carefully)}$$

We need to add $A$. We have $CF \rightarrow A$, so we need both $C$ and $F$ to derive $A$. We have $F$ from $B$, but not $C$.

Add $C$: We need to find an FD whose LHS is a subset of $\{B, F\}$. None exists directly.

Try $\{B, C\}^+$:

$$= \{B, C, D, E, F, A\} \quad \text{(by applying all FDs systematically)}$$

Let us trace step-by-step:

$$\text{Init: } \{B, C\}$$
$$\text{Apply } C \rightarrow D: \quad \{B, C, D\}$$
$$\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: \quad \{B, C, D, E\}$$
$$\text{Apply } B \rightarrow F: \quad \{B, C, D, E, F\}$$
$$\text{Apply } CF \rightarrow A: \quad \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$$

So $\{B, C\}^+ = R$. Now check if a proper subset of $\{B, C\}$ also determines $R$:
- $\{B\}^+ = \{B, F\} \neq R$.
- $\{C\}^+ = \{C, D, E\} \neq R$.

Therefore, **$\{B, C\}$ is a candidate key.**

Are there other candidate keys? Try $\{A, B\}^+$:
- $AB \rightarrow C \Rightarrow \{A, B, C\}$
- $C \rightarrow D \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D\}$
- $D \rightarrow E \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E\}$
- $B \rightarrow F \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$

So $\{A, B\}^+ = R$. Check minimality: $\{A\}^+ = \{A\}$; $\{B\}^+ = \{B, F\}$. Neither equals $R$. So **$\{A, B\}$ is also a candidate key.**

**Final list of candidate keys:** $\{A, B\}$ and $\{B, C\}$.

**Step 2: Identify Prime and Non-Prime Attributes.**

- **Prime attributes:** $A$, $B$, $C$ (each appears in at least one candidate key).
- **Non-prime attributes:** $D$, $E$, $F$.

**Step 3: Identify the Highest Normal Form.**

Check each FD against the BCNF/3NF conditions.

| FD | LHS is Superkey? | RHS is Prime? | Satisfies 3NF? | Satisfies BCNF? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $AB \rightarrow C$ | Yes ($\{A, B\}$ is a CK) | $C$ is prime | Yes | Yes |
| $C \rightarrow D$ | No | $D$ is **non-prime** | **No** | **No** |
| $D \rightarrow E$ | No | $E$ is **non-prime** | **No** | **No** |
| $CF \rightarrow A$ | Check closure $\{C, F\}^+$: $C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$; need $A$ via $CF \rightarrow A$ itself, so it stops. So $\{C, F\}^+ \neq R$. No. | $A$ is prime | Yes | **No** |
| $B \rightarrow F$ | No | $F$ is **non-prime** | **No** | **No** |

Since $C \rightarrow D$ violates 3NF (LHS not a superkey, RHS not prime), the relation is **not even in 2NF/3NF** — verifying 2NF would also fail because of the partial/transitive nature of $C \rightarrow D$ when $C$ is part of candidate key $\{B, C\}$.

**Conclusion:** $R$ is in **1NF only**. It needs to be normalized.

**Step 4: Decompose into BCNF (Lossless).**

Split on the violating FD $C \rightarrow D$:
- $R_1(C, D, E)$ with FDs: $C \rightarrow D$, $D \rightarrow E$.
- $R_2(A, B, C, F)$ with FDs: $AB \rightarrow C$, $CF \rightarrow A$, $B \rightarrow F$.

Continue decomposition:

Split $R_1$ on $D \rightarrow E$:
- $R_{1a}(D, E)$ with FD: $D \rightarrow E$.
- $R_{1b}(C, D)$ with FD: $C \rightarrow D$.

Now check $R_2(A, B, C, F)$ for BCNF. Violating FD: $B \rightarrow F$:
- $R_{2a}(B, F)$ with FD: $B \rightarrow F$.
- $R_{2b}(A, B, C)$ with FDs: $AB \rightarrow C$.

Check $R_{2b}$: is $AB \rightarrow C$ BCNF? Yes ($\{A, B\}$ is a key of $R_{2b}$).

**Final BCNF Decomposition:**
- $R_{1a}(D, E)$
- $R_{1b}(C, D)$
- $R_{2a}(B, F)$
- $R_{2b}(A, B, C)$

All four are in BCNF. The decomposition is **lossless** at every step (verified by $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{C\}$, and $C \rightarrow D$ makes $C$ a key of $R_1$).

### 3.3 Lossless-Join & Dependency-Preservation — Quick Verification Framework

Given decomposition $R \to \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n\}$ with $F$ as original FD set and $F_i$ as the projection of $F$ onto $R_i$:

1. **Lossless-Join Test (Binary case):** $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ or $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$.
2. **Dependency-Preservation Test:** $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \dots \cup F_n)^+ \stackrel{?}{=} F^+$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:** 14-mark design questions almost always demand *both* the BCNF decomposition *and* a verification of the lossless-join property. Dependency-preservation is asked when 3NF is the target.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Normal Form Hierarchy (Conceptual Ladder)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["UNNORMALIZED FORM"] --> node2["FIRST NORMAL FORM 1NF"]
    node2 --> node3["SECOND NORMAL FORM 2NF"]
    node3 --> node4["THIRD NORMAL FORM 3NF"]
    node4 --> node5["BOYCE CODD NORMAL FORM BCNF"]
    node3 -.-> node6["FOURTH NORMAL FORM 4NF"]
    node5 -.-> node6
    node6 -.-> node7["FIFTH NORMAL FORM 5NF"]
    style node1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style node2 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style node4 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style node5 fill:#9D4EDD,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style node6 fill:#FF6BCB,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style node7 fill:#6BFFB5,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The solid arrows show the **mandatory progression** covered in the KTU Module 3 syllabus (1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF). The dotted arrows show extended forms (4NF, 5NF) for completeness — these are typically *not* asked in KTU 2024 DBMS exam questions.

### 4.2 The Normalization Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START Given Relation R and FD set F"] --> stepA["STEP 1 Verify 1NF Eliminate multi valued attributes"]
    stepA --> stepB["STEP 2 Identify Candidate Keys using Attribute Closure Algorithm"]
    stepB --> stepC["STEP 3 Identify Prime and Non Prime Attributes"]
    stepC --> stepD["STEP 4 Check for Partial Dependencies to test 2NF"]
    stepD --> decA{"Violation Found"}
    decA -- YES --> stepE["Decompose by removing partial dependency"]
    decA -- NO --> stepF["Check for Transitive Dependencies to test 3NF"]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> decB{"Violation Found"}
    decB -- YES --> stepG["Decompose by removing transitive dependency"]
    decB -- NO --> stepH["Check Determinants for Superkey Property to test BCNF"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> decC{"Any Determinant NOT a Superkey"}
    decC -- YES --> stepI["Decompose to enforce BCNF"]
    decC -- NO --> endA["END Relation is in BCNF"]
    stepI --> endA
    style startA fill:#4D96FF,color:#fff
    style endA fill:#6BCB77,color:#000
    style stepA fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepB fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepC fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepD fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepF fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepH fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style stepE fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style stepG fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style stepI fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Anomaly-to-Normal-Form Diagnostic Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    anomaly1["INSERTION ANOMALY"] --> cause1["Caused by transitive FD and missing non key data"]
    anomaly2["UPDATE ANOMALY"] --> cause2["Caused by redundant storage of same fact"]
    anomaly3["DELETION ANOMALY"] --> cause3["Caused by mixing independent facts in one tuple"]
    cause1 --> fix["FIX Apply Higher Normal Form"]
    cause2 --> fix
    cause3 --> fix
    fix --> result["Lossless Dependency Preserving Decomposition"]
    style anomaly1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style anomaly2 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style anomaly3 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style cause1 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style cause2 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style cause3 fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style fix fill:#6BCB77,color:#000
    style result fill:#4D96FF,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define functional dependency. Explain with an example the difference between a partial functional dependency and a transitive functional dependency.*

**Model Answer:**

A **functional dependency (FD)** is a constraint, denoted as $X \rightarrow Y$, between two sets of attributes $X$ and $Y$ of a relation schema $R$, which asserts that for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in $R$, if $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$, then $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$. In simpler words, $Y$ is uniquely determined by $X$.

- **Partial FD:** A non-prime attribute is functionally dependent on *part* of a candidate key. *Example:* In relation $ENROLL(Roll, Sub, Marks, Name)$ with CK $\{Roll, Sub\}$, the dependency $Roll \rightarrow Name$ is *partial* because $Roll$ alone (a part of the CK) determines $Name$.
- **Transitive FD:** A non-prime attribute is functionally determined by another non-prime attribute. *Example:* In $EMP(EID, Dept, HOD)$ with FDs $EID \rightarrow Dept$ and $Dept \rightarrow HOD$, the FD $EID \rightarrow HOD$ is *transitive*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a candidate key? How is it different from a super key and a primary key?*

**Model Answer:**

- A **super key** is any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple in a relation.
- A **candidate key** is a *minimal* super key — no proper subset of it can uniquely identify a tuple.
- A **primary key** is the candidate key *selected* by the database designer to be the official unique identifier for tuples; other candidate keys become *alternate keys*.

*Example:* In $STUDENT(Roll, Aadhar, Name)$, both $\{Roll\}$ and $\{Aadhar\}$ are candidate keys (minimal), $\{Roll, Aadhar\}$ is a super key (but not minimal), and the designer picks, say, $\{Roll\}$ as the primary key.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

Consider a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ with the following set of functional dependencies:
$$F = \{ A \rightarrow B,\; C \rightarrow D,\; E \rightarrow F,\; AC \rightarrow E,\; B \rightarrow C \}$$

**(a) Find all the candidate keys of $R$.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Identify the highest normal form in which $R$ exists. If it is not in BCNF, decompose it into BCNF and verify the lossless-join property.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Finding Candidate Keys

**Step 1:** Find attributes that **never appear on the RHS** of any FD.
- $A$: appears on LHS only. **Must be in every CK.**
- $B$: on RHS. Not required.
- $C$: on RHS. Not required.
- $D$: on RHS. Not required.
- $E$: on RHS. Not required.
- $F$: on RHS. Not required.

So $A$ is essential. Now compute $\{A\}^+$:

$$\{A\}^+ = \{A, B\} \quad \text{(using } A \rightarrow B\text{)}$$

$$= \{A, B, C\} \quad \text{(using } B \rightarrow C\text{)}$$

$$= \{A, B, C, D\} \quad \text{(using } C \rightarrow D\text{)}$$

Stuck. We cannot derive $E$ or $F$ yet (we need $AC$ to derive $E$). So $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D\} \neq R$.

**Step 2:** Try adding one more attribute to $\{A\}$ that might help.

Try $\{A, E\}^+$:
- $\{A, E\}$
- $A \rightarrow B \Rightarrow \{A, B, E\}$
- $B \rightarrow C \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, E\}$
- $C \rightarrow D \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E\}$
- $E \rightarrow F \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$

So $\{A, E\}^+ = R$. Check minimality:
- $\{A\}^+ \neq R$
- $\{E\}^+ = \{E, F\} \neq R$

Therefore, **$\{A, E\}$ is a candidate key.**

Try $\{A, C\}^+$:
- $\{A, C\}$
- $A \rightarrow B \Rightarrow \{A, B, C\}$
- $C \rightarrow D \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D\}$
- $AC \rightarrow E \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E\}$
- $E \rightarrow F \Rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$

So $\{A, C\}^+ = R$. Check minimality:
- $\{A\}^+ \neq R$
- $\{C\}^+ = \{C, D\} \neq R$

Therefore, **$\{A, C\}$ is also a candidate key.**

Try $\{A, F\}^+$: $F$ only appears on RHS, so $\{A, F\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ (since $E \rightarrow F$ is the only way to derive $F$, and we already have $E$). Minimality holds.

So $\{A, F\}^+ = R$. Check minimality:
- $\{A\}^+ \neq R$
- $\{F\}^+ = \{F\} \neq R$

Therefore, **$\{A, F\}$ is also a candidate key.**

**List of Candidate Keys:** $\{A, E\}$, $\{A, C\}$, $\{A, F\}$.

**Prime attributes:** $A$, $C$, $E$, $F$.
**Non-prime attributes:** $B$, $D$.

---

#### Part (b): Highest Normal Form & BCNF Decomposition

**Check each FD against BCNF/3NF conditions:**

| FD | LHS is Superkey of R? | RHS Prime? | 3NF? | BCNF? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | No (e.g., $\{A\}$ is not a CK of $R$ — only $\{A,E\},\{A,C\},\{A,F\}$ are) | $B$ is non-prime | **No** | **No** |
| $C \rightarrow D$ | No | $D$ is non-prime | **No** | **No** |
| $E \rightarrow F$ | No | $F$ is prime | Yes | **No** |
| $AC \rightarrow E$ | Yes (CK) | $E$ is prime | Yes | Yes |
| $B \rightarrow C$ | No | $C$ is prime | Yes | **No** |

Since $A \rightarrow B$ violates 3NF (LHS not a superkey, RHS is non-prime), $R$ is **not in 3NF, not in 2NF** — it is in **1NF only**.

**Decompose into BCNF (lossless, step by step):**

**Step 1:** Split on $A \rightarrow B$ (the most basic violation):
- $R_1(A, B)$ with FDs: $A \rightarrow B$.
- $R_2(A, C, D, E, F)$ with FDs: $C \rightarrow D$, $E \rightarrow F$, $AC \rightarrow E$.

Verify lossless: $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{A\}$, and $A \rightarrow B$ means $A$ is a key of $R_1$. ✅

**Step 2:** In $R_2$, violating FD: $C \rightarrow D$. Split:
- $R_{2a}(C, D)$ with FD: $C \rightarrow D$.
- $R_{2b}(A, C, E, F)$ with FDs: $E \rightarrow F$, $AC \rightarrow E$.

Verify lossless: $R_{2a} \cap R_{2b} = \{C\}$, and $C \rightarrow D$ makes $C$ a key of $R_{2a}$. ✅

**Step 3:** In $R_{2b}(A, C, E, F)$, violating FD: $E \rightarrow F$ and $B \rightarrow C$ (the latter is not in $R_{2b}$). Split on $E \rightarrow F$:
- $R_{2b1}(E, F)$ with FD: $E \rightarrow F$.
- $R_{2b2}(A, C, E)$ with FDs: $AC \rightarrow E$.

Verify lossless: $R_{2b1} \cap R_{2b2} = \{E\}$, and $E \rightarrow F$ makes $E$ a key of $R_{2b1}$. ✅

**Step 4:** Check $R_{2b2}(A, C, E)$: FD is $AC \rightarrow E$. Is $\{A, C\}$ a superkey of $R_{2b2}$? Yes (it's the only CK). ✅ BCNF.

**Final BCNF Decomposition:**
- $R_1(A, B)$
- $R_{2a}(C, D)$
- $R_{2b1}(E, F)$
- $R_{2b2}(A, C, E)$

All four are in BCNF, and the decomposition is **lossless** at every step.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Candidate Key

**Step 1:** Identify mandatory attributes (never on RHS).
- $ID$: LHS only. **Must be in every CK.**
- $Proj$: LHS only. **Must be in every CK.**
- $Dept$, $HOD$, $Budget$: on RHS. Not mandatory.

So every CK contains $\{ID, Proj\}$. Compute $\{ID, Proj\}^+$:
- $\{ID, Proj\}$
- $ID \rightarrow Dept \Rightarrow \{ID, Proj, Dept\}$
- $Dept \rightarrow HOD \Rightarrow \{ID, Proj, Dept, HOD\}$
- $HOD \rightarrow Budget \Rightarrow \{ID, Proj, Dept, HOD, Budget\}$

So $\{ID, Proj\}^+ = R$. Check minimality:
- $\{ID\}^+ = \{ID, Dept, HOD, Budget\}$ (using transitivity) — **wait, this DOES include all except Proj!** Hmm. Re-check: is there any FD that derives Proj from ID? No. So $\{ID\}^+ = \{ID, Dept, HOD, Budget\} \neq R$.
- $\{Proj\}^+ = \{Proj\}$ (Proj is on LHS only, never on RHS, so no FD determines it alone). $\{Proj\}^+ \neq R$.

Therefore, **$\{ID, Proj\}$ is the unique candidate key.**

**Prime attributes:** $ID$, $Proj$.
**Non-prime attributes:** $Dept$, $HOD$, $Budget$.

#### Part (b): Highest Normal Form & 2NF Decomposition

Check each FD:

| FD | LHS Superkey? | RHS Prime? | 2NF? | 3NF? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $ID \rightarrow Dept$ | No (only $\{ID, Proj\}$ is CK) | $Dept$ non-prime | **No** | **No** |
| $Dept \rightarrow HOD$ | No | $HOD$ non-prime | **No** | **No** |
| $HOD \rightarrow Budget$ | No | $Budget$ non-prime | **No** | **No** |
| $\{ID, Proj\} \rightarrow$ Duration | Yes | $Duration$ non-prime | No (this is full FD — OK) | No |
| $ID, Proj \rightarrow$ Dept (partial!) | $ID$ alone is not full CK | $Dept$ non-prime | **Violates 2NF** | — |

Since there are partial dependencies of non-prime attributes on parts of the composite key, $R$ is in **1NF only**.

**Decompose to BCNF:**

Step 1: Split on $ID \rightarrow Dept$:
- $R_1(ID, Dept, HOD, Budget)$ with FDs: $ID \rightarrow Dept$, $Dept \rightarrow HOD$, $HOD \rightarrow Budget$.
- $R_2(ID, Proj, Duration)$ with FD: $\{ID, Proj\} \rightarrow Duration$.

Step 2: $R_1$ has transitive dependency. Split on $Dept \rightarrow HOD$:
- $R_{1a}(Dept, HOD, Budget)$ with FDs: $Dept \rightarrow HOD$, $HOD \rightarrow Budget$.
- $R_{1b}(ID, Dept)$ with FD: $ID \rightarrow Dept$.

Step 3: $R_{1a}$ still has transitive dependency. Split on $HOD \rightarrow Budget$:
- $R_{1a1}(HOD, Budget)$ with FD: $HOD \rightarrow Budget$.
- $R_{1a2}(Dept, HOD)$ with FD: $Dept \rightarrow HOD$.

**Final BCNF Decomposition (Lossless):**
- $R_{1b}(ID, Dept)$
- $R_{1a2}(Dept, HOD)$
- $R_{1a1}(HOD, Budget)$
- $R_2(ID, Proj, Duration)$

All four are in BCNF and the decomposition is lossless at every split.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing minimality check:** Students often declare a key without verifying that no proper subset of it determines the full relation. Always check subset closures.
> 2. **Confusing 3NF with BCNF:** In 3NF, a FD $X \rightarrow A$ is allowed if $A$ is a prime attribute (even if $X$ is not a superkey). BCNF forbids this. KTU questions frequently exploit this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting lossless verification:** Decomposing into BCNF without showing the $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ test costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 4. **Stop at 3NF when BCNF is asked:** Many students stop at 3NF because it's "good enough" in practice. If the question says "decompose into BCNF," you *must* proceed until BCNF is achieved.
> 5. **Ignoring dependency preservation:** BCNF is *not always* dependency-preserving. If the question asks for *both* lossless and dependency-preserving, the target is 3NF (not BCNF).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Normalization** = systematic process to reduce redundancy & eliminate insertion, update, and deletion anomalies.
- **Functional Dependency** $X \rightarrow Y$ is the formal building block of normalization.
- **Armstrong's Axioms** (Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity) generate $F^+$.
- **Attribute Closure $X^+$** is the algorithmic way to find keys and test FDs.
- **Candidate Key** = minimal super key; **Prime** attribute = appears in some candidate key; **Non-prime** = does not.
- **1NF:** All attribute values are atomic (no repeating groups / multi-valued attributes).
- **2NF:** 1NF + no partial dependency of non-prime attributes on a candidate key.
- **3NF:** 2NF + no transitive dependency of non-prime attributes on a key; equivalently, $X \rightarrow A$ is OK only if $X$ is a superkey OR $A$ is prime.
- **BCNF:** 3NF + every determinant is a superkey (strictly stronger than 3NF).
- **Lossless Join** is guaranteed by both BCNF and 3NF decompositions; **Dependency Preservation** is guaranteed by 3NF but not always by BCNF.
- The **strict hierarchy** is: 5NF ⊃ 4NF ⊃ BCNF ⊃ 3NF ⊃ 2NF ⊃ 1NF.
- **Killer tip for 14-mark questions:** Always show (i) the candidate key computation, (ii) prime vs non-prime classification, (iii) explicit violation analysis per FD, (iv) stepwise decomposition, and (v) lossless-join verification at each step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Dependency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functional Dependency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Functional Dependency — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Functional Dependency (FD)**, denoted as $X \rightarrow Y$, is a constraint between two sets of attributes $X$ and $Y$ of a relation schema $R$. Formally, for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in any legal relation state $r(R)$:

$$\text{If } t_1[X] = t_2[X], \text{ then } t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$$

In plain words: **$X$ functionally determines $Y$**, or equivalently, **$Y$ is functionally dependent on $X$**.

- The attribute set $X$ is called the **Determinant** (left-hand side, LHS).
- The attribute set $Y$ is called the **Dependent** (right-hand side, RHS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Word-for-Word):**
> "A functional dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ holds on relation schema $R$ if and only if, for every permissible instance $r$ of $R$, whenever two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ agree on the attributes $X$, they also agree on the attributes $Y$."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aadhaar → Name" Intuition

Imagine a government database storing citizens. Every citizen has a unique **Aadhaar Number** and a **Name**.

- If I tell you someone's Aadhaar Number (say, `1234-5678-9012`), you immediately know their **Name**.
- But the reverse is NOT true — knowing a name does not uniquely identify the Aadhaar (many people share names).

In database terms:
$$\text{Aadhaar} \rightarrow \text{Name}$$

Just like a mathematical function $f(x) = y$, where each $x$ maps to **exactly one** $y$, in a FD, each value of the determinant $X$ maps to **exactly one** value of the dependent $Y$.

### Another Classic Example
Consider a `STUDENT(roll_no, name, course, dept, marks, grade)` relation:

$$\text{roll\_no} \rightarrow \{\text{name, course, dept, marks, grade}\}$$

A roll number is **unique** to each student, so knowing the roll number reveals *all* other attributes. Hence, roll_no is the **primary key**, and it functionally determines every other attribute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Functional Dependencies are **semantic** constraints — they arise from the *meaning* of the data, not from the data values themselves. We know `roll_no → name` not because of what is currently in the table, but because of what the attributes *mean* in the real world.

## 1.3 Why Functional Dependencies Matter (The Big Picture)

FDs are the **mathematical foundation** of database normalization. They are used to:

1. **Detect Redundancy** — If $X \rightarrow Y$ holds, then $Y$ is stored only once per unique $X$, avoiding duplication.
2. **Design Better Schemas** — Normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF) are all defined in terms of FDs.
3. **Reason About Data** — Through inference rules, we can derive new FDs from existing ones.
4. **Lossless-Join Decomposition** — FDs guide how to split tables without losing information.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Functional Dependencies (Board-Exam Hot Topic)

Let $X \rightarrow Y$ be a functional dependency on schema $R$.

### A. Based on the Attribute Relationship

| Type | Condition | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Trivial FD** | $Y \subseteq X$ (dependent is a subset of the determinant) | `{roll_no, name} \rightarrow roll\_no` |
| **Non-Trivial FD** | $Y \not\subseteq X$ (but $X \cap Y$ may be non-empty) | `{roll_no, name} \rightarrow dept` |
| **Completely Non-Trivial FD** | $X \cap Y = \emptyset$ (no common attributes) | `roll_no \rightarrow dept` |

### B. Based on Full / Partial Dependency (Critical for 2NF)

| Type | Definition | KTU Use Case |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Full Functional Dependency** | $X \rightarrow Y$, and no proper subset of $X$ determines $Y$ | `{roll_no, course} \rightarrow grade` |
| **Partial Functional Dependency** | $X \rightarrow Y$, but some proper subset of $X$ also determines $Y$ | `roll_no \rightarrow name` (within `{roll_no, course}`) |
| **Transitive Dependency** | $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, and $Y \not\rightarrow X$ | `roll_no \rightarrow dept \rightarrow hod` |

## 2.2 Armstrong's Axioms (Inference Rules) — The Core Toolkit

Armstrong's axioms are a **complete** and **sound** set of inference rules to derive all FDs logically implied by a given FD set $F$.

### I. Three Primary (Basic) Rules

1. **Reflexivity (R1):** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$.
2. **Augmentation (R2):** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$ for any $Z$.
3. **Transitivity (R3):** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$.

### II. Three Additional (Derived) Rules

4. **Union (Additivity):** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow YZ$.
5. **Decomposition (Projectivity):** If $X \rightarrow YZ$, then $X \rightarrow Y$ and $X \rightarrow Z$.
6. **Pseudo-Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $YZ \rightarrow W$, then $XZ \rightarrow W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Always use the primary axioms (R1, R2, R3) for full marks. Derived rules (Union, Decomposition, Pseudo-Transitivity) are bonus shortcuts but must be correctly cited.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| **Closure of Attribute Set** | $X^+ = X \cup \{A \mid X \rightarrow A \text{ is derivable from } F\}$ | Find all attributes determined by $X$ |
| **Closure of FD Set** | $F^+ = \{X \rightarrow Y \mid F \models X \rightarrow Y\}$ | All FDs logically implied by $F$ |
| **Member of $F^+$ Test** | $Y \subseteq X^+$ | Decide if $X \rightarrow Y \in F^+$ |
| **Superkey Test** | $X^+ \supseteq R$ | Decide if $X$ is a superkey of $R$ |
| **Candidate Key** | Minimal $X$ such that $X^+ = R$ | Primary key candidate |
| **Canonical Cover ($\vert F_c \vert$)** | Reduced equivalent FD set with no redundant attrs/FDs | Used in decomposition algorithms |
| **Extraneous Attribute** | $A \in Y$ is extraneous in $X \rightarrow Y$ if $A \in X^+$ of $(X - \{A\})$ | Simplify FDs |
| **Equivalence of FD Sets** | $F \equiv E$ iff $F^+ = E^+$ | Compare two sets of FDs |
| **Valid FD Check** | $\vert X \cap Y \vert$ analysis for triviality | Classify FD type |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in real engineering:** In enterprise systems (banking, e-commerce, healthcare), FDs are used by the database engine to **validate data integrity**, **plan query optimizations**, and **automatically denormalize** for performance — making them central to backend engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing the Attribute Closure $X^+$

This is the **single most important algorithm** for KTU 2024 exams. Let us work through it methodically.

### Problem Setup
Let $R = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and $F$ be the following set of FDs:

$$
F = \{ A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; CG \rightarrow H \text{ (N/A here)}, \ldots \}
$$

For a cleaner example, let us redefine:

$$
F = \{ A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E, \; CE \rightarrow F \}
$$

**Find $(AC)^+$ — the closure of attribute set $\{A, C\}$.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Initialize** $(AC)^+ = \{A, C\}$.

$$
\text{closure}^{(0)} = \{A, C\}
$$

**Step 2: Find FDs whose LHS is a subset of $(AC)^+$ initially.**

- $A \rightarrow BC$ : LHS = $\{A\}$ is a subset of $\{A, C\}$ → **APPLY**.
- $B \rightarrow D$ : LHS = $\{B\}$ is NOT a subset → skip.
- $D \rightarrow E$ : LHS = $\{D\}$ is NOT a subset → skip.
- $CE \rightarrow F$ : LHS = $\{C, E\}$; $E \notin \{A, C\}$ → skip.

After applying $A \rightarrow BC$:

$$
\text{closure}^{(1)} = \{A, C\} \cup \{B, C\} = \{A, B, C\}
$$

**Step 3: Re-check FDs against the updated closure $\{A, B, C\}$.**

- $A \rightarrow BC$ : Already applied (no change).
- $B \rightarrow D$ : LHS = $\{B\} \subseteq \{A, B, C\}$ → **APPLY**.

After applying $B \rightarrow D$:

$$
\text{closure}^{(2)} = \{A, B, C\} \cup \{D\} = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

**Step 4: Re-check again with $\{A, B, C, D\}$.**

- $D \rightarrow E$ : LHS = $\{D\} \subseteq \{A, B, C, D\}$ → **APPLY**.

After applying $D \rightarrow E$:

$$
\text{closure}^{(3)} = \{A, B, C, D\} \cup \{E\} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
$$

**Step 5: Final check with $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$.**

- $CE \rightarrow F$ : LHS = $\{C, E\} \subseteq \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ → **APPLY**.

After applying $CE \rightarrow F$:

$$
\text{closure}^{(4)} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} \cup \{F\} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}
$$

**Step 6: Termination Check.** No new attributes are added in the next pass.

$$
\boxed{(AC)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since $(AC)^+ = R$, the attribute set $\{A, C\}$ is a **superkey** of $R$. To check if it is a **candidate key**, we would now test whether proper subsets $\{A\}^+$ and $\{C\}^+$ equal $R$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Finding the Minimal (Canonical) Cover $F_c$

**Problem:** Given $F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; B \rightarrow D, \; AB \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E\}$, find $F_c$.

### Step 1: Decompose RHS into Single Attributes (using Decomposition Rule)

Rewrite each FD so that the RHS has only one attribute:

$$
F' = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; AB \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E\}
$$

### Step 2: Remove Extraneous Attributes from LHS

Consider $AB \rightarrow D$:

- Test if $A$ is extraneous: Compute $\{B\}^+ = \{B, D, E\}$ using $B \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow E$. Since $D \in \{B\}^+$, attribute $A$ is **extraneous**.
- The FD reduces to $B \rightarrow D$, but this is **already present** in $F'$, so we can **remove** $AB \rightarrow D$.

$$
F'' = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E\}
$$

### Step 3: Remove Redundant FDs

Test each FD:

- $A \rightarrow B$ : Without it, $\{A\}^+ = \{A, C\}$. Since $B \notin \{A, C\}^+$, this FD is **NOT redundant**. Keep it.
- $A \rightarrow C$ : Without it, $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, D, E\}$. Since $C \notin \{A, B, D, E\}^+$, this FD is **NOT redundant**. Keep it.
- $B \rightarrow D$ : Without it, $\{B\}^+ = \{B\}$. Since $D \notin \{B\}^+$, this FD is **NOT redundant**. Keep it.
- $D \rightarrow E$ : Without it, $\{D\}^+ = \{D\}$. Since $E \notin \{D\}^+$, this FD is **NOT redundant**. Keep it.

$$
\boxed{F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; B \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E\}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Canonical covers are used in **synthesis algorithms** (3NF decomposition) to produce lossless, dependency-preserving decompositions — a cornerstone of database physical design.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Verifying FD Equivalence

**Problem:** Show that $F = \{A \rightarrow C, \; AP \rightarrow D, \; C \rightarrow Q\}$ and $G = \{A \rightarrow CDQ, \; AC \rightarrow PQ, \; C \rightarrow Q\}$ are equivalent.

### Verification: $F \subseteq G^+$

For each FD in $F$, check if it is derivable from $G$:

- $A \rightarrow C$ : From $A \rightarrow CDQ$, by Decomposition, $A \rightarrow C$. ✓
- $AP \rightarrow D$ : From $A \rightarrow CDQ$, we get $A \rightarrow D$. By Augmentation with $P$, $AP \rightarrow DP$, then by Decomposition, $AP \rightarrow D$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow Q$ : Given directly in $G$. ✓

### Verification: $G \subseteq F^+$

- $A \rightarrow CDQ$ : From $A \rightarrow C$ (given), and we need $D$ and $Q$. $A \rightarrow C$ and $C \rightarrow Q$ gives $A \rightarrow Q$ (Transitivity). $AP \rightarrow D$ and $A \rightarrow P$ (Trivial) gives $A \rightarrow D$ (Pseudo-Transitivity with $A \rightarrow P$, $AP \rightarrow D$ → $AA \rightarrow D$ → $A \rightarrow D$). Combined: $A \rightarrow CDQ$. ✓
- $AC \rightarrow PQ$ : $AC \rightarrow C$ (Trivial), and $C \rightarrow Q$ gives $AC \rightarrow Q$. For $P$: $A \rightarrow AP$ (Augmentation with $P$, then Transitivity). $AC \rightarrow APQ$, so $AC \rightarrow PQ$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow Q$ : Given directly in $F$. ✓

Since $F \subseteq G^+$ and $G \subseteq F^+$, we have $F \equiv G$. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: FD Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Functional Dependency X implies Y] --> rootB[Type 1: Trivial]
    rootA --> rootC[Type 2: Non Trivial]
    rootA --> rootD[Type 3: Completely Non Trivial]

    rootB[Type 1: Trivial] --> rootB1[Y is subset of X]
    rootB1 --> rootB2[Example: AB implies A]

    rootC[Type 2: Non Trivial] --> rootC1[Y is not subset of X, but X intersect Y is not empty]
    rootC1 --> rootC2[Example: AB implies B]

    rootD[Type 3: Completely Non Trivial] --> rootD1[X intersect Y is empty]
    rootD1 --> rootD2[Example: SSN implies Name]

    rootA --> rootE[Type 4: Full FD]
    rootA --> rootF[Type 5: Partial FD]
    rootA --> rootG[Type 6: Transitive FD]

    rootE[Type 4: Full FD] --> rootE1[No proper subset of X determines Y]
    rootE1 --> rootE2[Example: roll_no course implies grade]

    rootF[Type 5: Partial FD] --> rootF1[Some proper subset of X determines Y]
    rootF1 --> rootF2[Example: roll_no course implies name]

    rootG[Type 6: Transitive FD] --> rootG1[X implies Y, Y implies Z, Y not imply X]
    rootG1 --> rootG2[Example: roll_no implies dept implies HOD]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Armstrong's Axioms Inference Tree

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Reflexivity R1: Y subset X then X implies Y] --> A1E[Example: AB implies A]
    A2[Augmentation R2: X implies Y then XZ implies YZ] --> A2E[Example: A implies BC, AB implies BCB]
    A3[Transitivity R3: X implies Y, Y implies Z, then X implies Z] --> A3E[Example: A implies B, B implies C, then A implies C]

    A1 --> A4[Derived Union Rule]
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4[Derived: Union: X implies Y, X implies Z, then X implies YZ] --> A4E[Example: A implies B, A implies C, then A implies BC]

    A1 --> A5[Derived Decomposition Rule]
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A5
    A5[Derived: Decomposition: X implies YZ, then X implies Y, X implies Z] --> A5E[Example: A implies BC, then A implies B and A implies C]

    A1 --> A6[Derived Pseudo Transitivity]
    A2 --> A6
    A3 --> A6
    A6[Derived: Pseudo Transitivity: X implies Y, YZ implies W, then XZ implies W] --> A6E[Example: A implies B, BC implies D, then AC implies D]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Attribute Closure Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Start: Input F, attribute set X] --> P1[Initialize X plus equals X]
    P1 --> P2{Loop: Any FD W implies V in F such that W is subset of X plus}
    P2 -- Yes, FD found --> P3[Add V to X plus: X plus equals X plus union V]
    P3 --> P2
    P2 -- No FD applicable --> P4[Termination: X plus is now closed]
    P4 --> P5{Does X plus equal R?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[X is a superkey of R]
    P5 -- No --> P7[X is not a superkey]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define functional dependency. Explain trivial and non-trivial FDs with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **functional dependency** $X \rightarrow Y$ is a constraint specifying that in any relation $r(R)$, two tuples having the same value of $X$ must have the same value of $Y$. Formally: $t_1[X] = t_2[X] \Rightarrow t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$.

- **Trivial FD:** The dependent $Y$ is a subset of the determinant $X$, i.e., $Y \subseteq X$. Example: $\{A, B\} \rightarrow A$ (since $A \subseteq \{A, B\}$). Trivial FDs are always satisfied by every relation.
- **Non-Trivial FD:** $Y$ is not a subset of $X$. Example: $A \rightarrow B$ (since $B \not\subseteq \{A\}$).
- **Completely Non-Trivial FD:** $X \cap Y = \emptyset$. Example: $A \rightarrow B$ where $A$ and $B$ are disjoint.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Armstrong's axioms.**

**Model Answer:**

Armstrong's axioms are a set of inference rules for deriving all functional dependencies logically implied by a given set $F$. They are **sound** (derive only valid FDs) and **complete** (derive all valid FDs).

1. **Reflexivity:** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$.
2. **Augmentation:** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$ for any attribute set $Z$.
3. **Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$.

Additionally, three derived rules — **Union**, **Decomposition**, and **Pseudo-Transitivity** — follow from these.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the relation $R = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ with the set of functional dependencies:

$$F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow F\}$$

Compute $(AB)^+$ and state whether $\{A, B\}$ is a candidate key of $R$. Justify your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the canonical (minimal) cover $F_c$ for the set of FDs given above. Show all steps clearly.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Computing $(AB)^+$

We apply the closure algorithm iteratively.

**Initialization:**
$$
(AB)^+ = \{A, B\}
$$

**Iteration 1:** Check FDs whose LHS $\subseteq \{A, B\}$.
- $A \rightarrow BC$: LHS = $\{A\} \subseteq \{A, B\}$ → apply. Add $\{B, C\}$ to closure.
- $B \rightarrow D$: LHS = $\{B\} \subseteq \{A, B\}$ → apply. Add $\{D\}$ to closure.

$$
(AB)^+ = \{A, B\} \cup \{B, C\} \cup \{D\} = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Check FDs against $\{A, B, C, D\}$.
- $CD \rightarrow E$: LHS = $\{C, D\} \subseteq \{A, B, C, D\}$ → apply. Add $\{E\}$.

$$
(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Check FDs against $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$.
- $E \rightarrow F$: LHS = $\{E\} \subseteq \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ → apply. Add $\{F\}$.

$$
(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}
$$

**Termination:** No new attributes can be added.

$$
\boxed{(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $(AB)^+ = R$, the set $\{A, B\}$ is a **superkey** of $R$.

**To test if it is a candidate key**, we check proper subsets:
- $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R$ (since $A \rightarrow BC$, and from $B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F$).

Since $\{A\}^+ = R$, attribute $A$ alone is a superkey, and therefore $\{A, B\}$ is **NOT a candidate key** (it is not minimal). The candidate key is $\{A\}$.

#### Part (b) — Finding the Canonical Cover $F_c$

**Step 1: Decompose the RHS into single attributes.**

Given $F = \{A \rightarrow BC, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow F\}$, the FD $A \rightarrow BC$ is decomposed as:

$$
F' = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow F\}
$$

**Step 2: Remove extraneous attributes from LHS.**

Each LHS here is a single attribute (or in the case of $CD \rightarrow E$, two attributes). Check $CD \rightarrow E$:

- Is $C$ extraneous? Compute $\{D\}^+ = \{D, F\}$ (via $D$ trivial and $E \rightarrow F$? No, $D$ doesn't appear on any LHS). So $\{D\}^+ = \{D\}$. $E \notin \{D\}^+$, so $C$ is **not** extraneous.
- Is $D$ extraneous? Compute $\{C\}^+ = \{C\}$. $E \notin \{C\}^+$, so $D$ is **not** extraneous.

No LHS attribute is extraneous.

**Step 3: Remove redundant FDs.**

Test each FD by removing it and checking if the rest still derive it:

- **Test $A \rightarrow B$**: Remove it. $\{A\}^+ = \{A, C\}$ (using $A \rightarrow C$). $B \notin \{A, C\}^+$. **NOT redundant.** Keep.
- **Test $A \rightarrow C$**: Remove it. $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, D, F\}$ (using $A \rightarrow B$, $B \rightarrow D$, $E \rightarrow F$? — but $E$ is not in closure). So $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, D\}$. $C \notin \{A, B, D\}^+$. **NOT redundant.** Keep.
- **Test $CD \rightarrow E$**: Remove it. $\{C, D\}^+ = \{C, D\}$ (since $C$ and $D$ are not on any other LHS). $E \notin \{C, D\}^+$. **NOT redundant.** Keep.
- **Test $B \rightarrow D$**: Remove it. $\{B\}^+ = \{B\}$. $D \notin \{B\}^+$. **NOT redundant.** Keep.
- **Test $E \rightarrow F$**: Remove it. $\{E\}^+ = \{E\}$. $F \notin \{E\}^+$. **NOT redundant.** Keep.

$$
\boxed{F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow C, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow F\}}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the following:
   (i) Full functional dependency
   (ii) Partial functional dependency
   (iii) Transitive dependency

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the relation $R = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with FDs $F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; AE \rightarrow D\}$, find all candidate keys of $R$ using the attribute closure method.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Dependency Types (Conceptual Answer)

Consider the relation `STUDENT_MARKS(roll_no, course, instructor, marks, grade)`.

**(i) Full Functional Dependency:** An attribute $Y$ is fully functionally dependent on a composite key $X$ if and only if $Y$ depends on the *entire* $X$, not on any proper subset of $X$.

In `STUDENT_MARKS`:
- Primary Key = $\{roll\_no, course\}$
- `grade` is **fully** functionally dependent on $\{roll\_no, course\}$ because grade depends on the combination — neither `roll_no` alone nor `course` alone determines the grade.

**(ii) Partial Functional Dependency:** $Y$ depends on only a *proper subset* of the composite key $X$.

- `instructor` is partially dependent on $\{roll\_no, course\}$ because `instructor` depends *only* on `course` (a course has one instructor), not on `roll_no`.

**(iii) Transitive Dependency:** A dependency $X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$ where $X$ does not functionally determine $Y$ through $Z$ directly, but transitively.

- `roll_no → dept`, and `dept → HOD_name`. So `roll_no → HOD_name` is a **transitive dependency**.

#### Part (b) — Finding All Candidate Keys

**Step 1: Find which attributes never appear on RHS** (these must be in every candidate key).

Looking at the RHS of each FD in $F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; AE \rightarrow D\}$:
- Attributes on RHS: $\{B, C, D\}$
- Attributes that NEVER appear on RHS: $\{A, E\}$

Therefore, $\{A, E\}$ must be in **every** candidate key. This is the **essential attribute set**.

**Step 2: Start with $\{AE\}^+$.**

$$
(AE)^+ = \{A, E\}
$$

Apply $A \rightarrow B$:

$$
(AE)^+ = \{A, E, B\}
$$

Apply $B \rightarrow C$:

$$
(AE)^+ = \{A, E, B, C\}
$$

Apply $C \rightarrow D$:

$$
(AE)^+ = \{A, E, B, C, D\} = R
$$

So $\{A, E\}^+ = R$, meaning $\{A, E\}$ is a **superkey**.

**Step 3: Check if any proper subset is a superkey.**

Proper subsets of $\{A, E\}$: $\{A\}$ and $\{E\}$.

- $\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$. Missing $E$. Not a superkey.
- $\{E\}^+ = \{E\}$. Not a superkey.

Since neither proper subset is a superkey, $\{A, E\}$ is **minimal**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Only Candidate Key} = \{A, E\}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Stop after the first superkey found** — Many students stop after finding one key. Always verify whether proper subsets also form a key to determine if it is a *candidate* key.
> 2. **Forgetting essential attributes** — If an attribute never appears on the RHS, it *must* be in the candidate key. Missing this loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Misapplying Augmentation** — Augmentation adds the *same* set to both sides, not arbitrary attributes. Use $XZ \rightarrow YZ$, not $XZ \rightarrow Y$.
> 4. **Skipping iteration passes** — The closure algorithm requires *multiple passes* until no new attribute is added. Stopping early gives an incomplete closure.
> 5. **Confusing FDs with multivalued dependencies (MVDs)** — $X \rightarrow Y$ is *uniqueness* of $Y$ per $X$; $X \rightarrow\rightarrow Y$ (MVD) is *set independence*. Do not mix them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Functional Dependencies (Module 3):**

- **FD Definition:** $X \rightarrow Y$ means the LHS *uniquely determines* the RHS — like a pure mathematical function.
- **Trivial vs Non-Trivial:** Trivial FDs are always true; non-trivial ones carry semantic information.
- **Full vs Partial:** Full dependency needs the *whole* composite key; partial uses only a part (drives 2NF).
- **Transitive:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ causes redundancy (drives 3NF).
- **Armstrong's Axioms (3 Primary):** Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity — sound and complete.
- **Derived Rules (3):** Union, Decomposition, Pseudo-Transitivity.
- **Attribute Closure $X^+$:** Iterative algorithm using LHS-subset checks. If $X^+ = R$, then $X$ is a superkey.
- **Candidate Key:** Minimal superkey — found by testing whether all proper subsets of a superkey are also superkeys.
- **Essential Attributes:** Attributes that never appear on any RHS *must* belong to every candidate key.
- **Canonical Cover $F_c$:** (1) Make RHS singleton, (2) remove extraneous LHS attributes, (3) remove redundant FDs.
- **Equivalence:** $F \equiv G$ iff $F \subseteq G^+$ and $G \subseteq F^+$.
- **FDs in Real Systems:** Used in schema design (normalization), integrity checking, and query optimization in all major RDBMSs (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Armstrong's Axioms** are a complete set of inference rules introduced by William W. Armstrong in 1974 that are used to generate all **functional dependencies (FDs)** logically implied by a given set of functional dependencies. In the context of the KTU 2024 DBMS syllabus, they form the formal logical foundation of **FD inference**, enabling the derivation of the **closure of a set of FDs (denoted F⁺)** and the **closure of attribute sets (denoted X⁺)**.

Let **R** be a relation schema, and let **F** be a set of functional dependencies defined on **R**. The notation **F ⊨ X → Y** means *"FD X → Y is logically implied by F"*. Armstrong's axioms provide a sound and complete mechanism to derive such implications using only symbolic manipulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Rules"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are building a family tree. You are given only **3 basic facts** (the parents). Using only those 3 facts, you can logically deduce the existence of grandparents, cousins, uncles, and so on — without anyone explicitly telling you. In the same way, Armstrong's axioms give you **3 primary facts (primary rules)** from which you can derive every other valid FD in the database. The "parents" are the original FDs in F, and the "extended family" is the **closure F⁺**.

## 1.3 Why Armstrong's Axioms Matter in KTU Examinations

- They are the **core inference engine** behind normalization (especially 2NF, 3NF, BCNF derivations).
- They answer the question: *"If I know these FDs hold, what other FDs must also hold?"*
- They are **sound** (never produce a wrong FD) and **complete** (can produce every correct FD).
- They are foundational for computing **canonical covers**, **minimal covers**, and **attribute closures**.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| R | A relation schema (set of attributes) |
| F | A given set of functional dependencies on R |
| F⁺ | The closure of F (all FDs implied by F) |
| X⁺ | The closure of attribute set X under F |
| X → Y | A functional dependency where X determines Y |
| α, β, γ | Greek letters typically used to denote attribute sets |
| ⊨ | Semantic entailment (logically implies) |
| ⊢ | Syntactic derivation (provable using axioms) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary (Axiomatic) Inference Rules

Armstrong identified **three irrefutable, irreducible rules** from which every other valid rule can be derived. These form the irreducible kernel of the inference system.

> [!NOTE]
> **A1. Reflexivity (IR1)**
> If **β ⊆ α** (i.e., β is a subset of α), then **α → β** always holds.
> *Meaning: A set of attributes always determines itself or any of its subsets. This is a trivial, always-true dependency.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A2. Augmentation (IR2)**
> If **α → β** holds, then **γα → γβ** holds for any attribute set γ.
> *Meaning: You may append the same attributes to both sides of a valid FD and the FD remains valid.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A3. Transitivity (IR3)**
> If **α → β** holds and **β → γ** holds, then **α → γ** holds.
> *Meaning: Functional dependence is transitive. If A determines B, and B determines C, then A determines C.*

## 2.2 The Three Secondary (Derived) Inference Rules

These rules are **not independent** — they can be algebraically proven from A1, A2, and A3. They are, however, much more convenient in practical KTU examination problems and are therefore always highlighted.

> [!NOTE]
> **A4. Union (Composition)**
> If **α → β** holds and **α → γ** holds, then **α → βγ** holds.
> *Practical Use: The most-used derived rule — combines the right-hand sides of two FDs with the same left-hand side.*

> [!NOTE]
> **A5. Decomposition (Projective)**
> If **α → βγ** holds, then **α → β** holds and **α → γ** holds.
> *Practical Use: The opposite of Union. Splits a composite right-hand side into individual FDs.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A6. Pseudotransitivity**
> If **α → β** holds and **γβ → δ** holds, then **αγ → δ** holds.
> *Practical Use: A powerful transitivity variant where partial overlap of left and right sides is permitted.*

## 2.3 Additional Derived Rules (Frequently Tested)

> **A7. Composition Rule:** If α → β and γ → δ, then αγ → βδ.
>
> **A8. Accumulation / Self-Determination:** If α → β, then αγ → β (for any γ).
>
> **A9. Extended Reflexivity:** If α → β, then αγ → βγ.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Rule | Statement | Type | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A1. Reflexivity | β ⊆ α ⟹ α → β | Primary | High |
| A2. Augmentation | α → β ⟹ γα → γβ | Primary | High |
| A3. Transitivity | α → β, β → γ ⟹ α → γ | Primary | Very High |
| A4. Union | α → β, α → γ ⟹ α → βγ | Derived | Very High |
| A5. Decomposition | α → βγ ⟹ α → β, α → γ | Derived | Very High |
| A6. Pseudotransitivity | α → β, γβ → δ ⟹ αγ → δ | Derived | Medium |
| A7. Composition | α → β, γ → δ ⟹ αγ → βδ | Derived | Medium |
| A8. Accumulation | α → β ⟹ αγ → β | Derived | Medium |

## 2.5 Closure of a Set of Functional Dependencies (F⁺)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **closure of F**, written **F⁺**, is the set of all functional dependencies that can be inferred from F using Armstrong's axioms.
> $$F^{+} = \{ X \rightarrow Y \mid F \models X \rightarrow Y \}$$

To compute F⁺ in a KTU exam, the standard algorithm is:

1. Start with F (the given FDs).
2. Apply Reflexivity to add trivial FDs.
3. Apply Augmentation to expand FDs.
4. Apply Transitivity to chain FDs.
5. Repeat until no new FDs can be added.

## 2.6 Closure of Attribute Sets (X⁺) — The Examination Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Given an attribute set **X**, its closure **X⁺** is the set of all attributes that are functionally determined by X under F.
> $$X^{+} = \{ A \mid F \models X \rightarrow A \}$$

**The KTU Algorithm to Compute X⁺ (F⁺-free, faster):**

1. Initialize result = X.
2. Repeat:
   a. For each FD **Y → Z** in F, if **Y ⊆ result**, then add Z to result.
   b. Until result does not change.
3. Return result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Use:** A key FD **X → Y** is in F⁺ if and only if **Y ⊆ X⁺**. This single equivalence is the foundation of nearly every KTU numerical problem on this topic.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Normalization:** Determining which FDs violate BCNF or 3NF.
- **Lossless Join Verification:** Checking if a decomposition preserves dependencies.
- **Schema Refinement:** Computing candidate keys via attribute closures.
- **Query Optimization:** FDs allow the optimizer to recognize equivalent query plans.
- **Data Integrity Enforcement:** When designing CHECK constraints and triggers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proving the Union Rule (A4) from Primary Axioms

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that if α → β and α → γ, then α → βγ.

**Step 1.** Given: **α → β** (Hypothesis 1)

**Step 2.** Given: **α → γ** (Hypothesis 2)

**Step 3.** Apply **A2 (Augmentation)** to Step 1 with γ appended on both sides:
Result: **αγ → βγ** … (*Equation 3*)

**Step 4.** Apply **A2 (Augmentation)** to Step 2 with α appended on both sides:
Result: **αα → αγ**

**Step 5.** By **Reflexivity**, αα = α, so we have **α → αγ** … (*Equation 5*)

**Step 6.** Apply **A2 (Augmentation)** to Equation 5 with β appended:
Result: **αβ → αγβ** … (*Equation 6*)

**Step 7.** Apply **Reflexivity** simplification: αβ ⊇ α, so **αβ** is equivalent to using α on the left in conjunction with the original.

**Step 8.** Combine Equation 3 and Equation 5 using **A3 (Transitivity)**:
α → αγ and αγ → βγ, therefore **α → βγ** … (*Proven*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "using Armstrong's axioms, prove…", the answer must cite the rule (e.g., "by augmentation") and show the new FD generated at each step. Skipping the justification costs full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving the Decomposition Rule (A5)

**Goal:** Prove that if α → βγ, then α → β and α → γ.

**Step 1.** Given: **α → βγ** (Hypothesis)

**Step 2.** Since **β ⊆ βγ**, by **A1 (Reflexivity)**: βγ → β

**Step 3.** Apply **A3 (Transitivity)** to Step 1 and Step 2:
α → βγ and βγ → β, therefore **α → β** … (*Proven — first part*)

**Step 4.** Since **γ ⊆ βγ**, by **A1 (Reflexivity)**: βγ → γ

**Step 5.** Apply **A3 (Transitivity)** to Step 1 and Step 4:
α → βγ and βγ → γ, therefore **α → γ** … (*Proven — second part*)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving Pseudotransitivity (A6)

**Goal:** If α → β and γβ → δ, then αγ → δ.

**Step 1.** Given: **α → β**

**Step 2.** Apply **A2 (Augmentation)** with γ on both sides:
Result: **αγ → βγ** … (*Equation 2*)

**Step 3.** Reorder: **βγ → γβ** (by trivial attribute set equivalence under augmentation/reflexivity).

**Step 4.** Apply **A2** to the given **γβ → δ** with α on the left (extended reflexivity):
Actually, the cleaner derivation: since βγ = γβ as a set, Equation 2 gives **αγ → γβ**.

**Step 5.** Given **γβ → δ**.

**Step 6.** Apply **A3 (Transitivity)** to Step 4 and Step 5:
αγ → γβ and γβ → δ, therefore **αγ → δ** … (*Proven*)

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Computing Attribute Closure (The KTU Numerical Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given R(A, B, C, D, E, F) and F = { A → BC, CD → E, E → F, B → DE }. Compute (AD)⁺.

**Iteration-by-Iteration Trace:**

| Step | FD Used | Reason | Attributes Added | Current (AD)⁺ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Init | — | Initial set | — | {A, D} |
| 1 | A → BC | A ∈ (AD)⁺ | B, C | {A, B, C, D} |
| 2 | CD → E | C, D ∈ (AD)⁺ | E | {A, B, C, D, E} |
| 3 | B → DE | B ∈ (AD)⁺ | D (already), E (already) | {A, B, C, D, E} |
| 4 | E → F | E ∈ (AD)⁺ | F | {A, B, C, D, E, F} |
| End | — | No new attributes | — | {A, B, C, D, E, F} |

**Final Answer:**
$$(AD)^{+} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since (AD)⁺ = R, the attribute set {A, D} is a **superkey** of R. By further checking minimality (i.e., neither {A}⁺ = R nor {D}⁺ = R), we can determine whether it is a **candidate key**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Verifying FD Membership in F⁺

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Using the same F above, verify whether the FD **BD → F** is in F⁺.

**Algorithm:** Compute (BD)⁺ and check if F ∈ (BD)⁺.

| Step | FD Used | Reason | Added | (BD)⁺ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Init | — | — | — | {B, D} |
| 1 | B → DE | B ∈ (BD)⁺ | E | {B, D, E} |
| 2 | E → F | E ∈ (BD)⁺ | F | {B, D, E, F} |
| 3 | CD → E | C ∉ (BD)⁺ | — | No change |
| 4 | A → BC | A ∉ (BD)⁺ | — | No change |

**Final (BD)⁺ = {B, D, E, F}**

Since F ∈ (BD)⁺, we conclude:
$$F \models BD \rightarrow F$$

Therefore **BD → F ∈ F⁺**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Rule

| Scenario in the Problem | Best Rule to Apply | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Combining two FDs with the same LHS | A4. Union | Combines RHS efficiently |
| Splitting a composite RHS | A5. Decomposition | Isolates atomic FDs |
| LHS of one FD overlaps RHS of another | A6. Pseudotransitivity | Handles partial overlap |
| Need to expand a set to trigger an FD | A2. Augmentation | Builds the LHS prerequisite |
| Chaining through a sequence of FDs | A3. Transitivity | Connects LHS to RHS via intermediate |
| Checking if a trivial FD is implied | A1. Reflexivity | Direct subset check |
| Verifying an FD belongs to F⁺ | Compute X⁺ | Membership test via closure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Armstrong's Inference System

```mermaid
graph TD
    primary1["A1 REFLEXIVITY"] --> union["A4 UNION"]
    primary2["A2 AUGMENTATION"] --> union
    primary1 --> decomp["A5 DECOMPOSITION"]
    primary2 --> decomp
    primary3["A3 TRANSITIVITY"] --> union
    primary3 --> decomp
    primary2 --> pseudo["A6 PSEUDOTRANSITIVITY"]
    primary3 --> pseudo
    primary2 --> comp["A7 COMPOSITION"]
    primary3 --> comp
    primary2 --> accum["A8 ACCUMULATION"]
    primary3 --> accum
    primary2 --> extref["A9 EXTENDED REFLEXIVITY"]
    primary1 --> extref
    union --> closure["FD CLOSURE F PLUS"]
    decomp --> closure
    pseudo --> closure
    comp --> closure
    closure --> attrclos["ATTRIBUTE CLOSURE X PLUS"]
    attrclos --> candkey["CANDIDATE KEY DETECTION"]
    attrclos --> bcnf["BCNF 3NF VERIFICATION"]
    attrclos --> cancover["CANONICAL COVER"]
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flow for Computing Attribute Closure (X⁺)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START: Input F and attribute set X"] --> init["Initialize result equal to X"]
    init --> loopchk{"Did result change in last pass?"}
    loopchk -- Yes --> iterate["Iterate over each FD in F"]
    iterate --> check{"Is LHS of FD subset of result?"}
    check -- Yes --> add["Add RHS of FD to result"]
    add --> iterate
    check -- No --> iterate
    iterate --> loopchk
    loopchk -- No --> output["OUTPUT: result is X PLUS"]
    output --> usage["Used for: candidate key check, FD membership in F PLUS, normalization"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FD Inference

```mermaid
graph LR
    userinput["USER INPUT"] --> relschema["RELATION SCHEMA R"]
    userinput --> fdset["FD SET F"]
    relschema --> engine["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
    fdset --> engine
    engine --> ax1["A1 REFLEXIVITY MODULE"]
    engine --> ax2["A2 AUGMENTATION MODULE"]
    engine --> ax3["A3 TRANSITIVITY MODULE"]
    ax1 --> derived["DERIVED RULES MODULE"]
    ax2 --> derived
    ax3 --> derived
    derived --> fplus["F PLUS CLOSURE GENERATOR"]
    derived --> xplus["X PLUS ATTRIBUTE CLOSURE"]
    fplus --> output["SEMANTIC ENTailMENT F MODELS X to Y"]
    xplus --> output
    output --> norm["NORMALIZATION DECISIONS"]
    output --> key["CANDIDATE KEY IDENTIFICATION"]
```
