Since $A \in R_1$, the candidate key is preserved. **Final 3NF Decomposition:** $$\boxed{\rho = \{R_1(A, B, C, D), \; R_2(B, E, F)\}}$$ **Dependency Preservation Check:** Every FD in $F_c$ involves attributes entirely within one relation: - $A \rightarrow BCD$ → checked in $R_1$ ✓ - $B \rightarrow EF$ → checked in $R_2$ ✓ - **Preserved.** ✓ **Lossless Join Check:** Apply the test on $R_1 \bowtie R_2$: - Common attribute: $B$. - $B \rightarrow EF$ (from $R_2$), so $B$ is a key of $R_2$. - **Lossless join holds.** ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **Given $R(A, B, C, D, E, F, G)$ with $F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; ACD \rightarrow E, \; BF \rightarrow G, \; F \rightarrow G, \; A \rightarrow E\}$.** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the canonical cover $F_c$ of $F$. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Synthesize a 3NF decomposition and verify that it is dependency-preserving and lossless. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Finding $F_c$ [7 marks]** **Given:** $$F = \{A \rightarrow B, \; ACD \rightarrow E, \; BF \rightarrow G, \; F \rightarrow G, \; A \rightarrow E\}$$ **Step 1: Singleton RHS [1 mark]** All RHS are already singletons. $F_1 = F$. **Step 2: Remove Extraneous Attributes [3 marks]** **Test $ACD \rightarrow E$:** - Is $A$ extraneous? Compute $(CD)^+_{F_1}$: $$CD^+ = \{C, D\}$$ $E \notin \{C, D\}$. $A$ is **not extraneous**. - Is $C$ extraneous? Compute $(AD)^+_{F_1}$: $$AD^+ = \{A, D, B, E\} \text{ (via } A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow E\text{)}$$ $E \in \{A, B, D, E\}$. $C$ **is extraneous**. Remove $C$. - Now FD is $AD \rightarrow E$. Is $D$ extraneous? Compute $(A)^+_{F_1}$: $$A^+ = \{A, B, E\}$$ $E \in \{A, B, E\}$. $D$ **is also extraneous**. Remove $D$. - Reduced to $A \rightarrow E$ (already in $F$). **Test $BF \rightarrow G$:** - Is $B$ extraneous? Compute $(F)^+_{F_1}$: $$F^+ = \{F, G\}$$ $G \in \{F, G\}$. $B$ **is extraneous**. Remove $B$. - Reduced to $F \rightarrow G$ (already in $F$). **$F_2$ after Step 2:** $$F_2 = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow E, \; F \rightarrow G\}$$ **Step 3: Remove Redundant FDs [3 marks]** **Test $A \rightarrow B$:** $A^+_{\{A \rightarrow E, F \rightarrow G\}} = \{A, E\}$. $B \notin$. Keep. **Test $A \rightarrow E$:** $A^+_{\{A \rightarrow B, F \rightarrow G\}} = \{A, B\}$. $E \notin$. Keep. **Test $F \rightarrow G$:** $F^+_{\{A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow E\}} = \{F\}$. $G \notin$. Keep. --- **Final Minimal Cover:** $$\boxed{F_c = \{A \rightarrow B, \; A \rightarrow E, \; F \rightarrow G\}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — 3NF Synthesis [7 marks]** **Step 1: Group by LHS:** - $A \rightarrow \{B, E\}$ - $F \rightarrow \{G\}$ **Step 2: Create Relations:** - $R_1(A, B, E)$ from $A \rightarrow BE$ - $R_2(F, G)$ from $F \rightarrow G$ **Step 3: Find Candidate Key of $R$:** Compute $A^+_{F_c} = \{A, B, E\}$. Need to add $C, D, F, G$. Add $F$: $F^+ = \{F, G\}$. Now have $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}$ ✓ So candidate key: $AF$ (or $ACF$, $ADF$, etc., but $AF$ is minimal). $AF \notin R_1 \cup R_2$? Let's check: - $A \in R_1$, $F \in R_2$. The pair $AF$ is **not contained** in any single relation. - So we must **add a relation for the candidate key**. **Final 3NF Decomposition:** $$\boxed{\rho = \{R_1(A, B, E), \; R_2(F, G), \; R_3(A, F, C, D)\}}$$ (Attributes $C, D$ can be placed with $AF$ in $R_3$ since they only appear on the LHS originally.) **Dependency Preservation:** - $A \rightarrow BE$ → preserved in $R_1$ ✓ - $F \rightarrow G$ → preserved in $R_2$ ✓ - All FDs preserved ✓ **Lossless Join Check:** - $R_1 \cap R_2 = \emptyset$ — but $R_3$ shares $A$ with $R_1$ and $F$ with $R_2$. - Apply chase / table algorithm — verified lossless. ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping Step 1:** Students often forget to decompose multi-attribute RHS. The result is technically still a "cover" but **not a canonical cover**. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Extraneous vs. Redundant:** Showing extraneous check on whole FD (Step 3 logic) instead of attribute-level (Step 2 logic) loses full marks for that step. > 3. **Order of Operations:** Always perform Step 1 → Step 2 → Step 3. Reversing leads to wrong answers. > 4. **Re-using Removed FDs:** When testing redundancy in Step 3, the FD being tested **must be excluded** from the closure computation. Students often include it, leading to spurious "not redundant" conclusions. > 5. **Forgetting the Candidate Key:** In 3NF synthesis, forgetting to add a relation for the candidate key (when not contained in any existing relation) loses 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Minimal Cover $F_c$ is the **simplest, irreducible** set of FDs equivalent to $F$ (i.e., $F_c^+ = F^+$). - **Three-Step Algorithm:** **(1)** Singleton RHS → **(2)** Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes → **(3)** Remove Redundant FDs. - **Key Distinction:** *Extraneous* = removable **attribute** in LHS. *Redundant* = removable **FD** from the set. - **Membership Test:** $A$ is extraneous in $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ iff $\beta \subseteq (\alpha - A)^+_F$. - **Redundancy Test:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is redundant iff $\beta \subseteq \alpha^+_{F - \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta\}}$. - **Always re-evaluate:** After removing a redundant FD, do **not** add it back even if other tests seem to need it. - **Use in 3NF Synthesis:** Group $F_c$ by LHS → each group becomes a relation → ensure candidate key is preserved. - **Use in BCNF:** $F_c$ helps identify violations $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ where $\alpha$ is not a superkey. - **Lossless Join Test:** Common attribute between two relations must be a key of at least one of them. - **Dependency Preservation Test:** Each FD in $F_c$ must be checkable within a single relation of the decomposition. - **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Questions on Minimal Cover typically appear as **Part A (3 marks)** for definition/properties or **Part B (7–14 marks)** for full computation with subsequent 3NF/BCNF decomposition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Memory Hook:** Think **"Singletons first, Shrink LHS, Strip Redundancy"** — the three-step mantra for $F_c$ computation under KTU 2024 evaluation. ---

topics.title: First Normal Form (1NF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** First Normal Form (1NF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. First Normal Form (1NF) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A relation (table) $R$ is said to be in **First Normal Form (1NF)** if and only if every cell of the relation contains an **atomic (indivisible / single-valued)** value, and **no attribute (column)** of the relation is allowed to hold a **set of values, an array, a tuple, or a repeating group**. Furthermore, the relation must possess a **well-defined primary key** that uniquely identifies each tuple (row) and a unique ordering of rows/columns as per the relational model.

Mathematically expressed:

$$R \text{ is in 1NF} \iff \forall t \in R,\ \forall A \in \text{Schema}(R):\ t[A] \text{ is atomic and single-valued}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Relation (Table)
- $t$ = Tuple (Row)
- $A$ = Attribute (Column)
- $t[A]$ = Value of attribute $A$ in tuple $t$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Library Card" Mental Model:**
> Imagine a librarian's paper register where a single student's row contains *multiple phone numbers* squashed into a single cell written as `"9876543, 8123456, 7012345"`. This register is *not* in 1NF because the **PHONE** column is a "junk drawer" holding many values. To bring it into 1NF, the librarian must create one card per (Student, Phone) combination — every cell now holds exactly **one** number. The *shape* of the table changes, but the **information** is preserved.

## 1.3 The Three Golden Rules of 1NF

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Mandatory Conditions for a Relation to Qualify as 1NF:**
> 1. **Atomicity Rule** — Each cell must contain exactly one indivisible value (no lists, no sets, no JSON blobs, no nested tables).
> 2. **No Repeating Groups Rule** — Columns that repeat the same kind of information (e.g., `Phone1`, `Phone2`, `Phone3`) are forbidden; all such attributes must be flattened into separate rows.
> 3. **Primary Key Existence Rule** — A primary key (composite or simple) must be defined that guarantees row-level uniqueness for every tuple.

## 1.4 Why 1NF is the Foundation of the Normalization Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, 1NF is treated as the **entry gate** to the entire normalization family tree (**1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF → 4NF → 5NF**). A relation that violates 1NF cannot, by definition, satisfy any higher normal form. Codd's relational algebra requires all operands to be 1NF-compliant because non-1NF relations break the **closure property** of relational operations (e.g., a JOIN or PROJECTION on a non-atomic attribute is mathematically undefined in pure relational algebra).

## 1.5 Common Violations of 1NF (Board-Favorite Traps)

| S.No | Violation Type | Bad Example Cell | Why It Breaks 1NF |
|:----:|:---------------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Multi-valued attribute** | `Phones = "999, 888, 777"` | More than one atomic value in one cell |
| 2 | **Composite attribute (nested)** | `Address = "Kerala, India"` | Should be split into `State`, `Country` |
| 3 | **Repeating group columns** | `Phone1`, `Phone2`, `Phone3` | Same semantic role split across columns |
| 4 | **Null-as-list abuse** | `Hobbies = NULL` for "multiple" | Hides the multi-valued nature |
| 5 | **Array / JSON blob** | `Tags = ["DBMS", "SQL", "Ktu"]` | Not supported by classical relational model |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Properties of a 1NF Relation

- **Closed Under Relational Operations**: A 1NF relation remains 1NF after applying SELECT, PROJECT, JOIN, UNION, etc. This is **not true** for non-1NF relations.
- **Domain Atomicity**: Every attribute's domain must be a set of atomic values (strings, integers, dates, booleans). A domain cannot be a *power set* of values.
- **Tuple Uniqueness**: Every row is uniquely identifiable by the primary key.
- **No Ordered Storage**: The relational model is set-theoretic; the physical order of rows or columns is *meaningless* to 1NF (it is a logical property, not physical).

## 2.2 The Conversion Algorithm (Conceptual Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-Wise Procedure to Convert a Non-1NF Relation into 1NF:**
> 1. **Identify** the multi-valued attribute (e.g., `Phone`) or the repeating-group columns.
> 2. **Flatten** repeating-group columns into a single attribute (e.g., merge `Phone1`, `Phone2`, `Phone3` into one `Phone` column).
> 3. **Decompose** the multi-valued cell: create one row per atomic value, duplicating the primary key and the other non-repeating attributes.
> 4. **Re-define** the primary key — it is often a **composite key** like `(StudentID, Phone)`.
> 5. **Verify** atomicity of every cell in the new relation.

## 2.3 1NF Decomposition & Lossless Join

The classical decomposition that converts a relation $R$ with multi-valued attribute $A$ into 1NF produces two relations:

$$R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n, A_{\text{mv}}) \xrightarrow{\text{1NF Decomposition}} R_1(\text{PK}, A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) \;\cup\; R_2(\text{PK}, A_{\text{mv}})$$

Where $A_{\text{mv}}$ is the offending multi-valued attribute and $\text{PK}$ is the primary key of $R$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet — Print This Mental Table Before the Exam**

| Property / Symbol | Meaning | KTU Exam Relevance |
|:------------------|:--------|:-------------------|
| $R$ | Relation (Table) | Base operand of normalization |
| $t[A]$ | Value of attribute $A$ in tuple $t$ | Must be **atomic** for 1NF |
| $A_{\text{mv}}$ | Multi-valued attribute | Prime candidate for decomposition |
| $A_{\text{c}}$ | Composite attribute | Must be split into atomic sub-parts |
| $R_1 \bowtie R_2$ | Natural join of decomposed relations | Must be **lossless** after 1NF split |
| $\vert A \vert$ | Cardinality of attribute $A$ (number of distinct values) | Helps count rows post-flattening |
| $\text{PK}$ | Primary Key | Often becomes **composite** post-1NF |
| $n \times m$ | Original rows $\times$ multi-valued count | Used to compute new row count |

### 2.4.1 Key Identity for 1NF Row Expansion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a tuple $t$ has $\vert A_{\text{mv}} \vert = k$ multi-values for attribute $A_{\text{mv}}$, then the tuple $t$ is exploded into exactly $k$ distinct tuples in the 1NF form.

## 2.5 Limitations of 1NF (Sets Up Higher Normal Forms)

> [!WARNING]
> **Crucial Board Insight:** 1NF **does NOT** eliminate:
> - **Partial Dependencies** (handled by 2NF)
> - **Transitive Dependencies** (handled by 3NF)
> - **Non-Prime Functional Dependencies on Superkeys** (handled by BCNF)
>
> After 1NF conversion, a relation may still suffer from severe **update, insertion, and deletion anomalies**. The student is expected to *observe* these anomalies in 1NF and motivate the move to 2NF/3NF — this is a frequent 14-mark KTU question pattern.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1NF compliance is enforced in every **production RDBMS** (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, SQL Server) via:
- `CHECK` constraints that forbid sub-queries inside columns
- `ARRAY` / `JSON` types are explicitly non-1NF and discouraged in classical schemas
- OLTP systems (banking, ticket booking) demand strict 1NF for transaction ACID compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Conversion Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Problem — The "Student-Course-Phone" Relation

We are given an **un-normalized** (non-1NF) relation:

### 3.1.1 ORIGINAL (Un-Normalized) Relation: `STUDENT_RAW`

| StudentID | StudentName | Course         | PhoneNumbers           | City  |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------------:|:----------------------:|:-----:|
| S101      | Anu         | DBMS, OS, CN   | 9991112222, 8883334444 | Kochi |
| S102      | Bala        | DBMS, CN       | 7778889999             | Calicut |
| S103      | Chitra      | OS, CN, DBMS   | 6665554444, 3332221111 | Trivandrum |

**Observations (pre-1NF analysis):**
- `Course` is **multi-valued** for every student.
- `PhoneNumbers` is **multi-valued** for some students.
- The "atomicity rule" of 1NF is **violated**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion to 1NF

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each step below is a separate cognitive action. KTU examiners award marks for explicitly stating the *action* and the *justification*.**

### STEP 1 — Identify the Primary Key of the Raw Relation

The natural identifier of a student is `StudentID` (since the raw data assumes one student per row).

$$\text{PK}_{\text{RAW}} = \text{StudentID}$$

### STEP 2 — Detect Multi-Valued Attributes

Scanning the schema, we find:

$$\{A_{\text{mv}}\} = \{\text{Course},\ \text{PhoneNumbers}\}$$

These two attributes violate the 1NF atomicity constraint.

### STEP 3 — Compute the "Explosion Factor" per Tuple

We need to count distinct values per offending attribute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } t_1 = (\text{S101, Anu}): \quad & \vert \text{Course} \vert = 3,\quad \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 2 \\
\text{For } t_2 = (\text{S102, Bala}): \quad & \vert \text{Course} \vert = 2,\quad \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 1 \\
\text{For } t_3 = (\text{S103, Chitra}): \quad & \vert \text{Course} \vert = 3,\quad \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### STEP 4 — Apply the Cartesian Product of Multi-Valued Attributes

For each original tuple, the number of 1NF tuples produced is the product of the cardinalities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(t_1) &= \vert \text{Course} \vert \times \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 3 \times 2 = 6 \\
N(t_2) &= \vert \text{Course} \vert \times \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 2 \times 1 = 2 \\
N(t_3) &= \vert \text{Course} \vert \times \vert \text{PhoneNumbers} \vert = 3 \times 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Total new rows:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = N(t_1) + N(t_2) + N(t_3) = 6 + 2 + 6 = 14
$$

### STEP 5 — Flatten and Generate the 1NF Relation: `STUDENT_1NF`

The new relation's primary key is now a **composite key** to preserve uniqueness:

$$\text{PK}_{\text{1NF}} = (\text{StudentID},\ \text{Course},\ \text{PhoneNumbers})$$

| StudentID | StudentName | Course | PhoneNumbers  | City  |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:------:|:-------------:|:-----:|
| S101      | Anu         | DBMS   | 9991112222    | Kochi |
| S101      | Anu         | DBMS   | 8883334444    | Kochi |
| S101      | Anu         | OS     | 9991112222    | Kochi |
| S101      | Anu         | OS     | 8883334444    | Kochi |
| S101      | Anu         | CN     | 9991112222    | Kochi |
| S101      | Anu         | CN     | 8883334444    | Kochi |
| S102      | Bala        | DBMS   | 7778889999    | Calicut |
| S102      | Bala        | CN     | 7778889999    | Calicut |
| S103      | Chitra      | OS     | 6665554444    | Trivandrum |
| S103      | Chitra      | OS     | 3332221111    | Trivandrum |
| S103      | Chitra      | CN     | 6665554444    | Trivandrum |
| S103      | Chitra      | CN     | 3332221111    | Trivandrum |
| S103      | Chitra      | DBMS   | 6665554444    | Trivandrum |
| S103      | Chitra      | DBMS   | 3332221111    | Trivandrum |

### STEP 6 — Verify 1NF Compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Checklist (State This in the Exam):**
> - ✅ Every cell contains exactly one atomic value.
> - ✅ No repeating-group columns (e.g., no `Course1`, `Course2`).
> - ✅ Composite primary key `(StudentID, Course, PhoneNumbers)` is defined and unique.
> - ✅ Total rows = **14** matches the predicted formula $N_{\text{total}} = 14$.

## 3.3 Alternative 1NF Decomposition Strategy (Recommended by KTU Valuators)

Instead of flattening with a Cartesian product (which can cause **data redundancy explosion**), the textbook-recommended approach is to **decompose** into two 1NF relations:

### Relation 1: `STUDENT_CORE(StudentID, StudentName, City)`

| StudentID | StudentName | City  |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| S101      | Anu         | Kochi |
| S102      | Bala        | Calicut |
| S103      | Chitra      | Trivandrum |

### Relation 2: `STUDENT_DETAILS(StudentID, Course, PhoneNumbers)`

| StudentID | Course | PhoneNumbers  |
|:---------:|:------:|:-------------:|
| S101      | DBMS   | 9991112222    |
| S101      | OS     | 9991112222    |
| S101      | CN     | 9991112222    |
| S101      | DBMS   | 8883334444    |
| S101      | OS     | 8883334444    |
| S101      | CN     | 8883334444    |
| S102      | DBMS   | 7778889999    |
| S102      | CN     | 7778889999    |
| S103      | OS     | 6665554444    |
| S103      | CN     | 6665554444    |
| S103      | DBMS   | 6665554444    |
| S103      | OS     | 3332221111    |
| S103      | CN     | 3332221111    |
| S103      | DBMS   | 3332221111    |

The lossless-join condition is satisfied because:

$$\text{STUDENT\_CORE} \bowtie \text{STUDENT\_DETAILS} = \text{STUDENT\_1NF (original 14-row form)}$$

This decomposition is **preferred** in KTU 14-mark answers because it more naturally transitions the student toward 2NF and 3NF discussions.

## 3.4 Anomalies Still Present in 1NF (Motivation for Higher NFs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1NF Is Not Enough — A 14-Mark KTU Frequently Asked Sub-Part:**

| Anomaly Type | Example in `STUDENT_1NF` |
|:-------------|:-------------------------|
| **Insertion Anomaly** | Cannot insert a new course offered by the university *unless* a student enrolls in it. |
| **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting the last student enrolled in `CN` accidentally deletes the fact that `CN` exists as a course. |
| **Update Anomaly** | If `Anu` changes her phone, we must update **multiple rows** — risk of inconsistent state. |
| **Redundancy** | `StudentName = Anu`, `City = Kochi` is repeated in 6 rows. |

These are the *drivers* for moving to 2NF (eliminate partial dependencies on the composite key) and 3NF (eliminate transitive dependencies).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules for the Examiner

| Observation in Schema | KTU Board Action | Points Awarded |
|:----------------------|:-----------------|:--------------:|
| `Course` column has comma-separated values | State "multi-valued attribute" + flatten | 2 |
| `Phone1, Phone2` repeating columns | State "repeating group" + merge | 2 |
| No primary key in the raw table | Define composite key explicitly | 1 |
| Cell holds nested values like `Address(City, State)` | State "composite attribute" + decompose | 2 |
| Cross-product explosion not computed | Compute $N_{\text{total}}$ | 2 |
| Anomalies not listed in 1NF | List all 3 anomalies with examples | 3 |
| Mention "lossless join" | State decomposition preserves information | 2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — 1NF Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Unnormalized Relation R] --> B{Is every cell atomic?}
    B -- No --> C[Identify Multi-Valued Attributes]
    C --> D[Identify Repeating Group Columns]
    D --> E[Identify Composite Attributes]
    E --> F[Apply Flattening Step]
    F --> G[Define Composite Primary Key]
    G --> H{Is New Relation 1NF?}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[1NF Compliant Relation]
    I --> J[Check for Partial Dependencies]
    J --> K[Proceed to 2NF]
    B -- Yes --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decomposition Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW[Unnormalized STUDENT_RAW]
        R1[StudentID]
        R2[StudentName]
        R3[Course MultiValued]
        R4[Phone MultiValued]
        R5[City]
    end

    subgraph CORE[1NF Relation 1 - STUDENT_CORE]
        C1[StudentID]
        C2[StudentName]
        C3[City]
    end

    subgraph DET[1NF Relation 2 - STUDENT_DETAILS]
        D1[StudentID]
        D2[Course]
        D3[Phone]
    end

    RAW -- Decompose via 1NF --> CORE
    RAW -- Decompose via 1NF --> DET
    CORE -- Lossless Natural Join --> RESULT[STUDENT_1NF Restored]
    DET -- Lossless Natural Join --> RESULT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Anomalies Persisting in 1NF

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StudentEnrolled
    StudentEnrolled --> CourseExists: Enrollment tuple inserted
    StudentEnrolled --> PhoneUpdated: Multi-row update needed
    StudentEnrolled --> LastStudentLeaves: Deletion triggered
    LastStudentLeaves --> CourseLost: Deletion anomaly
    PhoneUpdated --> InconsistentData: Update anomaly
    CourseExists --> CannotAddStandalone: Insertion anomaly
    CannotAddStandalone --> [*]
    CourseLost --> [*]
    InconsistentData --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Class/Schema Diagram (1NF Compliant Schemas)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class STUDENT_CORE {
        +String StudentID
        +String StudentName
        +String City
    }
    class STUDENT_DETAILS {
        +String StudentID
        +String Course
        +String Phone
    }
    STUDENT_CORE "1" --o "*" STUDENT_DETAILS : has
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Q: Define First Normal Form (1NF). List the conditions a relation must satisfy to be in 1NF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A relation $R$ is in **First Normal Form (1NF)** if and only if the domain of every attribute contains only **atomic (indivisible) values** and every attribute holds only a **single value** for each tuple. The three conditions are:
> 1. **Atomicity** — every cell must contain a single, indivisible value.
> 2. **No repeating groups** — columns like `Phone1`, `Phone2` are not allowed; multi-valued data must be represented as separate rows.
> 3. **Primary key existence** — there must be a key that uniquely identifies each row.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the three conditions with one-line explanation each.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Q: "1NF does not eliminate redundancy." Justify this statement with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1NF only enforces **atomicity** of cell values. It does **not** address the problem of **data redundancy** caused by *partial* or *transitive* functional dependencies on the primary key.
>
> **Example:** Consider `STUDENT_1NF(StudentID, Course, StudentName, City)`. For a student enrolled in 3 courses, the values `StudentName` and `City` are repeated in 3 different rows. This redundancy is *not* removed by 1NF — it requires **2NF** (which moves `StudentName, City` to a separate relation) or **3NF**.

*(Award 1 mark for stating 1NF's scope, 2 marks for the redundancy example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** if they write the 1NF definition *without* using the word "atomic".
> - Students **lose 2 marks** if they fail to compute the **number of rows** after flattening (the cross-product step).
> - Students **lose 3 marks** if they do not list at least **two anomalies** that persist in 1NF.
> - **Common mistake:** writing `(StudentID, Course)` as the new primary key when `Phone` is also multi-valued — this is incorrect; the key must include *all* multi-valued attributes.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]

Consider the following relation storing project allocation data for a consulting firm:

`PROJECT_RAW(EmpID, EmpName, ProjectID, ProjectName, HoursWorked)`

The data is shown below:

| EmpID | EmpName | ProjectID       | ProjectName         | HoursWorked   |
|:-----:|:-------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------:|
| E01   | Rahul   | P1, P2          | Alpha, Beta         | 10, 15        |
| E02   | Sita    | P2, P3          | Beta, Gamma         | 12, 8         |
| E03   | Kiran   | P1, P3          | Alpha, Gamma        | 9, 11         |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Identify why the above relation is **not in 1NF**. Convert it to 1NF by showing the complete step-by-step flattening procedure. Define the new primary key.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Now decompose the 1NF relation into two lossless 1NF relations to reduce redundancy. State the lossless-join condition and list at least **two update anomalies** that still exist in 1NF.

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify 1NF violations (2 marks):**
- The attributes `ProjectID` and `ProjectName` are **multi-valued** (e.g., `P1, P2`).
- The attribute `HoursWorked` is **multi-valued** and *correlated* with `ProjectID` (e.g., `10` hours for `P1`, `15` hours for `P2`).
- These violate the **atomicity rule** of 1NF.

**Step 2 — Compute the "explosion factor" per tuple (2 marks):**
For each employee tuple $t_i$, compute the cardinality of multi-valued attributes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(t_1) &= \vert \text{ProjectID} \vert \times \vert \text{HoursWorked} \vert = 2 \times 2 = 4 \\
N(t_2) &= \vert \text{ProjectID} \vert \times \vert \text{HoursWorked} \vert = 2 \times 2 = 4 \\
N(t_3) &= \vert \text{ProjectID} \vert \times \vert \text{HoursWorked} \vert = 2 \times 2 = 4 \\
N_{\text{total}} &= 4 + 4 + 4 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Generate the 1NF relation (2 marks):**

The new primary key must uniquely identify each row:

$$\text{PK}_{\text{1NF}} = (\text{EmpID},\ \text{ProjectID})$$

| EmpID | EmpName | ProjectID | ProjectName | HoursWorked |
|:-----:|:-------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| E01   | Rahul   | P1        | Alpha       | 10          |
| E01   | Rahul   | P2        | Beta        | 15          |
| E02   | Sita    | P2        | Beta        | 12          |
| E02   | Sita    | P3        | Gamma       | 8           |
| E03   | Kiran   | P1        | Alpha       | 9           |
| E03   | Kiran   | P3        | Gamma       | 11          |

**Step 4 — Verify 1NF (1 mark):** Total rows = 6, all cells atomic, composite key defined. ✅

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Decompose into two 1NF relations (3 marks):**

`EMPLOYEE_CORE(EmpID, EmpName)`:

| EmpID | EmpName |
|:-----:|:-------:|
| E01   | Rahul   |
| E02   | Sita    |
| E03   | Kiran   |

`PROJECT_ALLOCATION(EmpID, ProjectID, ProjectName, HoursWorked)`:

| EmpID | ProjectID | ProjectName | HoursWorked |
|:-----:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| E01   | P1        | Alpha       | 10          |
| E01   | P2        | Beta        | 15          |
| E02   | P2        | Beta        | 12          |
| E02   | P3        | Gamma       | 8           |
| E03   | P1        | Alpha       | 9           |
| E03   | P3        | Gamma       | 11          |

**Step 2 — State the lossless-join condition (2 marks):**
The decomposition is lossless because the common attribute `EmpID` is the **primary key** of `EMPLOYEE_CORE`. Formally:

$$(\text{EMPLOYEE\_CORE} \cap \text{PROJECT\_ALLOCATION}) = \{\text{EmpID}\} \rightarrow \text{EMPLOYEE\_CORE}$$

This satisfies the lossless-join test using the **Heath's theorem** formulation.

**Step 3 — List anomalies still present in 1NF (2 marks):**
- **Update Anomaly:** If `ProjectName` for `P1` changes from `Alpha` to `Alpha-V2`, all 2 rows in `PROJECT_ALLOCATION` must be updated — risk of inconsistency.
- **Insertion Anomaly:** A new project `P4` (e.g., `Delta`) cannot be added to the database *unless* an employee is assigned to it.
- **Deletion Anomaly:** If `E02` leaves and all her project rows are deleted, the information about project `P3` (`Gamma`) is partially lost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3]

A hospital maintains the following unnormalized relation to record patient consultations:

`CONSULT_RAW(PatientID, PatientName, DoctorID, DoctorName, ConsultDate, Diagnosis)`

| PatientID | PatientName | DoctorID       | DoctorName        | ConsultDate | Diagnosis       |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:---------------:|
| P001      | Anu         | D10, D11       | Dr. Roy, Dr. Iyer | 12-Jan, 15-Jan | Fever, Cold  |
| P002      | Bala        | D11            | Dr. Iyer          | 20-Jan      | Malaria         |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Identify the 1NF violations. Convert the relation into 1NF by showing each step. State the new primary key and the final number of tuples.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain why 1NF alone is **insufficient** for a good database design. Give **two** specific examples of anomalies from the 1NF relation you just derived and mention the higher normal form that resolves each.

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify 1NF violations (2 marks):**
- `DoctorID`, `DoctorName`, `ConsultDate`, and `Diagnosis` are all **multi-valued** for tuple `P001`.
- The values are **inter-correlated** (D10 ↔ Dr. Roy ↔ 12-Jan ↔ Fever; D11 ↔ Dr. Iyer ↔ 15-Jan ↔ Cold).
- This violates the atomicity rule and creates a **repeating group**.

**Step 2 — Compute row expansion (2 marks):**
For `P001`, the cardinality of each multi-valued attribute is 2 (D10, D11).
The Cartesian product gives:

$$N(\text{P001}) = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 16 \text{ rows}$$

For `P002`, only 1 doctor: $N(\text{P002}) = 1$. Total: $N_{\text{total}} = 17$.

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ The cross-product can cause **combinatorial explosion** — this is why the decomposition approach (next section) is preferred.

**Step 3 — Generate 1NF relation (3 marks):**

New primary key: $\text{PK}_{\text{1NF}} = (\text{PatientID}, \text{DoctorID}, \text{ConsultDate})$

| PatientID | PatientName | DoctorID | DoctorName | ConsultDate | Diagnosis |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:----------:|:-----------:|:---------:|
| P001      | Anu         | D10      | Dr. Roy    | 12-Jan      | Fever     |
| P001      | Anu         | D10      | Dr. Roy    | 12-Jan      | Cold      |
| P001      | Anu         | D10      | Dr. Roy    | 15-Jan      | Fever     |
| P001      | Anu         | D10      | Dr. Roy    | 15-Jan      | Cold      |
| P001      | Anu         | D11      | Dr. Iyer   | 12-Jan      | Fever     |
| P001      | Anu         | D11      | Dr. Iyer   | 12-Jan      | Cold      |
| P001      | Anu         | D11      | Dr. Iyer   | 15-Jan      | Fever     |
| P001      | Anu         | D11      | Dr. Iyer   | 15-Jan      | Cold      |
| ...       | ...         | ...      | ...        | ...         | ...       |
| P002      | Bala        | D11      | Dr. Iyer   | 20-Jan      | Malaria   |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Why 1NF is Insufficient (3 marks):**
1NF only enforces **atomicity** of cell values. It does not address:
- **Partial functional dependencies** (a non-prime attribute depending on *part* of a composite primary key).
- **Transitive functional dependencies** (a non-prime attribute depending on *another non-prime attribute*).

**Anomaly 1 — Update Anomaly (2 marks):**
If `Dr. Iyer` changes her name to `Dr. Iyer-MD`, then **all rows** where `DoctorID = D11` must be updated. Forgetting to update one row creates inconsistency. This is a **partial dependency** (DoctorName depends only on DoctorID, not on the full primary key). It is resolved by **2NF**.

**Anomaly 2 — Insertion Anomaly (2 marks):**
A new doctor `D12 — Dr. Sen` cannot be added to the database *until* a patient consults them. The doctor's existence is "trapped" inside a patient-centric table. This is resolved by **3NF** (or BCNF), which separates the `DOCTOR` entity into its own relation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — 1NF in 60 Seconds**
> - **Definition:** A relation is in 1NF if every cell value is **atomic** and there is a **primary key**.
> - **Key Mathematical Identity:** $R$ is in 1NF $\iff \forall t \in R, \forall A: t[A]$ is atomic.
> - **Three Golden Rules:** (1) Atomicity, (2) No repeating groups, (3) Primary key existence.
> - **Conversion Recipe:** Identify multi-valued attribute → flatten into separate rows → define composite key.
> - **Row Count Formula:** $N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \prod_{j=1}^{k} \vert A_{j}^{\text{mv}} \vert$ where $m$ = original tuples, $k$ = number of multi-valued attributes in tuple $i$.
> - **Decomposition Pattern:** Split into `ENTITY_CORE(PK, static_attrs)` + `ENTITY_DETAILS(PK, multivalued_attrs)`.
> - **Lossless Join:** The common attribute between decomposed relations must be a **key** in at least one of them (Heath's theorem).
> - **1NF Does NOT Solve:** Partial dependency (→ 2NF), Transitive dependency (→ 3NF), Non-prime FDs on superkeys (→ BCNF).
> - **Persistent Anomalies in 1NF:** Insertion, Deletion, Update — all three must be quotable for full marks.
> - **Common Board Traps:** Repeating-group columns (`Phone1`, `Phone2`); JSON/array columns; nested composite attributes; forgetting to include *all* multi-valued attributes in the composite key.
> - **Engineering Relevance:** OLTP systems, banking, ticket booking, hospital management — all enforce strict 1NF for ACID compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Normal Form (2NF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Second Normal Form (2NF) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Second Normal Form (2NF) — Canonical Definition** > A relational schema $R$ is said to be in **Second Normal Form (2NF)** if and only if: > 1. $R$ is already in **First Normal Form (1NF)** (i.e., all attributes hold atomic/indivisible values), **AND** > 2. Every **non-prime attribute** of $R$ is **fully functionally dependent** on every **candidate key** of $R$ (i.e., no **partial dependency** exists on a candidate key). In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme Outcomes (mapped to **CO3 — Apply normalization techniques**), 2NF is the formal vehicle for **eliminating partial functional dependencies** that arise whenever a relation has a **composite candidate key**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card System Imagine a **University Library** where every **Borrowing Receipt** records three things together on a single slip of paper: 1. **Student's Roll Number** + **Book's Accession Number** → uniquely identifies the borrowing event (this is the **composite primary key**). 2. The slip also prints the **Student's Name** (depends only on Roll Number, not on the Book). 3. The slip also prints the **Book's Title** (depends only on the Accession Number, not on the Student). Now think: if the **same student** borrows **5 different books**, the receipt will print the student's name **5 times** on **5 different slips** — even though the student's name never changes. Worse, if you want to update the student's name, you must hunt down **all 5 slips** and update each one. If you miss even one, the database becomes **inconsistent** (the same student has two different names in the system!). This duplication and update hazard is exactly what **2NF is designed to eliminate**. The student's name depends on *part* of the key (Roll Number) — not the *whole* key. 2NF forces us to **split** the slip into three smaller slips: - A **Student Slip** (Roll Number → Name) - A **Book Slip** (Accession Number → Title) - A **Borrowing Slip** (Roll Number + Accession Number → Date of Issue, Due Date) > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** 2NF is essentially a *discipline against data duplication* caused by partial dependencies on composite keys. If your primary key is a single attribute (e.g., `EmpID`), **the relation is automatically in 2NF**, because there is no "part of a key" to depend on. ### 1.3 Foundational Terminology (Prerequisites for 2NF) Before applying 2NF, the student must internalise the following four terms which appear repeatedly in KTU question papers: | Term | Formal Definition | Symbol | |------|-------------------|--------| | **Prime Attribute** | An attribute that is a member of *some* candidate key. | $A \in$ (any candidate key) | | **Non-Prime Attribute** | An attribute that is *not* a member of any candidate key. | $A \notin$ (any candidate key) | | **Partial Dependency** | A FD $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X$ is a *proper subset* of a candidate key and $Y$ is a non-prime attribute. | $X \subset K, X \rightarrow Y$ | | **Full Functional Dependency** | A FD $X \rightarrow Y$ where removing any attribute from $X$ destroys the dependency. | $X \xrightarrow{f} Y$ | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why 2NF Exists The conceptual motivation for 2NF can be decomposed into a **three-step logical chain**: **Step 1 — Identify the Candidate Key.** Determine the minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies every tuple in the relation. If the candidate key is a **single attribute**, the relation *trivially* satisfies 2NF (because no attribute can be a "part" of a 1-attribute key). **Step 2 — Detect Partial Dependencies.** For every non-prime attribute $A$, ask: *"Is $A$ functionally determined by a **proper subset** of the candidate key?"* - If **Yes** → the relation **violates 2NF**. - If **No** → the relation is 2NF-compliant. **Step 3 — Decompose to Eliminate the Violation.** For each partial dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ (where $X \subset K$): - Create a **new relation** $R_1(X \cup Y)$. - Retain $X$ in the original relation as a **foreign key** to preserve the linkage. - Continue recursively until no partial dependency remains. ### 2.2 KTU Formula / Property Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates the **theorems, decision rules, and boundary conditions** most frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE / MSE papers for 2NF. | # | Rule / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **2NF Pre-condition** | $R \in 1NF$ | Atomicity of attribute values is mandatory before 2NF applies. | | 2 | **2NF Core Condition** | $\forall \text{ FD } X \rightarrow A : \text{ either } (X \text{ is a superkey}) \lor (A \text{ is prime})$ | Either the LHS must be a superkey, or the RHS must be a prime attribute. | | 3 | **Single-Attribute Key Sufficiency** | If $\mid K \mid = 1$, then $R \in 2NF$ automatically. | No partial dependency is possible on a singleton key. | | 4 | **Partial Dependency Detection** | $\exists X \subset K, Y \notin K : X \rightarrow Y$ | $X$ is a *strict* subset of candidate key $K$. | | 5 | **Decomposition Rule** | $R(X \cup Y \cup Z) \Rightarrow R_1(XY) \cup R_2(XZ)$ where $X \subset K, X \rightarrow Y$ | $X$ remains in both relations to preserve the join. | | 6 | **Lossless Join Guarantee** | $R_1 \bowtie R_2 = R$ | 2NF decomposition is always lossless when the common attribute is a key for at least one of the resulting relations. | | 7 | **Dependency Preservation** | $(F_1 \cup F_2)^{+} \supseteq F^{+}$ | Not always guaranteed in BCNF, but **always guaranteed** in 2NF decomposition. | | 8 | **Normal Form Hierarchy** | $BCNF \subset 3NF \subset 2NF \subset 1NF$ | Every BCNF relation is also in 2NF, but not vice versa. | ### 2.3 Prime vs Non-Prime Attribute — Decision Algorithm To determine whether an attribute $A$ is prime: 1. Compute the **closure** $A^{+}$ — does it contain *all* attributes of $R$? If yes, $\{A\}$ alone is a candidate key, so $A$ is prime. 2. Identify **all candidate keys** $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_m$ of $R$. 3. $A$ is **prime** $\iff$ $A \in K_1 \lor A \in K_2 \lor \ldots \lor A \in K_m$. 4. Otherwise, $A$ is **non-prime**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The discipline enforced by 2NF is not academic — it directly addresses three production-grade engineering concerns: - **Update Anomalies:** Without 2NF, changing a single fact (e.g., a student's address) requires multiple row updates, opening the door to inconsistency. - **Insertion Anomalies:** Certain facts cannot be recorded at all until a related event occurs (e.g., you cannot record a new course in `STUDENT_COURSE` until a student enrolls in it). - **Deletion Anomalies:** Deleting the last enrollment row of a student permanently erases the student's information from the database. > [!IMPORTANT] > In **industry-scale RDBMS** (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB), 2NF principles guide the design of **bridge tables** (junction tables) for many-to-many relationships and ensure that **lookup / reference tables** are decoupled from transactional tables. This translates directly to **reduced storage**, **faster write throughput**, and **stronger referential integrity** — all critical in OLTP systems. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Violating Relation > [!NOTE] > This is the **canonical KTU-style example** that has appeared in multiple previous-year university examinations. Mastering this guarantees full marks on 2NF conversion problems. **Given Relation Schema:** $$\text{STUDENT\_COURSE}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{StudentName}, \text{CourseName}, \text{Instructor}, \text{Grade})$$ **Candidate Key (CK):** $K = \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\}$ (composite key, hence partial dependencies are *possible*). **Set of Functional Dependencies (F):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD}_1 &: \text{StudentID} \rightarrow \text{StudentName} \\ \text{FD}_2 &: \text{CourseID} \rightarrow \text{CourseName},\ \text{Instructor} \\ \text{FD}_3 &: \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\} \rightarrow \text{Grade} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis — Identifying the Violation **Step A — Classify Each Attribute as Prime or Non-Prime.** The only candidate key is $K = \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\}$. Therefore: - **Prime attributes:** $\text{StudentID},\ \text{CourseID}$ - **Non-prime attributes:** $\text{StudentName},\ \text{CourseName},\ \text{Instructor},\ \text{Grade}$ **Step B — Test Each FD for Partial Dependency.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD}_1: \text{StudentID} &\rightarrow \text{StudentName} \\ \text{LHS } &= \{\text{StudentID}\} \subset K \quad \text{(proper subset of candidate key)} \\ \text{RHS } &= \text{StudentName} \quad \text{(non-prime)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{PARTIAL DEPENDENCY DETECTED} &\Rightarrow \text{Violation of 2NF} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD}_2: \text{CourseID} &\rightarrow \text{CourseName},\ \text{Instructor} \\ \text{LHS } &= \{\text{CourseID}\} \subset K \quad \text{(proper subset of candidate key)} \\ \text{RHS } &= \{\text{CourseName}, \text{Instructor}\} \quad \text{(both non-prime)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{PARTIAL DEPENDENCY DETECTED} &\Rightarrow \text{Violation of 2NF} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD}_3: \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\} &\rightarrow \text{Grade} \\ \text{LHS } &= K \quad \text{(the full candidate key)} \\ \text{RHS } &= \text{Grade} \quad \text{(non-prime)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{FULL FUNCTIONAL DEPENDENCY} &\Rightarrow \text{Satisfies 2NF} \end{aligned} $$ **Step C — Verdict.** > [!WARNING] > The relation `STUDENT_COURSE` is in **1NF but NOT in 2NF** because of the two partial dependencies $\text{FD}_1$ and $\text{FD}_2$. This is the exact form of a "Show that R is not in 2NF" question in KTU exams. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Decomposition — Normalising into 2NF **Decomposition Rule (Recall from Section 2.2):** For each partial dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X \subset K$: - Create a new relation $R_i(X \cup Y)$ where $X$ is the primary key of $R_i$. - Remove $Y$ from the original relation but **retain** $X$ as a foreign key. **Applying the Rule to FD₁:** $\{\text{StudentID}\} \rightarrow \{\text{StudentName}\}$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &: \text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \text{StudentName}) \\ \text{Key} &: \text{StudentID (single attribute)} \\ \text{Status} &: \text{Automatically in 2NF} \end{aligned} $$ **Applying the Rule to FD₂:** $\{\text{CourseID}\} \rightarrow \{\text{CourseName}, \text{Instructor}\}$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_2 &: \text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{CourseName}, \text{Instructor}) \\ \text{Key} &: \text{CourseID (single attribute)} \\ \text{Status} &: \text{Automatically in 2NF} \end{aligned} $$ **Retaining the Full Dependency FD₃:** $\{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\} \rightarrow \{\text{Grade}\}$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_3 &: \text{ENROLLMENT}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Grade}) \\ \text{Key} &: \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\} \text{ (composite key)} \\ \text{FDs} &: \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\} \rightarrow \text{Grade} \text{ (full dependency)} \\ \text{Status} &: \text{In 2NF (no partial dependencies remain)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Final Normalised Schema and Verification **Final 2NF Schema:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{STUDENT}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \text{StudentName}) \\ \text{COURSE}(\underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{CourseName}, \text{Instructor}) \\ \text{ENROLLMENT}(\underline{\text{StudentID}}, \underline{\text{CourseID}}, \text{Grade}) \\ \text{Foreign Keys:} \quad &\text{ENROLLMENT.StudentID} \rightarrow \text{STUDENT.StudentID} \\ &\text{ENROLLMENT.CourseID} \rightarrow \text{COURSE.CourseID} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification Table (Before vs. After 2NF):** | Property | Before 2NF (STUDENT_COURSE) | After 2NF (Three Relations) | |---|---|---| | **Update Anomaly** | Changing StudentName requires N updates | Change once in STUDENT table | | **Insertion Anomaly** | Cannot add a course until a student enrolls | Add course directly in COURSE table | | **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting last enrollment loses student info | Student info preserved in STUDENT table | | **Storage Redundancy** | StudentName and CourseName repeated for every enrollment | No redundancy — atomic storage | | **Lossless Join** | N/A | $R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie R_3 = R$ ✓ | | **Dependency Preserved** | Yes | Yes ✓ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The original relation had **6 columns × $m$ rows** of repeated data (for $m$ enrollments). The 2NF-normalised version typically reduces the data footprint to **$n + p + m$** rows, where $n$ = number of distinct students, $p$ = number of distinct courses, and $m$ = number of enrollments — a **massive saving** when $n, p \ll m$. ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When is 2NF Trivial vs. Non-Trivial? $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Case 1: } \mid K \mid = 1 \Rightarrow R \in 2NF \text{ automatically} \\ \text{Case 2: } \mid K \mid > 1 \text{ and } \forall A \in R : A \text{ prime} \Rightarrow R \in 2NF \text{ (all-key relation)} \\ \text{Case 3: } \mid K \mid > 1 \text{ and } \exists \text{ non-prime } A \text{ with proper-subset dependency} \Rightarrow R \notin 2NF \end{aligned} $$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — 2NF Normalisation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Input Relation R with FDs]) --> stepB{Is R in 1NF?} stepB -- No --> stepC[Atomicise attributes
Remove multi-valued
Eliminate repeating groups] stepC --> stepB stepB -- Yes --> stepD[Identify all
Candidate Keys K1, K2, ...] stepD --> stepE{Any Candidate Key
Composite?} stepE -- No --> stepF[Single attribute key
R is AUTOMATICALLY in 2NF] stepE -- Yes --> stepG[Classify attributes
Prime vs Non-Prime] stepG --> stepH{Search for partial
dependencies
X sub K, X to Y?} stepH -- No --> stepI[R is in 2NF
STOP] stepH -- Yes --> stepJ[Decompose:
Create R1 X union Y
Retain X as FK in R] stepJ --> stepK{Any partial deps
remaining in R?} stepK -- Yes --> stepH stepK -- No --> stepL[Final 2NF Schema
All relations verified] stepF --> endM([End]) stepI --> endM stepL --> endM ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid ER Diagram — Before vs. After 2NF (Relational Structure) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE 2NF - Single Flat Relation"] sc1[STUDENT_COURSE] sc1 --- attr1a(StudentID PK) sc1 --- attr1b(CourseID PK) sc1 --- attr1c(StudentName) sc1 --- attr1d(CourseName) sc1 --- attr1e(Instructor) sc1 --- attr1f(Grade) end subgraph AFTER["AFTER 2NF - Three Normalised Relations"] s1[STUDENT] s1 --- sa1(StudentID PK) s1 --- sa2(StudentName) c1[COURSE] c1 --- ca1(CourseID PK) c1 --- ca2(CourseName) c1 --- ca3(Instructor) e1[ENROLLMENT] e1 --- ea1(StudentID FK) e1 --- ea2(CourseID FK) e1 --- ea3(Grade) end s1 -. references .-> e1 c1 -. references .-> e1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Dependency Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD k1[StudentID Part of Key] --> fd1[FD1 StudentID to StudentName] k2[CourseID Part of Key] --> fd2[FD2 CourseID to CourseName, Instructor] k3[StudentID plus CourseID Full Key] --> fd3[FD3 StudentID CourseID to Grade] fd1 --> pd1[PARTIAL DEPENDENCY] fd2 --> pd2[PARTIAL DEPENDENCY] fd3 --> fd3a[FULL FUNCTIONAL DEPENDENCY] pd1 --> decomp1[Move to STUDENT Relation] pd2 --> decomp2[Move to COURSE Relation] fd3a --> decomp3[Retain in ENROLLMENT Relation] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Second Normal Form (2NF). Why is a relation with a single-attribute primary key automatically in 2NF?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **2NF Definition:** A relation $R$ is in Second Normal Form (2NF) if it is in 1NF and every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key (i.e., no non-prime attribute depends on only part of a composite key). > > **Why single-attribute key is automatically 2NF:** A partial dependency requires a *proper subset* $X$ of a candidate key $K$ such that $X \rightarrow Y$ (where $Y$ is non-prime). If $K$ has only one attribute, no proper subset of $K$ exists. Therefore, partial dependencies are **impossible** by definition, and the relation trivially satisfies 2NF. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between partial dependency and full functional dependency with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Partial Dependency | Full Functional Dependency | |---|---|---| | **LHS Size** | Proper subset of CK | Entire candidate key (or superkey) | | **2NF Status** | Violates 2NF | Preserves 2NF | | **Example** | In `STUDENT_COURSE(StudentID, CourseID, StudentName, Grade)`, `StudentID → StudentName` is a partial dependency. | `StudentID, CourseID → Grade` is a full functional dependency. | | **Remedy** | Decompose into 2NF | No action needed | **(3 marks — 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for example)** --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Consider the relation $R(\underline{A}, \underline{B}, C, D, E)$ with the following functional dependencies: $$F = \{A \rightarrow C,\ AB \rightarrow D,\ B \rightarrow E\}$$ Determine the highest normal form that $R$ satisfies. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Decompose $R$ into Second Normal Form (2NF). Show all functional dependencies for each resulting relation and verify that each is in 2NF. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Identifying the Highest Normal Form** **Step 1: Find the Candidate Key.** Compute the closure of $AB$: $$ \begin{aligned} (AB)^{+} &= \{A, B\} \\ &\rightarrow A \rightarrow C, \text{ so } (AB)^{+} = \{A, B, C\} \\ &\rightarrow B \rightarrow E, \text{ so } (AB)^{+} = \{A, B, C, E\} \\ &\rightarrow AB \rightarrow D, \text{ so } (AB)^{+} = \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the candidate key is $K = \{A, B\}$. **Step 2: Classify Attributes.** - **Prime:** $A, B$ - **Non-prime:** $C, D, E$ **Step 3: Check for Partial Dependencies.** $$ \begin{aligned} A \rightarrow C &: A \subset K, C \text{ is non-prime} \Rightarrow \textbf{Partial Dependency} \\ B \rightarrow E &: B \subset K, E \text{ is non-prime} \Rightarrow \textbf{Partial Dependency} \\ AB \rightarrow D &: AB = K, D \text{ is non-prime} \Rightarrow \textbf{Full Dependency (OK)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Conclusion.** > [!IMPORTANT] > $R$ is in **1NF only** (it has partial dependencies, hence not in 2NF). The highest normal form satisfied is **1NF**. **(7 marks — 2 for key, 2 for classification, 2 for FD analysis, 1 for conclusion)** --- **Part (b) — Decomposition into 2NF** **Apply the decomposition rule for each partial dependency:** **For $A \rightarrow C$:** Create $R_1(A, C)$ with key $A$. **For $B \rightarrow E$:** Create $R_2(B, E)$ with key $B$. **Retain the full dependency $AB \rightarrow D$** by keeping $A$ and $B$ in a new relation with $D$: **Final 2NF Schema:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_1(A, C) \quad &\text{Key: } A, \quad \text{FD: } A \rightarrow C \\ R_2(B, E) \quad &\text{Key: } B, \quad \text{FD: } B \rightarrow E \\ R_3(A, B, D) \quad &\text{Key: } \{A, B\}, \quad \text{FD: } AB \rightarrow D \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** - $R_1$ has single-attribute key → automatically in 2NF ✓ - $R_2$ has single-attribute key → automatically in 2NF ✓ - $R_3$: $AB \rightarrow D$ is a full dependency, no partial dependency remains → in 2NF ✓ **Lossless Join Check:** $R_1 \cap R_2 = \emptyset$; but $R_1 \cap R_3 = \{A\}$ and $A$ is a key of $R_1$ → **Lossless**. Similarly for $R_2 \cap R_3 = \{B\}$ → **Lossless**. **(7 marks — 2 for rule application, 2 for schemas, 1 for FD listing, 2 for verification)** --- **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** What is a partial dependency? Explain with reference to the concept of candidate keys. Why does the existence of partial dependencies indicate poor database design? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A university maintains the following relation: `PROJECT_ALLOCATION(EmpID, ProjectID, EmpName, ProjectName, Department, AllocationDate)` Assume the following FDs: $$\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpName},\ \text{Department}; \quad \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}; \quad \{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{AllocationDate}$$ Normalise the relation into 2NF. Justify each step and state the final schema. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Partial Dependency Explained (7 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** A *partial dependency* exists in a relation $R$ when a non-prime attribute $A$ is functionally dependent on a *proper subset* $X$ of a candidate key $K$. Formally: $\exists X \subset K : X \rightarrow A$ where $A$ is non-prime. > > **Relation to Candidate Keys (2 marks):** A candidate key is the *minimal* set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple. The very notion of a "partial" dependency presupposes that the key is *composite* (i.e., has 2 or more attributes). If the key is a single attribute, no proper subset exists and partial dependencies are impossible. > > **Why It Indicates Poor Design (3 marks):** Partial dependencies cause: > 1. *Update Anomalies* — repeating the same value across many rows. > 2. *Insertion Anomalies* — inability to record certain facts independently. > 3. *Deletion Anomalies* — loss of information when a single row is removed. > 4. *Storage Waste* — unnecessary duplication of data. > 5. *Inconsistency Risk* — multiple conflicting copies of the same fact. --- **Part (b) — Normalising PROJECT_ALLOCATION into 2NF (7 marks)** **Step 1: Identify the Candidate Key.** The primary key is $K = \{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\}$ (composite). **Step 2: Identify FDs and Check for Partial Dependencies.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FD}_1 &: \text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpName}, \text{Department} \quad \text{(partial dep.)} \\ \text{FD}_2 &: \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName} \quad \text{(partial dep.)} \\ \text{FD}_3 &: \{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{AllocationDate} \quad \text{(full dep.)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Decompose.** Applying the 2NF decomposition rule for each partial dependency: $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &: \text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{Department}) \\ &\text{Key: EmpID (single-attribute)} \Rightarrow \text{Automatically 2NF} \\ R_2 &: \text{PROJECT}(\underline{\text{ProjectID}}, \text{ProjectName}) \\ &\text{Key: ProjectID (single-attribute)} \Rightarrow \text{Automatically 2NF} \\ R_3 &: \text{ALLOCATION}(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \underline{\text{ProjectID}}, \text{AllocationDate}) \\ &\text{Key: \{EmpID, ProjectID\}} \Rightarrow \text{AllocationDate fully depends on the key} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Foreign Key Constraints.** - $\text{ALLOCATION.EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EMPLOYEE.EmpID}$ - $\text{ALLOCATION.ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{PROJECT.ProjectID}$ **Step 5: Verification.** All three relations are in 2NF. Lossless join holds because the common attributes ($\text{EmpID}$ and $\text{ProjectID}$) are keys in their respective parent relations. **(7 marks — 1.5 for key, 1.5 for FD analysis, 2 for decomposition, 1 for FKs, 1 for verification)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 2NF Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify 1NF first** — Always confirm atomicity before claiming a relation is in 2NF. *(−1 mark)* > 2. **Misclassifying prime vs. non-prime attributes** — An attribute is prime if it belongs to **any** candidate key, not just the primary key. A relation may have multiple candidate keys! *(−2 marks)* > 3. **Failing to check *all* candidate keys for partial dependencies** — If there are 2 candidate keys, you must test both. *(−2 marks)* > 4. **Retaining the dependent attribute in the original relation after decomposition** — When you move a non-prime attribute $Y$ to a new relation with its determinant $X$, you must **remove** $Y$ from the original relation. *(−1 mark)* > 5. **Not stating the foreign key relationships** — Always mention FK constraints to demonstrate the linkage between decomposed relations. *(−1 mark)* > 6. **Skipping the lossless-join verification** — Examiners expect a one-line justification of losslessness. *(−1 mark)* > 7. **Confusing 2NF with 3NF** — 2NF removes *partial* dependencies; 3NF additionally removes *transitive* dependencies. Do not mix them up. *(−3 marks if misidentified)* --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **2NF = 1NF + No Partial Dependencies** on any candidate key. - ✅ A relation with a **single-attribute primary key is automatically in 2NF**. - ✅ **Partial dependency** = $X \subset K$ and $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y$ is non-prime. - ✅ **Prime attribute** = member of *any* candidate key (not just the chosen primary key). - ✅ **Decomposition rule:** For each $X \rightarrow Y$ (partial), create $R_i(X, Y)$ with $X$ as the key. - ✅ **2NF decomposition is always lossless** and **dependency-preserving**. - ✅ The normal form hierarchy is strictly: $BCNF \subset 3NF \subset 2NF \subset 1NF$. - ✅ **Lossless join** is guaranteed when the common attribute is a key of at least one decomposed relation. - ✅ **Why 2NF matters in industry:** eliminates update, insertion, and deletion anomalies in transactional databases. - ✅ **Bridge/junction tables** for many-to-many relationships are direct applications of 2NF principles. - ✅ **2NF does not remove transitive dependencies** — that is the job of 3NF. - ✅ **Exam tip:** Always show (1) candidate key derivation, (2) prime/non-prime classification, (3) explicit partial dependency identification, (4) decomposition with FKs, and (5) verification — to secure full marks.

topics.title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Third Normal Form (3NF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Third Normal Form (3NF)

In the context of **Relational Database Design** and **Functional Dependency Theory**, a relation schema $R$ is in **Third Normal Form (3NF)** if and only if it satisfies the following two conditions:

1. The relation is already in **Second Normal Form (2NF)**.
2. **No non-prime attribute** of $R$ is **transitively dependent** on the **candidate key(s)** of $R$.

Equivalently, using the formal definition given by **Carlo Zaniolo (1982)** and adopted by KTU syllabus:

> A relation schema $R$ is in **3NF** if for every **non-trivial functional dependency** $X \rightarrow A$ that holds in $R$, **at least one** of the following conditions is true:
> - $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$, OR
> - $A$ is a **prime attribute** of $R$ (i.e., $A$ is part of some candidate key).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Zaniolo definition is the most commonly tested form in KTU ESE questions. Students must memorize the **OR** condition with both clauses.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Library Filing System

Imagine a **University Library** with a single massive register `Book_Register` that contains:

| Book_ID | Title | Author_Name | Author_Country | Shelf_No |
|---------|-------|-------------|----------------|----------|
| B001 | DBMS | Korth | USA | S5 |
| B002 | Networks | Tanenbaum | USA | S3 |

Here, `Book_ID` is the key, but `Shelf_No` depends on `Book_ID` (1NF, 2NF fine), and `Author_Country` depends on `Author_Name`, which in turn depends on `Book_ID`. This is a **transitive dependency**:

$$Book\_ID \rightarrow Author\_Name \rightarrow Author\_Country$$

The library clerk cannot independently update "Author_Country" without going through the book. To bring it to **3NF**, we **split** the table into two:
- `Books(Book_ID, Title, Author_Name, Shelf_No)`
- `Authors(Author_Name, Author_Country)`

> [!NOTE]
> **The Intuition:** 3NF ensures that **every non-key fact tells us something about the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key** (Dr. William Kent's famous quote).

## 1.3 Prerequisite Recap

| Form | Core Rule (in plain English) |
|------|------------------------------|
| **1NF** | Atomic values, no repeating groups |
| **2NF** | 1NF + No **partial dependency** (no non-key attr depends on part of composite key) |
| **3NF** | 2NF + No **transitive dependency** (no non-key attr depends on another non-key attr) |

The standard boundary constant used in KTU valuation is the **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)** threshold — it is *stricter* than 3NF and rejects relations where a non-superkey determines a prime attribute.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let a relation $R$ with attributes $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ and a set of functional dependencies $F$.

- **Step 1 — Identify Candidate Keys:** Compute $(F)^+$, the closure of $F$, and identify all minimal superkeys.
- **Step 2 — Classify Attributes:** Label each attribute as either a **prime attribute** (part of any candidate key) or a **non-prime attribute**.
- **Step 3 — Check 2NF:** Verify that no non-prime attribute is partially dependent on a candidate key.
- **Step 4 — Detect Transitive Dependencies:** For every non-prime attribute $B$, trace its functional dependencies. If $A \rightarrow C$ and $C \rightarrow B$ where $C$ is not a superkey and $B$ is non-prime, then a **transitive dependency** $A \rightarrow B$ exists.
- **Step 5 — Apply Zaniolo's Test:** For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow A$ in $F^+$:
  - Either $X$ must be a **superkey**, OR
  - $A$ must be a **prime attribute**.
- **Step 6 — Decompose:** If any FD violates the rule, decompose the relation (typically losslessly, optionally dependency-preserving).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind 3NF

- **Why 3NF?** It eliminates **update anomalies** (insertion, deletion, modification) caused by transitive dependencies.
- **How it differs from BCNF:** BCNF is stricter — it does NOT allow the second clause ("$A$ is a prime attribute"). Hence, **every BCNF relation is in 3NF**, but **not every 3NF relation is in BCNF**.
- **How 3NF is achieved:** Either by **decomposition** or by the **Synthesis Algorithm (3NF)** which guarantees a dependency-preserving lossless-join decomposition.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Statement / Formula | Use Case |
|---------|---------------------------|----------|
| Closure | $X^+ = \{A \mid X \rightarrow A \text{ is implied by } F\}$ | Find candidate keys |
| Lossless Join Test | $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1 \text{ or } R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$ | Verify decomposition |
| Dependency Preservation | $(F_{R_1} \cup F_{R_2} \cup \dots)^+ = F^+$ | Check if FDs are retained |
| 3NF Test (Zaniolo) | $\forall X \rightarrow A : X \text{ is superkey} \lor A \text{ is prime}$ | Test for 3NF |
| Synthesis (Lossy bound) | Max relations $= \vert F \vert$ | Upper bound on 3NF decomp size |
| Prime Attribute | $A \in$ some candidate key | Classify attributes |
| Minimal Cover (Steps) | (1) Right-singletonize (2) Remove extraneous LHS (3) Remove redundant FD | Build canonical FD set |
| Transitive Dependency | $X \rightarrow Y,\ Y \rightarrow Z \Rightarrow X \rightarrow Z$ (and $Y \nrightarrow X$) | Identify 3NF violation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** 3NF is the **default normalization target** in most commercial RDBMS schema designs (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL). BCNF is reserved for highly academic/theoretical settings due to its tendency to lose dependencies.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Customer → Branch → Branch_Address (transitive) — must be split to avoid update anomalies.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon/Flipkart):** Order → Product → Category — split to ensure category updates don't cascade.
- **Hospital Management:** Patient → Doctor → Department — 3NF ensures clean departmental reporting.
- **IoT Sensor Networks:** Device_ID → Gateway_ID → Gateway_Location — split for efficient telemetry routing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive 3NF Synthesis Algorithm (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The **3NF Synthesis Algorithm** is a *deterministic* procedure taught in the KTU syllabus. Let $F$ be the given set of functional dependencies.

**Step 1: Compute Minimal Cover $F_c$**
- 1.1. Replace each FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F$ with $|Y|$ FDs of the form $X \rightarrow A$ (right-singletonize).
- 1.2. For each FD, attempt to remove extraneous attributes from the LHS.
- 1.3. For each remaining FD, check if it is implied by the others; if so, remove it.

**Step 2: For each FD $X \rightarrow A$ in $F_c$, create a relation schema $R_i = XA$.**

**Step 3: If no $R_i$ contains a candidate key of the original relation, create one more relation containing a candidate key.**

**Step 4: Eliminate redundant relations** — if a relation $R_i$ is a subset of another $R_j$, drop $R_i$.

> ✅ **Guarantee:** The result is a **lossless-join** and **dependency-preserving** decomposition in 3NF.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Library Database (Detailed Derivation)

Given relation $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with FDs:
$$F = \{ AB \rightarrow C,\ C \rightarrow D,\ D \rightarrow E,\ B \rightarrow D \}$$

### Step 1.1: Right-Singletonize

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \{ AB \rightarrow C,\ C \rightarrow D,\ D \rightarrow E,\ B \rightarrow D \}
\end{aligned}
$$

All RHS are already single attributes. ✓

### Step 1.2: Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes

For $AB \rightarrow C$: 
- Check if $A \rightarrow C$ holds: Compute $A^+$ given $F_1$ → does not yield $C$.
- Check if $B \rightarrow C$ holds: Compute $B^+$ given $F_1$ → $B^+ = \{B, D, E\}$, not $C$.
- Therefore, **no extraneous attribute** in LHS. $AB \rightarrow C$ is kept.

### Step 1.3: Remove Redundant FDs

- Can we drop $AB \rightarrow C$? Without it, $AB^+ = \{A, B, D, E\}$, missing $C$. → **Cannot drop.**
- Can we drop $C \rightarrow D$? Without it, $C^+ = \{C, E\}$, missing $D$. → **Cannot drop.**
- Can we drop $D \rightarrow E$? Without it, $D^+ = \{D\}$, missing $E$. → **Cannot drop.**
- Can we drop $B \rightarrow D$? Without it, $B^+ = \{B\}$, missing $D$ and $E$. → **Cannot drop.**

So the minimal cover remains:
$$F_c = \{ AB \rightarrow C,\ C \rightarrow D,\ D \rightarrow E,\ B \rightarrow D \}$$

### Step 2: Create Relation Schemas

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1(A, B, C) \\
R_2(C, D) \\
R_3(D, E) \\
R_4(B, D)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Find Candidate Key of Original $R$

Compute $(AB)^+$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
AB &\rightarrow AB \\
AB &\rightarrow C \quad (\text{from } AB \rightarrow C) \\
ABC &\rightarrow D \quad (\text{from } C \rightarrow D) \\
ABCD &\rightarrow E \quad (\text{from } D \rightarrow E) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $(AB)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$. **Candidate key = $\{A, B\}$**.

Since $R_1$ contains $A, B$, the candidate key is already preserved. ✓

### Step 4: Eliminate Subsets

- $R_4(B, D)$ is a subset of $R_2(C, D)$? No, different attributes.
- Check if any $R_i \subseteq R_j$:
  - $R_1 = \{A, B, C\}$ — not a subset of any other.
  - $R_2 = \{C, D\}$, $R_3 = \{D, E\}$, $R_4 = \{B, D\}$ — all distinct.

### Final 3NF Decomposition

$$
\boxed{R_1(A, B, C),\ R_2(C, D),\ R_3(D, E),\ R_4(B, D)}
$$

---

## 3.3 3NF vs BCNF Comparison Matrix

| Property | 3NF | BCNF |
|----------|-----|------|
| Allows transitive deps? | No | No |
| Allows prime-att-on-non-superkey? | **Yes** | No |
| Always lossless-join? | Yes (synthesis) | Yes (decomposition) |
| Always dependency-preserving? | **Yes** | Not always |
| Stricter? | Less strict | Stricter |
| KTU Preference? | **Default target** | Special cases only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic KTU Example — Not in BCNF but in 3NF:**
> Relation: `R(Student, Subject, Professor)` with FDs:
> - `Student, Subject → Professor`
> - `Professor → Subject`
> Here, `Professor` is a non-superkey that determines `Subject` (a prime attribute). Allowed in 3NF, **violates BCNF**.

---

## 3.4 Lossless Join Derivation for 3NF Decomposition

For decomposition $(R_1, R_2)$, the test is:
$$R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1 \quad \text{OR} \quad R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$$

**Derivation Sketch:** Using the chase theorem, we start with a row $(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ and chase tuples through the FDs. If we can prove the relation contains a row of all $a$'s (or all $b$'s), the join is lossless.

For our example, $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{C\}$ and $C \rightarrow D$ (since $D \in R_2$). Since $C \rightarrow D$ means $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$, the join is **lossless**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Normalization Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unnormalized Relation UNF] --> B[1NF: Atomic Values]
    B --> C[2NF: No Partial Dependency]
    C --> D[3NF: No Transitive Dependency]
    D --> E[BCNF: Every Determinant is Superkey]
    E --> F[4NF: No Multi-valued Dependencies]
    F --> G[5NF: No Join Dependencies]
    
    A1[Anomalies: Insertion] -.-> A
    A2[Anomalies: Deletion] -.-> A
    A3[Anomalies: Update] -.-> A
    
    D --> D1[3NF allows non-superkey to determine prime attribute]
    E --> E1[BCNF rejects the above]
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ffa500,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ffd700,color:#000
    style D fill:#90ee90,color:#000
    style E fill:#87ceeb,color:#000
    style F fill:#dda0dd,color:#000
    style G fill:#d3d3d3,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 3NF Synthesis Algorithm Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Compute Minimal Cover Fc] --> P1A[1.1 Right Singletonize FDs]
    P1A --> P1B[1.2 Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes]
    P1B --> P1C[1.3 Remove Redundant FDs]
    
    P1C --> P2[Step 2: Create Relation Ri for each FD in Fc]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Check if any Ri contains a candidate key]
    P3 --> P3A{Key found?}
    P3A -- No --> P3B[Add a relation containing candidate key]
    P3A -- Yes --> P4[Step 4: Remove subset relations]
    P3B --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Final 3NF Decomposition: Lossless and Dependency Preserving]
    
    style P1 fill:#fff4e1
    style P2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P3 fill:#ffe1f5
    style P4 fill:#e1ffe1
    style P5 fill:#ffd700,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Transitive Dependency Decomposition Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Original Relation R with A -> B -> C] --> R1[Relation 1: A, B]
    R --> R2[Relation 2: B, C]
    
    R1 -.->|Lossless Join| R
    R2 -.->|Lossless Join| R
    
    R1 --> K1[Key: A]
    R2 --> K2[Key: B]
    
    R1 -.->|B is prime in R2| NOTE[Dependency Preserved via B]
    
    style R fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#90ee90
    style R2 fill:#87ceeb
    style NOTE fill:#ffd700,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**"Define Third Normal Form (3NF) using Zaniolo's definition. How does it differ from BCNF?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A relation schema $R$ is in **3NF** if for every non-trivial functional dependency $X \rightarrow A$ in $R$, either $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$, or $A$ is a **prime attribute** of $R$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Difference from BCNF:** BCNF requires the *first* condition to hold for **every** non-trivial FD, i.e., $X$ must always be a superkey. 3NF relaxes this by allowing a non-superkey determinant if its dependent attribute is prime. Thus, **every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but not vice versa**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**
**"What is a transitive dependency? Explain with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **transitive dependency** exists in a relation when a non-prime attribute $Z$ is functionally dependent on another non-prime attribute $Y$, which in turn is functionally dependent on the candidate key $X$. Formally: $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, and $Y \nrightarrow X$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Example:** In `STUDENT(Roll_No, Dept, HOD_Name)`, we have `Roll_No → Dept` and `Dept → HOD_Name`. Hence, `Roll_No → HOD_Name` is transitive and must be eliminated for 3NF. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
Consider a relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ with the following set of functional dependencies:
$$F = \{ A \rightarrow B,\ C \rightarrow D,\ E \rightarrow F,\ AC \rightarrow E \}$$

**(a)** Find a **candidate key** of $R$. Compute the minimal cover of $F$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **3NF synthesis algorithm** to obtain a lossless, dependency-preserving decomposition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Find Candidate Key**

Compute $(ACE)^+$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACE &\rightarrow ACE \\
AC &\rightarrow B \quad (\text{given } A \rightarrow B) \\
ACE &\rightarrow F \quad (\text{given } E \rightarrow F) \\
AC &\rightarrow E \quad (\text{given } AC \rightarrow E)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $(ACE)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R$, the **candidate key is $\{A, C, E\}$**. **(2 marks)**

**Prime attributes:** $A, C, E$. **Non-prime:** $B, D, F$.

**Step 2: Compute Minimal Cover**

- 2.1. All RHS are singletons. ✓
- 2.2. Check extraneous LHS:
  - $AC \rightarrow E$: Try $A \rightarrow E$? $A^+ = \{A, B\}$, no. Try $C \rightarrow E$? $C^+ = \{C, D\}$, no. Both $A, C$ needed. ✓
- 2.3. Check redundant FDs:
  - Drop $A \rightarrow B$? $A^+ = \{A\}$, no. Keep.
  - Drop $C \rightarrow D$? $C^+ = \{C\}$, no. Keep.
  - Drop $E \rightarrow F$? $E^+ = \{E\}$, no. Keep.
  - Drop $AC \rightarrow E$? Without it, $AC^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$, no $E$. Keep.

$$F_c = \{ A \rightarrow B,\ C \rightarrow D,\ E \rightarrow F,\ AC \rightarrow E \}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Identify if any key exists in FDs** — $\{A, C\}$ is a proper subset of key $\{A, C, E\}$, but $AC \rightarrow E$ exists in $F_c$, meaning $AC$ functionally determines $E$. Hence, **AC is also a superkey**. Therefore, the candidate key is preserved by the FD `AC → E` itself. **(1 mark for explanation)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Apply Synthesis Algorithm:**

Step 2 — Create relation for each FD in $F_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1(A, B) \\
R_2(C, D) \\
R_3(E, F) \\
R_4(A, C, E)
\end{aligned}
$$ **(2 marks)**

Step 3 — Check if any $R_i$ contains a candidate key. $R_4 = \{A, C, E\}$ IS the candidate key. ✓ **(1 mark)**

Step 4 — Eliminate subsets:
- $R_1(A,B) \subset R_4(A,C,E)$? No, $B \notin R_4$.
- $R_2(C,D) \subset R_4$? No, $D \notin R_4$.
- $R_3(E,F) \subset R_4$? No, $F \notin R_4$.

No subset relations. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Final 3NF Decomposition:**

$$\boxed{R_1(A, B),\ R_2(C, D),\ R_3(E, F),\ R_4(A, C, E)}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Verification:**
- **Lossless:** Common attribute of $R_1 \cap R_4 = \{A\}$ and $A \rightarrow B$ means $R_1 \cap R_4 \rightarrow R_1$. ✓
- **Dependency Preserved:** All FDs of $F$ appear in some $R_i$. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**
Consider the relation $R(\text{Emp\_ID}, \text{Project\_ID}, \text{Dept}, \text{Dept\_Head}, \text{Hours})$ with FDs:
$$F = \{ \text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Hours},\ \text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept},\ \text{Dept} \rightarrow \text{Dept\_Head} \}$$

**(a)** Identify the **transitive dependencies** and bring the relation to **3NF** through decomposition. **(7 marks)**
