**(b)** Verify that the decomposition is **lossless** and **dependency-preserving**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Identify Candidate Key**

Compute $(\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID})^+$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Hours} \\
&\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept} \\
&\text{Dept} \rightarrow \text{Dept\_Head}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $(\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID})^+ = \{\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID, Hours, Dept, Dept\_Head}\} = R$. ✓

**Candidate key = $\{\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID}\}$** **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Identify Transitive Dependencies**

- $\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept}$ (non-key to non-key)
- $\text{Dept} \rightarrow \text{Dept\_Head}$ (non-key to non-key)

Hence, the transitive chain is:
$$\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept} \rightarrow \text{Dept\_Head}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Compute Minimal Cover**

$F_c = \{ \text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Hours},\ \text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept},\ \text{Dept} \rightarrow \text{Dept\_Head} \}$ — all are minimal. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Decompose Using Synthesis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1(\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID, Hours}) \\
R_2(\text{Emp\_ID, Dept}) \\
R_3(\text{Dept, Dept\_Head})
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_1$ contains the candidate key. ✓ **(2 marks)**

**Final 3NF Schema:**

$$\boxed{R_1(\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID, Hours}),\ R_2(\text{Emp\_ID, Dept}),\ R_3(\text{Dept, Dept\_Head})}$$ **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Lossless Join Verification:**

Test each pair using the rule: $R_i \cap R_j \rightarrow R_i$ or $R_i \cap R_j \rightarrow R_j$.

- $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{\text{Emp\_ID}\}$. From FDs, $\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept}$ is in $R_2$, but is it in $R_1$? No. We need $\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID, Hours}$. The FD $\text{Emp\_ID, Project\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Hours}$ requires *both* attributes — $\text{Emp\_ID}$ alone does NOT determine $R_1$.

  → **Chase test required.** Apply chase: Start with table having rows for $R_1$ and $R_2$ on their respective attrs. Use $\text{Emp\_ID} \rightarrow \text{Dept}$ to propagate. Result: full row. ✓ **Lossless.** **(2 marks)**

- $R_1 \cap R_3 = \emptyset$. Use chase with all 3 relations: start with 3 rows, apply FDs iteratively. We get a row with all matching values. ✓ **Lossless.** **(2 marks)**

**Dependency Preservation Verification:**

- `Emp_ID, Project_ID → Hours` → in $R_1$. ✓
- `Emp_ID → Dept` → in $R_2$. ✓
- `Dept → Dept_Head` → in $R_3$. ✓

All FDs are preserved. **(1 mark)**

**Update Anomaly Check:** In the original relation, updating `Dept_Head` would require scanning all employee rows. After decomposition, only $R_3$ needs updating. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing prime and non-prime attributes** — many students misclassify attributes, leading to wrong candidate keys. **(-2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Forgetting to verify lossless join** — simply writing decomposition is *not enough*; KTU examiners explicitly test this. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Using BCNF rules when 3NF is asked** — 3NF allows prime-on-non-superkey, BCNF does not. Wrong test = **(-3 marks)**.
> 4. **Not computing minimal cover properly** — missing the extraneous LHS attribute step. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 5. **Omitting the chase test** for lossless join when common attributes don't directly determine a relation. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 6. **Confusing dependency preservation with lossless join** — these are *separate* properties. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **3NF = 2NF + No Transitive Dependency** (Zaniolo's equivalent: Superkey OR Prime on RHS).
- ✅ **Synthesis Algorithm** gives *guaranteed* lossless + dependency-preserving 3NF.
- ✅ **BCNF ⊂ 3NF** (every BCNF is 3NF, not vice versa).
- ✅ **Prime attribute** = attribute that belongs to *some* candidate key.
- ✅ **Transitive dependency** is the *key villain* 3NF eliminates.
- ✅ Always **right-singletonize** before computing minimal cover.
- ✅ **Chase test** is the universal algorithm for verifying lossless join.
- ✅ In KTU, default to **3NF** unless the question explicitly demands **BCNF**.
- ✅ Remember: 3NF is the **practical sweet spot** between anomalies and over-normalization.
- ✅ **Dependency preservation** is the *only* property 3NF guarantees over BCNF.

---

> 🎓 **End of Notes — Third Normal Form (3NF), Module 3, PBERT504**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 OBE Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (BCNF):** A relation schema **R** is in **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)** if and only if, for every non-trivial functional dependency **X → Y** in the closure **F⁺** of the dependency set **F**, the left-hand side **X** is a **superkey** of **R**. Equivalently, no non-trivial FD has a non-superkey determinant.

Mathematically:
$$\text{R is in BCNF} \iff \forall \text{ FD } (X \to Y) \in F^+ \text{ where } Y \not\subseteq X, \; X \text{ is a superkey of R}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "University Allocation" Intuition

Consider a college timetable cell. The combination **(Student, Subject)** is supposed to uniquely identify the **Professor** (because each subject is taught by exactly one professor for a given student). However, we also know that **Professor → Subject** (a professor specializes in a particular subject).

- A BCNF-violating table is like a **library catalog where a book can be located by its title or its shelf number, but a single book sometimes gets catalogued under two different shelf numbers** — duplication and ambiguity result.
- BCNF is like enforcing the rule: **"Anything that decides another column must itself be the unique key"**. A **Professor** alone decides a **Subject**, but a single professor is not a *unique identifier* of the row (many students map to one professor). Hence the table is **redundant** — a BCNF violation.
- The fix? **Split the table**: one for (Professor, Subject) and one for (Student, Professor). Now every determinant is a key.

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Bloom Level Anchors

| Bloom Level | Cognitive Action on BCNF |
|:---:|:---|
| **L1 – Remember** | Recite the BCNF definition |
| **L2 – Understand** | Differentiate BCNF from 3NF |
| **L3 – Apply** | Decompose a relation into BCNF |
| **L4 – Analyze** | Identify violating FDs and test lossless join via the Chase |
| **L5 – Evaluate** | Judge dependency preservation after BCNF decomposition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3, Normalization Block):**
> BCNF is the **strictest** practical normal form that every KTU-board expected answer must address after 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF. The hallmark difference: **3NF permits a non-key attribute on the LHS of an FD if the RHS is a prime attribute; BCNF permits no such exception.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 BCNF Conditions — The Five Golden Rules

1. **The relation must already be in 3NF** (BCNF ⊂ 3NF strictly).
2. For every non-trivial FD **X → Y**, the determinant **X must be a superkey** of **R**.
3. There must be **no transitive, partial, or non-superkey dependencies** in disguise.
4. The relation is **free of insertion, deletion, and update anomalies** of the kind introduced by non-key determinants.
5. **BCNF is always achievable** through decomposition, but **dependency preservation is NOT guaranteed** (this is a critical KTU caveat).

## 2.2 BCNF Decomposition Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

The standard greedy algorithm used in KTU model answers is as follows:

```
BCNF_Decompose(R, F):
  1. If R is already in BCNF, RETURN R.
  2. Else, find a non-trivial FD X → Y in F+ that VIOLATES BCNF
     (i.e., X is NOT a superkey of R).
  3. Decompose R into:
        R1 = X ∪ Y
        R2 = R − (Y − X)
  4. Recursively apply BCNF_Decompose on R1 and R2.
  5. STOP when all sub-relations satisfy BCNF.
```

**Why this works:** The decomposition splits the offending determinant (and its dependents) into its own relation, where **X becomes the key by construction**. The remaining attributes go to a second relation.

## 2.3 Lossless Join Verification — Two Methods

### Method A: Set Intersection Rule (Quick Test)
A binary decomposition **R → (R1, R2)** is **lossless** if and only if:
$$(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1 \quad \text{or} \quad (R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_2$$

### Method B: Chase / Tableau Method (For any decomposition)
Build an **n-column, m-row** table (m = number of decomposed relations). Initialize each cell with:
- **'a'** if the attribute is in that relation
- **'b'** otherwise

Apply each FD in **F** repeatedly: if two rows agree on the LHS, force their RHS symbols to be **equal** (replace **b's with a's**). **If any row becomes all 'a's → lossless**.

## 2.4 Dependency Preservation Property

A decomposition preserves a dependency **X → Y** if:
$$Y \subseteq X^+_{F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \dots \cup F_n}$$
where **F_i** is the projection of **F** onto relation **R_i**. A decomposition is **dependency preserving** if **all** original FDs survive this test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** The standard BCNF decomposition algorithm **always produces a lossless-join decomposition**, but it **may NOT preserve all dependencies**. This is the fundamental reason why **3NF is sometimes preferred over BCNF** in practice (a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

| # | Property | Condition / Formula | Guaranteed? |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | BCNF Membership | $\forall \text{ FD } X \to Y \in F^+, \; X \text{ is a superkey}$ | Definition |
| 2 | Lossless Decomposition | $(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1 \;\; \vert\;\; (R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_2$ | **Yes (always)** |
| 3 | Dependency Preservation | $\forall \text{ FD } f \in F, \; f \in (F_1 \cup F_2)^+$ | **Not guaranteed** |
| 4 | Anomaly Removal | Insertion, Deletion, Update anomalies | **Eliminated** |
| 5 | Relation with Two C.K. (disjoint) | Always decomposable to BCNF preserving all FDs | Conditional |
| 6 | Decomposition preserves F+ | $\pi_{R_1}(F) \cup \pi_{R_2}(F) \models F$ | **Not always** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking systems** — Branch → Assets dependency is enforced via separate relations to eliminate duplication of asset values across millions of accounts.
- **Hospital Management** — Doctor → Department dependency is normalized to avoid one department's information being stored N times.
- **E-Commerce Catalogs** — Product_Category → Tax_Rate normalized to allow easy tax revisions without touching every product record.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example (The "Professor-Student-Subject" Table)

**Given Relation:** R(**Stu_id, Subj, Prof**)

**Functional Dependencies:**
- **FD1:** {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof
- **FD2:** Prof → Subj

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Attribute Closures to Find Candidate Keys

$$\{Stu\_id, Subj\}^+ = \{Stu\_id, Subj\} \cup \{Prof\} = \{Stu\_id, Subj, Prof\}$$

$$\{Stu\_id, Prof\}^+ = \{Stu\_id, Prof\} \cup \{Subj\} = \{Stu\_id, Subj, Prof\}$$

$$\{Prof\}^+ = \{Prof, Subj\} \quad (\text{cannot reach } Stu\_id)$$

> ✅ **Candidate Keys (CK):** {Stu_id, Subj} and {Stu_id, Prof}
>
> ✅ **Prime Attributes:** All three (Stu_id, Subj, Prof)

---

### Step 2 — Check the Highest Normal Form

**1NF:** Holds (atomic values assumed).

**2NF:** Holds (no partial dependency on a non-prime attribute — every non-trivial FD's RHS is prime or a key).

**3NF Check:** Examine each non-trivial FD:

| FD | LHS Superkey? | RHS Prime? | 3NF Status |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | OK |
| Prof → Subj | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Subj is prime) | OK in 3NF |

**Highest normal form = 3NF.**

**BCNF Check:** The very same FD **Prof → Subj** violates BCNF because **Prof is not a superkey** of R (BCNF demands superkey, not just prime RHS).

> ❌ **R is NOT in BCNF.**

---

### Step 3 — Apply the BCNF Decomposition Algorithm

**Identify the violator:** FD2: Prof → Subj

Decompose:
$$R_1 = \text{Prof} \cup \text{Subj} = \{Prof, Subj\}$$
$$R_2 = R - (\text{Subj} - \text{Prof}) = \{Stu\_id, Prof\}$$

**Recursively check R₁:** FD = Prof → Subj. In R₁, **Prof⁺ = {Prof, Subj}** = R₁, so **Prof is a key**. ✅ **R₁ is in BCNF.**

**Recursively check R₂:** Non-trivial FDs? None project (Stu_id, Prof → Prof is trivial). ✅ **R₂ is in BCNF.**

> 🎯 **Final BCNF Decomposition:** R₁(Prof, Subj), R₂(Stu_id, Prof)

---

### Step 4 — Verify Lossless Join (Chase Method)

Initial tableau (rows = decomposed relations, columns = original attributes):

| Row | Stu_id | Subj | Prof |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R₁ | **b** | a | a |
| R₂ | a | **b** | a |

Apply **FD2: Prof → Subj** → Both rows have **a** in Prof column, so Subj must be made equal. The minimum (lexicographically: 'a') wins. The 'b' in R₁'s Subj becomes 'a':

| Row | Stu_id | Subj | Prof |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R₁ | b | **a** | a |
| R₂ | a | a | a |

Apply **FD1: {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof** → Rows do not agree on Stu_id (one has 'a', other 'b'). No change.

> ✅ **R₁ row is now all 'a's** → **Decomposition is LOSSLESS.**

---

### Step 5 — Verify Dependency Preservation

For each original FD, compute its closure under the projected FDs.

**Check FD1: {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof**

In R₂(Stu_id, Prof): we have Stu_id (✓), but Prof (✓) and we need Subj to fire FD1. Subj is not in R₂.

In R₁(Prof, Subj): we have Subj (✓) and Prof (✓), but Stu_id is not in R₁.

$$\{Stu\_id, Subj\}^+_{F_1 \cup F_2} = \{Stu\_id, Subj, Prof\} \cap \dots$$

Computing the combined closure systematically:

$$\{Stu\_id, Subj\}^+ = \{Stu\_id, Subj\} \xrightarrow{\text{not in single }R_i} \text{cannot apply FD1} \cup \text{Prof (missing from }R_2\text{) to fire FD1}$$

> ❌ **FD1 is NOT preserved.**

> [!WARNING]
> **This is THE classic KTU trap:** The BCNF decomposition is lossless, but **the FD {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof cannot be enforced** in the decomposed schema without a join. This is the textbook reason why some authors advocate **3NF over BCNF** when dependency preservation is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — BCNF Decomposition Decision Tree

| Check | Question | YES → | NO → |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Is R in BCNF? | Stop, R is normalized | Go to Check 2 |
| 2 | Does a non-trivial FD X → Y have X as a superkey? | Skip this FD | Pick this FD as violator |
| 3 | Split using X → Y into (X∪Y) and (R−(Y−X))? | Recurse on both pieces | Algorithm fails (shouldn't happen) |
| 4 | Can FD be preserved post-decomposition? | Ideal solution | Acceptable in KTU if lossless |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Proof of the Decomposition Correctness

**Claim:** If FD **X → Y** violates BCNF in R, the split **R₁ = X ∪ Y, R₂ = R − (Y − X)** always yields a lossless decomposition.

**Proof:**

$$R_1 \cap R_2 = (X \cup Y) \cap (R - (Y - X)) = X$$

By the violating FD, **X → Y** holds. Therefore:
$$R_1 \cap R_2 = X \to (X \cup Y) = R_1$$

By the lossless-join theorem:
$$(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1 \;\;\blacksquare$$

**Interpretation:** The decomposition is **structurally guaranteed lossless** because the LHS of the violator is always retained in both children.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — BCNF Decomposition Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start BCNF Decomposition]) --> inputNode[/Input Relation R and FD set F/]
    inputNode --> checkKey{Does every non-trivial FD X to Y have X as a superkey of R?}
    checkKey -- Yes --> returnR[/R is in BCNF, return R/]
    checkKey -- No --> pickFD[Identify a violating FD X to Y where X is NOT a superkey]
    pickFD --> splitR[Split R into R1 = X union Y and R2 = R minus Y minus X]
    splitR --> recurseLeft[Recursively check R1 against BCNF]
    splitR --> recurseRight[Recursively check R2 against BCNF]
    recurseLeft --> joinResult
    recurseRight --> joinResult
    joinResult[Combine results into final BCNF schema] --> endNode([Output: Lossless BCNF Decomposition])

    style startNode fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style endNode fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style checkKey fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px
    style pickFD fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 3NF vs BCNF Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph n3nf["3NF ACCEPTABLE SCOPE"]
        a1["FD: X to Y where X is NOT a superkey"] --> a2["Permitted ONLY if Y is a PRIME attribute"]
        a2 --> a3["Anomalies may still exist for non-superkey determinants"]
    end

    subgraph bcnf["BCNF STRICT SCOPE"]
        b1["FD: X to Y where X is NOT a superkey"] --> b2["STRICTLY FORBIDDEN - no exceptions"]
        b2 --> b3["Zero redundancy, full anomaly removal"]
    end

    n3nf -- "Stricter rules apply" --> bcnf
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Chase Lossless-Join Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Build n x m chase table] --> stepB[Step 2: Initialize cells with a or b based on attribute membership]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Pick an FD from F]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Find rows that AGREE on LHS of FD]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Force their RHS cells to be EQUAL pick the minimum a or b]
    stepE --> stepF{All FDs applied and rows stable?}
    stepF -- No --> stepC
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG{Any row is all a's?}
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[LOSSLESS decomposition confirmed]
    stepG -- No --> stepI[NOT lossless, restructure decomposition]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Why BCNF Decomposition Loses Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph origRel["ORIGINAL RELATION R"]
        r1["Attribute Group A"]
        r2["FD: Stu_id Subj to Prof"]
        r3["FD: Prof to Subj"]
    end

    subgraph dec1["R1: Prof Subj"]
        d1a["Enforces Prof to Subj"]
        d1b["Prof is KEY here"]
        d1c["FD is preserved"]
    end

    subgraph dec2["R2: Stu_id Prof"]
        d2a["No non-trivial FD survives"]
        d2b["Stu_id Prof is superkey"]
        d2c["FD1 CANNOT be checked without join"]
    end

    origRel --> dec1
    origRel --> dec2
    dec1 -. "Split by Prof" .-> dec2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define BCNF. How does it differ from 3NF? Give one example of a relation that is in 3NF but not in BCNF. (3 Marks) — *Bloom: L2 Understand*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A relation R is in **BCNF** if, for every non-trivial functional dependency X → Y in F⁺, **X is a superkey** of R.

**Difference from 3NF:** 3NF allows X to be a non-superkey **only if** Y is a prime attribute. **BCNF removes this exception** — X **must** be a superkey unconditionally.

**Example:** R(Stu_id, Subj, Prof) with FDs {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof and Prof → Subj. Since Prof → Subj has a non-superkey LHS, R is in 3NF (Subj is prime) but **not in BCNF**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** "Every BCNF decomposition is lossless, but not every BCNF decomposition is dependency preserving." Justify this statement with a suitable example. (3 Marks) — *Bloom: L4 Analyze*

**Model Answer:**

**Justification:** The BCNF decomposition algorithm always splits along an FD X → Y such that X is retained in both children, guaranteeing $(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1$ and hence lossless join.

However, when an FD uses attributes **distributed across multiple decomposed relations**, it cannot be enforced in any single relation.

**Example:** R(Stu_id, Subj, Prof) decomposed to R₁(Prof, Subj) and R₂(Stu_id, Prof). The FD {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof **cannot be verified** in either R₁ (no Stu_id) or R₂ (no Subj) without performing a join — hence **not dependency preserved**, even though the decomposition is lossless.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Bloom: L3 Apply + L4 Analyze*

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

Consider the relation **R(Stu_id, Subj, Prof)** with the following FDs:
- **FD1:** {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof
- **FD2:** Prof → Subj

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Find all candidate keys of R. Determine the **highest normal form** R satisfies. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Decompose R into **BCNF**. Verify that the decomposition is **lossless** (using the chase method) and check whether it is **dependency preserving**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Candidate Keys and Highest Normal Form (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Attribute Closures**

| Set | Closure Computation | Includes all of R? |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| {Stu_id, Subj}⁺ | = {Stu_id, Subj, Prof} (via FD1) | ✅ Yes → CK₁ |
| {Stu_id, Prof}⁺ | = {Stu_id, Prof, Subj} (via FD2) | ✅ Yes → CK₂ |
| {Prof}⁺ | = {Prof, Subj} | ❌ |
| {Subj}⁺ | = {Subj} | ❌ |
| {Stu_id}⁺ | = {Stu_id} | ❌ |

**Candidate Keys:** {Stu_id, Subj} and {Stu_id, Prof}.
**Prime attributes:** Stu_id, Subj, Prof (all of them).

**Step 2: Normal Form Check**

| Normal Form | Verdict | Reason |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1NF | ✅ | All attributes are atomic |
| 2NF | ✅ | No partial dependency on a candidate key |
| 3NF | ✅ | FD2 (Prof → Subj) has Prof as a non-superkey LHS, but Subj is **prime** — allowed in 3NF |
| BCNF | ❌ | FD2 has a non-superkey LHS, and **BCNF disallows this exception** |

> **Highest Normal Form: 3NF**

---

#### Part (b) — BCNF Decomposition, Lossless Check, Dependency Preservation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the BCNF Violator**

The violating FD is **FD2: Prof → Subj** (Prof is not a superkey).

**Step 2: Decompose**

$$R_1 = \{Prof, Subj\} \quad ; \quad R_2 = R - (\{Subj\} - \{Prof\}) = \{Stu\_id, Prof\}$$

**Check R₁:** Prof → Subj holds, Prof⁺ in R₁ = {Prof, Subj} = R₁, so **Prof is a key**. ✅ BCNF
**Check R₂:** No non-trivial FD projects. ✅ BCNF

**Final BCNF:** R₁(Prof, Subj), R₂(Stu_id, Prof)

**Step 3: Lossless Join via Chase**

Initial tableau:

| Row | Stu_id | Subj | Prof |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R₁ | b | a | a |
| R₂ | a | b | a |

Apply **FD2 (Prof → Subj):** both rows have 'a' in Prof → equalize Subj (min 'a'):

| Row | Stu_id | Subj | Prof |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R₁ | b | **a** | a |
| R₂ | a | a | a |

**R₁ row is all 'a's** → ✅ **Lossless join confirmed.**

**Step 4: Dependency Preservation**

| FD | Preserved? | Reasoning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| FD1: {Stu_id, Subj} → Prof | ❌ | Requires both Stu_id (in R₂) and Subj (in R₁) to be available simultaneously; cannot be checked within any single decomposed relation. |
| FD2: Prof → Subj | ✅ | Both Prof and Subj are in R₁, so the FD is locally enforceable. |

> **Conclusion:** The BCNF decomposition is **lossless** ✅ but **NOT dependency preserving** ❌.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *Bloom: L3 Apply + L5 Evaluate*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

Consider the relation **R(Branch, Account, Cust_name, Cust_city, Assets)** representing a banking scenario, with the following FDs:
- **FD1:** Branch → Assets
- **FD2:** Cust_name → Cust_city
- **FD3:** {Cust_name, Account} → Branch

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Determine all **candidate keys** of R. Identify the **prime** and **non-prime** attributes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Decompose R into **BCNF** relations. Verify the **lossless join** property for each step using either the intersection rule or the chase method. Comment on **dependency preservation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Candidate Keys and Prime/Non-Prime Attributes (7 Marks)

**Closures:**

| Set | Closure | Superkey? |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| {Cust_name, Account}⁺ | = {Cust_name, Account, Cust_city (FD2), Branch (FD3), Assets (FD1)} | ✅ Yes → CK |
| {Account}⁺ | = {Account} | ❌ |
| {Branch, Account}⁺ | = {Branch, Account, Assets} | ❌ (no Cust_name) |
| {Cust_name, Branch}⁺ | = {Cust_name, Branch, Cust_city, Assets} | ❌ (no Account) |

**Sole Candidate Key:** {Cust_name, Account}

**Prime attributes:** Cust_name, Account
**Non-prime attributes:** Branch, Cust_city, Assets

---

#### Part (b) — BCNF Decomposition with Lossless Verification (7 Marks)

**Check 3NF/BCNF violations:**

| FD | LHS Superkey? | Status |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Branch → Assets | ❌ (Branch is not a superkey) | **BCNF Violator #1** |
| Cust_name → Cust_city | ❌ | **BCNF Violator #2** |
| {Cust_name, Account} → Branch | ✅ | OK |

**Decomposition Step 1:** Apply Branch → Assets:
$$R_1 = \{Branch, Assets\}, \quad R_2 = \{Branch, Account, Cust\_name, Cust\_city\}$$

- **R₁ in BCNF?** Branch is a key of R₁ (Branch⁺ = {Branch, Assets}). ✅
- **R₂ in BCNF?** Cust_name → Cust_city still violates. **No.**

**Decomposition Step 2:** Apply Cust_name → Cust_city on R₂:
$$R_{21} = \{Cust\_name, Cust\_city\}, \quad R_{22} = \{Cust\_name, Account, Branch\}$$

- **R₂₁ in BCNF?** Cust_name is a key. ✅
- **R₂₂ in BCNF?** {Cust_name, Account} is a key, FD3 holds. ✅

**Final BCNF Schema:** R₁(Branch, Assets), R₂₁(Cust_name, Cust_city), R₂₂(Cust_name, Account, Branch)

**Lossless Join Verification (Intersection Rule):**

| Step | Decomposition | Intersection | Common → ? | Lossless? |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | R → (R₁, R₂) | {Branch} | Branch → R₁ (via FD1) | ✅ |
| 2 | R₂ → (R₂₁, R₂₂) | {Cust_name} | Cust_name → R₂₁ (via FD2) | ✅ |

> **Entire decomposition is LOSSLESS.**

**Dependency Preservation:**

| FD | Preserved? | Reason |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Branch → Assets | ✅ | Local in R₁ |
| Cust_name → Cust_city | ✅ | Local in R₂₁ |
| {Cust_name, Account} → Branch | ✅ | Local in R₂₂ |

> 🎯 **All dependencies preserved** — this is one of the *lucky* cases where BCNF gives us the best of both worlds.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls in BCNF Questions (Compiled from KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute F⁺** — students check only F, not F⁺. BCNF requires the **closure** to be checked.
> 2. **Confusing "prime attribute" with "key"** — prime ≠ superkey. A prime attribute is a member of *some* candidate key, not a key itself.
> 3. **Not recursively checking both decomposed relations** — BCNF decomposition is recursive; one of the children may still violate BCNF.
> 4. **Skipping the Chase verification** — KTU specifically demands either the **intersection rule** OR the **chase tableau** with the table drawn clearly.
> 5. **Omitting the dependency preservation comment** — even if all FDs are preserved, the student must **explicitly state** which FDs are preserved and which are lost. Default assumption: *BCNF need not preserve dependencies* — a 1–2 mark deduction if ignored.
> 6. **Using FD notation inconsistently** — always write X → Y, not X=Y or X determines Y. Stick to textbook symbols.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **BCNF Definition:** Every non-trivial FD's LHS **must** be a superkey — **no exceptions**.
- ✅ **BCNF ⊂ 3NF** — every BCNF relation is automatically in 3NF, but not vice versa.
- ✅ **Algorithm:** Find violating FD X → Y → Split into (X∪Y) and R−(Y−X) → Recurse.
- ✅ **Lossless join is guaranteed** by the BCNF algorithm (proof: X is the common intersection).
- ✅ **Dependency preservation is NOT guaranteed** — a critical KTU gotcha.
- ✅ **Chase Method:** Build table → Apply FDs → If any row is all 'a's → Lossless.
- ✅ **Intersection Rule:** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1$ or $(R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_2$ implies lossless.
- ✅ **The classic 3NF-but-not-BCNF case:** R(Stu_id, Subj, Prof) with Prof → Subj.
- ✅ **The Chase test is binary-decision proof** — it is a constructive algorithm, not a heuristic.
- ✅ **Prime attribute:** A member of *some* candidate key — do not confuse with "prime number".
- ✅ **Non-trivial FD:** Y ∩ X = ∅ (RHS not in LHS) — critical for BCNF check.
- ✅ **BCNF removes all redundancy** that arises from non-key determinants.
- ✅ **Decomposition may not be unique** — multiple valid BCNF decompositions can exist for the same R.

### 🧠 Memory Trick (KTU Exam Mnemonic)

> **"BCNF = 'Be Careful, Not Forgiving'"**
> - **B** — Both LHS and the relation must agree
> - **C** — Check F⁺, not just F
> - **N** — No exceptions (unlike 3NF)
> - **F** — For every non-trivial FD

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Question Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Typical Marks |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| "Define BCNF + differentiate from 3NF" | Very High | 3 |
| "Find CK + Highest NF for given R" | Very High | 7 |
| "Decompose into BCNF + Lossless check" | Very High | 7–14 |
| "Why BCNF may not preserve dependencies?" | High | 3–7 |
| "Chase method trace for a decomposition" | Medium | 5–7 |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Processing Concepts - Overview of Concurrency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transaction Processing Concepts - Overview of Concurrency Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transaction — The Fundamental Unit of Database Work

A **transaction** is a logical unit of database processing that includes one or more database access operations (read, write, update, delete) executed as an **indivisible logical unit of work**. In the context of multi-user DBMS environments, a transaction is the atomic unit of recovery, consistency, and concurrency control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A transaction is a sequence of operations performed on a database that transforms the database from one consistent state to another. It must either be completed in its entirety (committed) or have no effect at all (aborted/rolled back).

### 1.2 Transaction States — The Lifecycle Diagram

Every transaction in a DBMS moves through a well-defined state machine:

1. **Active State**: The initial state. Transaction starts here and remains here while it is executing its read/write operations.
2. **Partially Committed State**: After the final operation has been executed, but before the system confirms the commit is permanent (i.e., just before the COMMIT statement succeeds).
3. **Committed State**: After successful completion. All changes are permanently recorded in the database. Once committed, the transaction leaves the system.
4. **Failed State**: After the discovery that normal execution can no longer proceed.
5. **Aborted State**: After the transaction has been rolled back and the database has been restored to its prior consistent state.

## 1.3 The ACID Properties — Non-Negotiable Cornerstones

> [!NOTE]
> **ACID** stands for **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — the four mandatory properties that any reliable transaction system must guarantee.

| Property | Meaning | Mechanism in DBMS |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution | Transaction Manager (uses `UNDO` logs) |
| **Consistency** | DB moves from one valid state to another | Programmer + Integrity Constraints |
| **Isolation** | Transactions appear to execute serially | Concurrency Control Component |
| **Durability** | Committed changes survive crashes | Recovery Manager (uses `REDO` logs) |

## 1.4 What is Concurrency Control?

**Concurrency Control** is the set of mechanisms, protocols, and algorithms used by a Database Management System to **manage simultaneous (concurrent) operations on the database without letting them interfere with each other in a way that produces incorrect results**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Concurrency control is the process of coordinating concurrent transactions such that the concurrent execution of transactions yields the same result as some serial execution of the same transactions, while preserving database consistency.

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Teller Analogy:**
> Imagine a small bank branch with a **single vault** but multiple tellers serving customers at different counters. Two customers (transactions) arrive at the same time:
> - Customer A wants to **withdraw ₹5,000** from a joint account that has ₹6,000.
> - Customer B wants to **withdraw ₹4,000** from the same account.
>
> If both tellers read the balance (₹6,000) at the same instant, both will see sufficient funds, and both will dispense cash — leaving the vault with **-₹3,000** (a negative balance, which is **inconsistent and unacceptable**).
>
> **Concurrency Control = the vault's rule-book** that says *"only one teller can check the balance AND update the vault at a time, OR if multiple tellers act in parallel, the system must use locks/checkers so that the final outcome matches what would have happened if customers were served one after another."*

## 1.6 Why is Concurrency Control Needed?

Without proper concurrency control, several **anomalies** can corrupt the database:

- **Lost Update Problem** — Two transactions update the same data item, and the second update overwrites the first.
- **Dirty Read (Temporary Update) Problem** — A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction.
- **Unrepeatable Read (Incorrect Summary) Problem** — A transaction reads the same data item twice and gets different values.
- **Phantom Problem** — A transaction re-executes a query and finds new tuples added by another transaction.

The **isolation property** of ACID is implemented **entirely through concurrency control techniques**.

## 1.7 Overview of Concurrency Control Techniques

The major families of concurrency control protocols are:

1. **Lock-Based Protocols** (e.g., 2-Phase Locking — 2PL)
2. **Timestamp-Based Protocols**
3. **Validation-Based / Optimistic Protocols** (also called Certification-based)
4. **Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC)**

Each technique aims to **produce only serializable schedules** while maximizing the degree of parallelism.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Schedules — The Blueprints of Transaction Execution

A **schedule** (also called a **history**) is a sequence of interleaved operations from one or more transactions, preserving the order of operations within each individual transaction.

### 2.1.1 Types of Schedules

| Schedule Type | Definition | Validity |
|---------------|------------|----------|
| **Serial Schedule** | All operations of one transaction complete before another begins | Always correct (no concurrency anomalies) |
| **Non-Serial Schedule** | Operations of transactions are interleaved | May or may not be correct |
| **Serializable Schedule** | A non-serial schedule whose effect is **equivalent to some serial schedule** | Correct |
| **Conflict-Serializable** | Serializable based on **conflict equivalence** | Most widely used criterion |
| **View-Serializable** | Serializable based on **view equivalence** (weaker than conflict) | Correct but harder to test |
| **Recoverable Schedule** | If $T_j$ reads a value written by $T_i$, then $T_i$ must commit before $T_j$ | Required for recovery |
| **Cascadeless Schedule** | Transactions only read committed values | Avoids cascading rollbacks |
| **Strict Schedule** | Transactions cannot read or write an item written by an uncommitted transaction | Strongest isolation |

## 2.2 Conflict Serializability — The Gold Standard

Two operations in a schedule **conflict** if they belong to different transactions, operate on the **same data item**, and **at least one of them is a WRITE**.

There are three possible conflicts:
1. **Read–Write Conflict** (R–W) — Unrepeatable Read
2. **Write–Read Conflict** (W–R) — Dirty Read
3. **Write–Write Conflict** (W–W) — Lost Update

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A schedule is **conflict serializable** if it is conflict-equivalent to some serial schedule. This is tested using a **Precedence Graph**.

## 2.3 Precedence Graph Algorithm

A **Precedence Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph where:
- Each vertex represents a transaction.
- An edge $T_i \to T_j$ exists if $T_j$ must come after $T_i$ in any equivalent serial schedule (i.e., they have a conflicting operation where $T_i$'s operation precedes $T_j$'s).

### Algorithm (Step-by-Step):
1. Create a node for every transaction.
2. For every pair of conflicting operations $op_i \in T_i$ and $op_j \in T_j$ where $op_i$ appears before $op_j$ in the schedule, draw a directed edge from $T_i$ to $T_j$.
3. If the graph has **no cycle**, the schedule is **conflict-serializable**.
4. If a **cycle exists**, the schedule is **NOT conflict-serializable**.

## 2.4 View Serializability — A Weaker but Broader Criterion

Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are **view-equivalent** if:
1. They have the **same initial reads** (same transaction reads the initial value of each data item).
2. They have the **same read-from relationships** (if $T_j$ reads a value written by $T_i$ in $S$, the same must hold in $S'$).
3. They have the **same final writes** (the same transaction writes the final value of each data item).

> [!NOTE]
> Every conflict-serializable schedule is view-serializable, but the converse is not always true.

## 2.5 Recoverability — Ensuring Failure Safety

| Property | Condition |
|----------|-----------|
| **Recoverable** | For any $T_j$ that reads from $T_i$, $T_i$ commits before $T_j$ |
| **Avoids Cascading Aborts (ACA)** | Transactions only read committed values |
| **Strict** | No transaction reads or writes a value written by an uncommitted transaction |

A hierarchy holds: **Strict ⊂ ACA ⊂ Recoverable ⊂ Serializable**.

## 2.6 Two-Phase Locking (2PL) — The Dominant Locking Protocol

In **2-Phase Locking**, every transaction issues lock and unlock requests in two phases:
1. **Growing Phase**: A transaction may acquire locks but cannot release any.
2. **Shrinking Phase**: A transaction may release locks but cannot acquire any new ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The point of transition between growing and shrinking is called the **LOCK POINT** — typically when the transaction has acquired all of its locks.

**2PL guarantees conflict serializability** but does **not** guarantee freedom from **deadlocks** and **cascading aborts**.

### Variants of 2PL:

| Variant | Restriction | Benefit |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Basic 2PL** | Growing then shrinking phase | Conflict serializable |
| **Conservative 2PL (Static 2PL)** | All locks acquired at the start; no locks acquired during execution | Deadlock-free |
| **Strict 2PL** | All exclusive (write) locks held until commit/abort | Cascadeless, recoverable |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | All locks (read and write) held until commit/abort | Strictest, common in practice |

## 2.7 Lock Types

- **Shared Lock (S-Lock)**: For reading. Multiple transactions can hold an S-lock on the same item simultaneously.
- **Exclusive Lock (X-Lock)**: For writing. Only one transaction can hold an X-lock; no S-locks allowed concurrently.

**Lock Compatibility Matrix:**

| Held → / Requested ↓ | **S-Lock** | **X-Lock** |
|----------------------|------------|------------|
| **S-Lock** | ✔ Granted | ✖ Denied |
| **X-Lock** | ✖ Denied | ✖ Denied |

## 2.8 Timestamp-Based Protocol (Overview)

Each transaction $T_i$ is assigned a **unique timestamp** $TS(T_i)$ at startup. Every data item $X$ is tagged with:
- **W-timestamp(X)** — largest timestamp of a transaction that successfully wrote X.
- **R-timestamp(X)** — largest timestamp of a transaction that successfully read X.

The protocol **rejects (rolls back)** any operation that would violate the chronological order of timestamps, ensuring serializability based on timestamp ordering.

## 2.9 Optimistic (Validation) Protocol (Overview)

Used in low-contention environments. Three phases per transaction:
1. **Read Phase**: Transaction executes freely, writing to a local copy.
2. **Validation Phase**: The system checks if the local copy can be safely applied without violating serializability.
3. **Write Phase**: If validation passes, local changes are applied to the database.

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the following formula sheet as a rapid reference during exam revision.**

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Boundary / Constraint |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Total possible serial schedules of $n$ transactions | $n!$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Non-serial schedules (count) | Total interleavings minus serial ones | Grows exponentially |
| Precedence Graph Edge Rule | $T_i \to T_j$ if $T_i$ op precedes $T_j$ conflicting op | Same data item, $\geq 1$ write |
| Serializable Test | Acyclic precedence graph | Conflict-serializable iff acyclic |
| 2PL Lock Point | Last lock acquired before first unlock | Must exist within transaction |
| Conservative 2PL | All locks acquired upfront | No deadlock possible |
| Strict 2PL | All X-locks held till commit/abort | Cascadeless, recoverable |
| Timestamp Read Condition | $TS(T_i) \geq$ W-timestamp$(X)$ | Else, reject and rollback $T_i$ |
| Timestamp Write Condition | $TS(T_i) \geq$ R-timestamp$(X)$ AND $TS(T_i) \geq$ W-timestamp$(X)$ | Else, reject and rollback $T_i$ |
| Lock Compatibility (S, X) | $S \cap S = S$, all else = No | Two S-locks OK on same item |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

Concurrency control is essential in:
- **Banking & Financial Systems** (ATM networks, UPI gateways)
- **Airline Reservation Systems** (millions of concurrent seat bookings)
- **E-Commerce Platforms** (inventory management during flash sales)
- **Healthcare Information Systems** (patient records updated by multiple doctors/nurses)
- **Telecommunications Billing Systems** (high-throughput call-data records)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Testing Conflict Serializability Using a Precedence Graph

### Given Schedule $S$:

| Time | Operation | Txn | Item |
|------|-----------|-----|------|
| 1 | read(A) | $T_1$ | A |
| 2 | read(B) | $T_1$ | B |
| 3 | write(A) | $T_2$ | A |
| 4 | read(A) | $T_3$ | A |
| 5 | write(A) | $T_1$ | A |
| 6 | read(B) | $T_3$ | B |
| 7 | read(B) | $T_2$ | B |
| 8 | write(B) | $T_1$ | B |

### Step 1: Identify Conflicting Operations

We look for pairs of operations on the same data item where **at least one is a write** and they belong to **different transactions**.

**For data item A:**

| Pair | Ops | Txns | Conflict Type |
|------|-----|------|---------------|
| (1, 3) | R(A), W(A) | $T_1, T_2$ | R–W |
| (1, 4) | R(A), R(A) | $T_1, T_3$ | NOT a conflict (both reads) |
| (1, 5) | R(A), W(A) | $T_1, T_1$ | Same transaction → skip |
| (3, 4) | W(A), R(A) | $T_2, T_3$ | W–R |
| (3, 5) | W(A), W(A) | $T_2, T_1$ | W–W |
| (4, 5) | R(A), W(A) | $T_3, T_1$ | R–W |

**For data item B:**

| Pair | Ops | Txns | Conflict Type |
|------|-----|------|---------------|
| (2, 6) | R(B), R(B) | $T_1, T_3$ | NOT a conflict |
| (2, 7) | R(B), R(B) | $T_1, T_2$ | NOT a conflict |
| (2, 8) | R(B), W(B) | $T_1, T_1$ | Same transaction → skip |
| (6, 7) | R(B), R(B) | $T_3, T_2$ | NOT a conflict |
| (6, 8) | R(B), W(B) | $T_3, T_1$ | R–W |
| (7, 8) | R(B), W(B) | $T_2, T_1$ | R–W |

### Step 2: Build the Precedence Graph

We draw an edge $T_i \to T_j$ for each conflict where $T_i$'s operation precedes $T_j$'s operation.

**Edges from A-conflicts:**
- $T_1 \to T_2$ (from pair 1,3: $T_1$'s R(A) precedes $T_2$'s W(A))
- $T_2 \to T_3$ (from pair 3,4: $T_2$'s W(A) precedes $T_3$'s R(A))
- $T_2 \to T_1$ (from pair 3,5: $T_2$'s W(A) precedes $T_1$'s W(A))
- $T_3 \to T_1$ (from pair 4,5: $T_3$'s R(A) precedes $T_1$'s W(A))

**Edges from B-conflicts:**
- $T_3 \to T_1$ (from pair 6,8: $T_3$'s R(B) precedes $T_1$'s W(B)) — already present
- $T_2 \to T_1$ (from pair 7,8: $T_2$'s R(B) precedes $T_1$'s W(B)) — already present

### Step 3: Compile All Edges

The complete set of unique edges:

$$
T_1 \to T_2, \quad T_2 \to T_3, \quad T_2 \to T_1, \quad T_3 \to T_1
$$

### Step 4: Detect Cycles

A **cycle** exists when we can travel from a node back to itself following directed edges.

**Cycle check for $T_1$:**
- Start at $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$ — **Yes, this is a cycle!**

**Cycle check for $T_2$:**
- Start at $T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1 \to T_2$ — **Yes, this is also a cycle!**

### Step 5: Conclusion

Since the precedence graph contains cycles, the schedule $S$ is **NOT conflict-serializable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Always explicitly state both the edge list AND the cycle. A common mistake is to identify conflicts but forget to actually test for cycles — examiners specifically look for the cycle trace.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: View Serializability

### Given Schedule $S$:

| Time | Op | Txn | Item |
|------|----|----|------|
| 1 | R(A) | $T_1$ | A |
| 2 | W(A) | $T_2$ | A |
| 3 | R(A) | $T_3$ | A |
| 4 | W(A) | $T_3$ | A |
| 5 | R(B) | $T_1$ | B |
| 6 | W(B) | $T_1$ | B |
| 7 | R(B) | $T_2$ | B |
| 8 | W(B) | $T_2$ | B |

### Step 1: Check Initial Reads (from initial database)

- $T_1$ does NOT read initial A (it reads A written by... wait, step 1 reads A, but no prior write exists). So $T_1$ reads **initial A** before $T_2$ writes A.
- $T_3$ reads A **after** $T_2$ wrote A — so $T_3$ reads from $T_2$, not from initial.

**Initial reads:** $T_1$ reads initial A, $T_1$ reads initial B.

### Step 2: Check Read-From Relationships

- $T_3$ reads A from $T_2$ (step 3 reads A written in step 2).
- $T_2$ reads B from $T_1$ (step 7 reads B written in step 6).

### Step 3: Check Final Writes

- Final write on A: $T_3$ (step 4).
- Final write on B: $T_2$ (step 8).

### Step 4: Compare with Serial Schedule $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$

- **Initial reads**: $T_1$ reads initial A & B ✔; $T_2, T_3$ would read post-$T_1$ values in serial — but in $S$, $T_2$ writes A **before** $T_1$ reads A. ✗ Mismatch.

### Step 5: Compare with Serial Schedule $T_2 \to T_1 \to T_3$

- **Initial reads**: In serial, $T_2$ would read initial A, but in $S$, $T_1$ reads initial A. ✗ Mismatch.

### Step 6: Conclusion

No serial schedule is view-equivalent to $S$. Therefore, $S$ is **NOT view-serializable** either.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Testing 2PL Compliance

### Given Transaction $T_1$:

| Step | Operation | Lock Action |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | read(A) | Lock-X(A) granted |
| 2 | A := A - 50 | (computation) |
| 3 | write(A) | (uses existing X-lock on A) |
| 4 | read(B) | Lock-X(B) granted |
| 5 | B := B + 50 | (computation) |
| 6 | write(B) | (uses existing X-lock on B) |
| 7 | commit | Unlock-X(A), Unlock-X(B) |

### Phase Analysis:

**Growing Phase (Steps 1–6):** All locks are being acquired, none released.

$$
\text{Growing phase: } [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
$$

**Shrinking Phase (Step 7):** Locks are being released, none acquired.

$$
\text{Shrinking phase: } [7]
$$

### Step: Identify the Lock Point

The **lock point** is the moment in time when the transaction has obtained all of its locks — right after the last lock is acquired.

$$
\text{Lock point of } T_1 = \text{End of Step 4 (lock-X(B))}
$$

### Step: Conclusion

This transaction **satisfies the 2PL protocol** because all locks were acquired in the growing phase (Steps 1–4) before any lock was released (Step 7).

> [!NOTE]
> If even a single unlock operation is interleaved between two lock operations, the protocol is **violated**, even if the schedule remains serializable by other means.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Schedule $S$ | Conflict-Serializable? | Recoverable? | Cascadeless? | Strict? |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------|--------------|---------|
| Serial $T_1 T_2$ | ✔ Trivially | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| A non-serial with conflict | ✔ if precedence graph acyclic | Depends on commit order | Depends | Depends |
| Blind Write schedule | ✘ conflict; ✔ view (sometimes) | — | — | — |
| Dirty-read schedule | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Transaction Start]) --> nodeB[Active State]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Partially Committed State]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Committed State]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Failed State]
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Aborted State]
    nodeD --> nodeG([Transaction End])
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A transaction begins in the *Active* state, transitions to *Partially Committed* when its last statement executes, then either moves to *Committed* (success) or *Failed* (error). From *Failed*, it moves to *Aborted* after rollback and then ends.

## 4.2 Concurrency Control Techniques — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cc[Concurrency Control Techniques]
    cc --> lockBased[Lock Based Protocols]
    cc --> tsBased[Timestamp Based Protocols]
    cc --> optBased[Optimistic Validation Based Protocols]
    cc --> mvcc[Multi Version Concurrency Control]

    lockBased --> basic2pl[Basic Two Phase Locking]
    lockBased --> cons2pl[Conservative Static Two Phase Locking]
    lockBased --> strict2pl[Strict Two Phase Locking]
    lockBased --> rigor2pl[Rigorous Two Phase Locking]

    tsBased --> toBasic[Basic Timestamp Ordering]
    tsBased --> toStrict[Strict Timestamp Ordering]

    optBased --> readPhase[Read Phase]
    optBased --> validPhase[Validation Phase]
    optBased --> writePhase[Write Phase]
```

## 4.3 Precedence Graph of Worked Example 1 (with cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1[T1] -- T1 to T2 --> t2[T2]
    t1 -- T3 to T1 --> t3[T3]
    t2 -- T2 to T3 --> t3
    t2 -- T2 to T1 --> t1
    t3 -- T3 to T1 --> t1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Cycle Trace:** Observe how $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$ forms a closed loop. The presence of this cycle in the precedence graph confirms that the schedule is **not conflict-serializable**. KTU examiners award marks specifically for explicitly tracing and labeling such a cycle.

## 4.4 Lock Compatibility Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqS([Request S-Lock]) --> mat{Matrix Lookup}
    reqX([Request X-Lock]) --> mat
    mat -- S held, S requested --> grantS([Granted])
    mat -- S held, X requested --> deny([Denied])
    mat -- X held, S requested --> deny
    mat -- X held, X requested --> deny
    mat -- Nothing held, anything --> grantS
    mat -- Nothing held, X requested --> grantX([Granted])
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Concurrency Control in a DBMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userApp[User Application Layer]
    userApp --> txMgr[Transaction Manager]
    txMgr --> conCtrl[Concurrency Control Subsystem]
    txCtrl[Concurrency Control Module] --> lockMgr[Lock Manager]
    txCtrl --> tsMgr[Timestamp Manager]
    txCtrl --> valMgr[Validation Manager]
    lockMgr --> buffer[Buffer Manager]
    tsMgr --> buffer
    valMgr --> buffer
    buffer --> disk[Disk Storage]
    recMgr[Recovery Manager] --> disk
    recMgr -.-> txMgr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The Transaction Manager coordinates with both the Concurrency Control Subsystem (to enforce isolation) and the Recovery Manager (to enforce atomicity/durability). This is the exact reason a transaction in 2PL may still need recovery mechanisms for crash handling.

## 4.6 Schedule Classification Flow (Decision Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([A Given Schedule S]) --> q1{Is S serial?}
    q1 -- Yes --> serial([Correct and recoverable])
    q1 -- No --> q2{Build precedence graph}
    q2 --> q3{Cycle in graph?}
    q3 -- No --> conflictSer([Conflict Serializable])
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Check view equivalence}
    q4 -- Yes --> viewSer([View Serializable only])
    q4 -- No --> notSer([NOT Serializable])
```

---
