# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define a transaction. List and briefly explain the ACID properties of a transaction.**

#### Model Answer:

A **transaction** is a logical unit of database processing that consists of a sequence of database operations (read, write, update, delete) which must be executed as an atomic, indivisible unit.

The four ACID properties are:

1. **Atomicity**: A transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit — either all operations succeed (commit) or none take effect (abort).
2. **Consistency**: A transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another, preserving all integrity constraints.
3. **Isolation**: The execution of a transaction appears to be isolated from other concurrent transactions, even though they may run in parallel.
4. **Durability**: Once a transaction commits, its changes are permanently stored in the database and survive any subsequent system failure.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is conflict serializability? How is it tested using a precedence graph?**

#### Model Answer:

**Conflict Serializability** is a property of a schedule where the schedule produces the same result as some serial schedule of the same transactions, with respect to the order of conflicting operations.

It is tested by constructing a **Precedence Graph**:

- Each transaction is represented as a node.
- For every pair of conflicting operations in the schedule belonging to different transactions, a directed edge is drawn from the earlier transaction to the later one.
- **The schedule is conflict-serializable if and only if the precedence graph has no cycle.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

### Question A: Concurrency Control and Serializability

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following schedule $S$ of three transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$:

| Time | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| 1 | $T_1$: read(X) |
| 2 | $T_1$: write(X) |
| 3 | $T_2$: read(X) |
| 4 | $T_2$: write(X) |
| 5 | $T_1$: read(Y) |
| 6 | $T_1$: write(Y) |
| 7 | $T_3$: read(X) |
| 8 | $T_3$: read(Y) |
| 9 | $T_3$: write(X) |

**(i)** Identify all conflicting operations between the transactions.

**(ii)** Construct the precedence graph for the schedule.

**(iii)** Is the schedule conflict-serializable? Justify with a cycle trace if not.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** protocol with its growing and shrinking phases. Discuss the **Strict 2PL** variant and explain how it avoids cascading aborts.

### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Identify Conflicts (data items X and Y)**

**For X:**
| Pair | Ops | Txns | Conflict? |
|------|-----|------|-----------|
| (1, 3) | R(X), R(X) | $T_1, T_2$ | No (both reads) |
| (1, 4) | R(X), W(X) | $T_1, T_2$ | Yes — R/W |
| (1, 9) | R(X), W(X) | $T_1, T_3$ | Yes — R/W |
| (2, 3) | W(X), R(X) | $T_1, T_2$ | Yes — W/R |
| (2, 4) | W(X), W(X) | $T_1, T_2$ | Yes — W/W |
| (2, 7) | W(X), R(X) | $T_1, T_3$ | Yes — W/R |
| (2, 9) | W(X), W(X) | $T_1, T_3$ | Yes — W/W |
| (3, 4) | R(X), W(X) | $T_2, T_2$ | Skip (same txn) |
| (3, 9) | R(X), W(X) | $T_2, T_3$ | Yes — R/W |
| (4, 7) | W(X), R(X) | $T_2, T_3$ | Yes — W/R |
| (4, 9) | W(X), W(X) | $T_2, T_3$ | Yes — W/W |
| (7, 9) | R(X), W(X) | $T_3, T_3$ | Skip |

**For Y:**
| Pair | Ops | Txns | Conflict? |
|------|-----|------|-----------|
| (5, 8) | R(Y), R(Y) | $T_1, T_3$ | No |
| (6, 8) | W(Y), R(Y) | $T_1, T_3$ | Yes — W/R |

**Step 2: Edges from Conflicts**

| Conflict (Earlier → Later) | Edge |
|---------------------------|------|
| $T_1$ R(X) before $T_2$ W(X) | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| $T_1$ R(X) before $T_3$ W(X) | $T_1 \to T_3$ |
| $T_1$ W(X) before $T_2$ R(X) | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| $T_1$ W(X) before $T_2$ W(X) | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| $T_1$ W(X) before $T_3$ R(X) | $T_1 \to T_3$ |
| $T_1$ W(X) before $T_3$ W(X) | $T_1 \to T_3$ |
| $T_2$ R(X) before $T_3$ W(X) | $T_2 \to T_3$ |
| $T_2$ W(X) before $T_3$ R(X) | $T_2 \to T_3$ |
| $T_2$ W(X) before $T_3$ W(X) | $T_2 \to T_3$ |
| $T_1$ W(Y) before $T_3$ R(Y) | $T_1 \to T_3$ |

**Step 3: Unique Edges**

$$
T_1 \to T_2, \quad T_1 \to T_3, \quad T_2 \to T_3
$$

**Step 4: Precedence Graph**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1[T1] --> nodeT2[T2]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT3[T3]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3
```

**Step 5: Cycle Check**

No cycle exists. Path $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$ is acyclic.

**Step 6: Conclusion**

The schedule $S$ **is conflict-serializable**, equivalent to the serial schedule $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$.

### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL):**

A transaction $T$ in 2PL is divided into two successive phases:

1. **Growing Phase**: The transaction acquires all the locks it needs (lock-X or lock-S on data items) but **cannot release any lock**.
2. **Shrinking Phase**: The transaction releases its locks but **cannot acquire any new lock**.

The instant of transition between the two phases is called the **lock point** of the transaction.

**2PL guarantees conflict serializability** because the order of lock points determines the serial order equivalent to the schedule.

**Strict Two-Phase Locking (Strict 2PL):**

In Strict 2PL, all **exclusive (X) locks** held by a transaction are held **until the transaction commits or aborts**. Shared locks may be released early.

**How Strict 2PL avoids cascading aborts:**

A cascading abort occurs when a transaction $T_2$ reads a value written by uncommitted $T_1$, and then $T_1$ aborts — forcing $T_2$ to also abort.

In Strict 2PL:
- Since $T_1$ holds its X-locks until commit, no other transaction can read (or write) the items $T_1$ has updated.
- Therefore, no other transaction can ever read uncommitted data.
- This eliminates the chain reaction of cascading aborts.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Basic 2PL | Conservative 2PL | Strict 2PL |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------------|
| Deadlock-free | No | Yes | No |
| Cascadeless | No | No | Yes |
| Recoverable | Yes | Yes | Yes |

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

### Question B: Transaction States, Problems, and Concurrency Control Techniques

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **transaction state diagram** clearly marking the possible transitions. Also, illustrate the **Lost Update Problem** with a concrete example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **timestamp-based concurrency control protocol**. Explain how the **Thomas Write Rule** modifies the basic timestamp ordering algorithm with a suitable example.

### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Transaction State Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Begin]) --> activeA[Active]
    activeA --> partialA[Partially Committed]
    activeA --> failedA[Failed]
    partialA --> committedA[Committed]
    partialA --> failedA
    failedA --> abortedA[Aborted]
    committedA --> endA([End])
    abortedA --> endA
```

**Explanation of States:**

- **Active**: The initial state; the transaction executes its read/write operations here.
- **Partially Committed**: Reached after the final statement has been executed; the system is about to confirm permanence.
- **Committed**: All operations have completed successfully; the changes are permanent.
- **Failed**: Normal execution can no longer proceed (e.g., due to a constraint violation).
- **Aborted**: The transaction has been rolled back, and the database is restored to its pre-transaction state.

**Lost Update Problem — Example:**

Initial value of balance $X = 1000$.

| Time | $T_1$ (Withdraw ₹200) | $T_2$ (Withdraw ₹100) |
|------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | read(X) → 1000 | — |
| 2 | — | read(X) → 1000 |
| 3 | X := X - 200 = 800 | — |
| 4 | — | X := X - 100 = 900 |
| 5 | write(X) → 800 | — |
| 6 | — | write(X) → 900 |

**Final balance: ₹900** — but the correct balance should be ₹700 (1000 − 200 − 100).

The **₹200 withdrawal by $T_1$ was overwritten by $T_2$'s update** — this is the **Lost Update Problem**.

**Resolution using 2PL**: If $T_1$ acquires an X-lock on X at time 1, $T_2$'s read(X) at time 2 is blocked until $T_1$ commits, preventing the lost update.

### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Timestamp-Based Concurrency Control Protocol:**

Each transaction $T_i$ is assigned a **unique timestamp** $TS(T_i)$ at the time it starts. For each data item $X$, the system maintains two timestamps:

- **W-timestamp(X)**: The largest timestamp of any transaction that has successfully written X.
- **R-timestamp(X)**: The largest timestamp of any transaction that has successfully read X.

**Read Operation by $T_i$ on X (Algorithm):**

1. If $TS(T_i) < \text{W-timestamp}(X)$, then $T_i$ is trying to read a value of X that was already overwritten by a younger transaction. **Reject (rollback) $T_i$** with a new timestamp.
2. Otherwise, allow the read, and set $\text{R-timestamp}(X) = \max(\text{R-timestamp}(X), TS(T_i))$.

**Write Operation by $T_i$ on X (Algorithm):**

1. If $TS(T_i) < \text{R-timestamp}(X)$, then $T_i$ is trying to write a value of X that was already read by a younger transaction. **Reject and rollback $T_i$**.
2. If $TS(T_i) < \text{W-timestamp}(X)$, then $T_i$ is trying to write an obsolete value. **Reject and rollback $T_i$**.
3. Otherwise, allow the write, and set $\text{W-timestamp}(X) = TS(T_i)$.

**Thomas Write Rule (Modification):**

Thomas observed that in the case where $TS(T_i) < \text{W-timestamp}(X)$, the write can sometimes be **safely ignored** rather than rejected, because the previous write was from a transaction with a *younger* timestamp — i.e., the newer write would have overwritten $T_i$'s write anyway.

**Modified Write Algorithm with Thomas Write Rule:**

1. If $TS(T_i) < \text{R-timestamp}(X)$, **reject and rollback** $T_i$.
2. If $TS(T_i) < \text{W-timestamp}(X)$, **ignore the write** (do not perform it, but do not rollback $T_i$ either).
3. Otherwise, perform the write and update W-timestamp.

**Example of Thomas Write Rule:**

Let $T_1$ have $TS = 1$, $T_2$ have $TS = 2$, and $T_3$ have $TS = 3$. Suppose the operations occur in this order on item X with initial value 100:

- $T_3$ writes X = 300 → W-timestamp(X) = 3
- $T_1$ writes X = 150

Without Thomas Write Rule: $TS(T_1) = 1 < \text{W-timestamp}(X) = 3$, so $T_1$ is **rejected and rolled back**.

With Thomas Write Rule: Since $T_1$ is older than the current W-timestamp, the write is **ignored** (skipped). $T_1$ is not rolled back. This preserves **view serializability** (the equivalent serial order is $T_3$ then $T_1$, but the value written by $T_1$ is never observed anyway).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse "rejected" with "ignored"** in Thomas Write Rule. Rejection = rollback the entire transaction; Ignored = skip just that write.
> - When asked "is 2PL deadlock-free?" — the answer is **NO for basic 2PL**, but **YES for conservative 2PL**. Examiners deduct marks for blanket statements.
> - In precedence graph problems, students often draw the *wrong direction* of edges. Remember: edge goes from the **earlier** conflicting transaction to the **later** one.
> - For ACID, mark-bearing keywords are **"atomic," "consistent," "isolated," "durable"** — using synonyms like "permanent" or "separate" loses marks.
> - In timestamp problems, always check **both the read condition and the write condition** for the *same* transaction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Transaction Processing & Concurrency Control**

- [x] A **transaction** is a logical unit of work; it must be **atomic, consistent, isolated, durable (ACID)**.
- [x] The transaction lifecycle has **5 states**: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted.
- [x] A **schedule** is a sequence of interleaved operations from concurrent transactions.
- [x] A schedule is **serializable** if its effect equals some serial schedule of the same transactions.
- [x] **Conflict-serializable** ⇔ **Acyclic precedence graph**. This is the most testable criterion.
- [x] Two operations **conflict** if they belong to different transactions, operate on the same data item, and at least one is a write.
- [x] **View-serializable** is a weaker (broader) criterion — every conflict-serializable schedule is view-serializable, but not vice versa.
- [x] **Recoverable** schedules ensure that if $T_j$ reads from $T_i$, then $T_i$ commits before $T_j$.
- [x] **Cascadeless** schedules: no transaction reads data from an uncommitted transaction.
- [x] **Strict** schedules: no transaction reads or writes a data item written by an uncommitted transaction.
- [x] Hierarchy: **Strict ⊂ Cascadeless ⊂ Recoverable ⊂ Serializable**.
- [x] **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** has two phases: Growing (acquire) and Shrinking (release). The lock point is the transition.
- [x] **Strict 2PL** holds all X-locks until commit/abort — guarantees cascadeless recovery.
- [x] **Conservative (Static) 2PL** acquires all locks upfront — guarantees deadlock-free execution.
- [x] **Shared (S) locks** allow concurrent reads; **Exclusive (X) locks** are exclusive for writes.
- [x] **Timestamp Ordering (TO)** uses TS, R-timestamp, and W-timestamp to enforce chronological ordering of transactions.
- [x] **Thomas Write Rule** allows ignoring (not rejecting) obsolete writes by older transactions.
- [x] **Optimistic (Validation)** protocols have three phases: Read, Validation, Write — best for low-contention environments.
- [x] The four main concurrency anomalies are: **Lost Update, Dirty Read, Unrepeatable Read, Phantom**.
- [x] $n$ transactions have $n!$ possible serial schedules.
- [x] A precedence graph edge $T_i \to T_j$ is created when $T_i$'s conflicting operation appears **before** $T_j$'s in the schedule.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transaction Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transaction**: A transaction is a **logical unit of program execution that accesses and possibly updates various data items** in a database. It is the fundamental building block of any concurrent and reliable database system, encapsulating a sequence of read and write operations that must be executed **atomically** as a single, indivisible unit.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, the **Transaction Model** defines the conceptual framework that governs how a sequence of database operations (reads and writes) is bundled, executed, monitored, and recovered. It specifies the permissible states a transaction can occupy during its life cycle, the operations allowed within it, and the guarantees (ACID properties) provided by the DBMS.

A transaction can be expressed abstractly as:

$$T_i \;:\; \text{read}(X) \;\rightarrow\; \text{write}(Y) \;\rightarrow\; \cdots \;\rightarrow\; \text{commit} \;\vert\; \text{abort}$$

where $T_i$ is the transaction identifier, $X$ and $Y$ are database data items, and the transaction ends with exactly one of two terminal outcomes: **commit** or **abort**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The ATM Cash Transfer**: Imagine you walk up to an ATM to transfer **₹5,000** from your Savings Account (Account A) to your Current Account (Account B). Two operations must happen together:
> 1. Debit ₹5,000 from Account A
> 2. Credit ₹5,000 into Account B
>
> If the system fails *between* these two steps (say, after debiting A but before crediting B), your **₹5,000 simply vanishes** into thin air. To prevent this, the bank groups both operations into a single **transaction** — a *logical unit of work*. Either **both succeed** (money moves from A to B), or **both fail** (the debit is rolled back, A is restored). This is the essence of the transaction model: it is a contract of *all-or-nothing execution*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions

> [!TIP]
> **Key Quantitative Benchmarks in Transaction Processing**:
> - **ACID Properties** = **4** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)
> - **Transaction States** = **5** (Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted)
> - **Basic Operations** = **2** (read_item, write_item)
> - **Terminal Operations** = **2** (commit, rollback/abort)
> - **Schedule Types** = **3** (Serial, Non-serial but serializable, Non-serializable)

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4, the *Transaction Model* is the **entry point** that lays the conceptual foundation for serializability (Module 4 continuation). Do not confuse the *transaction itself* with the *schedule* of transactions. A **transaction** is a single program; a **schedule** is the chronological execution of *multiple* transactions interleaved or in sequence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Transaction Model

The transaction model can be decomposed into the following structured components:

#### 2.1.1 Basic Database Access Operations
- **`read_item(X)`**: Reads the value of data item $X$ from the database into a program variable (typically local memory/buffer).
- **`write_item(X)`**: Writes the value of a program variable back into the database data item $X$.

#### 2.1.2 Transaction Termination Operations
- **`commit`**: Persists all changes made by the transaction permanently in the database. Once committed, the transaction **cannot be undone**.
- **`rollback` / `abort`**: Undoes all changes made by the transaction, restoring the database to its state *before* the transaction began.

#### 2.1.3 Transaction States (State Transition Logic)
A transaction moves through **five distinct states** during its life cycle:

1. **Active**: The initial state. Transaction stays here while it is executing read/write operations.
2. **Partially Committed**: Reached after the *final statement* of the transaction has executed (just before commit).
3. **Committed**: Reached after successful commit. All changes are permanently stored.
4. **Failed**: Reached if normal execution can no longer proceed (logical error, system crash).
5. **Aborted**: Reached after the transaction has been rolled back. The system is restored to its prior consistent state. The transaction may be restarted later, depending on the cause of failure.

#### 2.1.4 The ACID Guarantee
- **Atomicity**: Either *all* operations of the transaction are reflected in the database, or *none* are. (The "all-or-nothing" rule.)
- **Consistency**: Execution of a transaction in isolation preserves database consistency — it must transition the database from one *valid state* to another.
- **Isolation**: Even though multiple transactions may execute concurrently, each transaction must behave *as if* it were executing alone. Intermediate states of one transaction are invisible to others.
- **Durability**: Once a transaction commits, its effects on the database must *never be lost*, even in the face of subsequent system failures, crashes, or power outages.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates the high-yield conceptual "formulas" and definitions you must memorize for KTU board exams. Symbols use LaTeX `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Concept** | **Formal Definition / Expression** | **Application / When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction Symbol | $T_i = \text{sequence of read/write operations ending in commit or abort}$ | Represents a single logical unit of work |
| Read Operation | $\text{read}_i(X)$ or $r_i(X)$ | Retrieves value of item $X$ for transaction $T_i$ |
| Write Operation | $\text{write}_i(X)$ or $w_i(X)$ | Updates value of item $X$ for transaction $T_i$ |
| Commit Terminal | $\text{commit}_i$ or $c_i$ | Successful, permanent end of $T_i$ |
| Abort Terminal | $\text{abort}_i$ or $a_i$ | Unsuccessful end; rolls back $T_i$ |
| Atomicity Condition | $(\forall T_i) : T_i \text{ executes entirely OR not at all}$ | The "all-or-nothing" property |
| Isolation Property | $\text{For all } T_i, T_j \text{ concurrent} : \text{effects of } T_i \text{ are invisible to } T_j \text{ until } T_i \text{ commits}$ | Prevents dirty reads, lost updates |
| Durability Condition | $\text{If } \text{commit}_i \text{ occurs, then all writes of } T_i \text{ survive any subsequent failure}$ | Guarantees permanence post-commit |
| Transaction States | $\vert \text{Active}, \text{Partially Committed}, \text{Committed}, \text{Failed}, \text{Aborted} \vert = 5$ | Life-cycle model |
| Schedule Definition | $S = \langle \text{interleaved order of operations from } T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n \rangle$ | Represents concurrent execution |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The transaction model is the *backbone* of mission-critical engineering applications:
> - **Banking & Finance**: ATM withdrawals, NEFT/RTGS transfers, stock trading platforms
> - **E-Commerce**: Order placement, payment processing, inventory deduction (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart)
> - **Airline / Railway Reservation Systems**: Seat booking where two users cannot be sold the same seat
> - **Telecommunications**: Call detail record (CDR) updates, prepaid recharge systems
> - **Healthcare Information Systems**: Patient record updates where partial modifications are catastrophic
>
> Without a robust transaction model, modern digital infrastructure would be impossible to engineer safely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Anatomy of a Single Transaction

Consider a banking database with two accounts, $A$ and $B$, both starting with balances **₹1,000**. We want to transfer **₹100** from $A$ to $B$. A *single transaction* $T$ that performs this transfer can be written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &: \text{read}(A) \\
  &\rightarrow A := A - 100 \\
  &\rightarrow \text{write}(A) \\
  &\rightarrow \text{read}(B) \\
  &\rightarrow B := B + 100 \\
  &\rightarrow \text{write}(B) \\
  &\rightarrow \text{commit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step trace:**

1. **Step 1** — `read(A)`: Fetches the value of $A = 1000$ into a local variable.
2. **Step 2** — `A := A - 100`: Performs arithmetic in the local buffer. Local copy of $A$ becomes $900$. The database on disk is **still 1000** at this point.
3. **Step 3** — `write(A)`: Writes the new value back to the database. The on-disk value of $A$ is now $900$.
4. **Step 4** — `read(B)`: Fetches $B = 1000$.
5. **Step 5** — `B := B + 100`: Local copy of $B$ becomes $1100$.
6. **Step 6** — `write(B)`: On-disk value of $B$ becomes $1100$.
7. **Step 7** — `commit`: The transaction is declared successful. The new database state is permanently $\{A=900, B=1100\}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: If a system failure occurs at **Step 3** (after writing A but before writing B), the transaction model dictates that the DBMS must **undo the partial update** to $A$ so that the database remains in a consistent state. This is the *Atomicity* guarantee in action — the *Recovery Manager* is responsible for executing this rollback using the system log.

### 3.2 State Transition Derivation

The transition from one transaction state to another follows deterministic rules. We can formally enumerate them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Active} &\xrightarrow{\text{last statement executes}} \text{Partially Committed} \\
\text{Partially Committed} &\xrightarrow{\text{commit successful}} \text{Committed} \\
\text{Partially Committed} &\xrightarrow{\text{commit fails / crash}} \text{Failed} \\
\text{Active} &\xrightarrow{\text{error detected}} \text{Failed} \\
\text{Failed} &\xrightarrow{\text{rollback completed}} \text{Aborted}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning behind each transition:**

- **Active → Partially Committed**: Triggered immediately after the *last instruction* of $T$ is executed. The transaction is *almost* done but its effects are not yet durable.
- **Partially Committed → Committed**: Triggered only after the system successfully writes the *commit log record* to durable storage. From here, **no rollback is possible**.
- **Partially Committed → Failed**: If a crash or write failure occurs while the commit log is being written, the system may not be able to confirm success, so it transitions to Failed.
- **Active → Failed**: If a logical/programming error (e.g., division by zero, constraint violation) is detected mid-execution, the system transitions directly to Failed.
- **Failed → Aborted**: The recovery system performs a *rollback* using the log. After rollback completes, the transaction reaches the terminal Aborted state.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Each ACID Property is Required

| **Property** | **Failure Without It (Real-World Scenario)** | **What the DBMS Component Provides It** |
|---|---|---|
| Atomicity | Money vanishes during a transfer if crash occurs midway (₹500 debited, never credited) | Transaction-Manager / Recovery-Manager using system log |
| Consistency | Database ends up in a state that violates business rules (e.g., negative balance allowed) | Integrity constraints, triggers, application logic |
| Isolation | Two customers book the same airline seat simultaneously; both get confirmed tickets | Concurrency-Control Manager (locking / timestamping) |
| Durability | After confirming payment, the order is lost during a power outage | Recovery-Manager writes to non-volatile storage + log |

### 3.4 Derivation of Concurrent Execution Necessity

Suppose we have two transactions, $T_1$ and $T_2$, that each take **10 ms** of CPU time and **100 ms** of I/O disk access. If executed **serially**:

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = 2 \times (10 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms}) = 220 \text{ ms per pair of transactions}
$$

If executed **concurrently** with overlapping I/O (CPU of one overlaps with disk of another):

$$
T_{\text{concurrent}} \approx 10 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms} = 220 \text{ ms}
$$

But for **N transactions**, the improvement is more dramatic. For *N = 1000* transactions:
- Serial throughput ≈ $\frac{1000}{220 \text{ s}} \approx 4.5$ transactions/second
- Concurrent throughput (with proper overlap) can be **10×–100× higher** in real systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely *why* the transaction model must define both **isolation** (so concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other) and **serializability** (so the result equals some serial order). These are the high-yield topics that follow in the rest of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transaction State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nodeActive
    nodeActive : Active State
    nodeActive : Executing read or write operations

    nodePartial : Partially Committed
    nodePartial : Last statement executed

    nodeCommit : Committed
    nodeCommit : Changes made permanent

    nodeFailed : Failed
    nodeFailed : Normal execution cannot continue

    nodeAborted : Aborted
    nodeAborted : Rollback completed system restored

    nodeActive --> nodePartial : last statement executed
    nodePartial --> nodeCommit : commit successful
    nodePartial --> nodeFailed : commit failed or crash
    nodeActive --> nodeFailed : logical error detected
    nodeFailed --> nodeAborted : rollback complete
    nodeCommit --> [*]
    nodeAborted --> [*]
```

### 4.2 ACID Properties Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Model] --> B[Atomicity]
    A --> C[Consistency]
    A --> D[Isolation]
    A --> E[Durability]

    B --> B1[All operations succeed]
    B --> B2[Or none succeed]
    B --> B3[Provided by Recovery Manager]

    C --> C1[Database integrity preserved]
    C --> C2[Valid state to valid state]
    C --> C3[Enforced by Integrity Constraints]

    D --> D1[Concurrent execution safe]
    D --> D2[Intermediate results hidden]
    D --> D3[Provided by Concurrency Control Manager]

    E --> E1[Committed changes permanent]
    E --> E2[Survive system crashes]
    E --> E3[Provided by Recovery Manager and Stable Storage]
```

### 4.3 Transaction Execution Flow (Read–Write–Commit Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Transaction Start] --> A2[read item X]
    A2 --> A3[Local computation]
    A3 --> A4[write item X]
    A4 --> A5{More operations?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A2
    A5 -- No --> A6[Last statement executed]
    A6 --> A7[Partially Committed State]
    A7 --> A8{Commit or Abort?}
    A8 -- Commit --> A9[Committed State]
    A8 -- Abort --> A10[Aborted State]
    A9 --> A11[Transaction End]
    A10 --> A12[Rollback via System Log]
    A12 --> A11
```

### 4.4 System Component Interaction (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userApp[Application Program] --> txnMgr[Transaction Manager]
    txnMgr --> concCtrl[Concurrency Control Manager]
    txnMgr --> recMgr[Recovery Manager]
    txnMgr --> logMgr[Log Manager]
    logMgr --> sysLog[System Log on Stable Storage]
    concCtrl --> bufferMgr[Buffer Manager]
    recMgr --> bufferMgr
    bufferMgr --> diskMgr[Disk Storage Manager]
    diskMgr --> dbFiles[Database Files on Disk]
    sysLog -.-> recMgr
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram**: The *Transaction Manager* is the orchestrator. It coordinates the *Concurrency Control Manager* (for isolation), the *Recovery Manager* (for atomicity and durability), and the *Log Manager* (for persistent recording of all operations). The *Buffer Manager* mediates between in-memory and on-disk storage. This architecture is the standard textbook model (Silberschatz, Korth, Sudarshan style) for explaining how ACID is implemented in real RDBMS systems like Oracle, PostgreSQL, and MySQL InnoDB.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a transaction in the context of a DBMS. List the four ACID properties that a transaction must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A *transaction* is a logical unit of program execution that accesses and possibly updates various data items in a database. It is an atomic sequence of database operations (reads and writes) terminated by either a `commit` (success) or an `abort` (failure).

The four **ACID** properties are:
1. **Atomicity** — All operations of the transaction are executed, or none are.
2. **Consistency** — The transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another.
3. **Isolation** — Even when transactions execute concurrently, each appears to execute in isolation from others.
4. **Durability** — Once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent and survive any subsequent system failure.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks if all 4 ACID properties are correctly named with a one-line definition. Many students lose 0.5–1 mark by missing the "one consistent state to another" phrasing in Consistency.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Explain the various states a transaction can be in during its execution. State the conditions under which a transaction moves from the "Partially Committed" state to the "Committed" state.**

**Model Answer:**

A transaction can exist in one of the following **five states**:
- **Active**: The initial state, during which the transaction is executing its read and write operations.
- **Partially Committed**: The state reached after the *final statement* of the transaction has been executed.
- **Committed**: The terminal success state, after the changes are permanently recorded.
- **Failed**: The state reached when normal execution can no longer continue.
- **Aborted**: The terminal failure state, after the system has rolled back all changes.

**Transition from Partially Committed → Committed:** This transition occurs only when the transaction's *commit log record* has been successfully written to durable (non-volatile) storage. Until this point, the transaction is in a fragile "partially committed" state — if a crash occurs, the recovery system may not be able to confirm the commit and will roll back the transaction. After the commit log is safely on stable storage, the transaction transitions irreversibly to the **Committed** state.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 4 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** With a neat state transition diagram, describe the different states a transaction can be in. Explain the role of the recovery system in transitioning a transaction from the **Failed** state to the **Aborted** state. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define the **ACID** properties of a transaction. For each property, give **one real-world banking scenario** where the absence of that property would cause a problem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The transaction state diagram consists of **five states** connected by transitions as described in Section 4.1 of these notes:

1. **Active** → executes `read_item` and `write_item` operations on data items.
2. **Active → Partially Committed**: After the *final statement* of the transaction has executed.
3. **Partially Committed → Committed**: After the commit record is successfully written to stable storage.
4. **Active → Failed** OR **Partially Committed → Failed**: When a logical/programming error is detected, or a system crash occurs.
5. **Failed → Aborted**: The recovery system performs a rollback.

**Role of the Recovery System in the Failed → Aborted Transition:**

When a transaction enters the **Failed** state, the database may be in an *inconsistent* state (e.g., only some of the operations have been applied). The recovery system's role is to:
- **Step 1:** Read the *system log* in reverse chronological order to identify all operations performed by the failed transaction.
- **Step 2:** Use the *before-images* (old values) stored in the log to **undo** every write operation performed by the failed transaction.
- **Step 3:** Write an *abort log record* to mark the transaction as terminated.
- **Step 4:** Restore the database to a consistent state reflecting the *pre-transaction* values.

This is the **UNDO** phase of the recovery algorithm (the ARIES-style algorithm in modern systems uses both UNDO and REDO phases). After this rollback completes, the transaction reaches the **Aborted** state — the database is consistent, and the transaction is officially terminated.

*(Award 4 marks for the state diagram and 3 marks for the recovery system explanation.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

| **Property** | **Definition** | **Banking Scenario Where Absence Causes Problem** |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | A transaction is all-or-nothing. | A money transfer debits ₹10,000 from Account A but the system crashes before crediting Account B. Without atomicity, ₹10,000 simply disappears. |
| **Consistency** | The database moves from one valid state to another. | A loan approval transaction sets a customer's outstanding loan to a negative value. Without consistency enforcement, the bank's accounting rules are violated. |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions behave as if executed serially. | Two customers simultaneously withdraw the last ₹5,000 from a joint account. Without isolation, both reads may return a balance of ₹5,000, and both withdrawals "succeed," making the balance negative. |
| **Durability** | Committed changes survive crashes. | A customer transfers ₹50,000 to a merchant, the system confirms "Transaction Successful" (commit), but a power outage occurs. Without durability, after power is restored, the transfer is gone. |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each correctly stated definition + scenario, totaling 6 marks, plus 1 mark for overall presentation/clarity.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Explain the **read_item** and **write_item** operations of a database system. What is the significance of the *system log* in supporting the ACID properties? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how a transaction works in a banking database. Show how the **Isolation** property is essential when two concurrent money transfer transactions are executed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Read Operation (`read_item(X)`):**
- The DBMS locates the disk block containing data item $X$ and copies it into a memory buffer.
- The value is then transferred to the program variable requested by the transaction.
- This operation does *not* modify the database.

**Write Operation (`write_item(X)`):**
- The DBMS locates the disk block containing data item $X$.
- The current value of $X$ in the buffer is replaced with the new value.
- The block is marked as *dirty* (modified but not yet flushed to disk).
- The actual disk update may occur later, controlled by the buffer manager.

**Significance of the System Log in Supporting ACID:**

The *system log* is a persistent, append-only file stored on stable (non-volatile) storage. Every database operation is recorded in the log *before* it is executed (this is the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** protocol). The log supports ACID in the following ways:

- **Atomicity**: The log contains *before-images* that allow the system to UNDO partial transactions that fail.
- **Durability**: The log contains *after-images* (commit records) that allow the system to REDO committed transactions whose updates were lost in a crash.
- **Consistency & Isolation**: The log records transaction boundaries (begin, commit, abort) and lock acquisitions, which are used to verify serializability and consistent state transitions.

*(Award 2 marks for read_item, 2 marks for write_item, and 3 marks for the significance of the system log.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Example — Single Transaction (Money Transfer):**

Consider two accounts, $A$ and $B$, both with balance ₹1,000. A transaction $T$ that transfers ₹200 from $A$ to $B$ executes as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &: \text{read}(A); \; A := A - 200; \; \text{write}(A); \\
  &\quad \text{read}(B); \; B := B + 200; \; \text{write}(B); \; \text{commit};
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Isolation is Essential — Concurrent Execution Example:**

Suppose two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ execute concurrently, both transferring ₹200 from $A$ to $B$ (a bug, but possible in practice).

- If $T_1$ and $T_2$ are **not isolated**, the following interleaving may occur:
  1. $T_1$ reads $A = 1000$.
  2. $T_2$ reads $A = 1000$ (instead of $A = 800$ after $T_1$'s update).
  3. $T_1$ writes $A = 800$.
  4. $T_2$ writes $A = 800$ (overwriting $T_1$'s correct update!).
  5. $T_1$ commits.
  6. $T_2$ commits.

Final balance of $A = 800$ — but ₹400 should have been debited (₹200 twice). **Money has been created out of thin air** ($B$ is credited twice while $A$ is debited only once).

**How Isolation Solves This:**

With proper isolation (e.g., using locks), $T_2$ would be *blocked* until $T_1$ commits. $T_2$ would then read the updated value $A = 800$ and correctly compute the new $A = 600$. This is the **lost update problem**, prevented by the *Isolation* property.

*(Award 3.5 marks for the example, 2 marks for the concurrent interleaving analysis, and 1.5 marks for explaining how isolation solves the lost-update problem.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Transaction Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Partially Committed" with "Committed"**: Many students write "the transaction is committed" right after the last statement executes. This is **wrong**. After the last statement executes, the transaction is in the **Partially Committed** state. It only reaches the **Committed** state after the commit log record is safely on stable storage. *Cost: Up to 1 mark lost per occurrence.*
>
> 2. **Listing ACID properties without one-line definitions**: In 3-mark questions, the KTU board expects the *full name + one-line meaning* for each property. Writing just "A, C, I, D" without expansion will cost you at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Transaction and Schedule**: A *transaction* is a single program's operations. A *schedule* is the combined interleaving of *multiple* transactions. In Part B questions, examiners may deliberately use the term "schedule" to test whether you understand this distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the Log/Recovery System**: For questions on ACID (especially Atomicity and Durability), always mention the **system log** and **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)**. Examiners reward keywords like "log," "before-image," "after-image," and "stable storage."
>
> 5. **Wrong State Diagram Arrows**: When drawing the state diagram, do not draw an arrow directly from "Partially Committed" to "Aborted." The correct path is: **Partially Committed → Failed → Aborted**. This is a frequently tested trap.
>
> 6. **Using `commit` and `abort` interchangeably**: `commit` is a *successful* end; `abort` (or `rollback`) is a *failed* end. Never say "the transaction is committed when it fails."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Transaction Model (Module 4)**
>
> - **Transaction** = a logical unit of work; a sequence of read/write operations ending in **commit** (success) or **abort** (failure).
> - **Two basic operations**: `read_item(X)` and `write_item(X)`.
> - **Five transaction states**: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted.
> - **State transitions to remember**:
>   - Active → Partially Committed (after last statement)
>   - Partially Committed → Committed (after commit log written)
>   - Partially Committed → Failed (if commit fails or crash)
>   - Active → Failed (if logical error)
>   - Failed → Aborted (after rollback)
> - **ACID Properties**:
>   - **A**tomicity — All or nothing. *(Provided by: Recovery Manager + System Log)*
>   - **C**onsistency — Valid state to valid state. *(Provided by: Integrity Constraints + Triggers)*
>   - **I**solation — Transactions appear to run alone. *(Provided by: Concurrency Control Manager + Locks)*
>   - **D**urability — Committed changes are permanent. *(Provided by: Recovery Manager + Stable Storage)*
> - **System Log** = persistent, append-only file on non-volatile storage. Uses **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** protocol.
> - **Transaction ≠ Schedule**. A transaction is a single program; a schedule is the combined execution of multiple transactions.
> - **Concurrent execution** is needed for performance (CPU–I/O overlap), but it requires isolation to prevent anomalies like the *lost update problem*.
> - **Exam-ready keywords to drop in answers**: ACID, atomicity, partial commit, system log, WAL, before-image, after-image, recovery manager, concurrency control, isolation, durability, schedule, serial, serializable.
> - **Common real-world examples**: ATM cash withdrawal, NEFT transfer, online shopping checkout, airline ticket booking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance of Concurrency Control and Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Significance of Concurrency Control and Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Concurrency Control?

**Concurrency Control** is the set of protocols, mechanisms, and techniques used in a Database Management System (DBMS) to manage simultaneous (concurrent) access to a shared database by multiple users or transactions, ensuring that the database remains in a **consistent state** without violating data integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *"Concurrency Control is the process of coordinating concurrent transactions such that the net effect on the database is equivalent to the effect of executing these transactions serially in some order."*

## 1.2 What is Recovery in DBMS?

**Recovery** is the mechanism that ensures the database is restored to a **consistent and correct state** after any kind of failure, including transaction failures, system crashes, power outages, or media errors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *"Database Recovery is the process of restoring the database to the most recent consistent state that existed prior to the occurrence of a failure."*

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy: The Bank Counter

Imagine a **single bank account** shared by two ATMs in different cities. Two customers withdraw money at the **exact same second**.

### Without Concurrency Control (The Chaos Scenario):
- ATM A reads balance: ₹10,000
- ATM B reads balance: ₹10,000
- ATM A deducts ₹5,000 → writes back ₹5,000
- ATM B deducts ₹3,000 → writes back ₹7,000
- **Final balance: ₹7,000** (BUT ₹8,000 was actually withdrawn! The bank LOSES ₹3,000!)

This is the **Lost Update Problem**.

### Without Recovery (The Disaster Scenario):
While updating the balance, a sudden power cut occurs. The disk now contains:
- Old balance: ₹10,000 (never written fully)
- New balance: garbage values (partially written)

Without recovery, the customer's money is lost forever.

### The Solution: Concurrency Control + Recovery
- **Concurrency Control** = Traffic signals at a junction. Ensures transactions don't crash into each other.
- **Recovery** = The CCTV and accident insurance. Ensures that if something goes wrong, the system can be rewound to a safe state.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Concurrency Control and Recovery are the **TWO PILLARS** that enforce the **ACID Properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) of database transactions.

## 1.4 The Four Critical Problems Solved by Concurrency Control

| Problem | Description | Real-world impact |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Lost Update Problem** | Two transactions read and update the same data, one update is overwritten | Money vanishes from accounts |
| **Dirty Read Problem** | A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction | Decisions based on invalid data |
| **Non-Repeatable Read** | A transaction reads same data twice and gets different values | Inconsistent reports |
| **Phantom Read** | New rows appear/disappear when re-executing a query | Inventory count errors |

## 1.5 The Three Standard Metrics in Transaction Processing

- **Throughput** = Number of transactions completed per unit time (target: **maximize**)
- **Response Time** = Time taken for a single transaction (target: **minimize**)
- **Waiting Time** = Time a transaction spends in the lock queue (target: **minimize**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The significance of concurrency control is that it **maximizes throughput** (allows parallel execution) **without sacrificing consistency**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Concurrency Control Significant? (The "Why")

Concurrency Control exists to solve problems that arise due to the **Three Fundamental Goals of Database Systems** being in conflict:

1. **High Performance** → Run many transactions simultaneously (parallelism)
2. **Data Consistency** → Database integrity must be maintained
3. **User Transparency** → Users should not see each other's intermediate work

> [!NOTE]
> Without concurrency control, the database is forced to execute transactions **serially** (one after another), which is **safe but extremely slow**. Concurrency control allows **interleaving** while maintaining **serial equivalence** (the same result as serial execution).

## 2.2 The Significance Pillars (Structured Logic)

### Pillar 1: Enforcing Serializability
- A schedule is **serializable** if its effect is equivalent to some serial schedule.
- **Types of Serializability:**
  - **Conflict Serializability** (based on conflicting operations: Read-Write, Write-Read, Write-Write)
  - **View Serializability** (based on the same final database state and reads)

### Pillar 2: Preventing the Concurrency Anomalies
- Lost Update, Dirty Read, Unrepeatable Read, Phantom Read (covered in Section 1.4)

### Pillar 3: Handling Deadlocks
- **Deadlock Definition:** A state where two or more transactions are **permanently blocked**, each waiting for a resource held by the other.
- **Detection:** **Wait-for graph** contains a cycle.
- **Resolution:** Abort one transaction (the **victim**) to break the cycle.

## 2.3 Why is Recovery Significant? (The "Why")

Databases exist in a world of **failures**. Without recovery:
- The **Atomicity** property is violated (partial transactions remain in DB)
- The **Durability** property is violated (committed changes are lost)
- The **Consistency** property is violated (database enters an invalid state)

### Classification of Failures (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Failure Type | Example | Recovery Strategy |
|--------------|---------|-------------------|
| **Transaction Failure** | Logical error, abort | UNDO the transaction |
| **System Crash (Soft Failure)** | Power outage, OS crash | REDO committed, UNDO uncommitted |
| **Media Failure (Hard Failure)** | Disk head crash, fire | Restore from backup + REDO from log |
| **Communication Failure** | Network drop in distributed DB | Reconnect and resynchronize |

## 2.4 The ACID Properties — Where Significance Resides

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The significance of Concurrency Control and Recovery is best understood as the **enforcement mechanism for ACID**:

| Property | Concurrency Control Role | Recovery Role |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **A**tomicity | Schedules atomic units of work | UNDO partial transactions |
| **C**onsistency | Ensures serializable schedules | Restores consistent state |
| **I**solation | Prevents interference between txns | Ensures txns are independent |
| **D**urability | Committed data persists | REDO committed transactions |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Schedule Equivalence** | $S_1 \equiv S_2$ iff $S_1, S_2$ produce same final DB state | Logical equivalence |
| **Conflict Operations** | $R_i(X), W_j(X)$ where $i \neq j$ | $R-W, W-R, W-W$ are conflicts |
| **Precedence Graph** | Edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ | Cycle = Not Conflict Serializable |
| **Number of Schedules** | Total schedules of $n$ txns: $\dfrac{(2n)!}{2^n}$ (approx.) | Excluding aborts |
| **Serial Schedules Count** | $n!$ schedules | For $n$ transactions |
| **Log-Based Recovery** | $\text{New Value} = \text{Old Value} + \Delta$ | $\Delta$ = logged change |
| **Checkpoint Frequency** | $\text{Checkpoint every } T \text{ time units}$ | $T$ = tunable parameter |
| **Steal/No-Steal Policy** | Steal: can write to disk before commit | No-Steal: opposite |
| **Force/No-Force Policy** | Force: write all to disk on commit | No-Force: opposite |
| **Recovery Time** | $T_{recovery} = T_{UNDO} + T_{REDO}$ | Dominated by I/O |
| **Throughput** | $\text{TPS} = \dfrac{N_{txns}}{T_{total}}$ | Transactions per second |
| **Lock Compatibility** | $\text{Shared}+\text{Shared} = \text{OK}$ | $\text{Exclusive}+X = \text{Block}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** Prevents double-debit during simultaneous ATM withdrawals
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Prevents overselling of limited-stock products
- **Airline Reservation:** Ensures two users cannot book the same seat
- **Hospital Management:** Maintains patient data integrity across departments
- **Stock Trading Platforms:** Guarantees atomic buy/sell transactions
- **Distributed Databases (Google Spanner, Cassandra):** Critical for multi-node consistency

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** The "Significance" of these mechanisms in industry is quantified by the **cost of failure** — Amazon estimates a single minute of database downtime costs ~**$220,000+** (2024 estimates), making concurrency control and recovery a non-negotiable investment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Significance Mapping

This matrix links each significance aspect to its problem, mechanism, and outcome.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Problem} &\xrightarrow{\text{Motivates}} \text{Mechanism} \xrightarrow{\text{Enforces}} \text{ACID Property} \xrightarrow{\text{Yields}} \text{Outcome} \\
\text{Lost Update} &\to \text{Locking} \to \text{Isolation} \to \text{Correct Balance} \\
\text{Dirty Read} &\to \text{2PL} \to \text{Atomicity} \to \text{No Phantom Changes} \\
\text{System Crash} &\to \text{Log-based Recovery} \to \text{Durability} \to \text{No Data Loss} \\
\text{Disk Failure} &\to \text{Backup + Archive Log} \to \text{Atomicity} \to \text{Full Restoration}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Serial Execution is Insufficient

### Step 1: Define Throughput
Let $N$ be the number of transactions to be processed, and $t$ be the average time per transaction in serial mode.

$$
\text{Throughput}_{serial} = \frac{N}{N \cdot t} = \frac{1}{t}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce Concurrency
Suppose $k$ transactions can run concurrently (multi-programming level = $k$).

$$
\text{Throughput}_{concurrent} = \frac{k \cdot N}{N \cdot t} = \frac{k}{t}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Speedup Ratio
Taking the ratio of concurrent to serial throughput:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{\text{Throughput}_{concurrent}}{\text{Throughput}_{serial}} \\
&= \frac{k/t}{1/t} \\
&= k
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result
A speedup of $k$ means concurrent execution is **k times faster** than serial execution. This is precisely **why concurrency control is significant**: it allows us to **approach the theoretical speedup of $k$** while still maintaining consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of Step 4:** Without concurrency control, we cannot achieve this speedup, because we are forced into serial mode. With concurrency control (e.g., 2PL, Timestamp Ordering), we can interleave transactions safely, achieving nearly $k$-fold throughput improvement.

## 3.3 Derivation: Conflict Serializability Test Using Precedence Graph

### Step 1: Define the Precedence Graph
Given a schedule $S$ with transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, construct a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$
- An edge $T_i \to T_j$ exists iff $T_i$ executes a conflicting operation **before** $T_j$.

### Step 2: Apply the Cycle Detection Theorem
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Conflict Serializability):** A schedule $S$ is conflict serializable **if and only if** its precedence graph $G$ is **acyclic**.

### Step 3: Worked Example — Verify Serializability
**Schedule S:**
$$
S: R_1(A), R_2(A), W_1(A), R_3(A), W_2(A), W_3(A)
$$

**Step 3a: Identify Conflicts**

| Pair | Operations | Conflict? | Edge |
|------|------------|-----------|------|
| $T_1, T_2$ | $R_1(A), W_2(A)$ | Yes (R-W) | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| $T_1, T_2$ | $W_1(A), W_2(A)$ | Yes (W-W) | $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| $T_2, T_3$ | $R_3(A), W_2(A)$ | Yes (W-R) | $T_2 \to T_3$ |
| $T_1, T_3$ | $W_1(A), W_3(A)$ | Yes (W-W) | $T_1 \to T_3$ |

**Step 3b: Construct the Precedence Graph**
- Edges: $T_1 \to T_2$, $T_2 \to T_3$, $T_1 \to T_3$

**Step 3c: Detect Cycles**
- Is there a cycle? $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$? **No cycle.**
- Reverse: $T_3 \to \ldots$? **No reverse path exists.**

**Step 3d: Conclusion**
Since the precedence graph is **acyclic**, schedule $S$ is **conflict serializable**. The equivalent serial order is $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Log-Based Recovery — The REDO/UNDO Principle

### Step 1: Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Rule
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WAL Rule:** Before any database modification is written to disk, the corresponding log record **must** be written to stable storage first.

### Step 2: Define Log Records
$$
\langle T_i, X, \text{Old\_Value}, \text{New\_Value} \rangle
$$

### Step 3: Recovery Algorithm
For a system crash, the recovery manager performs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: REDO Phase} \quad &\text{— Re-apply all updates of committed txns} \\
\text{Step 2: UNDO Phase} \quad &\text{— Reverse all updates of uncommitted txns} \\
\text{Step 3: Final State} \quad &DB \to \text{Consistent State}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Why This Works (Significance Proof)
- **Atomicity:** UNDO ensures aborted txns leave no trace
- **Durability:** REDO ensures committed txns are never lost
- **Idempotency:** Both operations are idempotent — applying them multiple times yields the same result, which is critical because recovery itself may fail

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Concurrency Control vs. Recovery

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concurrency Control} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Recovery} \\
\text{Forward-looking} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Backward-looking} \\
\text{Prevents future anomalies} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Repasts past failures} \\
\text{Operates during execution} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Operates after crash} \\
\text{Uses locks, timestamps} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Uses logs, checkpoints} \\
\text{Enforces Isolation} &\quad\text{vs.}\quad \text{Enforces Atomicity \& Durability}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Significance Synthesis:** Concurrency Control is the **proactive shield**; Recovery is the **reactive armor**. Together, they form a complete transaction management system. **KTU 2024 expects students to articulate this duality clearly in answers.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
