## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Proof: Why Atomicity Requires Logging

**Problem Statement:** Show that without a **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)**, atomicity cannot be guaranteed for a transaction $T$ that performs $k$ write operations.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

Let a transaction $T$ perform the following sequence:

$$
T: \quad r(X), \, w(X), \, w(Y), \, w(Z), \, c
$$

where $c$ denotes the commit. Suppose the system crashes **between** two of the write operations. Without logging, the database state on disk is **inconsistent** — some writes have been flushed, others not.

We define the **partial-effect problem** formally:

$$
\text{Effect}(T) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} w_i(\text{item}_i)
$$

If the crash occurs after the $j$-th write ($1 < j < k$), the visible effect is:

$$
\text{Effect}_{visible}(T) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{j} w_i(\text{item}_i), \quad j < k
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Effect}_{visible}(T) \subsetneq \text{Effect}(T)
$$

This violates atomicity because the transaction is "half-done." Therefore, the system must record the **intent to write** in a stable log **before** the actual write hits disk. This is the essence of **Write-Ahead Logging**.

$$
\boxed{\text{WAL Rule: Log record for } w_i(X) \text{ must be flushed to disk BEFORE } X \text{ itself is written.}}
$$

### 3.2 Detailed ACID Demonstration Using a Sample Transaction

Consider a banking scenario with two accounts: $A$ and $B$, with initial balances $A = 1000$, $B = 0$.

**Transaction $T$:** Transfer ₹1000 from $A$ to $B$.

$$
T = \{r(A), \, w(A \leftarrow A - 1000), \, r(B), \, w(B \leftarrow B + 1000), \, c\}
$$

**Trace Through ACID:**

**Step 1 — Atomicity Check:**
$$
\text{Either} \quad A = 0, B = 1000 \quad \text{(success)}, \quad \text{or} \quad A = 1000, B = 0 \quad \text{(no change)}
$$

There is **no partial state** allowed. If the system fails after the first write, an **UNDO** operation restores $A = 1000$.

**Step 2 — Consistency Check:**
$$
\text{Total money} = A + B = 1000 \quad \text{(invariant)} \quad \text{before } T
$$
$$
\text{Total money} = A + B = 0 + 1000 = 1000 \quad \text{(invariant)} \quad \text{after } T
$$

The sum-consistency constraint $\Sigma = A + B$ is preserved.

**Step 3 — Isolation Check:**

Suppose a concurrent transaction $T'$ reads $A$ *between* the two writes of $T$. The two possible interleavings yield:

- **Interleaving 1:** $T$ completes, then $T'$ reads → $T'$ sees $A = 0$.
- **Interleaving 2:** $T'$ reads first → $T'$ sees $A = 1000$.

Without isolation, $T'$ would see $A = 0$ (after first write) and $B = 0$ (before second write), creating an illusion that **money vanished**. Isolation prevents this.

**Step 4 — Durability Check:**
$$
\text{Once } c_i \text{ is written to the log on disk,} \quad A = 0, B = 1000 \text{ is permanent.}
$$

Even if the system crashes immediately after, the **REDO** operation during recovery replays the log to restore this state.

### 3.3 Concurrency Problem Matrix — Where Isolation Fails

| Problem | Cause | ACID Property Violated | Countermeasure |
|---------|-------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Lost Update Problem** | Two transactions read and write the same item concurrently | Isolation | Two-Phase Locking (2PL) |
| **Dirty Read Problem** | $T_2$ reads uncommitted data from $T_1$, which then aborts | Isolation & Atomicity | Strict 2PL, Isolation levels |
| **Non-Repeatable Read** | $T_2$ reads $X$ twice, gets different values due to $T_1$ update | Isolation | Repeatable Read isolation level |
| **Phantom Read** | New rows appear in a re-executed range query | Isolation | Serializable isolation level |
| **Incorrect Summary** | Aggregate query reads data being modified | Isolation | Snapshot Isolation |
| **Write Skew** | Concurrent transactions read each other's writes | Isolation & Consistency | Serializable scheduling |

### 3.4 Two-Phase Locking (2PL) — The Mechanism Behind Isolation

The **Two-Phase Locking Protocol** is the most common technique used to guarantee the **Isolation** property. It has two phases:

$$
\text{Phase 1 (Growing)}: \text{Acquire all locks, no releases.}
$$
$$
\text{Phase 2 (Shrinking)}: \text{Release all locks, no acquisitions.}
$$

Mathematically, for any transaction $T$:

$$
\forall t_1, t_2 \text{ in execution of } T: \quad \text{if } \text{unlock}(T, t_1) \text{ before } \text{lock}(T, t_2), \text{ then } t_2 \le t_1.
$$

In simpler terms: **once $T$ releases its first lock, it cannot acquire any new lock**. This guarantees **conflict-serializability**, which is the formal definition of isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2PL guarantees serializability but does NOT prevent deadlocks.** The DBMS uses techniques like wait-die, wound-wait, or timeout-based detection to handle deadlocks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a transaction in DBMS. List the four desirable properties of a transaction (ACID).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **transaction** is a logical unit of work in a database that consists of one or more database access operations such as read, write, update, or delete, treated as an atomic unit.

The four desirable properties of a transaction are collectively known as **ACID properties**:

1. **Atomicity** — The transaction executes as a single, indivisible unit. Either all operations succeed or none take effect.
2. **Consistency** — A transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another, preserving all integrity constraints.
3. **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions execute as if they were running serially, without interfering with each other.
4. **Durability** — Once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent and survive system failures.

*(Word limit as per KTU: 80–100 words. Each property carries ~0.5 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the role of the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) in ensuring atomicity of a transaction.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** is a recovery mechanism used by the DBMS to guarantee the **Atomicity** property. The rule is simple but strict:

> *Before any modification of a database item $X$ is written to disk, the log record describing the change must first be flushed to non-volatile storage.*

**Working Mechanism:**

1. When a transaction $T$ issues a write $w_i(X)$, a log record `<T, X, old_value, new_value>` is first appended to the log buffer and flushed to disk.
2. Only after the log is safely persisted is $X$ itself updated on disk.
3. If a crash occurs mid-transaction, the recovery manager uses the log to perform an **UNDO** for uncommitted transactions, rolling back partial changes.
4. If a crash occurs after commit, **REDO** operations restore committed changes.

This ensures that **no partial transaction state** ever becomes permanent, satisfying the "all or nothing" requirement of atomicity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Description Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, Module-level Part B questions offer **internal choice**. We present **Question A** and **Question B** as two independent alternatives.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Comprehensive Theory + Scenario*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the ACID properties of a transaction. Explain **Atomicity** and **Durability** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following two concurrent transactions operating on bank accounts $A$ and $B$ where initial values are $A = 1000$, $B = 500$:

$$
T_1: \quad r_1(A), \, w_1(A \leftarrow A - 100), \, r_1(B), \, w_1(B \leftarrow B + 100)
$$
$$
T_2: \quad r_2(B), \, w_2(B \leftarrow B - 200), \, r_2(A), \, w_2(A \leftarrow A + 200)
$$

Demonstrate how the **Isolation** property is violated without proper concurrency control. Which isolation-related problem occurs here? *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution:**

**(a) ACID Properties — Definition and Examples (7 marks):**

The four desirable properties of a transaction — collectively called **ACID** — are:

**1. Atomicity:** A transaction is an atomic (indivisible) unit. Either all of its operations execute and commit, or none take effect.

- **Example:** In a fund transfer of ₹5000 from Account A to Account B, both the debit (A = A − 5000) and the credit (B = B + 5000) must happen. If the system fails after the debit but before the credit, atomicity demands an **UNDO** to restore A.

**2. Consistency:** A transaction must map the database from one **valid consistent state** to another, preserving all integrity rules (e.g., `total_balance = constant`).

- **Example:** If a CHECK constraint enforces `balance ≥ 0`, a transaction attempting to withdraw more than the available balance is aborted to preserve consistency.

**3. Isolation:** Concurrent transactions must appear to execute serially. The intermediate states of one transaction are invisible to others.

- **Example:** Two bank tellers processing the same account must not see each other's half-completed updates.

**4. Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent — surviving crashes, power loss, or disk errors.

- **Example:** A confirmed online hotel booking must not vanish if the server crashes 5 seconds after the confirmation.

**Mechanism for Atomicity & Durability:** Both are guaranteed by the **Recovery Manager** using the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)**. The WAL rule is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Log record flushed to disk} \;\; \text{BEFORE} \;\; \text{Data item written to disk}}
$$

For atomicity, on failure, the recovery manager executes **UNDO** on the log. For durability, on crash recovery, it executes **REDO** from the log.

---

**(b) Isolation Violation — Analysis (7 marks):**

**Initial State:** $A = 1000$, $B = 500$. So total money = $1500$.

**Transaction $T_1$:** Transfers ₹100 from $A$ to $B$.
**Transaction $T_2$:** Transfers ₹200 from $B$ to $A$.

**Possible Interleaving (Without Isolation):**

| Step | Operation | $A$ | $B$ | Active Transaction |
|------|-----------|-----|-----|---------------------|
| 1 | $r_1(A)$ | 1000 | 500 | $T_1$ |
| 2 | $w_1(A \leftarrow 900)$ | 900 | 500 | $T_1$ |
| 3 | $r_2(B)$ | 900 | 500 | $T_2$ |
| 4 | $w_2(B \leftarrow 300)$ | 900 | 300 | $T_2$ |
| 5 | $r_1(B)$ | 900 | 300 | $T_1$ |
| 6 | $w_1(B \leftarrow 400)$ | 900 | 400 | $T_1$ |
| 7 | $r_2(A)$ | 900 | 400 | $T_2$ |
| 8 | $w_2(A \leftarrow 1100)$ | 1100 | 400 | $T_2$ |

**Final State:** $A = 1100$, $B = 400$. Total = $1500$ (sum is preserved).

**However, the problem is the Lost Update:**

- $T_1$ read $A = 1000$ and wrote $A = 900$.
- $T_2$ later **overwrote** $A$ with $1100$ based on its own read of $A = 900$ (after $T_1$'s write), effectively **erasing $T_1$'s debit**.

Wait — in this case, the operations are actually overwriting in a way that *seems* to preserve the sum. Let me re-examine carefully.

**Correct Analysis:** The classic lost update occurs when both transactions read the **same initial value** and both write back. Let's adjust:

Suppose $T_2$ reads $A$ first (before $T_1$ writes to it):

| Step | Operation | $A$ | $B$ |
|------|-----------|-----|-----|
| 1 | $r_2(A)$ | 1000 | 500 |
| 2 | $r_1(A)$ | 1000 | 500 |
| 3 | $w_1(A \leftarrow 900)$ | 900 | 500 |
| 4 | $w_2(A \leftarrow 1200)$ | 1200 | 500 |

Here, $T_1$'s debit of $100$ is **lost** — $A$ should reflect both $T_1$'s debit and $T_2$'s credit, but $T_1$'s write was overwritten by $T_2$.

**Isolation Property Violated:** Yes — $T_1$ and $T_2$ interfere with each other's reads and writes. The result depends on the **interleaving order**, which is non-deterministic.

**Concurrency Problem Identified:** **Lost Update Problem** (a direct violation of Isolation).

**Solution:** Use **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** — when $T_1$ reads $A$, it acquires a shared lock; before writing, it upgrades to an exclusive lock. This blocks $T_2$ from reading $A$ until $T_1$ commits, enforcing a serial order.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice — Mechanism-Focused*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Isolation** and **Consistency** properties in detail. How does the DBMS enforce isolation in practice? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the schedule $S$:

$$
S = r_1(X), \, r_2(X), \, w_1(X), \, r_3(X), \, w_2(X), \, c_2, \, w_3(X), \, c_3, \, c_1
$$

Determine whether this schedule is **conflict-serializable**. If yes, identify the equivalent serial order. Justify using the **precedence graph** method. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Isolation and Consistency (7 marks):**

**Consistency Property:**

A transaction must preserve database consistency. The database has a set of **integrity constraints** — primary key, foreign key, unique, check, not null, and triggers. A transaction is consistent if:

- It begins with a **consistent state** of the database.
- It executes without violating any constraint.
- It terminates in a **consistent state**.

If any constraint is violated mid-execution, the transaction must be aborted (the DBMS detects this via the **Integrity Constraint Checker**). For example, if a transaction tries to set `age = -5` on a person record with a CHECK constraint `age >= 0`, it is rejected.

**Isolation Property:**

When multiple transactions execute concurrently, the DBMS must give each one the **illusion of being the sole user**. This is called **Isolation**. In reality, the DBMS may interleave operations of multiple transactions for performance — but the *effect* must be equivalent to some serial execution.

**How DBMS Enforces Isolation:**

1. **Locking (Two-Phase Locking):** Transactions acquire locks on data items before reading/writing. 2PL ensures conflict-serializability.

2. **Timestamp Ordering:** Each transaction gets a unique timestamp. Conflicts are resolved based on timestamp order — older transactions get priority.

3. **Multiversion Concurrency Control (MVCC):** Multiple versions of a data item are kept. Readers see a snapshot, writers create new versions. Used in PostgreSQL and Oracle.

4. **Optimistic Concurrency Control:** Transactions execute without locks, validate at commit, and abort if conflicts are detected.

5. **Isolation Levels:** SQL provides tunable isolation — `READ UNCOMMITTED < READ COMMITTED < REPEATABLE READ < SERIALIZABLE`. Higher levels prevent more anomalies but reduce concurrency.

---

**(b) Conflict-Serializability Test Using Precedence Graph (7 marks):**

**Schedule $S$:**
$$
r_1(X), \, r_2(X), \, w_1(X), \, r_3(X), \, w_2(X), \, c_2, \, w_3(X), \, c_3, \, c_1
$$

**Step 1: Identify Conflicting Operations**

Conflicts occur when two operations from different transactions access the same item, and at least one is a write.

| Conflict Pair | Transactions | Reason |
|---------------|--------------|--------|
| $r_2(X), w_1(X)$ | $T_2 \to T_1$ | Read-Write on $X$ |
| $r_3(X), w_1(X)$ | $T_3 \to T_1$ | Read-Write on $X$ |
| $w_1(X), w_2(X)$ | $T_1 \to T_2$ | Write-Write on $X$ |
| $r_3(X), w_2(X)$ | $T_3 \to T_2$ | Read-Write on $X$ |
| $w_2(X), w_3(X)$ | $T_2 \to T_3$ | Write-Write on $X$ |
| $w_1(X), w_3(X)$ | $T_1 \to T_3$ | Write-Write on $X$ |

**Step 2: Build the Precedence Graph**

The edges are: $T_2 \to T_1$, $T_3 \to T_1$, $T_1 \to T_2$, $T_3 \to T_2$, $T_2 \to T_3$, $T_1 \to T_3$.

Notice that we have **cycles**:
- $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$ (cycle of length 3)
- $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_1$ via direct edge $T_1 \to T_2$ and $T_2 \to T_1$

Since the precedence graph contains a **cycle**, the schedule $S$ is **NOT conflict-serializable**.

**Conclusion:** The schedule $S$ violates the **Isolation** property, since its effect cannot be reproduced by any serial execution of $T_1, T_2, T_3$. To make it serializable, a concurrency control protocol like **2PL** must be applied.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the four properties:** Many students write "ACID" but cannot describe how each property is *enforced* mechanically. Marks are awarded for the **mechanism** (WAL, 2PL, recovery), not just definitions.
> 2. **Ignoring the conflict detection in serialization:** When asked about conflict-serializability, students often skip the precedence graph step. KTU specifically awards 3–4 marks for the **graph construction**.
> 3. **Mixing up Lost Update with Dirty Read:** These are distinct problems. *Lost Update* = two transactions write, one's write is overwritten. *Dirty Read* = reading uncommitted data that gets rolled back.
> 4. **Forgetting Write-Ahead Log Rule:** When explaining Atomicity, the WAL rule ("log first, then data") is the **technical justification** — without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring 2PL deadlock caveat:** 2PL guarantees serializability but **does not guarantee deadlock-freedom**. Many students incorrectly state the opposite.
> 6. **Missing isolation levels in SQL:** The four SQL isolation levels (`READ UNCOMMITTED`, `READ COMMITTED`, `REPEATABLE READ`, `SERIALIZABLE`) are high-yield, especially for Part A questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Desirable Properties of Transactions (ACID)**

- ✅ **ACID** = **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability.
- ✅ A **transaction** is a logical unit of work — either fully executed or fully rolled back.
- ✅ Transaction states: **Active → Partially Committed → Committed** (success path) or **Active → Failed → Aborted** (failure path).
- ✅ **Atomicity** → "All or nothing." Implemented via **Log-Based UNDO** and **Write-Ahead Logging**.
- ✅ **Consistency** → DB moves between valid states. Enforced by **integrity constraints** and the **transaction program logic**.
- ✅ **Isolation** → Concurrent transactions behave as if serial. Enforced by **Concurrency Control** — 2PL, Timestamp Ordering, MVCC.
- ✅ **Durability** → Committed data persists. Implemented via **Log-Based REDO** and **Checkpointing**.
- ✅ **WAL Rule:** Log record for any change must be **flushed to disk before the data item itself**.
- ✅ **Concurrency Problems:** Lost Update, Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read, Write Skew.
- ✅ **2PL Phases:** Growing (acquire locks) and Shrinking (release locks). No new locks can be acquired once a release happens.
- ✅ **2PL caveat:** Guarantees **conflict-serializability** but **not deadlock-freedom**.
- ✅ **SQL Isolation Levels (lowest to highest):** `READ UNCOMMITTED < READ COMMITTED < REPEATABLE READ < SERIALIZABLE`.
- ✅ **Precedence Graph Test:** If the graph has a cycle → **NOT conflict-serializable**; **Isolation** is violated.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** ATM withdrawal, online shopping cart, ticket booking — all rely on ACID guarantees.
- ✅ **Common KTU question type:** "Explain the ACID properties with examples" — expect 7 to 14 marks, always tie each property to its enforcement mechanism.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Desirable Properties of Transactions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Serial Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)**, a **Schedule (History)** is defined as an ordered sequence of database operations belonging to one or more concurrent transactions, executed in a specific time order. Formally, a schedule `S` is a linear ordering of the read, write, commit, and abort operations of `n` transactions `T1, T2, ..., Tn` such that the relative order of operations within each individual transaction is preserved.

A **Serial Schedule** is a special category of schedule in which the operations of all transactions are executed **one after another in a strict non-overlapping (atomic-at-the-transaction-level) sequence**. In a serial schedule, the first transaction completes all of its operations (read, write, commit) entirely before the next transaction begins any operation. There is **zero interleaving** of operations from different transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight**
> *"A schedule S of n transactions T1, T2, ..., Tn is called serial if, for each transaction Ti, all the operations of Ti are executed consecutively in the schedule without any interleaved operations belonging to other transactions."*

Mathematically, a serial schedule of `n` transactions is any permutation `σ` of `{T1, T2, ..., Tn}` such that:

$$S_\sigma = (\text{all ops of } T_{\sigma(1)}) \;\cup\; (\text{all ops of } T_{\sigma(2)}) \;\cup\; \dots \;\cup\; (\text{all ops of } T_{\sigma(n)})$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Bank Teller Window Analogy:**

Imagine a bank has **3 customers** standing in line at a single teller counter. Each customer needs to perform multiple operations (withdraw cash, deposit cheque, check balance, update passbook). 

- **Serial Schedule** = Customer 1 enters the counter, does ALL their operations, then leaves. Only after Customer 1 is completely done, Customer 2 enters and does ALL their operations. Then Customer 3. The transactions are **strictly sequential**, like a relay race where one runner finishes the entire lap before the next runner starts.

- **Non-Serial Schedule** = Customer 1 starts, hands over a form, the teller switches to Customer 2 mid-way, then comes back to Customer 1, then switches again. This is **interleaving** (the topic of our next module).

The serial schedule is the "ideal safe mode" — it guarantees that the database will never face conflicts because only one transaction is active at any instant.

### 1.3 Standard Notations Used

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `r_i(X)` | Transaction `Ti` **reads** data item `X` |
| `w_i(X)` | Transaction `Ti` **writes** data item `X` |
| `c_i` | Transaction `Ti` **commits** |
| `a_i` | Transaction `Ti` **aborts** |

The notation `op_i` represents a database operation belonging to transaction `i`. For a schedule to be **serial**, the operations of each transaction must appear as a **contiguous block** in the schedule with no foreign operations interspersed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Serial schedules are the **gold standard of correctness** in DBMS. The concurrency control protocols (locking, timestamping, etc.) aim to produce schedules that are **equivalent to some serial schedule** (called *serializable schedules*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Properties of a Serial Schedule

A schedule `S` is serial if and only if it satisfies **all** of the following structural properties simultaneously:

1. **Transaction Contiguity (Atomic Block Property):** For every transaction `Ti` appearing in schedule `S`, the set of all operations of `Ti` forms a **contiguous subsequence** of `S`. Formally:

   $$\forall i, j : (\text{op}_i \prec \text{op}_j \text{ in } S) \Rightarrow i = j$$

   In plain English: no operation of `Ti` appears between any two operations of `Tj` when `i ≠ j`.

2. **Order Preservation per Transaction:** The relative order of operations within a single transaction is preserved (this is trivially true since each transaction defines a total order on its own operations).

3. **No Cross-Transaction Interleaving:** The execution timeline shows **no overlaps** between the active intervals of any two different transactions.

4. **Two-Way Serializability (Trivially True):** Any serial schedule is automatically:
   - Conflict-serializable (by definition, since there are no conflicts to analyze)
   - View-serializable
   - Recoverable
   - Cascadeless
   - Strict

### 2.2 Number of Possible Serial Schedules

For `n` transactions operating on the same database, the total number of distinct serial schedules is exactly `n!` (n factorial), since each transaction can be placed in any position relative to the others.

| Number of Transactions (n) | Number of Serial Schedules (n!) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 |
| 3 | 6 |
| 4 | 24 |
| 5 | 120 |
| **n** | **n!** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"For 4 concurrent transactions, how many serial schedules are possible?"* — The answer is **24**. This is a 2-mark sure-shot question.

### 2.3 Why Serial Schedules Are Considered "Correct"

Every serial schedule maintains database consistency because:

- **Isolation is absolute**: Each transaction operates on a database state that is either the initial state or the result of a previously completed transaction. There is no "mid-flight" visibility of uncommitted data.
- **No Lost Update**: Since only one writer is active, no two transactions can simultaneously modify the same data item.
- **No Dirty Read**: Since transactions do not overlap, no transaction can read data written by an uncommitted transaction.
- **No Unrepeatable Read**: The database state remains stable for the entire duration of a transaction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Serial Schedules | $N_{serial} = n!$ | For `n` distinct transactions |
| Active Intervals | $[t_{start}(T_i), t_{end}(T_i)]$ | Disjoint for serial schedules |
| Contiguity Check | $\forall i \neq j : \text{ops}(T_i) \cap \text{ops}(T_j) = \emptyset$ in timeline | No overlapping operations |
| Recoverability | Always satisfied | Trivially true |
| Cascadeless Property | Always satisfied | Trivially true |
| Strictness | Always satisfied | Trivially true |
| Serializability (Conflict) | Always satisfied | Vacuously true |
| Serializability (View) | Always satisfied | Vacuously true |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Serial schedules, while safe, are **impractical in real systems** because they destroy throughput. Modern DBMS engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle) use:

- **Lock-Based Protocols** (2PL, Strict 2PL)
- **Timestamp Ordering**
- **Multiversion Concurrency Control (MVCC)**

…to execute transactions in parallel while producing schedules **equivalent to some serial schedule**. The set of all serial schedules forms the **correctness criterion** against which all concurrent executions are validated. The "Serial Equivalence Theorem" states:

> *"A schedule S is correct if and only if it produces the same final database state and intermediate read-from relationships as some serial schedule."*

This theorem is the **bedrock of all concurrency control theory** taught in Module 4 of the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: Identifying a Serial Schedule

Let us work through a rigorous case study. Consider two transactions `T1` and `T2` operating on database items `A` and `B`:

**Transaction Definitions:**

$$T_1 : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; c_1$$
$$T_2 : r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; c_2$$

**Candidate Schedule S1:**

$$S_1 : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; c_1;\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; c_2$$

**Step-by-Step Verification:**

| Step | Operation | Transaction | Check |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $r_1(A)$ | T1 | First op of T1 ✓ |
| 2 | $w_1(A)$ | T1 | Contiguous to step 1 ✓ |
| 3 | $r_1(B)$ | T1 | Contiguous to step 2 ✓ |
| 4 | $w_1(B)$ | T1 | Contiguous to step 3 ✓ |
| 5 | $c_1$ | T1 | Final op of T1 ✓ |
| 6 | $r_2(A)$ | T2 | First op of T2 — starts AFTER T1 ends ✓ |
| 7 | $w_2(A)$ | T2 | Contiguous to step 6 ✓ |
| 8 | $r_2(B)$ | T2 | Contiguous to step 7 ✓ |
| 9 | $w_2(B)$ | T2 | Contiguous to step 8 ✓ |
| 10 | $c_2$ | T2 | Final op of T2 ✓ |

**Conclusion:** `S1` is a **serial schedule** in the order `T1 → T2`.

---

**Candidate Schedule S2:**

$$S_2 : r_1(A);\; r_2(A);\; w_1(A);\; w_2(A);\; r_1(B);\; r_2(B);\; w_1(B);\; w_2(B);\; c_1;\; c_2$$

**Step-by-Step Verification:**

| Step | Operation | Transaction | Issue |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $r_1(A)$ | T1 | Start of T1 |
| 2 | $r_2(A)$ | T2 | **INTERLEAVED** — violates contiguity ✗ |
| 3 | $w_1(A)$ | T1 | T1 ops are split by T2 op |

**Conclusion:** `S2` is a **non-serial (interleaved) schedule** because operations of `T1` and `T2` are interspersed. The T1 block is broken into 3 pieces: steps (1, 3, 5, 7) — a clear violation of the contiguity property.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Enumeration: All Serial Schedules for n=3

For three transactions `T1`, `T2`, `T3`, the number of possible serial schedules is `3! = 6`. Let us enumerate them completely:

| Order # | Serial Schedule Notation | Sequence |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $S_{123}$ | All T1 ops → All T2 ops → All T3 ops |
| 2 | $S_{132}$ | All T1 ops → All T3 ops → All T2 ops |
| 3 | $S_{213}$ | All T2 ops → All T1 ops → All T3 ops |
| 4 | $S_{231}$ | All T2 ops → All T3 ops → All T1 ops |
| 5 | $S_{312}$ | All T3 ops → All T1 ops → All T2 ops |
| 6 | $S_{321}$ | All T3 ops → All T2 ops → All T1 ops |

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\text{Set of all serial schedules} = \{\sigma \in S_n : S_\sigma = T_{\sigma(1)} \rightarrow T_{\sigma(2)} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow T_{\sigma(n)}\}$$

where $S_n$ is the symmetric group on `n` elements, and $\sigma$ is a permutation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties Comparison

This is the **single most important matrix for KTU exams** comparing serial schedules against non-serial schedules:

| Property | Serial Schedule | Non-Serial (Interleaved) Schedule |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Interleaving of operations | **None** | Present |
| Number of possible schedules (for `n` transactions) | $n!$ | $\gg n!$ (exponentially larger) |
| Conflict-Serializability | **Trivially Yes** | Not guaranteed — must be tested |
| View-Serializability | **Trivially Yes** | Not guaranteed — must be tested |
| Recoverability | **Always Yes** | Not guaranteed |
| Cascadelessness | **Always Yes** | Not guaranteed |
| Strictness | **Always Yes** | Not guaranteed |
| Throughput (Operations/sec) | **Low** | High |
| Database Consistency | **Preserved by construction** | Must be verified |
| Real-World Usage | Theoretical benchmark | Used in production DBMS |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: 4 Transactions

**Problem (KTU Typical):** *"How many serial schedules are possible for 4 concurrent transactions? List them symbolically."*

**Solution:**

The number of serial schedules is given by:

$$N_{serial} = 4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24$$

Symbolic enumeration of the 24 schedules:

$$S_{1234}, S_{1243}, S_{1324}, S_{1342}, S_{1423}, S_{1432}$$
$$S_{2134}, S_{2143}, S_{2314}, S_{2341}, S_{2413}, S_{2431}$$
$$S_{3124}, S_{3142}, S_{3214}, S_{3241}, S_{3412}, S_{3421}$$
$$S_{4123}, S_{4132}, S_{4213}, S_{4231}, S_{4312}, S_{4321}$$

**Total = 24 serial schedules** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Serial Schedule Execution Timeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **disjoint active intervals** of transactions in a serial schedule of 3 transactions T1, T2, T3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph timeline["EXECUTION TIMELINE (Time flows left to right)"]
        direction LR
        t1_block["T1 ACTIVE INTERVAL\nr1, w1, c1\nBLOCK BOUNDARY"]:::t1style
        boundary1{{SEQUENTIAL BOUNDARY\nT1 commits before T2 starts}}:::boundary
        t2_block["T2 ACTIVE INTERVAL\nr2, w2, c2\nBLOCK BOUNDARY"]:::t2style
        boundary2{{SEQUENTIAL BOUNDARY\nT2 commits before T3 starts}}:::boundary
        t3_block["T3 ACTIVE INTERVAL\nr3, w3, c3\nBLOCK BOUNDARY"]:::t3style
    end

    t1_block --> boundary1 --> t2_block --> boundary2 --> t3_block

    classDef t1style fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef t2style fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef t3style fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef boundary fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Serial vs Non-Serial Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Multiple Transactions\nT1, T2, T3\narriving concurrently"]:::input

    B{"CONCURRENCY\nCONTROLLER\nDecision Point"}:::decision

    C["SERIAL SCHEDULE\nT1 ENTIRELY → T2 ENTIRELY → T3 ENTIRELY\nNo Interleaving\nSafe but Slow"]:::serial
    D["NON-SERIAL SCHEDULE\nr1, r2, w1, w2, r3, c1, c2, c3\nInterleaved Operations\nFast but Risk of Conflict"]:::nonserial

    E["FINAL DATABASE STATE\nConsistent"]:::good
    F["FINAL DATABASE STATE\nMay be Inconsistent\nNeeds Serializability Test"]:::bad

    A --> B
    B -->|"Strict Mode\n(e.g., Single-User DB)"| C
    B -->|"High-Performance Mode\n(e.g., Multi-User DBMS)"| D

    C --> E
    D --> F

    classDef input fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef serial fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef nonserial fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef good fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef bad fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Operation-Level Diagram: Step-by-Step Serial Execution

```mermaid
gantt
    title Serial Schedule Gantt Chart - T1 then T2
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section T1
    r1_A   :t1op1, 0, 1
    w1_A   :t1op2, 1, 1
    r1_B   :t1op3, 2, 1
    w1_B   :t1op4, 3, 1
    c1     :t1op5, 4, 1
    section Boundary
    Committed :crit, boundary, 5, 1
    section T2
    r2_A   :t2op1, 6, 1
    w2_A   :t2op2, 7, 1
    r2_B   :t2op3, 8, 1
    w2_B   :t2op4, 9, 1
    c2     :t2op5, 10, 1
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Is the Given Schedule Serial?

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["INPUT: A Schedule S with n transactions"]:::startnode
    q1{"Q1: Are all operations of T1\ncontiguous in S?"}:::q1
    q2{"Q2: Are all operations of T2\ncontiguous in S?"}:::q2
    qn{"Qn: Are all operations of Tn\ncontiguous in S?"}:::qn
    yes["SERIAL SCHEDULE\nClassification Confirmed"]:::yesnode
    no["NON-SERIAL SCHEDULE\nInterleaving Detected"]:::nonode

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|"NO"| no
    q1 -->|"YES"| q2
    q2 -->|"NO"| no
    q2 -->|"YES"| qn
    qn -->|"NO"| no
    qn -->|"YES"| yes

    classDef startnode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef q1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef q2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef qn fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef yesnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef nonode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

### 4.5 Permutation Tree: Enumerating n=3 Serial Schedules

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["n = 3 Transactions\nT1, T2, T3"]:::root
    first1["Position 1"]:::level1
    first2["Position 1"]:::level1
    first3["Position 1"]:::level1

    a12["Position 2"]:::level2a
    a13["Position 2"]:::level2a
    a21["Position 2"]:::level2b
    a23["Position 2"]:::level2b
    a31["Position 2"]:::level2c
    a32["Position 2"]:::level2c

    leaf["T1 T2 T3"]:::leaf
    leaf2["T1 T3 T2"]:::leaf
    leaf3["T2 T1 T3"]:::leaf
    leaf4["T2 T3 T1"]:::leaf
    leaf5["T3 T1 T2"]:::leaf
    leaf6["T3 T2 T1"]:::leaf

    root --> first1
    root --> first2
    root --> first3

    first1 -->|"If T1 is first"| a12
    first1 -->|"If T1 is first"| a13
    first2 -->|"If T2 is first"| a21
    first2 -->|"If T2 is first"| a23
    first3 -->|"If T3 is first"| a31
    first3 -->|"If T3 is first"| a32

    a12 --> leaf
    a13 --> leaf2
    a21 --> leaf3
    a23 --> leaf4
    a31 --> leaf5
    a32 --> leaf6

    classDef root fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef level1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level2a fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level2b fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level2c fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define a **serial schedule** with a suitable example. Why is it considered the baseline for correctness in DBMS?

**Model Answer:**

A **serial schedule** is a schedule in which the operations of all concurrent transactions are executed one after another without any interleaving. In other words, each transaction runs to completion before the next transaction begins.

**Example:** Consider two transactions `T1` and `T2`. A serial schedule is:

$$S : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; c_1;\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; c_2$$

Here, all operations of `T1` are executed first, followed by all operations of `T2`. There is **no overlap** between the transactions.

**Why it is the baseline for correctness:** A serial schedule is considered the baseline because it inherently guarantees database consistency. Since only one transaction is active at a time, there are no issues like lost updates, dirty reads, or unrepeatable reads. The DBMS uses serial schedules as the **reference standard** — any concurrent (non-serial) schedule is considered correct if and only if it produces a result equivalent to some serial schedule (the principle of *serializability*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Use the phrase "contiguous block of operations" and mention "no interleaving" to score full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** How many **serial schedules** are possible for `n = 5` transactions? Justify your answer.

**Model Answer:**

The number of possible serial schedules for `n` transactions is given by `n!` (n factorial), because we are essentially counting the number of ways to arrange `n` distinct transactions in a linear order.

For `n = 5`:

$$N_{serial} = 5! = 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 120$$

**Justification:** Each transaction must be placed in a unique position. The first position can be filled by any of the 5 transactions, the second by any of the remaining 4, and so on. By the multiplication principle, the total number of arrangements is `5! = 120`.

**General formula:**

$$N_{serial} = n!$$

---

### 📕 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

## 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Consider two transactions `T1` and `T2` defined as follows. List **all possible serial schedules** and demonstrate that they are correct.

$$T_1 : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; c_1$$
$$T_2 : r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; c_2$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Given the schedule `S` below, determine whether it is a serial schedule. If not, identify the specific interleaving violation.

$$S : r_1(A);\; r_2(A);\; w_1(A);\; w_2(A);\; c_1;\; c_2$$

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Identify the number of transactions.
There are `n = 2` transactions: `T1` and `T2`.

**Step 2:** Compute the number of possible serial schedules.

$$N_{serial} = 2! = 2$$

**Step 3:** Enumerate the serial schedules explicitly.

**Serial Schedule 1: T1 → T2**

$$S_1 : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; c_1;\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; c_2$$

In `S1`, all operations of `T1` appear first, followed by all operations of `T2`. The contiguity property is satisfied.

**Serial Schedule 2: T2 → T1**

$$S_2 : r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; c_2;\; r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; c_1$$

In `S2`, all operations of `T2` appear first, followed by all operations of `T1`. The contiguity property is satisfied.

**Step 4:** Demonstrate correctness.

Both `S1` and `S2` are serial schedules, so by definition:
- They preserve database consistency.
- There are no conflicts because only one transaction is active at a time.
- All ACID properties, particularly **Isolation**, are trivially satisfied.

**Final Database State:**
- If initial value of `A = 1000`, and `T1` adds `500` while `T2` subtracts `200`:
  - After `S1` (T1 → T2): `A = 1000 + 500 - 200 = 1300`
  - After `S2` (T2 → T1): `A = 1000 - 200 + 500 = 1300`

Both serial schedules yield the **same final value** of `A`, confirming consistency.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Apply the contiguity test for each transaction.

| Position | Operation | Transaction | T1 Contiguous? | T2 Contiguous? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $r_1(A)$ | T1 | — | — |
| 2 | $r_2(A)$ | T2 | Broken at position 2 ✗ | — |
| 3 | $w_1(A)$ | T1 | Broken at position 2 ✗ | Broken at position 3 ✗ |
| 4 | $w_2(A)$ | T2 | — | Broken at position 3 ✗ |
| 5 | $c_1$ | T1 | — | — |
| 6 | $c_2$ | T2 | — | — |

**Step 2:** Identify the violation.

The operations of `T1` appear at positions **1, 3, 5** — they are **not contiguous** because position 2 is occupied by `T2`. Similarly, the operations of `T2` appear at positions **2, 4, 6** — also **not contiguous**.

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

$$S \text{ is a NON-SERIAL schedule (interleaved schedule)}$$

The interleaving pattern is: $r_1 \rightarrow r_2 \rightarrow w_1 \rightarrow w_2 \rightarrow c_1 \rightarrow c_2$, which violates the contiguity property required for a serial schedule.

---

## 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **characteristics/properties** of a serial schedule. Why is concurrency control in DBMS designed to emulate serial schedules?

**(b)** [7 Marks] For **4 transactions** `T1, T2, T3, T4`, calculate the total number of possible serial schedules. Show the first 4 and last 4 schedules in lexicographic order.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A serial schedule possesses the following **key characteristics**:

1. **Atomic Transaction Blocks:** Each transaction appears as a single, unbroken block of operations. There is no interleaving with any other transaction.

2. **No Conflicts:** Since transactions do not overlap, no two operations from different transactions can access the same data item simultaneously. Thus, issues like lost updates, dirty reads, and unrepeatable reads are **structurally impossible**.

3. **Trivial Serializability:** Every serial schedule is automatically conflict-serializable, view-serializable, recoverable, cascadeless, and strict.

4. **Database Consistency Preservation:** The database transitions from one consistent state to another through a sequence of consistent states, each owned by exactly one transaction.

5. **Isolation (Maximum Level):** Each transaction runs as if it is the **only** transaction in the system. The I (Isolation) of the ACID properties is satisfied at the highest level.

**Why concurrency control emulates serial schedules:**

In real-world multi-user DBMS, multiple transactions run simultaneously to maximize **throughput** and **resource utilization**. However, true serial execution would cripple performance. The goal of concurrency control protocols (2PL, timestamp ordering, MVCC) is to allow interleaved execution while ensuring that the final outcome is **equivalent to some serial schedule**. This principle is called **serializability** and is the cornerstone of correct concurrent database access.

> *"The database engine's promise to the user: 'Even though your transaction runs alongside 1000 others, you will observe the database as if you were running alone.'"*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$N_{serial} = 4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24$$

**Step 2:** Enumerate the first 4 and last 4 schedules.

**First 4 schedules (lexicographic order):**

| # | Schedule |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $S_{1234} : T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4$ |
| 2 | $S_{1243} : T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| 3 | $S_{1324} : T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_4$ |
| 4 | $S_{1342} : T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_2$ |

**Last 4 schedules:**

| # | Schedule |
| :---: | :--- |
| 21 | $S_{4312} : T_4 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ |
| 22 | $S_{4321} : T_4 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
| 23 | (Omitted in display for brevity) |
| 24 | (Omitted in display for brevity) |

**Step 3:** Final verification.

The 24 serial schedules are the total number of permutations of the 4 transactions. Any of these 24 schedules is a valid serial schedule, and the concurrency control mechanism aims to produce a schedule that is **equivalent to at least one of them**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "serial schedule" with "serializable schedule":** A serializable schedule is a non-serial schedule whose effect is equivalent to some serial schedule. Students often write "serial schedule" when they mean "serializable schedule" — this loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `n!` formula:** Always mention the formula explicitly before applying it. Writing only the numerical answer (e.g., "24") without the formula loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not identifying the violation in non-serial schedules:** When asked "is this schedule serial?", you must explicitly state **which transaction's contiguity is broken** and at **which position**.
> 4. **Ignoring the ACID connection:** Examiners love questions that link serial schedules to the **Isolation** property. Always mention the word "Isolation" in your answer.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **schedule** is an ordered sequence of operations from one or more transactions.
- ✅ A **serial schedule** has **zero interleaving** — each transaction completes before the next begins.
- ✅ The **contiguity property** is the defining test: each transaction's operations must form a **single contiguous block**.
- ✅ Number of serial schedules for `n` transactions = **`n!`**.
- ✅ Serial schedules are **trivially correct** — they satisfy all serializability, recoverability, cascadelessness, and strictness properties by construction.
- ✅ Serial schedules are **impractical** for real systems because they offer **low throughput**; modern DBMS use **concurrency control protocols** to emulate them.
- ✅ Serial schedules serve as the **theoretical baseline** — the goal of all concurrency control is to produce a schedule equivalent to a serial one.
- ✅ Serial schedules are linked to the **I (Isolation)** of the ACID properties.
- ✅ The **default assumption** in DBMS theory is that the initial database state is **consistent**, and every transaction (executed alone) preserves consistency.
- ✅ For KTU exams, memorize the `n!` formula, the contiguity test, and the difference between "serial" and "serializable."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrent and Serializable Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concurrent and Serializable Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **schedule** is an ordered sequence (chronological execution order) of operations drawn from a set of concurrently executing transactions, where the order of operations within each individual transaction is preserved.

A schedule is classified into three categories:

- **Serial Schedule**: A schedule in which transactions are executed one after the other in a non-interleaved (atomic) manner. The next transaction begins only after the previous transaction completely finishes.
- **Concurrent (Interleaved) Schedule**: A schedule in which operations from multiple transactions are interleaved in time, allowing CPU/Disk I/O overlap to improve throughput.
- **Serializable Schedule**: A concurrent schedule that is guaranteed to produce the same final database state and have the same effect on the database as *some* serial schedule executing the *same* set of transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The two principal notions of equivalence used to test serializability are:
> 1. **Conflict Equivalence** (most commonly tested)
> 2. **View Equivalence**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **single ATM lobby with one physical cash dispenser** shared by three customers, Alice, Bob, and Clara, who all arrive at the same time and begin withdrawing/depositing in parallel.

- A **Serial Schedule** would mean: Alice finishes completely (steps 1, 2, 3, 4) → then Bob starts completely → then Clara starts. Safe, but slow.
- A **Concurrent Schedule** would mean: Alice inserts card → Bob inserts card → Alice enters PIN → Clara inserts card → Bob enters amount → and so on. Fast, but *what if Bob's withdrawal reads the balance before Alice's deposit is committed?* The bank vault could become inconsistent.

The bank solves this using the **ACID protocol**: it requires that the *interleaved* (concurrent) execution must be **equivalent** in outcome to *some* safe serial order. This equivalence is called **serializability**. The bank's compliance officer is the *concurrency control manager* of the DBMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions Cheat Sheet**:
> - **Transaction (T)**: A logical unit consisting of `read`, `write`, `commit`, `abort` operations.
> - **Operation Pair (Conflict)**: Two operations from different transactions form a conflict if they access the **same data item**, **belong to different transactions**, and **at least one is a write**.

## 1.3 The Three Types of Conflicts

| Symbol | Conflict Type | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **WR** | Write–Read | Dirty Read anomaly |
| **RW** | Read–Write | Unrepeatable Read anomaly |
| **WW** | Write–Write | Lost Update anomaly |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse **Read–Read (RR)** as a conflict. Reading the same item twice is *never* a conflict — the order of reads does not change the result. Only operations involving at least one **WRITE** can conflict.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conflict Serializability — The "Why" and "How"

### The "Why"
A schedule $S$ is conflict serializable if and only if it is *conflict equivalent* to *some* serial schedule. We test this using a **Precedence Graph** (also called **Serialization Graph** or **Conflict Graph**).

### The "How" — Stepwise Logic

1. **Identify transactions** $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$ participating in the schedule.
2. **Create a node** in the directed graph for every transaction.
3. **Scan the schedule left to right**:
   - If transaction $T_i$ executes a `read(X)` and later transaction $T_j$ executes a `write(X)` → draw a directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$.
   - If $T_i$ executes `write(X)` and later $T_j$ executes `read(X)` → draw edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$.
   - If $T_i$ executes `write(X)` and later $T_j$ executes `write(X)` → draw edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$.
4. **Test for cycles**:
   - **No cycle** ⇒ Schedule is **Conflict Serializable** (CSR).
   - **Cycle exists** ⇒ Schedule is **NOT Conflict Serializable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Theorem (Korth & Silberschatz)**:
> A schedule $S$ is **Conflict Serializable** if and only if its **precedence graph is acyclic**.

## 2.2 View Serializability — The Broader Notion

View serializability is more permissive than conflict serializability. A schedule $S$ is **view equivalent** to a serial schedule $S'$ if:

1. **Initial Read Condition**: For every data item $X$, if $T_i$ reads the *initial value* of $X$ in $S$, then $T_i$ must also read the initial value of $X$ in $S'$.
2. **Final Write Condition**: For every data item $X$, the transaction that performs the *final write* on $X$ in $S$ must also perform the final write on $X$ in $S'$.
3. **Read-From Condition**: If $T_i$ reads a value of $X$ written by $T_j$ in $S$, then $T_i$ must also read the value of $X$ written by $T_j$ in $S'$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Serializability (most restrictive → least restrictive)**:
> Serial ⊂ Conflict Serializable (CSR) ⊂ View Serializable (VSR) ⊂ All Schedules
> Every CSR schedule is VSR, but not every VSR schedule is CSR.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Rule / Equation | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of possible serial schedules** for $n$ transactions | $n!$ | KTU frequently asks: "How many serial schedules are possible for 3 transactions?" → $3! = 6$ |
| **Conflict Condition** | $\text{op}_i(X) \sim \text{op}_j(X) \iff T_i \neq T_j \land \text{item same} \land (\text{at least one is write})$ | Determines whether to draw an edge in the precedence graph |
| **Edge Rule** | $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ exists $\iff$ $T_i$ executes a conflicting op *before* $T_j$ on the same item | Building the precedence graph |
| **CSR Test** | $\text{Acyclic Precedence Graph} \iff \text{Conflict Serializable}$ | Final verdict of testing |
| **VSR Test (Polygraph)** | Uses *polygraph* with blind writes — beyond KTU depth | Rarely tested directly |
| **Recoverable Schedule** | For every $T_j$ reading from $T_i$, $T_j$ commits *after* $T_i$ commits | Avoids cascading aborts |
| **Cascadeless Schedule** | $T_j$ reads a value written by $T_i$ only *after* $T_i$ has committed | Stronger than recoverable |
| **Strict Schedule** | No $T_j$ reads/writes a value written by $T_i$ until $T_i$ commits/aborts | Strongest isolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant for KTU**: For a schedule involving $n$ transactions, the **maximum** number of nodes in the precedence graph is $n$, and the **maximum** number of directed edges is $n(n-1)$. For a **cyclic** test, the smallest cycle length is **2** (mutual edge between two nodes).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & E-Commerce**: Conflict serializability is the mathematical guarantee behind online transaction processing (OLTP) systems like Oracle, MySQL InnoDB, and PostgreSQL.
- **Distributed Systems (Kubernetes, Raft consensus)**: Conflict detection principles are extended to distributed databases (e.g., Spanner, CockroachDB) using **vector clocks** and **conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs)**.
- **Version Control (Git)**: Merge conflict detection uses analogous precedence logic to determine which commit logically precedes another.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Precedence Graph

### Schedule S1
| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — | — |
| 2 | `write(X)` | — | — |
| 3 | — | `read(X)` | — |
| 4 | — | `write(X)` | — |
| 5 | — | — | `read(X)` |
| 6 | — | — | `read(Y)` |
| 7 | — | `read(Y)` | — |
| 8 | — | `write(Y)` | — |

### Step 1: Create One Node Per Transaction
Nodes created: $T_1, T_2, T_3$.

### Step 2: Scan Left to Right for Conflicting Pairs

**Step 1 vs Step 3**: $T_1$ reads $X$, $T_2$ reads $X$ → Read–Read on same item → **No conflict, no edge**.

**Step 1 vs Step 4**: $T_1$ reads $X$, $T_2$ writes $X$ → Read–Write conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**.

**Step 2 vs Step 3**: $T_1$ writes $X$, $T_2$ reads $X$ → Write–Read conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$** (duplicate, ignore).

**Step 2 vs Step 4**: $T_1$ writes $X$, $T_2$ writes $X$ → Write–Write conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**.

**Step 4 vs Step 5**: $T_2$ writes $X$, $T_3$ reads $X$ → Write–Read conflict → **Edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$**.

**Step 4 vs Step 6**: $T_2$ writes $X$, $T_3$ reads $Y$ → Different data items → **No conflict**.

**Step 6 vs Step 7**: $T_3$ reads $Y$, $T_2$ reads $Y$ → Read–Read → **No conflict**.

**Step 6 vs Step 8**: $T_3$ reads $Y$, $T_2$ writes $Y$ → Read–Write conflict → **Edge $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$**.

### Step 3: Construct the Precedence Graph
Final edges:
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
- $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$

### Step 4: Cycle Detection

Following edges from $T_2$: $T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2$. This is a **cycle of length 2** between $T_2$ and $T_3$.

### Step 5: Verdict
$$
\text{Cycle Detected} \implies S_1 \text{ is NOT Conflict Serializable}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the conflict serializable test:** Although the precedence graph contained edges $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ and $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$, the presence of *any* cycle (here between $T_2$ and $T_3$) immediately disqualifies the schedule. You do not need to analyze transitive paths once a cycle is found.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — A Comprehensive Serializability Test

### Schedule S2
| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — | — |
| 2 | — | `write(X)` | — |
| 3 | `write(X)` | — | — |
| 4 | — | `read(Y)` | — |
| 5 | `read(Y)` | — | — |
| 6 | — | — | `read(X)` |
| 7 | — | — | `write(X)` |
| 8 | — | — | `read(Y)` |

### Conflict Analysis Matrix

| Earlier Op | Later Op | Data Item | Conflict Type | Edge Drawn |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Step 1: $T_1$ reads $X$ | Step 2: $T_2$ writes $X$ | $X$ | RW | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ |
| Step 1: $T_1$ reads $X$ | Step 3: $T_1$ writes $X$ | $X$ | — | Same transaction, skip |
| Step 2: $T_2$ writes $X$ | Step 3: $T_1$ writes $X$ | $X$ | WW | $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
| Step 2: $T_2$ writes $X$ | Step 6: $T_3$ reads $X$ | $X$ | WR | $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| Step 2: $T_2$ writes $X$ | Step 7: $T_3$ writes $X$ | $X$ | WW | $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| Step 3: $T_1$ writes $X$ | Step 6: $T_3$ reads $X$ | $X$ | WR | $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| Step 3: $T_1$ writes $X$ | Step 7: $T_3$ writes $X$ | $X$ | WW | $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$ |
| Step 4: $T_2$ reads $Y$ | Step 5: $T_1$ reads $Y$ | $Y$ | RR | No edge |
| Step 4: $T_2$ reads $Y$ | Step 8: $T_3$ reads $Y$ | $Y$ | RR | No edge |
| Step 5: $T_1$ reads $Y$ | Step 8: $T_3$ reads $Y$ | $Y$ | RR | No edge |

### Unique Edges
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$  *(from Steps 2→3)*
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$

### Cycle Detection
Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ and edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ form a **2-cycle** between $T_1$ and $T_2$.

### Verdict
$$
S_2 \text{ is NOT Conflict Serializable}
$$

## 3.3 Deriving Equivalent Serial Schedule (When CSR Exists)

### Schedule S3
| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — | — |
| 2 | — | `read(Z)` | — |
| 3 | `write(X)` | — | — |
| 4 | — | `read(Y)` | — |
| 5 | `read(Z)` | — | — |
| 6 | — | — | `read(Y)` |
| 7 | `write(Z)` | — | — |
| 8 | — | `write(Z)` | — |
| 9 | — | `write(Y)` | — |
| 10 | — | — | `write(Y)` |

### Conflict Pairs
- Step 1 ($T_1$ reads $X$) vs Step 3 ($T_1$ writes $X$): same transaction, skip.
- Step 2 ($T_2$ reads $Z$) vs Step 5 ($T_1$ reads $Z$): RR, skip.
- Step 2 ($T_2$ reads $Z$) vs Step 7 ($T_1$ writes $Z$): RW → $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$.
- Step 2 ($T_2$ reads $Z$) vs Step 8 ($T_2$ writes $Z$): same transaction, skip.
- Step 4 ($T_2$ reads $Y$) vs Step 6 ($T_3$ reads $Y$): RR, skip.
- Step 4 ($T_2$ reads $Y$) vs Step 9 ($T_2$ writes $Y$): same transaction, skip.
- Step 4 ($T_2$ reads $Y$) vs Step 10 ($T_3$ writes $Y$): RW → $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$.
- Step 6 ($T_3$ reads $Y$) vs Step 9 ($T_2$ writes $Y$): WR → $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$.
- Step 6 ($T_3$ reads $Y$) vs Step 10 ($T_3$ writes $Y$): same transaction, skip.
- Step 7 ($T_1$ writes $Z$) vs Step 8 ($T_2$ writes $Z$): WW → $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.

### Edges
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
- $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$

### Verdict
The edges $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ and $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$ create a **2-cycle** between $T_2$ and $T_3$.

$$
\boxed{S_3 \text{ is NOT Conflict Serializable}}
$$

## 3.4 Numerical Computation — Number of Serial Schedules

For $n$ transactions, the number of distinct serial schedules equals the number of permutations of the transactions:

$$
N_{\text{serial}} = n! = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \dots \times 1
$$

**For $n = 3$ transactions:**

$$
N_{\text{serial}} = 3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6
$$

The 6 serial schedules are explicitly:

$$
(T_1, T_2, T_3), \quad (T_1, T_3, T_2), \quad (T_2, T_1, T_3), \quad (T_2, T_3, T_1), \quad (T_3, T_1, T_2), \quad (T_3, T_2, T_1)
$$

**For $n = 4$ transactions:**

$$
N_{\text{serial}} = 4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: A concurrent schedule is *guaranteed* to be correct only if it is equivalent to **at least one** of these $n!$ serial schedules. The precedence graph efficiently tells us *which* one.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Precedence Graph Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Transaction T1"]
    node2["Transaction T2"]
    node3["Transaction T3"]
    node4["Read X"]
    node5["Write X"]
    node6["Read Y"]
    node7["Write Y"]
    node8["Read Z"]
    node9["Write Z"]
    node10["Schedule Scan Left to Right"]
    node11["Identify Conflict Pairs"]
    node12["Draw Directed Edge Ti to Tj"]
    node13["Check for Cycle via DFS"]
    node14["Output Acyclic = CSR Yes"]
    node15["Output Cyclic = CSR No"]
    node1 --- node2
    node2 --- node3
    node3 --- node1
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14
    node13 --> node15
    node4 --- node5
    node6 --- node7
    node8 --- node9
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Serializability Testing

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputA["Concurrent Schedule S"]
    inputB["Transaction Set T1 to Tn"]
    process1["Parse Operations in Order"]
    process2["Conflict Detection Engine"]
    process3["Precedence Graph Builder"]
    process4["Cycle Detection Algorithm DFS"]
    output1["Conflict Serializable CSR Yes"]
    output2["Conflict Serializable CSR No"]
    output3["Equivalent Serial Schedule"]
    inputA --> process1
    inputB --> process1
    process1 --> process2
    process2 --> process3
    process3 --> process4
    process4 --> output1
    process4 --> output2
    process3 --> output3
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Schedule Classifications

```mermaid
graph TB
    level1["All Possible Schedules"]
    level2["Serializable Schedules"]
    level3["View Serializable VSR"]
    level4["Conflict Serializable CSR"]
    level5["Serial Schedules"]
    level1 --> level2
    level2 --> level3
    level3 --> level4
    level4 --> level5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: The arrows in the hierarchy represent *subset* relations. Each inner set is fully contained in the outer set. Therefore, every **Serial** schedule is trivially **CSR**, every **CSR** is **VSR**, but not vice versa.

## 4.4 Conflict Type Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    op1["Operation Pair Op i and Op j"]
    check1["Same Data Item X"]
    check2["Different Transactions Ti not equal Tj"]
    check3["At Least One Operation is WRITE"]
    result1["NO CONFLICT"]
    result2["CONFLICT DETECTED"]
    result3["Add Edge Ti to Tj in Precedence Graph"]
    op1 --> check1
    check1 --> result1
    check1 --> check2
    check2 --> result1
    check2 --> check3
    check3 --> result1
    check3 --> result2
    result2 --> result3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a conflict serializable schedule? Explain the concept of a precedence graph.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A schedule is **conflict serializable** if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by *swapping* non-conflicting operations. Two operations conflict if they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a write operation.

A **precedence graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph where:
- **Vertices (V)**: Each node represents a transaction $T_i$ in the schedule.
- **Edges (E)**: A directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ exists if $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ in any equivalent serial schedule, i.e., they have a conflicting operation pair with $T_i$ executing first.

**Test Rule**: The schedule is conflict serializable **if and only if** the precedence graph is **acyclic** (contains no cycle).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List the three types of conflicts between operations. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Conflict Type | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Write–Read (WR)** | $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ reads $X$ | Dirty Read problem |
| **Read–Write (RW)** | $T_i$ reads $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$ | Unrepeatable Read problem |
| **Write–Write (WW)** | $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$ | Lost Update problem |

(1 mark per correct conflict with example)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Consider the following schedule $S$ of three transactions $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_3$:**

| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — | — |
| 2 | `write(X)` | — | — |
| 3 | — | `read(X)` | — |
| 4 | — | `write(X)` | — |
| 5 | — | — | `read(Z)` |
| 6 | — | `read(Y)` | — |
| 7 | — | — | `read(Y)` |
| 8 | — | `write(Y)` | — |
| 9 | `read(Z)` | — | — |
| 10 | — | — | `write(Z)` |

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
**Construct the precedence graph for the schedule and determine whether the schedule is conflict serializable. Justify your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify all conflicting pairs:**

Scanning left to right on each data item:

- **Item X**: 
  - $T_1$ writes $X$ at step 2, $T_2$ reads $X$ at step 3 → WR conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**
  - $T_1$ writes $X$ at step 2, $T_2$ writes $X$ at step 4 → WW conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**
  - $T_2$ writes $X$ at step 4, no later op on $X$ from $T_3$ → No edge.
- **Item Y**:
  - $T_2$ reads $Y$ at step 6, $T_2$ writes $Y$ at step 8 → same transaction, skip.
  - $T_2$ reads $Y$ at step 6, $T_3$ reads $Y$ at step 7 → RR, no conflict.
  - $T_3$ reads $Y$ at step 7, $T_2$ writes $Y$ at step 8 → RW conflict → **Edge $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$**
  - $T_3$ reads $Y$ at step 7, no later writes on $Y$ from $T_1$ → No edge.
- **Item Z**:
  - $T_3$ reads $Z$ at step 5, $T_1$ reads $Z$ at step 9 → RR, no conflict.
  - $T_3$ reads $Z$ at step 5, $T_3$ writes $Z$ at step 10 → same transaction, skip.
  - $T_1$ reads $Z$ at step 9, $T_3$ writes $Z$ at step 10 → RW conflict → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$**

**Step 2 — Unique edges**:
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$

**Step 3 — Cycle detection**:
Edges: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, $T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2$. 
- From $T_1$ we can reach $T_2$ (directly) and $T_3$ (directly).
- From $T_3$ we can reach $T_2$.
- From $T_2$ there are no outgoing edges.
- No path returns to $T_1$. **No cycle exists**.

**Step 4 — Verdict**:
The precedence graph is acyclic, therefore the schedule $S$ **IS Conflict Serializable**.

**Step 5 — Equivalent Serial Schedule**:
The topological order from the precedence graph is:

$$
T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2
$$

The equivalent serial order is $(T_1, T_3, T_2)$.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
**If the schedule is conflict serializable, list the equivalent serial schedules. State two anomalies that this schedule avoids due to its serializability.**

**Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Serial Schedules**:
Based on the precedence graph edges $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$, $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$, the *only* topological ordering is:

$$
\boxed{(T_1, T_3, T_2)}
$$

This is the **only** serial schedule equivalent to $S$ via conflict equivalence.

**Anomalies Avoided**:
1. **Lost Update Problem**: Because the order of writes on $X$ is fixed ($T_1$ must precede $T_2$), $T_1$'s update cannot be lost by $T_2$'s overwrite.
2. **Dirty Read / Unrepeatable Read**: Because the read on $Y$ by $T_3$ is ordered *before* $T_2$'s write on $Y$, and the reads of $T_2$ on $Y$ occur after $T_2$'s own writes within the same transaction, the schedule ensures consistent read behavior on $Y$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Consider the following two schedules $S_A$ and $S_B$.**

**Schedule $S_A$**:

| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — | — |
| 2 | — | `read(X)` | — |
| 3 | — | — | `read(X)` |
| 4 | `write(X)` | — | — |
| 5 | — | `write(X)` | — |
| 6 | — | — | `write(X)` |

**Schedule $S_B$**:

| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — |
| 2 | — | `write(X)` |
| 3 | `read(Y)` | — |
| 4 | — | `read(Y)` |
| 5 | `write(Y)` | — |

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
**Test whether Schedule $S_A$ is conflict serializable. Draw the precedence graph.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify conflicts on item X:**

- Step 1: $T_1$ reads $X$, Step 4: $T_1$ writes $X$ → same transaction, skip.
- Step 1: $T_1$ reads $X$, Step 5: $T_2$ writes $X$ → RW → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**
- Step 1: $T_1$ reads $X$, Step 6: $T_3$ writes $X$ → RW → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$**
- Step 2: $T_2$ reads $X$, Step 4: $T_1$ writes $X$ → WR → **Edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$**
- Step 2: $T_2$ reads $X$, Step 5: $T_2$ writes $X$ → same transaction, skip.
- Step 2: $T_2$ reads $X$, Step 6: $T_3$ writes $X$ → RW → **Edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$**
- Step 3: $T_3$ reads $X$, Step 4: $T_1$ writes $X$ → WR → **Edge $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$**
- Step 3: $T_3$ reads $X$, Step 5: $T_2$ writes $X$ → WR → **Edge $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$**
- Step 4: $T_1$ writes $X$, Step 5: $T_2$ writes $X$ → WW → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**
- Step 4: $T_1$ writes $X$, Step 6: $T_3$ writes $X$ → WW → **Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$**
- Step 5: $T_2$ writes $X$, Step 6: $T_3$ writes $X$ → WW → **Edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$**

**Step 2 — Unique edges**:
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
- $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$
- $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$

**Step 3 — Cycle detection**:
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ — a **2-cycle**.
- $T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$ — a **2-cycle**.
- $T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2$ — a **2-cycle**.

Multiple cycles exist. **The schedule $S_A$ is NOT conflict serializable.**

**Explanation**: Every transaction both precedes and follows every other transaction in some path, making it impossible to find any linear order that preserves all conflict relations.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
**Test whether Schedule $S_B$ is view serializable. Justify your answer with all three view equivalence conditions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Schedule $S_B$ (Reorganized)**:
| Step | $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `read(X)` | — |
| 2 | — | `write(X)` |
| 3 | `read(Y)` | — |
| 4 | — | `read(Y)` |
| 5 | `write(Y)` | — |

**Possible Serial Schedules** for comparison: $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_1)$.

**Condition 1 — Initial Reads**:
- In $S_B$, $T_1$ reads initial value of $X$ at step 1.
- In $S_B$, $T_2$ does **not** read initial value of $X$.
- In $S_B$, $T_1$ reads initial value of $Y$ at step 3 (assuming $T_1$'s read happens before any write to $Y$).
- In $S_B$, $T_2$ reads $Y$ at step 4 *after* $T_1$ writes it (no — $T_1$ writes $Y$ at step 5). So $T_2$'s read of $Y$ at step 4 is from the initial database.

- Serial $(T_1, T_2)$: $T_1$ reads initial $X$ ✓, $T_1$ reads initial $Y$ ✓, $T_2$ reads initial $X$ (no — it just writes) ✓, $T_2$ reads initial $Y$ (no — by step 4, $T_1$ hasn't written $Y$ yet) ✓. **Initial reads match.**

**Condition 2 — Final Writes**:
- In $S_B$, the final write on $X$ is by $T_2$ at step 2.
- In $S_B$, the final write on $Y$ is by $T_1$ at step 5.
- In serial $(T_1, T_2)$: $T_2$ is the last to write on $X$ ✓. $T_2$ is the last to write on $Y$ — but in $S_B$, $T_1$ writes $Y$ last. **Mismatch.**

**Condition 3 — Read-From**:
- $T_1$'s read of $X$ at step 1 is from the initial database.
- $T_2$'s read of $Y$ at step 4 is from the initial database (since $T_1$ hasn't written $Y$ yet at that point).
- In serial $(T_1, T_2)$: $T_1$'s read of $X$ is from initial ✓. $T_2$'s read of $Y$ is from $T_1$ — but in $S_B$, $T_2$ reads initial $Y$. **Mismatch.**

**Verdict**:
$$
S_B \text{ is NOT View Serializable}
$$

**Engineering Justification**: The lack of view serializability means that no serial execution can reproduce the exact read-from dependencies of $S_B$, indicating the schedule is unsafe for any isolation level below Serializable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Pipe Symbol Trap**: When drawing the precedence graph as a table, do **not** use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside any cell to denote absolute value or division. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX or simply rephrase (e.g., "absolute value of x") to avoid breaking the table.
> 2. **Missing Read-Read Distinction**: Students often mistakenly draw edges for read-read pairs. **Read-Read is NEVER a conflict** — you will lose 1 mark per such erroneous edge.
> 3. **Same-Transaction Op Skip**: Operations within the *same* transaction (even on the same item, like read then write) do not create edges. Many students lose marks here.
> 4. **Topological Order in CSR**: When a schedule IS conflict serializable, you must give the equivalent *serial* order (topological sort) explicitly. Stating only "acyclic" without the serial order loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Cycle vs Acyclic Verdict**: Always state "Cycle found / No cycle found" *and* the final verdict. A vague "the graph is cyclic so it's bad" loses 1 mark for lack of precision.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Schedule**: A chronological sequence of read/write operations from multiple transactions preserving intra-transaction order.
- **Serial Schedule**: Non-interleaved execution; $n$ transactions yield $n!$ possible serial orders.
- **Conflict**: Occurs between operations of *different* transactions on the *same data item* where at least one is a **write** (WR, RW, WW only).
- **Conflict Serializability (CSR)**: Schedule equivalent to a serial schedule via swap of non-conflicting operations.
- **Precedence Graph**: Directed graph with edges $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ for each conflict where $T_i$ executes first.
- **Acyclic Precedence Graph** $\iff$ **Conflict Serializable Schedule** (the cardinal theorem).
- **View Serializability (VSR)**: Weaker notion based on three conditions — initial reads, final writes, read-from equivalence.
- **Hierarchy**: Serial $\subset$ CSR $\subset$ VSR $\subset$ All Schedules.
- **Recoverable Schedule**: Every $T_j$ that reads from $T_i$ commits *after* $T_i$ commits (avoids cascading aborts).
- **Cascadeless Schedule**: Strengthens recoverable by requiring $T_j$ reads only *after* $T_i$ commits.
- **Strict Schedule**: Strongest form — no read/write of an uncommitted value is permitted.
- **Anomalies Prevented by CSR**: Lost Update, Dirty Read, Unrepeatable Read, Phantom (at the cost of reduced concurrency).
- **Engineering Use**: Forms the mathematical basis for concurrency control in PostgreSQL (MVCC + SSI), MySQL InnoDB, and Oracle (SSX).
- **KTU Exam Tip**: For 14-mark questions, always (1) draw the precedence graph explicitly, (2) list all conflict pairs in a table, (3) state cycle/acyclic, (4) provide equivalent serial order if CSR holds.
- **Key Constant to Memorize**: $n!$ for the number of serial schedules is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question.
- **Two-Node Cycle Detection**: A 2-node cycle (mutual edge $T_i \leftrightarrow T_j$) is the *smallest* possible cycle and the most common reason for non-CSR verdicts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conflict Equivalence and Conflict Serializability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Conflict Equivalence and Conflict Serializability # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Database Management Systems (DBMS), a **schedule** is a chronological sequence of execution of concurrent transactions that preserves the order of operations within each individual transaction. When multiple transactions run concurrently, the DBMS must interleave their operations in such a way that the resulting database state remains consistent. A schedule is classified into three primary categories based on its execution structure: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition - Schedule Taxonomy** > - **Serial Schedule:** A schedule in which transactions are executed one after another, with no interleaving of operations from different transactions. Transaction $T_2$ begins only after $T_1$ completely finishes. > - **Non-Serial Schedule (Concurrent/Interleaved):** A schedule where operations from multiple active transactions are interleaved in time. > - **Serializable Schedule:** A non-serial schedule whose effect on the database is **equivalent** to some serial schedule. It produces the same final state and reads the same values as the serial schedule it mimics. ## 1.2 Types of Equivalence in DBMS To formally compare two schedules, DBMS theory defines the following equivalence relationships: | Equivalence Type | Definition | KTU Module Coverage | |:-----------------|:-----------|:--------------------| | **Result Equivalence** | Two schedules produce the same final database state after execution. | Reference Only | | **View Equivalence** | Two schedules read the same values and produce the same final output for every transaction. | Module 4 / 5 | | **Conflict Equivalence** | Two schedules involve the same set of transactions, perform the same operations, and order every pair of conflicting operations identically. | **Primary Focus - Module 4** | ## 1.3 The Concept of "Conflict" Between Operations > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Conflicting Operations** > Two operations from different transactions $T_i$ and $T_j$ (where $i \neq j$) on the same data item $X$ are said to be in **conflict** if and only if **at least one** of the operations is a `WRITE` operation. They must also not belong to the same transaction. The three possible conflict pairs are explicitly: 1. **Read-Write Conflict ($R$-$W$):** $T_i$ reads $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$ — Conflicting 2. **Write-Read Conflict ($W$-$R$):** $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ reads $X$ — Conflicting 3. **Write-Write Conflict ($W$-$W$):** $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$ — Conflicting > **Read-Read ($R$-$R$):** Always **non-conflicting**, since two reads never alter the data. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Chef" Intuition Imagine two chefs, $T_1$ and $T_2$, sharing a single kitchen with one shared ingredient jar containing salt ($X$). - If both chefs are **only reading** the label on the salt jar, there is **no conflict** — they both can inspect it freely. - If chef $T_1$ is **about to add salt** to the dish (write), and chef $T_2$ **tastes** the dish (reads), the result of $T_2$'s taste depends on **whether** $T_1$ has already added salt.
