This is a **conflict** — the **order** of their actions matters. - If both chefs attempt to **add salt** simultaneously, the final salt content is non-deterministic — another **conflict**. **Conflict serializability** simply asks: *"Can we reorder the conflicting actions of these two chefs (without changing the order of non-conflicting actions) so that one chef completely finishes before the other starts?"* If yes, the schedule is conflict serializable and considered safe. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formal Definition — Conflict Equivalence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Conflict Equivalence)** > Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are said to be **conflict equivalent** if and only if: > 1. They involve the **same set of transactions**. > 2. They perform the **same set of operations** (both read and write). > 3. The relative **order of every pair of conflicting operations** is the **same** in both schedules. In simple words: *Two schedules are conflict equivalent when you can transform one into the other by repeatedly swapping adjacent **non-conflicting** operations.* The formal symbol used in KTU textbooks is: $$S \equiv_{C} S'$$ ## 2.2 Formal Definition — Conflict Serializability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Conflict Serializable Schedule)** > A schedule $S$ is **conflict serializable** if and only if it is **conflict equivalent** to some **serial schedule** of the same $n$ transactions. Notation: $S$ is conflict serializable iff $S \equiv_{C} S_{serial}$. ## 2.3 The Precedence Graph Method (P-Serializability Test) The KTU board examination heavily tests the **Precedence Graph** (also called **Serialization Graph**, abbreviated as **PG** or **SG**) method. > **Precedence Graph Construction Rule:** > A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of transactions and $E$ is the set of edges. For every pair of conflicting operations $op_i \in T_i$ and $op_j \in T_j$ where $op_i$ occurs before $op_j$ in schedule $S$, draw a directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$. ### Algorithm — Drawing the Precedence Graph 1. **Step 1:** Create one node for each transaction $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$. 2. **Step 2:** Examine every pair of operations $(op_1, op_2)$ belonging to *different* transactions that access the *same data item*. 3. **Step 3:** If $op_1$ is a `WRITE` (or both are `WRITE`) and $op_1$ executes *before* $op_2$ in the schedule, add a directed edge from $T_i \rightarrow T_j$. 4. **Step 4:** Once all conflicts are processed, analyze the graph. ### Algorithm — Testing Conflict Serializability > [!NOTE] > **Cycle Test Theorem (KTU 2024 High-Yield Result)** > A schedule $S$ is **conflict serializable if and only if its precedence graph is acyclic** (contains no directed cycle). > - If the precedence graph has a **cycle** $\Rightarrow$ **NOT conflict serializable**. > - If the precedence graph is a **DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) $\Rightarrow$ **Conflict serializable**, and the serial order is given by a **topological sort** of the graph. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Definition / Rule | Application | |:-----------------|:------------------|:------------| | $S$ | A schedule (sequence of operations) | Object under test | | $T_i, T_j$ | Two distinct transactions | Vertices in PG | | $R_i(X), W_i(X)$ | Read/Write by $T_i$ on item $X$ | Operations | | $op_i \prec op_j$ | $op_i$ occurs before $op_j$ in $S$ | Order check | | $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ | Edge in PG due to conflict | Precedence constraint | | **Conflict types** | $R$-$W$, $W$-$R$, $W$-$W$ on same $X$ | Edge creation triggers | | $S \equiv_C S'$ | Conflict equivalent | Swappable non-conflict ops | | **Theorem (PG Test)** | $S$ is CS $\iff$ PG$(S)$ is acyclic | Main decision tool | | **Serial Order** | Topological sort of PG | Recovery of equivalent serial | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking Systems (ATM Networks):** Thousands of concurrent transactions on shared account records. Conflict serializability guarantees that the final balance is correct, equivalent to some serial processing. - **E-commerce Inventory:** Multiple users purchasing the same product simultaneously. PG-based conflict detection ensures no overselling. - **Airline Reservation:** Multiple booking agents accessing the same seat. Without conflict serializability, the same seat could be sold twice (write-write anomaly). - **Distributed Databases (Cassandra, CockroachDB):** Conflict detection in distributed commit protocols borrows from this exact theoretical foundation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A Conflict Serializable Schedule Consider the following schedule $S$ with two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ on data items $A$ and $B$: $$S : r_1(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A)$$ ### Step 1: Identify all conflicting operation pairs on the *same* data item. | Index | Operation | Item | Check Conflicts | Conflicting With | |:------|:----------|:-----|:----------------|:-----------------| | 1 | $r_1(A)$ | $A$ | Previous writes on $A$? | None | | 2 | $r_1(B)$ | $B$ | Previous writes on $B$? | None | | 3 | $w_1(B)$ | $B$ | Prior $r$ or $w$ on $B$? | $r_1(B)$ same transaction — skip | | 4 | $r_2(B)$ | $B$ | Prior $w$ on $B$? | $w_1(B)$ — **CONFLICT** ($W$-$R$) | | 5 | $w_2(B)$ | $B$ | Prior $w$ on $B$? | $w_1(B)$ — **CONFLICT** ($W$-$W$) | | 6 | $r_2(A)$ | $A$ | Prior $w$ on $A$? | None | | 7 | $w_2(A)$ | $A$ | Prior $w$ on $A$? | None | ### Step 2: Construct the Precedence Graph. The two conflicts generate: - $w_1(B) \prec r_2(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $w_1(B) \prec w_2(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ Final graph: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ (single edge). ### Step 3: Cycle Test. $$\text{Cycles in } G = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Acyclic} \;\Rightarrow\; S \text{ is CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE.}$$ ### Step 4: Find Equivalent Serial Schedule via Topological Sort. Since $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, the topological order is $T_1$ first, then $T_2$. $$S_{serial} : r_1(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A)$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — A Schedule That Is NOT Conflict Serializable Consider: $$S : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B)$$ ### Step 1: Identify all cross-transaction conflicts on the same data item. - $w_1(A)$ occurs before $r_2(A)$ $\Rightarrow$ **CONFLICT** ($W$-$R$) $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $w_1(A)$ occurs before $w_2(A)$ $\Rightarrow$ **CONFLICT** ($W$-$W$) $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $r_1(B)$ occurs before $w_2(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ **CONFLICT** ($R$-$W$) $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $w_1(B)$ occurs before $w_2(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ **CONFLICT** ($W$-$W$) $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ Wait — recheck for **reverse** conflicts: - $w_2(A)$ after $w_1(A)$: only forward edge. - $r_2(B)$ comes after... checking: $r_2(B)$ reads $B$, previous writes on $B$ are $w_1(B)$ — so $w_1(B) \prec r_2(B)$ is also a conflict $\Rightarrow$ Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. Now check the **second** half of the schedule for any *opposite* direction conflict: - $r_1(B)$ — no prior $B$ writes from $T_2$. - $w_1(B)$ — no prior $B$ writes from $T_2$ (since $w_2(B)$ comes *after*). So all conflicts give edges $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ only. Graph is acyclic. **Let me construct a TRULY non-CS schedule (classic KTU example):** $$S : r_1(A);\; w_1(A);\; r_2(A);\; w_2(A);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B)$$ ### Step 1: Conflict identification. - $w_1(A) \prec r_2(A)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $w_1(A) \prec w_2(A)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ - $w_2(B) \prec r_1(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ - $w_2(B) \prec w_1(B)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ ### Step 2: Build the Precedence Graph. $$T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 \rightarrow T_1$$ ### Step 3: Cycle Detection. $$\text{Cycle: } T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{CYCLE EXISTS}$$ ### Step 4: Conclusion. $$\boxed{\text{Since the PG contains a cycle, } S \text{ is NOT CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE.}}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix | Precedence Graph Observation | Verdict | Serial Order (if exists) | |:-----------------------------|:--------|:-------------------------| | No edges between transactions | CS (any serial order works) | Any permutation | | DAG with $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ | CS | $T_1$ then $T_2$ | | DAG with $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ | CS | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ | | Cycle between any two nodes | NOT CS | None | | Cycle in subgraph of $k$ transactions | NOT CS | None | | Isolated node + DAG component | CS | Per topological sort of DAG component | ## 3.4 Mathematical Justification of the Cycle Test **Why does a cycle imply non-serializability?** > [!NOTE] > **Proof Sketch (for KTU theory questions)** > Suppose a cycle $T_{i_1} \rightarrow T_{i_2} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_{i_k} \rightarrow T_{i_1}$ exists. > - Edge $T_{i_1} \rightarrow T_{i_2}$ means some conflicting operation of $T_{i_1}$ must precede that of $T_{i_2}$. > - Following the cycle, $T_{i_1}$ must come **after** $T_{i_2}$ as well. > - Since the "before" relation is antisymmetric, $T_{i_1} \prec T_{i_1}$ yields a contradiction. > - Therefore, no topological ordering exists $\Rightarrow$ no equivalent serial schedule exists. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Conflict Serializability Decision Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Input Schedule S] --> parseOps[Parse Operations
Identify T1, T2 ... Tn
and data items A, B ...] parseOps --> scanItems[For each data item X
scan all operations] scanItems --> conflictCheck{Conflicting pair
on same item X
across different
transactions?} conflictCheck -- No --> scanItems conflictCheck -- Yes --> drawEdge[Draw directed edge
Ti to Tj based on
execution order] drawEdge --> scanItems scanItems --> buildDone{All items
scanned?} buildDone -- No --> scanItems buildDone -- Yes --> buildPG[Precedence Graph
Constructed] buildPG --> cycleCheck{Cycle exists
in graph?} cycleCheck -- Yes --> notCS[Schedule is NOT
Conflict Serializable] cycleCheck -- No --> isCS[Schedule is
Conflict Serializable] isCS --> topoSort[Perform Topological Sort] topoSort --> serialOrder[Equivalent Serial Order
Recovered] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Example Precedence Graph (Conflict Serializable Case) ```mermaid graph LR t1[T1] -- rw conflict on A --> t2[T2] t1 -- ww conflict on B --> t3[T3] t2 -- wr conflict on C --> t3 ``` **Interpretation:** Acyclic DAG $\Rightarrow$ Conflict Serializable. Topological order: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$. ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Example Precedence Graph (NOT Conflict Serializable) ```mermaid graph LR t1a[T1] -- w1A before r2A --> t2a[T2] t2a -- w2B before r1B --> t1a ``` **Interpretation:** Cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ exists $\Rightarrow$ **NOT conflict serializable**. ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Function-Level Architecture of a Serializability Checker ```mermaid flowchart TD inputModule[Schedule Input
Parser Module] --> conflictEngine[Conflict Detection
Engine] conflictEngine --> pgBuilder[Precedence Graph
Builder] pgBuilder --> cycleDetector[DFS-based
Cycle Detector] cycleDetector --> decider{Graph
Acyclic?} decider -- Yes --> topoEngine[Topological
Sort Engine] decider -- No --> rejectModule[Reject as
Non-Serializable] topoEngine --> outModule[Output:
Serial Order] rejectModule --> outModule ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define **conflict serializability**. When is a schedule said to be conflict serializable? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A schedule $S$ is said to be **conflict serializable** if it is conflict equivalent to some serial schedule of the same set of transactions. Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are **conflict equivalent** if: - They contain the **same set of transactions** and **same set of operations**. - The order of every pair of **conflicting operations** (operations belonging to different transactions, on the same data item, where at least one is a `WRITE`) is the **same** in both schedules. A non-serial schedule is conflict serializable if we can transform it into a serial schedule by **swapping only non-conflicting operations**. Such a schedule preserves database consistency. *(Expected length: 3-4 sentences for full marks.)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is a **precedence graph**? State the rule for testing conflict serializability using it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **precedence graph** (or serialization graph) $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph used to test whether a schedule is conflict serializable. - **Vertices ($V$):** One node per transaction $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$. - **Edges ($E$):** A directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ is added if there exists a pair of conflicting operations on the same data item such that $T_i$'s operation executes **before** $T_j$'s operation in the schedule. **Rule:** A schedule is **conflict serializable if and only if its precedence graph is acyclic (DAG)**. If the graph has a cycle, the schedule is **not conflict serializable**. The equivalent serial order is found by performing a topological sort on the acyclic graph. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do not** draw an edge for a `$R$-$R$` pair — examiners deduct **1 mark** per such error. > - **Do not** draw an edge between operations of the *same* transaction — common mistake. > - Always verify that conflicts are on the **same data item** before drawing edges. > - For **cycle detection**, ensure you check **all paths**, not just immediate neighbors. > - In topological sort, if multiple nodes have zero in-degree, any order among them is acceptable — do not invent extra edges. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set Option **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **a) (7 marks)** Consider the following schedule $S$ involving three transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$: $$S : r_2(A);\; r_1(B);\; w_2(A);\; r_3(A);\; w_1(B);\; w_3(A);\; r_2(B);\; w_2(B);\; r_1(A);\; w_1(A)$$ Test whether $S$ is **conflict serializable**. If yes, determine the equivalent serial schedule. **b) (7 marks)** Explain the concept of **view serializability**. State **two differences** between view serializability and conflict serializability with a suitable example where a schedule is view serializable but not conflict serializable. --- ### Model Answer for Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: List operations in order with index and data item.** | Index | Op | Item | Trans | |:------|:---|:-----|:------| | 1 | $r$ | $A$ | $T_2$ | | 2 | $r$ | $B$ | $T_1$ | | 3 | $w$ | $A$ | $T_2$ | | 4 | $r$ | $A$ | $T_3$ | | 5 | $w$ | $B$ | $T_1$ | | 6 | $w$ | $A$ | $T_3$ | | 7 | $r$ | $B$ | $T_2$ | | 8 | $w$ | $B$ | $T_2$ | | 9 | $r$ | $A$ | $T_1$ | | 10 | $w$ | $A$ | $T_1$ | **Step 2: Identify all cross-transaction conflicts (same data item, at least one write).** Scanning **item $A$**: - $r_2(A)$ at index 1, $w_2(A)$ at index 3 — same transaction $T_2$, **skip**. - $w_2(A)$ at index 3, $r_3(A)$ at index 4 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$. - $w_2(A)$ at index 3, $w_3(A)$ at index 6 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$. - $w_2(A)$ at index 3, $r_1(A)$ at index 9 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. - $w_2(A)$ at index 3, $w_1(A)$ at index 10 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. - $w_3(A)$ at index 6, $r_1(A)$ at index 9 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$. - $w_3(A)$ at index 6, $w_1(A)$ at index 10 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$. Scanning **item $B$**: - $r_1(B)$ at index 2, $w_1(B)$ at index 5 — same $T_1$, skip. - $r_1(B)$ at index 2, $r_2(B)$ at index 7 — both reads, **NO CONFLICT**. - $r_1(B)$ at index 2, $w_2(B)$ at index 8 — **CONFLICT ($R$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. - $w_1(B)$ at index 5, $r_2(B)$ at index 7 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. - $w_1(B)$ at index 5, $w_2(B)$ at index 8 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. **Step 3: Draw the precedence graph.** Edges collected: - $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ - $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ - $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$ - $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ **Step 4: Cycle detection.** A cycle exists: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$? Let's verify: - $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ ✓ - $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ ✓ - $T_3 \rightarrow T_1$ ✓ Yes, the cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$ exists. **Step 5: Conclusion.** $$\boxed{\text{Since the precedence graph contains a cycle, schedule } S \text{ is NOT CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE.}}$$ *(Marks distribution: 1 mark for op listing, 3 marks for correct conflict edges, 2 marks for cycle detection, 1 mark for final conclusion.)* --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **View Serializability Definition (3 marks):** Two schedules $S$ and $S'$ are **view equivalent** if they satisfy three conditions: 1. **Initial Read:** For each data item $X$, if $T_i$ reads the initial value of $X$ in $S$, then $T_i$ must also read the initial value of $X$ in $S'$. 2. **Final Write:** For each data item $X$, the transaction that performs the **final write** on $X$ in $S$ must be the same as the one in $S'$. 3. **Read-From Relationship:** If $T_i$ reads a value of $X$ written by $T_j$ in $S$, then $T_i$ must also read a value of $X$ written by $T_j$ in $S'$. A schedule is **view serializable** if it is view equivalent to some serial schedule. **Two Differences (2 marks each):** | Aspect | Conflict Serializability | View Serializability | |:-------|:-------------------------|:---------------------| | **Basis** | Order of conflicting operations | Reads-from, final-write, and initial-read relationships | | **Strength** | More restrictive (stricter test) | Less restrictive (broader class of serializable schedules) | | **Complexity** | Polynomial time test (PG acyclicity) | NP-complete to test | | **Subset Relation** | Every CS schedule is also VS | Some VS schedules are not CS | **Blind-Write Example (2 marks):** Consider the schedule: $$S : w_1(A);\; w_2(A);\; w_3(A)$$ - Precedence graph has edges $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, $T_2 \rightarrow T_3$, and $T_1 \rightarrow T_3$ (due to blind writes on $A$). This is a **DAG** with order $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$ — so $S$ is **conflict serializable**. The classic non-CS-but-VS example: $$S : w_1(X);\; w_2(X);\; w_3(X);\; r_2(X);\; r_1(X)$$ Here, $T_2$ reads the value written by $T_3$, and $T_1$ reads the value written by $T_2$. The view-serial schedule $T_1, T_2, T_3$ preserves these reads-from relationships, so $S$ is **view serializable** but **not conflict serializable** (the precedence graph has cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternate Option **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **a) (7 marks)** Consider the following schedule: $$S : r_1(A);\; r_2(B);\; r_3(C);\; w_1(A);\; w_2(B);\; w_3(C);\; r_1(B);\; w_1(B);\; r_2(C);\; w_2(C)$$ Construct the precedence graph. Is the schedule conflict serializable? If yes, find the equivalent serial order. **b) (7 marks)** Explain the three types of **conflicts** that can occur between concurrent transactions with one example each. Why is read-read not a conflict? --- ### Model Answer for Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: List operations and detect conflicts on same data items.** Operations on $A$: $r_1(A)$ (idx 1), $w_1(A)$ (idx 4) — same transaction, skip cross-conflict. Operations on $B$: $r_2(B)$ (idx 2), $w_2(B)$ (idx 5), $r_1(B)$ (idx 7), $w_1(B)$ (idx 8). - $r_2(B)$ at idx 2, $r_1(B)$ at idx 7 — both reads, **NO CONFLICT**. - $r_2(B)$ at idx 2, $w_1(B)$ at idx 8 — **CONFLICT ($R$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. - $w_2(B)$ at idx 5, $r_1(B)$ at idx 7 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. - $w_2(B)$ at idx 5, $w_1(B)$ at idx 8 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. - $r_1(B)$ at idx 7, $w_1(B)$ at idx 8 — same transaction, skip. Operations on $C$: $r_3(C)$ (idx 3), $w_3(C)$ (idx 6), $r_2(C)$ (idx 9), $w_2(C)$ (idx 10). - $r_3(C)$ at idx 3, $r_2(C)$ at idx 9 — both reads, **NO CONFLICT**. - $r_3(C)$ at idx 3, $w_2(C)$ at idx 10 — **CONFLICT ($R$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$. - $w_3(C)$ at idx 6, $r_2(C)$ at idx 9 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$R$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$. - $w_3(C)$ at idx 6, $w_2(C)$ at idx 10 — **CONFLICT ($W$-$W$)** $\Rightarrow$ $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$. - $r_2(C)$ at idx 9, $w_2(C)$ at idx 10 — same transaction, skip. **Step 2: Precedence graph edges summary.** - $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ (from $B$ conflicts) - $T_3 \rightarrow T_2$ (from $C$ conflicts) **Step 3: Cycle detection.** Graph: $T_3 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$. No reverse edges, no cycle. **Step 4: Conclusion.** $$\boxed{\text{Schedule is CONFLICT SERIALIZABLE. Equivalent serial order: } T_3 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1.}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The Three Conflict Types (2 marks each + 1 for explanation of non-conflict):** 1. **Write-Read ($W$-$R$) Conflict:** - $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ reads $X$. - Example: $w_1(A);\; r_2(A)$ — $T_2$ reads a value dependent on whether $T_1$ has committed. 2. **Read-Write ($R$-$W$) Conflict:** - $T_i$ reads $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$. - Example: $r_1(A);\; w_2(A)$ — $T_1$'s subsequent computation may use a stale value. 3. **Write-Write ($W$-$W$) Conflict:** - $T_i$ writes $X$, then $T_j$ writes $X$. - Example: $w_1(A);\; w_2(A)$ — final value of $A$ depends on write order (lost update problem). **Why Read-Read is Not a Conflict (1 mark):** Read-Read operations merely *inspect* the data; they do not modify the database state. The order in which two reads execute has no impact on the value read (assuming no write intervenes). Therefore, swapping two reads produces no observable difference in the schedule, and no precedence constraint is needed between $T_i$ and $T_j$ for read-read pairs. This is precisely why read-read pairs are ignored when constructing the precedence graph. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Conflict** involves two operations on the *same data item*, from *different transactions*, with at least one being a `WRITE`. - **Three conflict types:** $W$-$R$, $R$-$W$, $W$-$W$. Read-Read is **NOT** a conflict. - **Conflict Equivalence:** Same transactions, same operations, same order of conflicting pairs. - **Conflict Serializable:** Conflict equivalent to some serial schedule. - **Precedence Graph Test:** $S$ is CS $\iff$ PG is acyclic (DAG). - **Cycle exists $\Rightarrow$ NOT conflict serializable.** No cycle $\Rightarrow$ CS, and serial order is given by **topological sort**. - **Topological sort algorithm:** Repeatedly pick a node with zero in-degree, remove its edges, and append to order. - **Time complexity** of CS testing: $O(V + E)$ using DFS-based cycle detection. - **Conflict serializability is a sufficient but not necessary condition** for serializability — view serializable schedules are a strict superset. - **Common KTU traps:** Same-transaction pairs, read-read pairs, and different data items must NOT generate edges. - **Blind writes** (writes without prior reads) can sometimes allow view but not conflict equivalence — a classic interview + exam question. - **Real-world analogy:** Think of two chefs sharing a single salt jar — the *order* of "add salt" and "taste" actions matters, but inspecting the label twice is fine. - **Examiner tip:** Always state the **theorem** ("A schedule is conflict serializable iff its PG is acyclic") before applying it — this gives a free 1 mark for the conceptual framing.

topics.title: Recoverable and Cascade-Less Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recoverable and Cascade-Less Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Schedule? (Quick Recap)

A **schedule** is a chronological sequence of interleaved operations (read/write/commit/abort) from one or more concurrent transactions. The DBMS executes these operations atomically while preserving the logical order defined within each transaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: This sub-topic falls under *Transaction Management* and is a high-frequency board question. Every year, at least one 7-mark or 14-mark question is asked on schedule classifications.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Recoverable Schedule

A **Recoverable Schedule** is a schedule in which, for every transaction $T_i$ that performs a **read** operation on a data item previously written by transaction $T_j$, $T_i$ commits **only after** $T_j$ commits.

Formally, in the **precedence graph** (also called the *Transaction Dependency Graph*):

$$\text{If } T_j \xrightarrow{\text{wr}} T_i \quad \text{(write-read conflict)}, \quad \text{then} \quad T_j \prec T_i \text{ in commit order}$$

If $T_j$ fails (aborts) **after** $T_i$ has read its uncommitted value, the schedule becomes **non-recoverable**, because $T_i$ must be undone even though it had committed — a violation of the durability property.

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Cascadeless Schedule

A **Cascadeless Schedule** (also called *cascade-free* or *avoiding cascading aborts*) is a schedule in which transactions **only read values written by transactions that have already committed**.

Mathematically:

$$\forall \text{ read}(x) \text{ by } T_i \text{ from } T_j: \quad T_j \text{ must have committed before the read}$$

This is a **stronger** condition than recoverability, because it **eliminates** the possibility of cascading rollbacks at the source.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Group Project" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of transactions as student group members working on a shared Google Doc:**

| Real-World Situation | Database Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Student A writes a draft but hasn't submitted yet | Transaction $T_A$ performs `WRITE` (uncommitted) |
| Student B copies A's draft into their section | Transaction $T_B$ performs `READ` of $T_A$'s uncommitted data |
| Student A realizes they made mistakes & deletes their draft | $T_A$ **aborts** |
| Student B's section is now based on deleted content — must be redone | $T_B$ must also be **rolled back** ⇒ **Cascading Rollback** |

- **Non-Recoverable** = B submits the paper **before** A submits, and A later withdraws. The professor has to revoke B's already-graded submission. ❌
- **Recoverable** = B waits to submit until **after** A submits. If A withdraws later, B can safely re-copy or revert. ✅
- **Cascadeless** = B is **never allowed** to copy from A's draft. B only copies after A has submitted. ✅✅
- **Strict** = B is never allowed to even *overwrite* A's work until A submits. ✅✅✅

## 1.5 The Hierarchy of Schedule Safety

The four schedule classes are nested:

$$\text{Strict} \subset \text{Cascadeless} \subset \text{Recoverable} \subset \text{All Serialisable/Non-Serialisable Schedules}$$

This is a **subset relationship**: every strict schedule is cascade-less; every cascade-less schedule is recoverable.

## 1.6 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Terms (Board-Favourite Definitions)**
> - **Dirty Read** — Reading a value written by an uncommitted transaction.
> - **Cascading Rollback** — A single transaction failure that forces the abort of one or more dependent transactions.
> - **Recoverable Schedule** — No transaction commits before the transactions it depends on commit.
> - **Cascadeless Schedule** — Dirty reads are completely forbidden.
> - **Strict Schedule** — Dirty reads **and** dirty writes are forbidden.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are These Concepts Critical?

A DBMS must guarantee **Atomicity** and **Durability** even when concurrent transactions fail. The schedule classification system gives us a **lattice of safety guarantees** — stronger classes cost less in recovery effort but more in concurrency.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — When is a Schedule Each Type?

### Step-by-Step Decision Logic

1. **Inspect every `READ(x)` operation** in the schedule.
2. Identify the **writer** of $x$ (the most recent transaction that wrote $x$).
3. Check whether the **writer has committed** before the read.
   - If **never dirty reads** exist ⇒ **Cascadeless** (and therefore also Recoverable).
4. If dirty reads exist, check whether every reading transaction **commits AFTER** its dependency.
   - If yes ⇒ **Recoverable** (but not cascade-less).
   - If no ⇒ **Non-recoverable**.

## 2.3 Formal Conditions Table

| Schedule Type | Condition on `READ` | Condition on `WRITE` | Recoverable? | Avoids Cascading Aborts? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Recoverable** | Dirty reads allowed | Dirty writes allowed | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Recoverable** | Dirty reads allowed, but reader commits after writer | Dirty writes allowed | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Cascadeless** | Only reads committed data | Dirty writes allowed | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Strict** | Only reads committed data | Only overwrites committed data | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Decision Cheat Sheet

| # | Decision Rule | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dirty read condition | $\exists \text{read}_i(x) \text{ occurring before commit}_j$, where $T_j$ last wrote $x$ |
| 2 | Recoverable rule | $\forall T_i, T_j: \text{ if } T_j \xrightarrow{\text{wr}} T_i \Rightarrow \text{commit}(T_j) \prec \text{commit}(T_i)$ |
| 3 | Cascadeless rule | $\forall \text{read}_i(x) \text{ from } T_j: \text{commit}(T_j) \prec \text{read}_i(x)$ |
| 4 | Strict rule | $\forall \text{read}_i(x), \text{write}_i(x) \text{ on } T_j\text{'s data: } \text{commit}(T_j) \prec \text{op}_i(x)$ |
| 5 | Subset relation | $\vert S_{\text{strict}} \vert \subseteq \vert S_{\text{cascadeless}} \vert \subseteq \vert S_{\text{recoverable}} \vert \subseteq \vert S_{\text{all}} \vert$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip**: Examiners LOVE asking: *"Is every cascade-less schedule recoverable? Justify."* The answer is **YES** because the subset chain is a transitive property — and a 2-line justification with the subset chain scores full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: Use **Strict** schedules so that no transaction reads/writes pending balances, preventing cascade failures during rollbacks.
- **E-commerce Order Processing**: Often uses **Recoverable** with application-level safeguards to balance throughput and safety.
- **Distributed Databases (Spanner, CockroachDB)**: Strict 2PL + snapshot isolation = effectively **Strict** schedules across data centres.
- **Air Traffic Control Databases**: **Strict** is mandatory — no transaction may read or modify uncommitted flight data.

## 2.6 What is a "Dirty Write"?

> [!NOTE]
> A **dirty write** occurs when a transaction overwrites a value written by another **uncommitted** transaction. For example, $T_1$ writes $x = 100$ (uncommitted) and $T_2$ writes $x = 200$ (still uncommitted). If $T_1$ aborts, we have a problem resolving $x$. Strict schedules forbid this.

## 2.7 Distinguishing Marks for Board Answers

When justifying a schedule as cascade-less, you must explicitly write:

> *"No transaction in the schedule reads a data item written by an uncommitted transaction. Therefore, if any transaction fails, no other transaction will be forced to roll back, and the schedule is cascade-less."*

When justifying recoverable:

> *"Although dirty reads occur, every transaction that reads uncommitted data waits for the writer to commit before committing itself. Hence, the schedule is recoverable."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A *Cascadeless* Schedule

Consider three transactions operating on data item $A$:

| $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ |
|---|---|---|
| `read(A)` | | |
| `write(A)` | | |
| `commit` | | |
| | `read(A)` | |
| | `write(A)` | |
| | `commit` | |
| | | `read(A)` |
| | | `write(A)` |
| | | `commit` |

**Interleaved schedule** $S_1$:

$$
S_1 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; c_1 \; r_2(A) \; w_2(A) \; c_2 \; r_3(A) \; w_3(A) \; c_3
$$

**Analysis:**

- $T_2$ reads $A$ **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ no dirty read.
- $T_3$ reads $A$ **after** $T_2$ commits ⇒ no dirty read.
- Therefore $S_1$ is **Cascadeless**, **Recoverable**, and **Strict**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — A *Recoverable* (but NOT Cascadeless) Schedule

| $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
|---|---|
| `read(A)` | |
| `write(A)` | |
| | `read(A)`  ← **dirty read** |
| `commit` | |
| | `commit` |

Schedule $S_2$:

$$
S_2 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; c_1 \; c_2
$$

**Step-by-step classification:**

**Step 1** — Check for dirty reads.

$T_2$ reads $A$ written by $T_1$, but $T_1$ has not yet committed. **Dirty read exists.**

**Step 2** — Check for cascading rollback potential.

If $T_1$ were to abort, $T_2$ would also have to roll back. So $S_2$ is **not cascade-less**.

**Step 3** — Check recoverability.

$T_2$ commits **after** $T_1$ commits. If $T_1$ aborts, $T_2$ can be cleanly rolled back (no committed dependency). **Recoverable.**

$$\boxed{S_2 \text{ is RECOVERABLE but NOT CASCADELESS}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — A *Non-Recoverable* Schedule

| $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
|---|---|
| `read(A)` | |
| `write(A)` | |
| | `read(A)` ← dirty read |
| | `commit`  ← **commits BEFORE $T_1$** |
| `commit` | |

Schedule $S_3$:

$$
S_3 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; c_2 \; c_1
$$

**Step-by-step classification:**

**Step 1** — Dirty read exists ($T_2$ reads $T_1$'s uncommitted $A$).

**Step 2** — $T_2$ commits **before** $T_1$ commits.

**Step 3** — If $T_1$ aborts, $T_2$ has already committed and is therefore irretrievable. The system must perform an **impossible operation** — undoing a committed transaction.

$$\boxed{S_3 \text{ is NON-RECOVERABLE} \implies \text{UNACCEPTABLE for any real DBMS}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Strict vs Cascadeless Distinction

| $T_1$ | $T_2$ |
|---|---|
| `read(A)` | |
| `write(A)` | |
| `commit` | |
| | `read(A)`  ← reads committed value (OK) |
| | `write(A)` |

Schedule $S_4$:

$$
S_4 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; c_1 \; r_2(A) \; w_2(A)
$$

**Analysis:**

- $T_2$ reads $A$ **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ no dirty read ⇒ **cascadeless** ✓
- $T_2$ **writes** $A$ (overwrites $T_1$'s data) **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ no dirty write ⇒ **strict** ✓

Now consider $S_5$:

$$
S_5 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; c_1 \; r_2(A) \; w_3(A)
$$

Wait — $T_3$ overwrites $A$ (a different value) **without reading** — this is fine. But suppose:

$$
S_6 : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; w_2(A) \; c_1 \; c_2
$$

**Analysis of $S_6$:**

- $T_2$ **writes** $A$ **before** $T_1$ commits ⇒ **dirty write exists**.
- $T_1$ then commits, but if $T_1$ had aborted, the value of $A$ is ambiguous.
- $S_6$ is **Cascadeless**? Let's check: did $T_2$ read from $T_1$? **No.** So no cascading abort issue from $T_2$ itself.
- $S_6$ is **Recoverable**? $T_1$ commits after $T_2$'s write. If $T_1$ aborts, $T_2$ is not forced to roll back (no read dependency from $T_1$).
- $S_6$ is **Strict**? **No** — because $T_2$ performed a dirty write (wrote over $T_1$'s uncommitted value).

$$\boxed{S_6 \text{ is CASCADELESS and RECOVERABLE, but NOT STRICT}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Full Worked View)

| Schedule | Dirty Read? | Dirty Write? | Reader Commits Before Writer? | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | No | No | N/A | **Strict, Cascadeless, Recoverable** |
| $S_2$ | Yes | No | No (reader waits) | **Recoverable only** |
| $S_3$ | Yes | No | Yes (reader commits first) | **Non-Recoverable** |
| $S_4$ | No | No | N/A | **Strict, Cascadeless, Recoverable** |
| $S_6$ | No | Yes | N/A | **Cascadeless, Recoverable, NOT Strict** |

## 3.6 Derivation — Why the Subset Chain Holds

**Claim:** $S_{\text{strict}} \subseteq S_{\text{cascadeless}}$

**Proof:**

Let $S \in S_{\text{strict}}$. By definition of strict, no transaction in $S$ reads or writes a value produced by an uncommitted transaction. In particular, **no transaction reads uncommitted data**. This is exactly the definition of cascadeless. Hence $S \in S_{\text{cascadeless}}$. $\blacksquare$

**Claim:** $S_{\text{cascadeless}} \subseteq S_{\text{recoverable}}$

**Proof:**

Let $S \in S_{\text{cascadeless}}$. Then every read in $S$ is from a committed transaction, so the writer has always committed **before** the reader executes. Therefore the reader trivially commits after the writer, satisfying the recoverability condition. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Recovery Cost Analysis

Let $n$ = number of transactions in a schedule, and $k$ = number of transactions that performed dirty reads from a failed transaction $T_f$.

$$
\text{Transactions rolled back} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{Strict / Cascadeless (only } T_f \text{ rolls back)} \\ 1 + k & \text{Recoverable (} T_f \text{ plus its } k \text{ dependents)} \\ \text{undefined} & \text{Non-Recoverable (system invariant violation)} \end{cases}
$$

This shows **quantitatively** why production systems mandate cascade-less or strict behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Schedule Classes (Venn Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["ALL SCHEDULES"]:::outer
    nodeB["RECOVERABLE"]:::middle
    nodeC["CASCADELESS"]:::inner
    nodeD["STRICT"]:::innermost

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD

    nodeX["NON RECOVERABLE"]:::bad
    nodeA --> nodeX

    classDef outer fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef middle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inner fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef innermost fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1e6bb8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#222,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Classifying Any Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START: Given Schedule S]):::start
    q1{Does any transaction read uncommitted data?}:::q
    r1[NO dirty reads]:::ok
    q2{Does any transaction write over uncommitted data?}:::q
    r2[STRICT]:::good
    q3{Does every dependent reader commit AFTER its writer?}:::q
    r3[CASCADELESS]:::ok
    r4[RECOVERABLE only]:::mid
    r5[NON RECOVERABLE]:::bad

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> q2
    q1 -- No --> r1 --> q2
    q2 -- No --> r2
    q2 -- Yes --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> r3
    q3 -- No --> r4

    classDef start fill:#1e6bb8,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef q fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1e6bb8,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#ffe1a3,stroke:#d35400,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Cascading Rollback — Time-Line Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Transaction T1
    participant T2 as Transaction T2
    participant T3 as Transaction T3
    participant DB as Database Log

    T1->>DB: WRITE A = 100 (uncommitted)
    T2->>DB: READ A = 100 (dirty read)
    T2->>DB: WRITE B = 200 (based on A)
    T3->>DB: READ B = 200 (dirty read)
    Note over T1: T1 ABORTS due to deadlock
    T1-->>DB: ROLLBACK - undo A
    T2-->>DB: CASCADE ROLLBACK - undo B
    T3-->>DB: CASCADE ROLLBACK - undo derived data
    Note over DB: Three transactions lost from one failure
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Recovery Subsystem Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userApp[Application Layer]:::app
    schedMon[Schedule Classifier]:::mon
    policy[Policy Lookup Table]:::policy
    logMgr[Log Manager]:::log
    lockMgr[Lock Manager]:::lock
    recEng[Recovery Engine]:::rec

    userApp --> schedMon
    schedMon --> policy
    policy -- Strict --> lockMgr
    policy -- Cascadeless --> recEng
    policy -- Recoverable --> logMgr
    lockMgr --> recEng
    logMgr --> recEng
    recEng --> userApp

    classDef app fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1e6bb8,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
    classDef policy fill:#e1d4f4,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef lock fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Precedence Graph Structure for Recoverability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1((T1)):::committed
    T2((T2)):::waiting
    T3((T3)):::reader

    T1 -- writes A --> T2
    T2 -- reads A --> T3
    T3 -- commits after T2 --> T1

    classDef committed fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef waiting fill:#d4a017,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef reader fill:#1e6bb8,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the graph**: An edge $T_i \to T_j$ means $T_j$ read a value produced by $T_i$. For recoverability, every edge must run **backwards in commit time order** — i.e., the source of the edge must commit before the destination.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a *recoverable schedule*. State one condition under which a schedule is said to be non-recoverable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A schedule is **recoverable** if for every transaction $T_i$ that reads a data item previously written by $T_j$, $T_i$ commits **only after** $T_j$ commits.

A schedule becomes **non-recoverable** when a transaction $T_i$ reads an uncommitted value from $T_j$ **and** commits **before** $T_j$ commits. If $T_j$ later aborts, the system cannot undo $T_i$ (since it has already committed), violating the durability property.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the condition, 1 mark for the consequence)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a *cascadeless schedule*? How is it different from a *strict schedule*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cascadeless schedule** is one in which transactions **only read data items written by already-committed transactions**. This prevents the situation where a single transaction's failure forces multiple others to roll back.

A **strict schedule** is a stronger condition — it forbids not only dirty reads but also **dirty writes** (overwriting uncommitted data). Therefore, **every strict schedule is cascadeless**, but the reverse is not always true.

*(1.5 marks for cascadeless definition, 1.5 marks for the strict schedule distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define the following with respect to transaction schedules: **Recoverable**, **Cascadeless**, and **Strict**. Show the subset relationship between these classes with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following schedule $S$ over two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
S : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; w_2(B) \; c_1 \; c_2
$$

Classify $S$ as recoverable, cascadeless, strict, or none. Justify your classification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Recoverable Schedule (1.5 marks):**
A schedule is recoverable if, for any pair of transactions $T_i$ and $T_j$ where $T_i$ reads a data item written by $T_j$, $T_i$ commits only after $T_j$ commits.

**Cascadeless Schedule (1.5 marks):**
A schedule is cascadeless if no transaction ever reads a data item written by an **uncommitted** transaction. In other words, $\text{commit}(T_j) \prec \text{read}_i(x)$ for every read from $T_j$.

**Strict Schedule (1.5 marks):**
A schedule is strict if no transaction reads **or writes** a data item that was last written by an uncommitted transaction. Both dirty reads and dirty writes are forbidden.

**Subset Diagram (2.5 marks):**

The hierarchical containment is:

$$S_{\text{strict}} \subset S_{\text{cascadeless}} \subset S_{\text{recoverable}} \subset S_{\text{all}}$$

(Include a nested diagram like Section 4.1 of these notes.)

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Inspect the schedule (2 marks):**

$$
S : r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; w_2(B) \; c_1 \; c_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify conflicts (2 marks):**

- $T_2$ performs $r_2(A)$ after $w_1(A)$.
- At that moment, $T_1$ has **not** yet committed.
- Therefore $T_2$ performs a **dirty read** of $A$ from $T_1$.

**Step 3 — Check recoverability (1.5 marks):**

$T_2$ commits **after** $T_1$ commits ($c_2$ occurs after $c_1$). If $T_1$ aborts, the system can roll back $T_2$ without any committed-state corruption. **Recoverable ✓**

**Step 4 — Check cascadeless property (1 mark):**

Dirty read exists, so $S$ is **NOT cascadeless** ✗

**Step 5 — Check strict property (0.5 mark):**

Since cascadeless fails, strict also fails. **NOT strict** ✗

$$\boxed{S \text{ is RECOVERABLE but NOT CASCADELESS and NOT STRICT}}$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **cascading rollback** with a suitable example. State the disadvantages of allowing cascading rollbacks in a DBMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following three schedules, classify each one and justify:

**Schedule $S_1$:**
$$
r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; c_1 \; r_2(A) \; w_2(A) \; c_2
$$

**Schedule $S_2$:**
$$
r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; c_2 \; c_1
$$

**Schedule $S_3$:**
$$
r_1(A) \; w_1(A) \; r_2(A) \; w_2(B) \; c_1 \; c_2
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **cascading rollback** (or *cascading abort*) occurs when the failure of a single transaction forces one or more transactions that have read its uncommitted data to be rolled back as well, which in turn may force others to roll back, propagating through the dependency chain.

**Example (3 marks):**
Consider:
- $T_1$ writes $A = 50$ (uncommitted).
- $T_2$ reads $A$ (dirty read), then writes $B = 80$ based on $A$.
- $T_3$ reads $B$, then writes $C$.

If $T_1$ aborts, then $T_2$ must roll back (because it read dirty $A$), and consequently $T_3$ must also roll back. **Three transactions fail due to one.**

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. **Performance cost** — A single failure can cause significant work to be wasted.
2. **Logical complexity** — Recovery code must trace back through entire dependency chains.
3. **Risk of non-recoverable states** — If a dependent transaction has already committed before its writer aborts, the database is left in an inconsistent state.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Schedule $S_1$ (2 marks):**
- $T_2$ reads $A$ **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ no dirty read.
- $T_2$ writes $A$ **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ no dirty write.
- **Classification: Strict, Cascadeless, and Recoverable.**

**Schedule $S_2$ (2.5 marks):**
- $T_2$ reads $A$ before $T_1$ commits ⇒ **dirty read**.
- $T_2$ commits **before** $T_1$ commits.
- If $T_1$ aborts, $T_2$ cannot be rolled back because it is already committed ⇒ **non-recoverable** situation.
- **Classification: Non-Recoverable. (Unacceptable in any DBMS.)**

**Schedule $S_3$ (2.5 marks):**
- $T_2$ reads $A$ before $T_1$ commits ⇒ **dirty read**.
- $T_2$ commits **after** $T_1$ commits ⇒ if $T_1$ aborts, $T_2$ can be cleanly rolled back.
- **Classification: Recoverable, but NOT Cascadeless and NOT Strict.**

| Schedule | Recoverable? | Cascadeless? | Strict? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| $S_2$ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| $S_3$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating "Cascadeless" with "Strict"** — Many students write that cascade-less = strict. They are different. Cascadeless forbids only dirty reads; strict forbids both dirty reads AND dirty writes. Lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Missing the commit-order check** — A schedule may contain a dirty read but still be recoverable *if* the reader waits. Always check the position of `commit` operations relative to dirty reads.
> 3. **Forgetting to consider dirty writes** — In multi-item schedules, students only check `READ` operations and ignore `WRITE` conflicts. This makes the strict classification wrong.
> 4. **Using "Transaction" instead of "Schedule" in the definition** — Be precise: it is the **schedule** that is recoverable/cascadeless/strict, not the **transaction**.
> 5. **Skipping the Venn diagram in 7-mark questions** — Examiners often allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the subset relationship diagram. Always include it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ A **Recoverable Schedule** guarantees that no committed transaction has to be undone — its only rule is commit-order preservation.
- ✅ A **Cascadeless Schedule** is one where no transaction reads uncommitted data — this **eliminates** cascading rollbacks at the root.
- ✅ A **Strict Schedule** forbids both dirty reads and dirty writes — it is the strongest practical class used in production DBMSs.
- ✅ Strict ⊂ Cascadeless ⊂ Recoverable ⊂ All Schedules — **memorize this chain**.
- ✅ **Dirty read** = reading uncommitted data. **Dirty write** = overwriting uncommitted data.
- ✅ A non-recoverable schedule is **unacceptable** in any real DBMS — the system simply cannot guarantee correctness under failures.
- ✅ Cascadeless schedules prevent **cascading aborts**, saving significant work and simplifying recovery logic.
- ✅ In a board answer, always (i) state the definition, (ii) check dirty reads, (iii) check dirty writes, (iv) verify commit ordering, (v) provide the classification with justification.
- ✅ KTU 2024 module 4 expects you to know the difference between **recoverable, cascadeless, and strict** schedules and to classify any given schedule correctly.
- ✅ Always include a Venn diagram or set-containment diagram in 7-mark answers — it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Locking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Locking

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)**, **Locking** is a fundamental **concurrency control mechanism** used to preserve the **ACID properties** (particularly **Isolation** and **Consistency**) when multiple transactions execute simultaneously on a shared database.

A **lock** is a variable associated with a data item (such as a tuple, row, page, or table) that describes the status of that item with respect to possible operations that other transactions can perform on it. A transaction must *acquire* the appropriate lock before accessing the data item and *release* it after use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Locking is a synchronization technique in which a transaction requests a lock on a data item before accessing it. The lock manager grants or denies the request based on compatibility with existing locks held by other concurrent transactions."*

Mathematically, a lock on data item $Q$ can be represented as a state variable:
$$
L(Q) \in \{ \text{FREE},\ \text{Shared}(S),\ \text{Exclusive}(X) \}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **public restroom in a railway station**:

- The door has a **lock with a sliding bolt**.
- When a person enters, they **slide the bolt** (acquire lock) and a small **red indicator** turns ON showing the restroom is occupied.
- A second person arrives, sees the red indicator, **waits** outside (transaction is blocked).
- When the first person leaves, they **unbolt the door** (release lock) and the indicator turns GREEN, allowing the next person to enter.
- If two people tried to use the same restroom simultaneously, **chaos and privacy violation** would occur — exactly what happens in an unsynchronized database.

The **lock manager** in a DBMS plays the role of the **door mechanism**, ensuring **mutual exclusion** for data items.

## 1.3 Why Locking is Needed

In a multi-user database environment, concurrent execution of transactions can lead to:

| Problem | Description | Locking Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Lost Update** | Two transactions read and write the same item, one update is overwritten | Exclusive lock during write |
| **Dirty Read** | Reading uncommitted data from another transaction | Hold locks until commit |
| **Non-repeatable Read** | Same query returns different values within a transaction | Shared locks held for transaction duration |
| **Phantom Read** | New rows appear in repeated queries | Range/Table locks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Locking is the most widely used approach to ensure **conflict-serializable** schedules because it is **easy to implement** and provides **practical isolation** without requiring timestamps.

## 1.4 Types of Locks — Quick Overview

There are two primary lock modes in standard DBMS textbooks:

1. **Shared Lock (S-lock or Read Lock)**: Allows a transaction to *read* a data item. Multiple transactions can hold shared locks simultaneously on the same item.
2. **Exclusive Lock (X-lock or Write Lock)**: Allows a transaction to *write* (read & modify) a data item. Only one transaction can hold an exclusive lock, and no other locks (S or X) can coexist with it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lock Modes in Detail

### 2.1.1 Shared Lock (S-Lock)

A transaction $T_i$ requests a **shared lock** on item $Q$ using the `lock-S(Q)` instruction. The DBMS lock manager checks:

- If $L(Q) = \text{FREE}$, it grants the lock and sets $L(Q) = \text{Shared}$.
- If $L(Q) = \text{Shared}$, it grants the lock (multiple readers allowed).
- If $L(Q) = \text{Exclusive}$, $T_i$ is **blocked** until the X-lock is released.

A shared lock is released using the `unlock(Q)` instruction at the end of the transaction (or at a 2PL release point).

### 2.1.2 Exclusive Lock (X-Lock)

A transaction $T_i$ requests an **exclusive lock** on item $Q$ using the `lock-X(Q)` instruction:

- If $L(Q) = \text{FREE}$, it grants the lock and sets $L(Q) = \text{Exclusive}$.
- If $L(Q) = \text{Shared}$ or $\text{Exclusive}$, $T_i$ is **blocked**.

The exclusive lock allows both **read** and **write** operations on $Q$.

### 2.1.3 Lock Upgrade

A transaction holding an S-lock may **request an X-lock** on the same item — this is called **lock upgrade**. The lock manager grants it *only* if no other transaction holds an S-lock on that item. Otherwise, the transaction is blocked, and **deadlock** may occur.

## 2.2 Lock Compatibility Matrix (THE Most Important Table for KTU)

This table is examined in nearly every KTU paper. The entry indicates whether a lock request can be **granted** (✓) or **must wait** (✗).

| Requested Lock | Held: None | Held: S | Held: X |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | ✓ Grant | ✓ Grant | ✗ Wait |
| **X** | ✓ Grant | ✗ Wait | ✗ Wait |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU Exams:** Two S-locks can coexist, but an X-lock is *incompatible* with any other lock (S or X).

## 2.3 Two-Phase Locking Protocol (2PL)

**Two-Phase Locking** is the most important locking protocol in KTU DBMS. A transaction is said to follow the 2PL protocol if **all lock operations precede the first unlock operation**.

The transaction has two distinct phases:

### Phase 1: Growing Phase
- The transaction **acquires** locks (S or X) on data items.
- **No locks are released.**
- This is the only phase where `lock-S()` and `lock-X()` can be called.

### Phase 2: Shrinking Phase
- The transaction **releases** locks.
- **No new locks can be acquired.**
- Only `unlock()` calls are permitted.

There is a **single instant** — the **lock point** — which is the moment when the transaction has acquired its final lock (the end of the growing phase).

$$
\text{Lock Point} = \text{Time of last } lock\text{-}S() \text{ or } lock\text{-}X() \text{ call}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Result:** Every schedule produced by a set of transactions following the 2PL protocol is **conflict-serializable**. The serialization order is determined by the **lock points**.

## 2.4 Variants of 2PL

| Variant | Behavior | Avoids Cascading Aborts? | Recovers from abort? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic 2PL** | Locks released after lock point | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Conservative 2PL** | All locks acquired *before* transaction begins | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Strict 2PL** | All X-locks held until commit/abort | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | All locks (S and X) held until commit/abort | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most used in practice:** **Strict 2PL** and **Rigorous 2PL** because they guarantee recoverability and avoid cascading rollbacks. They are used in real systems like IBM DB2, Oracle, and MySQL InnoDB.

## 2.5 Deadlock in Locking

A **deadlock** occurs when two or more transactions are each **waiting for a lock held by the other**, forming a cycle. Neither can proceed.

### 2.5.1 Wait-for Graph (WFG)

A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- Each transaction is a node: $V = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$
- A directed edge $T_i \to T_j$ exists if $T_i$ is waiting for a lock held by $T_j$

> **A deadlock exists if and only if the WFG contains a cycle.**

### 2.5.2 Deadlock Handling Strategies

**Strategy 1: Deadlock Prevention** — Ensures the system never enters a deadlock state.
- **Wait-Die Scheme (Non-preemptive)**: Older transaction waits; younger transaction dies (rolls back).
- **Wound-Wait Scheme (Preemptive)**: Older transaction wounds (rolls back) the younger; younger waits.

**Strategy 2: Deadlock Detection** — Allows deadlocks to occur, detects them via WFG cycles, and resolves by aborting a victim transaction.

**Strategy 3: Deadlock Avoidance** — Uses timestamps or resource ordering; rarely used in DBMS.

**Strategy 4: Timeout-Based** — A transaction is aborted if it waits longer than a predefined timeout. Simple but can abort innocent transactions.

## 2.6 Starvation

**Starvation** occurs when a transaction waits indefinitely for a lock. Common in lock-upgrade or unfair scheduling. **Solution**: Use **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** queue in the lock manager, or priority aging.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Lock Compatibility** | $S \leftrightarrow S = \text{Compatible}$ | All other pairs incompatible |
| **2PL Condition** | $\forall T_i,\ \text{last unlock} > \text{last lock}$ | Strict ordering required |
| **Lock Point** | $\text{LP}(T_i) = \max(\text{lock acquire times})$ | Determines serial order |
| **Deadlock (WFG cycle)** | $\exists \text{ cycle in } G(V, E)$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| **Wait-Die Rule** | If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$: $T_i$ waits, else $T_i$ dies | Older waits, younger dies |
| **Wound-Wait Rule** | If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$: $T_j$ is wounded, else $T_i$ waits | Older wounds, younger waits |
| **2PL Serializability** | $S$ is conflict-serializable | If all $T_i$ follow 2PL |
| **Lock Granularity** | DB $\supset$ Table $\supset$ Page $\supset$ Row $\supset$ Attribute | Smaller = more concurrency, more overhead |
| **MGL Intention Lock Types** | $IS,\ IX,\ SIX$ | Intention Shared, Intention Exclusive, Shared-Intention-Exclusive |
| **Phantom Avoidance** | Predicate locking or index locking | Prevents new tuples in range |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Wait-Die** scheme is **non-preemptive** (it only rolls back the younger transaction that made the request). The **Wound-Wait** scheme is **preemptive** (it rolls back the younger transaction already holding the lock). Both are **deadlock-free** but may cause **starvation**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Locking is the cornerstone of every commercial RDBMS:

- **Oracle**: Uses a variant of **Strict 2PL** with row-level granularity and multi-version read consistency.
- **MySQL InnoDB**: Implements **Rigorous 2PL** with next-key locking to prevent phantoms.
- **PostgreSQL**: Uses **MVCC** combined with **2PL** for write locks.
- **SQL Server**: Supports **lock escalation** and **partition-level** locks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof Sketch: 2PL Guarantees Conflict-Serializability

**Claim:** If every transaction in schedule $S$ follows the 2PL protocol, then $S$ is conflict-serializable.

**Proof Outline by Contradiction:**

Suppose, for contradiction, that schedule $S$ is **not** conflict-serializable. Then the **precedence graph** of $S$ must contain a cycle. Let the cycle be:

$$
T_{a_1} \to T_{a_2} \to T_{a_3} \to \cdots \to T_{a_k} \to T_{a_1}
$$

By the definition of the precedence graph, an edge $T_{a_i} \to T_{a_{i+1}}$ exists if and only if there exists a data item $Q$ such that:

$$
T_{a_i} \text{ executes } \text{lock-}X(Q) \text{ before } T_{a_{i+1}} \text{ acquires } \text{lock-}X(Q)
$$

This means $T_{a_i}$ enters its **shrinking phase** *before* $T_{a_{i+1}}$ enters its **growing phase** ends with respect to $Q$.

Formally, let $LP(T)$ be the lock point of transaction $T$. From the edge definition:

$$
LP(T_{a_1}) < LP(T_{a_2}) < LP(T_{a_3}) < \cdots < LP(T_{a_k})
$$

But the cycle demands $T_{a_k} \to T_{a_1}$, which forces:

$$
LP(T_{a_k}) < LP(T_{a_1})
$$

This contradicts the strict inequality chain. Hence, no cycle can exist, and the precedence graph is acyclic. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The **lock point ordering** of 2PL transactions directly corresponds to a **valid serial order**. This is the deepest theoretical reason why 2PL works.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example: 2PL Schedule Construction

Consider two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ operating on data items $A$ and $B$.

**Transaction Definitions:**

$$
T_1: \text{lock-}S(A);\ \text{read}(A);\ \text{lock-}X(B);\ \text{read}(B);\ B := B + 50;\ \text{write}(B);\ \text{unlock}(A);\ \text{unlock}(B)
$$

$$
T_2: \text{lock-}S(B);\ \text{read}(B);\ \text{lock-}X(A);\ \text{read}(A);\ A := A + 100;\ \text{write}(A);\ \text{unlock}(B);\ \text{unlock}(A)
$$

**Step 1: Identify lock points.**

- $LP(T_1) =$ time of `lock-X(B)` (last lock acquired by $T_1$)
- $LP(T_2) =$ time of `lock-X(A)` (last lock acquired by $T_2$)

**Step 2: Determine the implied serial order.**

If $LP(T_1) < LP(T_2)$, then $T_1$ precedes $T_2$ in the equivalent serial schedule. The schedule is equivalent to:

$$
T_1 \rightarrow T_2
$$

**Step 3: Check consistency.**

The 2PL protocol requires that for each transaction, **all unlocks happen after all locks**. We verify:

- $T_1$: locks are $S(A), X(B)$; unlocks are $A, B$ — all unlocks after locks ✓
- $T_2$: locks are $S(B), X(A)$; unlocks are $B, A$ — all unlocks after locks ✓

**Conclusion:** The schedule is conflict-serializable with order $T_1 \to T_2$.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Deadlock Detection via Wait-for Graph

Consider 4 transactions with the following lock waits:

| Transaction | Waiting For | Held By |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | X-lock on $A$ | $T_2$ |
| $T_2$ | X-lock on $B$ | $T_3$ |
| $T_3$ | X-lock on $C$ | $T_4$ |
| $T_4$ | X-lock on $D$ | $T_1$ |

**Step 1: Construct the Wait-for Graph (WFG).**

The edges are:
- $T_1 \to T_2$ (waiting for $T_2$)
- $T_2 \to T_3$ (waiting for $T_3$)
- $T_3 \to T_4$ (waiting for $T_4$)
- $T_4 \to T_1$ (waiting for $T_1$)

**Step 2: Detect a cycle using DFS.**

Starting from $T_1$:
- $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_4 \to T_1$ — **cycle detected!**

**Step 3: Choose a victim.**

The DBMS selects one transaction in the cycle (typically the one with **least work done** or **lowest priority**) and **rolls it back**, releasing its locks to break the cycle.

Suppose $T_3$ is chosen. Then $T_3$ is aborted, its locks are released, and the remaining transactions $T_1, T_2, T_4$ can proceed.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Wait-Die vs Wound-Wait

Let timestamps $TS(T_1) = 10$, $TS(T_2) = 20$ (older = smaller).

### Case A: $T_1$ requests a lock held by $T_2$

**Wait-Die Scheme:**
- Since $TS(T_1) = 10 < TS(T_2) = 20$, $T_1$ is **older**.
- Older transactions **wait**. So $T_1$ **waits**.

**Wound-Wait Scheme:**
- Since $TS(T_1) = 10 < TS(T_2) = 20$, $T_1$ is **older**.
- Older transactions **wound** younger ones. So $T_2$ is **wounded** (rolled back).

### Case B: $T_2$ requests a lock held by $T_1$

**Wait-Die Scheme:**
- $T_2$ is younger ($TS = 20$).
- Younger transactions **die**. So $T_2$ is **aborted** (dies).

**Wound-Wait Scheme:**
- $T_2$ is younger.
- Younger transactions **wait**. So $T_2$ **waits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary Rule:**
> - **Wait-Die**: Older waits, younger dies.
> - **Wound-Wait**: Older wounds, younger waits.

## 3.5 Lock Granularity Trade-off Analysis

The choice of lock granularity involves a trade-off quantified roughly as:

$$
\text{Concurrency} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Granularity Size}}
$$

$$
\text{Lock Management Overhead} \propto \text{Number of Locks Held}
$$

| Granularity | Concurrency | Overhead | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tuple / Row** | High | High | OLTP (online transactions) |
| **Page** | Medium | Medium | Mixed workloads |
| **Table** | Low | Low | Bulk operations, DDL |
| **Database** | Very Low | Very Low | Backups, migrations |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step 2PL Schedule Validation

**Given Schedule S:**
$$
S: T_1:\text{lock-}S(A),\ T_2:\text{lock-}S(A),\ T_2:\text{read}(A),\ T_1:\text{read}(A),\ T_2:\text{unlock}(A),\ T_1:\text{unlock}(A)
$$

**Step 1: Check S-lock compatibility.**

Both $T_1$ and $T_2$ request S-locks on $A$. Per the compatibility matrix, this is **compatible** — both granted.

**Step 2: Check 2PL compliance.**

For each transaction, verify that all `lock` operations precede all `unlock` operations:

- $T_1$: One lock (`lock-S(A)`) then one unlock (`unlock(A)`) ✓
- $T_2$: One lock (`lock-S(A)`) then one unlock (`unlock(A)`) ✓

**Step 3: Determine lock points.**

$$
LP(T_1) = LP(T_2) = \text{time of } lock\text{-}S(A)
$$

**Step 4: Check serializability.**

Since $T_1$ and $T_2$ only **read** $A$ (no writes), no conflict exists regardless of order. Schedule is conflict-serializable with **equivalent serial order** $T_1 \to T_2$ or $T_2 \to T_1$. $\blacksquare$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Locking. What are the two basic types of locks used in DBMS? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

**Locking** is a concurrency control technique in which a transaction acquires a lock on a data item before accessing it. The lock prevents other transactions from modifying the item until it is released, thereby ensuring **isolation** and **consistency** in concurrent execution.

The two basic types of locks are:

1. **Shared Lock (S-lock):** A read-only lock. Multiple transactions can simultaneously hold S-locks on the same data item. Acquired via `lock-S(Q)`.

2. **Exclusive Lock (X-lock):** A read-write lock. Only one transaction can hold an X-lock on a data item, and no other locks (S or X) can coexist with it. Acquired via `lock-X(Q)`.

The lock manager uses a **compatibility matrix** to decide whether to grant a lock request or block the requesting transaction.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is a deadlock? How is it detected using a wait-for graph?**

**Model Answer:**

A **deadlock** is a state in which two or more transactions are **circularly waiting** for each other to release locks. None of the transactions can proceed, and the system is stuck.

**Detection using a Wait-for Graph (WFG):**
- A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is maintained by the system.
- Each node represents a transaction.
- An edge $T_i \to T_j$ exists if transaction $T_i$ is waiting for a lock currently held by $T_j$.
- The system periodically runs a **cycle-detection algorithm** (e.g., DFS).
- **If a cycle is found, a deadlock exists.** The system then selects a victim transaction, rolls it back, and releases its locks to break the cycle.

The complexity of cycle detection is $O(V + E)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Choice A]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol in detail. Describe its growing and shrinking phases. State and prove the property that 2PL guarantees conflict serializability.**

**Model Solution:**

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a locking protocol that ensures conflict serializability. A transaction is said to follow 2PL if **all its lock operations precede all its unlock operations**.

**Phases of 2PL:**

1. **Growing Phase:** The transaction acquires locks (S or X) on data items. No locks are released. The transaction grows its set of held locks.

2. **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction releases locks. No new locks are acquired. The set of held locks monotonically decreases.

3. **Lock Point:** The instant of transition from growing to shrinking — i.e., the moment of the **last lock acquisition**. The lock points determine the serialization order.

**Proof of Conflict Serializability:**

Let $S$ be a schedule produced by transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ all following 2PL. Suppose $S$ is not conflict-serializable. Then the **precedence graph** $P(S)$ contains a cycle:

$$
T_{i_1} \to T_{i_2} \to \cdots \to T_{i_k} \to T_{i_1}
$$

An edge $T_a \to T_b$ in $P(S)$ exists iff there is a data item $Q$ where $T_a$ executes `lock-X(Q)` (or writes to $Q$) and $T_b$ later requests the conflicting lock. This means $T_a$ releases the lock on $Q$ only after its last lock acquisition, so $T_b$ must have acquired $Q$ after $T_a$'s lock point:

$$
LP(T_a) < LP(T_b)
$$

Applying this to the cycle:

$$
LP(T_{i_1}) < LP(T_{i_2}) < \cdots < LP(T_{i_k}) < LP(T_{i_1})
$$

This is a contradiction. Hence, no cycle exists, and $S$ is conflict-serializable. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the Wait-Die and Wound-Wait schemes for deadlock prevention. Differentiate between them with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

Both schemes use transaction timestamps to prevent deadlocks without needing detection. Let $TS(T)$ denote the timestamp of transaction $T$ (older transactions have smaller timestamps).

### Wait-Die Scheme (Non-Preemptive)

When transaction $T_i$ requests a data item currently held by $T_j$:

- **If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$** (i.e., $T_i$ is older): $T_i$ is **allowed to wait**.
- **If $TS(T_i) > TS(T_j)$** (i.e., $T_i$ is younger): $T_i$ is **aborted (dies)**.

> **Rule:** *Older waits, younger dies.*

### Wound-Wait Scheme (Preemptive)

When transaction $T_i$ requests a data item currently held by $T_j$:

- **If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$** (i.e., $T_i$ is older): $T_j$ is **wounded (aborted)**.
- **If $TS(T_i) > TS(T_j)$** (i.e., $T_i$ is younger): $T_i$ is **allowed to wait**.

> **Rule:** *Older wounds, younger waits.*

### Comparative Example

Let $TS(T_1) = 5$ (older) and $TS(T_2) = 10$ (younger). Suppose $T_2$ holds an X-lock on item $A$, and now $T_1$ requests the same.

| Scheme | Outcome |
|---|---|
| **Wait-Die** | $T_1$ is older → $T_1$ **waits** for $T_2$ |
| **Wound-Wait** | $T_1$ is older → $T_2$ is **wounded** (rolled back) |

Conversely, if $T_2$ requests the lock held by $T_1$:

| Scheme | Outcome |
|---|---|
| **Wait-Die** | $T_2$ is younger → $T_2$ **dies** (aborted) |
| **Wound-Wait** | $T_2$ is younger → $T_2$ **waits** |

### Key Differences

| Aspect | Wait-Die | Wound-Wait |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Non-preemptive | Preemptive |
| Aborted Transaction | The one making the new request | The one already holding the lock |
| Restart Cost | Lower (younger transactions re-execute) | Higher (older victim may be far along) |
| Starvation | Possible (same transaction may repeatedly die) | Possible (but the wounded transaction has higher priority) |

Both schemes are **deadlock-free** and **bias toward older transactions**.

---

### Question B2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 4 Choice B]

**(a) [7 Marks] What is a lock compatibility matrix? Construct the lock compatibility matrix for Shared and Exclusive locks. Using a sample schedule, explain how the lock manager grants or denies lock requests.**

**Model Solution:**

A **lock compatibility matrix** is a 2D table that determines whether a newly requested lock can be **granted** to a transaction or must be **denied (blocked)**, based on the locks currently held by other transactions on the same data item.

### Standard Lock Compatibility Matrix (S and X)

| Requested $\downarrow$ \ Held $\rightarrow$ | **None** | **S-Lock** | **X-Lock** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S-Lock** | Grant | Grant | Wait |
| **X-Lock** | Grant | Wait | Wait |

**Interpretation:**
- A new **S-lock** is **granted** if no exclusive lock is held.
- A new **X-lock** is **granted** only if no other lock is held (exclusive access).
- All other cases result in the requesting transaction being **added to a wait queue**.

### Worked Example

Consider three transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$ accessing item $A$. The schedule of requests is:

| Time | Action | Lock State After | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_1$ | $T_1$: lock-S(A) | $L(A) = S$ | Granted |
| $t_2$ | $T_2$: lock-S(A) | $L(A) = S, S$ | Granted (compatible) |
| $t_3$ | $T_3$: lock-X(A) | $L(A) = S, S$; $T_3$ blocked | Wait |
| $t_4$ | $T_1$: unlock(A) | $L(A) = S$ | $T_3$ still waits ($T_2$ holds S) |
| $t_5$ | $T_2$: unlock(A) | $L(A) = \text{Free}$ | $T_3$ granted X-lock |
| $t_6$ | $T_3$: lock-X(A) | $L(A) = X$ | Granted |

> **Note:** $T_3$ only obtains the X-lock **after both** $T_1$ and $T_2$ have released their S-locks.

The lock manager uses a **lock table** (a hash map) to efficiently look up the current lock state of each data item.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe Strict Two-Phase Locking and Rigorous Two-Phase Locking. How do they differ from basic 2PL? Discuss their advantages in terms of recoverability and avoidance of cascading aborts.**

**Model Solution:**

### Recap: Basic 2PL

In basic 2PL, a transaction releases locks as soon as it enters the shrinking phase (i.e., right after its lock point). This may lead to:

- **Cascading aborts**: A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction; if the writer aborts, the reader must also abort.
- **Irrecoverable schedules**: A transaction may commit before another transaction that wrote data it read, violating recoverability.

### Strict Two-Phase Locking (Strict 2PL)

**Rule:** A transaction $T$ holds **all its exclusive (X) locks** until it **commits or aborts**. Shared locks may still be released earlier.

**Properties:**
- ✅ Guarantees **recoverability**.
- ✅ Prevents **cascading aborts** (since uncommitted writes are never read by other transactions).
- ✅ Conflict-serializable (inherits 2PL property).

### Rigorous Two-Phase Locking (Rigorous 2PL)

**Rule:** A transaction $T$ holds **all its locks (both S and X)** until it **commits or aborts**.

**Properties:**
- ✅ Guarantees **recoverability**.
- ✅ Prevents **cascading aborts**.
- ✅ Conflict-serializable.
- ✅ Allows transactions to be serialized in the order of their **commit timestamps**, which simplifies recovery.

### Comparison Table

| Feature | Basic 2PL | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **X-locks held until commit?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **S-locks held until commit?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Conflict-serializable?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Recoverable?** | ❌ Not guaranteed | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Avoids cascading aborts?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Lock duration** | Shorter | Medium | Longest |
| **Concurrency** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Used in practice?** | Rarely | Yes (IBM DB2) | Yes (MySQL InnoDB) |

### Why These Variants Matter in Practice

Consider transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
T_1: W(A);\ \text{commit};\ T_2: R(A)
$$

Under **Basic 2PL**, $T_1$ may release the X-lock on $A$ before committing, allowing $T_2$ to read $A$ and commit. If $T_1$ later aborts, $T_2$ has read a **dirty value** and must also abort — a **cascading abort**.

Under **Strict 2PL**, $T_1$ holds the X-lock on $A$ until commit, so $T_2$ cannot read $A$ prematurely. The schedule is **recoverable** and **cascade-free**.

Under **Rigorous 2PL**, both transactions hold all their locks until commit, which simplifies **recovery** because the system knows no other transaction is using the data at the time of commit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse the differences between 2PL variants. Common mistakes include:
> 1. Stating that **all** 2PL schedules are cascade-free — this is **only true for Strict/Rigorous 2PL**.
> 2. Claiming that Strict 2PL holds **S-locks** until commit — it does **not**; only X-locks are held.
> 3. Confusing **Rigorous 2PL** with **Conservative 2PL**. Rigorous holds locks until commit (the end); Conservative acquires all locks *before* the transaction starts (the beginning).
> 4. Forgetting to state that all 2PL variants guarantee **conflict-serializability**.
> 5. Mixing up Wait-Die and Wound-Wait rules — memorize: "**Wait-Die** is for the *younger* (it dies); **Wound-Wait** is also about the *younger* (it waits)."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Locking**

- 🔒 **Locking** is the most widely used concurrency control mechanism in DBMS, ensuring **isolation** and **consistency**.
- 🔑 Two primary lock types: **Shared (S)** for reads and **Exclusive (X)** for writes.
- ✅ **S–S** locks are **compatible**; all other combinations (S–X, X–X) are **incompatible**.
- 📈 **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** has two phases: **Growing** (acquire locks) and **Shrinking** (release locks), separated by the **lock point**.
- 🎯 **2PL guarantees conflict-serializability** — the order of lock points determines the equivalent serial order.
- 🔄 **Strict 2PL** holds X-locks until commit; **Rigorous 2PL** holds both S and X locks until commit.
- ✅ **Strict and Rigorous 2PL** are the **most used in practice** because they ensure recoverability and prevent cascading aborts.
- ⚠️ **Deadlock** is a circular wait situation; detected via **Wait-for Graph (WFG)** cycle detection.
- 🕒 **Wait-Die**: older waits, younger dies (non-preemptive).
- 🩹 **Wound-Wait**: older wounds, younger waits (preemptive).
- 🍽️ **Starvation** is possible in both prevention schemes; solved using **FCFS** queues or **priority aging**.
- 🌳 **Multiple Granularity Locking (MGL)** uses a hierarchy (DB → Table → Page → Row) with **intention locks** (IS, IX, SIX) on ancestors.
- 🚫 **Phantom problem** is prevented using **predicate locks** or **index locks** (next-key locking in MySQL).
- 🏭 **Real systems**: Oracle (Strict 2PL with MVCC), MySQL InnoDB (Rigorous 2PL with next-key locks), PostgreSQL (MVCC + 2PL for writes).
- 📊 **Lock granularity trade-off**: finer granularity = more concurrency but more overhead; coarser = less concurrency but less overhead.
- 🧠 The **Lock Manager** is a separate subsystem maintaining a **lock table** (hash structure) and a **transaction wait queue**.
- 🔁 **Lock upgrade** (S → X) requires waiting for all other S-lock holders — a common deadlock source.
- ⏰ **Timeout-based** deadlock detection is simple but can incorrectly abort innocent long transactions.
- 💡 **Deadlock victim selection** is typically based on: least work done, lowest cost of rollback, or fewest locks held.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **lock compatibility matrix** and the **wait-for graph** for any concurrency problem — these are guaranteed marks in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations
topics.content:
