> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a **concurrency control protocol** used in Database Management Systems to guarantee **conflict-serializability** of concurrent transactions. It is a rule that requires every transaction to follow a strict two-phase discipline on lock acquisition and release.

**Formal Definition (Korth & Silberschatz Style, KTU Standard):**
A transaction $T_i$ is said to follow the **Two-Phase Locking Protocol** if **all locking operations (`lock-S`, `lock-X`, `upgrade`) precede the first unlock operation (`unlock`)** in $T_i$. The transactions are thus divided into two well-defined, non-overlapping phases:

1. **Phase 1 — The Growing Phase:** The transaction may acquire (and upgrade) locks but **may not release any lock**.
2. **Phase 2 — The Shrinking Phase:** The transaction may release (and downgrade) locks but **may not acquire any new lock**.

The boundary between the two phases is called the **Lock Point** of the transaction — the moment when the transaction has acquired its final (last) lock.

Mathematically, for any transaction $T_i$ with lock operations $\{L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n\}$ and unlock operations $\{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_m\}$ ordered by execution time, the 2PL condition is:

$$
\forall \, j, k : \text{if } U_j \text{ is the first unlock and } L_k \text{ occurs after } U_j, \text{ then } L_k \text{ does not exist.}
$$

Equivalently, no lock may be acquired after any unlock has been issued.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Photographer at a Wedding 🎥**
>
> Imagine a wedding where one photographer must take **two types of shots** before leaving the venue:
> 1. **Candid shots** (shared/S-lock) — two photographers can shoot the same bride simultaneously.
> 2. **Solo portrait shots** (exclusive/X-lock) — only one photographer can have the bride.
>
> To avoid chaos, the head photographer enforces a **two-phase rule**:
> - **Phase 1 (Gathering):** The photographer first collects *all* required cameras, lenses, and lighting equipment. They keep accumulating gear but never return any.
> - **Phase 2 (Shooting):** Once the photographer steps onto the floor with all their equipment, they can hand back a lens or two between shots, but they cannot ask for a new tripod in the middle.
>
> The moment they have the last item (e.g., a memory card) is the **Lock Point** — after this, they are locked into a *use-and-return* pattern. This single rule guarantees that no two photographers will fight for the same exclusive shot at the same time, no matter how uncoordinated they are.

In database terms:
- **Cameras/lenses** = **Locks** on data items.
- **Gathering all equipment** = **Growing Phase** (acquire only).
- **Shooting and returning gear** = **Shrinking Phase** (release only).
- **Picking up a new item mid-shoot** = **Violation of 2PL** (would cause non-serializable behavior).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Growing Phase | $G(T_i)$ | Interval in which $T_i$ only acquires locks |
| Shrinking Phase | $S(T_i)$ | Interval in which $T_i$ only releases locks |
| Lock Point | $LP(T_i)$ | The instant the last lock is acquired |
| Shared Lock | S | Read-only lock; multiple S allowed |
| Exclusive Lock | X | Read/Write lock; mutually exclusive |
| Lock Upgrade | U | Convert S → X (counts as acquisition) |
| Lock Downgrade | D | Convert X → S (counts as release) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2PL protocol is **sufficient** (not necessary) for conflict-serializability. There exist serializable schedules that are **not** 2PL-compliant, but every 2PL-compliant schedule is guaranteed to be conflict-serializable. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

## 1.4 Lock Compatibility Matrix (Reference)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Requested} \backslash \text{Held} & \text{None} & \text{S} & \text{X} \\
\hline
\textbf{S} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \times \\
\hline
\textbf{X} & \checkmark & \times & \times \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

`✓` = compatible (granted), `×` = incompatible (waits).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Why and How

### Step 1: Why is 2PL needed at all?
Without locking, two concurrent transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ writing to the same item $A$ can produce:
- **Lost Update Problem** — $T_1$'s write is overwritten by $T_2$.
- **Dirty Read Problem** — $T_2$ reads uncommitted data from $T_1$, which later rolls back.
- **Unrepeatable Read** — $T_1$ reads $A$ twice and sees different values.

Locking alone prevents the first two. **The two-phase rule** is what extends it to *guarantee global serializability* across the whole schedule.

### Step 2: How does 2PL enforce serializability?
The protocol forces the **precedence graph (serialization graph)** of any 2PL schedule to be **acyclic**. The formal proof rests on the **Lock Point Ordering Theorem** (see SECTION 3).

### Step 3: When does a transaction "lock" an item?
The system calls `lock-S(Q)` or `lock-X(Q)` on the **Lock Manager**, a subsystem that maintains a lock table $\{ \text{item} \to (\text{type}, \text{holder queue}) \}$. If the lock is incompatible, the transaction is **blocked** (added to the wait queue).

### Step 4: What is the Lock Point exactly?
If $T_i$ acquires locks in the order $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k$ and the **last** of these ($L_k$) is granted at time $t^*$, then:

$$
LP(T_i) = t^*
$$

After $t^*$, $T_i$ may only unlock. The set of lock-points of all committed transactions gives a **topological order** equivalent to a serial schedule.

## 2.2 The Three Official Variations of 2PL

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Differentiate between Basic 2PL, Conservative 2PL, Strict 2PL, and Rigorous 2PL."* Below is the canonical taxonomy.

### Variation A — **Basic 2PL (the "Plain" version)**
- Locks may be released **as soon as** the transaction enters the shrinking phase.
- Guarantees conflict-serializability ✅
- Does **NOT** prevent **cascading rollbacks** ❌
- Does **NOT** prevent **deadlocks** ❌
- Locks held until end = recoverable but not cascadeless.

### Variation B — **Conservative 2PL (Static 2PL / Pre-declaration 2PL)**
- The transaction must acquire **ALL** required locks **before** the transaction begins execution.
- Growing phase = single instant at start. Shrinking phase = remainder.
- **No deadlocks possible** (all locks obtained atomically) ✅
- **No cascading rollbacks** ✅
- **Drawback:** Requires prior knowledge of all data items — impractical for long transactions.
- Starvation still possible.

### Variation C — **Strict 2PL**
- All **exclusive (X) locks** must be held **until the transaction commits or aborts**.
- Shared (S) locks may still be released early.
- Prevents **cascading rollbacks** ✅
- Prevents **dirty reads** ✅
- Deadlocks still possible ❌
- **Most widely used in commercial DBMSs** (e.g., SQL Server default isolation levels).

### Variation D — **Rigorous 2PL**
- All locks (both S and X) are held **until commit/abort**.
- Equivalent to Strict 2PL for write-skew-free workloads.
- **No cascading rollbacks** ✅
- **No dirty reads** ✅
- Easier to implement (no read-lock bookkeeping post-commit).
- Used in academic texts (e.g., Ramakrishnan-Gehrke) as the gold standard.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — All 4 Variations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — appears in nearly every KTU Module-4 question paper, either as a 3-mark short note or as a 7-mark sub-part.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Property} & \textbf{Basic 2PL} & \textbf{Conservative} & \textbf{Strict} & \textbf{Rigorous} \\
\hline
\text{Conflict-Serializable?} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{No Deadlock?} & \times & \checkmark & \times & \times \\
\hline
\text{No Cascading Abort?} & \times & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{No Dirty Read?} & \times & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Knows data items in advance?} & \times & \checkmark & \times & \times \\
\hline
\text{Used in real DBMS?} & \text{Rare} & \text{Never} & \checkmark & \times \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule / Theorem | Statement | KTU Use |
|---|----------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | 2PL Lock Rule | $\forall i,j : U_i \text{ precedes no later } L_j$ | Def. |
| 2 | Lock Point Order | $LP(T_i) < LP(T_j) \Rightarrow T_i \rightarrow T_j$ in precedence graph | Serializability proof |
| 3 | Cycle Theorem | A schedule is CSR $\iff$ its precedence graph is acyclic | CSR check |
| 4 | Conservative 2PL | $|\{L\}| = n$ at $t=0$ | Implementation |
| 5 | Strict 2PL | $X\text{-locks released at commit/abort}$ | Cascadeless |
| 6 | Rigorous 2PL | $S \text{ and } X \text{ released at commit/abort}$ | Easiest impl. |
| 7 | Lock Upgrade | $S \rightarrow X$ **counts as acquire** | Must obey 2PL |
| 8 | Lock Downgrade | $X \rightarrow S$ **counts as release** | Must obey 2PL |
| 9 | Deadlock Detection Wait-for | Edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if $T_i$ waits for $T_j$ | Graph cycle |
| 10 | Wait-Die Rule | Older survives; younger dies | Timestamp based |
| 11 | Wound-Wait Rule | Older wounds younger; younger waits | Timestamp based |
| 12 | Starvation | Unfair lock granting; FIFO queue resolves it | Practice |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems (ATM, NEFT):** Strict 2PL underpins the **REPEATABLE READ** and **SERIALIZABLE** isolation levels in MySQL InnoDB, Oracle, and PostgreSQL to prevent double-debit and lost updates.
- **E-commerce Inventory:** Prevents two users from buying the last item simultaneously (race condition).
- **Airline Reservation Systems:** Conservative 2PL is impractical; Strict 2PL is the de-facto standard.
- **Distributed DBs (Cassandra, Spanner):** Hybrid 2PL + MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) is the modern trend.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Theorem 1 — 2PL Guarantees Conflict-Serializability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Sufficiency of 2PL).** *Every schedule produced by a set of transactions obeying the 2PL protocol is conflict-serializable.*

### Proof (Step-by-Step, KTU Examination Style)

**Setup.** Consider a schedule $S$ produced by transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ that all obey 2PL. Suppose, for contradiction, that the precedence graph $P(S)$ contains a cycle:

$$
T_{i_1} \rightarrow T_{i_2} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_{i_k} \rightarrow T_{i_1}
$$

**Step 1: Interpret each edge.**
An edge $T_a \rightarrow T_b$ in $P(S)$ exists iff there exist conflicting operations $O_a$ in $T_a$ and $O_b$ in $T_b$ such that $O_a$ precedes $O_b$ in $S$, and at least one is a `write`.

**Step 2: Apply 2PL constraint.**
Since $T_a$ and $T_b$ are 2PL, the lock on the conflicting item $Q$ must be acquired by $T_a$ before $T_b$. Therefore:

$$
LP(T_a) \text{ (with respect to item } Q) \text{ occurs before } LP(T_b)
$$

In particular, considering the *last* lock acquired by each transaction, we have:

$$
LP(T_a) < LP(T_b) \quad \text{for every edge } T_a \rightarrow T_b
$$

**Step 3: Order the lock points.**
Applying this inequality along the cycle:

$$
LP(T_{i_1}) < LP(T_{i_2}) < \cdots < LP(T_{i_k}) < LP(T_{i_1})
$$

**Step 4: Derive contradiction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LP(T_{i_1}) &< LP(T_{i_2}) \\
&< LP(T_{i_3}) \\
&< \cdots \\
&< LP(T_{i_k}) \\
&< LP(T_{i_1})
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields $LP(T_{i_1}) < LP(T_{i_1})$, which is the **strict inequality $x < x$** — a contradiction.

**Step 5: Conclude.**
Hence no cycle can exist. $P(S)$ is a DAG, and $S$ is **conflict-serializable**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Theorem 2 — Lock Point Ordering Equivalence

**Claim.** If $T_i$ acquires the **last** of its locks at time $t_i$ (the lock point), and $T_j$ does so at $t_j$, with $t_i < t_j$, then the serialization order is $T_i$ precedes $T_j$.

### Proof

For any pair of conflicting operations on some item $Q$ between $T_i$ and $T_j$:
- If $T_i$ accesses $Q$ after $T_j$, then $T_j$ must have held the lock on $Q$ earlier. But $T_j$ cannot acquire any new lock after $t_j$.
- Since $t_i < t_j$, $T_i$ holds the lock at $t_i$, before $T_j$'s lock point.
- Therefore, $T_i$'s conflicting op on $Q$ precedes $T_j$'s conflicting op on $Q$ in the schedule.

Thus $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ in the precedence graph, and the lock-point time order gives a valid **topological sort** of all transactions. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — A 2PL Schedule

Consider two transactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{read}(A); \text{write}(A); \text{read}(B); \text{write}(B); \\
T_2 &: \text{read}(B); \text{write}(B); \text{read}(A); \text{write}(A);
\end{aligned}
$$

**Construct a 2PL-compliant interleaving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Time} \quad \textbf{Op} \quad \textbf{Phase of } T_1 \quad \textbf{Phase of } T_2 \\
&t_1: \quad \text{lock-S}(A,T_1) \rightarrow \text{grow} \quad - \\
&t_2: \quad \text{read}(A,T_1) \rightarrow \text{grow} \quad - \\
&t_3: \quad \text{lock-X}(A,T_1) \rightarrow \text{grow} \quad - \\
&t_4: \quad \text{write}(A,T_1) \rightarrow \text{grow} \quad - \\
&t_5: \quad \text{lock-S}(B,T_1) \rightarrow \text{grow} \quad - \\
&t_6: \quad \text{unlock}(A,T_1) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad - \\
&t_7: \quad \text{lock-S}(B,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_8: \quad \text{unlock}(B,T_1) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_9: \quad \text{read}(B,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{10}: \quad \text{lock-X}(B,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{11}: \quad \text{write}(B,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{12}: \quad \text{lock-S}(A,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{13}: \quad \text{read}(A,T_2) \rightarrow \text{shrink} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{14}: \quad \text{commit}(T_1) \rightarrow \text{end} \quad \text{grow} \\
&t_{15}: \quad \text{commit}(T_2) \rightarrow \text{end} \quad \text{end}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of 2PL compliance:**
- For $T_1$: Last acquire = $t_5$ (`lock-S(B)`). First unlock = $t_6$. No acquire after $t_6$. ✅
- For $T_2$: Last acquire = $t_{12}$ (`lock-S(A)`). First unlock occurs at commit $t_{15}$. No acquire after $t_{15}$. ✅

**Precedence Graph:** No conflicting operations are reversed in a way that produces a cycle, so it is acyclic → conflict-serializable (equivalent serial order: $T_1 \to T_2$).

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — A Non-2PL Counter-example

Suppose the same transactions execute as:

$$
t_1: \text{lock-S}(A,T_1) \;\; t_2: \text{read}(A,T_1) \;\; t_3: \text{unlock}(A,T_1)
$$
$$
t_4: \text{lock-X}(A,T_2) \;\; t_5: \text{write}(A,T_2) \;\; t_6: \text{lock-S}(B,T_2)
$$
$$
t_7: \text{lock-X}(B,T_1) \;\; \cdots
$$

**Violation:** $T_1$ unlocks $A$ at $t_3$ and then later acquires a lock on $B$ at $t_7$. This is a **2PL violation**. Such a schedule can produce **non-serializable** behavior.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Variation

| Scenario | Recommended Variation | Reason |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| Online Banking (NEFT) | **Strict 2PL** | Cascadeless + recoverable |
| Short read-only reports | **Basic 2PL** | Low conflict, fast release |
| Batch updates (known workload) | **Conservative 2PL** | No deadlock |
| Stock trading engine | **Rigorous 2PL** | Maximum safety |
| Long ad-hoc OLAP queries | **MVCC + Snapshot Isolation** | Avoids locking altogether |
| Distributed multi-master | **2PL + 2PC (Two-Phase Commit)** | Atomicity across nodes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Two-Phase Locking State Machine for a Transaction

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the lifecycle of a 2PL transaction. Each node is a legal state; each edge is a permitted transition.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> startTx
    startTx --> growing : BEGIN
    growing --> growing : lock_S(Q) granted
    growing --> growing : lock_X(Q) granted
    growing --> growing : upgrade S to X
    growing --> lockPoint : last lock acquired
    lockPoint --> shrinking : first unlock issued
    shrinking --> shrinking : unlock(Q)
    shrinking --> shrinking : downgrade X to S
    shrinking --> committed : COMMIT
    shrinking --> aborted : ROLLBACK
    committed --> [*]
    aborted --> [*]
    growing --> aborted : deadlock victim
    growing --> aborted : user abort
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **single sink of the growing phase** is the `lockPoint` state.
> - Once a transaction enters `shrinking`, **no edge returns it to `growing`** — this is the essence of 2PL.
> - The only exits from either phase are `committed` or `aborted`.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Lock-Compatibility Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction requests lock on item Q] --> B{What type?}
    B -->|Shared S| C{Does any T hold X-lock on Q?}
    B -->|Exclusive X| D{Does any T hold S or X lock on Q?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Block T - add to wait queue]
    C -->|No| F[Grant S-lock]
    D -->|Yes| E
    D -->|No| G[Grant X-lock]
    E --> H[Holder releases or aborts]
    H --> I{Recheck compatibility}
    I -->|OK| J[Grant pending lock]
    I -->|Conflict persists| E
    F --> K[2PL Phase check - Grow or Shrink]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{In growing phase and lock granted?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Stay in growing phase]
    L -->|No, this is an unlock| N[Enter shrinking phase]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2PL System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TXN_LAYER [Transaction Layer]
        T1[Transaction T1]
        T2[Transaction T2]
        T3[Transaction Tn]
    end

    subgraph LOCK_MGR [Lock Manager Subsystem]
        LQ[Lock Request Queue]
        LT[(Lock Table item: type, holder)]
        LGP[Lock Granting Policy FIFO or Priority]
        DG[Deadlock Detector Wait-For Graph]
    end

    subgraph DATA_LAYER [Data Storage Layer]
        DB[(Database Items A B C D)]
        BL[Buffer Pool]
    end

    subgraph RECOVERY [Recovery Module]
        LOG[(Transaction Log REDO UNDO]
        AB[Abort Handler]
    end

    T1 -->|lock-S X unlock| LQ
    T2 --> LQ
    T3 --> LQ
    LQ --> LGP
    LGP --> LT
    LT -->|granted or blocked| LQ
    LGP --> DG
    DG -->|cycle detected| AB
    AB -->|roll back victim| LOG
    T1 -->|read write| BL
    T2 --> BL
    T3 --> BL
    BL --> DB
    LT -->|lock release triggers| BL
    AB -->|undo| BL
```

**Block-Level Architecture Explanation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **TXN_LAYER** | User-level transactions issuing `read`, `write`, `commit` |
| **Lock Request Queue** | Serializes all lock/unlock requests |
| **Lock Table** | Maps each data item to `(lock_type, holder_Tx_id, wait_queue)` |
| **Lock Granting Policy** | Decides FIFO vs priority vs fair queueing |
| **Deadlock Detector** | Periodically runs a cycle-detection DFS on the wait-for graph |
| **Abort Handler** | Picks a victim, writes UNDO log, releases its locks |
| **Buffer Pool** | In-memory cache of dirty/clean data pages |
| **Transaction Log** | Write-Ahead Log (WAL) for REDO/UNDO recovery |

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Comparison Timeline of the Four 2PL Variations

```mermaid
gantt
    title Lock-Holding Timeline Across 2PL Variations
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Basic 2PL
    Growing Phase (T1)      :a1, 0, 4
    Shrinking Phase (T1)    :a2, after a1, 6

    section Conservative 2PL
    Acquire ALL Locks       :crit, b1, 0, 1
    Execute                 :b2, after b1, 5
    Release All             :b3, after b2, 1

    section Strict 2PL
    Growing Phase           :c1, 0, 3
    S-locks release early   :c2, 4, 2
    X-locks held to commit  :crit, c3, 3, 4

    section Rigorous 2PL
    Growing Phase           :d1, 0, 3
    All locks to commit     :crit, d2, 3, 4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Gantt chart:** Notice how the *X-locks held to commit* bar in Strict 2PL, and *All locks to commit* in Rigorous 2PL, extend all the way to the right edge — visually demonstrating why these variants prevent cascading aborts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol. What is meant by the "lock point" of a transaction?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **2PL Protocol (2 Marks):** Two-Phase Locking is a concurrency control protocol that requires every transaction to divide its lock operations into two distinct, non-overlapping phases:
> 1. **Growing Phase:** Only `lock-S`, `lock-X`, and `lock upgrade (S→X)` operations are permitted.
> 2. **Shrinking Phase:** Only `unlock` and `lock downgrade (X→S)` operations are permitted.
> Once the transaction issues its first unlock, it can never acquire another lock.
>
> **Lock Point (1 Mark):** The *lock point* of a transaction $T_i$ is the moment in time when $T_i$ has acquired its **last (final) lock** before entering the shrinking phase. All lock points of committed transactions define a total order equivalent to a serial schedule.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *Differentiate between Strict 2PL and Rigorous 2PL in terms of lock release behavior.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL |
> |--------|-----------|--------------|
> | S-lock release | May be released before commit | Held until commit/abort |
> | X-lock release | Held until commit/abort | Held until commit/abort |
> | Cascading aborts | Prevented | Prevented |
> | Implementation ease | Moderate (need to track S vs X) | Easy (release all at once) |
> | Used in | SQL Server, MySQL InnoDB | Mostly academic texts |
>
> **Key Sentence (1 Mark):** In **Strict 2PL**, only exclusive locks are held until commit; shared locks can be released early. In **Rigorous 2PL**, *all* locks (both S and X) are held until commit/abort, making it a strict superset.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Explain the Two-Phase Locking protocol with a neat diagram. Show that 2PL guarantees conflict-serializability using the lock point ordering theorem.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of 2PL (2 Marks):**
A transaction $T_i$ follows the 2PL protocol if all its lock operations precede the first unlock operation. The transaction is thus partitioned into:
- **Growing Phase:** Acquires locks (S, X, upgrade). No releases.
- **Shrinking Phase:** Releases locks (unlock, downgrade). No acquisitions.

**Step 2 — Diagram (2 Marks):**
The standard timeline diagram shows lock-point at the boundary:

```
   GROWING PHASE            |  SHRINKING PHASE
   ~~~ lock  lock  lock ~~~|~~~ unlock unlock ~~~ commit
                          LP(T_i)
```

**Step 3 — Lock Point Theorem (1 Mark):**
For any two transactions $T_i$ and $T_j$, if their lock points satisfy $LP(T_i) < LP(T_j)$, then $T_i$ must precede $T_j$ in every conflict-equivalent serial schedule.

**Step 4 — Proof of Conflict-Serializability (2 Marks):**
Assume for contradiction that the precedence graph $P(S)$ contains a cycle $T_{i_1} \to T_{i_2} \to \cdots \to T_{i_k} \to T_{i_1}$. For each edge $T_a \to T_b$, the lock point order gives $LP(T_a) < LP(T_b)$. Composing around the cycle yields $LP(T_{i_1}) < LP(T_{i_1})$, a contradiction. Hence $P(S)$ is acyclic and $S$ is conflict-serializable. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** *Consider the following two transactions. Write a 2PL-compliant schedule and show its precedence graph.*

$$
T_1: \text{read}(A), \text{write}(A), \text{read}(B), \text{write}(B)
$$
$$
T_2: \text{read}(B), \text{write}(B), \text{read}(A), \text{write}(A)
$$
*Is the schedule conflict-serializable? If so, find the equivalent serial order.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify conflict pairs (1 Mark):**
- $T_1$ writes $A$; $T_2$ writes $A$ → conflict.
- $T_1$ writes $B$; $T_2$ writes $B$ → conflict.
- $T_1$ reads $B$; $T_2$ writes $B$ → conflict.
- $T_1$ writes $A$; $T_2$ reads $A$ → conflict.

**Step 2 — Construct a 2PL-compliant schedule (3 Marks):**

| Time | Operation | Phase of $T_1$ | Phase of $T_2$ |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | `lock-X(A), T1` | growing | — |
| 2 | `read(A), T1` | growing | — |
| 3 | `write(A), T1` | growing | — |
| 4 | `lock-X(B), T1` | growing | — |
| 5 | `read(B), T1` | growing | — |
| 6 | `write(B), T1` | **LP(T1)** | — |
| 7 | `unlock(A), T1` | shrinking | — |
| 8 | `unlock(B), T1` | shrinking | — |
| 9 | `lock-X(B), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 10 | `read(B), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 11 | `write(B), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 12 | `lock-X(A), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 13 | `read(A), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 14 | `write(A), T2` | shrinking | growing |
| 15 | `unlock(B), T2` | shrinking | shrinking |
| 16 | `unlock(A), T2` | shrinking | shrinking |
| 17 | `commit(T1), commit(T2)` | end | end |

**Step 3 — Verify 2PL (1 Mark):** For $T_1$, last lock = step 4, first unlock = step 7. No lock after step 7. ✅ For $T_2$, last lock = step 12, first unlock = step 15. ✅

**Step 4 — Precedence Graph (1 Mark):**
All conflicting operations of $T_1$ precede those of $T_2$. So $P(S) = \{T_1 \to T_2\}$, which is a **DAG**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 Mark):** The schedule is **conflict-serializable**, and the equivalent serial order is **$T_1 \to T_2$**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the four variations of Two-Phase Locking: Basic, Conservative, Strict, and Rigorous. Use a comparative table covering: deadlock-freedom, cascade-freedom, and prior knowledge of data items.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Brief Introduction (1 Mark):**
2PL has four canonical variations, each trading off safety properties (deadlock-freedom, cascade-freedom) against implementation flexibility.

**Step 2 — Comparative Table (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Property} & \textbf{Basic} & \textbf{Conservative} & \textbf{Strict} & \textbf{Rigorous} \\
\hline
\text{Deadlock-free?} & \text{No} & \text{Yes} & \text{No} & \text{No} \\
\hline
\text{Cascade-free?} & \text{No} & \text{Yes} & \text{Yes} & \text{Yes} \\
\hline
\text{Needs prior item list?} & \text{No} & \text{Yes} & \text{No} & \text{No} \\
\hline
\text{Lock release point} & \text{Any time after LP} & \text{All at commit} & \text{X at commit} & \text{All at commit} \\
\hline
\text{Practical use} & \text{Rare} & \text{Never} & \text{Common} & \text{Academic} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 — Explanation of Each (3 Marks):**

- **Basic 2PL:** Original protocol. Allows early release of locks. Susceptible to deadlocks and cascading aborts.
- **Conservative 2PL:** Acquires *all* locks at the start. Deadlock-free because no cyclic wait can form. Requires the transaction to know its full read/write set in advance — impractical for long transactions.
- **Strict 2PL:** Holds all X-locks until commit/abort. Prevents dirty reads and cascading aborts. Industry standard.
- **Rigorous 2PL:** Holds *all* locks (S and X) until commit. Simplest to implement, ensures serial-equivalent behavior, but reduces concurrency for read-heavy workloads.

**(b)** *What is a deadlock? Explain the Wait-Die and Wound-Wait schemes used to prevent deadlocks in 2PL. Suppose two transactions $T_1$ (timestamp 5) and $T_2$ (timestamp 8) are in conflict, with $T_1$ holding a lock that $T_2$ wants. Apply both schemes.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Deadlock (1 Mark):**
A **deadlock** is a state in which two or more transactions are **cyclically waiting** for each other to release locks, with none able to proceed. In the wait-for graph, a cycle indicates deadlock.

**Step 2 — Wait-Die Scheme (Non-preemptive) (2 Marks):**
**Rule:** If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$ (i.e., $T_i$ is **older**), then $T_i$ is **allowed to wait**. Otherwise, $T_j$ (the **younger** requester) is **killed (dies)** and restarted later with the **same timestamp**.

**Step 3 — Wound-Wait Scheme (Preemptive) (2 Marks):**
**Rule:** If $TS(T_i) < TS(T_j)$ (i.e., $T_i$ is older), then $T_j$ (younger) is **wounded** — its lock is preempted and it is rolled back. Otherwise, $T_j$ simply **waits**.

**Step 4 — Application to the Given Scenario (2 Marks):**
Given: $TS(T_1) = 5$ (older), $TS(T_2) = 8$ (younger). $T_1$ holds a lock; $T_2$ requests it.

- **Wait-Die:** $T_2$ is younger and wants $T_1$'s lock. Since $TS(T_1) < TS(T_2)$, the **older** ($T_1$) is allowed to wait — but $T_1$ doesn't want any lock here. The rule applies to the *requester*. $T_2$ (younger) requests a lock from older $T_1$, so by the die branch, $T_2$ **dies** and is restarted later with $TS = 8$.
- **Wound-Wait:** $T_2$ requests a lock from older $T_1$. By the wound branch, $T_2$ is **wounded** — it is rolled back. (Same final effect, different philosophical category — preemptive vs non-preemptive.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Confusing Wait-Die with Wound-Wait:** Many students write *"older dies"* — that is **wrong**. In **Wait-Die**, the **younger dies**; in **Wound-Wait**, the **younger is wounded**. Memorize: *Wait-Die → Die = Younger; Wound-Wait → Wound = Younger*. (2-mark loss if reversed.)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention timestamp retention:** When a transaction is rolled back in either scheme, it must be **restarted with its original timestamp**, not a new one. Losing this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Saying 2PL is "deadlock-free":** Only **Conservative 2PL** is deadlock-free. Basic, Strict, and Rigorous 2PL **can** deadlock. (1-mark loss.)
> 4. **Drawing the precedence graph incorrectly:** The edge direction $T_i \to T_j$ means "$T_i$ must come before $T_j$". Reversing arrows is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- 🔑 **2PL Definition:** All locks acquired before the first unlock. Two phases: **Growing** and **Shrinking**.
- 🔑 **Lock Point:** Moment of last lock acquisition. Bounds the two phases.
- 🔑 **Sufficiency Theorem:** 2PL ⇒ Conflict-Serializable (proved via lock-point ordering contradiction).
- 🔑 **Necessity:** 2PL is **not** necessary — some CSR schedules are not 2PL.
- 🔑 **Lock Compatibility:** S-S ✓, S-X ✗, X-X ✗.
- 🔑 **Four Variations — Memorize This Triangle:**

  ```
              Conservative 2PL
                  /\
                 /  \   (no deadlock)
                /    \
      Rigorous 2PL --- Strict 2PL
              \        /
               \      /  (cascadeless)
                \    /
              Basic 2PL
  ```

- 🔑 **Most-Used Variation in Industry:** **Strict 2PL** (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB).
- 🔑 **Deadlock Schemes:**
  - **Wait-Die** → Non-preemptive → Younger dies → Restart with same TS.
  - **Wound-Wait** → Preemptive → Younger is wounded → Restart with same TS.
- 🔑 **Starvation:** Avoided by FIFO queueing or transaction priority aging.
- 🔑 **Lock Upgrade (S→X)** counts as a **lock acquisition** (must obey 2PL).
- 🔑 **Lock Downgrade (X→S)** counts as a **lock release** (must obey 2PL).
- 🔑 **Cascading Rollback:** Prevented by Strict and Rigorous 2PL; **NOT** prevented by Basic 2PL.
- 🔑 **Deadlocks:** Prevented **only** by Conservative 2PL; detected by wait-for graph cycles in others.
- 🔑 **Common KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Differentiate 2PL variations + prove 2PL guarantees CSR + solve a schedule problem." Practice this exact combination.
- 🔑 **Formula to memorize:** For 2PL, the algebraic identity is the **acyclic** nature of the precedence graph under the **strict ordering of lock points**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log-Based Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Log-Based Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Log-Based Recovery** is a fundamental **Database Recovery Technique** that utilizes a dedicated system log (also called the *transaction log* or *journal*) to record every modification operation performed by concurrent transactions against the database. The log is a sequence of log records maintained on **stable (non-volatile) storage**, capturing sufficient information (such as old values, new values, transaction identifiers, and timestamps) to enable the DBMS to reconstruct the database to a consistent state following a system crash, transaction failure, or media failure.

The recovery subsystem guarantees the **ACID properties** of transactions, specifically **Atomicity** (all-or-nothing execution) and **Durability** (committed changes survive crashes), by consulting the log during the **Redo** and **Undo** phases of the restart procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Log-Based Recovery is a recovery technique in which a log of all modifications made by a transaction is maintained on stable storage before the actual database is updated. This log is consulted during the recovery process to undo (rollback) uncommitted transactions and redo committed transactions after a system failure."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **bank accountant** handling 500 customer ledgers. Every time a transaction (say, a withdrawal of ₹5,000 from Mr. A's account) is initiated, before you **erase the old balance** in the master ledger book, you make a **timestamped carbon copy entry** in a separate **Audit Notebook** (the LOG) noting:
- Who made the change (Transaction ID)
- Which account was changed (Data Item)
- What the old balance was (Old Value)
- What the new balance is (New Value)

If the master book falls into water (system crash), you can:
- **UNDO** the entry: If the transaction was not finished (e.g., a power cut occurred mid-update), you restore the old balance from the Audit Notebook.
- **REDO** the entry: If the transaction was completed but the ink smudged before drying (lost update on disk), you re-write the new balance from the Audit Notebook.

This **Audit Notebook = The Log**, and the rule "**write to the Audit Notebook before touching the master book**" is the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The log acts as the *black box flight recorder* of the database. As long as the black box survives, we can reconstruct the flight (database state) and prevent irreversible damage.

---

## 1.3 Why Log-Based Recovery is Mandatory in KTU

| Failure Type | Log's Role |
|---|---|
| **Transaction Failure** (e.g., division by zero) | Log used to **UNDO** the aborting transaction |
| **System Crash** (power failure) | Log used to identify committed vs. uncommitted; **REDO** & **UNDO** |
| **Media Failure** (disk crash) | Log + archive backup reconstruct the database |
| **Logical Error** (constraint violation) | Log-based **rollback** to a savepoint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — Stable Storage:** In KTU theory, *stable storage* is an idealized storage that **survives all failures**. In practice, it is approximated using **mirrored disks / RAID / NVRAM**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Log Record

Every modification generates a log record of the canonical form:

$$\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$$

Where:
- $T_i$ → Unique **Transaction Identifier**
- $X_j$ → **Data Item** (record/field) being modified
- $V_{\text{old}}$ → **Before Image** (BFIM — Before Image of $X_j$)
- $V_{\text{new}}$ → **After Image** (AFIM — After Image of $X_j$)

### 2.1.1 Types of Log Records (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Log Record Type | Format | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Record** | $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ | Captures actual data modifications |
| **Commit Record** | $\langle T_i, \text{commit} \rangle$ | Marks successful end of $T_i$ |
| **Abort Record** | $\langle T_i, \text{abort} \rangle$ | Marks rolled-back end of $T_i$ |
| **Begin Record** | $\langle T_i, \text{begin} \rangle$ | Marks start of $T_i$ |
| **Checkpoint Record** | $\langle \text{checkpoint}, L \rangle$ | Synchronization point; $L$ = list of active transactions |

---

## 2.2 The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Log-Based Recovery (WAL):**
> *"Before a data item $X_j$ is modified on stable storage, the log record corresponding to that modification **MUST** be physically written to stable storage."*

This is the **single most important rule** for KTU exams. Without WAL, the log may not contain information needed to recover a value, rendering recovery impossible.

---

## 2.3 The Two Modification Strategies

### A. Deferred Update (No-Undo/Redo Algorithm variant)
- Database is updated **only AFTER** the transaction reaches its **partially committed state**.
- During execution, all updates are written **only to the log and local buffers**.
- The actual database on disk is updated only when `commit` log record is written.
- **Recovery:** Only **REDO** needed; no UNDO required.

### B. Immediate Update (The Standard Log-Based Method)
- Database may be updated **before** the transaction commits.
- Log records for updates are generated **immediately** and flushed per WAL.
- **Recovery:** Both **UNDO** (for uncommitted) and **REDO** (for committed but lost) needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus prioritizes the **Immediate Update** technique with full **Undo/Redo recovery algorithm** using checkpoints.

---

## 2.4 The Undo / Redo Paradigm

For any data item $X_j$ modified by transaction $T_i$, the recovery manager consults the log:

| Condition of $T_i$ | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $T_i$ has **commit** log record AND changes not yet on disk | **REDO $T_i$** | Committed changes must persist (**Durability**) |
| $T_i$ has **no commit** record | **UNDO $T_i$** | Uncommitted changes must be rolled back (**Atomicity**) |
| $T_i$ committed **before last checkpoint** | **No action** | Already on disk and consistent |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Log-Based Recovery

| # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Update Log Record | $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ | Recovery information |
| 2 | Write-Ahead Logging | $\text{LOG\_FLUSH}(L) \Rightarrow \text{DB\_WRITE}(X_j)$ | Crash-safety guarantee |
| 3 | UNDO Rule | $T_i \notin \text{Committed} \Rightarrow \text{UNDO}(T_i)$ | Atomicity |
| 4 | REDO Rule | $T_i \in \text{Committed} \land \neg \text{OnDisk} \Rightarrow \text{REDO}(T_i)$ | Durability |
| 5 | Checkpoint Active Set | $L = \{ T_i \mid T_i \text{ started but not committed}\}$ | Minimizes recovery work |
| 6 | Recovery Boundary | Process log **backwards from end** to **last checkpoint** | Efficiency |
| 7 | Log Force at Commit | On `commit`, force log block to stable storage | Ensures durability |
| 8 | Force-Log-At-Commit | $T_i$ commit $\Rightarrow$ Force all $T_i$ log records | Standard in PostgreSQL, Oracle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Note:** Modern RDBMS engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB) implement a refined version of this algorithm called **ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics)**, developed at IBM. ARIES uses **Log Sequence Numbers (LSN)**, **compensation log records (CLRs)**, and **steal/no-force buffer management**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Log-Based Recovery is the **backbone of mission-critical systems**:
- **Banking Systems:** Every ₹-transaction logged to enable rollback on failure.
- **Airline Reservations:** Seats are reserved/de-reserved atomically using logs.
- **E-commerce Carts:** Cart operations are recoverable through session-level logs.
- **Distributed Databases (Spanner, CockroachDB):** Uses distributed consensus logs (Paxos/Raft) which are essentially synchronized, replicated logs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Working of Immediate Log-Based Recovery

### Algorithm: The Recovery Process (Undo/Redo with Checkpoint)

The recovery algorithm executes in **three sequential phases** when the system restarts after a crash:

---

#### **Phase 1: Identification Phase (Backward Scan)**

**Step 1:** Initialize two empty sets:
- **REDO-List** $\leftarrow \emptyset$
- **UNDO-List** $\leftarrow \emptyset$

**Step 2:** Scan the log **backwards** (from the most recent record to the oldest) until the **last checkpoint record** is encountered.

**Step 3:** Upon encountering a log record $r$:
- If $r = \langle T_i, \text{commit} \rangle$ → Add $T_i$ to **REDO-List**
- If $r = \langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ → Add $T_i$ to **REDO-List** if not already present
- If $r = \langle T_i, \text{begin} \rangle$ → Add $T_i$ to **UNDO-List**

**Step 4:** For each transaction $T_i$ in the **active set $L$** of the last checkpoint:
- If $T_i \in \text{REDO-List}$ → keep in REDO-List
- Else → Add to **UNDO-List**

---

#### **Phase 2: UNDO Phase (Rollback Uncommitted Transactions)**

For each transaction $T_i \in \text{UNDO-List}$:

**Step 5:** Scan the log **backwards** for records of $T_i$.

**Step 6:** For each $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ found:
- Restore the database: $X_j \leftarrow V_{\text{old}}$

**Step 7:** Write a compensation record: $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}} \rangle$ to the log (so a future crash doesn't attempt to undo again).

**Step 8:** After all $T_i$ records are undone, append $\langle T_i, \text{abort} \rangle$ to the log.

---

#### **Phase 3: REDO Phase (Replay Committed Transactions)**

For each transaction $T_i \in \text{REDO-List}$:

**Step 9:** Scan the log **forwards** for records of $T_i$ from the **beginning** of its log segment.

**Step 10:** For each $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$:
- Set: $X_j \leftarrow V_{\text{new}}$

**Step 11:** Continue until $\langle T_i, \text{commit} \rangle$ is reached; the transaction is now durable.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

Consider the following log sequence (L1 to L9 in chronological order) for transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$:

$$\text{L1: } \langle T_1, \text{begin} \rangle$$
$$\text{L2: } \langle T_1, A, 1000, 950 \rangle$$
$$\text{L3: } \langle T_2, \text{begin} \rangle$$
$$\text{L4: } \langle T_2, B, 2000, 2050 \rangle$$
$$\text{L5: } \langle \text{checkpoint}, \{T_1, T_2\} \rangle$$
$$\text{L6: } \langle T_1, \text{commit} \rangle$$
$$\text{L7: } \langle T_3, \text{begin} \rangle$$
$$\text{L8: } \langle T_3, C, 500, 600 \rangle$$
$$\text{L9: } \langle T_2, \text{commit} \rangle$$

**System crashes just after L9** (during the write of the corresponding database block). Apply the recovery algorithm.

### Step-by-Step Recovery Execution:

**Step 1 (Init):** $\text{REDO-List} = \emptyset$, $\text{UNDO-List} = \emptyset$.

**Step 2 (Scan Backward from L9 to L5/checkpoint):**
- L9: $\langle T_2, \text{commit} \rangle$ → $T_2 \in \text{REDO-List}$
- L8: $\langle T_3, C, 500, 600 \rangle$ → $T_3 \in \text{REDO-List}$ (preliminary, no commit yet)
- L7: $\langle T_3, \text{begin} \rangle$ → $T_3 \in \text{UNDO-List}$
- L6: $\langle T_1, \text{commit} \rangle$ → $T_1 \in \text{REDO-List}$
- L5 (Checkpoint): Active set $L = \{T_1, T_2\}$. Both are in REDO-List, so no transfer to UNDO.

**Step 3 (Final State of Lists):**
$$\text{REDO-List} = \{T_1, T_2\}, \quad \text{UNDO-List} = \{T_3\}$$

**Step 4 (UNDO Phase for $T_3$):** Scan L8 backward → Set $C \leftarrow 500$. Write $\langle T_3, C, 500 \rangle$ as compensation. Append $\langle T_3, \text{abort} \rangle$.

**Step 5 (REDO Phase for $T_1$):** Forward scan from L2 → Set $A \leftarrow 950$. Stop at L6 (commit).

**Step 6 (REDO Phase for $T_2$):** Forward scan from L4 → Set $B \leftarrow 2050$. Stop at L9 (commit).

### Final Database State After Recovery:
$$\boxed{A = 950, \quad B = 2050, \quad C = 500}$$

This is the **atomically consistent** state — $T_1$ and $T_2$ effects are durable, $T_3$ effect is rolled back.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — When UNDO vs REDO?

$$\text{Action}(T_i) = \begin{cases} \text{UNDO} & \text{if } T_i \notin \text{Committed} \\ \text{REDO} & \text{if } T_i \in \text{Committed} \land \text{not on disk} \\ \text{No Op} & \text{if } T_i \in \text{Committed} \land \text{on disk before crash} \end{cases}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Log-Based Recovery Manager

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Transaction Ti initiates write] --> B[Generate Log Record L Tx Xj Vold Vnew]
    B --> C{Write Ahead Logging Rule}
    C -- Log Force --> D[Write Log Record to Stable Storage]
    D --> E[Update Data Item Xj in Main Memory Buffer]
    E --> F{Buffer Full or Threshold}
    F -- Yes --> G[Flush Buffer Page to Disk Database]
    F -- No --> H[Keep in Buffer]
    A2[Transaction Ti requests COMMIT] --> I[Generate T commit Log Record]
    I --> J[Force Log to Stable Storage]
    J --> K[Transaction Ti is COMMITTED]
    K1[System Crash Restart] --> L[Recovery Manager Activated]
    L --> M[Scan Log Backward to Last Checkpoint]
    M --> N[Build REDO-List and UNDO-List]
    N --> O[UNDO Phase: Restore Vold for all UNDO-List Tx]
    O --> P[REDO Phase: Set Vnew for all REDO-List Tx]
    P --> Q[Database in Consistent State]
```

## 4.2 Recovery Phase Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1 IDENTIFICATION]
        S1[Initialize REDO-List empty] --> S2[Initialize UNDO-List empty]
        S2 --> S3[Scan log backward to checkpoint L]
        S3 --> S4[Mark committed Tx as REDO]
        S3 --> S5[Mark uncommitted Tx in checkpoint as UNDO]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2 UNDO]
        U1[For each Tx in UNDO-List] --> U2[Scan log backward for Tx records]
        U2 --> U3[Set Xj to Vold]
        U3 --> U4[Write Compensation Log Record CLR]
        U4 --> U5[Append T abort to log]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3 REDO]
        R1[For each Tx in REDO-List] --> R2[Scan log forward for Tx records]
        R2 --> R3[Set Xj to Vnew]
        R3 --> R4[Stop at T commit]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

## 4.3 Transaction Lifecycle with Log Points

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active : T begin log
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : Last statement executed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : T commit log forced to stable storage
    Active --> Aborted : T abort log written
    PartiallyCommitted --> Aborted : Failure detected
    Committed --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
    note right of Committed : Log records flushed via FORCE
    note right of Aborted : Log used to UNDO partial updates
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define Log-Based Recovery. List the different types of log records used in log-based recovery.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Log-Based Recovery is a database recovery technique in which a log (or journal) is maintained on stable storage to record all the modifications made by transactions. This log is consulted during the recovery process to **undo** uncommitted transactions and **redo** committed transactions after a system failure, ensuring atomicity and durability.

**Types of Log Records (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Update Record:** $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ — captures data modification.
2. **Commit Record:** $\langle T_i, \text{commit} \rangle$ — marks successful termination.
3. **Abort Record:** $\langle T_i, \text{abort} \rangle$ — marks rolled-back termination.
4. **Checkpoint Record:** $\langle \text{checkpoint}, L \rangle$ — synchronization point.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State and explain the Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol. Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1.5 Marks):**
The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol states that *before a data item $X_j$ is modified on stable storage (disk), the log record corresponding to that modification must be physically written to stable storage.*

**Significance (1.5 Marks):**
WAL is essential because it guarantees that the log always contains the **Before Image (BFIM)** and **After Image (AFIM)** of every data item. Without WAL, a crash between the log write and the database write would leave the database in an unrecoverable state — the recovery manager would lack the information needed to undo or redo the modification.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Describe the structure of a log record in log-based recovery. Explain the immediate update recovery technique with the Undo/Redo algorithm in detail.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Log Record Structure (2 Marks):**
A log record is a 4-tuple: $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$
- $T_i$ = Transaction identifier
- $X_j$ = Data item (file/record/field)
- $V_{\text{old}}$ = Value of $X_j$ before update (BFIM)
- $V_{\text{new}}$ = Value of $X_j$ after update (AFIM)

**2. Immediate Update Technique (2 Marks):**
In the immediate update technique, the database itself may be updated **before** the transaction reaches its commit point. However, the log records are generated and forced to stable storage **immediately** per the WAL rule. This is the most flexible and widely used approach in commercial DBMS.

**3. Undo/Redo Algorithm (3 Marks):**
The recovery procedure after a system crash is:
- **Step 1:** Scan the log **backwards** from the end to the last checkpoint.
- **Step 2:** Maintain two lists: **UNDO-List** (transactions that did not commit) and **REDO-List** (transactions that committed but whose updates may not be on disk).
- **Step 3:** **UNDO** all transactions in the UNDO-List by restoring each $X_j$ to $V_{\text{old}}$ (backward scan).
- **Step 4:** **REDO** all transactions in the REDO-List by setting each $X_j$ to $V_{\text{new}}$ (forward scan).
- **Step 5:** The database is now in a consistent state.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Consider the following log of transactions executed by a DBMS. The system crashes immediately after the last log record is written. Use the Undo/Redo algorithm to recover the database, given the initial values: $A = 1000, B = 2000, C = 700, D = 400$.**

| Log Sequence | Log Record |
|---|---|
| L1 | $\langle T_1, \text{begin} \rangle$ |
| L2 | $\langle T_1, A, 1000, 900 \rangle$ |
| L3 | $\langle T_2, \text{begin} \rangle$ |
| L4 | $\langle T_2, B, 2000, 2100 \rangle$ |
| L5 | $\langle \text{checkpoint}, \{T_1, T_2\} \rangle$ |
| L6 | $\langle T_1, \text{commit} \rangle$ |
| L7 | $\langle T_3, \text{begin} \rangle$ |
| L8 | $\langle T_3, C, 700, 600 \rangle$ |
| L9 | $\langle T_2, \text{commit} \rangle$ |

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Scan log backward from L9 to L5 (last checkpoint).**

- **L9:** $\langle T_2, \text{commit} \rangle$ → **$T_2$ → REDO-List** ✓
- **L8:** $\langle T_3, C, 700, 600 \rangle$ → $T_3$ added to REDO-List (provisional).
- **L7:** $\langle T_3, \text{begin} \rangle$ → **$T_3$ → UNDO-List** ✓
- **L6:** $\langle T_1, \text{commit} \rangle$ → **$T_1$ → REDO-List** ✓
- **L5 (Checkpoint):** Active set $L = \{T_1, T_2\}$. Both are in REDO-List, so they remain there. $T_3$ (not in checkpoint active set) stays in UNDO-List.

**Final Lists:**
$$\text{REDO-List} = \{T_1, T_2\}, \quad \text{UNDO-List} = \{T_3\}$$

**Step 2: UNDO Phase for $T_3$ (2.5 Marks):**
Scan L8 backward → Restore $C$ to $V_{\text{old}} = 700$.
- Write compensation record $\langle T_3, C, 700 \rangle$.
- Append $\langle T_3, \text{abort} \rangle$.

**Step 3: REDO Phase for $T_1$ (1.5 Marks):**
Forward scan from L2 → Set $A = 900$. Stop at L6 (commit).

**Step 4: REDO Phase for $T_2$ (1.5 Marks):**
Forward scan from L4 → Set $B = 2100$. Stop at L9 (commit).

**Final Database State:**
$$\boxed{A = 900, \quad B = 2100, \quad C = 700, \quad D = 400}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing UNDO and REDO order:** Some students UNDO before REDO. The correct standard is: identify both lists first, then UNDO, then REDO. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Checkpoint Active Set:** Many students ignore L5's active set $\{T_1, T_2\}$ and miss the nuance of pre-checkpoint transactions. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to write the $\langle T_i, \text{abort} \rangle$ record** at the end of UNDO. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Forward scan for UNDO and backward for REDO** is the WRONG direction. UNDO scans backward; REDO scans forward. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the concept of checkpoints in log-based recovery. How do checkpoints improve the efficiency of the recovery process?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Concept of Checkpoint (3 Marks):**
A **checkpoint** is a synchronization mechanism in log-based recovery. When the system takes a checkpoint, the following actions occur in sequence:
- **Step 1:** Suspend all transaction execution temporarily.
- **Step 2:** Force-write all updated database buffers from main memory to disk.
- **Step 3:** Write a $\langle \text{checkpoint}, L \rangle$ log record to stable storage, where $L$ is the list of active transactions at the checkpoint instant.
- **Step 4:** Resume transaction execution.

**2. Improvement of Efficiency (4 Marks):**
- **Without Checkpoint:** During recovery, the system must scan the **entire log** from the very first record to identify committed and uncommitted transactions. For a log containing millions of records from years of operation, this is computationally prohibitive.
- **With Checkpoint:** The recovery manager scans the log only **backward from the crash point to the last checkpoint**, not from the beginning of time. This drastically reduces the volume of log records to be processed.
- **Reduces Redundant Work:** Transactions that committed **before** the last checkpoint are guaranteed to have all their changes on disk (because the checkpoint forced a flush). They do not need to be redone.
- **Active Set Pruning:** The checkpoint's active set $L$ allows the system to determine which transactions definitely require UNDO (those that started before checkpoint but did not commit) and which require REDO (those that committed after checkpoint but whose changes may not be on disk).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Differentiate between the Deferred Update and Immediate Update modification techniques in log-based recovery. Which is more commonly used in practice and why?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| Comparison Aspect | Deferred Update | Immediate Update |
|---|---|---|
| **Database Modification Timing** | Database updated **only after** partial commit | Database may be updated **anytime** during execution |
| **Log Update Timing** | Log updated during execution | Log updated immediately (per WAL) |
| **Recovery Algorithm** | **REDO-only** (No UNDO) | **UNDO + REDO** (full) |
| **Atomicity Guarantee** | Inherently preserved (DB never reflects uncommitted data) | Maintained via UNDO of uncommitted Tx |
| **Durability Guarantee** | Maintained via REDO of committed Tx | Maintained via REDO of committed Tx |
| **Concurrency** | Lower (no write locks until commit) | Higher (incremental locking possible) |
| **Storage Access** | All writes concentrated at commit time | Writes spread throughout transaction |
| **Performance** | Slower for long transactions | Faster for long transactions |

**Common Practice (1 Mark):**
The **Immediate Update** technique is more commonly used in commercial DBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB) because:
- It allows **finer-grained concurrency control** (steal policy).
- It is more **practical for long-running transactions**.
- Modern systems use **ARIES** (a refined Immediate Update algorithm) which is highly efficient.
- Deferred Update has the drawback of **buffering all updates** in memory, leading to high memory pressure for long transactions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B:**
> 1. **Writing recovery steps out of order** (e.g., REDO before identification). The strict order is: **IDENTIFY → UNDO → REDO**. Marks are awarded for the **correct sequencing**.
> 2. **Not mentioning the Compensation Log Record (CLR)** in modern ARIES-based recovery (relevant for higher-order KTU questions).
> 3. **Ignoring the WAL rule** when explaining either modification technique — the WAL is non-negotiable.
> 4. **Confusing BFIM (Before Image) with AFIM (After Image)** in the log record tuple. UNDO uses BFIM ($V_{\text{old}}$); REDO uses AFIM ($V_{\text{new}}$).

---

## 📋 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

✅ **Log-Based Recovery** uses a sequence of log records on **stable storage** to recover from failures.

✅ **Log Record Format:** $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{\text{old}}, V_{\text{new}} \rangle$ — the *core* of the syllabus.

✅ **WAL (Write-Ahead Logging):** Log must be written to stable storage **before** the actual database update.

✅ **Five Log Record Types:** Update, Commit, Abort, Begin, Checkpoint.

✅ **Immediate Update** allows DB modifications during execution; **Deferred Update** postpones them until commit.

✅ **Recovery Algorithm has 3 Phases:**
1. **IDENTIFY** — Build REDO-List and UNDO-List (backward scan to checkpoint).
2. **UNDO** — Backward scan; restore $V_{\text{old}}$ for UNDO-List Tx.
3. **REDO** — Forward scan; apply $V_{\text{new}}$ for REDO-List Tx.

✅ **Checkpoints** improve recovery efficiency by reducing the log scan range and pruning the active transaction set.

✅ **UNDO Rule:** Uncommitted transactions → restore to $V_{\text{old}}$.

✅ **REDO Rule:** Committed transactions whose changes may not be on disk → apply $V_{\text{new}}$.

✅ **Force-Log-at-Commit:** On transaction commit, all log records must be flushed to stable storage.

✅ **BFIM (Before Image)** = old value; **AFIM (After Image)** = new value.

✅ **Stable Storage** = idealized non-volatile storage (RAID, mirrored disks in practice).

✅ **Modern Refinement:** **ARIES Algorithm** (used in IBM DB2, PostgreSQL) is the state-of-the-art descendant of basic log-based recovery.

✅ **Atomicity & Durability** are the two ACID properties directly guaranteed by log-based recovery.

✅ **Backward scan for UNDO**, **forward scan for REDO** — remember this directional rule.

✅ **Compensation Log Records (CLRs)** are written during UNDO to prevent repeated undo on system re-failure (ARIES concept).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Check-Pointing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBERT504 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Check-Pointing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Check-Pointing** in the context of Database Management Systems (DBMS) is a *recovery mechanism* used by the transaction manager and recovery subsystem to periodically synchronize the database state with the contents of the system log. A checkpoint is a designated, time-stamped point of consistency where:

1. All log records that were residing in the **main memory log buffer** are forcefully flushed (force-written) to the **stable/disk-based log file**.
2. All modified buffer pages (dirty pages) in the **database buffer pool** are written back to the physical database on **stable storage**.
3. A special log record called `<CHECKPOINT>` (or `CKPT`) is appended to the log.
4. The checkpoint record's LSN (Log Sequence Number) is stored in a safe, well-known location (often the **restart file** or **master record**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Recovery Techniques):** Checkpointing is the precursor to every recovery strategy (deferred update, immediate update, ARIES, log-based recovery). Without checkpoints, recovery would require redoing/undoing *every* transaction from the beginning of time, which is computationally impossible.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Saved Game" Metaphor)

Imagine you are playing a 3-hour open-world video game. You cannot complete the entire game in one sitting. The game lets you press **SAVE** every 30 minutes. 

- The **video game world state** = the **database on disk**.
- The **active console RAM** = the **DBMS buffer pool in main memory**.
- The **automatic background saves / manual save points** = **checkpoints**.
- The **"Continue from last save" feature** = the **recovery process after a crash**.

If your console suddenly crashes at the 2-hour-45-minute mark, you don't have to replay from minute 0 (which would be 2:45 hours of rework). You simply **reload from the last checkpoint** (say, at 2:30), replay only the last 15 minutes of actions, and you are back in business.

**Translating this to DBMS:**

> [!NOTE]
> - Without checkpoint → Recovery must scan the *entire* log from the start of history (extremely expensive, O(N) where N = total log records ever written).
> - With checkpoint → Recovery begins from the *last checkpoint* onward, drastically reducing cost to O(C) where C = number of log records since the last checkpoint.
> - A checkpoint converts a *catastrophic* full-log recovery into a *bounded, tractable* localized recovery.

## 1.3 The Core Three Sub-Tasks of a Checkpoint

A checkpoint operation in KTU/Korth/Silberschatz terminology consists of three atomic sub-actions:

1. **Suspend Transaction Processing** (or use *fuzzy* checkpointing – which is a more advanced concept).
2. **Force-Write All Updated Buffer Pages to Disk** (write all `<Ti, X, old_value, new_value>` log records to stable storage; flush dirty pages).
3. **Append `<CHECKPOINT>` record to Log and Flush Log**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Checkpointing

| Metric | Symbol | Description | Unit |
|--------|--------|-------------|------|
| Log Sequence Number | $LSN$ | Monotonically increasing identifier for each log record | Integer |
| Transaction Table Entry | $T_i$ | Active transaction identifier at checkpoint | Symbol |
| Dirty Page Table Entry | $D_i$ | List of buffer pages modified but not yet flushed | Page ID |
| Master Record LSN | $M_{LSN}$ | Address of the most recent checkpoint record | Integer |
| Recovery Time | $T_R$ | Time taken to recover from last checkpoint | Seconds |
| Checkpoint Interval | $\Delta t_c$ | Time between successive checkpoints | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** The checkpoint must be **idempotent** — repeated execution of a checkpoint must produce the same result, and partial checkpoints (incomplete writes) must not corrupt the database.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Why is Checkpointing Needed?

In a DBMS, every transaction $T_i$ performs read/write operations. The recovery subsystem uses the log to reconstruct state. However:

- The log grows **monotonically and unboundedly**.
- Without checkpointing, every crash triggers a **complete log replay** (redo + undo of all transactions) — this is **prohibitively expensive**.
- Many transactions may have committed long ago and their effects are already flushed to disk; replaying them is pure waste.

**The Why, in one line:**
> *Checkpointing marks a point in the log after which the recovery subsystem can guarantee a deterministic, bounded search-space during crash recovery.*

## 2.2 The "How" — Step-by-Step Mechanics of a Checkpoint

The following is the canonical KTU/Korth textbook algorithm for executing a checkpoint:

### Algorithm: Simple (Non-Fuzzy) Checkpoint

**Step 1 — Quiesce the System**
- Halt the acceptance of new transactions.
- Wait for all currently active transactions to complete or abort.
- *(This is the "stop-the-world" cost of simple checkpointing.)*

**Step 2 — Flush All Log Records to Stable Storage**
- Force-write the entire content of the in-memory log buffer to the log file on disk.
- This ensures every committed transaction's log record is durable.

**Step 3 — Flush All Modified Pages to Disk**
- Write all dirty pages from the buffer pool to the physical database files on disk.
- This brings the on-disk database into a *consistent* state reflecting all committed work.

**Step 4 — Append and Flush `<CHECKPOINT>` Record**
- Write a special log record with the format:
  `<CHECKPOINT, [list of active transactions]>`
- Flush this record to the log on disk.

**Step 5 — Resume Normal Operation**
- New transactions are accepted again.

## 2.3 The Transaction Table & Dirty Page Table (Advanced ARIES-Style Checkpoint)

Modern KTU syllabus (post-2024 scheme) emphasizes the **ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics)** style of checkpointing, which avoids halting transactions. Here, the checkpoint log record contains two structures:

### A. Transaction Table ($TT$)
A list of currently active (uncommitted) transactions with metadata:
- $T_i$ : Transaction ID
- $LastLSN(T_i)$ : The LSN of the most recent log record written by $T_i$
- $Status(T_i)$ : `Active`, `Committed`, or `Aborted`

### B. Dirty Page Table ($DPT$)
A list of pages in the buffer pool that have been modified but not yet written to disk:
- $PageID$ : Identifier of the dirty page
- $RecLSN$ : LSN of the log record that first caused this page to be dirty

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Log Sequence Number increment | $LSN_{n+1} = LSN_n + 1$ | Each log record gets a unique monotonically increasing ID |
| 2 | Master Record Update | $M_{LSN} \leftarrow LSN_{CKPT}$ | Restart file points to the latest checkpoint |
| 3 | Recovery Search Bound | $T_R \propto \log_{size}(\text{log}) - LSN_{CKPT}$ | Recovery cost is proportional to log length *after* the last checkpoint |
| 4 | Checkpoint Frequency Trade-off | $\Delta t_c = \dfrac{C_{cp}}{C_{recovery}}$ | Optimal interval balances checkpoint cost vs. recovery cost |
| 5 | Transactions to Redo Set | $Redo = \{T_i \mid T_i \text{ committed after } CKPT\}$ | Set of transactions to re-apply during recovery |
| 6 | Transactions to Undo Set | $Undo = \{T_i \mid T_i \text{ active at } CKPT \text{ and didn't commit}\}$ | Set of transactions to roll back during recovery |
| 7 | Forced Write Guarantee | $W_{disk}(T_i) \implies \text{durable}$ | Once on disk, data survives crashes (the WAL principle) |
| 8 | WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) Constraint | $LSN(flush(P)) > LSN(last\_log\_record(T_i) \mid T_i \text{ modified } P)$ | Log records must hit disk *before* the corresponding page does |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **WAL (Write-Ahead Logging)** rule and the **Checkpoint rule** together form the foundation of crash-safety. WAL says: *log before page*. Checkpoint says: *periodically commit all log to disk*. Together they guarantee the **ARIES recovery theorem** — any committed transaction is durable; any aborted transaction is invisible.

## 2.5 Recovery Algorithm Using a Checkpoint

When the system crashes and restarts, the recovery manager executes:

**Phase 1 — Analysis Pass**
- Start from the last checkpoint (using $M_{LSN}$).
- Scan forward to build the **Redo set** and **Undo set**.

**Phase 2 — Redo Pass**
- For each transaction in the **Redo set**, re-apply all logged updates.
- This restores the database to the state at the moment of crash.

**Phase 3 — Undo Pass**
- For each transaction in the **Undo set**, roll back updates (by writing `<Ti, X, new_val, old_val>` compensation log records, called **CLRs** — Compensation Log Records).
- Aborted/active transactions are thus cleanly reversed.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: Checkpointing every few seconds ensures that ATM transaction recovery is bounded.
- **E-Commerce**: Prevents loss of shopping cart data on server crash.
- **Stock Trading Platforms**: Sub-second checkpoint intervals handle thousands of TPS (transactions per second).
- **Cloud Databases** (e.g., AWS Aurora, Google Spanner): Implement **fuzzy checkpointing** that doesn't halt transactions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations: Optimal Checkpoint Interval

Let us mathematically derive the **optimal checkpoint frequency** for a database system. This is a high-yield KTU analytical problem.

### Problem Setup

Let:
- $t_f$ = time taken to perform one checkpoint (flush log + flush dirty pages) [seconds]
- $n$ = number of log records generated per unit time [records/second]
- $T$ = total time between system start and crash [seconds]
- $C$ = total number of checkpoints performed before crash
- $\Delta t_c$ = interval between consecutive checkpoints [seconds]

### Derivation

The total work done by the recovery subsystem at crash time is proportional to the number of log records since the *last* checkpoint. If checkpoints occur at regular intervals of $\Delta t_c$:

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship between variables:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{T}{\Delta t_c} \right\rfloor \\
\text{Recovery Cost} \ R &= n \cdot \Delta t_c \\
\text{Checkpoint Cost} \ C_{total} &= C \cdot t_f = \dfrac{T}{\Delta t_c} \cdot t_f
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define the total cost function:**

The total cost (checkpointing + recovery) is the sum of the cost of performing all checkpoints and the cost of recovery from the last checkpoint:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} \ TC(\Delta t_c) &= C_{total} + R \\
TC(\Delta t_c) &= \dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{\Delta t_c} + n \cdot \Delta t_c
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize the Total Cost (apply calculus optimization):**

To find the optimal $\Delta t_c^*$, we differentiate $TC(\Delta t_c)$ with respect to $\Delta t_c$ and set it to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\,TC}{d\,\Delta t_c} &= -\dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{(\Delta t_c)^2} + n = 0 \\
\implies \quad \dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{(\Delta t_c)^2} &= n \\
\implies \quad (\Delta t_c)^2 &= \dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{n} \\
\implies \quad \Delta t_c^* &= \sqrt{\dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{n}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find minimum total cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TC_{min} &= \dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{\sqrt{T t_f / n}} + n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T t_f}{n}} \\
&= \sqrt{T \cdot t_f \cdot n} + \sqrt{T \cdot t_f \cdot n} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{T \cdot t_f \cdot n}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\Delta t_c^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{T \cdot t_f}{n}} \quad \text{and} \quad TC_{min} = 2\sqrt{T \cdot t_f \cdot n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimal checkpoint interval is the **geometric mean** of the total time-to-crash and the time-per-checkpoint, scaled by log-generation rate. Increasing the checkpoint frequency (smaller $\Delta t_c$) *reduces* recovery cost but *increases* checkpoint overhead — and vice versa. The optimal point balances both.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Transitions During Checkpoint

The following table summarizes the **state** of a transaction $T_i$ across checkpoint events:

| Time Point | $T_i$ Status Before | Action Taken | $T_i$ Status After | Log Record Written |
|------------|---------------------|--------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| $t_0$ | Active | $T_i$ starts | Active | `<START T_i>` |
| $t_1$ | Active | $T_i$ issues UPDATE | Active | `<T_i, X, v_1, v_2>` |
| $t_{ckpt}$ | Active | Checkpoint initiated | Active (listed in $TT$) | `<CHECKPOINT, [T_i, T_j]>` |
| $t_{ckpt}^+$ | Active | Checkpoint completed | Active | — (no extra record) |
| $t_2$ | Active | $T_i$ issues COMMIT | Committed | `<COMMIT T_i>` |
| $t_3$ | Committed | — | Committed (durable) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transaction listed in the **Transaction Table** of a `<CHECKPOINT>` record is one that was *uncommitted* at the time of the checkpoint. The recovery system uses this to determine which transactions were in-flight.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying Redo and Undo Sets

**Scenario:** The log (in order) contains:

1. `<T1, START>`
2. `<T1, A, 100, 200>`
3. `<T2, START>`
4. `<T2, B, 50, 75>`
5. `<CHECKPOINT, [T1, T2]>` ← Transaction Table at checkpoint
6. `<T1, COMMIT>`
7. `<T3, START>`
8. `<T2, B, 75, 80>`
9. `<T3, C, 10, 20>`
10. `<T2, COMMIT>`
11. *<-- CRASH -->*

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the last checkpoint (record #5).**

At checkpoint #5: Active transactions = $\{T1, T2\}$.

**Step 2 — For each transaction, check if it committed after the checkpoint:**

- $T1$: Committed at #6 (after #5) → **Redo Set**
- $T2$: Committed at #10 (after #5) → **Redo Set**
- $T3$: Never committed (started at #7, no COMMIT record) → **Undo Set**

**Step 3 — Final classification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Redo Set} &= \{T1, T2\} \\
\text{Undo Set} &= \{T3\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Recovery Actions:**

- **REDO T1**: Re-apply `<T1, A, 100, 200>` → Set A = 200.
- **REDO T2**: Re-apply `<T2, B, 50, 75>` and `<T2, B, 75, 80>` → Set B = 80.
- **UNDO T3**: Reverse `<T3, C, 10, 20>` by writing a CLR: `<T3, C, 20, 10>` → Set C = 10.

> [!NOTE]
> The transactions listed in the checkpoint's Transaction Table serve as the *initial* set of suspects. From there, the recovery manager scans forward to filter out those that subsequently committed (move to Redo) versus those that never committed (move to Undo).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Flow of Checkpointing

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a checkpointing subsystem within a DBMS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Manager] --> B[Log Buffer in Main Memory]
    B --> C[Stable Log File on Disk]
    A --> D[Database Buffer Pool]
    D --> E[Physical Database on Disk]
    F[Checkpoint Manager Module] --> B
    F --> D
    F --> G[Master Record File]
    F --> C
    H[Recovery Manager] --> G
    H --> C
    H --> E
    I[Transaction Table TT] --> F
    J[Dirty Page Table DPT] --> F
    K[System Clock Trigger] --> F
    L[Transaction Commit Event] --> F
```

### Description of the Flow

- **Transaction Manager** issues read/write operations that generate log records.
- **Log Buffer** is the in-memory staging area for log records.
- **Checkpoint Manager** is the central coordinating module that orchestrates the three-step flush.
- **Transaction Table (TT)** and **Dirty Page Table (DPT)** are the bookkeeping structures that get appended to the `<CHECKPOINT>` log record.
- **Recovery Manager** consults the **Master Record File** on startup to find the most recent checkpoint LSN, then uses the log to redo/undo transactions.

## 4.2 State Machine of a Checkpoint Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "IDLE - Normal Operation" as Idle
    state "TRIGGER - Time / Log Size / Commit Threshold" as Trigger
    state "QUIESCE - Halt New Transactions" as Quiesce
    state "FLUSH LOG - Force-Write Log Buffer" as FlushLog
    state "FLUSH DIRTY PAGES - Write Buffer to Disk" as FlushPages
    state "WRITE CHECKPOINT RECORD" as WriteCKPT
    state "UPDATE MASTER RECORD" as UpdateMaster
    state "RESUME - Normal Operation" as Resume
    Idle --> Trigger: Periodic Timer / Threshold
    Trigger --> Quiesce: Begin Checkpoint
    Quiesce --> FlushLog: Active TXs Paused
    FlushLog --> FlushPages: Log on Stable Storage
    FlushPages --> WriteCKPT: Dirty Pages on Disk
    WriteCKPT --> UpdateMaster: CKPT Log Appended
    UpdateMaster --> Resume: M_LSN Updated
    Resume --> Idle
    Quiesce --> Idle: Abort Checkpoint
```

## 4.3 Recovery Flow Using Checkpoint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Crash Detected] --> B[Restart: Load Master Record]
    B --> C[Locate Last Checkpoint LSN]
    C --> D[Analysis Pass: Build TT and DPT]
    D --> E{Transaction Status?}
    E -->|Committed after CKPT| F[Redo Set]
    E -->|Active at CKPT and not committed| G[Undo Set]
    F --> H[Redo Pass: Re-apply Updates]
    G --> I[Undo Pass: Write CLRs]
    H --> J[Database Restored to Pre-Crash State]
    I --> J
    J --> K[System Ready for New Transactions]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Memory vs Disk State at Checkpoint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEMORY[Main Memory - Volatile]
        LB[Log Buffer]
        BP[Buffer Pool - Dirty Pages]
        TT[Transaction Table]
        DPT[Dirty Page Table]
    end
    subgraph DISK[Stable Storage - Non-Volatile]
        SLF[Stable Log File]
        PDB[Physical Database Files]
        MR[Master Record - Holds Last CKPT LSN]
    end
    LB -.FORCE WRITE.-> SLF
    BP -.FLUSH.-> PDB
    TT --> CHKPT[CHECKPOINT Log Record]
    DPT --> CHKPT
    CHKPT --> SLF
    SLF --> MR
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Checkpointing in DBMS. Why is it necessary? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A *checkpoint* is a mechanism in DBMS by which the system periodically forces all modified data from the main memory buffer to the disk and writes a special `<CHECKPOINT>` record to the log. It is necessary because:

1. It **bounds the recovery time** — recovery begins from the last checkpoint instead of the start of the log.
2. It **reduces the recovery cost** by avoiding redundant redo of long-completed transactions.
3. It **synchronizes memory and disk state** to a known consistent point.
4. It enables efficient implementation of the **transaction table** and **dirty page table** in modern recovery algorithms (e.g., ARIES).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students only mention "saving data to disk" without explaining *why* this is critical for crash recovery. Always link checkpointing to the **reduction of recovery work** and the **bounded log search space**.

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between `<CHECKPOINT>` record and `<COMMIT>` record in the log? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `<CHECKPOINT>` Record | `<COMMIT>` Record |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Purpose | Marks a periodic synchronization point | Marks successful end of a single transaction |
| Contents | List of active transactions + dirty page info | Transaction ID only |
| Frequency | Periodic (timer/log-size threshold) | Per transaction |
